title: 1 Kings 1:1–4
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 1:1–4 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 1:1–4 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 1:1–4 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

The storyteller wants to tell us about the history of the kings of Israel from the end of David's reign of Israel. The storyteller uses the books of 1 and 2 Kings to continue the story of the people of Israel that began in the books of Joshua, Judges, and 1 and 2 Samuel. First, the storyteller tells us about the history of the united kingdom of Israel. Then the storyteller tells us about the divided kingdoms of Israel in the north and Judah in the south. Then the storyteller tells us about the end of the kingdoms of Israel and Judah until the Babylonians destroyed Jerusalem. The storyteller starts his story by telling us about King David. David was king for a long time and was very old. David lived for many years and was still a king when he was very old.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What do the leaders of your community or nation do when they get very old? Do the leaders continue to lead, or do they stop and let someone else become the leader? Pause this audio here.

The storyteller shows that David did not have the strength and health to continue to be a strong and powerful king. But it was only David's body that was not working well. David could still think very well.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: If possible, talk about leaders who led your people or community even when they were old. What signs of old age did people see and talk about? How did people feel about having a leader who was very old? Pause this audio here.

When David was very old, David got cold very easily and did not get warm when people put covers or blankets over him.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about older people in your community. How do those people stay warm or try to get warm if they feel cold? Pause this audio here.

David's servants talked to David. These servants were more like advisors to David, or officials in David's palace. The servants told David that the servants would look for a young woman. The servants wanted the young woman to be unmarried and a virgin, someone who had not had sexual intercourse with a man. The servants wanted the young woman to come to be with David and to serve David by taking care of David. The servants wanted the young woman to share David's bed and to be in David's arms. We do not know what the servants wanted to happen when the woman was in David's arms. It is possible that the servants just wanted the woman to keep David warm. However, the servants may have wanted to see if David could have more strength and health by having sexual intercourse with a young woman. At the time of David, some people believed that when an older man had sexual intercourse with a young woman, the young woman would give the man more strength and make him younger.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about someone in your community who has married or shared their home with someone much younger than themself. What did people say when the person began the relationship? How did people think that the younger person would affect or change the older person? Pause this audio here.

The servants searched all the different parts of Israel for a beautiful young woman. We do not know if the servants searched for the woman or if they got other people to search for the woman. The servants wanted the woman to be physically beautiful. The servants found a woman called Abishag who lived in Shunem. Shunem was a village near Mount Gilboa, south-west of Lake Galilee.

Stop here and look at a map of Israel that shows the city of Jerusalem and the village of Shunem. Pause this audio here.

Abishag left Shunem and went to David in Jerusalem. Abishag served and looked after David. David did not know Abishag, which means David did not have sexual intercourse with Abishag. Abishag was not David's concubine or wife. We do not know if David chose not to have sexual intercourse with Abishag or if David was not able to have sexual intercourse with Abishag. The storyteller uses the information that David did not have sexual intercourse with Abishag as a sign to show us that David was not strong and powerful and was not able to rule Israel well.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 1:1–4 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has three scenes.

In the first scene: David is very old and his body is not working well so he cannot get warm. David's servants, or advisors, suggest to David that the servants find a young woman to look after David and help keep David warm.

In the second scene: The servants look for a beautiful young woman in all of Israel. The servants find Abishag from Shunem. The servants take Abishag to Jerusalem.

In the third scene: Abishag looks after David but David does not have sexual relations with Abishag.

The characters in this passage are:

  • David
  • David's servants, who are his advisors
  • And Abishag from Shunem

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

The storyteller starts the story at a time when David was very old. David was not just an older man, but David was at a time in his life when David's body was not working well because he was very old.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do you talk about someone who is very old? Pause this audio here.

David's body did not work very well, so David could not stay warm. David's body did not work well for some time, and it was a problem that David had more than one time. David's servants noticed that David's body was not working well to keep David warm. The servants were people who worked for and served David to help David lead Israel. These servants were not like the servants who did domestic tasks like cleaning or cooking.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What do you call people who work closely with and help the most important leader in your people group or nation? Pause this audio here.

The servants used very polite language to talk to David. The servants referred to David as another person in the conversation, even though the servants talked directly to David. The servants used a formal title, "our lord the king," to refer to David instead of using David's name.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do you change your language when you talk to someone who is very important and you want to show respect? Pause this audio here.

The servants organised a search for a beautiful, young, unmarried woman throughout all of Israel. The servants wanted the woman to be a female servant or helper for David. The woman would help David by lying in David's bosom. A bosom is the chest of a person.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What do you call a young, unmarried woman who has not had sexual intercourse? Pause this audio here.

The servants found Abishag in the village of Shunem and took Abishag to King David in Jerusalem. Just as David was very old, Abishag was very beautiful. Abishag was more beautiful than most beautiful women.

Stop here and look at a map of Israel and find Shunem and Jerusalem, if necessary. Pause this audio here.

Abishag took care of David. Abishag also waited on David, which means Abishag did small tasks to help David and served David. David did not know Abishag. This means that David did not have sexual intercourse with Abishag.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Sometimes people use a polite word or phrase to refer to things that people may feel upset or embarrassed to talk about. What words or phrases, if any, would you use in this way to talk about sexual intercourse? Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 1:1–4 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has three scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • David
  • David's servants, who are his advisors
  • And Abishag from Shunem

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

David is very old and his body is not working well so David cannot get warm. David's servants suggest to David that the servants find a young woman to look after David and help warm David.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I have lived a very long time; or
  • I feel cold. Even with extra blankets, I cannot get warm; or
  • My body is getting weak but I can still rule Israel.

Ask the person playing David's servants, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Maybe a young woman will warm our king and help him to feel younger; or
  • We need to do something to help our king; or
  • We are worried that David is too old to be king.

Continue the drama.

The servants look for a beautiful young woman in all of Israel. The servants find Abishag from Shunem. The servants take Abishag to Jerusalem.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing David's servants, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We need to find a very beautiful young woman for our king; or
  • The woman must not be married to anybody; or
  • Abishag is very beautiful. Abishag must come to be with our king.

Ask the person playing Abishag from Shunem, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel honoured that people chose me to look after our king; or
  • I am worried to go from my small village to the city of Jerusalem; or
  • What will I need to do to serve my king?

Continue the drama.

Abishag looks after David but David does not have sexual relations with Abishag.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Abishag, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I lie in my king's bed and keep my king warm; or
  • I am still a virgin; or
  • Am I a servant? The king did not make me his concubine or wife.

Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Abishag is very beautiful and very helpful to me; or
  • I like how Abishag looks after me; or
  • I have not had sexual relations with Abishag.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 1:1–4 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

King David was very old.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for king. Look up king in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. For more information on David, refer to the Master Glossary. Pause this audio here.

David could not stay warm even when people put covers or clothes for a bed over David.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for covers. Pause this audio here.

David's servants, or advisors, decided to look for a young virgin woman to serve David. These men are a special kind of servant who look after the personal needs of a leader and advise the leader.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for servants. You can use the word for servants, which is also in the Master Glossary. Or, you can use a word that means advisors or people who help a leader make good decisions about the leader's personal life. Pause this audio here.

The servants looked for a young virgin woman throughout Israel. A virgin is a person who is unmarried and has not had sexual intercourse with anyone. You will remember that Israel is the nation of people that Yahweh specially chose.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for Israel and for virgin. Israel is in the Master Glossary. If you have already translated these words in another book of the Bible, use the same words that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

The servants found Abishag in Shunem and brought her to David. Abishag took care of David and waited on or served David.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 1:5–10
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 1:5–10 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 1:5–10 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 1:5–10 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

You will remember from the previous story that David is very old. David's body is not working very well, and when David's servants gave a beautiful young virgin to David, David did not have sexual relations with her. The storyteller gives us this information to show that David might not be able to rule Israel well and will die soon.

In this story, the storyteller starts to tell us about David's oldest living son, Adonijah. Adonijah, David's fourth son, was very proud and thought that he was the right person to be the next king of Israel. Adonijah boasted that he wanted to be king. Adonijah's mother was Haggith. You will remember from 2 Samuel that David's first son, Amnon, and David's third son, Absalom, are dead. We do not know what happened to Chileab, David's second son, but he probably died as a child. People thought David was very old and not able to rule Israel. Adonijah decided to be the next king.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What happens when your most important leader gets old and cannot lead anymore? What happens if the people lose confidence in the leader's ability to lead? Who decides which person will become the most important leader next? Pause this audio here.

Adonijah told people that he wanted to be king. Adonijah decided to make himself look like a king, so Adonijah gathered together chariots and men to look after the horses for the chariots. A chariot is a two-wheeled cart that two horses pull along. A man or two men stand in the chariot to control the horses. A horse has four legs. People ride on horses, and sometimes horses pull carts or other wheeled vehicles along. In David's time, people used chariots in war to attack people, but important people also travelled in chariots during ceremonies and parades.

Stop here and look at a picture of a person driving a chariot as a group. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and look at a picture of a horse as a group. Pause this audio here.

Adonijah also gathered 50 men to run in front of the chariots when Adonijah was in one of the chariots. The 50 men would help to clear a path for the chariots to move along.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How would you know that your most important leader was going past you? What vehicles or animals does the leader ride, and who rides with the leader? Pause this audio here.

The storyteller pauses the action in the story and gives information about Adonijah to help us understand why Adonijah was proud and thought he should be king. The storyteller tells us that David never made Adonijah unhappy by questioning why Adonijah did something. The storyteller is telling us that David did not discipline Adonijah and did not stop Adonijah from doing whatever Adonijah wanted to do. The storyteller also tells us that Adonijah was a very handsome man. People around Adonijah probably gave Adonijah special treatment because he was very handsome. Also, Adonijah was the son born after Absalom. After Absalom died, Adonijah was the next oldest son of David, and people gave Adonijah special treatment as the eldest living son of the king. Adonijah was used to people giving him what he wanted, and Adonijah believed that he should be the next king.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about someone you know who believed that people should treat that person differently and better, or give them something special, because they were the oldest child. How did the person make their feelings known to people around them? What did the person do to make you notice them? Pause this audio here.

The storyteller returns to the story. Adonijah discussed his desire to become king with some important people in Israel. Joab was the leader of Israel's army, and you will remember that Zeruiah was Joab's mother. You will also remember that Joab did not always follow David's commands. Abiathar was a very important priest in Jerusalem. We do not know why Abiathar supported Adonijah. It is possible that, as David got older, David preferred Zadok the priest and made Abiathar unhappy. Joab and Abiathar decided to support Adonijah and help Adonijah become king.

The storyteller now tells us that a different group of people did not support Adonijah. Zadok was an important priest in Jerusalem and had a good relationship with David. Benaiah was the leader of a special group of fighters, the Kerethites and Pelethites, who protected David. Nathan the prophet was an important advisor to David. You may remember that Nathan gave David's son, Solomon, a special name that means "God loves him," to show David and Israel that Yahweh favored Solomon. We do not know who Shimei and Rei were. Shimei was possibly someone who later became an administrator for Solomon, but he was not the same person as Shimei, son of Gera, who cursed David. David's mighty men were David's special group of guards, or fighting men.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about a time when one group of people supported a person to do something, but another group of people did not support the person. Pause this audio here.

Sometime after Adonijah discussed his desire to be king with Joab and Abiathar, Adonijah prepared a special ceremony. Adonijah gathered together some animals to sacrifice. We do not know why Adonijah sacrificed animals, but it was probably part of a ceremony to make Adonijah king of Israel. Adonijah was trying to do everything to make himself look like the king of Israel, even though Yahweh did not choose Adonijah to be the king of Israel. You will remember that a sacrifice is a gift to God and that people often put a sacrifice on a stone platform or flat rock. Usually, a priest would sacrifice the animals, and Adonijah probably asked a priest to sacrifice the animals. Adonijah sacrificed sheep, oxen, including male, female, and young animals, and special male cattle that people had made fat and ready for people to sacrifice. Oxen and cattle are the same type of animal.

Stop here and look at a picture of a sheep as a group. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and look at a picture of oxen or cattle as a group. Pause this audio here.

Adonijah sacrificed the animals on a rock called the Stone of Zoheleth or the Serpent's Stone near the Spring of En Rogel. En Rogel was near Jerusalem, and the Israelites used the spring to mark the border between the lands of Judah and Benjamin. The Israelites thought springs of water were special places. A spring is a place where clean water comes up out of the ground.

Stop here as a group and look at a map of Judah and Benjamin in Israel. Find Jerusalem and the Spring of En Rogel. Pause this audio here.

Adonijah invited important people who supported him to the ceremony. The storyteller first tells us that Adonijah invited all of Adonijah's brothers. These brothers had the same father, David, but had different mothers. All Adonijah's brothers at the ceremony were King David's sons. Adonijah also invited special officials who served King David in the land of Judah. We do not know why Adonijah invited the officials from Judah, but you will remember that Adonijah was born in Hebron, in Judah. Judah was the name of one of Jacob's sons, and therefore the name of one of the 12 tribes of Israel. Each tribe had a special territory of land called by the name of the tribe.

Adonijah did not invite other people who were important and supported David, but not Adonijah. Adonijah did not invite Nathan, Benaiah, the mighty men, or Adonijah's brother, Solomon. Solomon was also the son of David but did not have the same mother as Adonijah. You will remember from the end of 1 Chronicles that David has probably already appointed Solomon to be the next king of Israel.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 1:5–10 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has four scenes.

In the first scene: Adonijah told people that Adonijah wanted to be the next king. Adonijah gathered chariots, horsemen, and 50 men together to make himself look like a king.

In the second scene: The storyteller gives us reasons why Adonijah was confident and proud. David never questioned anything Adonijah did. Also, Adonijah was handsome, and Adonijah was David's oldest living son.

In the third scene: Adonijah talked to Joab and Abiathar about Adonijah's desire to become king. Joab and Abiathar supported Adonijah to be king. Zadok, Benaiah, Nathan, Shimei, Rei, and David's mighty men did not support Adonijah to be king.

In the fourth scene: Adonijah prepared a ceremony on the Stone of Zoheleth near the Spring of En Rogel. Adonijah sacrificed animals and invited his brothers and the king's officials from Judah. Adonijah did not invite Nathan, Benaiah, David's mighty men, or Solomon.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Adonijah
  • The storyteller
  • Joab and Abiathar, Adonijah's supporters
  • Zadok, Benaiah, Nathan, Shimei, Rei, and David's mighty men
  • Adonijah's brothers
  • The royal officials from Judah
  • And Solomon

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

This story happened when David was very old, and people were worried that David was not strong enough to be king. The storyteller tells us this story immediately after he tells us that David was very old and that David's body was weak. Adonijah responds to David's old age and weakness and decides that he should be king. Adonijah's mother was Haggith, one of David's wives.

Adonijah raises himself up or puts himself forward. When a person puts themselves forward or makes themselves high, the person thinks that they are very good at doing something, and that other people should see how good that person is. The person might boast or say lots of good things about themselves and be proud. Adonijah tells people that Adonijah will be the next king.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How would you describe what Adonijah does to make people know that Adonijah wants to be king? Pause this audio here.

After Adonijah boasts to people and says that he will be the king, Adonijah gets ready chariots, horsemen, and 50 men to run in front of Adonijah's chariot. Adonijah wants to look like an important and rich king when he moves from one place to another.

The storyteller pauses the narrative to give us reasons why Adonijah thinks he should be king and why Adonijah decides to behave like a king. The storyteller tells us that David never made Adonijah unhappy by questioning Adonijah's behaviour or choices. Adonijah always did everything that Adonijah wanted to do. Adonijah was very handsome. A handsome man has a very attractive face and body that people like to look at. Adonijah was more handsome than many handsome people.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Have one person in your group tell a story about another person. In the middle of the story, have the person pause and give the group some more information about the character of the person in their story. Then have the person continue to tell the story. How does the additional information about the person's character help you to understand the story? Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you say that Adonijah was a very handsome man? Pause this audio here.

Lastly, the storyteller reminds us that Haggith gave birth to Adonijah after Maacah gave birth to Absalom. Adonijah was David's fourth son and David's oldest living son.

Adonijah conferred with or talked to Joab and Abiathar to find out if Joab and Abiathar would support Adonijah to be king of Israel. Abiathar was a priest, a male descendant of Aaron who served Yahweh by making sacrifices to Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do you describe a conversation when someone talks to a person or people to find out what they think about an idea or action? Pause this audio here.

Joab and Abiathar decided to support Adonijah to become the new king. The storyteller does not tell us that Adonijah talked to Zadok and the other people, but they all knew that Adonijah wanted to be king. Zadok, Benaiah, Nathan the prophet, Shimei, Rei, and David's mighty men did not support Adonijah, and they did not join Adonijah's group of supporters. You may remember that a prophet was someone who heard directly from Yahweh and gave Yahweh's messages to the Israelites.

Sometime after Joab and Abiathar supported Adonijah to be king, Adonijah decided to sacrifice animals as a gift to Yahweh. Adonijah went to a place called the Stone of Zoheleth. The word Zoheleth is similar to the word that means to crawl and glide in the original language. Some people call it the Serpent's Stone because a snake moves in such a way that it slides and glides over the earth. Some people think that the rock was smooth and easy to slide over. Other people think that the rock was near where the rock slid down a slope.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What will you call the Stone of Zoholeth or the Serpent's Stone in your translation? Pause this audio here.

This stone was near the spring called En Rogel. A spring is a place where water naturally comes up through the ground.

Adonijah wanted important people to see the sacrifices and possibly share the meat as part of a feast to celebrate Adonijah becoming king. Adonijah invited all of the rest of King David's sons. The storyteller calls these sons Adonijah's brothers, but although they all had the same father, they had different mothers than Adonijah.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What do you call the relationship between people who have the same father but a different mother to each other? Pause this audio here.

Royal officials from Judah also went to the Stone of Zoheleth. The officials were not part of the royal family, but they worked for David to manage David's kingdom in Judah.

Adonijah did not invite Nathan the prophet, Benaiah, David's mighty men, or Solomon, his brother. Solomon and Adonijah also had different mothers.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 1:5–10 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has four scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Adonijah
  • The storyteller
  • Joab and Abiathar, Adonijah's supporters
  • Zadok, Benaiah, Nathan, Shimei, Rei, and David's mighty men
  • Adonijah's brothers
  • The royal officials from Judah
  • And Solomon

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Adonijah told people that he wanted to be the next king. Adonijah gathered chariots, horsemen, and 50 men together to make Adonijah look like a king.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Adonijah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I think that I should be the next king; or
  • Look at me! I am rich and powerful and will make a good king; or
  • Make way for me! Look at all my men making a way for me to come.

Continue the drama.

The storyteller gives us reasons why Adonijah was confident and proud. David never questioned anything Adonijah did. Also, Adonijah was handsome and was David's oldest living son.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the storyteller, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • David was not a good parent to Adonijah; or
  • David should have punished Adonijah when Adonijah did something wrong or was too proud; or
  • People always said yes to Adonijah and did what he wanted because of who Adonijah was and what he looked like.

Continue the drama.

Adonijah talked to Joab and Abiathar about Adonijah's desire to become king. Joab and Abiathar supported Adonijah to be king. Zadok, Benaiah, Nathan, Shimei, Rei, and David's mighty men did not support Adonijah to be king.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Adonijah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Will you support me to be the next king? or
  • I am glad that powerful people like Joab and Abiathar support me and will help me be king; or
  • I am disappointed that Zadok and the other people do not support me. I hope that those people are not a problem for me.

Ask the person playing Joab and Abiathar, Adonijah's supporters, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • It is time to have a new and strong king; or
  • If Adonijah is king, we will be powerful too; or
  • Adonijah is the oldest son of David.

Ask the person playing Zadok, Benaiah, Nathan, Shimei, Rei, and David's mighty men, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Yahweh did not choose Adonijah to be king; or
  • We are worried that Adonijah thinks that he will be the next king of Israel; or
  • We know that Solomon should be the next king.

Continue the drama.

Adonijah prepared a ceremony on the Stone of Zoheleth near the Spring of En Rogel. Adonijah sacrificed animals and invited his brothers and the king's officials from Judah. Adonijah did not invite Nathan, Benaiah, David's mighty men, or Solomon.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Adonijah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • This ceremony will make me king of Israel; or
  • All of my brothers will call me king of Israel; or
  • The officials from Judah will see that I am now king and tell other people.

Ask the person playing Adonijah's brothers, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We will show respect to Adonijah because he is older than us; or
  • We do not know if Adonijah is king or not.

Ask the person playing the royal officials from Judah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Is Adonijah king? Is David still king? or
  • Adonijah is making a great sacrifice to Yahweh; or
  • We wonder what will happen now?

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 1:5–10 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Adonijah put himself forward and wanted to be king. Use the same word or phrase for king as you used in previous passages. For more information on king, refer to the Master Glossary.

Adonijah got chariots ready.

Stop here and look at a picture of a person driving a chariot as a group, if needed. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for chariots. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Adonijah got horsemen or charioteers ready. The horsemen or charioteers rode in the chariots and controlled the horses.

Stop here and look at a picture of a horse as a group, if needed. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for horsemen. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

David never got angry with Adonijah. Some translations say that David never rebuked Adonijah. When a person rebukes someone else, the person tells that person that what they have done is wrong. David did not say anything to or punish Adonijah when Adonijah did or said something wrong.

Stop here and discuss as a group what words or phrases you will use to say that David never got angry with or rebuked Adonijah. Pause this audio here.

Abiathar was a priest, a descendant of Aaron who served Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for priest. Look up priest in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Nathan the prophet did not support Adonijah. A prophet is a person who gives messages from God to the people.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for prophet. Look up prophet in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

David's special guard or mighty men did not join Adonijah. The special guard is around 30 brave and special soldiers who served David in David's army. These men were great fighters for David. You may remember these men from 2 Samuel 23:8.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for mighty men, or special guard. If you have already translated this phrase in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Adonijah sacrificed animals. Adonijah killed and burned animals as a gift to God.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for sacrifice. Look up sacrifice in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Adonijah sacrificed sheep.

Stop here and look at a picture of a sheep as a group, if needed. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for sheep. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Adonijah sacrificed cattle or oxen. This is the general name for all types of cows or oxen, including male, female, and young animals.

Stop here and look at a picture of oxen as a group. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for cattle or oxen. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Adonijah sacrificed fattened calves. People gave young cows or oxen extra food to make the cows fat and ready for other people to sacrifice the cows.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for fattened calves. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Adonijah sacrificed animals at the Stone of Zoheleth or the Serpent's Stone. Zoheleth means to glide, slide, or crawl in the original language.

Stop here and discuss as a group what place name you will use for Stone of Zoheleth. Pause this audio here.

Adonijah invited all his brothers. Adonijah's brothers all had the same father but had different mothers.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for brother, especially in this situation. Pause this audio here.

Adonijah invited the royal officials of Judah, the area of Israel where the tribe of Judah lived. The royal officials were men in Judah who served and worked in Judah for the king of Israel.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for Judah. For more information about Judah, refer to the Master Glossary. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller uses the word "servant" for these royal officials, even though they did not do the daily tasks of house cleaning or food preparation. These men served the king by advising him and helping him govern the country.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for royal officials. If you have already translated this word or phrase in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. If you choose to use the word servant here, refer to the Master Glossary for more information.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 1:11–27
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 1:11–27 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 1:11–27 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 1:11–27 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

This story happens when David is very old, and some people in Israel think Israel needs a new king. You will remember from the earlier story that Adonijah, David's oldest living son, decided to be king. Adonijah organised a special ceremony to make himself the king of Israel. Adonijah invited his brothers and some officials to the ceremony.

In this story, Nathan the prophet hears about Adonijah's ceremony. Nathan goes to talk to Bathsheba, the wife of David and the mother of Solomon. We do not know where Nathan and Bathsheba are when they talk together. Nathan worries about Adonijah becoming king and knows that Nathan and Bathsheba need to take action to stop Adonijah. Nathan asks Bathsheba if Bathsheba knows or has heard from someone that Adonijah has made himself king of Israel. Nathan also says that David does not know that Adonijah made himself king. David did not choose Adonijah to be king of Israel after David. Nathan talks to Bathsheba to make a plan and to advise Bathsheba on how to keep herself and Solomon safe. Nathan knows that Adonijah thinks that Solomon is Adonijah's rival to become king of Israel. At the time of the story, it was common for new kings to kill a rival, someone who might challenge the new king's right to be king.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Talk about a time you met someone and made a plan together to talk to another person about something important. Pause this audio here.

Nathan tells Bathsheba what to do. Nathan wants Bathsheba to go to King David immediately. Nathan wants Bathsheba to be very polite and respectful of David and to remind David that Bathsheba is loyal to David as the king of Israel. Nathan tells Bathsheba to ask if David promised Bathsheba that Solomon would be the next king after David and sit on David's throne. There is no record in the Bible of David promising to Bathsheba that Solomon would be the next king after David. Only someone who is king can sit on David's throne, his special seat. It is very likely that David made the promise to Bathsheba. It is unlikely that Nathan and Bathsheba are pretending that David made the promise. Nathan uses Bathsheba to tell David what Adonijah is doing. Nathan wants Bathsheba to tell David about Adonijah in a way that pretends that David already knows what Adonijah has been doing. Bathsheba does not want to embarrass David by telling David that he does not know what is happening in his kingdom.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Tell a story about a time when you told someone something in a way that was kind and considerate so that the person was not embarrassed to hear the information. Pause this audio here.

Nathan tells Bathsheba that he, Nathan, will also go to David. Nathan will tell David the same things as Bathsheba. Nathan will confirm what Bathsheba said to David so that David believes Bathsheba.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story about a time when one person told another person something important, but the person who heard the information needed to hear the information from another person as well to believe what the first person said. Why did they need to hear the information from more than one person? Pause this audio here.

Bathsheba goes from the place where she spoke with Nathan to the king in his room. David is in a room where David sleeps and rests, in the middle of his large house or palace. The storyteller reminds us that, at the time of the story, David is very old and Abishag is looking after the king's personal needs. We do not know if Abishag is with David when Bathsheba talks to David. Bathsheba speaks and acts very respectfully to David. Bathsheba bows lower than she needs to so that David will know that Bathsheba needs to talk about something very important. David knows Bathsheba needs something from David. Bathsheba calls David "lord" and herself "David's servant" to show respect to David. Again, this shows David that Bathsheba needs to talk about something very important.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do you meet, greet, and show respect to someone who is very important? Pause this audio here.

Bathsheba reminds David of David's promise to Bathsheba that David spoke to Bathsheba at an earlier time. Bathsheba uses David's own words to Bathsheba to remind David of his promise. Bathsheba wants David to pay attention to the situation in Israel. David is unaware that Adonijah has become king. Bathsheba tells David about Adonijah's special ceremony, where Adonijah sacrificed many animals. Adonijah sacrificed cattle, fattened young cattle, and sheep. Bathsheba tells David that Adonijah invited all of King David's children. Bathsheba means David's male children. Adonijah did not invite all of David's sons because Adonijah did not invite Solomon. Bathsheba tells David that Adonijah invited Abiathar the priest and Joab, commander of David's army. Bathsheba is telling David which people support Adonijah. Bathsheba tells David that Adonijah did not invite Solomon. Bathsheba wants David to know that Solomon does not support Adonijah and that Adonijah thinks that Solomon is a rival for being king of Israel. Solomon is loyal to David.

Bathsheba tells David that all the eyes of the people in Israel are watching David. Everyone in Israel is watching and waiting to find out who David will choose to be the next king after David. The next person to sit on David's throne, or special seat of authority, will be the next king.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Tell a story about a time when people were waiting to find out who would be the next most important leader in your community or nation. Pause this audio here.

Bathsheba warns David about the time after David is sleeping with his ancestors or is dead. At the time of David, it was normal for the people to bury the body of a dead king alongside the bodies of other people in that king's family who had died before, so people talked about when someone died as "sleeping with his ancestors."

If David does not say that Solomon will be the next king, Bathsheba and Solomon will be in danger. Adonijah will treat Bathsheba and Solomon as criminals, people who broke the law, because Bathsheba and Solomon do not support Adonijah as king and want Solomon to be king. At the time of David, a king treated people as criminals if they did not support the king.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What happens to a person in your country or community who does not support the most important leader and wants to be the most important leader themself? Pause this audio here.

While Bathsheba was speaking with King David, Nathan arrived to speak with David. King David's servants told King David that Nathan wanted to speak with David. We know from later in the story that Bathsheba leaves David's room when Nathan arrives. Nathan uses the same words as Bathsheba to ask David if David has said that Adonijah will be the next king. Nathan tells David that the feast is happening at the same time as Nathan is talking to David. Nathan says that David's sons, the commanders of the army and Abiathar are showing their loyalty to Adonijah by calling Adonijah king and wishing Adonijah a long life as king.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do people in your nation show their loyalty to your most important leader? Pause this audio here.

Nathan tells David which people Adonijah did not invite to the ceremony. Nathan wants David to know which people are loyal to David. Nathan asks David if Adonijah is doing what David wants Adonijah to do. Nathan questions whether David has let Adonijah become king without involving his most trusted servants, such as Nathan. Nathan uses questions to tell David what is happening while showing respect to David.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 1:11–27 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has three scenes.

In the first scene: Nathan tells Bathsheba that Adonijah made himself king and they are in danger. Nathan tells Bathsheba to ask King David if David had promised that Solomon would be king and why Adonijah is ruling. Nathan says he will talk to David, too.

In the second scene: Bathsheba talks to David. Bathsheba bows and asks why Adonijah is king when David promised Solomon would be the next king. Bathsheba tells David about Adonijah's supporters and says Israel is waiting for David to choose the next king. Bathsheba warns David that if he does not choose the next king, Adonijah's supporters will kill Bathsheba and Solomon.

In the third scene: Nathan comes to speak with David. Nathan bows and asks if David chose Adonijah to be king. Nathan tells David about Adonijah's ceremony and who was there. Nathan also says who was not there. Then Nathan asks if David made Adonijah king without telling Nathan.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Nathan the prophet
  • Bathsheba, David's wife and Solomon's mother
  • King David
  • Abishag the Shunammite
  • And David's servants

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

This passage probably takes place in David's home, the city of David, in Jerusalem, on the same day as the previous story, where Adonijah sacrificed animals with his supporters. Nathan goes to talk to Bathsheba, Solomon's mother. At this time in the story, the storyteller does not use Nathan's formal title of prophet. Nathan asks if Bathsheba has not heard that Adonijah is king, and David does not know. Nathan uses the question to ask why Bathsheba is not trying to stop Adonijah from becoming the next king. If Bathsheba knows, Bathsheba should take action. Nathan is worried that Solomon will not be the next king and that Adonijah will kill Bathsheba and Solomon. Nathan suggests a way for Bathsheba to talk to David. Nathan suggests what Bathsheba should say to David. Nathan wants Bathsheba to be very polite and show lots of respect to David. This will show that Bathsheba is loyal to David. Nathan wants Bathsheba to use questions to find out what David wants to happen. Nathan wants Bathsheba to remind David of David's promise to Bathsheba. David promised Bathsheba that Solomon would be the next king and sit on David's throne. A throne is a special chair that a king or the most important leader sits on to do official business.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: If your king or most important leader has a special seat to sit on, what do you call this seat? Pause this audio here.

Nathan wants Bathsheba to ask David why Adonijah is king. Nathan and Bathsheba will use questions to David to suggest that Nathan and Bathsheba might be wrong about Solomon being the next king. Nathan and Bathsheba are sure that Solomon is supposed to be the next king, and that David does not know what is happening. Nathan does not want to embarrass or shame King David. Nathan tells Bathsheba that Nathan will "add his word," or talk to David immediately after Bathsheba and say the same things as Bathsheba. This will help David to know that what Bathsheba said was true.

In the second scene, Bathsheba goes to David's room, where Abishag cares for David. Bathsheba shows a lot of respect to David when Bathsheba goes to see David. Bathsheba bows low to show respect for David. We do not know if Bathsheba bows her body from the waist or if she kneels or lies down in front of David with her face to the floor. Bathsheba does this to show loyalty and great respect for David. David knows that Bathsheba wants something important. Bathsheba calls David "my Lord" and calls herself "David's servant". In this way, Bathsheba uses very polite language to show that this is an important visit and that Bathsheba is loyal to David. Bathsheba says David made a promise to Bathsheba in the name of Yahweh, David's God. Bathsheba wants to remind David that Yahweh, David's God, witnessed David's promise. We do not know if Bathsheba worshipped Yahweh.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Tell a story about a time when you went to talk to someone and reminded the person of something the person said to you at an earlier time. Pay attention to how you talk about what the person said to you at the earlier time. Pause this audio here.

Bathsheba tells David that Adonijah was making lots of animal sacrifices and inviting Adonijah's supporters in order to make himself king. Bathsheba tells David that Adonijah did not invite Solomon and that Solomon is also David's servant. Bathsheba says that all eyes are on David to know who will sit on the throne, to be king after David.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Think about the discussion you had in step two about waiting to find out who will be the next leader. How will you talk about the fact that everyone is watching and waiting to see what a person will do? Pause this audio here.

Bathsheba warns David about what might happen to Bathsheba and Solomon when David is dead. Bathsheba uses a polite way to talk about David's death when she refers to a time when David is sleeping or lying to rest with his ancestors.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do you say that someone royal or very important is dead? Pause this audio here.

If Adonijah is king, Adonijah and his supporters will treat Bathsheba and Solomon as they would treat people who break the law or are guilty because they did something wrong.

Immediately after Bathsheba finishes talking, Nathan arrives. David does not have time to respond to Bathsheba. King David's servants tell King David that Nathan the prophet wants to speak to David. The servants announce Nathan with his title as a prophet, a messenger from God. We do not know when Bathsheba leaves the room but she does not stay in the room when Nathan speaks to David. Nathan enters David's room. Nathan also bows low to David and touches his face to the floor to show respect to David.

Nathan follows his plan and questions if Adonijah is doing what David wants Adonijah to do. Even though Nathan knows that David does not know what has happened, Nathan gives David the opportunity to say it is what David wants. Nathan is helping David to avoid embarrassment. Nathan also tells David some of the same information that Bathsheba has told him about who is there at the feast. Nathan says that Adonijah's supporters are wishing Adonijah a long life as king to show their loyalty to Adonijah.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What words or phrases do you use to show loyalty to your king or most important leader? Pause this audio here.

Nathan also says that Adonijah did not invite Nathan, Zadok the priest, Benaiah son of Jehoiada, and Solomon. Nathan calls himself and Solomon "David's servants" to show that they are both loyal to David. Nathan asks David if David let Adonijah become king but did not tell his own servants, Nathan and Solomon, who will be the next king. Nathan does not believe that David did this. If David did something as important as naming the next king without involving the people who support David, this would upset and hurt David's supporters, such as Nathan.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 1:11–27 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has three scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Nathan the prophet
  • Bathsheba, David's wife and Solomon's mother
  • King David
  • Abishag the Shunammite
  • And David's servants

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Nathan tells Bathsheba that Adonijah made himself king and that they are in danger. Nathan tells Bathsheba to ask King David if David promised Solomon would be king and why Adonijah is ruling. Nathan says he will talk to David, too.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Nathan, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am really worried. I know that Yahweh wants Solomon to be the next king of Israel; or
  • Bathsheba and Solomon are in danger if Adonijah becomes king. We need to work together to stop Adonijah; or
  • David needs to know what Adonijah is doing. I know that David wants Solomon to be the next king.

Ask the person playing Bathsheba, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I know Adonijah has sacrificed lots of animals and made himself king, but I do not know what I can do; or
  • Adonijah will kill my son Solomon when my husband David dies; or
  • I am so relieved that Nathan has come to help us talk to David about Adonijah.

Continue the drama.

Bathsheba talks to David. Bathsheba bows and asks why Adonijah is king when David promised Solomon would be the next king. Bathsheba tells David about Adonijah's supporters and says Israel is waiting for David to choose the next king. Bathsheba warns David that if he does not choose the next king, people will kill Bathsheba and Solomon.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Bathsheba, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Years ago, I cared for my husband, but now Abishag looks after him; or
  • David is my king as well as my husband, and I love and respect him; or
  • Nathan and I know David promised Solomon would be the next king. We need David to understand that the wrong person is trying to be king; or
  • I need David to help us because Adonijah will kill Solomon and me if Adonijah is the next king; or
  • I need David to tell everyone what David wants.

Ask the person playing Abishag the Shunammite, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Something important is happening; or
  • Bathsheba is upset and worried; or
  • My king is getting so old.

Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Why is Bathsheba bowing down to me when she knows that she can just come to me? or
  • Adonijah is foolish and proud; or
  • Adonijah is wrong. Solomon must be the next king of Israel.

Continue the drama.

Nathan comes to speak with David. Nathan bows and asks if David chose Adonijah to be king. Nathan tells David about Adonijah's ceremony and who was there. Nathan also says who was not there. Then Nathan asks if David made Adonijah king without telling Nathan.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing David's servants, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Something is happening. Nathan the prophet looks worried; or
  • Quick! We must announce Nathan to our king; or
  • Let Bathsheba pass, because Bathsheba is leaving now.

Ask the person playing Nathan the prophet, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I must let David know what is happening, but I do not want to embarrass David; or
  • Adonijah has some powerful followers, but we have Zadok and Benaiah who support Solomon; or
  • What if David does not trust me? Maybe David does want Adonijah to be king and did not talk about it with me; or
  • I am loyal to David and will serve him all his life.

Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Now Nathan has arrived! Bathsheba told me the truth, and we have a big problem; or
  • I will make it clear to my sons and commanders of my army who I want to be the next king; or
  • I am so thankful for my loyal servant, Nathan. Nathan serves me well and has saved my wife Bathsheba and my son Solomon.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 1:11–27 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Adonijah has become king. Use the same word or phrase for king as you used in previous passages. For more information on king, refer to the Master Glossary.

Nathan calls David his lord. A lord is someone who is a master over other people, who has full authority, and who deserves for people to give him respect. When you address someone as "Lord," you are saying that person has authority over you.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for lord. Look up lord in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Nathan gives advice to Bathsheba. Nathan tells Bathsheba what Nathan thinks Bathsheba should do.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for advice. Pause this audio here.

Nathan wants Bathsheba to ask David if he swore to Bathsheba. If somebody swears something, they make a solemn promise or oath to a person or people.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for swear. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Look up oath in the Master Glossary for more information about making a strong promise. Pause this audio here.

David swore by Yahweh. Yahweh is God's personal name. In this passage, the storyteller calls God "Yahweh." Be sure to translate Yahweh in the same way as you have in previous passages, and remember that Yahweh is in the Master Glossary.

Yahweh is David's God, the God of Israel. God is the general title for the creator God.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for God. Look up God and Israel in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Nathan wants Bathsheba to call herself David's servant. A servant is a person who does work for someone else. Bathsheba does not work for David but does what David tells her to do because David is the king. Bathsheba calls herself a female servant to show respect to David. Later in the story, Nathan calls himself and Solomon male servants of David.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for servant. Look up servant in the Master Glossary for more information, and discuss if you need a separate word for female and male servants. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

David had promised Bathsheba that Solomon would sit on David's throne. A throne is a special seat for a king.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for throne. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Abishag is attending, or helping, David. Bathsheba bows very low to the king to show respect to the king.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for "bowed very low to show respect." If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Look up bow in the Master Glossary for more information. Pause this audio here.

Adonijah sacrificed cattle, fattened calves, and sheep. Use the same words or phrases for sacrificed, cattle, fattened calves, and sheep as you used in previous passages. For more information on sacrifice, refer to the Master Glossary.

Adonijah invited Abiathar the priest. Use the same word or phrase for priest as you used in previous passages. For more information on priest, refer to the Master Glossary.

Adonijah invited Joab, the commander of the army. A commander was a military leader who carried a staff to show he was a leader.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for commander. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

When David is dead, and people have buried David, people will treat Bathsheba and Solomon as criminals or as people who are guilty and who deserve to have Adonijah punish them.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for criminal. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Nathan the prophet arrives to speak to David. Use the same word or phrase for prophet as you used in previous passages. For more information on prophet, refer to the Master Glossary.

Nathan also tells David about Adonijah's ceremony and asks David if David wants Adonijah to be the next king of Israel.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 1:28–37
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 1:28–37 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 1:28–37 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 1:28–37 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

You will remember from the earlier stories that Adonijah was having a ceremony to become king of Israel. Nathan and Bathsheba knew about Adonijah's ceremony and went to talk to King David. Nathan and Bathsheba wanted David to know about Adonijah's ceremony and the people who supported Adonijah. Nathan and Bathsheba also wanted David to confirm Solomon as the next king of Israel after David. Nathan and Bathsheba told David the names of people who still supported David.

After David heard from Bathsheba and then from Nathan, David responded to what Bathsheba and Nathan told David. First, David asked his servants to bring Bathsheba into David's room. The servants brought Bathsheba to David. This time, Bathsheba stands in front of David to hear what David says to Bathsheba. Bathsheba does not bow down deeply. King David takes an oath. David makes a strong promise to Bathsheba and asks Yahweh to witness David's promise. David is sure that Yahweh is a living God and saves David from enemies and difficulties. Bathsheba can be just as sure that David will keep David's promise. David uses the same words to make his strong promise as David used at other times in his life.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: If possible, listen to your translation of 2 Samuel 4:9 and notice how David makes his oath. Pause this audio here.

David says to Bathsheba that today, David will fulfill the promise David made to Bathsheba some time before. We do not know when David made his promise. Sometime in the past, David promised Bathsheba that Solomon, Bathsheba's son, would be the next king of Israel. David says that David will make Solomon the next king today, and Solomon will sit on David's throne instead of David.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Talk about a promise that someone made to you in the past. As you talk about the promise, share the actual words the person spoke to make the promise. Pause this audio here.

Bathsheba shows David that she is very thankful when she bows down very low to David with her face touching the floor. We do not know if Bathsheba kneels on the floor and bows to place her face on the floor or if she lies down on the floor. Bathsheba speaks a blessing, words of praise, to David. Although Bathsheba says, "May my lord King David live forever," Bathsheba does not expect King David to live forever because he is very old and Bathsheba knows that he will die soon. Bathsheba's words show that Bathsheba is loyal to David.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What words or phrases would you use to celebrate or show thanks to your most important leader? Pause this audio here.

Bathsheba possibly leaves David after Bathsheba bows to David. After David has finished speaking to Bathsheba, David calls for the men who are loyal to David and support David. David asks his servants to bring the men to David in David's room. Zadok the priest, Nathan the prophet, and Benaiah son of Jehoiada arrive and enter David's room to speak with David.

David gives instructions to the three men. David wants the three men to make Solomon king of Israel. David talks about himself as the lord or master of the three men. David wants his servants to go with the three men. This time, David's servants are probably David's bodyguard, not the people who look after David in his room or his officials. This could be the same group of men whom Benaiah led as David's bodyguard.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about the different groups of people who serve or work for your most important leader. What do those people do? Pause this audio here.

David wants Solomon to ride David's mule. A mule is an animal that is born when a donkey mates with a horse. Mules were special and rare in Israel because Yahweh had told the Israelites not to allow one kind of animal to mate with a different kind of animal. David's mule is a female mule and a special animal for the king to ride on. David wants Solomon to look like a king.

Stop here and look at a picture of a person riding a mule as a group. Pause this audio here.

David wants the men to make Solomon the king at Gihon. Gihon is a spring just outside of Jerusalem. Gihon was an important source of water for Jerusalem, and you will remember that springs were special places for the Israelites. Adonijah had his ceremony next to a different spring. Gihon was on the side of the Kidron Valley. The men and Solomon went down the hill from Jerusalem into the valley next to Gihon.

Stop here and look at a map of Jerusalem and find the spring of Gihon. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What special place do you use to appoint or recognise a new most important leader of your country or people? Pause this audio here.

David wants Zadok and Nathan to anoint Solomon. You may remember that in Israel, a priest anoints a person to symbolise Yahweh's spirit coming on the person. Priests anoint the person whom people believe Yahweh has chosen. A priest anoints a person by pouring or dripping special oil onto the person's head. Zadok and Nathan will anoint Solomon. Zadok is David's most important priest, and Nathan the prophet brings messages to David from Yahweh. This adds authority to Solomon as king. When the priest anoints Solomon, Solomon will be the new king of Israel.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What rituals or ceremonies do you use to show that someone is the leader of your people? Pause this audio here.

When Zadok and Nathan anoint Solomon as king of Israel, David wants the people at Solomon's ceremony to blow a trumpet or horn to announce that Solomon is king. A person blows a horn to make a loud musical noise. At the time of David, people made horns or trumpets from the horns of a ram, which is a male sheep or goat. People blew horns at battles and important events.

Stop here and look at a picture of a person blowing a ram's horn as a group. Pause this audio here.

David also wants the people at the ceremony to announce that Solomon is king by shouting, "Long live King Solomon." The people use this phrase to celebrate Solomon and show that they are loyal to Solomon. You will remember from the earlier passage that Nathan told David that Adonijah's supporters shouted the same phrase at Adonijah's ceremony.

David wants Zadok, Nathan, and Benaiah to go with Solomon from Gihon up the hill to the City of David in Jerusalem. David wants Solomon to sit on David's throne to show that Solomon is the king of Israel. You will remember that a throne is a special seat for a king. David wants Solomon to be the new king of Israel and Judah. We do not know why David mentions both Judah and Israel. At the time Solomon became the new king, Israel included all the lands of the tribes of Israel, including Judah. Judah was not a separate kingdom from the rest of Israel.

Benaiah responds to everything David said with strong agreement. At the time of David, a person said "Amen" to agree with someone and say that everything would be done as the person said it would. Benaiah asks for Yahweh's blessing on David's wishes. Benaiah wants Yahweh to bless Solomon like Yahweh blessed David. Benaiah uses the king's throne as a symbol for the king's reign. Benaiah asks Yahweh to make Solomon a greater and more powerful king than King David.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What words of blessing do you say to each other to wish good things from God for a person?

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 1:28–37 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has four scenes.

In the first scene: King David asks his servants to bring Bathsheba to him in David's room.

In the second scene: King David promises to fulfill the promise that David made to Bathsheba in the past. David tells Bathsheba that today, Solomon will become the next king. Bathsheba is very thankful to David.

In the third scene: King David asks his servants to bring Zadok the priest, Nathan the prophet, and Benaiah son of Jehoiada to David in David's room.

In the fourth scene: King David instructs Zadok, Nathan, and Benaiah to make Solomon the king of Israel at Gihon. David tells Zadok, Nathan, and Benaiah to put Solomon on David's throne in Jerusalem after Zadok makes Solomon king of Israel. Benaiah gives his strong approval to David's plan and asks for Yahweh's blessing on Solomon to make Solomon a greater king than David.

The characters in this passage are:

  • King David
  • David's servants
  • Bathsheba
  • Zadok the priest and Nathan the prophet
  • And Benaiah, son of Jehoiada

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

We do not know when Nathan left David's room, but Nathan must have left David after Nathan spoke to David and before David asked the servants to bring Bathsheba back to David's room.
