We do not know how much time passed between David's conversation with Nathan and when David decided to call Bathsheba back to the room. We do not know if Nathan leaves the room before David asks the servants to bring Bathsheba, or if Nathan leaves the room when Bathsheba comes back into the room. All of Bathsheba and Nathan's visits to David's room happen on the same day and at the same time as Adonijah's ceremony to become king. David gives instructions to his servants to bring the different people to David's room.

When Bathsheba arrives in David's room, Bathsheba enters into King David's presence, the place where David is. David speaks to Bathsheba and makes an oath, a strong promise to Bathsheba. David's oath appeals to Yahweh as a witness to the oath, states the new promise for that day, and repeats the promise that David had made to Bathsheba before. David promises to complete or fulfill his previous promise on that day. After David speaks his promise to Bathsheba, Bathsheba bows to show loyalty, thankfulness, and respect to David. Also, Bathsheba wishes health and long life to her king to show her thanks and loyalty to David. Then Bathsheba leaves David.

Next, David speaks to his servants to ask his servants to bring Zadok, Nathan, and Benaiah to David's rooms. David uses the men's titles to identify the men to his servants. David probably asks his servants to bring the men to him quickly after Bathsheba leaves.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How often and when do you use people's official titles? Pause this audio here.

Zadok, Nathan, and Benaiah come to David in David's room. David gives the men orders to make Solomon king of Israel on that day. David wants his bodyguard to go with Zadok, Nathan, and Benaiah. David calls his bodyguard "servants" and refers to himself as the lord of the men. You will remember that a lord is a master over other people. David tells the men to go all together with Solomon down to the spring of Gihon. The spring of Gihon is in the Kidron Valley just outside Jerusalem and below the City of David, the part of Jerusalem where David lives.

Stop here and look at a map of Jerusalem and the spring of Gihon in the Kidron Valley, if needed. Pause this audio here.

When the men arrive at the spring of Gihon, David wants Zadok and Nathan to anoint Solomon and announce Solomon as king.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Have one person give instructions to the group about some things the group should do later that day. Pay attention to the language the person uses as they give one instruction and then the next instruction. Pause this audio here.

After the men make Solomon king, David wants the men to follow Solomon up to the City of David and to the building which contains David's throne. The City of David is on a hill above the spring of Gihon. David wants people to see Solomon as the new king. David wants the people of Israel to know that David has formally chosen or appointed Solomon as the new king.

Benaiah answers or responds to David's instructions with a strong affirmation or agreement. Benaiah uses the word "amen" to say that people will do the things David has ordered. Benaiah wishes Yahweh to make everything happen. Benaiah uses formal language to talk to David and to show respect to David. Benaiah wants Yahweh to be with Solomon in the same way that Yahweh protected David, Benaiah's lord. Benaiah uses David's throne as a symbol of David's reign and wants Solomon's reign to be even better than David's reign over Israel.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 1:28–37 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has four scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • King David
  • David's servants
  • Bathsheba
  • Zadok the priest and Nathan the prophet
  • And Benaiah, son of Jehoiada

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

King David asks his servants to bring Bathsheba to him in David's room.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing king David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I need to talk to Bathsheba again; or
  • I am so worried. Solomon must be the next king of Israel; or
  • I want Bathsheba to know that I will keep my promise to her.

Ask the person playing David's servants, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • The king is very worried; or
  • Quick! We must bring Bathsheba back here; or
  • Something very serious is happening.

Continue the drama.

King David promises to fulfill the promise that David made to Bathsheba in the past. David tells Bathsheba that today, Solomon will become the next king. Bathsheba is very thankful to David.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I love Bathsheba and want her to be safe and happy; or
  • I do not want Bathsheba to worry about Solomon; or
  • I am glad that Bathsheba knows that I will keep my promise to her.

Ask the person playing Bathsheba, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • My king is faithful and will keep his promise to me; or
  • I am so happy that David is strong today and will stop Adonijah; or
  • I thank God for my king and wish good things for him.

Continue the drama.

King David asks his servants to bring Zadok the priest, Nathan the prophet, and Benaiah son of Jehoiada to David in David's room.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I need the men who are faithful to come and help make Solomon king; or
  • I need to make Solomon king today; or
  • I am worried that people will think Adonijah is king before I can make Solomon king.

Ask the person playing David's servants, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • First, King David wanted us to bring Bathsheba and now three very important men! Something important is happening; or
  • Quick, find Zadok, Nathan, and Benaiah; or
  • We feel worried about King David.

Continue the drama.

King David instructs Zadok, Nathan, and Benaiah to make Solomon the king of Israel at Gihon. David tells Zadok, Nathan, and Benaiah to put Solomon on David's throne in Jerusalem after Zadok makes Solomon king of Israel. Benaiah gives his strong approval to David's plan and asks for Yahweh's blessing on Solomon to make Solomon a greater king than David.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Now we will make everything right; or
  • I am so happy and relieved that these men are faithful to me; or
  • I want these men to make Solomon king immediately, and for everyone to see that this is what I want.

Ask the person playing Zadok the priest and Nathan the prophet, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Now our king will make everything right; or
  • We will anoint Solomon and make him our next king today; or
  • Everyone will see that Solomon is the next king of Israel.

Ask the person playing Benaiah, son of Jehoiada, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Yes! Let us do this; or
  • I hope Yahweh helps us to do everything David wants us to do; or
  • I think Solomon will be a great king with Yahweh's help.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 1:28–37 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

King David calls for Bathsheba to come back to David. Use the same word or phrase for king as you used in previous passages. For more information on king, refer to the Master Glossary.

Bathsheba comes into the king's presence. A person's presence is the place where they are. Bathsheba goes into the place where the king is.

Stop here and discuss as a group how you will describe the king's presence, the place where the king is. Pause this audio here.

King David takes an oath and makes a solemn promise to Bathsheba.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for oath. Look up oath in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

David makes a promise to Bathsheba. David says that as sure as Yahweh, the God of Israel lives, David will do what he promised to Bathsheba before. Yahweh is the personal name for God, the almighty, the God of Israel. Be sure to translate Yahweh, God, and Israel in the same way as you have in previous passages, and remember that Yahweh, God, and Israel are in the Master Glossary.

David promises that Solomon, Bathsheba's son, will be the next king. A son is a male child of his parents.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for son. Look up son in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Bathsheba bows down to David, her lord or master, and shows loyalty and great respect for David. Use the same words or phrases for lord or master, and bow down as you used in previous passages. For more information on bow and lord or master, refer to the Master Glossary.

David asks his attendants to call Zadok the priest, Nathan the prophet, and Benaiah son of Jehoiada. Use the same words or phrases for priest and prophet as you used in previous passages. For more information on priest and prophet, refer to the Master Glossary.

David tells Zadok, Nathan, and Benaiah to take their lord's servants with them. David's servants in this case are his bodyguards.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for servants in this case. The storyteller calls these men servants in the story, but they are probably the bodyguards that Benaiah led. Discuss as a group if you want to call these men servants, bodyguards, or officials. Look up servant in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

David wants Solomon to mount or sit on and ride David's mule.

Stop here and look at a picture of a person riding a mule as a group. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for mule. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

David wants Zadok and Nathan to anoint Solomon as king. When someone anoints someone else, it means they drip or pour sweet-smelling oil over that person in order to appoint them for a special task.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for anoint. Look up anoint in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

David wants people to blow a horn or trumpet when Solomon becomes king.

Stop here and look at a picture of a person blowing a ram's horn as a group. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for horn. Look up horn in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

David wants Solomon to sit on David's throne and reign in David's place. Use the same word or phrase for throne as you used in previous passages. To reign means to rule a country as a king.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for reign. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

David tells Zadok and Nathan that David appointed Solomon the ruler over Israel and Judah. Judah is the name of one of the tribes of Israel. Judah is the tribe that David is from. Use the same word for Judah as you have previously, and remember that Judah is in the Master Glossary.

A ruler is a general term for someone who rules over a country. People often use this term for a ruler whom someone appoints.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for ruler. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Benaiah said "Amen" to David. Amen is a word in the original language, and a person says this word as a strong agreement with what another person says.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for amen. Look up amen in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Benaiah says that Yahweh is the God of his lord, the king.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 1:38–53
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 1:38–53 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 1:38–53 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 1:38–53 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous passages, Nathan and Bathsheba told King David that David's son Adonijah was making himself the next king of Israel. David told Bathsheba and the men loyal to David that Solomon would be the next king of Israel. David told Nathan the prophet, Zadok the priest, and Benaiah the leader of David's bodyguard to make Solomon the next king of Israel on that same day. In this passage, the men who are loyal to David make Solomon king of Israel and stop Adonijah. Zadok the priest, Nathan the prophet, and Benaiah son of Jehoiada leave David's room and join with the Kerethites and Pelethites. The Kerethites and Pelethites were David's personal fighting men. The Kerethites and Pelethites were not Israelites, but they had served David as his special guard for many years. David's supporters go to Solomon and put Solomon on David's mule. You will remember that a mule is a special animal for important people to ride.

Stop here and look at a picture of a man riding a mule as a group. Pause this audio here.

All the men leave the City of David, in Jerusalem on the hill, and go with Solomon down the hill to the spring of Gihon in the Kidron Valley. Solomon rides David's mule.

Stop here and look at a map of Jerusalem and the surrounding area and find Gihon. Pause this audio here.

Zadok takes the special oil from the special tent and carries it to the spring of Gihon to use on Solomon. You may remember that David ordered people to make a special tent to contain the ark of the covenant when David brought the ark to Jerusalem. You will also remember that a person would put special scented olive oil on another person to anoint that person. People kept this oil in a container that people made from animal horn. We do not know what shape the container was. When the men arrive at the spring of Gihon, Zadok the priest anoints Solomon with this special oil.

Stop here and look at a picture of containers that people made from animal horn. Pause this audio here.

When Zadok anoints Solomon, Zadok makes Solomon king of Israel. Some people blow horns or trumpets that people made from rams' horns to make a loud musical noise. You will remember that people blew horns at important events and battles. The people at Gihon use the horns to announce that Solomon is now the king of Israel.

Stop here as a group and look at a picture of a person blowing a ram's horn trumpet. Pause this audio here.

All the people at the spring of Gihon shout, "Long live King Solomon!" The people use this phrase to celebrate Solomon and show that they are loyal to Solomon.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do you celebrate and announce a new most important leader in your nation or people group? Pause this audio here.

King Solomon goes back up the hill from Gihon to Jerusalem and to the City of David. The people at Gihon follow Solomon while they are playing pipes. People make a musical noise with pipes by blowing into the instruments. People make pipes from wood, ivory, or bone. Sometimes people use a reed as a mouthpiece to blow into the pipes or flutes.

Stop here and look at a picture of people playing pipes. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What musical instruments do people in your community use to make music by blowing into them? What kind of noise do these instruments make? Pause this audio here.

The people shout and make happy noises to celebrate King Solomon. The people make a very loud noise so that the people feel like the ground or the earth might shake or break.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Talk about a time when you were with people who made so much noise that you could feel the noise in your body and through your feet from the ground. Pause this audio here.

Adonijah is at the Stone of Zoheleth near the spring of En Rogel with the people Adonijah invited to see him become king of Israel. Adonijah and his guests are finishing the feast, the large meal to celebrate Adonijah as king. Adonijah and his guests hear the people at Gihon and in Jerusalem celebrating their new king with horns. Joab, one of Adonijah's important supporters, asks why the noise from horns is coming from Jerusalem.

Stop here and look at a map of Jerusalem and the surrounding area. Notice how close the spring of En Rogel is to the spring of Gihon and Jerusalem. Pause this audio here.

Suddenly, Jonathan, son of Abiathar the priest, arrives at Adonijah's celebration. You may remember that Jonathan was a supporter of David when Absalom wanted to be king. In this story, Jonathan probably supports Adonijah. You will remember that Abiathar also supports Adonijah to be king. We do not know why Jonathan is not already at Adonijah's celebration, but he was in Jerusalem and saw Solomon become king.

Adonijah welcomes Jonathan and calls Jonathan a worthy man. A worthy man is a man who is trustworthy and reliable. Adonijah expects Jonathan to bring good news to Adonijah. Jonathan tells Adonijah that he does not bring good news. Jonathan brings bad news to Adonijah and his supporters. Jonathan shows respect to David and calls King David "our lord," the master of Adonijah and Jonathan. Jonathan tells Adonijah and his supporters that King David made Solomon king on that day. Jonathan tells Adonijah about all the ways King David showed the people that David wanted Solomon to be king. Jonathan tells Adonijah that the people who do not support Adonijah do support Solomon to be king. Jonathan tells Adonijah that Zadok and Nathan anointed Solomon as king at the spring of Gihon. Jonathan tells Adonijah that the people in Jerusalem are making a very loud noise to celebrate King Solomon. Jonathan adds to his story by telling Adonijah that Solomon sat on the royal throne, another sign that Solomon is now king. Jonathan then tells Adonijah that David's royal officials congratulated King David for choosing Solomon. Jonathan tells Adonijah what the officials said to David. The officials prayed for Yahweh to make Solomon more well-known and more praised than David. The officials want Solomon's reign to be even better than David's. Finally, Jonathan tells Adonijah about David's actions and what David said to the royal officials in David's room. David was in bed and bowed his body down to Yahweh. David bowed to show respect and thanks to Yahweh. David was grateful to Yahweh and praised Yahweh, the most powerful heavenly being in Israel. David thanked Yahweh for letting David see Solomon become the next king of Israel.

Jonathan's story worries the people Adonijah invited to his celebration. All the people at Adonijah's celebration did not do what David wanted and did not support Solomon. The people are frightened that the new king, Solomon, will punish them by killing them. Adonijah is frightened and thinks Solomon will punish Adonijah by killing him. Adonijah leaves the Stone of Zoheleth near En Rogel and goes to the altar of burnt offerings or sacrifice. The altar of sacrifice is probably at the tent for the ark of the covenant in Jerusalem.

Stop here and look at a map of Jerusalem and the surrounding area, and find the tent for the ark of the covenant. Pause this audio here.

You may remember from the book of Exodus that Bezalel made the corners of the altar of burnt offerings into shapes like animal horns. People called the shapes "horns," but people made these horns of bronze, not animal horns.

Stop here and look at a picture of the altar of burnt offerings as a group. Pause this audio here.

Adonijah goes to the altar to claim safety from Solomon. In Israel, if a person killed another person by accident, the person went to the altar and held the horns to be safe. The person asked the priests not to punish the person for killing someone. In Israel, the king could kill or execute a person for killing another person. If the king executed a person, the king or one of the king's soldiers would kill that person quickly with a sword. Adonijah did not kill anybody, but Adonijah did not want Solomon to kill him with a sword.

Stop here and look at a picture of a sword as a group. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What places, if any, can people go to for safety from punishment in your community or country? Pause this audio here.

People go to Solomon to tell Solomon about Adonijah at the altar. People tell Solomon that Adonijah is frightened of Solomon and is holding onto the horns of the altar. People tell Solomon that Adonijah wants King Solomon to swear or promise not to kill Adonijah with a sword. Adonijah calls himself the servant of Solomon and gives Solomon the title of king. Adonijah knows Solomon has authority over Adonijah and is now the king of Israel.

Solomon decides to wait and see how Adonijah behaves. Solomon gives Adonijah an opportunity to be worthy, to be trustworthy and reliable. If Adonijah behaves well, Solomon will not let even one hair from Adonijah fall to the ground. This means that Solomon will not let anything harm Adonijah.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story about a time when you said that you would protect someone and not let anything bad happen. Pause this audio here.

Solomon says that if he or his people find evil in Adonijah, then Adonijah will die. Solomon will judge Adonijah by Adonijah's actions. King Solomon sends men to get Adonijah from the altar and bring Adonijah to King Solomon. Adonijah comes to King Solomon and bows to King Solomon to show respect to Solomon. Solomon tells Adonijah to go from where they are to Adonijah's home.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 1:38–53 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has eight scenes.

In the first scene: Zadok the priest, Nathan the prophet, Benaiah son of Jehoiada, the Kerethites, and the Pelethites go with Solomon, who rides on David's mule, from the city of David down to the spring of Gihon.

In the second scene: Zadok anoints Solomon with oil that he takes from the tent where the ark is. People blow horns and shout, "Long live King Solomon!"

In the third scene: Solomon goes back up to the City of David. All the people at the spring of Gihon follow Solomon to the city and play pipes and make a loud and happy noise.

In the fourth scene: Near En Rogel, Adonijah and his supporters hear the loud noise from Jerusalem. Joab wonders why there is a loud noise from Jerusalem. Adonijah welcomes Jonathan. Jonathan tells everyone that David has made Solomon king and that Solomon sits on David's throne. Royal officials are happy, and David thanks Yahweh for allowing David to see Solomon become king. Everyone at Adonijah's celebration is frightened and leaves En Rogel.

In the fifth scene: Adonijah goes from En Rogel to the tent where the ark is and holds onto the horns of the altar.

In the sixth scene: People tell Solomon that Adonijah is frightened and thinks Solomon will kill him. Adonijah wants Solomon to promise not to kill Adonijah for trying to become king. Solomon replies to say that Adonijah needs to show Solomon that he is a worthy man. If Adonijah does the wrong thing again, Solomon will kill Adonijah.

In the seventh scene: King Solomon sends men to the tent where the altar is to bring Adonijah to King Solomon.

In the eighth scene: Adonijah meets with King Solomon, and Solomon tells Adonijah to go home.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Zadok the priest
  • Nathan the prophet
  • Benaiah, son of Jehoiada
  • The Kerethites and the Pelethites
  • Solomon
  • People following Solomon
  • Adonijah
  • Adonijah's guests
  • Joab
  • Jonathan, son of Abiathar
  • And King Solomon's men

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the last passage, King David gave Nathan the prophet, Zadok the priest, and Benaiah son of Jehoiada instructions to make Solomon the next king. In this passage, Zadok the priest, Nathan the prophet, and Benaiah son of Jehoiada follow David's instructions. You will notice that the storyteller gives Zadok, Nathan, and Benaiah their titles as well as their names.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you refer to Zadok, Nathan, and Benaiah in your translation? Are you going to use the titles that show what they do, like priest, prophet, and son of Jehoiada, every time, sometimes, or just at the beginning of the passage? Pause this audio here.

Zadok, Nathan, and Benaiah join the Kerethites and Pelethites and go with Solomon to Gihon. All of these people travel with Solomon to guard and protect Solomon.

The storyteller tells us that Zadok uses a container of special oil to anoint Solomon. Zadok collected the container from the tent where the ark of the covenant was, between the time when David told Zadok what to do and when the people went to Gihon to anoint Solomon.

After Zadok anoints Solomon, people at Gihon blow horns to announce that Solomon is now the king. The people at Gihon shout, "Long live King Solomon!" to celebrate Solomon and show support for Solomon. King Solomon and the rest of the people at the spring of Gihon go from Gihon back up to the City of David, which is the part of Jerusalem where David lives. As the group of people goes to Jerusalem, people make a loud musical noise on pipes and celebrate with loud shouts.

Stop here and look at a picture of people playing pipes as a group, if needed. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller tells us that the ground shook with the sound. The ground did not actually shake, but the storyteller uses the phrase to help us know that the noise was very loud.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What phrase or saying do you use to help people know that a noise or an event was very loud? Pause this audio here.

At the same time, Adonijah is with the people whom Adonijah invited to celebrate Adonijah as the new king. The people are Adonijah's guests. Everyone is finishing eating a feast, a big and special meal for celebrations.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What do you call a big and special meal that you eat at celebrations or on special days? Pause this audio here.

Joab is with Adonijah and hears the sound of horns. En Rogel is less than one kilometre from Gihon, so Adonijah and his supporters can hear the loud celebrations. People with Solomon are blowing the horns.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about how loud a sound needs to be for the people in one of the places in the story to hear what happened in the other place in the story. How might you describe that type of sound? Pause this audio here.

Joab wants to know why so much noise is coming from the city of Jerusalem. At the same time that Joab speaks, Jonathan arrives. Adonijah welcomes Jonathan. Adonijah calls Jonathan a worthy man, someone who is reliable and trustworthy.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What do you call a person who you know is reliable, trustworthy, and honest? Pause this audio here.

Adonijah thinks Jonathan brings good news. Jonathan replies and tells Adonijah that he does not bring good news. Jonathan tells Adonijah and Adonijah's guests that David has made Solomon king. Jonathan calls King David "our lord," the person who is master over Adonijah and Jonathan. Jonathan shows respect to David. Jonathan tells Adonijah all the things that happened to make Solomon king: Solomon rode King David's mule. At Gihon, Zadok and Nathan anointed Solomon to make Solomon king. We do not know why Jonathan says both men anointed Solomon when the storyteller earlier tells us that Zadok anointed Solomon to become king. It is possible that Nathan put oil on Solomon after Zadok or was beside Zadok when Zadok anointed Solomon. After the people made Solomon king at Gihon, the people went up to Jerusalem while they cheered or shouted happily. People are shouting and making a lot of noise in the city. There is so much sound that the sound fills the city, and people can hear it everywhere. Jonathan tells Adonijah that the noise from the city is what Adonijah can hear. Jonathan then adds to his story and tells Adonijah more about Solomon becoming king.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Have one person tell the group the first part about an exciting event. Let some people from the group comment on the story, and then have the person tell the group the next exciting part of the event. Pay attention to how the person connects the story together. Pause this audio here.

Jonathan tells Adonijah that Solomon sits on the royal throne, David's special seat. Jonathan also tells Adonijah that David's royal officials went to David to congratulate David. The officials approve of what David has done and are pleased that Solomon is king. Jonathan tells Adonijah what the officials said to David. Jonathan describes the events in Jerusalem to the people at the Stone of Zoheleth. David and the officials are not present in this scene. The officials say that they pray that more people will praise Solomon than the people who have praised David. The officials call Yahweh "your God," the God of David, even though the royal officials also serve Yahweh. The officials pray that Yahweh will make Solomon's throne greater. The officials do not want Solomon's real throne to be bigger in size. Instead, the officials use the throne as a symbol for Solomon's reign. David responds to his royal officials and bows down on his bed. Jonathan tells Adonijah that David prayed to Yahweh to praise Yahweh for letting David see the next king of Israel sit on David's throne.

After Jonathan finishes telling his story, all Adonijah's guests are very worried and get up from the feast and go away from the feast to different places. Adonijah goes to the place where the altar of sacrifice is and holds onto the bronze horns on the corners of the altar. The altar of sacrifice is probably near the tent where the ark of the covenant is, in Jerusalem.

Stop here as a group and look at a picture of the altar of sacrifice if needed. Pause this audio here.

In the City of David, some people tell Solomon that Adonijah is afraid of King Solomon and is holding onto the altar. The people tell Solomon that Adonijah wants King Solomon to swear or promise not to kill Solomon's servant, Adonijah. We do not know who the people are who talk to Solomon about Adonijah. Solomon hears Adonijah name Solomon king and Adonijah as Solomon's servant. Adonijah is not the personal servant of Solomon, meaning someone who will do hard work around the house. Adonijah means that he will be obedient to Solomon as Adonijah's king and master.

Solomon replies to the people to say that Solomon will not harm or kill Adonijah if Adonijah is worthy. Solomon uses special language to say that Solomon will not harm Adonijah in any way, not even a single hair on his head. However, Solomon will kill Adonijah if Adonijah does something to show Solomon that Adonijah is not worthy or is evil. Solomon uses more special language, "evil found in him," to describe Adonijah being unworthy.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Think about the story that you told in step two when you talked about protecting someone. What phrases did you use to describe when you protected someone and did not let anything bad happen to a person? Pause this audio here.

Solomon sends some of his servants to bring Adonijah from the tent where the altar of sacrifice is. Adonijah comes to Solomon and bows down to Solomon to show respect to Solomon. Solomon tells Adonijah to go to Adonijah's home. We do not think Solomon wants Adonijah to stay in his home all the time, but just wants Adonijah to go away from Solomon at this time.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 1:38–53 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has eight scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Zadok the priest
  • Nathan the prophet
  • Benaiah, son of Jehoiada
  • The Kerethites and the Pelethites
  • Solomon
  • People following Solomon
  • Adonijah
  • Adonijah's guests
  • Joab
  • Jonathan, son of Abiathar
  • And King Solomon's men

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Zadok the priest, Nathan the prophet, Benaiah son of Jehoiada, the Kerethites, and the Pelethites go with Solomon, who rides on David's mule, from the city of David down to the spring of Gihon.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Zadok the priest, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We need to make Solomon our new king as soon as possible; or
  • Come on, everybody! We will go to the spring of Gihon to make Solomon our king.

Ask the person playing Nathan, Benaiah, the Pelethites, and the Kerethites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We need to go quickly; or
  • This is what Yahweh wants for Israel; or
  • We will protect Solomon like we protected King David.

Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel amazed. I did not expect to become king today; or
  • King David honours me. It is very special to ride his mule; or
  • Now Adonijah will know that Yahweh wants me to be king of Israel.

Continue the drama.

Zadok anoints Solomon with oil that he takes from the tent where the ark is. People blow horns and shout, "Long live King Solomon!"

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Zadok the priest, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • This oil is holy. I am honoured to anoint Solomon as our new king; or
  • This is our new king! Tell everyone the good news; or
  • We have done it! Solomon is king, not Adonijah.

Ask the person playing Nathan, Benaiah, the Pelethites, and the Kerethites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Blow the horns hard! We want everyone to hear that we have a new king; or
  • We are happy to have a new king; or
  • We follow King Solomon! We think Solomon will be a wonderful king.

Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel important and special. I am now the king of Israel; or
  • I will serve my people and follow Yahweh; or
  • Everyone is making an incredible noise.

Continue the drama.

Solomon goes up to the City of David. All the people at the spring of Gihon follow Solomon back up to the city and play pipes and make a loud and happy noise.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Look at all these people celebrating me; or
  • I feel overwhelmed and humbled; or
  • Now I will go to sit on my father's throne to show everyone that I am king of Israel.

Ask the person playing the people following Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Solomon looks young and powerful; or
  • We are happy to have a new king to look after us and Israel; or
  • Make a happy noise! Blow the pipes to celebrate our new king; or
  • We are very loud. We can feel the noise around us.

Continue the drama.

Near En Rogel, Adonijah and his supporters hear the loud noise from Jerusalem. Joab wonders why there is a loud noise from Jerusalem. Adonijah welcomes Jonathan. Jonathan tells everyone that David has made Solomon king and that Solomon sits on David's throne. Royal officials are happy, and David thanked Yahweh for allowing David to see Solomon become king. Everyone at Adonijah's celebration is frightened and leaves En Rogel.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Adonijah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • What a noise! What is going on in Jerusalem? or
  • Here is Jonathan. Jonathan will tell us why people are celebrating and blowing horns; or
  • This is not good news. Nathan, Zadok, and Benaiah were clever and worked quickly. Now everyone knows Solomon is the new king; or
  • Lots of important people support Solomon; or
  • I am frightened. Solomon will kill me because I tried to become king of Israel.

Ask the person playing Joab, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • What is going on? or
  • Everything has gone wrong now; or
  • Solomon will be angry with us. I am in danger.

Ask the person playing Jonathan, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Adonijah thought he was the new king, but Solomon is the new king; or
  • Everyone needs to know what happened at Gihon and in Jerusalem; or
  • Listen to what the royal officials said and what David said. Everyone supports Solomon; or
  • I am very worried.

Ask the person playing Adonijah's guests, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • People are blowing horns in Jerusalem. What is happening? or
  • David and Solomon were clever. Adonijah cannot be king now; or
  • We are in danger. We are frightened that Solomon will punish us for supporting Adonijah.

Continue the drama.

Adonijah goes from En Rogel to the tent where the ark is and holds onto the horns of the altar.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Adonijah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I need to go somewhere safe where someone will protect me; or
  • I will hold onto the altar and ask the priests to keep me safe; or
  • I am so frightened. Solomon will want to kill me.

Continue the drama.

People tell Solomon that Adonijah is frightened and thinks Solomon will kill him. Adonijah wants Solomon to promise not to kill Adonijah for trying to become king. Solomon replies to say that Adonijah needs to show Solomon that he is a worthy man. If Adonijah does the wrong thing again, Solomon will kill Adonijah.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King Solomon's men, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Adonijah is right to be frightened. Solomon might kill Adonijah; or
  • I wonder what Solomon will do? or
  • I feel respect for my new king.

Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am angry, but I will wait and see if Adonijah supports me as king; or
  • Maybe I will kill Adonijah if he does not support me; or
  • I feel powerful.

Continue the drama.

King Solomon sends men to the tent where the altar of sacrifice is to bring Adonijah to King Solomon.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I want Adonijah to come and see me; or
  • Adonijah does not need to ask for safety at the altar.

Ask the person playing King Solomon's men, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We will bring Adonijah to our new king; or
  • Adonijah is foolish. Adonijah looks desperate as he holds the horns of the altar.

Continue the drama.

Adonijah meets with King Solomon, and Solomon tells Adonijah to go home.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I think Adonijah is foolish. I will see what Adonijah does now; or
  • I feel merciful; or
  • I am happy that I am king and Adonijah is not the king; or
  • I have had enough of this man. I do not want to see Adonijah anymore.

Ask the person playing Adonijah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am so frightened. What will Solomon do to me? or
  • I am grateful to Solomon. I thought Solomon would kill me; or
  • What will I do now?

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 1:38–53 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Zadok the priest, Nathan the prophet, Benaiah son of Jehoiada, the Kerethites, and the Pelethites go with Solomon, who rides on David's mule, from the city of David to Gihon. Use the same words or phrases for priest, prophet, and mule as you used in previous passages. For more information on priest and prophet, refer to the Master Glossary.

Zadok took the horn of oil from the tent. The horn of oil was a container that people had made from an animal horn. Special oil for anointing people was inside the container.

Stop here and look at a picture of containers made from animal horn if needed. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for the horn of oil. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Zadok took the horn of oil from the tent where David had put the ark of the covenant. This was a special, or holy, tent where Yahweh lived among his people.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use to describe this tent in Jerusalem. Some translations say that this was a "holy tent" that was special because Yahweh lived there. Listen to your translation of 2 Samuel 6:17 if possible, and hear how you translated tent in that story. Pause this audio here.

Zadok anoints Solomon. People blow horns or trumpets made from ram's horns. Use the same words or phrases for anoint and horns as you used in previous passages. For more information on anoint and horns, refer to the Master Glossary.

People at Gihon shout, "Long live King Solomon." Use the same phrase for "long live the king" as you used in previous passages. For more information on king, refer to the Master Glossary.

People follow Solomon from Gihon to Jerusalem and play pipes.

Stop here and look at a picture of people playing pipes if needed. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for pipes. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Adonijah and Adonijah's guests hear the noise as they finish their feast. A feast is a special meal to celebrate an event.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for feast. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Jonathan calls King David lord and says that Solomon sat on the royal throne. Use the same words or phrases for lord and throne as you used in previous passages. For more information on lord, refer to the Master Glossary.

Jonathan says that the royal officials, or servants, congratulated David. Royal officials are people who serve David and help David to rule Israel. Use the same word or phrase for royal officials as you used in previous passages. If you used the word for servants, remember that servant is in the Master Glossary.

Jonathan tells Adonijah that David bowed down to worship Yahweh, the God of Israel and praised or blessed Yahweh. To praise or bless someone means to say good things about them. Use the same words or phrases for bowed down and Yahweh as you used in previous passages. For more information on bow, Yahweh, God, and Israel, refer to the Master Glossary.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for praise. For more information about praise, refer to praise or bless in the Master Glossary. Pause this audio here.

Adonijah goes to the tent of the Lord's presence and holds onto the horns of the altar. Bezalel made the corners of the altar of burnt offerings into shapes like animal horns.

Stop here and look at a picture of the altar of burnt offerings as a group if needed. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for horns. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

The altar is the altar of sacrifice or burnt offerings, the place where the priests make burnt offerings to Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for altar. Look up altar in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Adonijah wants Solomon to swear an oath or make a solemn promise to Adonijah that Solomon will not kill Solomon's servant, Adonijah. Use the same words or phrases for "swear an oath" and servant as you used in previous passages. For more information on oath and servant, refer to the Master Glossary.

Adonijah does not want Solomon to kill Adonijah with a sword.

Stop here and look at a picture of a sword as a group if needed. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for sword. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Solomon says that Adonijah will die if Solomon finds evil in Adonijah. Evil is the opposite of good, and a person with evil in them willingly does bad things to hurt other people.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for evil. Look up evil in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this idea in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Solomon tells Adonijah to go to Adonijah's home. A person's home is the house or shelter where a person lives and rests.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for home. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 2:1–12
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 2:1–12 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 2:1–12 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 2:1–12 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the last story, King David made his son, Solomon, king of Israel. Therefore, until David's death, King David and King Solomon were ruling Israel together. This story takes place a short time after King David made Solomon king. The storyteller assumes that we remember the stories about how Joab killed Abner and Amasa, how Barzillai helped David, and how Shimei cursed David. Joab had killed Abner after Abner had made peace with David. Joab had also killed Amasa, after David had made Amasa the commander of his army, because Joab was jealous of Amasa. Shimei had cursed David when David escaped from Absalom, but David had promised not to kill him on his way back to Jerusalem after defeating Absalom. Barzillai and his sons had helped David and brought him food.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about what you remember about how Joab killed Abner and Amasa, as well as how Barzillai and Shimei treated King David in the previous stories in the book of 2 Samuel. Pause this audio here.

When King David knew that his time to die was near, King David spoke his last words to King Solomon. It is not clear how long King David lived after he made Solomon king, but it is likely that King David died a short time after that.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell stories of a time when someone in your community knew that they would soon die. What last words did they say, and to whom did they speak them? How long did that person live after that? How do people in your culture feel when someone gives their last words? Pause this audio here.

King David told King Solomon that he was about to die, just like everybody else on earth must die. King David commanded King Solomon to be strong and to act like a man or an adult. King David was telling King Solomon to have courage and strength to do what Yahweh said at all times, even when he was scared or in danger. King David commanded King Solomon to pay attention and obey what Yahweh his God required. King David usually called Yahweh his own God, but this time, King David called Yahweh Solomon's God. King David wanted Solomon to have a true and personal relationship with Yahweh.

King David told Solomon to act like a man by walking in the paths of Yahweh. King David was telling Solomon to live and behave the way Yahweh has commanded in the law of Moses. Yahweh's laws and commands include the specific things Yahweh told the people of Israel to obey, such as the ten commandments and the rules for sacrifices and offerings.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Talk about the laws and commands that you have in your community, and arrange the laws into groups. For example, you can group the laws into laws about religion, customs, ceremony, society, property, and any other groups you can think of. Pause this audio here.

Yahweh had commanded every person who became king of Israel to write down a copy of all the teachings of Moses and to read them regularly. Yahweh said that the king must respect and obey Yahweh by carefully following the words of the law of Moses.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about the religious duties and responsibilities of rulers in your community. What laws and commands must your ruler keep? Explain who gave these laws and commands and how you communicate them to each new ruler. Pause this audio here.

King David told Solomon that if Solomon obeyed Yahweh, Solomon would be successful and Yahweh would fulfil his promise to David. Yahweh had promised David that if David's descendants sincerely obeyed Yahweh, Yahweh would make sure that one of David's descendants was always king of Israel.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about a time when one generation has given the job of leading your community to a younger generation. When, how, and why did they do this? Pause audio here.

After King David told Solomon to stay loyal to God, King David reminded Solomon of what Joab, son of Zeruiah, had done. Joab killed two army commanders of Israel, Abner and Amasa, during a time of peace, not war. Abner had once fought for King Saul against David, but later made peace and joined David. Joab killed Abner to get revenge because Abner had killed Joab's brother. Amasa had led Absalom's army when Absalom rebelled against David. Even though David told his men not to kill Absalom, Joab disobeyed and killed Absalom. Later, David forgave Amasa and made him the new army commander instead of Joab. Joab then tricked Amasa and killed him, too. It is likely that David was not powerful enough to punish Joab when he committed these crimes because David's enemies were trying to kill David at the time. David says that Joab got innocent blood on his belt and on his sandals. It is not clear whether David means he got innocent blood on his own belt and sandals or on Joab's belt and sandals. If David means David's own belt and sandals, David might be saying that Joab made David guilty for the murders because King David was in charge of what the army did. If David means that Joab got the innocent blood on Joab's own belt and sandals, David might be saying that Joab is responsible.

Stop here and look at a picture of sandals together as a group. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell of a time when someone disobeyed their leader or commander and did something wrong or killed someone. Who did the people of your community say was responsible? Pause this audio here.

David told Solomon to use his wisdom to deal with Joab in a clever way. David told Solomon not to let the grey head of Joab go down to the world of the dead in peace. David is saying that Joab should not die peacefully of old age, but that Joab should die violently. Solomon needed a lot of wisdom to punish Joab, the powerful military commander, because it was possible that Joab could lead some of the army in a rebellion against Solomon. It is likely that King David realises that Joab could be dangerous to Solomon's rule and also does not want people to consider David and Solomon to be guilty of murder.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk of people who are as powerful or more powerful than a leader and the challenges the leader has to control those people. Pause this audio here.

David also tells Solomon to deal kindly with the sons of Barzillai the Gileadite because they were loyal to David and met David when he was escaping from Absalom with food and provisions. David tells Solomon to let the sons of Barzillai be among those who eat at Solomon's table. David is asking Solomon to provide or take care of the sons, not necessarily that they should sit at Solomon's table and eat. David had wanted to reward their father Barzillai by bringing him to Jerusalem where David would provide for Barzillai, but Bazillai was too old and chose to stay at his farm.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about how your leaders reward those people who have been loyal to them, especially in times of rebellion and division. Pause this audio here.

Then King David told Solomon to also remember that Shimei, the son of Gera, had called down curses from heaven on David when David went to Mahanaim. Shimei was from the tribe of Benjamin and a relative of Saul. Shimei lived in Bahurim which was not too far from Jerusalem on the road that went toward the Jordan River, northeast of the Mount of Olives. When David was returning to Jerusalem after David's army defeated Absalom, Shimei met King David, with 1,000 men of Benjamin, at the Jordan River and begged King David to forgive him. King David told Solomon that he had promised Shimei, in the name of Yahweh, that he would not kill him. David told Solomon that Solomon should not consider and treat Shimei as innocent. David told Solomon to be wise and make sure that Shimei did not grow old and die peacefully but violently.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell stories of a leader who seriously punished someone who had wronged the leader. Focus on stories where the leader had to be clever in the way he punished the person. How did the leader make sure that the people did not think that he was illegally punishing the person? Pause this audio here.

King David slept with his fathers, which means that David died. People buried David in the City of David. People also called Jerusalem the City of David because it was David who captured it in war from the Jebusites. The Israelites buried people in caves or tombs. Often family members shared a tomb. Therefore, when someone died, people said that he "slept with his fathers." This phrase also shows that the person died peacefully.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do you bury members of the same family in your community? Pause this audio here.

King David had ruled for seven years in Hebron and 33 years in Jerusalem, a total of 40 years as king of the people of Israel. After David died, Solomon sat on the throne of his father, or became king, and Solomon's kingdom was strong and secure.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 2:1–12 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has five scenes.

In the first scene: When King David knew that he would soon die, he commanded Solomon to be strong and to obey Yahweh and everything that Yahweh commanded in the law of Moses. King David told Solomon that if Solomon was faithful to obey Yahweh, then Yahweh would be faithful to fulfill the promise he made to King David.

In the second scene: King David told Solomon to remember what Joab, the son of Zeruiah, did to King David. David told Solomon to deal with Joab in a wise way for the way Joab killed Abner, son of Ner, and Amasa, son of Jether.

In the third scene: King David told Solomon to be kind to the sons of Barzillai because they were kind to King David when he was escaping from Absalom.

In the fourth scene: King David told Solomon to remember Shimei, who had cursed King David on the day he went to Mahanaim. Although King David had promised Shimei that he would not kill him, King David told Solomon not to consider Shimei innocent or to let him die a peaceful death.

In the fifth scene: King David died, and people buried David in Jerusalem. King Solomon ruled Israel in the place of his father, David, and Yahweh made Solomon's kingdom very strong.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Yahweh
  • King David
  • King Solomon
  • Joab, the son of Zeruiah
  • Abner, the son of Ner
  • Amasa, the son of Jether
  • Barzillai the Gileadite and his sons
  • Absalom
  • And Shimei, the son of Gera

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, King David knew he was about to die, so he gave his last command to Solomon. King David told Solomon that he was about to go the way of all the earth. Because everyone must die, the people of Israel talked about death as going the way of all the earth.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How does your community talk about death? How would you say that someone is about to die in a gentle and appropriate way in your language? Pause this audio here.

King David commanded Solomon to be strong and show himself a man, or act like an adult. King David was not telling Solomon to be physically strong. Instead, David wanted Solomon to have courage and strength to follow Yahweh's commands, even when it was difficult to follow Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about someone who did what they believed was right and honest, made tough decisions, and stayed strong in difficult times. What is the difference between that kind of strength and physical strength, and how would you talk about it in your language? Pause this audio here.

King David explained that Solomon would show himself a man if he kept the charge of Yahweh, which means that Solomon should obey Yahweh. King David said that Solomon should walk in the ways of Yahweh. A person who walks in the ways of Yahweh is a person who obeys the will of Yahweh. King David usually called Yahweh his God, but now David calls Yahweh Solomon's God.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do you talk about people who obey God and do the things that God wants? Pay attention to the words and phrases you use. Pause this audio here.

King David told Solomon to walk in the way of Yahweh by obeying Yahweh's laws and commands. King David used several words to talk about these general laws and commands. First, King David told Solomon to keep Yahweh's statutes, which are often Yahweh's laws or rules about how to perform duties in the temple or any other specific instructions about how to live in Yahweh's society. King David also told Solomon to keep Yahweh's commandments, which are general rules for living. King David told Solomon to keep Yahweh's ordinances, which are the laws that Yahweh gave to the people to help them live together well. King David also told Solomon to keep the testimonies of Yahweh. The two stone tablets that Yahweh wrote the ten commandments on were called the testimony because they were testimony to, or proved, the special relationship that Yahweh had with the people of Israel. The testimonies of Yahweh are, therefore, another way to talk about the ten commandments.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Yahweh's statutes, commandments, ordinances, and testimonies were all ways that Yahweh talked about his laws and commands. When you talk about all of the general rules that your society has about how to live with God and live with each other in peace, what do you call those rules altogether? Pause this audio here.

King David told Solomon to do all that Yahweh had commanded in the law of Moses, so that Solomon would be successful in everything. King David said that if Solomon did so, Yahweh would fulfill his promise to David. David told Solomon what Yahweh had promised to do for David if David's children obeyed Yahweh. If David's descendants followed Yahweh with all their heart and soul, then someone from David's family would always sit on the throne of Israel. You will remember that sitting on the throne represents the rule of a king. A person who walks before Yahweh or obeys Yahweh faithfully is a person who decides to obey with his mind and his intelligence. And when the person walks before Yahweh with a faithful soul, they obey with their emotions.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How does your community talk about someone who is completely committed to something or someone both physically and emotionally? Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, King David told Solomon that Solomon himself knew what Joab did to King David. It is not clear how Solomon knew, but King David makes it clear that Solomon knew for himself what Joab had done. Joab had killed two commanders of the armies of Israel, Abner and Amasa, during peacetime. King David said that Joab did that to King David. It is likely King David means that Joab did these murders to David because as king, David was responsible for the murder of both Abner and Amasa. King David says that Joab avenged the blood of war or blood shed during a war in peacetime. Abner had killed Joab's brother during a war. But Joab killed Abner, to avenge his brother, after Abner had made peace with David.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What rules do you have in your community about people who kill during war and people who kill during peacetime? How do you refer to the people who kill other people and to the people who other people kill in war and in peace? Pause this audio here.

Joab had also killed Amasa, the son of Jether. David said Joab got innocent blood around his belt and shoes. It is not clear whether Joab got the innocent blood on David's belt and shoes or on Joab's belt and shoes. But it is most likely that Joab got blood on his own belt and shoes. David might have been complaining that Joab made David appear guilty for these crimes, but David was most likely saying that Joab was the one responsible for their deaths.

Stop here as a group and discuss someone who killed someone else as if they were in a war when they were not. Talk about the special language you have in your language to say that someone is guilty of killing an innocent person. Pause this audio here.

David told Solomon to use his wisdom or clever planning to deal with Joab. David told Solomon not to let Joab's gray head go down to Sheol in peace. In other words, David told Solomon not to let Joab die peacefully in his old age. The Israelites called old people "gray heads" because their hair had turned gray.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do you talk about old people? Pay attention to the words and phrases you use. Pause this audio here.

The people of Israel considered Sheol to be a dark place beneath the earth where all the dead people, good or bad, went.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How does your community speak of the world of the dead? Be careful that the words or phrases you use do not simply mean the grave or cemetery. Pause this audio here.

In the third scene, David also told Solomon to be kind to the sons of Barzillai because they had been loyal to David when he fled from Absalom. David told Solomon to make sure that the sons of Barzillai were among those who ate at Solomon's table. This does not necessarily mean that Barzillai's sons were to physically eat at Solomon's table. David was telling Solomon to take care of Barzillai's sons and repay their kindness to David with kindness.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Which words and phrases would you use in your language to talk about someone who a ruler is taking care of? Pause this audio here.

In the fourth scene, David reminded Solomon of Shimei, who cursed David but later begged for forgiveness. King David said that he made an oath or promised in the name of Yahweh that he would not kill Shimei by the sword. But now, King David told Solomon not to treat Shimei as innocent. It is likely that David was saying that the oath he made to Shimei did not include someone other than David killing Shimei by something other than a sword. King David said that Solomon was a wise man and would know how to bring Shimei's gray head down to the grave in blood. David was telling Solomon to make sure that Shimei died a violent death.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do you make sure promises in your language? And how do people in your language say that someone died a violent death without being offensive? Pause this audio here.

In the fifth scene, King David slept with his fathers, or died, and people buried him in Jerusalem, the City of David. King David's fathers include his ancestors, not just his own father.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Talk about famous stories in your culture where the hero of the story dies. How do people describe that the person died a peaceful death? Pause this audio here.

The storyteller tells us that King David was King of Israel for a total of 40 years. The first seven years David was king over the tribe of Judah. David ruled Judah from Hebron. King David then ruled all of Israel for 33 years from Jerusalem. After David died, Solomon sat on the throne of David his father or became king of Israel in the place of his father. Yahweh firmly established Solomon's kingdom. This is a way to say that Yahweh made Solomon's kingdom strong.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 2:1–12 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has five scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Yahweh
  • King David
  • King Solomon
  • Joab, the son of Zeruiah
  • Abner, the son of Ner
  • Amasa, the son of Jether
  • Barzillai the Gileadite and his sons
  • Absalom
  • And Shimei, the son of Gera

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

In the first scene, King David knew that he would soon die, so David commanded Solomon to be strong and to obey Yahweh and everything that Yahweh commanded in the law of Moses. King David told Solomon that if Solomon was faithful to obey Yahweh, then Yahweh would be faithful to fulfil the promise Yahweh made to King David.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel old and tired. I do not have the strength to live much longer. I am about to die; or
  • I am happy with the life I have lived. Yahweh has been good to me and blessed me; or
  • I want my son to be successful and to love Yahweh. I am worried about the dangerous people around my son.

Ask the person playing King Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel sad. It is hard to see my father like this. My heart is sorrowful; or
  • I must listen to my father and be strong. I have a big responsibility to rule after my father; or
  • I feel worried that I might fail. But I trust Yahweh will be with me.

Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel happy and pleased with David. David has been obedient and has ruled my people well; or
  • My love for Solomon means that I too feel the sadness that Solomon feels about his father; or
  • I love David. I love how careful David is to honor me. I love Solomon and the way he listens to his father. If Solomon obeys me, I will do all that I have promised David. That is my great desire, to bless the house of David.

Continue the drama.

In the second scene, King David told Solomon to remember what Joab, the son of Zeruiah, did to King David. David told Solomon to deal with Joab in a wise way for killing Abner, son of Ner, and Amasa, son of Jether.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Joab, the son of Zeruiah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel proud and strong. I have made myself a strong leader in Israel. I am untouchable. Even King David could not punish me. I will get even stronger when David dies; or
  • I am glad that I killed Abner and Amasa. I do not regret it.

Ask the person playing King David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel angry with Joab; or
  • I am worried that Joab will trouble Solomon as he has troubled me. Joab is able to destroy the kingdom or even remove my son from the throne; or
  • I hate to be associated with Joab's crimes. Someone must kill Joab so the people of Israel know that I am not guilty of the murders.

Ask the person playing King Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am angry with Joab. I can see how dangerous Joab is; or
  • I really want to punish Joab for his crimes; or
  • I feel confident that I can find a clever way to kill Joab.

Continue the drama.

In the third scene, King David told Solomon to be kind to the sons of Barzillai because they were kind to King David when he was escaping from Absalom.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the sons of Barzillai, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We love King David; or
  • The whole nation is very sad that King David is dying; or
  • As our father loved King David and was loyal to him, we will love and be loyal to King Solomon.

Ask the person playing King David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I love Barzillai; or
  • I very much desire that Barzillai's sons should be rewarded for the help they gave me; or
  • I feel loyal to Barzillai and I want my son to feel the same way.

Ask the person playing King Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel pleased with Barzillai and his sons; or
  • I love Barzillai and his sons because they showed my father love; or
  • I want to reward Barzillai and his sons as my father has asked.

Continue the drama.

In the fourth scene, King David told Solomon to remember Shimei who cursed King David on the day he went to Mahanaim. King David told Solomon not to consider Shimei innocent or let him die a peaceful death.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Shimei, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel good and free. King David promised me not to kill me; or
  • I feel safe from danger; or
  • I still hate King David because he took Saul's throne and I am not sad that he is dying.

Ask the person playing King David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I want to be careful for my son. My enemies are a threat to my son. I am worried; or
  • I feel angry with Shimei.

Continue the drama.

In the fifth scene, King David died, and the people buried David in Jerusalem. King Solomon ruled Israel in the place of his father, David, and his kingdom was firmly established.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel very sad. I miss my father; or
  • I feel very proud of what my father has done. My father has ruled Israel well and has left me a great kingdom.
