Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel pleased with David; or
  • I am glad that Solomon is doing all that his father commanded him

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 2:1–12 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

When King David knew that he was about to die, he commanded Solomon to keep the commands of Yahweh, his God. These commands included the ten commandments and all the requirements of Yahweh. Use the same words or phrases for king, Yahweh, and God as you used in previous passages. For more information on King, Yahweh, or God, refer to the Master Glossary.

King David told Solomon to walk in the paths of Yahweh and to keep Yahweh's statutes, commandments, ordinances, and testimonies as Yahweh gave them in the law of Moses. A statute is a law or rule about how to perform duties in the tabernacle or temple. A statute may also refer to a special required task or instructions for how to live in society. A commandment is a law, a rule, or an instruction. Yahweh gave Moses 10 commandments to give to the people of Israel. These commandments taught the Israelites how to live well together in a good relationship with Yahweh and each other. Ordinances usually refer to the ritual rules that Yahweh gave the people of Israel about offering sacrifices and feast celebrations. Yahweh wrote the ten commandments on two stone tablets. The people of Israel called the stone tablets with the law of Yahweh "the testimony." This law proved that Yahweh and the people of Israel had a covenant, or a special relationship.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for statutes, commandments, ordinances, and testimonies. If you decide to just use the general terms "laws" and "commands," discuss how you will say that. If you decide to use all four special words for these types of laws, look up statutes, commandments, ordinances or testimonies in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated these ideas in another book of the Bible, use the same words that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for the law of Moses. This law is the general, special instructions that Yahweh gave to Moses and includes all of these other types of laws. For more information about the law of Moses, refer to the Master Glossary. If you have already translated "law of Moses" in another book of the Bible, use the same words you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Yahweh promised David that if David's descendants walked faithfully before Yahweh with all their heart and soul, Yahweh would make sure that one of David's descendants would be on the throne of Israel, or be king of Israel. Use the same word for throne that you have used in previous passages. Someone's descendants are their children, their grandchildren, and all the children in their family for generations.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for descendants. For more information, refer to descendants in the Master Glossary. If you have translated this idea in another book of the Bible, use the same word here. Pause this audio here.

Walking faithfully is special language that describes how someone lives their life. A person who walks faithfully is a person who behaves the way Yahweh asks him to behave. That person obeys Yahweh. The heart is a muscle inside our body that keeps our blood flowing, but in the Bible, the heart is special language for the place where a person's thoughts, feelings, and decisions happen. The soul is the total invisible, inner part of a person. This part of a person may live on even after their body dies.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for "walk faithfully with all their heart and soul." Look up "heart" and "soul" in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated these ideas in another book of the Bible,use the same words that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

David told Solomon to remember that Joab, the son of Zeruiah, killed or shed the blood of the two commanders of Israel, Abner and Amasa. A commander is a military leader who is in charge of soldiers. Commanders in Israel's army led their soldiers in battle. Use the same word or phrase for commander as you used in previous passages.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for blood. When someone sheds the blood of someone, they kill that person. The Israelites believed that someone's life was in their blood. For more information about blood, refer to the Master Glossary. Pause this audio here.

Joab got blood on his belt around his waist and his sandals on his feet. A belt was a piece of cloth that people tied around their waist to hold their robe in place.

Stop here and discuss as a group what words or phrases you will use for belt and for sandals. If needed, look at a picture of sandals again. Pause this audio here.

David told Solomon to deal with Joab wisely and not to let his grey head go down to Sheol in peace. Sheol is the word throughout the Old Testament for the place of the dead. People thought that Sheol was under the earth. Therefore, the people talked about going down to Sheol. Sheol includes the ideas of death, the underworld, and a place where people buried dead bodies. It was also a place of darkness and silence, weakness, and no joy. Most ancient cultures had a concept of a place for the dead under the earth.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for Sheol. Look up Sheol in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

David told Solomon not to let Joab's gray head go down to Sheol in peace. When people live in peace, they are completely well. Those people have a complete life, and are living in harmony with each other, with nature, and most importantly, with Yahweh. Someone who lives in peace is content. Someone who dies in peace dies from natural causes like old age.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for peace. Look up peace in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

King David also told Solomon to remember that Shimei had cursed King David when David fled from Absalom. To curse someone is the opposite of to bless someone. When you bless someone, you wish that something good will happen to the person you bless. When you curse that person, you wish that something bad will happen to them. People believed that a curse would come true.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for curse. Look up curse in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

King David says that he swore to Shimei by Yahweh or made an oath not to kill Shimei with the sword. When people make a promise to each other, they can swear an oath to make the promise more serious. When someone swears an oath, they say that they will absolutely keep their promise. And if that person does not keep their promise, Yahweh should punish them. Use the same word or phrase for "swearing by Yahweh" as you used in previous passages. If you find it helpful, you may refer to oath in the Master Glossary.

David said that Solomon is wise and will not bring Shimei down to the grave in blood. A person who has wisdom understands many things and is able to make good decisions that lead to good results. When a person has certain skills, like the ability to build or to make something beautiful, the Bible also says that this person has wisdom.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for wisdom. Look up wisdom in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

After King David died, King Solomon became king, and Yahweh made Solomon's rule strong. A king's rule is the time and way he rules his people. In this case, Solomon's rule also refers to his authority and right to be king.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for rule. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 2:13–25
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 2:13–25 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 2:13–25 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 2:13–25 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the last few stories, King David became old, and David's son Adonijah tried to become the new king. However, Yahweh had chosen David's son Solomon to be the new king. Now David has died, and Solomon is the new king of Israel. In this story, Adonijah asks to marry a woman from David's household. However, Solomon sees that Adonijah wants to cause trouble for Solomon, and so Solomon kills Adonijah.

This story begins when Adonijah visits Bathsheba, Solomon's mother. Remember that in an earlier story, Bathsheba helped Solomon become king instead of Adonijah. So when Adonijah visits, Bathsheba wonders why Adonijah has come. Bathsheba may even be nervous, so she asks if Adonijah comes peacefully, or if Adonijah will cause trouble. Adonijah responds that he comes peacefully and wants to speak with Bathsheba.

Adonijah says that earlier, the people of Israel expected Adonijah to be the next king. In that time, a king's oldest son would usually become the new king. Adonijah was David's oldest living son. However, Adonijah says that things have changed and Yahweh chose Solomon to be king. Now Adonijah just wants to ask Bathsheba for something. Adonijah wants Bathsheba to ask Solomon to allow Adonijah to marry Abishag.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, who gives permission for a man to marry a woman? How does a man ask permission? Pause this audio here.

Remember that Abishag is a woman from the Israelite town of Shunem. When King David grew old, Abishag took care of King David. Abishag was not David's wife, but Abishag lay close to King David to keep King David warm. Abishag was a part of David's household and is now probably a part of Solomon's household. Adonijah needs Solomon's permission to marry Abishag. Remember also that Adonijah tried to become king instead of Solomon. So Solomon probably does not like Adonijah. But Adonijah knows that Bathsheba has power as the mother of the king and that Solomon will likely listen to his mother. So Adonijah politely asks Bathsheba to ask Solomon to allow Adonijah to marry Abishag. Bathsheba agrees.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: When are some times when, instead of asking someone for something yourself, you would ask another person to ask that person for you? Why would you do this? Tell a story about a time when this happened. Pause this audio here.

Bathsheba goes to Solomon. Normally, a king would stay seated on his throne, or special seat, when someone else entered the room. But to show respect for his mother, Solomon gets up and moves towards Bathsheba when Bathsheba arrives. Solomon even bows to his mother and tells people to bring a throne for his mother to sit on. Bathsheba sits on Solomon's right side. A person's right side was a place of honor. Solomon also responds very respectfully when Bathsheba says she has a request. Solomon is ready to listen to and help his mother!

Stop here and look at a picture of a throne together as a group. In your culture, how do you show respect to your mother? How might a king or a leader treat his mother? Pause this audio here.

However, when Bathsheba asks Solomon to allow Adonijah to marry Abishag, Solomon does not want to. Solomon wonders why Bathsheba would ask this. Solomon knows that Adonijah probably wants to cause trouble for Solomon. When David was alive, Abishag was probably a part of King David's household or harem. In that time, a king would have a harem, or a group of wives, female relatives, female servants, and other women that the king was in charge of. When a king died, the new king would take control over the old king's harem. Abishag was probably a part of David's harem and is now a part of Solomon's harem. Adonijah acts like Adonijah has the rights of a new king when Adonijah asks to marry Abishag. This is insulting to Solomon and may show that Adonijah will try again to become king instead of Solomon.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In this time, a king would often marry a woman as a way to gain power. For example, a king might marry the daughter of the king of another nation to form an alliance with that nation. Or, as in this story, a king's son might wish to marry an important woman in the king's household to gain authority in that household. In your culture, how do kings and leaders choose who they will marry? Who can marry members of the king's family or household? How could someone become more powerful or influential because of the person they marry? Pause this audio here.

Solomon says it is like Bathsheba asks Solomon to allow Adonijah to become king! Adonijah is David's oldest son and has tried to become king in the past. If Bathsheba asks for power for Adonijah, then it is like she also asks for power for Abiathar and Joab. Abiathar was a priest, and Joab was the head of the army when David was king. Solomon says this because these two men had supported Adonijah and wished to rule as Adonijah's officials. If Solomon allows Adonijah to marry Abishag, then Solomon might as well give power to Abiathar and Joab, too.

Solomon, however, will not give power to Adonijah or to his followers. Instead, Solomon will order someone to kill Adonijah. Earlier, Solomon allowed Adonijah to live as long as Adonijah did not do anything evil. But now, because of what Adonijah asked for, Adonijah will die. Solomon swears an oath, or a very solemn promise, that this will happen. In this time, when a person swore an oath, that person would often ask God to punish that person if the person did not keep their promise. Solomon says that Adonijah will certainly die today, as certainly as Yahweh lives.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, how do you show that you certainly will do what you promise to do? Pause this audio here.

Solomon knows that Yahweh is the one who gave Solomon full control of Israel, as Solomon's father, David, had. Yahweh promised to David that David's descendants would continue to rule. Yahweh kept that promise and has given Solomon and Solomon's descendants the authority to rule. Since Yahweh chose Solomon and promised these things to Solomon, Adonijah cannot become king. King Solomon orders Benaiah, son of Jehoiada, to kill Adonijah. So Benaiah kills Adonijah. In the next story, King Solomon will make Benaiah the leader of Solomon's army.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 2:13–25 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has three scenes.

In the first scene: Adonijah visits Bathsheba. Adonijah wants Bathsheba to ask Solomon to allow Adonijah to marry Abishag. Bathsheba agrees.

In the second scene: Bathsheba visits Solomon and asks if Adonijah can marry Abishag. Solomon is surprised that Bathsheba would ask this. Solomon knows Adonijah wants to be king, so Solomon swears that Adonijah will surely die. Solomon orders Benaiah to kill Adonijah.

In the third scene: Benaiah kills Adonijah.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Adonijah, Haggith's son
  • Bathsheba, Solomon's mother
  • The people of Israel
  • Yahweh
  • Solomon
  • Abishag of Shunem
  • Abiathar the priest
  • Joab, Zeruiah's son
  • David
  • And Benaiah, Jehoiada's son

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

This story begins after Solomon has become king. In the first scene, Adonijah visits Bathsheba. The storyteller reminds us that Haggith is the name of Adonijah's mother, while Bathsheba is Solomon's mother. Bathsheba first asks Adonijah if Adonijah comes peacefully. Adonijah responds that Adonijah comes peacefully and politely asks permission to say something to Bathsheba. Bathsheba says Adonijah may say it.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your language, how would you ask if someone will act in a peaceful or friendly way? How would you politely ask if you can speak to someone about something? Pause this audio here.

Adonijah then says that Adonijah had the kingdom, or had the right to become the next king. Adonijah emphatically says that Bathsheba knows this! Adonijah exaggerates and says that all the people of Israel looked to Adonijah or expected Adonijah to be king. Remember that a king's oldest son would usually become the new king, so Adonijah probably thought people expected Adonijah, David's oldest son, to be the next king. However, that did not happen. Adonijah says that Yahweh chose Solomon to be king. So now, Adonijah just wants to ask Bathsheba for something. Adonijah politely asks that Bathsheba not say no.

Bathsheba says that Adonijah can ask his question. Adonijah politely asks Bathsheba to ask King Solomon something for Adonijah. Adonijah says that Solomon will not say no to Bathsheba. Adonijah probably says this because Bathsheba is Solomon's mother. Adonijah wants Solomon to allow Adonijah to marry Abishag. Bathsheba agrees to speak to Solomon about this.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Adonijah speaks very politely to Bathsheba and asks permission both to speak and to ask Bathsheba for something. In your language, when you want to ask for something from someone important, how do you speak to that person in a polite and respectful way? How might the important person respond? Discuss how you want to translate Adonijah's polite questions and Bathsheba's responses. Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, Bathsheba goes to speak to Solomon. Solomon sits on a throne and may be in his palace. When Bathsheba arrives, Solomon stands up and bows to Bathsheba. Then Solomon sits on his throne again. Solomon has someone bring another throne for Bathsheba so that Bathsheba can sit in an honorable place at Solomon's right side.

Bathsheba says that she has a small request for Solomon and asks Solomon not to refuse her. Solomon responds that Bathsheba can ask and that Solomon will not say no. Bathsheba asks Solomon to allow Adonijah to marry Abishag.

However, Solomon does not like this request. Solomon asks Bathsheba why she would ask this. It is not clear if Solomon expects Bathsheba to answer this question or if Solomon wants to show that Solomon is surprised. Solomon says it is like Bathsheba asks for Adonijah to become king, since Adonijah is older than Solomon. Solomon also says it is like Bathsheba asks for power for the priest Abiathar and Joab, the son of Zeruiah, who support Adonijah. Bathsheba does not actually ask for Adonijah to rule with Abiathar and Joab. Solomon means that if Solomon agreed to Adonijah's request, then Solomon would help Adonijah to become king. Then Adonijah would make Abiathar and Joab officials.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: When someone asks you something very surprising, how do you show that you are surprised that they would ask this? As an activity, have one person in the group pretend to ask a second person for something important and surprising, like the second person's house. Ask the second person, "How do you feel?" How do you show that this is an unreasonable or crazy request? Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Solomon says that when Bathsheba asks for the marriage, it is like Bathsheba also asks for power for Adonijah, Abiathar, and Joab. Solomon means that this marriage would cause Adonijah, Abiathar, and Joab to become powerful. Discuss how you want to translate Solomon's surprise and response to Bathsheba here. Pause this audio here.

Instead of saying yes to Adonijah's request, Solomon swears by Yahweh, or makes a solemn promise in the name of Yahweh. With this oath, Solomon asks God to punish Solomon if Solomon does not keep his promise. Solomon says that in the same way that it is completely true that Yahweh is alive, people can also be completely sure that Solomon will order that Adonijah will die the same day. Solomon declares that Yahweh is the one who caused Solomon to sit on David's throne. To sit on a throne is a common way to say that a person is a ruler. Yahweh has also established Solomon's dynasty or made a house for Solomon. This house is not an actual building. Solomon's house means Solomon's descendants or family line. So here, this means that Yahweh causes Solomon and Solomon's family to continue to rule. Solomon may mention what Yahweh has done to show that Solomon is right to defend himself as king. Solomon then orders Benaiah, son of Jehoiada, to kill Adonijah.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do people make solemn promises in your language? How do people ask God or a god to be a part of the promise? Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss as a group how you want to translate Solomon's solemn promise here. First, Solomon asks God to punish Solomon if Solomon does not keep his promise. Then Solomon talks about what Yahweh has done for Solomon. David also makes a solemn oath in a similar way that Solomon does here. You can review how you translated it in 1 Kings 1:29–30. Pause this audio here.

In the third scene, Benaiah goes to the place where Adonijah is. Benaiah strikes Adonijah, or attacks Adonijah, so that Adonijah dies.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 2:13–25 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has three scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Adonijah, Haggith's son
  • Bathsheba, Solomon's mother
  • The people of Israel
  • Yahweh
  • Solomon
  • Abishag of Shunem
  • Abiathar the priest
  • Joab, Zeruiah's son
  • David
  • And Benaiah, Jehoiada's son

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Adonijah, the son of David and Haggith, visited Bathsheba, the mother of Solomon.

Bathsheba asked Adonijah, "Have you come to me in peace?"

Adonijah replied, "Yes, in peace." Then Adonijah asked, "May I tell you something?"

Bathsheba answered, "Tell me what it is."

Then Adonijah said, "You know that I had the right to be the king. All the people of Israel hoped that I would become king. But that did not happen. My younger brother is now the king, for this was what Yahweh chose to do. So now, I want to ask you something. Please do not say no."

Bathsheba said, "You can ask."

Adonijah said to Bathsheba, "Ask King Solomon something for me. King Solomon will not say no to you. Ask King Solomon to let me marry Abishag from Shunem."

"All right," Bathsheba answered. "I will ask King Solomon if you can marry Abishag."

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Adonijah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I do not want Bathsheba to think that I will try to become king instead of Solomon. I must be polite to Bathsheba so she will help me; or
  • I will pretend to accept that Solomon is king, but I still want to become king and rule! I do not like that Solomon is king instead of me; or
  • If I marry Abishag, I will become more important, because Abishag took care of King David; or
  • Solomon probably does not trust me because I tried to become king. Solomon will not listen to me, so I will ask Bathsheba to ask for me. Solomon will surely listen to his mother.

Ask the person playing Bathsheba, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am surprised that Adonijah has come to visit. Adonijah tried to become king even though David said my son would be the next king! or
  • I know a marriage with Abishag is an important marriage, but if Adonijah marries Abishag, maybe Adonijah will be happy. Then Adonijah will no longer cause trouble for Solomon; or
  • I am not sure why Adonijah wants to marry Abishag. I will speak with Solomon about this. I am sure Solomon will know what is best to do.

Continue the drama.

Bathsheba went to tell King Solomon what Adonijah had requested. King Solomon stood up to meet Bathsheba and bowed to her. Then Solomon sat down on his throne again. Solomon had someone bring another throne for his mother so that his mother could sit in honor at Solomon's right side. Then Bathsheba said to Solomon, "I want to ask you to do a small favor for me. Please do not say no."

Solomon responded, "Mother, you can ask me. I will not say no."

Bathsheba said, "Please allow your brother Adonijah to marry Abishag of Shunem."

But King Solomon responded, "Why do you ask if Adonijah can marry Abishag from Shunem? It is like you ask for Adonijah to become king, since Adonijah is older than I and wants that! Adonijah would rule Israel, and with him would be the priest Abiathar and Joab, the son of Zeruiah!"

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am glad that my mother has come to visit me. I love my mother and respect her; or
  • I am surprised that my mother, Bathsheba, would ask for something for Adonijah! Maybe she does not know how this marriage would help Adonijah become king; or
  • I am upset that Adonijah causes trouble for me. I am even more upset that Adonijah tried to get my mother to help him.

Ask the person playing Bathsheba, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am pleased that my son shows me respect; or
  • I am surprised that my son spoke to me like this! or
  • I am embarrassed that Solomon thinks that I would try to help Adonijah.

Continue the drama.

King Solomon made an oath in the name of Yahweh. Solomon said, "May God punish me, yes, punish me very severely if Adonijah does not die because of what Adonijah asked for. I swear this in the name of Yahweh, who certainly lives! Yahweh, who has given me the same full control of the kingdom as my father David had! Yahweh, who, as Yahweh promised, has given me and my family line the authority to rule! I swear that Adonijah will surely die today!"

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Adonijah will continue to cause trouble for me if Adonijah continues to live; or
  • Since Yahweh chose me to be king, I must continue to be king. I will stop anyone who tries to cause trouble for me; or
  • I am upset that Adonijah has asked for this. If Adonijah had not tried to cause trouble, I would have let Adonijah live in peace.

Continue the drama.

Then King Solomon ordered Benaiah, son of Jehoiada, to kill Adonijah. So Benaiah killed Adonijah.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 2:13–25 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Bathsheba asks if Adonijah comes peacefully. When people do not fight, they live at peace. Here, Bathsheba wants to know if Adonijah comes to cause trouble or comes in a friendly way. For more information on peace, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for peace as you used in previous passages.

Adonijah talks about the kingdom of Israel, which now belongs to Solomon. A kingdom is the territory or the people that a king rules.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for kingdom. Look up kingdom in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Adonijah says that all Israel expected Adonijah to be king. Israel is the name of the territory where the Israelites lived. Sometimes the word Israel means the Israelites, or the people of Israel, as it does here. For more information on Israel, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for Israel as you used in previous passages.

Solomon is king of Israel. A king is someone who rules over a city or a country. For more information on king, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for king as you used in previous passages.

Yahweh caused Solomon to be king. The name Yahweh was a very personal name for God. For more information on Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for Yahweh as you used in previous passages.

Solomon bows to Bathsheba. When a person bows down before someone else, they lower their head or the whole top of their body forward. The person does this to show respect and humility. For more information on bow, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for bow as you used in previous passages.

Stop here as a group and look at a picture of someone bowing. Pause this audio here.

Solomon sits on a throne and asks people to bring a throne for Bathsheba. A throne is a special chair that a ruler, a ruler's family members, or an important person would sit on. Use the same word or phrase for throne as you used in previous passages.

Solomon swears an oath. An oath is a very serious promise. For more information on oath, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for oath as you used in previous passages.

Solomon asks that God punish Solomon if Solomon does not do what Solomon promised. This is a general word for God. For more information on God, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for God as you used in previous passages.

Yahweh has made a dynasty or a house for Solomon. The house of Solomon refers not to a building, but to the people who belong to Solomon. Solomon's house means Solomon's descendants or family line. So here, this means that Yahweh caused Solomon's family to continue to rule as king.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for house. Look up "household" or "house of David" in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 2:26–35
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 2:26–35 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 2:26–35 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 2:26–35 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous story, we heard that King Solomon ordered Benaiah to kill Adonijah because Adonijah tried to become king. Now King Solomon is making sure that no one who is loyal to Adonijah remains to cause more problems. Abiathar the priest is one of the people who betrayed King Solomon and supported Adonijah. King Solomon orders Abiathar to go back to his home in Anathoth. Anathoth is one of the special cities that Yahweh set aside for the Levites or priests to live in. Anathoth is about five kilometers north of Jerusalem.

Stop here as a group and look at a map that shows the location of Anathoth. Pause this audio here.

King Solomon tells Abiathar that he deserves to die because he betrayed Solomon and supported Adonijah. However, King Solomon recognizes that in the past, Abiathar was a great support to King David and suffered through a lot of difficult times with King David. King Solomon also tells about how, in the past, Abiathar was one of the priests who carried the ark of the Sovereign Yahweh. The ark that the storyteller calls the ark of the Lord, or Adonai, Yahweh, here is the ark of the covenant that you have heard about in previous stories. The ark is a powerful symbol of Yahweh's presence with his people.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a picture of what the ark of Yahweh probably looked like.

Pause this audio here.

Because Abiathar has supported King David in the past, King Solomon does not kill Abiathar. However, King Solomon punishes Abiathar for supporting Adonijah and tells Abiathar he is no longer Yahweh's priest. Abiathar must leave Jerusalem and stay in Anathoth.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell about a time when a person deserved a severe punishment but received a less severe punishment. Why did the person receive the less severe punishment? Pause this audio here.

A long time ago, in a previous story, a prophet in a place called Shiloh prophesied that one day the family of Eli would no longer be a part of the priesthood. Abiathar is from the house, or family, of Eli, and now Abiathar is no longer Yahweh's priest. The prophecy is now fulfilled, or is true.

Joab, the commander of the army, who supported Adonijah, hears the news that Adonijah is dead. Joab also hears that Abiathar is no longer Yahweh's priest and that Solomon has sent Abiathar away to Anathoth. Joab is afraid that Solomon will also punish him because Joab supported Adonijah. Joab had been loyal to David before this, as he did not support Absalom when Absalom tried to become king before David died. But now, Joab has betrayed David and Solomon by supporting Adonijah. Joab runs into the tent of Yahweh and grabs the horns on two corners of the altar. The tent of Yahweh is where the people keep the ark of Yahweh in Jerusalem at this time. This area is also where the people offer sacrifices or burnt offerings on an altar to Yahweh. In this story, it is not clear whether this is the original tent of meeting we heard about in previous stories. David may have brought this tent to Jerusalem to put the ark of the covenant inside. Therefore, this altar could have been inside a tent, or it could have been outside in the courtyard, as it was when it was near the original tent.

Stop here and look at a picture of the tent of Yahweh as a group. Pause this audio here.

The altar is where people sacrifice animals as burnt offerings to Yahweh. Sometimes, when the priests sacrificed animals as a sin offering for a person who had sinned accidentally, they smeared the blood of the animals on the horns.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a picture of the altar of burnt offerings. Notice the horns on each corner of the altar. Pause this audio here.

Why does Joab grab onto the horns of the altar? The law that Yahweh gave to Moses says that if a person kills another person accidentally, that person can go to the altar and take hold of the horns. Then Yahweh protects that person, and no one can kill that person for the accidental death the person caused.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, what is the difference in punishment for someone who kills another person accidentally and someone who kills another person intentionally? Pause this audio here.

Remember, before King David died, he told his son Solomon to punish Joab for the sin of murdering Abner, the commander of Israel's army, and Amasa, the commander of Judah's army. Joab did not kill Abner and Amasa accidentally; Joab killed the two innocent men intentionally. Because of this sin, Joab is not really safe when he goes to the altar and holds onto the horns. Joab is also guilty because he did not support the king that Yahweh chose and anointed, King Solomon.

King Solomon hears that Joab is in the tent at the altar, and then Solomon commands Benaiah to go and kill Joab. Benaiah goes into the tent and tells Joab that the king commands him to come out. But Joab refuses to come out and says that he will stay and die in the tent by the altar. Benaiah goes back and tells King Solomon that Joab refuses to come out and says he will die in the tent.

Why does Benaiah not kill Joab right away? Maybe Benaiah does not feel it is right to kill someone inside the tent of Yahweh, especially someone who is holding onto the horns of the altar. But Solomon gives Benaiah permission to kill Joab inside the tent. Solomon says that this is how Yahweh will repay Joab for the murders of Abner and Amasa. Solomon says only Joab is guilty of the murders, not Solomon's father, David, because David did not know that Joab planned to kill Abner and Amasa. Solomon says that Abner and Amasa were very good men, better than Joab. Solomon says that the blood of the two men Joab killed will "rest on the head of Joab and his descendants forever." When people offer sacrifices to Yahweh, Yahweh requires the guilty person to put their hand on the head of the animal. In this way, the person transfers their own guilt to the animal, and then Yahweh removes the guilt through the sacrifice. Here, Solomon is saying that he wants Joab and his descendants to always keep the guilt. In contrast, Solomon says that Yahweh's peace or good fortune will be on David and his descendants and his kingdom forever.

Benaiah goes back into the tent of Yahweh and kills Joab. The story does not tell how Benaiah kills Joab, but he probably kills Joab with a sword. Then people bury Joab near his home, which is in a large area of open land, in the wilderness of Judah. We know from another story that Joab is from Bethlehem, but the storyteller does not mention that in this story.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a map of Bethlehem and the wilderness of Judah, where Joab's family buries him. Pause this audio here.

After Joab dies, King Solomon makes Benaiah the commander of the army. Solomon also appoints Zadok to replace Abiathar as priest. Remember, Zadok is the priest who anointed King Solomon.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 2:26–35 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has seven scenes.

In the first scene: King Solomon sends Abiathar the priest away from Jerusalem and says that Abiathar can no longer be Yahweh's priest. King Solomon explains why he is not punishing Abiathar with death. King Solomon tells Abiathar the priest to return to his fields, or his home, in Anathoth.

In the second scene: Joab hears that Adonijah is dead and that Solomon has removed Abiathar as Yahweh's priest and sent him to Anathoth. Joab becomes afraid, because he knows that King Solomon will also punish him because he was loyal to Adonijah. The storyteller does remind us, however, that Joab had not been loyal to Absalom when Absalom had tried to overthrow King David. Joab runs to the tent of Yahweh and grabs the horns on the altar of sacrifice.

In the third scene: Someone tells King Solomon that Joab is in the tent of Yahweh, and Solomon tells Benaiah to go and kill Joab.

In the fourth scene: Benaiah goes into the tent of Yahweh and confronts Joab. Joab refuses to come out and says that he will die there, beside the altar.

In the fifth scene: Benaiah returns to King Solomon and reports that Joab refuses to come out of the tent. King Solomon tells Benaiah to go ahead and kill Joab inside the tent. Solomon explains why he must punish Joab. Solomon also explains why it is acceptable to kill Joab, even though Joab is holding the horns of the altar inside the tent of Yahweh.

In the sixth scene: Benaiah follows King Solomon's command, returns to the tent of Yahweh, and strikes Joab down, or kills him with a sword. Then, people bury Joab at his home, out in the wilderness area of Judah.

In the seventh scene: King Solomon appoints Benaiah to be the commander of his army. Also, King Solomon appoints Zadok to be a priest to replace Abiathar.

The characters in this passage are:

  • King Solomon
  • A messenger, who tells Solomon about Joab
  • Abiathar the priest, who supported Adonijah
  • Joab, the commander of the army, who supported Adonijah
  • Benaiah, one of David's mighty warriors, who is loyal to Solomon
  • And Zadok, a priest loyal to Solomon

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

Remember, in the first scene, Solomon tells Abiathar to go back to Anathoth. We heard earlier that Anathoth is about five kilometers north of Jerusalem. When Solomon removed Abiathar from being priest, he was fulfilling the word, or message, of Yahweh. Yahweh had told Eli many years before at Shiloh that one day he would remove Eli's family from being priests.

If necessary, stop here and, as a group, look at a map to see where Anathoth is in relation to Jerusalem. Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, Joab runs to the tent of Yahweh. Joab is quickly escaping to a safe place. Joab takes hold of or grabs the horns on the altar of sacrifice, or the bronze altar. This altar was probably in the courtyard of the tabernacle, or the tent that David had brought to Jerusalem for the ark. The phrase "tent of Yahweh" refers to the entire tabernacle, not just the tent that covers the Holy Place and the Most Holy Place. Only priests were allowed to go inside the tent that covered the Holy Place.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a picture of the tabernacle that shows the courtyard, the altar of sacrifice, and the tent that covered the Holy Place and Most Holy Place. Even if this was not the original tent of meeting that Moses constructed, this tent probably looks very similar. Pause this audio here.

Remember, because of the law Yahweh gave to Moses, Joab thinks that grabbing the horns of the altar will protect Joab from punishment. The storyteller gives some background information. He reminds us that Joab supported Adonijah but did not support Absalom.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Consider if you need to mention this fact as two separate ideas, or together in one statement. How will you share this information about Joab's support of Adonijah but not of Absalom? Pause this audio here.

In the third scene, we do not know where Solomon is when he hears that Joab has gone to the altar and grabbed the horns. We do not know who tells Solomon about Joab.

In the fourth scene, the storyteller says that Benaniah enters the tent to confront Joab. This probably means that Benaiah goes into the courtyard area of the tabernacle, near the altar of sacrifice or the bronze altar.

In the fifth scene, Solomon explains why it is acceptable for Benaniah to kill Joab while Joab is holding onto the horns of the altar. Joab is guilty of intentionally killing two innocent men—Abner, the commander of Israel's armies, and Amasa, the commander of Judah's armies. Solomon says that Joab killed Abner and Amasa without telling David about it. Solomon says that Abner and Amasa "were better men and more upright than" Joab. Solomon says that "the guilt of their blood" will rest on the head of Joab and his descendants forever. This means that because Joab killed Abner and Amasa, Joab is guilty forever of this crime, and his family after him will also be guilty. It is impossible for Joab to be innocent.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do you talk about people who are guilty of committing a crime? How might this person's crime affect the people in their family and future generations? Pause this audio here.

Solomon also says that Yahweh's peace will be "on David and his descendants, his house, and his throne" forever. "House" and "throne" here do not mean an actual physical building or a piece of royal furniture. "House" refers to all of David's current and future family members and the people who live under his authority. "Throne" refers to David's kingdom and all of the kings that come after David, through his bloodline, or family line.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 2:26–35 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has seven scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • King Solomon
  • A messenger, who tells Solomon about Joab
  • Abiathar the priest, who supported Adonijah
  • Joab, the commander of the army, who supported Adonijah
  • Benaiah, one of David's mighty warriors, who is loyal to Solomon
  • And Zadok, a priest loyal to Solomon

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

In the first scene, King Solomon banishes Abiathar the priest and says that Abiathar can no longer serve as Yahweh's priest. King Solomon explains that he is not punishing Abiathar with death because Abiathar helped carry the ark of Yahweh and suffered through difficult times with David. However, Solomon tells Abiathar that he must move out of Jerusalem back to Abiathar's fields at Anathoth.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Abiathar, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I wish I had never supported Adonijah! or
  • I am so glad King Solomon is letting me live.

Continue the drama.

In the second scene, Joab hears the news that Adonijah has died and that Solomon sent Abiathar away. Joab runs into the tent of Yahweh and grabs the horns of the altar.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Joab, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • The law will protect me. Solomon cannot kill me here! or
  • I wonder if Solomon knows I killed Abner and Amasa.

Continue the drama.

In the third scene, someone tells King Solomon that Joab is in the tent of Yahweh, holding the horns of the altar. Solomon orders Benaiah to strike Joab down to kill him.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I remember my father David's dying wish. I want to honor my father. Here is my chance to punish Joab!

Continue the drama.

In the fourth scene, Benaiah tells Joab to come out of the tent. Joab refuses to come out and says he will die there inside the tent.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Joab, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • If Solomon kills me here in the tent of Yahweh, then he will be guilty of sin himself! I will take my chances.

Continue the drama.

In the fifth scene, Benaiah tells King Solomon that Joab refuses to come out of the tent. King Solomon tells Benaiah to go ahead and kill Joab inside the tent. The king explains that he must punish Joab because he is guilty of killing innocent people, or shedding innocent blood. The king says that Yahweh will repay Joab for the sin he committed when he killed Abner and Amasa.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Benaiah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • It cannot be right to kill someone inside the tent of Yahweh. I am afraid I will be punished, too!

Ask the person playing King Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Joab murdered Abner and Amasa intentionally. Therefore, I have a right to kill Joab in the tent of Yahweh.

Continue the drama.

In the sixth scene, Benaiah kills Joab. Joab's family buries him at his own home.

In the seventh scene, King Solomon appoints Benaiah to be the commander of his army. Also, King Solomon appoints Zadok to be a priest to replace Abiathar.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 2:26–35 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

King Solomon orders Abiathar to go back to his fields in Anathoth. This means that Abiathar must go back to his land or property in Anathoth. Throughout this story, the storyteller refers to King Solomon as "the king." For more information about "king," refer to the Master Glossary.

Abiathar is a priest. For more information about "priest," refer to the Master Glossary.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for fields or property that someone owns. Pause this audio here.

Abiathar is from the "house," or household, of Eli, and Solomon mentions that Yahweh's peace will be on David's "house" forever. "House" here does not mean a building that people live in. "House" means descendants, family line, and often other people who belong to them. For more information about "house," refer to "household" and "House of David" in the Master Glossary.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for "house of Eli" or "House of David." Pause this audio here.

King Solomon tells Abiathar that he deserves to die. This means that King Solomon has the right to put Abiathar to death because Abiathar supported Adonijah and not Solomon.

King Solomon says that Abiathar helped carry the ark of Adonai Yahweh. Use the same word or phrase for the "ark of Yahweh" that you have used in previous passages. For more information about the ark of the covenant or Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary. Translate these ideas the same way you did in previous passages.

This is the first time that the storyteller uses the name "Adonai Yahweh" in 1 Kings. The name "Adonai" means Lord, or master. This name for God tells us that God is all-powerful.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for Adonai Yahweh and for "ark of Adonai Yahweh." Look up Adonai in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller tells us that Solomon fulfilled the word of Yahweh about the household of Eli when he removed Abiathar as priest. When Yahweh speaks a word, Yahweh is giving a message to people.

Stop here and discuss as a group what phrase you will use for "word of Yahweh." See word in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this phrase in another book of the Bible, use the same phrase here. Pause this audio here.

Use the same word or phrase for the tent of Yahweh as you used in previous passages. In previous passages, the storyteller just called this a tent, but here the storyteller calls it the tent of Yahweh.

Use the same word for altar and horns of the altar as you used in previous passages. For more information about altar, refer to the Master Glossary.

King Solomon talks about commanders that Joab killed. Use the same word for army commanders as you used in previous passages.

King Solomon says that the guilt of Abner and Amasa's blood will rest on the head of Joab and his descendants forever. In other words, Joab and his descendants will always be guilty of murdering Abner and Amasa. Use the same words or phrases for blood and descendants as you have used previously, and refer to blood in the Master Glossary for more information.

Stop here and discuss as a group what phrase you will use for "his guilt will rest on his head." Although the storyteller does not use the word for "guilt" in this story, you may state that Joab will remain guilty, or that Joab will need punishment because of what he did. For more information about guilt, refer to the Master Glossary. If you have already translated this phrase in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

King Solomon calls Amasa and Abner more righteous than Joab. This means that these men acted fairly and obeyed Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for righteous. For more information about righteousness, refer to the Master Glossary. Pause this audio here.

King Solomon also says that peace will be on David and his descendants and his house and throne forever. Use the same words or phrases for David's descendants, David's house, and throne as you have used in previous passages. The meaning of peace here is not the opposite of war, but rather prosperity and being completely well. For more information on peace, refer to the Master Glossary. Translate peace in the same way as you have translated it previously.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 2:36–46
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 2:36–46 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 2:36–46 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 2:36–46 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In 2 Samuel, chapter 16, we heard about a man named Shimei, who was from the family of King Saul. When Shimei saw King David and his army running away from Absalom, Shimei cursed and insulted David, and threw rocks at David and his army. Shimei was angry with David for replacing Saul as king. At that time, King David promised that he would not kill Shimei. However, just before King David died, David instructed his son Solomon to make sure that someone punished Shimei for how he treated David.

Now we see Shimei again. King Solomon sends a message to Shimei to request a meeting. When King Solomon meets with Shimei, Solomon orders Shimei to build a house in Jerusalem and live there. However, Shimei must not go to any other place. Shimei must stay in Jerusalem. We know from the earlier story about Shimei that he lives in a place called Bahurim, a village outside Jerusalem. Bahurim is located on the road that leads from Jerusalem to Jericho.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a map that shows Jerusalem, Bahurim, and Jericho. Pause this audio here.

Solomon warns Shimei that if Shimei disobeys and leaves Jerusalem, Shimei will die. Solomon says to Shimei, "Your blood will be on your own head," or "you will be guilty." Solomon warns Shimei that he will only have himself to blame if he dies—Solomon will not be guilty of murder.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Share a story about a time when someone broke a promise, and then that person took responsibility for the consequences. Pause this audio here.

Solomon mentions the Kidron Valley. The Kidron Valley is a low-lying area that separates the walled city of Jerusalem from the Mount of Olives. The Kidron Valley goes from north to south and is on the eastern boundary of Jerusalem. If Shimei leaves Jerusalem from the east side, Shimei has to cross the Kidron Valley to go to another place. However, Solomon's command means that Shimei cannot leave Jerusalem from any direction.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a map that shows Jerusalem, the Kidron Valley, and the Mount of Olives. Pause this audio here.

Shimei agrees to do what Solomon says and swears an oath that he will stay in Jerusalem. But after a long time, three years later, two of Shimei's slaves run away from Shimei's property. The slaves may have been working on land or property that Shimei owns outside of Jerusalem.

Someone informs Shimei that the two slaves have gone to Gath, which is in Philistine territory. Gath is about 50 kilometers southwest of Jerusalem. The two slaves go to the king of Gath. The king's name is Achish. Shimei rides his donkey to Gath and searches for his slaves. Shimei finds the slaves and brings them back to Jerusalem with him.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a map that shows Jerusalem and Gath. Pause this audio here.

Someone tells King Solomon that Shimei left Jerusalem, went to Gath, and came back. Finally, here is Solomon's opportunity to carry out the last wishes of his father, David. King Solomon orders Shimei to come and see Solomon. Solomon reminds Shimei about the oath or promise Shimei made to Yahweh and to Solomon, that he would not leave Jerusalem. Solomon reminds Shimei that he already knows the result of breaking the oath—Shimei will die. Solomon asks Shimei, "Why did you not keep your oath?"

Stop here and discuss: Tell about a time when you or someone you know made an oath or a strong promise, and then did not keep it. What reasons did you or that person give for not keeping the promise? Pause this audio here.

King Solomon reminds Shimei about how he treated David badly long ago, when Shimei cursed and insulted David, and threw rocks at him. Solomon warns Shimei that now Yahweh will punish him for this wrong behavior. Solomon says this to make sure that Shimei understands that the punishment is from Yahweh, not from Solomon. According to King Solomon, Yahweh will bless Solomon, and David's throne will be "secure before Yahweh forever." This means that Solomon believes that David's kingdom will never end.

Once again, Solomon calls Benaiah, son of Jehoiada, to "strike down" a person. Benaiah is the commander of Solomon's army, and he is the man who killed both Adonijah and Joab in the previous stories. Benaiah follows Solomon's order and kills Shimei.

Now that Solomon has removed Adonijah, Joab, Abiathar, and Shimei, Solomon has full control over his kingdom. Solomon has fulfilled the last wishes of his father, David. David had asked Solomon to make sure that he punished both Joab and Shimei.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 2:36–46 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has four scenes.

In the first scene: King Solomon meets with Shimei and tells him to build a house in Jerusalem. King Solomon tells Shimei that Shimei must stay in Jerusalem. If Shimei leaves the city, he will die. Shimei agrees to stay in Jerusalem.

In the second scene: After Shimei has stayed in Jerusalem for about three years, his two slaves run away to Gath. Someone tells Shimei where the two slaves are, that they have gone to Achish, the king of Gath. Shimei leaves Jerusalem and goes to Gath to find and bring back his slaves.

In the third scene: Someone tells King Solomon that Shimei left Jerusalem and came back. King Solomon calls for Shimei to come see him. King Solomon reminds Shimei that he promised not to leave Jerusalem and asks why Shimei broke his promise. King Solomon reminds Shimei of the wrong way he treated David. Solomon orders Benaiah to kill Shimei, and Benaiah obeys.

In the fourth scene: We see that King Solomon's kingdom is now established.

The characters in this passage are:

  • King Solomon
  • Shimei
  • The messenger or messengers who tell Shimei to come and meet with King Solomon
  • Shimei's two slaves
  • Achish, king of Gath
  • The person who tells Shimei that his slaves are in Gath
  • The person who tells Solomon that Shimei left Jerusalem
  • The messenger or messengers who tell Shimei to come back to meet with King Solomon
  • And Benaiah

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, King Solomon orders Shimei to come and see him. King Solomon probably sends messengers or soldiers to Shimei. Then the messengers or soldiers bring Shimei to meet with Solomon. Solomon orders Shimei to build himself a house in Jerusalem, live there, and not go anywhere else. Then Solomon warns Shimei, "The day you leave and cross the Kidron Valley, you can be sure you will die; your blood will be on your own head." When Solomon mentions crossing the Kidron Valley, Solomon just means this as a way to show that someone is leaving Jerusalem. There are other ways to leave Jerusalem that do not go across the Kidron Valley. Solomon means that Shimei will definitely die if Shimei leaves Jerusalem.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Solomon warns Shimei that he will die the "day you leave" Jerusalem. In your language, what is the best way to say this as a strong warning, where there is no doubt that this consequence will happen? Pause this audio here.

Solomon tells Shimei, "Your blood will be on your own head" if Shimei leaves Jerusalem. This means that Shimei will only have himself to blame if he breaks his oath and leaves Jerusalem.

As a group, discuss another question: In your language, how do you say that someone is responsible for what happens if he breaks an oath or a promise? Pause this audio here.

Shimei answers Solomon and says, "What you say is good," which means "I agree with you." Shimei promises to do what Solomon orders, and Shimei humbles himself and says that he is Solomon's servant and calls Solomon "my lord the king."

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What is the best way in your language to show this whole conversation between Solomon and Shimei? The conversation begins when Solomon tells Shimei to build a house in Jerusalem and not go anywhere else. The conversation ends when Shimei agrees to do what Solomon says. Also, what words of respect do you use to show humility and respect to a person in authority? Pause this audio here.

The scene ends with the information that Shimei stays in Jerusalem for a long time. We know that "a long time" means a few years, because the next part of the story mentions that Shimei leaves Jerusalem "three years later."

The second scene happens three years later. This means what happens next happens about three years after Shimei swore to Solomon that he would not go outside of Jerusalem. Shimei learns that two of his slaves have run away to Achish, the king of Gath. It is not clear if the slaves ran away from Shimei's house in Jerusalem or from Shimei's property in the village of Bahurim. When Shimei hears that the slaves ran away, he rides his donkey to King Achish in Gath and looks for his slaves. The city of Gath is about 50 kilometers southwest of Jerusalem. After Shimei finds the slaves, he brings the slaves back to Jerusalem with him.

In the third scene, after Shimei is back home in Jerusalem with his slaves, someone tells King Solomon that Shimei left Jerusalem, went to Gath, and came back. Solomon again sends messengers or soldiers to get Shimei and bring Shimei to meet with Solomon. King Solomon reminds Shimei of his oath to Yahweh that he would not leave Jerusalem. Solomon also reminds Shimei about the warning of what would happen if Shimei left Jerusalem. Solomon repeats the conversation that he and Shimei had in scene one. Then Solomon asks Shimei, "Why did you not keep your oath to Yahweh and obey the command I gave you?" Solomon is not really asking Shimei for an answer to this question. Solomon asks the question because he wants to remind Shimei of Shimei's oath and show that Solomon knows Shimei disobeyed.

To use Yahweh's name to make a promise is very serious. If a person does not keep an oath or a promise made in Yahweh's name, that person is guilty of breaking the third commandment, which says, "You shall not take Yahweh's name in vain." The person who does this is not respecting Yahweh's name.

Next, Solomon reminds Shimei about the way Shimei cursed and insulted King David. Solomon says, "You know in your heart all the wrong you did to my father, David." Solomon means that even though Shimei does not say it out loud, Shimei really knows inside that he was wrong to curse and insult David.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your language, how do you say that someone "knows in their heart" the truth about something? Share about a time when you acted like everything was okay, but you "knew in your heart" that something was wrong. Pause this audio here.

Solomon continues on to say that now Yahweh will punish Shimei for his sin, that Yahweh will bless Solomon, and that "the throne of David" will continue forever. Remember, we learned in the previous story that "throne" here does not mean a piece of royal furniture. It means that David's kingdom will continue forever.

In the story, Solomon says many things to Shimei. The storyteller makes it clear who is speaking by reminding the listener this way: "The king also said to Shimei."

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: If one person in the story speaks many things, how will you remind your listener which person is speaking? Pause this audio here.

After Solomon is finished speaking to Shimei, he orders Benaiah to kill Shimei, and Benaiah obeys.

In scene four, we see that Solomon is now firmly in control of his kingdom. The storyteller says that Solomon's kingdom is in Solomon's hands, which means that Solomon has full power and control over his kingdom. Storytellers often used the idea of a person's hands to show that the person had power over that thing.

Stop here and discuss as a group: How will you talk about Solomon's kingdom being firmly in Solomon's hands? Pause this audio here.

The people who supported Adonijah or did not support Solomon's father, King David, no longer threaten King Solomon. This strong closing statement shows that everything that has happened in this story and the previous stories has given Solomon strong control of his kingdom.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 2:36–46 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has four scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • King Solomon
  • Shimei
  • The messenger or messengers who tell Shimei to come and meet with King Solomon
  • Shimei's two slaves
  • Achish, king of Gath
  • The person who tells Shimei that his slaves are in Gath
  • The person who tells Solomon that Shimei left Jerusalem
  • The messenger or messengers who tell Shimei to come back to meet with King Solomon
  • And Benaiah

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

In the first scene, King Solomon meets with Shimei and tells him to build a house in Jerusalem. King Solomon tells Shimei that he must stay in Jerusalem and that if Shimei leaves the city, Shimei will die. Shimei agrees that he will stay in Jerusalem.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Shimei, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am relieved that King Solomon is giving me a chance to live! or
  • I wonder if King Solomon knows about all the bad things I said to King David, his father; or
  • It will be hard for me to take care of my property in Bahurim if I am stuck here in Jerusalem.

Continue the drama.

In the second scene, Shimei's two slaves run away to Gath. Someone tells Shimei where the two slaves are. Shimei leaves Jerusalem and goes to Gath to find and bring back his slaves.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Shimei, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I have a right to go and reclaim my property, so it is okay for me to leave Jerusalem; or
  • I am nervous. I hope Solomon does not find out.

Continue the drama.

In the third scene, someone tells King Solomon that Shimei left Jerusalem and came back. King Solomon calls for Shimei to come see Solomon. King Solomon reminds Shimei that he promised not to leave Jerusalem and asks why Shimei broke his promise. King Solomon reminds Shimei of the wrong way Shimei treated David. Solomon orders Benaiah to kill Shimei, and Benaiah obeys.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Now I can finally fulfill my father David's wishes and give Shimei a just punishment; or
  • I cannot believe Shimei would break an oath he made to Yahweh and to me! What a fool.

Continue the drama.

In the fourth scene, we see that Solomon clearly has full control of his kingdom.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I hope God blesses me and my kingdom; or
  • I am a good son and king. I did everything my father, David, asked me to do.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 2:36–46 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

King Solomon sends for Shimei two different times in this story. "Sends for" means that Solomon sends messengers or soldiers to bring Shimei to Solomon. For more information about king, refer to the Master Glossary, and use the same word you have used in previous passages.

King Solomon says that if Shimei disobeys Solomon, Shimei's blood, or guilt, will be on his own head. Remember that this means that Shimei is responsible, or guilty, for his own wrong actions. If you use the word blood in your translation, use the same word or phrase for blood as you have used in previous passages, and remember that blood is in the Master Glossary. If you use the word guilt in your translation, use the same word or phrase for guilt as you have used in previous passages, and remember that guilt is in the Master Glossary.

When Shimei agrees to obey King Solomon, Shimei says that he is King Solomon's servant. This does not mean that Shimei becomes a servant in King Solomon's household. Shimei calls himself Solomon's servant to show humility and respect to King Solomon. To call himself a servant also shows that Shimei understands that now he is not free to come and go from Jerusalem as he pleases. Use the same word or phrase for servant as you used in previous passages. For more information on servant, refer to the Master Glossary.

Shimei calls King Solomon "my lord the king." Here, Shimei means that King Solomon is Shimei's master and has authority over him. Use the same word or phrase for "my lord the king" as you used in previous passages. For more information on lord, refer to the Master Glossary.

When Shimei leaves Jerusalem to search for the runaway servants, or slaves, in Gath, Shimei saddles his donkey. This means that Shimei got on his donkey and rode the donkey to Gath. For more information on donkey, refer to the Master Glossary, and use the same word for donkey that you have used in previous passages.

Stop here and look at a picture of a saddled donkey as a group. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller uses the same word for Shimei's slaves as he uses for a servant in other places. This kind of servant has no control over their own life. They cannot leave their master, and they usually have no choice over anything in their lives.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for servant when it means a slave who has no choice over who he works for or what he does. Use the same word for servant when it means this kind of slave as you have used in other passages in the Bible. Pause this audio here.

When Solomon speaks to Shimei the second time, Solomon asks, "Did I not make you swear by Yahweh..."? "Swear by" means that Shimei made an oath to Yahweh. Use the same word or phrase for swear by or oath as you used in previous passages. For more information on oath, refer to the Master Glossary.

Solomon says that Yahweh will repay Shimei for the wrong things he did against King David. Yahweh will punish Shimei for the evil things he did. Use the same word for repay as you used in previous passages. For more information about Yahweh and evil, refer to the Master Glossary, and use the same words as you have used in previous passages.
