When Solomon says "King Solomon will be blessed," Solomon says his own name. Solomon means that Yahweh will bless him. Use the same word for bless that you have used in previous passages. For more information on bless, refer to the Master Glossary.

Solomon also says that David's throne will remain secure before Yahweh forever. Next, we learn that Solomon's kingdom is strongly in his hands, or in his power. The phrases "the throne will remain secure" and "the kingdom is established" mean the same thing. Both phrases mean that the kingdom that David, Solomon, and their descendants rule is strong and will continue. Use the same words for throne and kingdom as you used in previous passages. For more information on kingdom, refer to the Master Glossary.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 3:1–15
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 3:1–15 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 3:1–15 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 3:1–15 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the last story, King Solomon made his kingdom safe by dealing with his enemies. In this story, King Solomon married the daughter of Pharaoh, the king of Egypt. Because of this, the kingdom of Israel and the kingdom of Egypt became allies. Kingdoms that were allies would not go to war with one another and would defend each other if another enemy attacked. To make a marriage between one king and the daughter of another king or his close relative was a common way people made this kind of alliance. It is likely that it was the king of Egypt who suggested this alliance because he saw that the kingdom of Israel was becoming stronger.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about a different community that your community would ask or has asked to help you in times of war. Why did this community help you? Describe your community's relationship with that community and explain the reason you are united. Pause this audio here.

King Solomon gave the daughter of the Pharaoh a temporary place to stay in Jerusalem, the City of David, until Solomon finished building his own palace. We know from stories in 1 Chronicles that during this time, King Solomon also built the temple of Yahweh and the wall around Jerusalem. Any place that the ark of the covenant had touched became holy. Therefore, King Solomon would not put Pharaoh's daughter in his father David's palace because the ark of the covenant of Yahweh was nearby.

This marriage shows that King Solomon had been able to establish his kingdom internationally. However, the people of Israel were still sacrificing at the high places. High places were local shrines or altars that people had dedicated to Yahweh. There were many of these high places in different places throughout Israel. Yahweh had commanded that the people of Israel make their offerings to Yahweh in the place where Yahweh had chosen, Jerusalem. But Solomon had not yet built the temple for the people to honor and worship Yahweh. It is likely that the storyteller wants us to see that there is still work to be done to make Solomon's kingdom perfect.

Solomon loved Yahweh by doing everything that Solomon's father, David, had commanded him. King David had commanded Solomon to obey everything that Yahweh had commanded in the law of Moses. However, Solomon still sacrificed and burned incense at the local altars. It is likely that the storyteller is showing that Solomon had not completely obeyed Yahweh yet because Solomon had not yet built the temple. The storyteller could also be explaining to us that Solomon had not completely obeyed Yahweh because Solomon was committed to the high places. Yahweh allowed people to sacrifice to Yahweh at the high places only until people built the temple. One day, King Solomon went to sacrifice at the altar that was in Gibeon. Gibeon was a great city located about ten kilometers to the northwest of Jerusalem.

Stop here and look at a map of Jerusalem to Gibeon as a group. Pause this audio here.

The altar at Gibeon was the primary altar of all the local altars in Israel because the bronze altar of sacrifice and the Tent of Meeting that Moses had made were in Gibeon. But the ark of the covenant was in another tent near King David's palace in Jerusalem.

Stop here and look at a picture of the bronze altar and the Tent of Meeting as a group. Pause this audio here.

King Solomon had gone to sacrifice in Gibeon many times and had offered many burnt offerings there. To make a burnt offering, the people of Israel killed the animal and burnt it completely on the altar. A person offered a burnt offering to dedicate himself to Yahweh as completely as he had burnt the animal. It is likely that King Solomon had gone to Gibeon to dedicate his kingdom to Yahweh.

While Solomon was in Gibeon, Yahweh appeared to Solomon at night in a dream and told Solomon to ask for anything that Solomon wanted Yahweh to give him. It was rare that Yahweh would appear or speak to someone in a dream.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Tell stories of people in the history of your culture who received a message from God or spirits through a dream. Pause this audio here.

Solomon responded to Yahweh while still in his dream. Solomon said that Yahweh had been kind to Yahweh's servant, David, and that David had behaved in a good, loyal, and honest way with Yahweh. Solomon said that Yahweh had shown David constant love and friendship by making one of David's sons king of Israel. Yahweh, Solomon's God, had made Solomon king of Israel even though Solomon was a little child. Solomon was not a little child when he became king, but Solomon was young and inexperienced. Solomon said that he did not know how to go out or come in or how to rule the many people of Israel, the people whom Yahweh had chosen to be Yahweh's own people. Solomon, therefore, asked Yahweh to give him an understanding heart that is able to know the difference between good and evil. The Israelites thought of the heart as the place where a person's feelings, thoughts, and intentions come from. Solomon was asking Yahweh for intelligence to rule. Solomon said that without Yahweh's help, no one would be able to rule Yahweh's many people.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Talk about the youngest leader your community has ever had. List the qualities that a leader needs in order to be able to rule successfully. What qualities of leadership did the youngest leader of your community have no experience in? Pause this audio here.

The words of Solomon were pleasing in the sight of Yahweh. The storyteller calls Yahweh by a respectful title that means master or lord. Yahweh told Solomon that Yahweh would give Solomon wisdom. Solomon had not asked for a long life, wealth, or revenge on his enemies. Instead, Solomon had asked for wisdom to rule justly. Yahweh said that Yahweh would give Solomon greater wisdom than any king who had come before Solomon or any king who would come after Solomon. Yahweh also said that Yahweh gave Solomon the things that Solomon had not asked for: wealth and honor, so that no other king could compare with Solomon as long as Solomon lived. Yahweh also promised Solomon that if Solomon walked in Yahweh's ways or obeyed Yahweh's laws and commands as David had done, Yahweh would give Solomon a long life.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about your greatest leader and the things that the leader owned. What would people in your community most want from all the things that the leader owned? Pause this audio here.

Solomon woke up and realized that Yahweh had spoken to Solomon in a dream. Solomon returned to Jerusalem and stood before the ark of the covenant of the Lord. The storyteller does not tell us why Solomon stood before the ark of the covenant, but it is likely to confirm and celebrate the agreement that Yahweh had made with Solomon in the dream. Solomon offered burnt offerings and peace or fellowship offerings. Solomon was likely dedicating himself to Yahweh with the burnt offerings and celebrating his relationship with Yahweh with the peace offerings. When people ate a meal together in that culture, they were showing the good relationships they had with each other. In a peace offering, people burned part of an animal on the altar, and they ate the rest of the meat. This was like having a meal with Yahweh, and it showed that the people were in a good relationship with Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How have the leaders of your community communicated with God or the spirits? How would the leaders physically respond to a message that God told them in a dream? Pause this audio here.

Then Solomon made a feast for all his servants. It is likely that this feast was somewhere else, not next to the ark of the covenant.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 3:1–15 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has seven scenes.

In the first scene: King Solomon made an alliance with Pharaoh, King of Egypt, by marrying

Pharaoh's daughter. King Solomon brought Pharaoh's daughter to live in the City of David until Solomon had finished building a palace for himself, Yahweh's temple, and the wall around Jerusalem.

In the second scene: The people were still making offerings at the high places, or local altars, because they had not yet built the temple to honor and worship Yahweh.

In the third scene: King Solomon loved Yahweh and obeyed all the commands that Solomon's father gave him, but Solomon still made offerings at the local altars. Solomon offered many burnt offerings at Gibeon, the most important local altar. One day, Yahweh appeared to King Solomon at Gibeon in a dream. Yahweh asked Solomon what gift Solomon would like from Yahweh.

In the fourth scene: Solomon said that Yahweh had shown Solomon's father, David, constant friendship by making Solomon king. Solomon told Yahweh how young and inexperienced Solomon was to rule Yahweh's great people. So Solomon asked Yahweh for wisdom to rule justly.

In the fifth scene: Yahweh was pleased that Solomon had asked for wisdom and not long life, riches, or the death of his enemies. So, Yahweh gave Solomon wisdom and also riches and a great reputation. Yahweh also promised to give Solomon a long life if Solomon obeyed Yahweh as David had.

In the sixth scene: Solomon went back to Jerusalem and offered burnt offerings and peace or fellowship offerings in front of the ark of the covenant of Yahweh.

In the seventh scene: Solomon made a feast for all his servants.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Yahweh
  • King Solomon
  • Pharaoh, king of Egypt
  • Pharaoh's daughter
  • The people of Israel
  • King David
  • And King Solomon's servants

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, King Solomon married the king of Egypt's daughter. People call the king of Egypt, "Pharaoh." Pharaoh is not the king of Egypt's personal name, but his title.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What titles do you give various leaders in your community? Pause audio here.

By marrying Pharaoh's daughter, King Solomon became the son-in-law of Pharaoh, and the kingdoms of Israel and Egypt became allies. Allies are kingdoms that do not attack each other. Instead, allies help each other if others attack them.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about two communities that have an alliance or an agreement of friendship. Discuss how the two communities confirmed their alliance. Pay attention to the words and phrases you use for alliance. Pause this audio here.

King Solomon gave Pharaoh's daughter a temporary place to live in Jerusalem. The people also called Jerusalem the City of David because David captured it from the Jebusites. Pharaoh's daughter lived in that temporary place until Solomon finished building his own house or palace, the house or temple of Yahweh, and the wall around Jerusalem. It took Solomon 13 years to build his palace.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What do you call the house of a king in your language? Pause this audio here.

The storyteller calls the temple "the house of Yahweh" because Yahweh's presence would be in the temple when the people put the ark there. Solomon started building the temple four years after he became king, and Solomon spent seven years building the temple. Jerusalem was a walled city. People built walls around important cities to protect the people and the wealth inside the city. It is likely that Solomon was extending or repairing a wall that already existed.

Stop here and look at a picture of an example of a walled city as a group. Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, the people of Israel were still sacrificing at the high places or local altars. The people had been doing this from the days when they entered the land with Joshua. Even at this time, the people were still sacrificing at the local altars because they had not yet built the house for Yahweh's name. When someone builds something for the name of Yahweh, it means that they are building it to honor and praise Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about temples, shrines, altars, or any places of worship in your country. By what names do people call them and why? How will you say that Solomon had not yet built the house for Yahweh's name? Pause this audio here.

High places were local altars or places of sacrifice. The tribes of the Canaanites that lived in the land before the people of Israel often built their altars on high places like hills and mountains, so people often knew these local altars as "high places." Many of the altars that the people of Israel built were not on hills or mountains, and the people of Israel dedicated these places to Yahweh. Therefore "high places" in this story refers to local sacred places that the people dedicated to Yahweh. Yahweh wanted the people to make their offerings in Jerusalem, but Solomon had not built the temple yet. Therefore, the people of Israel built many altars near their cities. After Solomon finished building the temple, Yahweh did not want his people to sacrifice at these altars.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Talk about the sacrifice rituals that your community and the communities around you perform. Compare the different places they perform their rituals. Pay attention to the words and phrases that you use for the places where people offer the sacrifices. Pause this audio here.

In the third scene, the storyteller tells us that Solomon loved Yahweh. Solomon showed that he loved Yahweh by following his father's statutes. David had commanded Solomon to obey all that Yahweh had commanded in the law of Moses. But even though Solomon loved Yahweh, Solomon still sacrificed at local altars. Solomon sacrificed at more than one local altar. The storyteller says that Solomon made sacrifices and made the smoke go up. To make the smoke go up could refer to burning an animal or vegetable offerings or burning incense. It is likely that Solomon made sacrifices and offered incense offerings. The contrast between Solomon loving Yahweh and sacrificing at the local altars shows that Solomon had not yet finished the work that Yahweh had given him. Solomon had not yet built the temple.

Solomon often went to sacrifice at the altar in Gibeon, a great city about 10 kilometers from Jerusalem. Over the years, Solomon had sacrificed at least 1,000 burnt offerings on the altar at Gibeon! The altar at Gibeon was the most important local altar because the bronze altar of sacrifice and the Tent of Meeting that Moses made were there. This time, Solomon had gone to Gibeon to offer burnt offerings, most likely to dedicate his kingdom to Yahweh. While Solomon was at Gibeon, Yahweh appeared to Solomon at night in a dream and God told Solomon to ask for anything he wanted. The storyteller uses the proper name for Yahweh and the more general word for God in the same statement.

In the fourth scene, Solomon, who was still dreaming, answered Yahweh. Solomon told Yahweh that Yahweh had shown steadfast, or loyal, love to Solomon's father, David. Solomon called David Yahweh's servant because David served and respected Yahweh. Solomon said that Yahweh showed steadfast love to David because David walked before Yahweh in faithfulness, in righteousness, and in uprightness of heart. Solomon was talking about David's behaviour and character toward Yahweh when he talked about how David walked before Yahweh. To walk in faithfulness is to be loyal, dependable, and trustworthy. When a person walks in righteousness with Yahweh, the person behaves in a right way because they have a good relationship with Yahweh. A person who walks in uprightness of heart is a person who is committed to do Yahweh's will. That person behaves honestly and desires to please Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about someone who has pretended to be something with his words, but his behaviour has been the opposite of his words; for example, a judge who takes bribes. Then discuss, which words or phrases do you use to describe the behaviour of an honest judge who does not take bribes? Pause this audio here.

Solomon said that Yahweh had continued to show steadfast love to David by giving David a son, Solomon, to sit on David's throne, which means to be king of Israel. Solomon spoke these words to Yahweh before he asked for what he wanted. Solomon wanted to thank and praise Yahweh.

Then Solomon told Yahweh that Yahweh, Solomon's own God, had made Solomon king even though Solomon was a little child. Solomon showed that he was committed to Yahweh and had a personal relationship with Yahweh when he called Yahweh his own God. Solomon said that he was a little child, or inexperienced, and did not know how to go out or come in. Going out and coming in is usually a special way to talk about the way a person leads an army to war. In this case, Solomon was saying that he was young and did not know how to rule or how to take his army to war. Solomon said that Solomon, the servant of Yahweh, was in the middle of the many or countless people that Yahweh had chosen. Solomon described the many people as a great and numberless people.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How can you describe or speak of a group of people so many that no one could count them? Pause audio here.

Solomon was telling Yahweh how small Solomon felt for the great work that Yahweh had given Solomon. Solomon asked Yahweh to give him an understanding heart to rule Yahweh's people and to know the difference between good and evil. Solomon was asking Yahweh to give Solomon wisdom and intelligence to rule justly. Then Solomon asked Yahweh if there was anyone who would be able to rule Yahweh's many people. Solomon did not expect an answer. Solomon was saying that without Yahweh's help, no one could rule Israel.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Describe the most honest, fair, and honorable leader or judge you have ever had. Talk about that person's behaviour and decisions, and how he treated different classes of people and how the community spoke about him. Pay attention to the words and phrases that you use to describe his character. Pause this audio here.

In the fifth scene, the storyteller calls Yahweh the lord, or master. This is a respectful title. Yahweh was pleased because Solomon asked for wisdom and did not ask for a long life, riches, or the death of his enemies. The storyteller repeats slowly and clearly each thing Solomon did not ask for so that he can show a strong contrast between what Solomon did ask for and what he did not ask for. Yahweh told Solomon to look and see how Yahweh would do what Solomon had asked for, and to watch how Yahweh would give Solomon greater wisdom than anyone who had lived before Solomon or would live after Solomon.

Stop here and discuss a word or phrase that you use in your language to focus the attention of the listener on the thing you are about to say next, as Yahweh did here when he asked Solomon to see what he would do for Solomon. Pause this audio here.

And Yahweh also gave Solomon the things that Solomon had not asked for, like wealth, honor, and respect. Yahweh said that no king would have riches or respect to compare with Solomon as long as Solomon lived. Yahweh also promised Solomon a long life, but only if Solomon walked in Yahweh's ways or obeyed Yahweh's statutes and commands as David did.

In the sixth scene, Solomon woke up and realized that he had been dreaming.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Have a person tell about a great dream that they had. After the person has told the dream, they should finish by saying how they woke up and realised that it was a dream. Pay attention to the words that the person uses to focus your attention on the fact that it was a dream. Pause this audio here.

Solomon returned to Jerusalem and came and stood before the ark of the covenant. The ark of the covenant was in a tent near David's palace. Solomon made burnt offerings and peace or fellowship offerings. People completely burnt the burnt offering on the altar, but they only burnt some of the peace offering and ate the rest.

In the seventh scene, Solomon made a feast for all his servants. It is likely that this was in a different place and a short time later.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 3:1–15 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has seven scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Yahweh
  • King Solomon
  • Pharaoh, king of Egypt
  • Pharaoh's daughter
  • The people of Israel
  • King David
  • And King Solomon's servants

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

King Solomon made an alliance with Pharaoh, the king of Egypt, when he married Pharaoh's daughter. King Solomon brought Pharaoh's daughter to live in the City of David until he had finished building a palace for himself, Yahweh's temple, and the wall around Jerusalem.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Pharaoh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel happy; or
  • I feel safe and protected by my strong ally; or
  • Egypt rejoices.

Ask the person playing Pharaoh's daughter, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel so happy to be married to a strong king; or
  • I feel loved and cherished; or
  • I feel special and honoured.

Ask the person playing King Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel respected; or
  • I feel excited. I have many great projects; or
  • I am pleased with my relationship with Pharaoh.

Continue the drama.

In the second scene, the people were sacrificing at the local altars because Solomon had not yet built the temple to honor and worship Yahweh.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the people of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We feel sad because we are spread across the country and worship at different places; or
  • We feel worried that we might worship in the wrong way; or
  • We feel hopeful that the temple will be built soon.

Continue the drama.

In the third scene, King Solomon loved Yahweh and obeyed all the commands that his father gave him, but Solomon still made offerings at the local altars. Solomon offered many burnt offerings at Gibeon, the leading local altar. One day, Yahweh appeared to King Solomon at Gibeon in a dream. Yahweh asked Solomon what gift Solomon would like from Yahweh.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I love Solomon; or
  • I am pleased with Solomon; or
  • Solomon respects me and is careful to walk before me as his father did. I want to bless Solomon.

Continue the drama.

In the fourth scene, Solomon said that Yahweh had shown his father, David, constant friendship when Yahweh made Solomon king. Solomon told Yahweh how young and inexperienced Solomon was to rule Yahweh's people. Solomon asked for wisdom.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel terrified. Yahweh is awesome. How can I see Yahweh and live? or
  • I feel blessed and loved by Yahweh. I want Yahweh to give me the steadfast love that he gave my father; or
  • I am scared that I will rule unjustly. I am weak and small, and there are so many people! I am terrified that I might make Yahweh angry if I do not lead Yahweh's people well. I need Yahweh's help. I am desperate.

Continue the drama.

In the fifth scene, Yahweh was pleased that Solomon had asked for wisdom and not long life, riches, or the deaths of his enemies. So Yahweh gave Solomon wisdom and also riches and a great reputation. Yahweh also promised to give Solomon a long life if Solomon obeyed Yahweh as David had.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel very happy; or
  • I love Solomon's heart. Solomon cares for the things that I care for; or
  • I feel generous. I will bless Solomon and give him more than he has asked for.

Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am amazed by Yahweh's love; or
  • I feel overwhelmed by Yahweh's generosity; or
  • I feel strengthened and ready to rule.

Continue the drama.

In the sixth scene, Solomon woke up and realized that it was a dream. Solomon went back to Jerusalem and offered burnt offerings and peace offerings in front of the ark of the covenant of Yahweh.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Wow! What an amazing dream. Yahweh spoke to me as he spoke to our father Jacob in a dream; or
  • I just want to be in the presence of Yahweh. I will stand before Yahweh in Jerusalem; or
  • I am dedicated to Yahweh. I feel blessed and honored greatly.

Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel honoured; or
  • I feel respected; or
  • I am pleased by the sweet smell of Solomon's sacrifice.

Continue the drama.

In the seventh scene, Solomon made a feast for all his servants.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the people of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We are rejoicing; or
  • We feel joy; or
  • Our king has made a great feast, and we are celebrating in Jerusalem.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 3:1–15 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Solomon made a marriage alliance with Pharaoh, the king of Egypt. Pharaoh was a title for the king of the country of Egypt.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for Pharaoh. Look up Pharaoh in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Use the same word or phrase for king as you used in previous passages. For more information on king, refer to the Master Glossary.

Solomon brought Pharaoh's daughter to live in the City of David until he finished building his own house or palace and the house of Yahweh. Use the same phrase for "City of David" as you have used in previous passages. The house of Yahweh is a way to talk about the place on earth where Yahweh lived among his people. Here, "house of Yahweh" refers to the temple.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for the house of Yahweh. Look up "house of God" and "temple" in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

A palace is the place or house where the king lives. Discuss this question as a group: What would you call the house of a king in your language? Pause audio here.

Use the same word or phrase for Yahweh as you used in previous passages. For more information on Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary.

The people were sacrificing or making offerings at the high places because the temple to honor Yahweh had not yet been built. Use the same word or phrase for sacrifice as you used in previous passages. For more information on sacrifice, refer to the Master Glossary.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for high places. Remember that people often built a high place on a hill or mountain, but a high place is any place where people sacrifice to their gods locally. If you have already translated "high place" in another book of the Bible, use the same word here. Pause this audio here.

Solomon loved Yahweh and was walking in the statutes of David, Solomon's father, except that Solomon sacrificed and made sacrifices to smoke at high places. Making sacrifices "smoke" likely refers to burning incense, but it is not clear. Statutes usually refer to a law or rule about how to perform duties in the tabernacle or temple. In this case, statutes refer to the instructions or commands that David gave to Solomon. Use the same word or phrase for statutes as you used in previous passages. For more information on statutes, refer to the Master Glossary.

Solomon used to offer 1,000 burnt offerings at the altar at Gibeon. A burnt offering is a form of sacrifice that people bring to God. When a person brings God a burnt offering, the person kills an animal and burns this animal completely on an altar. An altar is a place where people make offerings or sacrifices to God. An altar is like a table or a platform that people make of earth or large stones. People placed gifts for God on this table. Often, this meant that people then burned the gift. The gift could be an animal, food items, or sweet-smelling spices.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for burnt offerings. Look up burnt offerings in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Use the same word or phrase for altar as you used in previous passages. For more information on altar, refer to the Master Glossary.

Yahweh appeared to Solomon in a dream at night, and God asked Solomon to ask for anything that Solomon wanted. Dreams happen when you are sleeping. In a dream, you experience some events that are not really happening, or you see things that are not really there. Yahweh can use dreams to communicate with people. Use the same word or phrase for God as you used in previous passages. For more information on God, refer to the Master Glossary.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for dream. Look up dream in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Solomon said that Yahweh had shown his servant David steadfast love because David walked before Yahweh in faithfulness, righteousness, and uprightness of heart. A servant usually refers to someone who works for someone else, but in this case, it means that David is under the authority of Yahweh and is a worshipper of Yahweh. Steadfast love refers to loyal love from Yahweh. Sometimes people translate steadfast love as kindness. Steadfast love is the kindness or help that a leader or very important person gives to someone under their authority. Use the same word or phrase for servant and steadfast love as you used in previous passages. For more information on servant and steadfast love, refer to the Master Glossary.

Walking in faithfulness is to be loyal and dependable. Walking in faithfulness is similar in meaning to steadfast love, but the focus is clearly on being someone that people can trust to do the right thing. A person who walks in righteousness behaves in the right way. The person who wants to obey Yahweh and wants to do things that please Yahweh is righteous, even though they may still make mistakes and unintentionally disobey Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss as a group what words or phrases you will use for faithfulness and righteousness. Look up righteousness in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated these words in another book of the Bible, use the same words that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

"Uprightness of heart" is a special way to talk about having a good and honest heart. It means trying to do what is right and being fair and truthful.

Stop here and discuss as a group how you will describe "uprightness of heart." If you use the word for heart in your translation, use the same word for heart that you have used previously, and refer to heart in the Master Glossary for more information. Pause this audio here.

Solomon said that Yahweh had been faithful to his father, David, by appointing Solomon to sit on the throne that David had sat on. To sit on a throne is a special way to talk about someone ruling over a kingdom. Translate the idea of sitting on a throne in the same way that you previously translated it.

Solomon asked Yahweh for wisdom to judge Yahweh's people. When someone judges, they look at people's actions and decide if what the person does is right or wrong, and therefore if the person is guilty or innocent of breaking a law. A judge will punish the person if the judge decides that the person is guilty of doing wrong. In this case, Solomon talks about judging Yahweh's people as the thing that a ruler does to rule or govern people.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for judge. Look up judge in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Yahweh was pleased because Solomon had asked for wisdom to know justice. Justice is when someone treats someone else fairly according to the law. When justice happens in an entire society, then everybody treats each other equally and fairly. In a just society, people in authority punish those who do wrong and protect people who are poor and weak. Use the same word for wisdom that you have used previously, and remember that wisdom is in the Master Glossary.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for justice. Look up justice in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Yahweh summarizes what Solomon asks for, and Yahweh says that he will give Solomon a wise and understanding heart.

Stop here and discuss as a group how you will say that Yahweh will give Solomon a wise and understanding heart. Pause this audio here.

Yahweh also promised to give Solomon a long life if Solomon walked in the ways of Yahweh and kept Yahweh's statutes and commandments. Use the same word or phrase for commandments as you used in previous passages. For more information on commandments, refer to the Master Glossary.

Solomon returned to Jerusalem and stood before the ark of the covenant of Yahweh. The ark refers to the rectangular chest of acacia wood that Yahweh asked Moses to make. People sometimes call this chest the ark of the covenant because the laws that Yahweh gave Moses are a strong promise, or covenant, between Yahweh and his people. This law testifies, or shows, that the Israelites are Yahweh's special people.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for "ark of the covenant." In a previous story in 1 Kings, you have used the term "ark of Adonai Yahweh," but the storyteller calls the ark the "ark of the covenant" here. Look up "ark of the covenant" in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this phrase in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Solomon offered burnt offerings and peace or fellowship offerings and made a feast for all his servants. People offered peace or fellowship offerings to establish and maintain good relations with Yahweh. The people offered some of the meat of the animal to Yahweh and ate the rest of it.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for peace or fellowship offerings. Look up "peace or fellowship offerings" in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 3:16–28
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 3:16–28 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 3:16–28 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 3:16–28 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the last story, Yahweh appeared to Solomon in a dream and gave Solomon more wisdom than anyone who had ever lived before Solomon or would live after him. In this story, Solomon uses his wisdom to justly judge a difficult case.

One day, two prostitutes, or women who slept with men for money, came and stood before King Solomon. People of Israel could come to the king and stand before him so that he could judge their cases and decide who was right and who was wrong. The king of Israel was the highest judge in the land. When there was a difficult case that no other judge could solve, the people brought that case to the king.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Discuss the most difficult case that there has ever been in your community. Who gave the final decision in that case? Pause this audio here.

The first woman spoke and said to King Solomon that she and the second woman lived in the same house. The first woman said that she had given birth to a child while she lived with the other woman in the same house. And it happened that on the third day after the first woman had given birth, the second woman also gave birth to a child. The two women were alone in the house and there was no visitor with them. It is likely that she meant that there were no male customers there, but the main point is that the women were alone with their children.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, how long does a woman stay indoors after she gives birth? How long is it before anyone can visit the woman? Who comes to visit first: male friends, female friends, or both together? Pause this audio here.

Both women gave birth to a son. The first woman told the king that the son of the second woman had died because his mother rolled onto him at night. The second woman then took the boy who was still alive from the first woman whilst the first woman slept, and put her dead son next to the first woman. The second woman took the first woman's boy who was alive and put him on her own breast.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do newborn babies sleep at night in your culture? What kind of things do these babies' mothers do to protect their babies at night? Pause this audio here.

The first woman said that when she woke up in the morning to give her baby milk, she was shocked to find her baby dead. But when the morning light became brighter and she looked at the dead boy more closely, she was shocked to see that it was not the son that she had given birth to.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Tell stories of people who have stolen small babies or people who have had their small babies stolen from them. Also, discuss how a mother can tell that a newborn baby is not hers? Pause audio here.

While the first woman was telling the king these things, the second woman interrupted and challenged the first woman's story. The second woman said that it was not true and that the living child was her child and the dead child was the first woman's child. But the first woman insisted that the living child was hers and the dead child belonged to the second woman. The two women argued like this in front of the king.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Tell stories of people who have argued and shouted at one another in front of others. Talk about how those people accused and blamed one another. Describe the scene, the emotions, the kind of language and volume they used, and how they spoke to each other. How did the people who watched respond? Pause this audio here.

King Solomon said that both women claimed to be the mother of the living child and said that the other woman was the mother of the dead child. Then the king asked his servants to bring him a sword, and they brought him a sword.

Stop here and look at a picture of a sword as a group. Pause this audio here.

Then the king told his servants to cut the living child into two parts and to give a part to each of the two women.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Talk about the strangest decision a judge has ever made in your community. Describe how the people who listened to the case reacted when they heard the decision. If you know of any cases where the decision was cruel or extremely harsh, focus on those stories. Pause this audio here.

The true mother of the living child felt very strong mercy for her child because she loved him. So she begged the king strongly not to have the servants kill the boy. The true mother told the king to tell his servants to give the living boy to the other woman. The true mother preferred to lose the child than to see the servants kill him. But the other woman shouted that people should cut the living child in two so that neither of the two women would have him.

Stop here and talk about a time when you felt such strong compassion or mercy for someone that you had to say something to others about the person. Pause this audio here.

Then the king responded and told his servants not to kill the boy. The king said that the first woman, the woman who stopped the king from killing the child, was the true mother of the child. The king told his servants to give that woman the living child.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Talk about the person that the people of your community consider to be the wisest person. Tell stories of some of the things that this person did and how what he did shows his wisdom. Pause this audio here.

All the people of Israel heard about the just decision that the king had made in this case. Because of this, all the people of Israel stood in awe, or greatly respected, the king. And the people recognised that Yahweh had given King Solomon the wisdom of God to judge rightly.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Talk about a leader who people in your community believed was close to divine beings because of things that he was able to do. How did the people treat this leader?

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 3:16–28 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has six scenes.

In the first scene: Two prostitutes came and stood before King Solomon because they wanted him to settle a case for them.

In the second scene: The first woman told the king how she and the second woman had given birth to sons in the same house, three days apart. The first woman said that the second woman had killed her baby by sleeping on him. The second woman had then exchanged her dead baby in the middle of the night with the first woman's living son. The first woman said that she had been shocked to find her baby dead when she woke up to feed him, but when she looked at him closely in the morning, she realised that it was not her baby.

In the third scene: The second woman interrupted and said the living child was hers, and the dead child belonged to the first woman. The first woman said that the living baby was hers and the dead baby was the second woman's. The two women argued like this before the king.

In the fourth scene: King Solomon told his servants to bring a sword because both women claimed the living child. When people arrived with the sword, King Solomon told his servants to cut the living baby in two and give each woman half.

In the fifth scene: The true mother, the first woman, of the child felt great love for her child. She begged King Solomon not to kill the baby but to give the baby to the other woman. But the second woman wanted people to kill the baby so that neither of the women would get him. King Solomon said that the first woman was the true mother and gave the baby to her.

In the sixth scene: All the people of Israel heard about the judgment that the king had passed in that case. The people of Israel greatly respected King Solomon, and they recognised that God had given Solomon wisdom to judge justly.

The characters in this passage are:

  • God
  • King Solomon
  • The first woman
  • The second woman
  • The living child
  • The dead child
  • The servants of King Solomon
  • And all the people of Israel

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, two women who were prostitutes came to King Solomon and stood before him. We do not know how long after the previous story this story took place.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Talk about how you connect events in a story without giving a definite time. For example, you may say "one day" or "sometime later." Decide together the best word or phrase to use at the beginning of this story. Pause this audio here.

You will notice that the storyteller does not name the king as King Solomon, although we know that Solomon is the king. You may use King Solomon's name in this story if it would make the story clearer. When people of Israel had disagreements, they went to a judge for a solution. If the case was too difficult, the people brought the disagreement to the king. The two prostitutes therefore stood before the king because they wanted the king to judge their case.

Stop here and discuss the question as a group: What do you call women who sleep with men for money? Make sure you choose a word that is not too offensive to say publicly. Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, the storyteller tells us the words that the women spoke directly.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Select a well-known story in which the characters in the story speak to one another. Have one member of your group tell the story to the rest of the group in two different ways. In the first way, he or she should tell the group the words that the characters in the story spoke directly. In the other, he or she should tell the words of the character in the story indirectly by speaking for the character themselves. Decide which of the two styles makes the story easier to understand in your language. Pause this audio here.

The first woman addressed the king with a common phrase that the people of Israel used to start a conversation with someone more important than they were. People would begin by humbly asking for permission to speak. To do this, people would say something like, "Please, my lord."

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Which common phrases do you use in your community to ask permission to speak when you begin a conversation with very important people? Pause this audio here.

The first woman told King Solomon that she and the second woman lived in the same house and that she had given birth to a child while she lived there. On the third day after the first woman gave birth, the second woman also gave birth to a child. The people of Israel counted the day of a child's birth as its first day.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do you count the days of the life of a newborn baby? For example, if a child is born in the afternoon, how old will you say the child is the following afternoon? Pause this audio here.

The first woman said that there was no stranger with them, which means that the two women were alone in the house. The woman was saying that no one had visited their house since the day that the babies were born.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Have a person tell a story that includes a conversation between two unnamed women. Pay attention to the language the person uses to show which woman is talking in the story as they tell it. Pause this audio here.

The first woman then said that in the night, the second woman rolled onto her son and killed him. The storyteller does not tell us which night this happened, whether it was the night that the second woman's baby was born or sometime after that. It is also not clear whether the second woman rolled onto her son accidentally or on purpose, but it was most likely an accident. The first woman then said that after the second woman had killed her own baby, the second woman got up at midnight or in the middle of the night and exchanged their babies. The woman does not give an exact time of the night when this happened.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Discuss the names you use in your language to describe different parts of the night. Pause audio here.

The first woman said that the second woman took the living boy from the first woman whilst the first woman slept and put her dead son next to the first woman. The second woman then put the living boy on her own breast.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What do you call the part of the body where you hold close the people that you love, for example, where mothers hold their babies and keep them close? Pause this audio here.

The first woman referred to herself as the female servant of the king to show respect to the king. The first woman said that when she woke up in the morning, to nurse or feed her baby, she was shocked to find that her son was dead. But when the first woman looked at the baby closely, in the morning, she realized it was not the child that she had given birth to. The first woman uses language that shows that she was shocked and surprised both when she found a dead baby by her side and when she later realised that the dead child was not her own child. The first woman speaks of two mornings, probably because the first time she woke up, it was early morning and still dark, and the second time it was fully morning.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Have someone tell a story about a time they stayed in a rural village where there is no electricity, and something scared or surprised them in the dark. The person should describe the time of the night or the morning when this thing happened. If you have words that divide the hours of the night and morning, the person should use them. Pay attention to the words and phrases you use when you talk about being surprised and scared. Pause this audio here.

In the third scene, the second woman interrupted the first woman and said that the living child was hers and the dead child belonged to the first woman. The women were shouting and talking at the same time. The women argued like this before the king. It is likely that the women did this for a while.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In step two, you discussed two people who argued and shouted at each other in front of other people. Which words and phrases did you use to describe the two people who were arguing? Pause this audio here.

In the fourth scene, King Solomon repeated what both women were claiming. It is likely that King Solomon was frustrated with the women for arguing. Each woman claimed to be the mother of the living child and said the other woman was the mother of the dead child. So King Solomon asked his servants to bring him a sword. People had different kinds of swords at this time. It is not clear which specific type of sword the king asked for. The king's servant brought the king a sword, and the king told the servants to cut the living child in two and give each woman half.

In the fifth scene, the woman who was the true mother of the living boy begged the king not to kill the boy. At this point, the storyteller does not say which woman is which. The true mother called the king her lord or master and pleaded with him to tell his servants not to kill the boy but to give him to the other woman. The true mother used forceful language when she told the king that he must not kill the boy. The storyteller says that the woman felt burning mercy or compassion for the baby. In other words, the woman felt very strong compassion, or love, and mercy for her son.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Remember your discussion from step two about a time when you felt strong love and mercy for someone. Pay attention to the words and phrases you used to describe this kind of feeling. How will you talk about this woman's strong compassion and mercy? Pause this audio here.

But the other woman was shouting that the servants should cut the living child in two so that neither woman would have the baby.

King Solomon responded and told his servants not to kill the boy. King Solomon also used the same kind of strong language that the true mother had used when she told him not to kill the child. King Solomon told his servants to give this woman the child because she was the real mother. King Solomon knew that she was the true mother because she showed great compassion for the child and wanted him to live. The storyteller uses a word that does not make it clear which woman the king gave the baby to, but it is likely the first woman.

In the sixth scene, all the people of Israel heard about the king's wise decision and how he had given a just judgment.

Stop here and discuss as a group: In step two, you talked about the wisest person in your community and the things that person did to show their wisdom. Now discuss how the people of your community reacted to the wise things that person did. Pause this audio here.

The people stood in awe of or respected King Solomon because they recognized that Solomon had the wisdom of God to judge rightly.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 3:16–28 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has six scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • God
  • King Solomon
  • The first woman
  • The second woman
  • The living child
  • The dead child
  • The servants of King Solomon
  • And all the people of Israel

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Two prostitutes came and stood before King Solomon so that he could choose who was right and who was wrong in their case.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • My people need me; or
  • I feel pressure to be a good and just king; or
  • I love Yahweh, and I want my kingdom to be pleasing to Yahweh.

Continue the drama.

The first woman told the king how she and the other woman had given birth to sons in the same house, three days apart. The first woman said that the second woman had rolled over onto her baby so that he died, and then had exchanged him in the middle of the night with her living son. The first woman said that she had been shocked to find her baby dead when she woke up to feed him, but when she looked at him closely in the morning, she realised that it was not her baby.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the first woman, the mother of the living child, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel sorrow and great pain in my heart; or
  • I feel hopeful that the king will see the truth. I want my son back; or
  • I feel angry with this woman and sorry for her dead child.

Ask the person playing the second woman, the mother of the dead child, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel angry; or
  • I hate this woman; or
  • This is my child. I will not let him go!

Continue the drama.

The second woman interrupted and said the living child was hers and that the dead child belonged to the first woman. But the first woman said that the living baby was hers and the dead baby was the second woman's. The two women argued like this before the king.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the servants of King Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We feel confused by these women. Who is telling the truth? or
  • We are curious to see what the king will decide; or
  • We feel frustrated by the women shouting, and we do not understand their argument.

Continue the drama.

King Solomon told his servants to bring him a sword. When the servants brought the sword, King Solomon told his servants to cut the living baby in two and give each woman half of the baby.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel challenged by this difficult case; or
  • I am committed to finding the truth and doing the right thing; or
  • I am clever enough to find a solution.

Ask the person playing the first woman, the mother of the living child, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel horrified. I am scared for my child. My heart will break with sorrow for my son; or
  • I feel even more sad and broken than before. I do not want my child to die; or
  • I am ready to do anything to save my son.

Continue the drama.

The true mother of the child felt great pity and sadness for her child, and she begged King Solomon to give the baby to the other woman. But the other woman wanted the servants to kill the baby so that neither of the women would get the baby. King Solomon said that the first woman was the true mother and gave the baby to her.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the mother of the dead child, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am happy with the king's decision. Kill the child. I do not care; or
  • I feel envious that her son is alive, and mine is dead; or
  • I am angry enough to kill the child myself.

Ask the person playing King Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am happy my plan has worked; or
  • I feel compassion for the mother of the child; or
  • I am angry with the woman who lies.

Ask the person playing the servants of the king, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Wow! I am amazed by the king's wisdom; or
  • I am so happy with the simple solution to a difficult case; or
  • We are crying tears of joy with the mother of the child.

Continue the drama.

All the people of Israel heard about the judgment that the king had passed in that case. The people of Israel greatly respected King Solomon, and they recognised that God had given Solomon wisdom to judge justly.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the people of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel amazed; or
  • I am impressed; or
  • I am afraid to lie to the king or do anything evil. God has given the king great wisdom.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 3:16–28 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Two prostitutes came and stood before the king. A prostitute is a woman who sleeps with men for money.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for prostitutes. It is important that you choose a word that you can use in a public setting. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Use the same word or phrase for king as you used in previous passages. For more information on king, refer to the Master Glossary.

The first woman asked to speak to her lord, the king. Use the same word or phrase for "lord" or "master" as you used in previous passages. For more information on lord, refer to the Master Glossary.

The woman called herself King Solomon's female servant. If you have used a term for "female servant" in the past, use the same word or phrase for female servant here. For more information on servant, refer to the Master Glossary.

When all Israel or the people of Israel heard the judgment that the king had given, they were in awe of or greatly respected the king. The people of Israel recognise that Solomon had wisdom from God to do justice. Use the same word or phrase for Israel, judgment, wisdom, and God as you used in previous passages. For more information on Israel, judge, wisdom and God, refer to the Master Glossary.

Justice is when someone treats someone else fairly according to the law. When justice happens in an entire society, then everybody treats each other equally and fairly. In a just society, people in authority, like King Solomon, punish those who do wrong and protect people who are poor and weak. Use the same word or phrase for justice as you used in previous passages. For more information on justice, refer to the Master Glossary.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 4:1–19
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 4:1–19 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 4:1–19 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 4:1–19 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the last story, King Solomon used the wisdom that Yahweh had given him to decide a difficult case. All the people of Israel were amazed and respected Solomon when they saw the wisdom that Yahweh had given him.

In this story, the storyteller tells us how King Solomon organised the leaders or officials who worked under Solomon.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about how your ancestors used to organise leaders and their helpers in your community. If you organise leadership in your community differently today, discuss the differences. Pause this audio here.

Later, in the days of the next king, King Solomon's son, the nation of Israel divided into two kingdoms, Judah and Israel. King Solomon ruled all Israel before it was divided into Judah and Israel.

The storyteller lists the leaders that King Solomon chose to work under him according to their roles. Azariah, the son of Zadok, was the priest. It is likely that Azariah was really the grandson of Zadok, who was also a priest. The people of Israel often refer to someone as the son of their grandfather if the grandfather was a well-known or important man. It is likely that Azariah was the high priest, the most important and senior priest in all of Israel.

Elihoreph and Ahijah, both sons of a man named Shisha, were the king's secretaries or scribes. Secretaries were educated men who worked closely with the king and wrote things down for the king. These men probably had other responsibilities and were very important men in Israel.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Describe what kinds of people in your government write things down for the leader and assist the leader closely. Pause this audio here.

Jehoshaphat, son of Ahilud, was the recorder. A recorder was likely the person who kept records of what the king had done and the decisions that he had made. In the story of King Solomon, Jehoshapat is called a royal historian.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Who is responsible for keeping records of the history of your leaders? Pause this audio here.

Benaiah, son of Jehoiada, was the leader or commander of the army of Israel. Zadok and Abiathar were both priests. They were not the high priest. It is possible that the storyteller did not include Zadok and Abiathar in his list and that someone else later added them. In an earlier story, King Solomon had sent Abiathar away from Jerusalem because Abiathar had supported Adonijah. Yahweh had also promised to remove the family of Eli from being priests, and Abiathar was of the family of Eli.

In the days of King David, and before kings started to rule Israel, leaders had divided Israel according to the tribal territories. But Solomon divided Israel into 12 regions or districts and chose leaders for each district. It is likely that Solomon divided Israel in this new way so that Solomon could unite the people and give them one national identity rather than the 12 tribal identities they had before. The 12 districts would also be responsible for supplying food for King Solomon and his household. Azariah, the son of Nathan, was the one who supervised the leaders of each region.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: List the regions that your government leaders manage or organize. How do you divide these areas? Discuss the tribal identity of the regions and compare it to the national identity and unity of your country. Pause audio here.

Zabud the priest, Nathan's son, was the king's friend. The king trusted Zabud, and Zabud probably gave the king advice.

Ahishar was in charge of King Solomon's property. It is likely that Ahishar was also in charge of the palace officials, the palace, and other properties that belonged to the king.

Adoniram, the son of Abda, managed the unpaid workmen or laborers. It was common for kings in those days to force people, usually from countries they had defeated, to build buildings and walls around cities and to do other construction projects without pay. King Solomon's forced labor may have included some people of Israel.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Talk about leaders who forced other people to work for them or for the community without pay. Why did the leader force these people to work without pay? Pause this audio here.

Solomon had 12 governors over the 12 districts that he had created. King Solomon likely chose 12 districts to match the number of months in a year. The 12 governors of the districts were responsible for providing the king and his household with food. Each governor had to provide food for one month of the year. These 12 districts were in the north of Israel, and they did not include the area where the tribe of Judah lived.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Discuss how your community provides for the greatest chief, king, or leader and his family. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller lists the names of the governors of these districts.

Ben-Hur was the governor of the district around Mount Ephraim. Ben-Deker was the governor of the district that included the towns of Makaz, Shaalbim, Beth Shemesh, and Elon Bethhanan.

Ben-Hesed was the governor in charge of the towns of Arubboth and Socoh and all the land around Hepher.

Ben-Abinadab, who had married one of King Solomon's daughters, Taphath, was the governor of the district of Naphoth Dor.

Baana, son of Ahilud, was the governor in charge of the district that included the towns of Taanach and Megiddo and the whole region around the town of Beth Shan. This area was next to the town of Zarethan, which was below the town of Jezreel. Baana's territory stretched from Beth Shan to Abel Meholah and to the far side of the town of Jokmeam.

Ben-Geber was the governor of the district that included the city of Ramoth in the region of Gilead. The descendants of Jair, son of Manasseh, had settled in the villages there. Ben-Geber was in charge of their villages as well as the territory of Argob in Bashan. The region of Argob had 60 large cities. Each city had walls and gates with bronze bars. It was common for people to build walls around cities for protection. People locked the gates of the cities using bronze or metal bars.

Stop here as a group and look at a picture of a walled city and the gates of the city. Discuss this question as a group: How do you protect your cities or towns from invaders? Pause this audio here.

Ahinadab, the son of Iddo, was the governor of the district of Mahanaim. Mahanaim was a city east of the Jordan River in Gilead

Ahimaaz was the governor of the district that was in the land of Naphtali. Ahimaaz had married another of King Solomon's daughters, Basemath.

Baana, the son of Hushai, was the governor of the district that was in the land of Asher and the town of Aloth.

Jehoshaphat, the son of Paruah, was the governor in charge of the district that was in the land of Issachar.

Shimei, the son of Ela, was the governor in charge of the district that was in the territory of Benjamin.

Geber, son of Uri, was the governor in charge of the district of Gilead. This was where Sihon, king of the Amorites, and Og, king of the people of Bashan, had once ruled.

Stop here and discuss as a group how your country divides up areas that governors or area leaders lead. If you can, list the areas and the people who govern each area. Pause this audio here.

There was one governor who supervised all these 12 governors.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 4:1–19 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has three scenes.

In the first scene: King Solomon was ruling over all of Israel.

In the second scene: The storyteller lists the names of the chief officials of King Solomon and what they did.

In the third scene: The storyteller lists the 12 governors that King Solomon had chosen to be in charge of supplying the palace with food, each for one month of the year.

The characters in this passage are:

  • King Solomon
  • Azariah, son of Zadok, the priest
  • Elihoreph and Ahijah, sons of Shisha, the secretaries
  • Jehoshaphat, son of Ahilud, the recorder
  • Benaiah, son of Jehoiada, the commander of the army of Israel
  • Zadok and Abiathar, priests
  • Azariah, son of Nathan, leader of the district governors
  • Zabud, son of Nathan, the king's friend or special adviser
  • Ahishar, the palace administrator
  • Adoniram, son of Abda, the leader of the forced labor
  • The 12 district governors: Ben-Hur, Ben-Deker, Ben-Hesed, Ben-Abinadab, Baana son of Ahilud, Ben-Geber, Ahinadab son of Iddo, Ahimaaz, Baana son of Hushai, Jehoshaphat son of Paruah, Shimei son of Ela, Geber son of Uri
  • Taphath, daughter of Solomon and wife of Ben-Abinadab
  • Basemath, daughter of Solomon and wife of Ahimaaz
  • Sihon, king of the Amorites
  • And Og, king of Bashan

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, the storyteller tells us that Solomon was ruling all Israel. The storyteller may be telling us this because soon after King Solomon died, people divided Israel into two kingdoms. But during this time, Solomon ruled over all of the 12 tribes of Israel. King Solomon chose and organized the leaders who worked for him.

Stop and talk with your group: Discuss when the people in your community got a new leader and the things that the leader changed in the organisation of your community. For example, the leader might have changed the people in important positions or the way he used or taxed the land. Pause the audio here.

In the second scene, the storyteller lists the men who were King Solomon's most important officials. The people of Israel usually used one word for many kinds of leaders. People stated the job of the leader to make it specific which type of leader he was.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Discuss the leaders who work in your country's government. Try to rank their leadership positions based on how important each person is. Pause this audio here.

Azariah, son of Zadok, was the priest. The people of Israel sometimes call both sons and grandsons by the word "sons." It is likely that Azariah was actually Zadok's grandson.
