The storyteller says that Azariah was the priest in such a way that it is likely that this means that Azariah was the high priest, or the most important priest in Israel.

Elihoreph and Ahijah, the sons of Shisha, were the king's secretaries. We do not know exactly what the secretaries did, but they did more than the people we call secretaries today. A king dealt with many matters and made many judgments that he needed to remember. It is likely that Elihoreph and Ahijah were educated men who worked closely with the king and wrote things down. Elihoreph and Ahijah may even have served as representatives of the king. Jehoshaphat, son of Ahilud, was the recorder. It is also not clear exactly what a recorder did. In the story of David, the storyteller called Jehoshaphat a royal historian. A royal historian is a person who studies and writes about kings and queens and their children. It is also possible that a recorder was the king's spokesman who reported to the king and told the king's decisions to the people.

Stop and do this activity as a group: Have a person appear before a king. The person should tell the king the problems his community is facing. As this is happening, someone who plays the king's secretary should write down all the things that the person says. The king should call his recorder or royal historian and ask him how the kings who came before him dealt with similar issues. Pay close attention to the words and phrases that you use for secretary and royal historian. Pause the audio here.

Benaiah, son of Jehoiada, was the commander of the army of Israel.

Zadok and Abiathar were priests. The storyteller has already told us that Azariah, the son of Zadok, was the priest. It is possible that the storyteller may not have listed Zadok and Abiathar originally, and someone added their names later. You will remember that Solomon had sent Abiathar away from Jerusalem because Abiathar had supported Adonijah. Yahweh had also promised that he would remove Eli's family from being priests, and Abiathar was part of that family.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Discuss the ranks your community has for priests. What do you call the lower-ranked priest? Pause this audio here.

Azariah, son of Nathan, supervised the leaders of each region. Solomon divided Israel into districts and chose leaders for each one. Nathan made sure that each leader did their work correctly.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about someone who oversees a group of leaders to make sure the leaders do their work correctly. Pay attention to the words and phrases you use to describe that person's work. Pause this audio here.

Zabud, the son of Nathan, a priest of Yahweh, was the king's friend. "King's friend" was the title of the king's trusted and personal advisor.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Talk about a leader of your country who had to deal with difficult things or who had great successes. Tell of the people who were his closest advisors. What phrase or word would you use to describe the position of such a close advisor? Pause this audio here.

Ahishar managed Solomon's property. Ahishar was probably in charge of the palace and the palace workers.

Adoniram, son of Abda, managed the forced labor, or the people whom Solomon made to work against their will and without pay. It is possible that Adoniram is the same man who was also in charge of King David's forced labor.

Stop here and remember your discussion from step two about a leader who forced other people to work for him without pay. What did you call that leader, and what did you call the people whom the leader forced to work? Pause this audio here.

In the third scene, the storyteller lists the men that King Solomon had chosen to be in charge of the 12 districts he had created. Each of these men had to provide food from their district to the king and his household for one month. The king's household includes his family, servants, palace officials, and his horses.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about someone whom a leader has chosen to be in charge of a region within that leader's control. Choose a word that communicates the idea of someone whom the leader chooses to stand in place of the leader. Pay attention to the words and phrases that you use to describe that person. Pause the audio here.

The storyteller lists the names of these men and the districts they were in charge of.

Ben-Hur, or the son of Hur, was in charge of the hilly country around Ephraim.

Ben-Deker, or the son of Deker, was in charge of the cities of Makaz, Shaalbim, Beth Shemesh, and Elon Bethhanan.

Ben-Hesed, or the son of Hesed, was in charge of the cities of Arubboth, Socoh, and the land around Hepher.

Ben-Abinadab, or the son of Abinadab, was in charge of Naphoth Dor. Ben-Abinadab married Taphath, Solomon's daughter.

Baana, son of Ahilud, was in charge of the cities of Taanach, Megiddo, Beth Shan, Zarethan, Jezreel, Abel Meholah, and Jokmeam.

Ben-Geber, or the son of Geber, was in charge of the city of Ramoth in Gilead. The tent villages of Jair, son of Manasseh, were his, as well as the region of Argob in Bashan with its 60 walled cities with gates of bronze. These were well-protected cities.

Ahinadab, the son of Iddo, was in charge of the city of Mahanaim. Mahanaim was east of the Jordan River.

Ahimaaz was in charge of the land that belonged to the tribe of Naphtali. Ahimaaz had married another one of Solomon's daughters, called Basemath.

Baana, the son of Hushai, was in charge of the land that belonged to the tribe of Asher and the city of Aloth. This is the second man named Baana that the storyteller mentioned in the list, but the two men had different fathers.

Jehoshaphat, the son of Paruah, was in charge of the land that belonged to the tribe of Issachar.

Shimei, the son of Ela, was in charge of the territory that belonged to the tribe of Benjamin.

Geber, the son of Uri, was in charge of the region of Gilead. This is where Sihon, king of the Amorites, and Og, king of Bashan, used to rule.

One governor supervised all 12 men who were in charge of the districts. This person was likely Azariah, son of Nathan, who supervised the leaders of each region.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Remind each other of the list you made in step two of the areas that governors in your land lead. Pay attention to the way that you listed the names of the regional leaders and the areas that they lead. Practice telling the list from this passage in the most natural way that you can. Pause audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 4:1–19 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has three scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • King Solomon
  • Azariah, son of Zadok, the priest
  • Elihoreph and Ahijah, sons of Shisha, the secretaries
  • Jehoshaphat, son of Ahilud, the recorder
  • Benaiah, son of Jehoiada, the commander of the army of Israel
  • Zadok and Abiathar, priests
  • Azariah, son of Nathan, leader of the district governors
  • Zabud, son of Nathan, the king's friend or special adviser
  • Ahishar, the palace administrator
  • Adoniram, son of Abda, the leader of the forced labor
  • The 12 district governors: Ben-Hur, Ben-Deker, Ben-Hesed, Ben-Abinadab, Baana son of Ahilud, Ben-Geber, Ahinadab son of Iddo, Ahimaaz, Baana son of Hushai, Jehoshaphat son of Paruah, Shimei son of Ela, Geber son of Uri
  • Taphath, daughter of Solomon and wife of Ben-Abinadab
  • Basemath, daughter of Solomon and wife of Ahimaaz
  • Sihon, king of the Amorites
  • And Og, king of Bashan

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

In the first scene, the storyteller tells us that Solomon ruled all Israel.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel happy and excited to make some changes in the leadership of Israel; or
  • I am strengthened by the changes I have made; or
  • I feel strongly supported by the whole of Israel and the leaders I have chosen.

Continue the drama.

In the second scene, the storyteller lists the men who were King Solomon's high or chief officials.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Azariah, son of Zadok, the priest, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel happy because Yahweh has chosen me as high priest of his people; or
  • I am blessed to be high priest in a time when a godly king like Solomon is ruling; or
  • I feel grateful for King Solomon's wise rule.

Ask the person playing Azariah son of Nathan, the leader of the district governors, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel a huge responsibility to make sure that we provide all of the king's food; or
  • I am honoured that the king gave me such a high position; or
  • I really want to do well.

Ask the person playing Adoniram, son of Abda, the leader of the forced labor, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • This is wonderful; or
  • I am ready to do the work the king wants done; or
  • I am excited about the many building projects the king has. I will make sure the forced labor works hard.

Ask the person playing Zabud, son of Nathan, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel very special and important; or
  • I feel loved and honored by the king more than any other man; or
  • It is a serious responsibility to advise the king wisely.

Continue the drama.

The storyteller lists the 12 governors that King Solomon had chosen to be in charge of supplying the palace with food, each for one month of the year.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel a great responsibility to finish the work that Yahweh has given me; or
  • I am excited to organise the nation in the best way possible. I have many changes to make; or
  • I feel happy that my changes are working well.

Ask the person playing Azariah, son of Nathan, leader of the regional governors, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am happy and honoured that King Solomon chose me to govern the 12 district leaders; or
  • I have a great responsibility to make sure that the governors provide for the king and his household; or
  • I feel happy that people throughout the country respect me.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 4:1–19 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

King Solomon ruled over Israel. The storyteller lists King Solomon's high officials. Use the same words or phrases for king and Israel as you used in previous passages. For more information on king and Israel, refer to the Master Glossary.

High officials refer to leaders. The people of Israel use the same word for many different kinds of leaders. These leaders helped King Solomon in his government and in leading the people.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for high officials in this story. You may use the same word you used earlier in 1 Kings 1:9 and 1 Kings 1:47 to talk about King David's government and military officials. Pause this audio here.

Elihoreph and Ahijah were secretaries. Jehoshaphat, son of Ahilud, was the recorder. We do not know exactly what secretaries did, but the language suggests a job that included writing and speaking. It is possible that Elihoreph and Ahijah wrote things down for the king and maybe even represented the king as his messengers. We also do not know exactly what a recorder did, but the language suggests that the recorder helped the king remember what he and previous kings had decided about similar issues in the past. The storyteller called Jehoshaphat a royal historian in the story of David. Therefore, a recorder was the person who recorded the words and actions of the king.

Stop here and discuss as a group what words or phrases you will use for "secretaries" and "recorder." Use words that maintain a difference between the two jobs. You may want to listen to your translation of 2 Samuel 8:16–17, if possible, and use the same words here that you used there. Pause this audio here.

Benaiah, son of Jehoiada, was over all the army and Zadok and Abiathar were priests. The army refers to the warriors or fighting men of Israel. Benaiah was their leader or commander. Priests were people who worked in the tabernacle of Yahweh. Yahweh had appointed priests to be between Yahweh and the people. Use the same words or phrases for army, commander, and priest as you used in previous passages. For more information on priest, refer to the Master Glossary.

Azariah, son of Nathan, was in charge of the regional or district officials, and Zabud, son of Nathan, a priest, was the king's friend. "King's friend" was the title of the king's closest and trusted personal advisor. This person would have given the king advice about how to attack enemies, how to rule his country, and how to solve arguments among his people.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for "king's friend." If you have already translated 2 Samuel 15:37, you may listen to how you translated the idea of "king's friend" there and translate it in the same way here. Pause this audio here.

Ahishar was in charge of the king's palace, or household, and Adoniram, son of Abda, was in charge of the forced labour. Forced labour refers to all the people whom the king made to work for him without payment. Usually, kings made their enemies, whom they had captured in war, work for them.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for forced labour. Pause this audio here.

Ahishar took care of the people in the king's house, or palace. Ahishar also made sure that the king had everything he needed. In other words, Ahishar took care of the entire household, or the people who lived in the king's house. Look up household in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

King Solomon had appointed 12 governors who provided food for the king and his household. Governors were the men who represented the king to their district, or region of the country, and represented their district to the king. Governors were in charge of their own district on behalf of the king. Governors would also be responsible to the king if things did not go well in their district.

Stop here and discuss as a group what words or phrases you will use for these governors and their districts, or regions. If you have already translated these words in another book of the Bible, use the same words that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Ben-Geber was the governor of Ramoth Gilead. The villages, or very small towns, of Jair son of Manasseh in Gilead were his, as well as the region of Argob in Bashan and its 60 large walled cities with bronze bars on the gate. Bronze is a strong metal that people made when they mixed copper and tin. Use the same word or phrase for bronze as you used in previous passages. For more information on bronze, refer to the Master Glossary.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 4:20–28
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 4:20–28 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 4:20–28 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 4:20–28 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous story, the storyteller told us about Solomon's officials and the districts that they managed for Solomon. In this story, the storyteller describes how good life was for the Israelites while Solomon was king.

At the beginning of this story, the storyteller tells us that the people of Judah and Israel were as numerous as the sand on the seashore. You will remember that the storyteller sometimes uses the phrase "Judah and Israel" to talk about all of the people of Israel. The phrase "as numerous as the sand on the seashore" means a very large number, so large that it is impossible to count. The storyteller might use this phrase to remind his hearers of the promise that God made to Abraham that Abraham would have so many descendants they would be impossible to count, like grains of sand at the seashore.

The storyteller tells us two times that Solomon ruled over the lands from the Euphrates River in the north, down to the border with the land of the Philistines and the border of Egypt in the south. The second time the storyteller tells us about the borders of Solomon's territory, he names two towns: Tiphsah, which was the city farthest north in Solomon's kingdom, and Gaza, which was at the border with the Philistines' land.

Stop here and look at a map of Israel during Solomon's time that shows Tiphsah and Gaza. Pause this audio here.

Solomon ruled over land that went beyond the borders of the 12 tribes of Israel. The leaders of other nearby countries brought Solomon tribute. Tribute is money or gifts that a weaker ruler gives to a stronger ruler. Sometimes, the weaker rulers give tribute to stop the stronger ruler from attacking them. Other times, the weaker rulers give tribute to get the stronger ruler to protect them. This is one way Solomon became rich. When the storyteller says that all the kingdoms in the area served Solomon, it means the other kings knew Solomon was more powerful than they were, and they followed Solomon's commands.

Many people worked for King Solomon. Some of these people came to Solomon's palace to eat meals that the king provided. In this story, the storyteller tells us how much food Solomon needed each day for Solomon to feed all of his officials. Solomon's cooks used fine flour and coarse-ground flour to make bread, and they also served a large amount of meat each day.

Stop here and discuss these questions: Describe a situation in your country where an employer provides food for his employees. Does the employer feed his employees every day? What kind of food does the employer provide? Pause this audio here.

While Solomon was alive, the people of Israel lived safely because Solomon kept peace with all the countries around them. The town of Dan was in the far north of Israel. The town of Beersheba was in the far south of Israel. "From Dan as far as Beersheba" is a way to say "From one end of the country to another" or "throughout the entire country."

During this time, every family had its own grapevines and fig trees. The Israelites grew grapes to make wine, which was a common drink back then. A fig tree is a tree that grows tasty fruit called figs.

Stop here and look at a picture of a grapevine and a fig tree. Look also at a picture of a person who holds a fig. Pause this audio here.

In the Bible, grapevines and fig trees represent peace and prosperity. If the people had grapevines and fig trees, then the people had enough food and drink and did not have to worry.

Stop here and discuss this question: In your culture, what kind of food and drink do people need to have in order to feel like they have all that they need? Pause this audio here.

Even though Solomon got along well with his neighbors, Solomon also built a strong army. One way we know Solomon's army was powerful is that Solomon had many horses, chariots, and horsemen.

Stop here and discuss these questions: In the area where you live, how do you determine how strong an army is? Does the army's strength depend on the number of soldiers, or the number of guns, vehicles, bombs, or other weapons? Pause this audio here.

Solomon kept his horses in stalls. A stall is an enclosure inside a building that people specially build to keep horses in. Keeping horses inside protects the horses from wild animals and thieves. If a horse was especially valuable, its owner was more likely to keep it in a stall.

Stop here and look at a picture of a horse in a stall. Pause this audio here.

Solomon received food from the governors of the districts of Israel. The storyteller tells us that Solomon had plenty of food not only for the people who worked for him, but also for his horses. The governors of the districts brought barley for the horses to eat and straw to put in the horses' stalls. Barley is a kind of grain, similar to wheat. People usually grind wheat to make flour, while barley is better to eat whole.

Stop here and look at a picture of a head of barley. Pause this audio here.

Straw comes from the stalks of both barley and wheat. People put straw into a horse's stall to help keep the horse warm and give it a soft place to sleep. Each month, the district governor brought these supplies to the places where the army kept the horses so that the people could care for the horses well.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 4:20–28 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has four scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller tells us how big, strong, and wealthy Solomon's kingdom is.

In the second scene: The storyteller tells us about how much food Solomon and his officials ate each day at Solomon's palace

In the third scene: The storyteller describes how safe and secure everyone in Israel was during Solomon's reign.

In the fourth scene: The storyteller tells us about Solomon's horses and chariots.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The people of Judah and Israel
  • King Solomon
  • The people of the countries that Solomon conquered
  • And the district governors of Israel

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In scene one, the storyteller begins by telling us that the people of Judah and Israel were as many as the sand on the seashore. The phrase "as many as the sand on the seashore" uses the exact words that Yahweh had said to Abraham about his descendants many years before. When the storyteller said this, he wanted to remind his audience that Yahweh had assured Abraham that Abraham would have so many descendants that no one would be able to count them. If you have already translated the book of Genesis, then you have already translated this phrase. It would be helpful to go back and listen to how you translated it.

Stop here and look at a picture of sand on a seashore. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and listen to Genesis chapter 22 if it is available in your language. After the angel of Yahweh stopped Abraham from sacrificing Isaac, the angel gave Abraham a message from Yahweh that included the promise that Abraham's descendants would be as many as the sand on the seashore. Pay close attention to how you translated this phrase. Pause this audio here.

During the reign of Solomon, the people of Judah and Israel were eating, drinking, and rejoicing. This means that they had plenty of food and drink and felt very happy. The way the storyteller says this shows that this was how the people felt throughout all 40 years of Solomon's reign.

Solomon ruled over a large territory. The northern border of Solomon's territory was the Euphrates River. The Euphrates River is the modern-day name of a large river that flows through the modern-day countries of Syria and Iraq. Some translations of this passage just say "the river," but we recommend that you include the name of the river so that your audience will clearly understand which river the storyteller is talking about.

Stop here and look at a map of Israel during the reign of Solomon that shows the Euphrates River. Pause this audio here.

Even Solomon, with all his power, did not conquer the territory of the Philistines. The Philistines were a people group that lived along the coast of the Mediterranean Sea between the territory of Israel and the territory of Egypt. The Israelites frequently had conflict with the Philistines.

Solomon also ruled in areas where non-Israelites lived. These non-Israelites had to pay tribute to Solomon. Tribute is a gift or payment that one group gives to another group to show respect or keep the peace. Usually, the group that pays tribute is less powerful than the group that receives the payment.

The non-Israelites paid tribute to Solomon in order to live peacefully within his territory. When the storyteller says that the conquered people served Solomon, it does not mean that they became servants in his household. Rather, it means that they were loyal to Solomon and did whatever Solomon told them to do.

Stop here and discuss this question: In your language, what words or phrases do you have to describe a situation where one group is loyal to a leader and does whatever he or she tells them to do? Pause this audio here.

In scene two, the storyteller describes the large amount of food that Solomon's family, officials, and guests ate every day at Solomon's palace. When you listened to three different versions of this passage, you probably heard different words to describe the amount of fine flour and coarse flour, or meal, that people used every day in Solomon's palace. You might have heard the word "cors" to describe the amount of flour. This word sounds like the word in the original language, but Bible scholars do not agree on how big a cor was. You also might have heard the word "bushel," which is a modern-day measurement for grain. You also might have heard words like "kilograms" or "liters," which are modern-day measurements. We recommend you use a word here that your listeners will recognize. The correct amounts in modern-day measurements are around 9,000 liters of fine flour and 18,000 liters of coarse flour.

Stop here and discuss these questions: In your culture, how do you measure grain? How familiar are your people with the words that people used to measure grain? If you do not have common words to measure grain, how will you translate the word "cor"? Pause this audio here.

When you listened to this scene, you probably heard many different words for animals. The first two kinds of animals are both cows, but they ate different food. The first group ate grain to help them get big and fat. The second group lived in fields and ate grass. Most people thought the meat from the grain-fed cows tasted better than the meat from the grass-fed cows. You might hear the storyteller call them "10 stall-fed cows" and "20 grass-fed cows," or "10 fattened oxen" and "20 oxen from the pastures." We suggest you translate these phrases so it is clear they are the same kind of animal, but they ate different food.

Stop here and discuss how you will translate these two phrases. Pause this audio here.

The Israelites ate both sheep and goats. The word the storyteller used here can mean either animal. Deer, gazelles, and roebucks are similar kinds of animals that the Israelites hunted. The first kind of deer is a male deer that could weigh as much as 140 kilograms. Gazelles and roebucks were smaller.

Stop here and look at pictures of a deer, a gazelle, and a roebuck. Pause this audio here.

The last animal that the storyteller mentions is a type of bird. Bible scholars do not agree on what kind of bird this was. The Israelites raised many different types of birds for food. When you listened to three different versions of this passage, you might have heard the storyteller call this kind of bird "fattened fowl," "choice fowl," "fattened geese," or "choice poultry." All of these phrases mean that people fed the birds well to make them fat and good to eat.

Stop here and discuss these questions: What kind of birds do your people raise for food? What words do you have to describe birds that people raise for food? Pause this audio here.

In scene three, the storyteller tells us again about the borders of Solomon's land. This time, the storyteller includes the names of two towns on the borders. These towns are Tiphsah in the north and Gaza in the south.

The storyteller also says that Solomon had peace all around him. This means Solomon got along with all his neighbors and did not have to fight any battles. The Israelites believed Yahweh gave them this peaceful time as a blessing. From Dan to Beersheba, the Israelites were safe and secure. Dan was the name of a city in the far north of Israel, where members of the tribe of Dan lived. Beersheba was the name of a town in the far south of Israel, where members of the tribe of Simeon lived. "From Dan to Beersheba" is a way to say "throughout the land of Israel" or "in the entire land of Israel."

In scene four, the storyteller tells us about Solomon's horses, chariots, and horsemen. When you listened to three different versions of this story, you might have heard some say "Solomon had 40,000 stalls for his chariot horses," and others might have said "Solomon had 4,000 stalls for his chariot horses." We recommend that you use the number 40,000.

In this part of the story, you also might have heard that "Solomon had 12,000 horses" or that "Solomon had 12,000 horsemen." A horseman is someone who rides or takes care of horses. Bible scholars do not agree on which one the storyteller meant here, and either translation choice is acceptable.

A chariot is a kind of cart that soldiers would hitch to horses and use in battle. At least two soldiers rode in the chariot. One of these soldiers controlled the horses while the other soldier could shoot arrows at enemy soldiers, throw objects at them, or stab them with his sword. Horses are much faster than humans, so the soldiers in the chariot could move much faster than the enemy soldiers around them. The chariot also provided some protection for the soldiers inside.

Stop here and look at a picture of a chariot. Pause this audio here.

Solomon's 12 district governors did a good job of providing food and other supplies for the king. These deputies not only provided food for King Solomon, his family, and all of Solomon's officials, but the deputies also provided food and straw for Solomon's horses.

In this part of the story, the storyteller uses two different words for Solomon's horses. The first word is the normal word for horses. The second word means special horses that people used for a certain job. Bible experts do not agree on what the job was. But it seems like the horses needed to be fast, so most Bibles call them very fast horses.

Stop here and discuss how you will describe this group of horses. Pause this audio here.

Solomon put his horses in the chariot cities around Israel. The district governors had to make sure the supplies for the horses reached the places where the horses were. The storyteller says that the governors did a very good job of this and did not leave out anything. The governors brought the amount they needed to bring. This shows that Solomon's government worked effectively and took good care of both the people and the horses.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 4:20–28 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has four scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The people of Judah and Israel
  • King Solomon
  • The people of the countries that Solomon conquered
  • And the district governors of Israel

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

The people of Judah and Israel were as many as the sand on the seashore, and the people ate, drank, and rejoiced. Solomon ruled over all the kingdoms from the Euphrates River in the north to the land of the Philistines and the border of Egypt in the south. These countries brought tribute and served Solomon all the days of his life.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • My people have grown numerous and prosperous! The promises God gave to our ancestor Abraham have come true! or
  • It is so rare for my people to be at peace. I will work hard to preserve this time of strength and prosperity; or
  • I am grateful to Yahweh for all he has done for me and my people.

Ask the people playing the people of Judah and Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" They might answer things like:

  • We are so grateful for King Solomon because he has made our country strong and safe; or
  • We feel relieved that our people no longer have to worry about war or hunger; or
  • We love Yahweh. Yahweh has blessed us so much. Yahweh has been faithful to his promises.

Ask the people playing the rulers of the conquered lands, "What are you feeling or thinking?" They might answer things like:

  • I do not like to pay tribute to Solomon, but it is smarter to bring tribute than to wage war; or
  • Even though we are subject to Solomon, our people have survived, and Solomon treats us fairly well; or
  • Maybe if we make an alliance with Solomon, we can gain his favor and get some of Solomon's wealth.

Continue the drama.

Every day, Solomon's palace used 5,000 liters of fine flour and 10,000 liters of coarse flour, 10 fattened cattle and 20 pasture-fed cattle, 100 sheep and goats, as well as deer, gazelles, roebucks, and choice fowl.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We have so many people to feed. I hope we do not experience a shortage or famine; or
  • I am grateful that I am able to provide such good food for my people; or
  • Yahweh has truly blessed me and this nation.

Ask the people playing the people of Judah and Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" They might answer things like:

  • We feel proud that we live in such a great nation with so much good food; or
  • We feel pleased that God has given us such a good king; or
  • We hope that our nation will continue to be this prosperous and successful.

Continue the drama.

Solomon ruled over all the kingdoms west of the Euphrates River, from Tiphsah to Gaza, and there was peace with all the neighboring countries. During Solomon's lifetime, Judah and Israel lived in safety throughout the whole country from Dan to Beersheba, and each family had its own grapevines and fig trees.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I must continue to be strong enough to keep this nation safe and feed them well; or
  • I am glad that we are at peace with all our neighbors; or
  • I feel awe that Yahweh's promises to our ancestors have come true during my lifetime.

Ask the people playing the people of Judah and Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" They might answer things like:

  • Our ancestors would be so happy to know that our country is rich and safe; or
  • We are so glad that we do not have to worry about having enough food and wine! or
  • We hope that this prosperity and peace will continue for our children and grandchildren; or
  • We feel proud that our nation is such a great nation.

Continue the drama.

Solomon had 40,000 stalls of horses for his chariots, and 12,000 horsemen. The district governors faithfully provided food for King Solomon and his officials. Each governor made sure nothing was lacking during the month assigned to him. Each governor also brought to the proper places the amount they needed to bring of barley and straw for the horses and chariot horses.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel proud that our army is so large and strong; or
  • If any of these nations rebel against me, I feel confident that our army is strong enough to crush them; or
  • I am grateful that all my district governors are faithful and do their jobs well.

Ask the people playing the district governors, "What are you feeling or thinking?" They might answer things like:

  • It is a lot of work to feed everyone at the palace and all the chariot horses; or
  • I am happy to do my part to keep our country safe and secure; or
  • I am proud to serve such a great king as Solomon.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 4:20–28 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

The people of Judah and Israel were as many as the sand on the seashore. The people ate, drank, and rejoiced. Solomon ruled over all the kingdoms from the Euphrates River in the north to the land of the Philistines and the border of Egypt in the south. Be sure to translate Judah, Israel, kingdom, Euphrates River, and Philistines the same way that you have in previous passages. For more information on "Israel," "Judah," and "kingdom," refer to the Master Glossary.

If necessary, stop here and listen again to Genesis chapter 2. Pay attention to the name you used for the fourth river—this is the Euphrates River. Pause this audio here.

If necessary, stop here and listen again to 2 Samuel 23:16, where you translated the word for "Philistines." Pause this audio here.

These countries brought tribute and served Solomon all the days of his life.

Stop here and decide how you will translate the word "tribute." If necessary, listen again to 2 Samuel 8:6 to remember how you translated the word "tribute." Pause this audio here.

Every day, Solomon's palace used about 9,000 liters of fine flour and 18,000 liters of coarse flour, 10 fattened cattle and 20 pasture-fed cattle, 100 sheep and goats, as well as deer, gazelles, roebucks and choice fowl.

Stop here and decide how you will translate the names of these animals: fattened cattle, pasture-fed cattle, sheep and goats, deer, gazelles, roebucks, and fowl. Look at pictures again of deer, gazelle, and roebucks, if needed. Pause this audio here.

Solomon ruled over all the kingdoms west of the Euphrates River, from Tiphsah to Gaza, and Judah and Israel had peace with all the neighboring countries. Translate peace in the same way you did in previous passages in 1 Kings, and refer to the Master Glossary for more information about peace.

If necessary, stop here and listen to 1 Samuel 6:17 and be sure to translate the name "Gaza" the same way you did there. Pause this audio here.

During Solomon's lifetime, Judah and Israel lived in safety throughout the whole country from Dan to Beersheba, and each family had its own grapevines and its own fig trees.

If necessary, stop here and listen to your translation of 2 Samuel 24:15, and be sure to translate Dan and Beersheba the same way you did there. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and look at pictures of grapevines and fig trees again as a group. What will you call these in your translation? Pause this audio here.

Solomon had 40,000 stalls of horses for his chariots, and 12,000 horsemen. Be sure to translate chariot the same way that you did in previous passages.

The district governors faithfully provided food for King Solomon and his officials. Each governor made sure nothing was lacking during the month assigned to him. The governors also brought to the proper places their quotas of barley and straw for the horses and swift steeds. Be sure to translate "governor" the same way you did in previous passages.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 4:29–34
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 4:29–34 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 4:29–34 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 4:29–34 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

This section of 1 Kings describes how wise Solomon was. The ancient people believed that wisdom came from a god. But the Israelites knew that God gives people true wisdom. And God is willing to give wisdom to those who ask for it. We remember that when Solomon prayed to God, Solomon did not ask for long life, wealth, or that God would punish Solomon's enemies. Instead, Solomon asked God to give Solomon a mind that could understand the situation, so that Solomon could rule God's people in a wise and just way. Then God answered Solomon's prayer and gave Solomon wisdom. In this story, the storyteller reminds us that God gave Solomon wisdom. And the storyteller describes the greatness of Solomon's wisdom.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How does your culture treat a person who is very wise? Pause this audio here.

God gave Solomon a lot of wisdom and understanding. The storyteller says that Solomon knew as many things as grains of sand on a seashore! That is a lot of things to know! No one could understand or count all of Solomon's knowledge.

Stop here and look at a picture of a seashore as a group. Pause this audio here.

Then the storyteller gives us some people of the East and Egypt as examples of people of wisdom. The "people of the East" refers to the tribes that lived east and southeast of the Jordan River down to the Dead Sea.

Stop here as a group and look at a map of Israel and the areas that surround Israel. Pause this audio here.

People back then considered these nations to be very wise and often asked their wise men for guidance and answers. But Solomon was wiser than all the wise people in those two nations.

The storyteller mentioned four men whom the people of Israel would know. Solomon is wiser than Ethan the Ezrahite and the sons of Mahol, who were Heman, Calcol, and Darda.

Solomon wrote 3,000 proverbs and 1,005 songs. Both proverbs and songs often contained wise sayings and teachings. Many people believed that Solomon wrote the books of Proverbs, Ecclesiastes, and Song of Solomon, which are also in the Bible. People call these books wisdom writings.

[Solomon knew about many different subjects. Solomon knew about trees and plants, such as the Lebanon cedar trees and the hyssop plant.]{.mark} Cedar trees are tall and valuable trees that grow all over the country of Lebanon. At this time, people used cedars to build temples and palaces because cedar wood was very strong and beautiful. On the other hand, hyssop is a plant that commonly grows between the stones or bricks in walls. Solomon has a wide knowledge of trees and plants, from the biggest to the smallest.

Stop here and look at a picture of cedar trees and hyssop as a group. Pause this audio here.

Solomon also knew about the different types of animals, birds, reptiles, and fish. Solomon was familiar with the animals that live on the farm, wild animals, animals that fly in the air, animals that crawl both in water and on land, and animals that live in water.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, what kinds of animals live in your area? How do you group them together? Pause this audio here.

In this passage, the kings from other lands send people to learn from Solomon. As people listen to Solomon, Solomon influences the people and kings in nearby nations, which makes Solomon even more powerful.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, how do your leaders respond to a leader in their area who is very wise?

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 4:29–34 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has four scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller says that God gave Solomon great wisdom.

In the second scene: The storyteller compares Solomon's wisdom with other nations and other wise men.

In the third scene: The storyteller shows Solomon's knowledge of different topics.

In the fourth scene: The storyteller shows how people from all over the world come to hear Solomon's wisdom.

The characters in this passage are:

  • God
  • Solomon
  • Ethan, the Ezrahite
  • Heman, Calcol, and Darda, the sons of Mahol
  • And people who came from other nations

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, the storyteller identifies God as the one who gave wisdom, deep understanding, and a wide heart to Solomon. Although wisdom, understanding about many things, and a wide heart or knowledge may have similar meanings, these three words emphasize that Solomon is very wise.

The storyteller uses special language in order to describe the greatness of Solomon's wisdom. For example, the storyteller uses the image of the heart to say that Solomon had a wide knowledge of how things worked. In the past, people used the word for heart to describe the way that people think, feel, and make decisions. A person with a wide heart describes a person who knows things well.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your language, what words will you use for wisdom, knowledge, and a wide understanding or heart? Pause this audio here.

In fact, the storyteller compares the wisdom of Solomon to the sand on the seashore. Just as no one can count all the sand on the seashore, no one can count or measure the things that Solomon knows. Of course, this is an exaggeration. No human being can possibly know everything. But the storyteller wants to point out that Solomon was truly the wisest person at that time. A seashore is a place where the sea meets the land. The seashore is usually full of sand, stones, or rocks. Solomon knows about as many things as there are pieces of sand on a seashore, which no one can ever count. The storyteller means that no one can measure how much Solomon knows!

Stop here as a group and look at a picture of a seashore and sand. Discuss this question as a group: How will you describe a thing, or a group of things, that is so big that no one can measure it? Remember that the storyteller uses the sand on the seashore to show how great Solomon's wisdom is. In the previous passage, the storyteller used sand on the seashore to show how many descendants Abraham would have. Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, the storyteller continues to describe the wisdom of Solomon. The storyteller compares Solomon's wisdom with the wisdom of other nations and other wise people. The storyteller particularly mentions the wisdom of the people of the East, the nation of Egypt. Solomon even describes wise men from Israel. Solomon's wisdom is greater than Ethan the Ezrahite from a place called Ezrah, and the sons of Mahol: Heman, Calcol, and Darda. The storyteller uses these names to show that Solomon is wiser than the wise men in the Israelites' own country. The storyteller mentions the names of people whom the Israelites know. But the wisdom of Solomon is greater than these nations and people.

In the third scene, the storyteller gives specific examples of Solomon's wisdom and knowledge. The storyteller mentions that Solomon wrote 3,000 wise sayings and 1,005 songs. This shows that Solomon knows how to speak in wise and beautiful ways. Then the storyteller mentions that Solomon also knows about plants and trees. Solomon talks about cedar trees and hyssop. People know that cedar trees are big trees and hyssop plants are small plants. This shows that Solomon knows a lot about plants, from big to small plants. Finally, the storyteller mentions that Solomon also knows about all kinds of land animals, birds, reptiles, and fish. Reptiles are animals that can live in water and on land. This shows that Solomon knows a lot about animals, too. Solomon knows about many things in the world. Solomon knows how to speak well and compose songs. Solomon also knows a lot about plants and animals.

Stop here and discuss as a group how you will talk about the animals so that it is clear that Solomon knows about every kind of animal. Pause this audio here.

In the fourth scene, the storyteller shows that the nations around Israel heard about Solomon's great wisdom. So the kings of those nations sent people to come to Solomon to listen to Solomon speak wisely.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 4:29–34 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has four scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • God
  • Solomon
  • Ethan, the Ezrahite
  • Heman, Calcol, and Darda, the sons of Mahol
  • And people who came from other nations

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

In the first scene, the storyteller introduces God as the source of Solomon's great wisdom.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing God, "What are you feeling or thinking when you give Solomon great wisdom?" The person might answer things like:

  • I think I can trust Solomon with such a gift; or
  • I feel hopeful that Solomon will use the wisdom I gave him to lead the Israelites responsibly.

Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking when you receive the gift of wisdom from God?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel humbled because God gave me this gift of wisdom so I can properly lead the people of God.

Continue the drama.

In the second scene, the storyteller compares the wisdom of Solomon with that of other people. The storyteller talks about the people of the East, the people of Egypt, Ethan the Ezrahite, Heman, Calcol, and Darda. The wisdom of Solomon is far greater than these people.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking when you find out that you are the wisest person among all nations?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel proud that I am the wisest person among all nations; or
  • I think I can help other nations with my wisdom.

Ask the person playing the people of the East, the people of Egypt, Ethan the Ezrahite, Heman, Calcol, and Darda, "What are you feeling or thinking when you learn that there is a person who is wiser than you?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel amazed that a person can have such great wisdom; or
  • I think I need to meet that person and honor him; or
  • I am jealous of the wise person!

Continue the drama.

In the third scene, the storyteller gives actual examples of Solomon's great wisdom. Then the storyteller says that Solomon wrote a lot of proverbs and songs. Then the storyteller adds that Solomon knows a lot about plants and animals.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking because you know a lot about the different kinds of plants and animals?" The person might answer things like:

  • I think I can help more people as they farm and raise farm animals if I have such a wide understanding of plants and animals; or
  • I feel very proud because I have a wide knowledge of plants and animals compared to other people.

Continue the drama.

In the fourth scene, the storyteller mentions the effect of Solomon's great wisdom on other people. People from different kingdoms and nations will gather around Solomon. These people will stay and listen to Solomon.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking as people surround you, admire you, and want to listen to you?" The person might answer things like:

  • I think I need to praise God because he gave me great wisdom; or
  • I feel I have [become a great king, as I should.]{.mark}

Ask the person playing the people of the other nations, "What are you feeling or thinking when you come to Solomon?" The person might answer things like:

  • I think it is a rare opportunity to meet the wisest person on earth; or
  • I feel afraid to meet Solomon.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 4:29–34 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

In this story, the storyteller describes the wisdom that Solomon received from God. For more information on God, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for God as you used in previous passages.

The storyteller mentioned the word wisdom many times. For more information on wisdom, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word for wisdom as you used in previous passages.

God also gave Solomon deep knowledge or understanding and a wide heart. Wisdom, deep knowledge, and a wide or understanding heart all mean similar things. Someone understands well when they have knowledge about something and can respond appropriately because they know what to do. That person is able to make decisions well. We remember the prayer of Solomon from a past section in this book when Solomon prayed to Yahweh for wisdom. Back then, Solomon asked Yahweh for wisdom so that Solomon could understand and decide between what is right and what is wrong.

Stop here and decide as a group what words or phrases you will use for Solomon's deep knowledge and wide or understanding heart. If you use the word for heart in your translation, refer to the Master Glossary for more information about heart, and use the same word for heart that you have used in previous passages. Pause this audio here.

Solomon's wisdom was greater than the men from the East. East is the direction that the sun rises.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for East. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Solomon becomes very famous to the nations around Israel. A nation is a group of people who live together in a location. A nation also has a ruler that governs the people.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for nation. Look up "nation" in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Because of Solomon's wisdom, Solomon is able to write proverbs and songs. A proverb is a short, wise saying that is easy to remember.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for proverb. Look up "proverb" in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Solomon also knew a lot about plants and trees. This section talks about trees and plants, like cedar trees and hyssops. Cedar is a type of tree that grows in Lebanon. Hyssop is a type of plant that grows in Israel. In Bible times, people used hyssop for rituals and ceremonial cleansing. Use the same words or phrases throughout the book of Kings.

Stop here and look at a picture of cedar trees and hyssop as a group. Pause this audio here.

Finally, the storyteller mentioned reptiles as one of the groups of animals that Solomon knew about. Reptiles are animals that have dry skin with scales or bony plates. These animals usually lay eggs. Reptiles can also live in water and on land. Some kinds of reptiles are snakes, lizards, and turtles.

Stop here and look at a picture of reptiles as a group. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss as a group what words or phrases you will use for land animals, birds, reptiles, and fish. If you have already translated these words in another book of the Bible, use the same words that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller mentions the kings of the world. A king is someone who rules over a city or territory. For more information on kings, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for king as you used in previous passages.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 5:1–12
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 5:1–12 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 5:1–12 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 5:1–12 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

This story is also in 2 Chronicles 2:1–18. You can listen to your translation of 2 Chronicles 2:1–18 if you have already translated it. Since this story is different in 2 Chronicles, take note of each story's differences. Sometimes the order of the scenes differs. Notice the details carefully.

Pause this audio here.

Solomon is the new king of Israel. We also remember from the previous section that Solomon received great wisdom from God. In this section, Solomon is about to start a very important project. Solomon will lead the Israelites to build the temple of Yahweh.

In the past, King Hiram and King David were on good terms. King Hiram sent King David cedar logs and workers in order to build David's palace. This time, King Hiram of Tyre hears that the people of Israel have anointed Solomon as their king. Then King Hiram sends servants to represent Hiram before King Solomon.

Tyre is a city in Phoenicia, which is in the present-day country of Lebanon. The people of Tyre are rich because Tyre is located beside the sea, where people come in their ships to trade valuable things. The people of Tyre trade cedar logs from the forests of Lebanon. Cedar logs have good qualities for when people make houses and other buildings.

Stop here and look at a map of the countries near the Mediterranean Sea during this time. Then locate Israel, the forests of Lebanon, and Phoenicia as a group. Pause this audio here.

King Hiram wants to honor the friendship he had with King David and to continue that friendship with Solomon, the new king of Israel. Solomon also wants to continue the close friendship with King Hiram of Tyre. Then Solomon sends the servants of King Hiram back to Tyre with a message to King Hiram.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, how do you continue good relationships with other tribes or nations? For example, when a person becomes the new leader of a country, how do your leaders continue to keep peace between your countries? How do they show that the countries are still friends? Pause this audio here.

Solomon tells King Hiram that David wanted to build a temple for Yahweh while David ruled in Israel. But Yahweh did not allow David to build a temple for Yahweh because of the wars that David fought. David's mission was to defeat all the enemy nations and bring peace to Israel. Since David will not be able to build a temple for Yahweh, Yahweh told David that David's son, who will be the next king, will build the temple for Yahweh. Now, Solomon is the new king of Israel. Solomon is the one who will build the temple. So Solomon wants King Hiram's help to build the temple of Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Why do you think Yahweh wanted to wait until Israel was at peace before Yahweh allowed his kings to build a temple to him? Pause this audio here.

Solomon specifically asks King Hiram to provide cedar logs and wood cutters. We remember from the previous section that Solomon knows about cedar logs from the Lebanon mountains. But Tyre is not only famous for the cedar logs. The people there are also highly skilled woodworkers, especially the people from the city of Sidon in Phoenicia, whom people called Sidonians. In fact, while David was king, King Hiram of Tyre sent logs and woodworkers from Lebanon to help build a palace for David. That is why Solomon asks for wood from King Hiram. In return, Solomon promises King Hiram that Solomon will pay the wages of the woodworkers. Solomon also tells King Hiram that Solomon will send his servants to help with the work. These servants are not the kind of servants who work in the house, but people whom Solomon forces to work for him for a certain amount of time in order to complete some sort of building project.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What do your people grow or produce? What products do you grow in your land or trade with other countries? What skills do other people say that people in your area are good at? Pause this audio here.

King Hiram receives Solomon's message and praises Yahweh. King Hiram promises Solomon to give the cedar logs that Solomon asked for. King Hiram will also give Solomon cypress wood from cypress trees in addition to cedar wood.

Stop here and look at a picture of cedar trees and cypress trees as a group. Pause this audio here.

Then King Hiram explains his plans about how to bring the logs to Solomon. King Hiram will command Hiram's workers to get the logs from the Lebanon mountain range and bring them to the Mediterranean Sea. Then the workers will tie the logs together and make them into rafts. It will be easier for people to float those logs through the sea rather than to pull those logs through the land to Israel. Hiram's workers will tie the logs together to make rafts. Then boats will pull the rafts. When the workers arrive at the location that Solomon chooses, the workers will take the rafts apart. Solomon's workers can then transport those logs to the place where people will build the temple.

Stop here as a group and look at the map to find the forests of Lebanon, Phoenicia, Israel, and the Mediterranean Sea. Pause this audio here.

King Hiram agrees to do all these things for Solomon if Solomon will provide food for King Hiram and the people who live with King Hiram. Although the location of Tyre has many good trees, the land of Tyre was not good for people to grow crops. In order to solve this problem, the people of Tyre traded their logs for food or crops. Solomon follows the suggestion of Hiram. Hiram sends the logs, and then Solomon sends the food. Every year, Solomon gives 2,000 tons of wheat and 400,000 liters of pure olive oil. Wheat and olive oil are two main foods during this time.

Solomon's relationship with King Hiram shows that Yahweh really has given Solomon wisdom. We remember that Solomon had asked Yahweh for wisdom in order to lead the people in the right and just way. Because of Solomon's wisdom, King Solomon and King Hiram make a treaty. A treaty is a formal agreement between countries to keep peace and good relationships.

The storyteller shows us that one of the effects of King Solomon's good leadership is to make peace and treaties with other nations.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 5:1–12 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has four scenes.

In the first scene: King Hiram hears that Solomon is the new king of Israel. King Hiram then sends representatives to Solomon after he hears the news that the people anointed Solomon as the new king of Israel.

In the second scene: Solomon sends a message back to King Hiram.

In the third scene: King Hiram agrees to King Solomon's request.

In the fourth scene: King Hiram sends the wood, and King Solomon sends the food. King Solomon and King Hiram make a treaty with each other.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Yahweh, Solomon's God
  • King Hiram of Tyre
  • King Solomon
  • Hiram's representatives from Tyre
  • Sidonian woodworkers
  • King Hiram's household
  • Hiram's workers
  • And Solomon's workers

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, the storyteller reminds us that Hiram has been friends with Solomon's father, David, for a long time. Then Hiram hears that the people of Israel have anointed Solomon to be the new king. King Hiram of Tyre sends his servants, or special messengers, to King Solomon.

In the second scene, Solomon sends a message back to King Hiram about Solomon's plan to build the temple for Yahweh. Solomon explains to King Hiram why King David was not able to build the temple for the name of Yahweh, his God. When someone builds a "house" or temple for someone's name, that person is building that temple for the honor of that person. King David really wanted to build a house to honor Yahweh, his God. Yahweh did not allow David to build a temple for Yahweh because, while David was king of Israel, David fought many wars. David could not build the house for Yahweh when there was no peace in Israel.

Yahweh will only allow David to build a temple for Yahweh when Yahweh puts David's enemies "under the soles of his feet," or when David defeats and completely destroys all the enemies of Israel. When someone puts people "under their feet," they are saying that they have power over those people, or they destroy those people.

Stop here and discuss as a group how you will talk about Yahweh putting David's enemies under David's feet. Pause this audio here.

But this time is different. There is rest in Israel "on every side." This means that Israel is secure and has peace with the nations that surround the country of Israel.

Stop here and discuss as a group how you will talk about Solomon having rest on every side. Pause this audio here.

Now, it is the right time to build a temple to honor Yahweh. Yahweh himself said to David that David's son, whom Yahweh will "put on the throne" in David's place, will build a temple for the name of Yahweh. You will remember that a throne is a special seat that kings of that time sat on when they ruled. When a new king comes to the throne, it means that a new king begins to rule after the former king dies or stops ruling. Yahweh says that David's son, meaning Solomon, will build the temple for the name, or honor, of Yahweh. A person's name represents that person's reputation and their presence. Yahweh wants his people to build a house for Yahweh's name, which means that Yahweh wants his people to honor him and to recognize that Yahweh's presence will live in that temple.

After Solomon explains why Solomon will build the temple, Solomon then presents his request to King Hiram. Solomon has a wide knowledge of cedar trees, so he knows that cedar is a good wood to build things with. And this time, Solomon asks King Hiram to supply cedar wood for the Israelites to use to build the temple. Solomon also asks King Hiram for skilled people who can cut and do woodwork. Solomon will pay the workers because the people of Sidon are very skilled in woodworking.

In the third scene, King Hiram is happy with what Solomon said in his message. First, King Hiram praises Yahweh because Yahweh gave David a wise son to be the king of Israel. Then King Hiram agrees to what Solomon requested. But Hiram agrees to do something a bit different from what Solomon asked him to do. Hiram says he will also send cypress trees. Then King Hiram explains how Hiram's workers will bring the wood to Israel. Although Hiram says that he will work with the wood, he really means that his workers will do these things. Hiram's workers will cut down the trees and then bring the wood down to the port, or the place on the side of the sea where they will send the wood from. The workers will make the long pieces of wood into rafts by tying them together side by side so that they are like a flat boat. Then the workers will guide the rafts south down the side of the Mediterranean Sea to the place where the men of King Solomon will receive them. It is possible that the workers will use larger boats to pull the flat rafts of wood through the water. King Hiram's workers will take apart the rafts into the separate long pieces of wood. Then King Solomon can bring the long pieces of wood from the shore of the Mediterranean Sea to Jerusalem. And then, towards the end of King Hiram's letter, King Hiram asks Solomon to provide Hiram and his royal family with food in exchange for the wood.

Stop here and look at the map of the Mediterranean Sea and a picture of a raft as a group. Pause this audio here.

The final scene describes how Hiram and Solomon keep their agreement. King Hiram sends all the cedar and cypress wood that Solomon needs. Then, in exchange, King Solomon supplies King Hiram with 20,000 cors of wheat and 20,000 baths of olive oil each year. You will remember that a cor is a unit of measurement. Twenty thousand cors is about 6,000,000 liters of wheat. Twenty thousand baths of olive oil may be around 400,000 liters of olive oil. These are very large amounts of wheat and oil! You may just say that King Solomon sent King Hiram 20,000 measures of wheat and 20,000 measures of olive oil each year, because we are not sure exactly how much of each of these things he sent.

This exchange continues for many years. Then the storyteller mentions again that Yahweh gave Solomon wisdom, just like Yahweh promised Solomon. Then, finally, the storyteller describes the peace between King Solomon and King Hiram. The two kings continue to have peace because they make a treaty, or a solemn promise that they will not fight against each other.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 5:1–12 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has four scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Yahweh, Solomon's God
  • King Hiram of Tyre
  • King Solomon
  • Hiram's representatives from Tyre
  • Sidonian woodworkers
  • King Hiram's household
  • Hiram's workers
  • And Solomon's workers

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

King Hiram has been friends with King David for a long time. Now, King Hiram hears that the people have anointed King Solomon as the next king of Israel. King Hiram sends a message to King Solomon, and King Solomon sends a message back to Hiram.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel grateful to Yahweh for this great responsibility; or
  • I need to continue the good friendship my father had with King Hiram.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King Hiram, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel very happy that Yahweh gave Israel a new king; or
  • I think I need to continue the good relationship that I had with David with the new king of Israel.

Continue the drama.

King Solomon tells King Hiram that Solomon's father, David, could not build the temple for Yahweh because David fought many wars. Now King Solomon has rest from his enemies. Yahweh had told King David that King David's son would build the temple to Yahweh, and Solomon now plans to build the temple. King Solomon asks King Hiram to cut cedar trees from his area for Solomon and send them to Solomon in Israel.
