Solomon says that Solomon's workers will work with King Hiram, and Solomon will pay King Hiram's men. Solomon then says that he knows that the Sidonian men are the best at working with the cedar tree wood.

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing the Sidonians, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We feel very honored; or
  • We think we need to prove ourselves.

Continue the drama.

King Hiram praises Yahweh because Yahweh has given David a wise son to rule over Israel. King Hiram promises to send both cedar wood and cypress wood. King Hiram says that his workers will cut the trees and make them into rafts. Hiram's workers will tie the wood together into flat rafts and guide the rafts to float on the Mediterranean Sea from Tyre down the coast. Then Hiram's workers will break up the rafts into separate pieces of wood, and Solomon's men will take the wood to Jerusalem. Finally, King Hiram asks Solomon to provide him with food for Hiram's household.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King Hiram, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I can see that David's God is very wise! or
  • I want to help Solomon because I want his God to be kind to me.

Ask the person playing King Solomon, "What do you feel or think when King Hiram agrees to provide you with a supply of timbers?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel assured that King Hiram supports my project; or
  • I think King Hiram is a good friend and will help me.

Continue the drama.

Hiram supplies Solomon with cedar and cypress wood each year, and Solomon also sends Hiram a lot of wheat and olive oil every year. Yahweh gives Solomon wisdom, and King Hiram and King Solomon have peace between them. The two kings make a treaty, or an agreement, that they will not fight each other.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 5:1–12 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

In the first scene, King Hiram from Tyre sends his servants to represent him and give his greetings to King Solomon, whom the people had anointed king of Israel. For more information on Tyre, king, and anoint, refer to the Master Glossary.

In this story, the servants are not people who work in the house, but trusted messengers who represent their king.

Stop here and discuss as a group how you will translate servants in this story when it means a trusted messenger. If you choose to use the word for servant, you can refer to servant in the Master Glossary for more information.

In the second scene, Solomon shares his plan to build the temple, or a "house for the name of Yahweh his God." Use the same words or phrases for Yahweh and God as you have used previously, and refer to the Master Glossary for more information.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for the "house or temple for the name of Yahweh his God." Look up the house of God and temple in the Master Glossary for more information. Pause this audio here.

King Solomon says that he has rest on every side, and that he has no more enemies or evil things happening to his country.

Stop here and discuss as a group how you will say that Solomon has rest on every side, and no more enemies and evil things happening. Refer to the Master Glossary for more information about evil, if necessary. Pause this audio here.

Yahweh has told King David that he will put David's son on David's throne. Use the same word or phrase for throne as you have used in previous passages.

King Hiram blesses, or praises Yahweh. When someone blesses someone else, they wish good things for that person. When people bless or praise Yahweh, it means about the same thing, so you can say either bless or praise here. Use the same word or phrase for bless or praise as you did in 1 Kings 1:48 when King David praised Yahweh. For more information about bless and praise, refer to the Master Glossary.

King Hiram says that Yahweh has given David a wise son. Use the same word or phrase for wise as you have in previous passages, and refer to wisdom in the Master Glossary for more information.

King Hiram will send cedar and cypress logs to King Solomon. Cedar trees and cypress trees grow in the forest of Lebanon.

Stop here and look at a picture of cedar trees and cypress trees as a group. Discuss what you will call these trees in your translation. Pause this audio here.

Part of King Hiram's way to deliver the logs to King Solomon is to use rafts. Rafts are logs that people join together with ropes to serve as transportation over the sea. This sea was the Mediterranean Sea, which is a very large body of water. The rafts would float on the water south from the city of Tyre to a place on the coast of Israel.

Stop here and look at the map of Israel and Lebanon again. Find the city of Tyre on the map and the country of Israel. Also, look at a picture of a raft as a group. Discuss how you will talk about these rafts in your translation. Pause this audio here.

Hiram asks for food for his household. Use the same word or phrase for household that you have used in previous passages, and refer to household in the Master Glossary for more information.

In the final scene, the storyteller says that King Hiram keeps his promise of support to Solomon. In return, Solomon sends back 20,000 cors, or about 6,000,000 liters of wheat and 20,000 baths, or about 400,000 liters of olive oil. The Israelites grew wheat and olives. People then smashed olives to make olive oil.

Stop here and decide how you will translate these measurements of volume. Remember that you may just say that he sent 20,000 measures of wheat and olive oil each year. Look at pictures of wheat and olives as a group. Pause this audio here.

King Hiram and King Solomon had peace and made a treaty, or a covenant, not to fight with each other. Use the same word for peace as you have used in previous passages, and refer to the Master Glossary for more information about peace.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for covenant here, when it is a strong agreement between two countries not to fight each other. For more information about this word, you may refer to covenant in the Master Glossary.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 5:13–18
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 5:13–18 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 5:13–18 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 5:13–18 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

You can also listen to your translation of this same story in 2 Chronicles 2:1–18 if you have already translated it. Since this story in 2 Chronicles is slightly different, take note of their differences. Sometimes the order of the scenes differs.

Pause this audio here.

This story shows how King Solomon organizes his workers specifically to gather materials for people to build the temple. Solomon gathers workers from all over Israel and forces them to work for Solomon. Solomon gives the workers specific tasks. Solomon assigns Adoniram to take charge of the workers. Adoniram also served David during David's rule.

During Solomon's time, when a king had a building project, that king could order his citizens to work on the project for a certain amount of time. The king required these people to work without payment, but when their time was finished, they were free to go. This is different than when the Israelites forced foreigners that they had captured in war to become slaves and work for them without pay, and for a very long time. Remember that in the previous story, King Solomon promised King Hiram that King Solomon would send workers to Phoenicia, or Lebanon, to help gather the wood. King Solomon gathers 30,000 workers. Then King Solomon divides them into three groups of 10,000 workers in each group and sends them to Phoenicia, one group at a time. Each group of workers will work there for one month and then come back to stay in Israel for two months. So the Israelite workers have to work a total of four months each year in Phoenicia.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your people's history, what story can you tell about a time when a leader ordered a lot of people to work on a project together? How did your leader do that? Pause this audio here.

Meanwhile, Solomon also gathers workers who stay in the hill country of Israel to gather stone for the temple. There are a total of 150,000 workers for this group. Eighty thousand workers will quarry the stones from the hillside and cut them into different sizes. And there are 70,000 workers who will carry the stones to Jerusalem, where people are building the temple. Solomon also assigns 3,300 supervisors who will help supervise the work. Solomon orders the workers to dig out from the hillside huge numbers of high-quality stones. People will use these stones as the foundation for the temple. The foundation of any structure is very important. The foundation holds the structure together. Some people "dress" or cut high-quality stones, shape them into large square blocks, and then bury them under the ground. Then the people will pile other stones on top of these stones.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What materials do you use when you build big places of worship? Pause this audio here.

Stop here and look at pictures of a stone quarry, wood, and quarry stones as a group. Pause this audio here.

The last part of this story concludes the organization of the workers for the building of the temple. The skilled builders of King Solomon and the skilled builders of King Hiram, along with the workers from Gebal, prepared the wood and the stones to build the temple. Gebal is a city on the coast of Phoenicia that people call Byblos today.

Stop here and look at a map that shows the locations of Gebal or Byblos, Lebanon or Phoenicia, the Phoenician forest, the hill country of Israel, and Jerusalem.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 5:13–18 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has three scenes.

In the first scene: King Solomon sends three groups of 10,000 Israelites to help gather wood in Phoenicia. Each group goes to Phoenicia for one month. King Solomon assigns Adoniram to supervise the workers in Phoenicia.

In the second scene: 150,000 workers carry stones and wood, and cut stone. 3,300 supervisors supervise all of the workers who help build the foundation of the temple.

In the third scene: The storyteller summarizes the work that the workers of King Solomon, the workers of King Hiram, and the workers from the city of Gebal do.

The characters in this passage are:

  • King Solomon
  • Israelite workers
  • The workers whom King Solomon sent to Phoenicia in groups of 10,000 men each
  • The men who cut stone out of the ground
  • The men who carried the wood and stone
  • And the craftsmen who cut the stone
  • Adoniram
  • 3,300 supervisors
  • King Hiram
  • And King Hiram's skilled builders, including the workers from Gebal

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, the storyteller presents how King Solomon organizes the workers for the project. King Solomon gathers workers from all over Israel and assigns them to forced labor. The first group goes to Phoenicia. Solomon assigns 30,000 workers to work in Phoenicia. Solomon divides the 30,000 workers into three groups, with 10,000 workers in each group. The first group works in Phoenicia for a month and then goes back to Israel. Then the second group goes to Phoenicia and works there for one month. Then, after a month, the second group comes back to Israel. Then the third group goes to Phoenicia and works in Phoenicia for one month. While one of the groups is working in Phoenicia, the other two groups stay in Israel. King Solomon orders this rotation of work throughout the building project. You will remember that King Solomon had already appointed Adoniram in an earlier story to be in charge of forced labor.

Stop here and look at a map that shows the location of Phoenicia and Israel as a group. Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, the storyteller tells the number of King Solomon's workers who quarry, or dig, large stones from the hills of Israel. Eighty thousand workers will carve and dig out huge rocks from the hillside. Then 70,000 workers will carry the stones from the hillside to Jerusalem, where people will build the foundation of the temple. Solomon assigns 3,300 supervisors to instruct the workers. The supervisors will make sure that the workers follow King Solomon's instructions. King Solomon instructs the workers to take large blocks of high-quality, or very hard, stones. The workers will shape or cut these stones into squares and use them as the foundation for the temple.

Stop here and discuss as a group what words or phrases you will use for the men who dig the stones from the hills, the men who carry the stones to Jerusalem, the supervisors who make sure the men follow instructions, and the workers who will shape or cut the stones into square blocks for the foundation of the temple. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and look at pictures of the stone quarry, wood, and quarry stones again. How will you describe this in your translation? Pause this audio here.

In the third scene, the storyteller summarizes the work that King Solomon's and King Hiram's workers do with the wood and with the stone. The craftsmen, or skilled builders, from King Solomon, the skilled builders from King Hiram, and the workers of Gebal cut and prepare the stones and the wood so that they can build the temple foundation.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What words will you use to describe the skilled builders? Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 5:13–18 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has three scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • King Solomon
  • Israelite workers
  • The workers whom King Solomon sent to Phoenicia in groups of 10,000 men each
  • The men who cut stone out of the ground
  • The men who carried the wood and stone
  • And the craftsmen who cut the stone
  • Adoniram
  • 3,300 supervisors
  • King Hiram
  • And King Hiram's skilled builders, including the workers from Gebal

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

In the first scene, the storyteller introduces King Solomon. King Solomon divides the workers into three groups. King Solomon sends each group to work in Phoenicia for one month at a time. The rotation of the work will continue until they gather the materials they need to build the temple. King Solomon puts Adoniram in charge of the groups of people that Solomon forces to work for him.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King Solomon, "What are you feeling?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel very happy that Yahweh trusts me with this task.

Ask the person playing the workers, "What do you feel?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel thankful that I have the privilege to work in Phoenicia; or
  • I feel sad because I will be separated from my family.

Ask the person playing Adoniram, "What are you thinking or feeling?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel responsible for the result of the project; or
  • I feel honored to serve Yahweh and King Solomon through this project.

Continue the drama

In the second scene, King Solomon calls two more groups of workers. King Solomon commands one group of 80,000 workers to dig out rocks from the hillside. And then King Solomon commands the other group of 70,000 workers to carry those rocks from the hillside to Jerusalem, where they will build the temple. Then King Solomon assigns 3,300 men as supervisors and commands them to lead the workers.

Ask the person playing King Solomon, "What are you thinking when you choose Adoniram and the foremen to supervise the work in Phoenicia and Israel?" The person might answer things like:

  • I think there are so many workers that I could not supervise them all. I am afraid that this is too much work! or
  • I think there are many people who are capable of leading the workers. I am glad I have help!

Ask the person playing Adoniram and the foremen, "What are you feeling or thinking when King Solomon chooses you for this work?" The person might answer things like:

  • We feel responsible for the result of the project; or
  • We feel honored to serve Yahweh and King Solomon through this project.

Continue the drama.

In the third scene, the storyteller says that King Solomon's builders, King Hiram's builders, and the workers from the city of Gebal all work together to build the temple.

Ask the people playing as the workers of Gebal, "What are you feeling when you know that you are serving Yahweh alongside the workers of King Solomon and King Hiram?" The people of Gebal might answer things like:

  • We feel honored because we are serving Yahweh alongside King Solomon and King Hiram.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 5:13–18 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

In the first scene, King Solomon gathers workers from Israel to build the temple and places them into forced labor. King Solomon assigns the first large group of workers to work in Phoenicia. These workers will help the workers of King Hiram cut and prepare wood from Phoenicia. Use the same words or phrases for king, Israel, and temple as you used in previous passages. For more information on "king" and "temple," refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for "forced labor" as you used in 1 Kings 4:6.

In the second scene, King Solomon also gathers Israelite workers to dig stones from the hillside, workers to carry the stones to Jerusalem, and supervisors. Other workers will "dress" or cut those stones into different shapes and sizes. The supervisors manage and direct the workers.

Stop here and discuss as a group what words or phrases you will use for workers who dig stones from the hillside, workers who carry stones, supervisors, and workers who cut the stones into different shapes and sizes. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and look at the pictures of the stone quarry, wood, and quarry stones again. How will you describe this in your translation? Pause this audio here.

In the third scene, the storyteller summarizes the story and says that King Solomon's skilled builders, King Hiram's skilled builders, and the workers of Gebal cut and prepare the wood and stone.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for skilled builders.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 6:1–13
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 6:1–13 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 6:1–13 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 6:1–13 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous story, you learned about the workers that King Solomon used to prepare to build the temple. These men came from all around Israel to cut and shape stones for the temple.

In this story, Solomon begins to build the temple or house of Yahweh. This temple is where the Israelites will worship Yahweh. You will remember from a previous passage that Yahweh promised that David's son, Solomon, would build a temple for Yahweh. Although David gathered many of the materials people would need to build the temple, it was Solomon who now directed people as they did the work. We hear this same information in another book of the Bible as well.

Stop here and listen as a group: If you have already translated 2 Chronicles 3:1–14, where we hear this same information, listen to your translation of that passage as a group. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller says the people of Israel left Egypt 480 years before Solomon begins to build the temple. However, the storyteller may not mean exactly 480 years. The storyteller may use the number 480 to talk about the 12 generations of people who lived and died between the time the Israelites left Egypt and the time Solomon begins to build the temple. A generation refers to a group of people who lived at the same time and belonged to the same age group. The Israelites believed that a generation lasted about 40 years. So if 12 generations of people lived during this time, the total number of years that passed between the time the Israelites left Egypt and the time Solomon begins to build would be 480. However, storytellers in the Bible sometimes use the number 12 as special language, so it is also possible that the storyteller does not mean 12 actual generations, but just that all of the people who lived between the time of the exodus and the time when Solomon builds the temple have died.

Stop here and discuss as a group: When do people in your culture use numbers in a special way to mean something other than the number itself? Pause this audio here.

At the time of this story, Solomon has been the king over Israel for four years. In the second month of that year, in the spring month called Ziv, Solomon orders his workmen to begin to build Yahweh's temple. People probably called the month Ziv, which means "bloom," because flowers bloomed during that time of the year.

The storyteller provides details about the size and construction of the temple, but we do not know every detail about how the temple looked. Another passage in the Bible tells us that Solomon builds the temple in Jerusalem.

Solomon builds his temple for Yahweh in the shape of a rectangle. The temple's walls are 60 cubits long, 20 cubits wide, and 30 cubits high. A cubit is the way people talked about how long something was. One cubit is as long as the distance between a grown adult man's elbow and his fingertips, or about 50 centimeters. So the temple is about 30 meters long, 10 meters wide, and 15 meters high. There is a portico or porch at the front of the temple that stretches across the width of the front wall. A portico or porch is part of a structure with columns instead of walls to hold up the roof. Therefore, this area is 20 cubits or 10 meters long, and it extends 10 cubits or 5 meters out from the front wall of the temple.

Solomon directs his workmen to make openings in the walls of the temple. We are not sure exactly how these openings looked. These openings may have been decorative windows in the walls of the temple. These openings were probably wider on the inside than they were on the outside, and people may have made these openings to let smoke out of the main hall of the temple. The openings probably did not allow much light to enter the temple.

Solomon builds another structure against the outside of the walls of the main room and the inner sanctuary of the temple that goes along the sides and the rear of the temple. This outside structure contains small rooms. The workmen build the rooms on three levels. Some rooms are on the bottom level, which is 5 cubits or 2.5 meters wide. Some rooms are above the bottom level, and this middle level is 6 cubits or 3 meters wide. Some more rooms are on the top level, and this top level is 7 cubits or 3.5 meters wide. We do not know exactly how many rooms are in this structure that is separate from the temple.

Stop here and look at pictures of what the temple may have looked like, including the portico and the connected structure with rooms. Discuss as a group: What do places for worship look like in your culture? Pause this audio here.

Solomon builds ledges for support around the sides of the outer temple walls. Solomon does this so he will not have to insert any support beams into the walls of the temple. The support beams lay on the ledges. Solomon builds the temple with blocks of stone that workers had prepared at the quarry or deep pit where the workers found them. But at the place where Solomon builds the temple, no one hears noise from tools like a hammer, chisel, or other iron tools. A hammer is a tool people use to break things or hit nails. People used a hammer and a chisel together to break up and shape stone. No one hears any noise from tools at the temple during the entire time workers build the temple. When Yahweh gave Yahweh's laws to the people, Yahweh told the people that they should not use tools to shape the stones they used to build special places. Yahweh may have wanted the quiet from the lack of tools at the temple site to remind the people that the temple is holy and special.

Stop here as a group and look at a picture of a stone quarry, an iron hammer, and an iron chisel. Pause this audio here.

Solomon finishes the construction of the temple and uses long pieces of cedar wood for the flat roof. The kind of cedar wood Solomon used is very hard, so people use it to build structures. Solomon finishes the structure around the walls of the temple. We do not know how many rooms this structure contains, but each room or each level is about 5 cubits or 2.5 meters high from floor to ceiling. Planks or boards that people made of cedar wood connect these rooms to the temple.

Then Solomon hears a message from Yahweh. This message probably comes through a prophet whose name we do not know. This message is about the temple Solomon is building. Yahweh tells Solomon to obey all the laws and directions Yahweh has given. If Solomon lives this way, Yahweh will do what Yahweh promised to David, Solomon's father. Yahweh will live among the people of Israel in the temple, and Yahweh will never leave the people. Yahweh reminds Solomon [of the true significance of the temple. It is like a fulfillment of the promise Yahweh made to his people when he rescued them from Egypt and promised to live with them in the land Yahweh would give them. That is why this part of the story starts with the storyteller reminding us of the Israelites' journey from Egypt and ends by reminding us that Yahweh can now live with his people.]{.mark}

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 6:1–13 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has five scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller begins his story of when Solomon built the temple.

In the second scene: Solomon describes the length and width of the temple. Solomon builds the temple 60 cubits long, 20 cubits wide, and 30 cubits high, and the portico that stretches in the front extends out 10 cubits from the temple wall. Solomon also cuts openings like windows in the walls of the temple and builds a separate structure with three levels around the walls of the temple. The bottom level is 5 cubits wide, the middle level is 6 cubits wide, and the top level is 7 cubits wide. Wooden beams lay on the ledges of the temple walls and connect the temple to the other structure.

In the third scene: Solomon cuts the stones for the temple at another place, and no one hears the noise of any metal tools at the site of the temple.

In the fourth scene: Solomon describes the height and appearance of the separate structure. People enter the separate structure on the south side of the temple. The side rooms in the outside structure are 5 cubits high. Solomon connects this structure to the temple with wooden beams that people make out of cedar To complete the temple and the outside structure, Solomon uses long flat pieces of cedar wood to build the roof.

In the fifth scene: Yahweh gives Solomon a message that if Solomon obeys Yahweh's laws and commands, Yahweh will keep Yahweh's promise to King David. Yahweh will live in the temple and be with the people of Israel forever.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Solomon
  • People of Israel
  • And Yahweh

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, when Solomon has been king of Israel for four years, Solomon starts to build a house or temple for Yahweh. This temple will be a place for the people of Israel to worship Yahweh. People begin to construct the temple in the spring during the month of Ziv, which is the second month of Israel's year. The storyteller says it has been 480 years since the people of Israel left the land of Egypt.

The connection between the time Yahweh rescued the Israelites from Egypt and the time Solomon begins to build the temple lets the people of Israel know that both events are very important. Yahweh promised that Yahweh would lead the people of Israel to the land Yahweh promised them, and the people would live in the land with Yahweh forever. When Solomon builds a permanent temple to worship Yahweh, the people of Israel can remember that Yahweh keeps Yahweh's promise to live with the people in the land.

Stop here and discuss as a group: How do people in your culture remember when significant events happen? Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, Solomon instructs workers to build the temple. Even though the storyteller talks about how Solomon himself builds and decorates the temple, we know this means workers build and decorate the temple. The storyteller begins to talk about the width and length of the temple building. The temple Solomon begins to build is 60 cubits long, 20 cubits wide, and 30 cubits high. A cubit is a way to measure how long something is. Solomon builds a portico or porch at the front of the temple. This portico is as wide as the rest of the temple, and it extends 10 cubits from the temple. Solomon cuts openings in the walls of the temple. These openings are like windows, but we are not sure exactly how they looked. Workers probably cut these openings into the top part of the temple walls. The openings were not very wide, and they may have had frames or decorations around them. The openings did not have any glass in them.

Next, the storyteller describes the width of the separate structure around the temple. Solomon builds another structure against the outside walls of the temple that contains three levels with small rooms on each level. Solomon puts the window openings above these three levels in the wall of the temple. The lowest level is 5 cubits wide, the middle level is 6 cubits wide, and the top level is 7 cubits wide. Because we are not sure what shape these rooms may have been, they may have hung over one another so that the structure appeared to get wider from the bottom to the top level. Or, the rooms may have looked like stairs so that the wall appeared to get narrower from the bottom to the top level. Solomon cuts ledges into the walls of the temple. The wooden beams that support the outside structure sit on the ledges so that none of the wooden beams go into the walls of the temple.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Look at pictures or diagrams of how the temple may have looked. How will you describe the measurements and layout of the temple in a way that people in your culture can understand? Pause this audio here.

In the third scene, [workers cut all of the rocks they will use to build the temple so that the rocks will fit together tightly to make a strong, square building. Solomon has the workers cut the rocks at the quarry or mine so that no one hears any noise of iron tools, like a hammer or chisel, at the place where they build the temple.]{.mark} During the whole time Solomon builds the temple, no one hears noise from metal tools.

In the fourth scene, the storyteller talks more about the separate structure around the temple. Solomon puts the door to enter the rooms in the lowest level of the outside structure on the south side of the temple. The front of the temple faces east, and the rear of the temple faces west. Solomon builds a stairway for people to go up to the rooms on the middle and top levels. We do not know exactly how the stairway looked, but it may have been in the shape of a spiral.

Stop here as a group and look at a picture of a winding stairway. Pause this audio here

Then the storyteller talks about the height of the temple and its roof. Solomon completes the temple and puts a roof of wooden planks on the top. Solomon makes this roof of cedar wood, and it is probably flat. The outside structure Solomon builds has rooms or levels that are 5 cubits high. We do not know exactly how people use these rooms, but temple workers probably use some of them for storage. Wooden beams connect the structure with the rooms to the temple.

In the fifth scene, Solomon hears the word of Yahweh. Yahweh probably speaks to Solomon through a prophet. Prophets often used the phrase "word of Yahweh" to show people that they spoke words that came directly from Yahweh. Yahweh tells Solomon, "I want to say something to you about this temple you are building. You must obey all the laws and directions I have given you." In the Bible, when storytellers use statutes, ordinances, and commands together, we can simply say that these are Yahweh's laws and directions. Solomon should obey all that Yahweh says. Yahweh continues to tell Solomon, "If you live this way, I will do for you what I promised King David, your father. I will live here among my people, the people of Israel. I will not abandon them."

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 6:1–13 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has five scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Solomon
  • People of Israel
  • And Yahweh

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

During the fourth year of Solomon's reign, in the mid-spring month of Ziv, Solomon begins to build the temple. Four hundred and eighty years have passed since the Israelites left Egypt. The temple is 60 cubits long, 20 cubits wide, and 30 cubits high. The portico that stretches across the width of the temple in the front extends out 10 cubits from the front of the temple. Solomon also cuts openings like windows in the walls of the temple and builds a separate structure around the walls of the temple. This separate structure has three levels. The bottom level is 5 cubits wide, the middle level is 6 cubits wide, and the top level is 7 cubits wide. Wooden beams lay on the ledges of the temple walls and connect the temple to the other structure.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I rejoice because I can build a permanent house where we can worship Yahweh; or
  • I feel proud because I can build the temple out of all the materials my father, David, collected.

Continue the drama.

Workers cut the stones for the temple at another place, and no one hears the noise of any metal tools at the site of the temple. On the south side of the temple, Solomon makes a way to enter the separate structure that is outside the temple walls. From there, people can use a stairway to go up to the rooms in the middle and top levels. Solomon completes the side rooms in the outside structure, and each room is 5 cubits high. The workers connect this structure to the temple with beams they make from cedar wood. To complete the temple and the outside structure, Solomon uses long, flat pieces of cedar wood to build the roof.

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing the people of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We feel happy that our hard work has made a place for us to worship Yahweh; or
  • We feel amazed that we can build the temple that Yahweh promised King David.

Continue the drama.

Yahweh delivers a message to Solomon. Yahweh tells Solomon, "I want to say something to you about this temple you are building. You must obey all the laws and directions I have given you. If you live this way, I will do for you what I promised King David, your father. I will live here among my people, the people of Israel. I will not leave them."

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel pleased that Solomon has built this temple for me; or
  • I feel hopeful that Solomon and the people of Israel will obey my commands so I can live in this temple with my people, Israel.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 6:1–13 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Stop here and listen again as a group: If you have already translated 2 Chronicles 3:1–14, where we hear this same information, listen to your translation of that passage as a group. Pause this audio here.

When Solomon had been king over Israel for four years, in the springtime month of Ziv, which is the second month of the year in Israel, Solomon began to build the temple. Israel is the name of the territory where the Israelites lived. A king is someone who rules over a city or territory, like a region or a country. The temple is the place where the Israelites worshiped Yahweh. People also call the temple "the house of Yahweh." For more information on Israel, king, and temple, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same words or phrases for Israel, king, and temple as you used in previous passages.

Stop here and look at a picture of what the temple may have looked like as a group. Pause this audio here.

Solomon builds the temple for Yahweh. Yahweh is a name God gives himself. This name reminds people of the covenant or special promise God made to the Israelites. For more information on Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for Yahweh as you used in previous passages.

The temple Solomon builds is 60 cubits long, 20 cubits wide, and 30 cubits high. A cubit is a way to measure how long something is. One cubit is about as long as a man's arm from his elbow to his fingertips, which is about 50 centimeters.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for cubit. Look up cubit in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

The portico or entry room for the temple is 20 cubits wide, and it extends out 10 cubits from the front of the temple. A portico, which people sometimes call a porch, is part of a structure that has columns instead of walls to hold up the roof. Solomon cuts out narrow windows in the temple walls. Solomon then builds a structure around the outside of the temple walls. This structure has many small rooms in it.

Stop here and look at a picture of a portico and a picture of what the separate structure around the temple may have looked like. Discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for portico. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Workers shape the stones they will use to build the temple at the quarry. A quarry is usually a large, deep pit where workers take out stone. We know from previous stories that these quarries may have been in the hills of Israel. Use the same word or phrase for quarry that you have used in the previous passages.

At the place where the workmen build the temple, no one uses metal tools such as a hammer or chisel, so no one hears the noise of iron tools at the place where people build the temple. A hammer is a tool with a wooden handle and a metal end that people use to break things or to hit nails. People use a hammer and a chisel together to break up and shape stone. People probably made these tools out of iron.

Stop here and look at a picture of a hammer and a chisel. Discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for hammer and chisel. If you have already translated these words in another book of the Bible, use the same words that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller says that Solomon built the separate structure around the outside of the main room and the inner room, or sanctuary of the temple. The sanctuary is another word for the Most Holy Place, or the innermost room in the temple where the priests kept the ark of the covenant.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for sanctuary. Refer to sanctuary in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

The entrance to the structure with the rooms is on the south side of the temple. From that entrance, a person can go up a stairway to the middle level or to the top level. This is probably a winding stairway, which winds around a central post or column, so the stairs look like a spiral.

Stop here and look at a picture of a winding stairway. Discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for stairway. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Solomon completes the temple with a flat roof that people make with wooden planks. Then the word of Yahweh, or a message from Yahweh, comes to Solomon. Use the same word or phrase for "word" of Yahweh that you have used previously, and refer to "word" in the Master Glossary for more information.

Yahweh tells Solomon to obey all of Yahweh's statutes, ordinances, and commands. For more information on statutes, ordinances, and commands, refer to the Master Glossary. Remember that when a storyteller uses these words together, you may simply say that Yahweh tells Solomon to obey all of "Yahweh's laws and instructions."

Stop here and decide as a group if you will use the three words for statutes, ordinances, and commands, or if you will simply say "Yahweh's laws and instructions."

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 6:14–38
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 6:14–38 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 6:14–38 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 6:14–38 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous passage, Solomon began to make his workers build the temple for Yahweh. You will remember that, even though the storyteller says Solomon built the temple, we know Solomon directed many people to help build Yahweh's temple. Solomon used stone to build the temple walls and the structure around the temple. Yahweh then told Solomon that if Solomon obeyed Yahweh's commands, Yahweh would live among the people of Israel forever.

In this passage, you will hear more details about how Solomon builds and finishes the inside of the temple.

Stop here and listen as a group: If you have already translated 2 Chronicles 3:1–14, where we hear this same information, listen to your translation of that passage as a group. Pause this audio here.

Solomon covers the inside of the temple walls with flat wooden boards that people made from cedar trees. These boards cover the walls from the floor to the ceiling. Solomon also covers the floor with wooden boards that people made from cypress trees. People used cedar and something like cypress wood together to make expensive and special things. Solomon uses the best materials to show how much Solomon loves Yahweh.

Stop here and look at a picture of cypress and cedar trees as a group. Pause this audio here.

Solomon takes more cedar boards and makes a wall on the inside of the temple to create a separate room at the back of the temple. Solomon places this wall 20 cubits from the back wall of the temple. You will remember from previous passages that people used cubits to measure length, and a cubit measures the distance between a man's elbow and the tips of his fingers, or about 50 centimeters. Solomon uses this inside wall to create an inner special room that people call the inner sanctuary, or the Most Holy Place. The main hall of the temple, however, measures 40 cubits or about 20 meters long.

Stop here as a group and look at a diagram of the temple that includes the main hall and the Most Holy Place. Pause this audio here.

Cedar wood covers the inside of the entire temple, including the main hall and the Most Holy Place. Craftsmen carve pictures of gourd vines and flower blossoms into the wood. A gourd vine grows fruit that looks like a type of melon. People used carvings of gourds and flower blossoms to make things beautiful during the time Solomon built the temple. People seemed to especially like using gourds and flower blossoms for holy areas. Because cedar wood covers the stone blocks Solomon used to build the temple, no one can see any stone anywhere inside the temple.

Stop here and look at a picture of a gourd vine and flower blossom that people carved in wood as a group. Pause this audio here.

Solomon prepares the Most Holy Place in the temple. Solomon will put the ark of the covenant in the Most Holy Place. The ark of the covenant represents the presence of Yahweh and contains the tablets with Yahweh's law. The Most Holy Place measures 20 cubits or 10 meters long, 20 cubits or 10 meters wide, and 20 cubits or 10 meters high. We know from another passage in the Bible that the temple is 30 cubits high, so the Most Holy Place may have been higher up with stairs going up to it, or Solomon's workers may have left an open space above the Most Holy Place that was not part of the Most Holy Place. Solomon covers the inside walls of the Most Holy Place with good-quality gold. Solomon also covers the altar that people made of cedar with gold. Priests use the altar to burn incense, and they set the altar in the main hall, right outside the Most Holy Place.

Stop here and look at a picture of the ark of the covenant as a group. Look at a picture of gold metal. Pause this audio here.

Solomon also covers all the inside walls in the main hall of the temple with gold. Solomon places gold chains across the wall in front of the Most Holy Place. People use links of metal that they connect together to make chains. Solomon may have used these chains to hang a curtain. Solomon uses gold to cover the entire inside of the temple, which includes the main hall and the Most Holy Place.

Solomon carves two statues of cherubs out of olive wood for the Most Holy Place. You will remember from the previous passage that a cherub is a type of angel, and more than one cherub is cherubim. The cherubs may have guarded the ark because it was a special holy place, or they may represent Yahweh's throne. Solomon makes each cherub 10 cubits or 5 meters high. The first cherub has two wings that are each 5 cubits or 2.5 meters long, so the distance between the tips of the 2 wings is 10 cubits or 5 meters. The second cherub's wings are also 10 cubits long because the cherubs are the same size and shape. One cherub's wing tip touches one wall, and the other cherub's wing tip touches the opposite wall. The wings of the cherubim touch in the middle of the Most Holy Place. Solomon places the cherubim on the ground inside the Most Holy Place with their wings spread out. We know from another passage in the Bible that the cherubim face out, towards the main hall of the temple. Solomon will put the ark of the covenant under the cherubim's wings. Solomon covers the cherubim with gold.

Solomon decorates the walls in the Most Holy Place and the main hall in the temple with cherubs, palm trees, and flower blossoms. Solomon also covers the floor of the entire inside of the temple with gold.

Stop here and look at a picture of cherubim as a group. Look at a picture of a palm tree that people carved into wood. Pause this audio here.

In the doorway to the Most Holy Place, Solomon places double doors that people have made from wood from an olive tree. Solomon probably makes two doors that each open in the same direction, so the doorway can be wider than a single door. We are not sure if the doorway stretches over one-fifth of the wall to the Most Holy Place or if the doors had five sections. Solomon decorates the doors with carvings of cherubs, palm trees, and flower blossoms. Then, Solomon covers the carvings with a layer of gold. From another passage in the Bible, we know Solomon also places a curtain in the doorway to the Most Holy Place. The curtain may have remained over the entrance even when someone opened the doors so that no one could see into the Most Holy Place. Because Yahweh lives in the Most Holy Place, no one could enter or even look inside it. Only the high priest could go into the presence of Yahweh in the Most Holy Place, and the high priest only entered one time per year.

In the same way, Solomon puts double doors in the entrance to the temple. Solomon makes door frames out of wood from an olive tree. We are not sure if the doorway is one-fourth as wide as the whole temple or if Solomon makes double doors with four sections. Solomon makes the doors out of wood from a cypress tree. Each door probably has two sections that turn on hinges. Solomon decorates the sections with carvings of cherubim, palm trees, and flower blossoms and spreads gold over the carvings.

Now the storyteller talks more about the outside of the temple. Solomon makes the inner courtyard wall with rows of cut stone and cedar wood. Solomon also builds walls all the way around an open area just outside the temple. Solomon makes the walls out of three layers of cut stone, followed by a row of wooden beams that people make from cedar. We do not know how thick the wall is. Solomon may use this pattern because it makes the walls strong enough to survive an earthquake.

Solomon built the foundation for Yahweh's house during the fourth year that Solomon was king over Israel. Solomon began to build the temple during the month called Ziv, which is the second month of the year in Israel. When Solomon had been king over Israel for 11 years, Solomon completed the temple during the month of Bul, the eighth month of the year in Israel. Solomon's workers completed the temple exactly the way Solomon planned. Solomon actually took seven years and six months to completely build Yahweh's temple, but the storyteller shortens that number to seven years because the number seven represents wholeness or completion.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 6:14–38 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has four scenes.

In the first scene: Solomon finishes building the temple. Solomon covers the entire inside of the temple with wooden cedar boards and uses wooden cypress boards to cover the floor. Solomon builds a wall to separate the main hall from the Most Holy Place. Solomon covers all the stone in the temple with wood.

In the second scene: Solomon prepares the Most Holy Place. Solomon covers the Most Holy Place and the incense altar with gold and puts gold chains across the doorway. Solomon puts two cherubim in the Most Holy Place and covers them with gold. Solomon decorates the wall with carvings, covers the carvings with gold, and makes double doors that he covers with gold for the doorway.

In the third scene: Solomon makes double doors for the entrance to the temple and covers them with gold. Solomon decorates the doors with carvings. Solomon builds the walls of the inner courtyard.

In the fourth scene: Solomon takes seven years to build the temple.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Solomon
  • Workers
  • And Yahweh

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, Solomon builds and finishes the temple. Like the previous passage, the storyteller says Solomon does the work to build the temple, but the storyteller means that Solomon directs other people to do the work. Many workers help to build the temple of Yahweh.

Stop here and review as a group: For the previous passage, how did you help your audience understand that Solomon did not build the temple by himself? How will you do the same thing in this passage? Pause this audio here.

Solomon covers the stone on the inside of the temple walls with wooden boards that people make from cedar wood. Solomon covers the whole inside of the temple, from the floor to the ceiling, with cedar boards. Solomon covers every part of the walls inside the temple with cedar boards. Solomon covers the floor of the temple with boards that people make from cypress wood. Cedar trees and cypress trees look similar, and both trees have a pleasant smell. Solomon uses cedar boards to create a wall towards the back of the temple, 20 cubits from the back wall of the temple. This wall creates an inner sanctuary that Solomon calls the Most Holy Place. The main hall of the temple is 40 cubits long, and the Most Holy Place is 20 cubits long. Solomon uses cedar boards to cover the entire inside of the temple and carves pictures of gourds and flower blossoms into the wood of the walls. Solomon uses so much cedar wood that no one can see any stone on the inside of the temple because the wood covers all the stone.

In the second scene, Solomon prepares the Most Holy Place. Solomon will put the ark of the covenant in the Most Holy Place. The Most Holy Place is 20 cubits long, 20 cubits wide, and 20 cubits high. Solomon covers the inside of the Most Holy Place completely with high-quality gold. Solomon also covers the altar that sits right outside the Most Holy Place with gold. Solomon covers the whole inside of the temple, the main hall and the Most Holy Place, with gold. Solomon also puts gold chains across the doorway to the Most Holy Place.

Solomon makes two cherubim out of olive wood for the Most Holy Place. Each cherub is 10 cubits high, and each cherub has two wings. Each wing measures 5 cubits long, so both wings together measure 10 cubits. Solomon makes the cherubs exactly the same in size and in shape. Solomon places the cherubim inside the Most Holy Place with their wings stretched out. We know from another passage that the cherubim faced out towards the main hall of the temple. The cherubim's wings touch opposite walls, and the wings touch each other in the middle of the Most Holy Place. Solomon also covers the cherubim with gold.

Solomon carves pictures of cherubim, palm trees, and flower blossoms on all the walls inside the temple. Gold covers the walls and the floor of every part of the inside of the temple. Solomon makes double doors out of olive wood for the Most Holy Place. In the original language, we are not sure if the storyteller says these doors stretch across one-fifth of the wall to the Most Holy Place or if the doors contain five sections. Craftsmen carve pictures of cherubim, palm trees, and flower blossoms on the doors and cover the doors with gold.

Stop here and discuss as a group: What kinds of designs do you use to decorate the most important religious or holy items in your culture? How will you describe the designs Solomon used to decorate the inside of the temple? Pause this audio here.

In the third scene, Solomon makes double doors for the doorway that leads people from the outside into the main hall of the temple. Solomon makes these doors in the same way he made doors for the entrance to the Most Holy Place. In the original language, we are not sure if the storyteller says these doors measure one-fourth of the width of the whole temple, or if the doors contain four sections. Solomon makes the door frames out of olive wood, and Solomon uses cypress wood to make the doors. The doors turn on hinges. Solomon carves cherubim, palm trees, and flower blossoms into the doors and covers the carvings with an even layer of gold.

Stop here and discuss as a group: What words will you use to describe the different doors Solomon uses in the temple? Pause this audio here.

Solomon builds an inner court in front of the temple with walls that workers make out of one layer of cedar beams for every three layers of stone.

In the fourth scene, the storyteller says that Solomon laid the foundation for the temple during the fourth year of Solomon's reign as king. This happened during Ziv, which is the second month of the year in Israel. In the 11th year of Solomon's reign, Solomon finished building the temple. Solomon finished during the month of Bul, which is the eighth month of the year in Israel. Solomon built the temple exactly as he had decided to build it, and Solomon took seven years to complete it.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 6:14–38 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has four scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Solomon
  • Workers
  • And Yahweh

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Solomon finishes building the temple. Solomon uses boards of cedar wood to cover the inside walls of the temple, and Solomon uses boards of cypress wood to cover the floors. Solomon makes a wall inside the temple to separate the main hall from the Most Holy Place. Solomon covers the cedar boards with carvings of gourds and flower blossoms. No one can see any stone inside the temple.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel happy that I can make the inside of the temple so beautiful; or
  • I feel certain that Yahweh will like the temple I directed workers to build.

Continue the drama.

Solomon prepares the Most Holy Place, where Solomon will place the Ark of the Covenant. The Most Holy Place is 20 cubits wide, 20 cubits long, and 20 cubits high. Solomon covers the inside of the Most Holy Place with high-quality gold. Solomon also covers the altar for burning incense with gold. Solomon puts chains of gold across the entrance to the Most Holy Place.

Solomon makes two cherubim out of olive wood to put in the Most Holy Place, and Solomon covers the cherubim with gold. The cherubim's wings touch the opposite walls, and their wings touch each other in the middle of the Most Holy Place. The cherubim are the same in size and shape. Solomon carves pictures of more cherubim, palm trees, and flower blossoms on each wall and covers the carvings with gold. Solomon makes double doors for the doorway to the Most Holy Place, and Solomon covers the doors with gold.

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing the workers, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We feel pleased to be able to use our skills in carving wood and laying gold to create a beautiful place for Yahweh; or
  • We rejoice that Solomon wants to honor Yahweh with a permanent temple.

Continue the drama.

Solomon makes double doors for the entrance to the temple and covers the doors with gold. Solomon decorates the doors with carvings of cherubim, palm trees, and flower blossoms and covers the decorations with gold. Solomon builds the walls of the inner courtyard out of a layer of cedar beams, followed by three layers of stone. Solomon began to build the temple during the fourth year of his reign, during Israel's second month of Ziv. Solomon finished building the temple in the 11^th^ year of his reign during Israel's eighth month of Bul. Solomon took seven years to build the temple.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel happy that Solomon directs the people of Israel to honor me with such a beautiful structure; or
  • I feel confident that the people of Israel will remember me and obey me when they worship in this temple.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 6:14–38 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Stop here and listen again as a group: If you have already translated 2 Chronicles 3:1–14, where we hear this same information, listen to your translation of that passage again. Pause this audio here.

Solomon finishes building the temple for Yahweh. The temple is the place where the Israelites worshiped God. Yahweh is the name that God gives himself, and it reminds people of the covenant God made with the people of Israel. Use the same words or phrases for temple and Yahweh as you used in previous passages. For more information on temple and Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary.

Solomon covers the entire inside walls of the temple with boards of cedar wood, and Solomon uses boards of cypress wood to cover the floors. Solomon measures off 20 cubits at the back of the main hall and makes a wall between that space and the main hall. People used cubits to measure length, and a cubit measures about 50 centimeters. Use the same word or phrase for cubit as you used in previous passages. For more information on cubit, refer to the Master Glossary.

This wall separates the main hall from the inner "sanctuary" or Most Holy Place. The sanctuary means "a holy place," and people can use this word to mean either the Most Holy Place or the entire temple building itself. In this case, the inner sanctuary is another way to say the Most Holy Place, and the storyteller uses both the words "inner sanctuary" and "Most Holy Place" to describe the place where Yahweh lived in the temple. The Most Holy Place is the holiest place in the temple, and it is where Solomon placed the ark of the covenant.

Stop here and discuss as a group how you will say that Solomon built a wall to separate the main hall from the sanctuary, the Most Holy Place. Use the same words or phrases for sanctuary and Most Holy Place as you used in previous passages or in previous books of the Bible. For more information on "sanctuary" and "Most Holy Place," refer to the Master Glossary. Pause this audio here.

Solomon covers the cedar boards with carvings of gourds and flower blossoms, and no one can see any of the stones under the cedar boards. Solomon prepares to put the ark of the covenant inside the Most Holy Place. The ark refers to the box Moses made that contains God's law. The Israelites also believed that God lived above the ark. Use the same word or phrase for "ark of the covenant" that you used in previous passages. For more information on the ark of the covenant, refer to the Master Glossary.

Stop here and look at a picture of the ark of the covenant as a group. Pause this audio here.

Solomon covers the entire inside of the temple with gold, including the walls of the main hall and the Most Holy Place. Solomon also covers the altar of incense, which sits right outside the Most Holy Place, with gold. Gold is a yellow precious metal that is very expensive. Use the same word or phrase for gold as you used in previous passages.

Stop here and look at a picture of gold metal as a group. Pause this audio here.

Solomon makes two identical cherubs out of olive wood and covers them with gold. A cherub is a type of angel who guards holy spaces. When there is more than one cherub, people call this "cherubim."

Stop here and look at a picture of cherubim as a group. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for cherubim. Use the same words or phrases for cherubim as you used in previous books of the Bible. For more information on cherubim, refer to the Master Glossary. Pause this audio here.

Solomon makes double doors for the entrance to the Most Holy Place and for the entrance to the temple. Solomon covers the doors with carvings of cherubim, palm trees, and flower blossoms, and Solomon covers the doors evenly with gold. Double doors refer to a pair of doors that open on hinges and create a larger opening for a room.

Stop here and look at a picture of double doors as a group. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss what word or phrase you will use for double doors. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Solomon builds an inner courtyard wall with layers of cedar beams and stone, and Solomon finishes the temple. A courtyard is the open area around the temple building, so the wall surrounds the courtyard.

Stop here and discuss what word or phrase you will use for courtyard. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Solomon built the foundation of the temple in the fourth year of his reign, and Solomon finished in the 11th year of his reign. Solomon took seven years to complete the temple. Use the same word or phrase for foundation as you have used in previous passages.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 7:1–12
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 7:1–12 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 7:1–12 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 7:1–12 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous passage, you heard a description of how Solomon ordered workers to build the inside of the temple. Even though the storyteller said Solomon built the temple, we know that Solomon directed many workers to help build Yahweh's temple. Solomon built the main hall and the Most Holy Place and covered all of the inside of the temple with gold. Solomon took seven years to finish building the temple for Yahweh.

In this passage, however, Solomon builds a palace complex south of the temple. People build a palace, or a large home, for important people or rulers. The palace complex contains Solomon's home along with four other buildings. Solomon takes 13 years to finish building the entire palace complex.

Stop here and look at a picture of a palace from the time of Solomon as a group. Pause this audio here.

Solomon builds the first structure and calls it the Palace of the Forest of Lebanon. Solomon probably uses this building as a large ceremonial or assembly hall, and people may gather there to see King Solomon. Solomon also may store weapons or precious objects there. Workers make this palace with four rows of columns that they make from cedar wood. A column is a round pillar that stands upright and supports the structure of a building. These columns hold up beams that people make of cedar wood. People make these horizontal wooden beams to support the weight of a structure.

Solomon probably gives the hall its name because Solomon uses cedar wood from the forest of Lebanon to build it. Phoenicia, or modern-day Lebanon, is a country located north of Israel that has many forests of cedar trees. People may have thought that the columns in this structure look like a forest. A forest is a large area of land where many trees grow close together to cover the whole area.

Stop here and look at a picture of a forest of cedar trees as a group. Look at a picture of a column. Look at a map that shows the area of Phoenicia and the forests of Lebanon. Pause this audio here.

This palace measures 100 cubits or 50 meters long, 50 cubits or 25 meters wide, and 30 cubits or 15 meters high. You will remember from previous passages that people use cubits to measure length. One cubit measures about 50 centimeters. Solomon's workers make a flat roof by placing cedar planks over the cedar beams that sit on top of the columns. The cedar beams that rest on the columns help to support or hold up the roof. Solomon uses 45 beams, and Solomon puts 15 beams in each row.

The workers build the main hall for the Palace of the Forest of Lebanon with window openings for light. Solomon arranges these windows in three rows high up on opposite walls. All the door openings have frames with four sides. Solomon probably places the door frames in the front part of the hall in three rows on opposite walls so that the doors face each other. This palace probably has three upper levels around the sides of the building.

Solomon builds another structure with a colonnade or rows of columns that support the roof. People may have called this structure the Hall of Columns. This hall measures 50 cubits or 25 meters long and 30 cubits or 15 meters wide. A covered porch goes all the way across the front of the hall, and columns support the covering of the porch. We do not know how tall this hall is. We are not sure exactly how Solomon uses this hall, but people may wait in this hall before seeing King Solomon.

Solomon also builds a hall for his throne. A throne is a ceremonial chair for a king or ruler. Solomon plans to sit in this hall and judge the people of Israel. When a person judges, that person decides if the actions that another person does are right or wrong. Solomon probably listens when people in his kingdom have a disagreement that no one else can resolve, and then Solomon decides who is right. Solomon calls this hall the Hall of Justice. Solomon covers this entire hall, from the floor to the ceiling, with boards of cedar wood. The storyteller does not give measurements for this hall.
