Solomon probably builds his own home, where he actually lives, in a courtyard behind the Hall of Justice. Solomon builds his home in a similar way to the Hall of Justice. Solomon builds another palace that looks like his own home for the daughter of Pharaoh. Pharaoh's daughter was one of Solomon's wives. You may remember from a previous passage that Pharaoh was a title people used for the king of the country of Egypt. Because the storyteller gives no description, we do not know how Solomon's house looked or how the house for Pharaoh's daughter looked.

Solomon builds all these buildings, from the outside hall to the large courtyard, and from the floors to the ceilings, with high-quality, or very strong, stone blocks. Solomon probably uses limestone, a white-colored stone that people find in Israel. Workers cut these stone blocks carefully and smooth all their edges. Solomon lays the foundation for the buildings with large, high-quality stones. These stones measure 8 to 9 cubits or 4 to 5 meters across. On top of these foundation stones, Solomon places high-quality stones that people cut to the correct size for walls. Solomon also uses rows of cedar wood for the walls. These may be cedar beams, or they may be pieces of cedar wood that lie over the walls.

Solomon constructs the walls for the large courtyard around his house with a pattern of three rows of stone blocks followed by one row of cedar beams. Solomon had constructed the inside courtyard for the temple and the portico in a similar way. Solomon probably uses this method of construction for the walls because it makes the walls strong.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 7:1–12 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has three scenes.

In the first scene: Solomon takes 13 years to build a palace complex for himself. First, Solomon builds the Palace of the Forest of Lebanon. The palace has rows of columns and a roof of cedar wood. The palace has windows and doors in rows of three that face each other.

In the second scene: Solomon builds a hall with rows of columns and a portico in front of it. Solomon builds the Hall of Justice. Solomon sits on his throne in this hall and judges the people of Israel. Solomon builds a home for himself and a home for his wife, the daughter of the Pharaoh.

In the third scene: Solomon uses large blocks of high-quality stone to build each structure. Workers cut and smooth each stone. Solomon also uses cedar beams to build each structure. Solomon builds a large courtyard around his house with one layer of cedar beams and three layers of cut stone, just like Solomon built the wall for the courtyard of Yahweh's temple.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Solomon
  • Workers
  • Pharaoh
  • Pharaoh's daughter
  • And Yahweh

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, the storyteller interrupts his explanation of how Solomon builds the temple to talk about Solomon's palace complex. You will remember from previous passages that even though the storyteller says Solomon builds, Solomon tells workers to build structures for the temple and for his palace. After this passage, the storyteller will continue to talk about how Solomon builds and furnishes the temple.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Tell a story, but in the middle of your story, tell a different story. Then go back to finish your original story. How do you help your audience understand the order and details of your stories and how they relate to one another? Pause this audio here.

Even though Solomon took seven years to build the temple for Yahweh, Solomon takes 13 years to build a palace complex for himself. Solomon probably built and completed the temple first, and then Solomon begins to build his palace. Solomon finishes building all the structures included in his palace complex after 13 years.

Stop here and discuss as a group: How will you help your audience understand the different lengths of time Solomon took to build Yahweh's temple and Solomon's palace? Pause this audio here.

Solomon builds the Palace of the Forest of Lebanon. This palace measures 100 cubits or 50 meters long, 50 cubits or 25 meters wide, and 30 cubits or 15 meters high. The palace has four rows of columns or pillars that workers make from cedar wood, and these columns hold up beams that the workers also make from cedar. Above the beams, Solomon makes the roof of the palace out of cedar. Although we are not sure, most likely Solomon uses 45 beams to make three rows of 15 cedar beams. Solomon places windows in three rows that face each other high up on opposite walls. Solomon makes doorways in the front part of the palace. The doorways face each other in sets of three, and each doorway has a rectangular frame.

In the second scene, Solomon builds a hall or long room with many columns in rows, called colonnades, and people may have named this hall the Hall of Columns. This hall is 50 cubits or 25 meters long and 30 cubits or 15 meters wide. Across the front of this hall, Solomon builds a portico or porch, which is like a room without walls but with columns that hold up a roof. We are not sure if this hall was the entrance to the Palace of the Forest of Lebanon or the entrance to the next building, the Hall of Justice.

Solomon puts his throne in the Hall of Justice. Solomon sits on his throne to judge the people in the Hall of Justice. Solomon covers the entire inside of the Hall of Justice, from the floor to the ceiling, with boards of cedar wood. Solomon builds his own palace, where Solomon lives, in a similar way to the Hall of Justice. Solomon also builds a palace for his wife, the daughter of the Pharaoh, in a similar way.

Stop here and discuss as a group: How will you describe each building? What will you call each building? Pause this audio here.

In the third scene, Solomon constructs every building with high-quality, or very hard, stone. Solomon's workers carefully cut the stone and smooth its edges. From the outside halls to the large courtyard and from floor to ceiling, Solomon makes every structure out of this stone. Solomon uses large, high-quality stones that measure 8 to 10 cubits or 4 to 5 meters across to lay the foundations of the buildings. On top of the foundation, Solomon places more high-quality stones that workers cut to the right size to build the walls. Solomon also uses beams of cedar to make walls. People are not sure if these cedar beams overlay the stone walls or if these are ceiling beams. Scholars are not sure what the storyteller means in the original language. The large courtyard that surrounds these buildings has a wall that people make with three layers of cut stone blocks, followed by a layer of cedar beams. Solomon builds this wall in the same way Solomon built the wall in the inner courtyard and entryway of the temple.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 7:1–12 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has three scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Solomon
  • Workers
  • Pharaoh
  • Pharaoh's daughter
  • And Yahweh

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Solomon takes 13 years to build a palace complex for himself. The first building Solomon constructs is the Palace of the Forest of Lebanon. Solomon makes this building 100 cubits long, 50 cubits wide, and 30 cubits high. Solomon uses four rows of cedar pillars, and Solomon places cedar beams on top of the pillars. The beams help to support the roof that Solomon also makes of cedar. Solomon uses 45 beams with 15 beams in each row. Solomon builds windows in three rows that face each other, and Solomon makes rectangular door frames. Solomon puts the doors in sets of three in the front part of the palace. The doors also face each other.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am happy that Solomon has the resources to construct a building where he rules over the people of Israel; or
  • Solomon built my temple first, so I know Solomon loves and worships me; or
  • Solomon spent more time building the palace than the temple. I wonder how important I really am to Solomon; or
  • I feel hopeful that Solomon will use his palace complex to serve the people of Israel well.

Continue the drama.

Solomon builds the Hall of Columns, a hall with rows of columns and with a covered porch across the front. This hall is 50 cubits long and 30 cubits wide. Then Solomon builds the Hall of Justice, where Solomon sits on his throne to judge the people of Israel. Solomon covers the entire Hall of Justice, from the floor to the ceiling, with cedar wood. Solomon builds his own palace and a palace for Solomon's wife, the Pharaoh's daughter, in a similar way.

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing the workers, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We feel honored to help Solomon build these important buildings; or
  • We believe other countries will show us respect because we have such great resources to build a beautiful temple and palace.

Continue the drama.

Solomon uses high-quality stone to construct every building in the palace complex, from the outside to the courtyard and from the bottom to the top. Workers cut these stones to size and smooth their faces and edges. Solomon uses large, high-quality stones that are 8 to 10 cubits across to build a foundation. On top of the foundation, Solomon lays high-quality stones that workers cut to the right size for windows and cedar beams. Solomon surrounds the large courtyard with a wall that people make from three layers of cut stone and one layer of cedar beams. Solomon had also built the wall for the inner courtyard of the temple in the same way.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I rejoice in the beauty of my palace; or
  • I feel confident that the people of Israel will respect me even more when they see my beautiful palace next to the temple I built.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 7:1–12 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Solomon took 13 years to completely build his palace. In the original language, the storyteller says Solomon built "Solomon's own house," but we know it was a special house for kings. A palace is a large house for a king or other important ruler. Solomon constructed his palace and four other buildings to create a palace complex. Use the same word or phrase for palace as you used in previous passages.

Stop here and look at a picture of a palace in the same area and time as a group. Pause this audio here.

Solomon built the Palace of the Forest of Lebanon. A forest is a large area of land where many trees grow close together to cover the whole area.

Stop here and look at a picture of a forest of cedar trees. Discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for forest. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

The Palace of the Forest of Lebanon was 100 cubits long, 50 cubits wide, and 30 cubits high. People use a cubit to measure length. One cubit is the distance between a man's elbow and the tips of his fingers, or about 50 centimeters. Use the same word or phrase for cubit as you used in previous passages. For more information on cubit, refer to the Master Glossary.

Solomon's workers built columns that they made of cedar in the Palace of the Forest of Lebanon and in the colonnade, which is a row of columns. A column is a pillar that stands upright. Columns are usually cylindrical in shape and sometimes support other parts of a building. Use the same words for cedar wood as you have used in previous passages.

Stop here and discuss as a group what words or phrases you will use for column and for colonnade. Look at a picture of a column. If you have already translated these words in another book of the Bible, use the same words that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Solomon built a portico in front of the colonnade. A portico is an open structure with no walls and with columns that support a roof. Use the same word or phrase for portico as you used in previous passages.

Solomon placed his throne in the Hall of Justice. A throne is a ceremonial chair for a king. Use the same word or phrase for throne as you used in previous passages.

Solomon would judge, or make decisions about what was right or wrong, in the Hall of Justice. Solomon called it the Hall of Justice because that is the place where he would judge or make decisions fairly. Use the same words for justice and for judge as you have used in previous passages. Refer to justice and judge in the Master Glossary for more information.

Solomon made a palace for himself and a palace for his wife, the daughter of Pharaoh. Pharaoh is what people called the king of the country called Egypt. Use the same word or phrase for Pharaoh as you used in previous passages. For more information on Pharaoh, refer to the Master Glossary.

Solomon used high-quality, or very hard, stone to build every structure, from the foundation to the eaves, or the top of the roof. Use the same word or phrase for foundation as you have used in previous passages.

Solomon built the wall around the large courtyard out of layers of cut stone and cedar beams. Solomon built this wall in a similar way to the way he built the wall around the inner courtyard of the temple of Yahweh. Use the same word for courtyard that you have used in previous passages.

The temple is the place where the Israelites worshiped God. Yahweh is God's personal name for himself. Use the same words or phrases for temple and for Yahweh as you used in previous passages. For more information on temple and Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

acai.preferred_label: House of the Forest of Lebanon

title: 1 Kings 7:13–22
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 7:13–22 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 7:13–22 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 7:13–22 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous story, the storyteller interrupted the details about how Solomon built the temple to talk about Solomon's palace complex. Solomon built five buildings south of the temple, including the Palace of the Forest of Lebanon, a Hall of Columns, the Hall of Justice, a palace for Solomon, and a palace for the daughter of Pharaoh, who was Solomon's wife.

In this passage, the storyteller returns to talking about how Solomon builds and furnishes the temple. King Solomon sends a message to the city of Tyre, a city on the coast of the Mediterranean Sea. Solomon wants a man named Huram to help Solomon with the temple. In some translations, people call this man "Hiram," but this is not the same Hiram who is the king of Tyre. Huram's mother came from the Israelite tribe of Naphtali, but Huram's father had died. Huram's father had lived in Tyre and had been a craftsman who used bronze. Bronze is a metal that people make from copper and tin. Huram is a very skilled craftsman who works with bronze. Huram has lots of experience creating different types of objects from bronze. Huram travels from Tyre to Jerusalem and comes to work for Solomon. Huram completes every task that Solomon gives Huram.

Stop here and listen as a group: If you have already translated 2 Chronicles 3:15–17, which contains the same information, listen to your version of that passage now. Pause this audio here.

Look at a map that includes the cities of Tyre and Jerusalem. Pause this audio here.

Huram casts two bronze pillars. First, Huram heats copper and tin in a fire until it becomes so hot that it melts together to become bronze. Then Huram pours this hot liquid bronze into a mold or a shape of the pillar that people had dug into the ground. Then Huram waits for the bronze to cool down so that it hardens into the final shape of the pillar. Each pillar is 18 cubits or 9 meters high and 12 cubits or 6 meters around. You will remember from previous passages that a person uses a cubit to describe the length of an object, and a cubit measures about 50 centimeters. These pillars probably stood alone and did not support any part of the temple walls or roof. From another passage in the Bible, we know that these pillars are probably hollow or empty on the inside, and that the walls of these pillars that Huram makes from bronze metal are about four fingers thick. During this time, people used fingers to measure objects. Four fingers are about the width of a hand, or about 7.5 to 8 centimeters.

Stop here and look at a picture of bronze metal as a group. Pause this audio here.

Huram creates two capitals or tops to sit on top of each pillar. Huram makes these capitals out of bronze as well. Each capital measures 5 cubits or 2.5 meters tall. Huram hangs seven bronze chains on each capital for decoration. These chains may look like braids of metal that Huram twists together. Huram creates bronze objects that he shapes to look like pomegranate fruit. Pomegranates are reddish fruits about the size of a human fist. Huram puts these bronze objects in two rows around the chains to decorate each of the capitals.

Now, the storyteller gives more details about how Huram decorates the two large pillars. Huram molds the capitals or tops of each pillar in the shape of a lily flower. A lily is a red or white flower with four large petals. Each capital may look like a bronze bowl with bronze petals like a flower, or Huram may shape the capital like a bowl and carve petals into it.

Now the storyteller says that the capitals are 4 cubits or 2 meters high. Earlier, the storyteller said the capitals are 5 cubits high. This may mean that Huram makes two capitals on top of each other, one that measures 5 cubits high and one that measures 4 cubits high. The capitals sit on top of the pillars at the entrance to the temple. On the capital of each pillar, above the bowl-shaped part next to the network of chains, Huram places 200 bronze pomegranates in rows that go around the capital. Even though the storyteller gives details about the capitals, we are still not certain exactly how the capitals looked.

Stop here as a group and look at a picture of a metal decorative chain or braid, a pomegranate, and a picture of lilies. Pause this audio here.

Huram places the pillars in front of the entrance to Yahweh's temple. Huram names the two pillars. Huram names the pillar on the right or south side Jakin, and Huram names the pillar on the left or north side Boaz. The temple faces to the east, so these pillars sit on each side of the entrance to the temple. The name Jakin means, "Yahweh will establish," and the name Boaz means, "in Yahweh's strength." We are not sure why Solomon asks Huram to build the pillars, but the pillars may remind the people of Yahweh, who established the throne of King David and David's descendants and whose strength causes the king to rejoice.

So, Huram completes the work on the two pillars for Solomon.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 7:13–22 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has two scenes.

In the first scene: Solomon sends a message to Tyre and asks for a man named Huram to come to Jerusalem. Huram has great skill and experience in working with bronze, and Huram comes to work for King Solomon.

In the second scene: Huram casts two pillars out of bronze. Huram places capitals on top of each pillar. Huram makes both pillars the same height and width. Huram places chains and rows of bronze pomegranates on each capital for decoration. Huram shapes each capital like a lily flower. Huram names the pillar to the south Jakin, and Huram names the pillar to the north Boaz. Huram places the pillars in front of the entrance to the temple. And so Huram finishes the work on the pillars.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Solomon
  • The workers who helped Huram
  • And Huram

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, Solomon had already sent a message to Tyre. Solomon had asked a man named Huram to come and help Solomon complete the temple. Huram's mother came from the Israelite tribe of Naphtali. Huram's father, who came from Tyre, had died. Huram's father was a bronze craftsman, so Huram had great skill and knowledge about working with bronze. Huram knew how to make many objects out of bronze. Huram had agreed to help Solomon and had come to Jerusalem.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Solomon had already brought Huram to Jerusalem before the rest of this story happens. What words will help your audience understand that this part of the story has already happened? Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, Huram casts two pillars out of bronze. You will remember that people make bronze when they heat tin and copper together in a very hot fire so that it melts to create a different metal. Each pillar measures 18 cubits or 9 meters high and 12 cubits or 6 meters around. Huram makes a bronze capital for the top of each pillar. These capitals each measure 4 or 5 cubits high. On each pillar, Huram makes seven chains and weaves or braids those chains together at the top around the capital. Huram shapes the capitals like lily flowers. Huram also makes bronze objects that look like pomegranate fruit. Huram places the bronze pomegranates in two rows around each capital. Huram places 200 bronze pomegranates on each pillar on top of the bowl-shaped part and next to the network of bronze chains.

Stop here and discuss as a group: The storyteller's language can be difficult to understand or to picture because it is not very clear. How will you describe the capitals on top of the pillars in a way that your audience can understand? Pause this audio here.

Huram places each pillar with its lily-shaped capital in front of the entrance to Yahweh's temple. Huram names the pillar on the south side Jakin, which means "Yahweh will establish." Huram names the pillar on the north side Boaz, which means "in Yahweh's strength." And so Huram completes the work on the bronze pillars at the entrance to the temple.

Stop here and look at a drawing of what these pillars may have looked like as a group. Discuss what words you will use to describe the names of the pillars. How will you help your audience understand why the names of the pillars are important? Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 7:13–22 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has two scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Solomon
  • The workers who helped Huram
  • And Huram

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Solomon sends a message to Tyre and asks for a man named Huram to come to Jerusalem. Huram's mother comes from the tribe of Naphtali, but Huram's father, who was a craftsman who worked with bronze, has died. Huram has great skill and experience working with bronze, and Huram comes to work for King Solomon.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Huram, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel honored to help Solomon build a temple for Yahweh; or
  • I feel surprised that Solomon would choose me to help him because my father was not born in Israel.

Continue the drama.

Huram casts two pillars out of bronze. Each pillar is 18 cubits or 9 meters high and 12 cubits or 6 meters around. Huram makes a bronze capital for the top of each pillar. These capitals each measure 4 or 5 cubits high. Huram places a network of seven woven or braided chains on each capital on top of each pillar. Huram shapes the capitals like lily flowers. Huram makes bronze objects that look like pomegranate fruit. Huram places the bronze pomegranates in two rows around each capital. Huram places 200 bronze pomegranates on each pillar on top of the bowl-shaped part and next to the network of bronze chains.

Huram places each pillar with its lily-shaped capital in front of the entrance to Yahweh's temple. Huram names the pillar on the south side Jakin, which means "Yahweh will establish." Huram names the pillar on the north side Boaz, which means "in Yahweh's strength." And so Huram completes the work on the bronze pillars at the entrance to the temple.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Solomon "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel happy that these pillars will remind the Israelite people how Yahweh establishes our nation and gives strength to our people; or
  • I feel grateful that Huram can come to help make beautiful things in the temple.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 7:13–22 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Stop here and listen again as a group: If you have already translated 2 Chronicles 3:15–17, which contains the same information, listen to your version of that passage now. Pause this audio here.

Solomon sends a message to the city of Tyre. Tyre is a city in Phoenicia, a region located north of Israel on the coast of the Mediterranean Sea. Today, this area is a country called Lebanon. Look up Tyre in the Master Glossary for more information, and translate Tyre in the same way you have in previous passages.

Stop here and look at a map that includes the city of Tyre. Pause this audio here.

Solomon calls Huram. Huram's mother is a widow. A widow is a woman whose husband has died.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for widow. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Solomon wants Huram, a craftsman who is very skilled at working with bronze, to come help build the temple. Bronze is a metal that people make when they combine copper and tin. Use the same word or phrase for bronze as you used in previous passages. For more information on bronze, refer to the Master Glossary.

Huram casts two bronze pillars. Huram pours liquid metal into a mold or shape of the final object Huram desires. Then Huram allows the metal to cool.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for cast. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Huram molds two pillars, or tall vertical structures. Use the same word or phrase for pillar as you used in previous passages.

Stop here and look at a drawing of two pillars as a group. Pause this audio here.

Each pillar was 18 cubits high and 12 cubits around. A person uses a cubit to describe the length of an object. A cubit measures about as long as the distance between a man's elbow and his fingertips, or 50 centimeters. Use the same word or phrase for cubit as you used in previous passages. For more information on cubit, refer to the Master Glossary.

Huram made a capital for each pillar. A "capital" is special language that refers to an object on the top of a pillar or column.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for capital. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Huram decorates the capitals with a network of chains and with rows of pomegranates. These chains probably look like bronze twisted into a decorative braid. A pomegranate is a reddish fruit that is about the size of an orange.

Stop here and look at a picture of a metal chain or braid and a picture of a pomegranate as a group. Discuss what word or phrase you will use for chain and for pomegranate. If you have already translated these words in another book of the Bible, use the same words that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Huram shaped the capitals to look like lilies. Lilies are large red or white flowers with four petals.

Stop here and look at a picture of a lily as a group. Discuss what word or phrase you will use for lilies. If you have translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 7:23–39
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 7:23–39 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 7:23–39 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 7:23–39 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous passage, Solomon had asked Huram, the craftsman from Tyre, to help with the temple. Huram cast two large bronze pillars that he placed in front of the entrance to the temple. Huram decorated the pillars with bronze pomegranates and networks of chains. Huram completed this part of the temple.

In this passage, Huram continues to make items out of bronze for the temple.

Stop here and listen as a group: If you have already translated 2 Chronicles 3:15–4:10, which contains some of the same information, listen to your translation of that passage. Pause this audio here.

Next, Huram makes a large, round bronze bowl to hold water. People call this very large bowl the Sea. The Sea measures 10 cubits or 5 meters across the top, and it is 5 cubits or 2.5 meters high. Workers use a line of string that measures 30 cubits or 15 meters to measure around the Sea. Workers often used a line that people made of string to measure the distance around an object. You will remember from previous passages that people used cubits to measure the length of an object. Under the rim or edge of the Sea, Huram places bronze decorations in the shape of small gourds. A gourd is a fruit that grows in Israel. Gourds resemble melons or pumpkins, and people cut gourds in half lengthwise and use them to dip liquids. Huram uses 10 bronze gourds for every cubit or 20 bronze gourds for every meter. Huram arranges the gourds in two rows and uses the same mold to create the Sea and the gourds all at the same time.

Stop here and look at a picture of a gourd. Pause this audio here.

The Sea rests on top of 12 statues that look like bulls. A bull is a large animal with four legs. These bulls were probably oxen, which means they had been castrated. People often used oxen to help with farming. But people also killed bulls to eat or as sacrifices in the temple. The bulls face outward or away from each other and the bowl. Three bulls face north. Three bulls face east. Three bulls face south. And three bulls face west. The Sea sits on the flat backs of the bulls. We are not sure if Huram makes the bulls out of bronze or out of stone. The storyteller also does not tell us how big Huram makes the bulls, but we know Huram makes them big enough to support the Sea. The bulls may represent the 12 tribes of Israel.

The wall of the Sea is about a handbreadth, or four fingers thick. People used a handbreadth, or 7.5 centimeters, to measure distance. Huram makes the rim of the Sea curve outward like the rim of a cup or the petals of a lily flower. A lily is a large flower with white or red petals that curve outward. The Sea holds 2,000 baths of water. We are not sure exactly how much liquid a bath might measure, but the Sea probably holds around 40,000 liters. We know from another passage in the Bible that priests use the Sea to wash themselves.

Stop here and look at a picture of a bull. Look at a picture of a lily flower. Pause this audio here.

Next, Huram makes 10 bronze stands to hold water bowls. Each stand measures 4 cubits or 2 meters long, 4 cubits or 2 meters wide, and 3 cubits or 1.5 meters high. People can move these stands from one place to another within the temple. Huram makes each stand with bronze panels that attach to frames. The panels form the shape of a box with frames around each panel. Huram engraves figures of lions, bulls, and cherubim into the bronze panels and the frames. A lion is a large cat that eats other animals. People used lions to symbolize things like victory. A cherub is a type of angel, and cherubim are more than one cherub. Huram engraves decorative circular patterns or wreaths into the metal above and below the figures.

Stop here and look at a picture of a lion and cherubim as a group. Look at a decorative spiral pattern. Look at a picture of a stand that can move, similar to the one Huram makes. Pause this audio here.

Huram makes four bronze wheels attached to bronze axles at the bottom of each stand. The axle is a rod that goes through the center of each wheel and allows the wheel to turn. Each stand holds a water basin with four short bronze legs decorated with wreaths or circular patterns. Huram places an opening inside each frame to support a water basin. Huram makes the opening round, and the distance across the opening is 1.5 cubits or about 75 centimeters. Around the opening, Huram molds a circular frame that looks like a king's crown. This frame measures one cubit or one-half meter deep. Huram engraves designs around the crown. However, the metal panels that hold up the water basins are square, not round. Huram probably places these panels underneath the opening for the water basin.

Huram probably attaches the four wheels to the four corners of the bottom bronze panel. Then Huram attaches the axles to each stand. Each wheel measures 1.5 cubits or about 75 centimeters across. Huram builds the wheels to look like small chariot wheels. A chariot is a vehicle with two wheels that horses would pull, and people used chariots during wars. Huram makes the entire wheel, including the axles, rims, spokes, and hubs, out of bronze. The rim of a wheel is its outer edge. The spokes are rods that connect the hub, or central part of the wheel, to the rim.

Stop here and look at a picture of chariot wheels as a group. Note the axle, rim, spokes, and hub. Pause this audio here.

Huram makes a handle for each corner of each stand. Huram makes the stand and handles all at the same time from the same mold. People probably use the handles to push or pull the stands. Huram makes a circular support band for the top of each stand that measures one-half cubit or about 25 centimeters high. Huram makes the upper supports for each stand together with the panels in one piece. Huram decorates the supports and panels with figures of cherubim, lions, and palm trees. Palm trees have one large trunk with leaves that branch out on top. Huram covers every part of the stands that people can see with decorations, including wreaths. Huram creates each of the 10 stands in the same way. Huram uses the same mold for each stand, so the stands have the same size and shape.

Huram also makes 10 water basins out of bronze. Each basin holds 40 baths or about 1,000 liters of water and measures 4 cubits or 2 meters across the rim. One water basin sits on the top of each bronze stand. Huram puts five stands on the south side of the temple and five stands on the north side of the temple. Huram places the Sea on the southeast corner of the temple.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 7:23–39 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has three scenes.

In the first scene: Huram casts the large, round bronze water tank called the Sea and decorates it with gourds. Huram places the Sea on 12 figures that look like oxen bulls. Three bulls face in each direction. The rim of the Sea flares out like a lily flower or the rim of a cup.

In the second scene: Huram also makes 10 movable bronze stands to hold water basins. Huram decorates the stands with figures of lions, bulls, cherubim, and wreaths. Huram makes four bronze wheels with axles for each stand. Huram places a round opening like a crown inside each frame to support a water basin. Huram attaches the wheels to each stand.

In the third scene: Huram places handles at the corners of each stand. Huram decorates every part of the stands with engravings and wreaths. Each stand has the same size and shape. Huram also makes 10 water basins, one for each stand. Huram places the water basins and the Sea outside the temple.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Solomon
  • Yahweh
  • The workers who help Huram
  • And Huram

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, Huram casts a large, round water tank out of bronze. People call the tank the Sea. The tank measures 10 cubits across from rim to rim and is 5 cubits high. Huram measures the distance around the Sea to be 30 cubits. Below the rim of the Sea, Huram casts two rows of bronze gourds. Huram places 10 gourds for every cubit around the Sea. Huram casts the Sea and the gourds all at once from the same mold.

Huram makes 12 figures of bulls. We do not know the size of the bulls, but they are large enough to support the Sea. Huram makes the bulls out of bronze or stone. Huram places the Sea on the backs of the bulls. Three bulls face in each direction, north, south, east, and west. East is the direction that the sun rises. The Sea sits on the bulls' backs. The hindquarters of the bulls all face each other. Huram makes the Sea with a rim like a cup or a lily blossom. The bronze that makes the Sea is about a handbreadth or four fingers thick. The Sea holds 2,000 baths of liquid. We do not know the exact measure of a bath, but the Sea probably holds around 40,000 liters of water.

Stop here and discuss as a group: How do you measure the size and shape of things in your culture? What words will help you communicate the size and shape of these objects to your audience? Pause this audio here.

Discuss as a group how you will talk about north, south, east, and west. Remember that east is the direction that the sun rises from. West is the opposite direction of east. Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, Huram makes 10 movable stands out of bronze. Each stand measures 4 cubits long, 4 cubits wide, and 3 cubits high. Huram uses bronze panels that he attaches to a frame, so the stand looks like a box with frames around each panel. Huram decorates each panel with figures of lions, bulls, and cherubim. Huram also decorates the frames with wreaths or circular patterns.

Stop here and discuss as a group: How do people decorate important objects in your culture? Pause this audio here.

Huram puts four wheels with axles at the bottom of each stand. At each of the four corners of each stand, Huram places bronze supports like legs to hold a water basin. Huram also decorates these supports with wreaths. Huram makes an opening at the top of the frame of each cart. Huram makes the opening round, and the distance across the opening is 1.5 cubits. Around the opening, Huram molds a circular frame that looks like a crown. This frame measures one cubit deep. Huram decorates the area around the crown opening with more circular patterns. Even though the opening for the water basin is round, the metal panel that holds up the water basin is square.

Huram probably attaches the four wheels to the four corners of the bottom bronze panel. Then Huram attaches the axles to each stand. Each wheel measures 1.5 cubits across. Huram builds the wheels to look like small chariot wheels. Huram makes the entire wheel, including the axles, rims, spokes, and hubs, out of bronze.

Stop here and discuss as a group: How do you talk about objects that look like other objects in your culture? How will you describe an opening that looks like a crown or wheels like a chariot? Pause this audio here.

In the third scene, Huram makes a handle for each corner of each stand. Huram makes the stand and handles all at the same time from the same mold. Huram makes a circular support band for the top of each stand that measures one-half cubit high. Huram makes the upper supports for each stand together with the bronze panels in one piece. Huram decorates the supports and panels with figures of cherubim, lions, and palm trees. Huram covers every part of the stands that people can see with decorations that include wreaths or circular patterns. Huram creates each of the 10 stands in the same way. Huram uses the same mold for each stand, so all of the stands are the same size and shape.

Huram also makes 10 water basins out of bronze. Each basin probably holds around 1,000 liters of water, and each basin measures 4 cubits across the rim. One water basin sits on top of each bronze movable stand. Huram puts five stands with basins outside the south side of the temple, and Huram puts five stands with basins outside the north side of the temple. Huram puts the Sea outside the southeast corner of the temple.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 7:23–39 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has three scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Solomon
  • Yahweh
  • The workers who help Huram
  • And Huram

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Huram casts a large, round water tank out of bronze. Huram calls the tank the Sea. The tank measures 10 cubits across from rim to rim and is 5 cubits high. Huram measures the distance around the Sea to be 30 cubits. Below the rim of the Sea, Huram casts two rows of gourds. Huram places 10 gourds for every cubit around the Sea. Huram casts the Sea and the gourds all at once from the same mold. Huram makes 12 figures like bulls to hold the Sea. Three bulls face in each direction, and the Sea sits on the bulls' backs. The Sea is about four fingers thick, has a rim like a cup or a lily flower, and holds around 40,000 liters of water.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am happy that the priests will be able to wash themselves in something as beautiful as the Sea; or
  • I feel hopeful that Yahweh will bless Huram's work.

Continue the drama.

Huram makes 10 movable stands out of bronze. Each stand is 4 cubits long, 4 cubits wide, and 3 cubits high. Huram uses bronze panels that he attaches to a frame of supports. Huram decorates each panel with figures of lions, bulls, and cherubim. Huram also decorates the frames with wreaths or circular patterns.

Huram puts four wheels with axles at the bottom of each stand. At each of the four corners of each stand, Huram places bronze supports like legs to hold a water basin. Huram also decorates these supports with wreaths. Huram makes an opening at the top of the frame of each cart. Huram makes the opening round, and the distance across the opening is 1.5 cubits. Around the opening, Huram molds a circular frame that looks like a crown. This opening measures one cubit deep. Huram decorates the area around the crown opening with more circular patterns. Even though the opening for the water basin is round, the metal panel that holds up the water basin is square.

Huram probably attaches the four wheels to the four corners of the bottom bronze panel. Then Huram attaches the axles to each stand. Each wheel measures 1.5 cubits across. Huram builds the wheels to look like small chariot wheels. Huram makes the entire wheel, including the axles, rims, spokes, and hubs, out of bronze.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Huram, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel confident that Solomon will like the beautiful objects I make out of bronze; or
  • I feel pleased with the details of my decorations on the objects I make for the temple.

Continue the drama.

Huram makes a handle for each corner of each stand. Huram makes the stand and handles all at the same time from the same mold. Huram makes a circular support band for the top of each stand that measures one-half cubit high. Huram makes the upper supports for each stand together with the panels in one piece. Huram decorates the supports and panels with figures of cherubim, lions, and palm trees. Huram covers every part of the stands that people can see with decorations that include wreaths. Huram creates each of the 10 stands in the same way.

Huram also makes 10 water basins out of bronze. Each basin probably holds around 1,000 liters of water, and each basin measures 4 cubits across the rim. One water basin sits on top of each bronze movable stand. Huram puts five stands with basins outside the south side of the temple, and Huram puts five stands with basins outside the north side of the temple. Huram puts the Sea outside the southeast corner of the temple.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am happy that Solomon and Huram want my temple to be beautiful; or
  • I am pleased that Huram wants to use his knowledge about working with bronze to make my temple so beautiful.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 7:23–39 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Huram casts a large, round bronze bowl to hold water, and people call it the Sea. People pour liquid metal into a mold to cast the metal into a particular shape. Use the same word or phrase for cast as you used in previous passages.

Huram makes the Sea measure 10 cubits from rim to rim, 5 cubits high, and 30 cubits around. People use cubits to measure length. A cubit is the distance from a man's elbow to the tips of his fingers and measures about 50 centimeters. Use the same word or phrase for cubit as you used in previous passages. For more information on cubit, refer to the Master Glossary.

Huram decorates the Sea under its rim with metal objects that look like gourds. A gourd is a fruit that grows in Israel. Gourds resemble melons or pumpkins, and people cut gourds in half lengthwise and use them to dip or hold liquids like a cup or a bowl. Use the same word or phrase for gourd as you used in previous passages.

Stop here and look at a picture of a gourd as a group. Pause this audio here.

Huram places the sea on the backs of 12 statues of bulls. These bulls were probably oxen, which are large animals with four legs. People often used bulls to help with farming. But people also killed bulls to eat or as sacrifices in the temple.

Stop here and look at a picture of oxen as a group. Discuss what word or phrase you will use for bull. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the word or phrase that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Huram makes the bronze walls of the Sea a handbreadth thick. People used a handbreadth, or about four fingers or 7.5 centimeters, to measure distance.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for handbreadth. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Huram makes the rim or edge of the Sea like the rim of a cup or like a lily flower. A lily is a large flower with white or red petals that curve outward. Use the same word or phrase for lily as you used in previous passages.

Stop here and look at a picture of a lily flower as a group. Pause this audio here.

Huram makes the Sea large enough to hold 2,000 baths of water, which is about 40,000 liters. People used baths to measure the volume of liquid.

Stop here and discuss as a group how you will talk about these measurements. If you use the idea of a bath, decide what word or phrase you will use for bath. If you have used the word for bath in 1 Kings 5:11, use the same word you used there. Pause this audio here.

Huram makes the Sea and the 10 movable stands out of bronze, which is a metal that people make from copper and tin. Use the same word or phrase for bronze as you used in previous passages. For more information on bronze, refer to the Master Glossary.

Huram decorates the frames and panels of the movable stands with pictures of lions, bulls, and cherubim. A lion is a large carnivorous cat that people used to symbolize various things, like victory. A cherub is a type of angel that guards holy places, and cherubim are more than one cherub. Use the same word or phrase for cherubim as you used in previous passages. For more information on cherubim, refer to the Master Glossary.

Stop here and look at a picture of a lion and cherubim as a group. Discuss what word or phrase you will use for lion. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word or phrase as you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Huram places four bronze wheels on the bottom of each stand. The wheels looked like small chariot wheels with axles, rims, spokes, and hubs made from bronze. The axle is a rod that goes through the center of each wheel and allows the wheel to turn. The rim of a wheel is its outer edge. The spokes are rods that connect the hub, or the central solid part of the wheel, to the rim.

Stop here and look at a picture of a wheel including its axle, rim, spokes, and hub. Look at a picture of a chariot with its wheels. Discuss as a group what words or phrases you will use for axle, rim, spokes, and hub. If you have already translated these words in another book of the Bible, use the same words or phrases you have used there. Use the same word or phrase for chariot as you used in previous passages.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 7:40–51
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 7:40–51 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 7:40–51 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 7:40–51 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous passage, King Solomon directed Huram the craftsman to make the Sea that sits on the backs of the 12 large bulls. Huram also made 10 water basins and movable stands. Huram made all these things out of bronze metal.

In this passage, the storyteller gives a list of the furnishings, or the furniture and decorations, for the temple. Solomon directs Huram to make some objects out of bronze. Solomon directs other workers to make objects out of gold.

Stop here and listen as a group: If you have already translated 2 Chronicles 4:11–5:1, which contains the same information, listen to your translation now. Pause this audio here.

Huram makes small bowls for ashes, shovels, and small basins. People use the small bowls to carry ashes from the altar where people burn sacrifices. A shovel is a tool with a broad, flat blade and a handle that may have been just over one-half meter long. People use small shovels to move hot coals and to scoop ashes from the altar. People use small basins to catch the blood from animal sacrifices. These basins are probably a little bigger than the bowls people use to carry ashes. People worship Yahweh by making sacrifices on the altar, and then they sprinkle the blood from the basins onto the altar, so people call these basins sprinkling basins.

Stop here and look at a picture of a small bowl, a small shovel, and a small basin. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller gives a summary of all the work King Solomon gives Huram to complete for Yahweh's temple. This is what Huram makes out of bronze. Huram makes the two bronze pillars with the bowl-shaped capitals on top. You will remember that people make capitals as decorative tops for a pillar or column. Huram also makes the braided chains that decorate the capitals. Huram makes 400 images of pomegranates. Huram uses two rows of pomegranates to decorate each chain on the bowl-shaped capitals, one on top of each pillar. You will remember that a pomegranate is a reddish fruit about the size of a human fist. Huram makes 10 movable stands, and each stand holds a water basin. Huram makes the Sea and the 12 figures that look like bulls that hold up the Sea. Huram makes the bowls for ashes, shovels, and sprinkling basins. Huram makes each of these items out of bronze that people have rubbed to make it shiny, just as King Solomon directed him to do. People use these objects in Yahweh's temple.

Stop here and look at a picture of a pillar, a capital, chains, a pomegranate, a wash basin in a movable stand, and a bull as a group. Pause this audio here.

King Solomon orders his workers to make these objects out of bronze. The workers melt the bronze until it becomes liquid. Then the workers dig molds out of clay in the ground and pour the liquid bronze into the clay molds. When the bronze cools, it hardens into the shape of the mold. The workers do this in the lower plain of the Jordan River between the towns of Succoth and Zarethan. The plain of the Jordan refers to the lower valley of the Jordan River, which is south of the Dead Sea. Succoth was a town east of the Jordan River and west of the Jabbok River. Zarethan was a town east of the Jordan River, but we do not know exactly where it may have been. This land contained a lot of clay that workers used to make clay molds. Solomon does not weigh the objects Huram makes out of bronze because Huram makes so many objects. So, no one knows how much bronze people used to make the objects for the temple.

Stop here and look at a map of the valley of the Jordan River as a group. Find the cities of Succoth and Zarethan. Pause this audio here.

King Solomon also directs workers to make all the gold furnishings for the inside of Yahweh's temple. Solomon makes the gold altar for incense. Solomon makes the gold table where people put the bread of the Presence. Every week on the Sabbath day, or religious day of rest, people put fresh bread on the table as an offering to Yahweh. Workers probably make the altar and the table out of wood and cover the wood with good-quality gold. Solomon makes 10 lampstands out of high-quality gold and places five lampstands inside the main hall of the temple on each side of the door that goes into the Most Holy Place. The lampstands probably have a cylinder shape with tops and bottoms that flare outward. Solomon makes gold flowers that decorate the lampstands. Solomon also makes lamps and gold tongs for the fire. People probably make the lamps out of ceramic, and the lamps have a wick made of flax that uses olive oil as fuel. Tongs are a tool with two arms joined at one end that people can use to pick things up. People probably use gold tongs to trim wicks or to remove burned wicks from the lamps.

Stop here as a group and look at a picture of the altar, a lampstand with a lamp, and a pair of tongs. Pause this audio here.

Solomon also makes other objects out of high-quality gold, including basins, wick trimmers or shears that people use to cut wicks, sprinkling basins, dishes to carry incense, and censers or firepans to carry burning incense. Solomon also makes sockets or hinges out of bronze and covers them with gold. Solomon uses these sockets for the doors that lead into the Most Holy Place and for the doors that lead into the main hall of Yahweh's temple. People use sockets to help doors swing open or shut.

Stop here and look at a picture of a wick trimmer, a censer, and gold sockets or hinges as a group. Pause this audio here.

When King Solomon finishes all his work for Yahweh's temple, Solomon gathers all the things his father David had dedicated to Yahweh. David had given various valuable articles to Yahweh. People made some of these articles from silver and gold. Solomon puts all these things in the treasure room, or the rooms where Solomon keeps valuable treasures, inside Yahweh's temple.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Where might people keep valuable treasures in your culture?

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 7:40–51 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has two scenes.

In the first scene: Solomon had asked Huram to make several items out of polished bronze for Yahweh's temple, and Huram finishes that work. Workmen pour liquid bronze into clay molds in the ground in the plain of the Jordan River Valley. Solomon does not ask anyone to weigh the bronze, so no one knows how much bronze Huram uses.

In the second scene: Solomon's workers make the furnishings for Yahweh's temple out of high-quality gold. When Solomon knows the workers have finished, Solomon puts all the articles, including silver and gold that King David had dedicated to Yahweh, in the treasure room of the temple.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Huram
  • Solomon
  • Solomon's workers
  • And Yahweh

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, Huram makes objects out of bronze for people to use in Yahweh's temple. Huram makes small bowls for ashes, small shovels, and small basins for sprinkling blood.

King Solomon had directed Huram to make these items for Yahweh's temple out of bronze, and Huram completed the work. In this passage, the storyteller gives a summary of all the work Huram completed for Yahweh's temple. The storyteller has described most of these items already in previous passages.

Huram made the two large pillars that stand at the entrance to the temple. On top of each pillar rests a bowl-shaped capital, or decorative top. Huram decorated the capitals with chains. Huram placed two rows of chains on each capital. Huram decorated the chains with images that look like pomegranates. Pomegranates are reddish fruits about the size of a human fist. In another passage in the Bible, the storyteller says Huram made 400 images of pomegranates. Huram used two rows of pomegranates to decorate each chain on the bowl-shaped capitals, and Huram placed one capital on top of each pillar.

Huram also made 10 water basins on 10 movable stands. People used these basins to wash the things they used for burnt offerings. Huram made a large water tank called the Sea, and Huram made the 12 images that look like bulls that support the Sea. Priests use the Sea to wash themselves. Huram made the small bowls for ashes, the small shovels, and the small basins for sprinkling blood. Huram made all these objects out of bronze that workers polish until they look shiny.

Stop here and discuss as a group: The storyteller uses this passage to give a summary, or a list, of what Huram makes. Listen to your translation of 1 Kings 7:13–39, where the storyteller already talked about these objects that Huram made. Use this passage to remember how you already described these objects. Use the same words here for these objects as you used in previous passages. Pause this audio here.

Solomon had directed Huram and other workers to make these objects for Yahweh's temple. Solomon's workers cast the items by melting bronze and then pouring it into clay molds that the workers dug in the ground. The workers did this work in the plain of the Jordan River, which is the lower valley of the Jordan River between the towns of Succoth and Zarethan. Solomon did not ask any worker to weigh any of the bronze objects that Huram made because Huram made so many objects. So, no one knows how much bronze Huram and the workers used to make objects for Yahweh's temple.

Stop here and discuss as a group: How will you talk about Huram casting these objects in clay? Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, Solomon also makes the furnishings for the inside of Yahweh's temple out of pure or good-quality gold. However, even though the storyteller says Solomon makes these objects, Solomon does not actually do the work himself. Solomon directs his workers and craftsmen to make these objects out of gold.

Solomon makes the incense altar and the table that holds the bread of the Presence. Solomon makes 10 lampstands and puts five lampstands on each side of the entrance to the Most Holy Place. Solomon makes the gold flowers that decorate the lampstands, the lamps that go on the lampstands, and the tongs to trim or to remove the wicks from the lamps. Solomon makes bowls, wick trimmers, basins for sprinkling blood, dishes for incense, and censers to hold burning incense. Solomon makes sockets for the doors to the Most Holy Place and for the doors at the entrance to the main hall of the temple. Solomon probably makes these sockets out of bronze and covers the bronze with gold.

Stop here as a group and listen to your translation of Exodus 37:17–38:8. In this passage, Moses describes some similar objects that people made for the tabernacle. If you have already translated this passage, use your translation to help you talk about these objects that Huram made for the temple. Pause this audio here.

If it helps you, look again at a picture of a pillar, a capital, chains, a pomegranate, a wash basin in a movable stand, a bull, the altar, a lampstand with a lamp, a pair of tongs, a wick-trimmer, a censer, and gold sockets or hinges. Pause this audio here.

When Solomon makes all the objects for Yahweh's temple, Solomon brings all the gold and silver furnishings that his father, King David, had dedicated to Yahweh. Solomon puts these things in the treasure room in Yahweh's temple.

Stop here and discuss as a group: What kinds of furnishings do you use for holy places in your culture? How will you describe the special furnishings Solomon uses for Yahweh's temple? Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 7:40–51 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has two scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Huram
  • Solomon
  • Solomon's workers
  • And Yahweh

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Huram makes bowls for ashes, shovels, and small basins out of bronze. Now we hear a summary of the work King Solomon asks Huram to do. Huram makes several items out of bronze. Huram makes the two large pillars that stand at the entrance to the temple. On top of each pillar rests a bowl-shaped capital, or top. Huram decorates the capitals with chains. Huram places two rows of chains on each capital. Huram decorates the chains with images that look like pomegranates. Huram also makes 10 water basins on 10 movable stands. Huram makes a large water tank called the Sea, and Huram makes the 12 images like bulls that support the Sea.

Huram makes all these objects out of bronze that workers polish until they look shiny. Solomon's workers cast the items in clay molds that they dig in the ground. The workers do this work in the lower plain of the Jordan River between the towns of Succoth and Zarethan.

Solomon does not weigh any of the bronze objects that Huram makes because Huram makes so many objects. So, no one knows how much bronze Huram and the workers use to make objects for Yahweh's temple.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Huram, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel pleased when I see all the beautiful things I have made for Yahweh's temple; or
  • I feel happy that King Solomon chose me to help him complete Yahweh's temple.

Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel certain that Yahweh is pleased with Huram's work; or
  • I think the Israelite people and the other nations around us will feel impressed when they see Yahweh's temple.

Continue the drama.

Solomon also makes the furnishings for the inside of Yahweh's temple out of high-quality gold. Solomon makes the altar and the table that holds the bread of the Presence. Solomon makes 10 lampstands and puts five on each side of the entrance to the Most Holy Place. Solomon makes the gold flowers that decorate the lampstands, and Solomon makes the lamps and tongs. Solomon makes bowls, wick trimmers, basins for sprinkling blood, dishes for incense, and censers to hold burning incense. Solomon makes sockets for the doors to the Most Holy Place and for the doors at the entrance to the main hall of the temple.

When Solomon makes all the objects for Yahweh's temple, Solomon brings all the gold and silver furnishings that his father, King David, had dedicated to Yahweh. Solomon puts these things in the treasure room in Yahweh's temple.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel pleased to see the beautiful temple Solomon made for me to live in; or
  • I feel love for Solomon and for my people, the Israelites. The people have worked very hard to make me a beautiful place to live.

Ask the people playing Solomon's workers, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We feel weary because Solomon has required us to work very hard to build Yahweh's temple and to craft the objects in it; or
  • We feel happy that we have finished Yahweh's temple; or
  • We feel satisfied to see the beauty of Yahweh's temple.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 7:40–51 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Stop here and listen as a group: If you have already translated 2 Chronicles 4:11–5:1, which contains the same information, listen to your translation now. Pause this audio here.

Huram completes the items King Solomon asked him to make for Yahweh's temple. Yahweh is a name that God gives himself, and it reminds people of the covenant Yahweh made with the Israelites. Israelites worship Yahweh in the temple. Use the same words or phrases for king, Yahweh, and temple as you used in previous passages. For more information on Yahweh and temple, refer to the Master Glossary.

Huram makes two pillars for the front entrance of the temple. People usually make tall, vertical structures called pillars out of metal, wood, or stone. Pillars may help to support a building, or people may use pillars as decoration. Use the same word or phrase for pillar as you used in previous passages.

Stop here and look at a picture of a pillar as a group. Pause this audio here.

Huram puts a capital on the top of each pillar. The word capital is special language that people use to talk about the decorative top of a pillar in Solomon's temple. Huram shapes them to look like bowls and decorates the capitals with chains and pomegranates. People probably weave metal together in the shape of a braid to make these chains. Pomegranates are reddish fruits about the same size as a human's fist. Use the same words or phrases for capital, chains, and pomegranate as you used in previous passages.

Stop here and look at a picture of a capital, chains, and a pomegranate. Pause this audio here.

Huram makes 10 water basins in movable stands, the Sea and the bulls that support it, bowls and shovels for ashes, and small basins that hold blood from sacrifices. Huram makes all the objects for the temple out of bronze that people polish so that the objects look shiny. Bronze is a type of metal that people make from copper and tin. Use the same words or phrases for bulls and bronze as you used in previous passages. For more information on bronze, refer to the Master Glossary.

Huram and the other workers cast the bronze objects in the plain of the Jordan River between the towns of Succoth and Zarethan. The Jordan River is a long river that runs through Israel from north to south. Use the same word or phrase for Jordan River as you used in previous passages. For more information on Jordan River, refer to the Master Glossary.

Solomon makes the furnishings for Yahweh's temple. Furnishings include furniture and other items people use to decorate a building or a room. Use the same word or phrase for furnishings as you used in previous passages.

Solomon makes objects for Yahweh's temple out of gold. Gold is a precious yellow metal. Use the same word or phrase for gold as you used in previous passages.

Solomon makes the incense altar and the table for the bread of the Presence. An altar is a place where people make offerings or sacrifices to a god. The Israelites made sacrifices on the altar to Yahweh. Priests put 12 loaves of bread on a table just outside the Most Holy Place to remind the Israelites that Yahweh lives in the temple. The people call this the bread of the Presence. Use the same word or phrase for altar as you used in previous passages. For more information on altar, refer to the Master Glossary.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for bread of the Presence. For more information about "bread of the Presence," refer to the Master Glossary. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Solomon makes 10 lampstands and puts five on each side of the door to the Most Holy Place. People light wicks in lamps that they place on lampstands to provide light. Use the same word or phrase for lampstand as you used in previous passages.

Solomon makes tongs, wick trimmers, and censers for the temple. People make tools like tongs with two short arms joined at one end. People probably use tongs to pick things up or to remove burnt wicks. People probably make wicks out of flax that people can light with fire. People use wick trimmers to cut the wicks to keep the wicks from getting too long and burning too quickly. People use a censer to carry hot coals or burning incense.

Stop here and look at a picture of tongs, wick trimmers, and a censer. Discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for tongs, wick trimmer, and censer.
