If you have already translated these words in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Solomon makes sockets for the doors that go into the Most Holy Place and for the doors that lead into the main hall of the temple from the outside. People use sockets or hinges to help doors swing open and shut. People probably make sockets out of a stronger metal like bronze, and then Solomon covers them with gold. Use the same word or phrase for Most Holy Place as you have used in previous passages. Most Holy Place is in the Master Glossary.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for sockets. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Solomon places the items David dedicated to Yahweh in the treasuries in the temple. People put expensive and important gifts for Yahweh in the treasuries that Solomon made in Yahweh's temple.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for treasuries. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word or phrase that you have used there.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

resource.label: Bronze Basin and Stand in the Tabernacle
Bronze Basin and Stand in the Tabernacle
Bronze Basin and Stand in the Tabernacle

title: 1 Kings 8:1–11
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 8:1–11 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 8:1–11 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 8:1–11 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous story, King Solomon finished building and furnishing the temple. While the previous story shows all of the new items and parts of the temple, this story focuses more on items that King Solomon still needs to bring to the temple and how the Israelites move these items to the temple.

Stop here and review the photo of King Solomon's temple as a group. Pause this audio here.

King Solomon cannot complete the temple fully until the ark of the covenant, the Tent of Meeting, and any furnishings the priests use in the Tent of Meeting are in the temple. King Solomon asks the elders and heads or chiefs of the Israelite tribes to gather together and to join the people who will bring the ark of the covenant, the Tent of Meeting, and the furnishings to the temple. You will remember that the Tent of Meeting is the temporary tent that the Israelites used to meet with Yahweh before Solomon built the temple.

Stop here and look at a photo of the ark of the covenant as a group. Pause this audio here.

Stop and look at a photo or illustration of the Tent of Meeting and furnishings. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Think of a time when you were close to completing a task, but you had to wait for someone or something else to happen before you could finish. How did you feel? Discuss this question with the group. Pause this audio here.

It is important to pay attention to the people that King Solomon gathers together for this process. The term "elders" likely refers to the older men who oversee the people of Israel, and "heads" or "chiefs" likely means the men who are in charge of leading their households and different tribes. It is possible that these are all the same leaders, but it is also possible that there are different sets of leaders who help look after the people of Israel.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Think about the types of leadership in your community. Discuss as a group the different kinds of leaders and who takes care of the different groups of people in your community. Pause this audio here.

King Solomon wants the elders to walk with the people who will carry the ark, the Tent of Meeting, and the furnishings from Zion to King Solomon's location, north of Zion. People sometimes use Zion as another name for Jerusalem or the City of David. It is likely that the storyteller uses Zion here to show that King Solomon is moving the ark from King David's Jerusalem to the new location of the temple in a different place.

It is also important to notice the time of year when King Solomon starts this process. The storyteller says that King Solomon chooses the seventh month. The storyteller calls this month "Ethanim," which is a Canaanite name. This is unusual because the Israelites no longer [call this month by this name at the time the storyteller writes this book.]{.mark} However, the storyteller likely does this to show the time of year and month so that the reader can understand that the seventh month means the seventh month when Solomon finished the temple, and not the seventh month in the time that the storyteller wrote the story. The seventh month is likely the same as mid-September or October today.

At this time of year, the Israelites celebrated a feast called the Festival of Tabernacles. The Festival of Tabernacles was likely a perfect time for King Solomon, because the festival celebrates when the Israelites left Egypt and had to live in temporary shelters. The festival also celebrates how Yahweh gave the Israelites a permanent place to live after the Israelites wandered for years in the desert. The Festival of Tabernacles is a pilgrimage feast, which means that the tribes of Israel travel to Jerusalem for this festival. This also means that the elders, chiefs, and men of Israel are in Jerusalem at the time that King Solomon asks for them to come to the temple. It is likely that King Solomon finished the temple and then waited for this festival so that the people who were allowed to transport the ark of the covenant, the Tent of Meeting, and the furnishings could easily bring everything to the new temple. This also means that the people of Israel could all be at the temple together to see the dedication.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Think of celebrations or festivals you have in your community that help you remember specific days or events. How do you celebrate? Pause this audio here.

Only men from the tribe of Levi can carry the pieces from Zion to the new temple. The priests, who are from the tribe of Levi, are the only people Yahweh has allowed to carry the ark of the covenant. The Levites who are not priests are allowed to carry the Tent of Meeting and the furnishings from the Tent of Meeting. It is likely that King Solomon and the people of Israel traveled with the priests and other Levites. It is also likely that King Solomon and the people of Israel sacrificed cattle and sheep during the journey, as well as at the temple. The storyteller says that the people sacrificed so many cattle and sheep that they could not keep count of them all.

Stop here and look at pictures of cattle and sheep. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and look at a picture of what a priest looked like and the clothing they wore. Pause this audio here.

When the priests arrive at the temple, the priests carry the ark of the covenant into the temple's inner room, called the Most Holy Place. It is important to remember that there are two rooms within the building where the priests perform their priestly duties. The Holy Place is a room or space where all priests can go. Only the high priest can go into the Most Holy Place, which some people call the Inner Sanctuary.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a photo or diagram of the Holy Place and the Most Holy Place. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, what places or areas can only certain people go into? Pause this audio here.

The priests place the ark of the covenant under the wings of two cherubim angels that people made from olive wood and covered in gold. The cherubim are probably a larger version of the smaller cherubim that used to rest on the corners of the ark. The larger cherubim spread their wings over the entire ark and the carrying poles to show that this is Yahweh's resting place. The carrying poles for the ark are so long that a priest can see the poles as they are standing in the Holy Place, but not outside the Holy Place. This likely means that the poles press into the curtain that separates the Holy Place and the Most Holy Place. The storyteller says that the poles are still attached to the ark, but this likely means that the poles are there at the time the storyteller writes this. It is likely that the storyteller shares this section to help the reader visualize the new space of the temple.

Stop here and look at an illustration of cherubim. Also, look at an illustration of what the cherubim looked like in Solomon's temple. Pause this audio here.

It is important to remember that Moses put two tablets in the ark of the covenant. These flat stones represent the covenant that Yahweh made with the Israelites on Mount Horeb, also known as Mount Sinai. The storyteller says that these two tablets, which show God's covenant with Israel after they were free from Egypt, are the only items inside the ark of the covenant.

Stop here and look at an illustration of the two tablets that Moses put into the ark of the covenant. Pause this audio here.

After the priests place the ark of the covenant in the Most Holy Place and leave, a cloud fills the temple. The cloud is the presence and glory of Yahweh, and the cloud is so thick that the priests cannot carry out their priestly duties. It is important to remember that when Moses finished building the Tent of Meeting, a cloud covered the tent and Yahweh's glory filled the space. The storyteller likely uses this same description to show that Yahweh's presence is now in the new temple and that Yahweh is blessing the temple.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Think of a time when a room was so full that you could not go in. This could be a time when there were too many people in a room, or when a room was too hot or even filled with smoke. Imagine how it felt, and then discuss the cloud that filled the temple. Discuss how this might have felt and how you would feel.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 8:1–11 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has five scenes.

In the first scene: King Solomon gathers together the elders and chiefs of the people of Israel.

In the second scene: The priests bring the ark of the covenant to the temple, and the Levites bring the Tent of Meeting and furnishings to the temple.

In the third scene: The people of Israel sacrifice cattle and sheep.

In the fourth scene: The priests bring the ark of the covenant into the Most Holy Place.

In the fifth scene: The priests leave the Most Holy Place, and Yahweh's presence fills the temple.

The characters in this passage are:

  • King Solomon
  • Yahweh
  • Elders and chiefs of the people of Israel
  • Priests
  • Other Levites
  • And people of Israel

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, King Solomon has just finished building the temple. King Solomon wants the priests to bring the ark of the covenant and the Levites to bring the Tent of Meeting and furnishings from Zion to the temple near where King Solomon lives. The people of Israel are gathering in Jerusalem for the Festival of Tabernacles and can travel easily to King Solomon. King Solomon likely knows that the temple is not finished until the priests and Levites bring these items. King Solomon also likely chooses the Festival of Tabernacles as a time to do this because the festival requires all people of Israel to travel to Jerusalem.

Stop here and review the picture of King Solomon's Temple as a group. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss as a group: In this passage, a festival is a gathering where people celebrate an event or special occasion. In your language, how would you translate "festival"? Pause this audio here.

King Solomon gathers together the elders and chiefs of the people of Israel, who are probably already there for the festival. This is important because the leaders and chiefs are in charge of looking after the people and communicating King Solomon's commands to the people.

Stop here and discuss as a group: What words or phrases do you use in your culture for leaders or chiefs? How can you best translate this? Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, once the elders and leaders gather together with Solomon, the priests and Levites prepare to go to the temple. The priests carry the ark of the covenant with the poles. The other Levites carry the Tent of Meeting and the other furnishings from the Tent of Meeting. The elders and chiefs travel with the priests and Levites who transport all that King Solomon has asked for.

Stop here and look at a photo of the priests carrying the ark of the covenant as a group. Pause this audio here.

Stop and look at a photo or illustration of the Tent of Meeting. Pause this audio here.

Stop and look at the various furnishings people used in the Tent of Meeting. Pause this audio here.

It is important that the priests are the only ones who carry or touch the poles of the ark, just as Yahweh told them to. It is also important that only the other Levites carry the Tent of Meeting and furnishings, because Yahweh gave the Levites the duty to care for the temple.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Talk about duties in your culture that only certain people can perform. How can you use this information to talk about the Levites in this passage? Pause this audio here.

In the third scene, the priests, Levites, and other leaders arrive at the temple with King Solomon and the people of Israel. The people of Israel sacrifice so many cattle and sheep that it is impossible to count all of the sacrifices! The people are sacrificing in front of the ark of the covenant, in the temple courtyard, before the priests take the ark into the Most Holy Place. King Solomon and the people dedicate the temple to Yahweh and honor Yahweh with these sacrifices.

Stop here and look at pictures of cattle and sheep. Pause this audio here.

In the fourth scene, the priests carry the ark of the covenant through the Holy Place into the Most Holy Place. Only the priests can enter these spaces. The priests place the ark of the covenant under the outspread cherubim wings, leave the poles in the ark, and then leave and most likely close the curtain. After this moment, only the high priest can enter the Most Holy Place one day of the year.

Stop here and look at a picture of what a priest looked like and the clothing they wore. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and look at a photo or illustration of cherubim. Also, look at a photo or illustration of what the cherubim looked like in Solomon's temple. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Look again at the cherubim. Discuss as a group how you can describe the cherubim and what words you can use to show the difference between a real angel and the carved angel. Pause this audio here.

In the fifth scene, after the priests leave the Most Holy Place, a cloud fills the temple. The cloud represents Yahweh's presence and glory. The cloud is so thick that the priests cannot carry out their priestly duties, and the priests have to leave the sanctuary. Yahweh often showed that Yahweh was in a space when Yahweh filled that space with a cloud or bright light. The cloud is important because it shows that Yahweh now fills the temple and rests in the place that King Solomon built to honor Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss as a group: How will you describe the cloud? In your culture, how do you show that something like a cloud represents a person? For example, what kinds of symbols or objects show that a king lives in a certain place, even when you cannot see the king? Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 8:1–11 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has five scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • King Solomon
  • Yahweh
  • Elders and chiefs of the people of Israel
  • Priests
  • Other Levites
  • And people of Israel

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

After King Solomon finished building the temple, King Solomon sent a message to the elders and chiefs of the people of Israel. All of the people of Israel were in Jerusalem for the Festival of Tabernacles. King Solomon asked the elders and chiefs to go with the priests who carried the ark of the covenant to the new temple. The priests carried the ark with the poles, and the other Levites also carried the Tent of Meeting and its furnishings to the new temple.

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing priests, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • What an honor to carry the ark to the temple! or
  • We must be careful! or
  • We must not forget anything!

Continue the drama.

Once the elders and all leaders of Israel gathered together with Solomon, the priests and Levites went to the new temple. The priests walked and carried the ark of the covenant, and the other Levites also walked and carried the Tent of Meeting and its furnishings. The elders and chiefs of the tribes of Israel traveled to the temple with King Solomon and the other Israelites.

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing the other Levites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We must carry everything carefully for Yahweh; or
  • I am very tired from walking and carrying everything! or
  • I wonder what the temple will look like.

Continue the drama.

When all of the elders, leaders, priests, and other Levites reached the temple, the [priests stopped in the temple courtyard, and King Solomon and the people of Israel sacrificed so many cattle]{.mark} and sheep for Yahweh that it was impossible to count all of the sacrifices!

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Finally, everyone has arrived! or
  • I can now dedicate the temple properly! or
  • Surely Yahweh will be pleased with all of these sacrifices!

Ask the people playing the people of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • There cannot possibly be any more animals left! or
  • I wonder what Yahweh thinks about all of this! or
  • I feel excited to have the temple completed!

Continue the drama.

The priests carried the ark of the covenant through the Holy Place and into the Most Holy Place. The priests placed the ark of the covenant, with the two stone tablets that Moses put into the ark, under the cherubim that covered the ark of the covenant and the poles.

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing the priests, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • I am so relieved that we brought the ark here safely! or
  • I am in awe of the cherubim! or
  • I must make sure the curtain is closed.

Continue the drama.

After the priests left the ark of the covenant in the Most Holy Place, a cloud filled the entire temple. The cloud was Yahweh's presence, and Yahweh showed that he was pleased with the temple. The cloud was so thick that the priests could not enter the temple to perform their priestly duties. Yahweh's presence filled the temple, and all of the people could see that Yahweh was pleased and that Yahweh was now in the temple.

Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • King Solomon has truly honored and obeyed me! or
  • Everything looks exactly like I wanted it to; or
  • I love King Solomon and the people of Israel!

Ask the people playing the priests, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • How can we perform our duties in the temple if we cannot get in? or
  • I can feel Yahweh's presence here! or
  • We must help the people of Israel understand what is happening.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 8:1–11 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

In this passage, King Solomon summons the elders and chiefs or leaders of the people of Israel. The elders oversee the entire population of the people of Israel. The chiefs are leaders of different groups or tribes of people. Although the storyteller does not tell us the name of this festival, King Solomon chooses to do this during the time of the seventh month, during the Festival of Tabernacles. You will remember that the people of Israel celebrated the Festival of Tabernacles to remember when Yahweh provided shelter for them after they left Egypt.

Stop here and discuss as a group what words or phrases you will use for chiefs, elders, and Festival of Tabernacles, if you choose to use the name of the festival. Look up king, Israel, elders, and Festival of Tabernacles in the Master Glossary for more information.

King Solomon wants the priests and other Levites to bring the ark of the covenant, the Tent of Meeting, also known as the tabernacle, and the "holy" or special tent furnishings from their location in Jerusalem, also called Zion, north to where King Solomon has built the temple. Remember that the priests and Levites were both part of the tribe of Levi. Priests offered Sacrifices to Yahweh for the people of Israel. Other Levites served the temple in other ways, such as playing music, singing, and doing other tasks for the temple. The ark of the covenant was the oblong chest made of wood and covered in gold. The presence of Yahweh rested on top of the ark on the atonement seat. The tabernacle was the movable tent where Yahweh's presence rested. The temple became the permanent building for Yahweh's presence and the ark of the covenant. Use the same words or phrases for holy, priests, ark of the covenant, and temple as you used in previous passages. For more information on holy, priests, Levites, ark of the covenant, tabernacle, and temple, refer to the Master Glossary.

Stop here and discuss as a group what words or phrases you will use for Zion and Levites. Look up Levites and Zion in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated these words in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

The people of Israel sacrifice so many cattle and sheep that no one can count all of the animals. Use the same word or phrase for sacrificing as you used in previous passages. For more information on sacrifice, refer to the Master Glossary.

The priests take the ark of the covenant through the Holy Place into the Most Holy Place or inner sanctuary. The priests place the ark of the covenant under the cherubim with wings that stretch over the entire length of the ark and the carrying poles. Use the same words or phrases for cherubim, sanctuary, Holy Place, and Most Holy Place as you used in previous passages. For more information on cherubim, sanctuary, Holy Place, and Most Holy Place, refer to the Master Glossary.

When the priests leave the Most Holy Place, a cloud fills the temple, and Yahweh's presence and glory enters the temple to stay and rest there. The cloud is so thick that the priests cannot perform their priestly duties in the temple. The cloud represents the glory, or majesty, of Yahweh and Yahweh's presence.

Stop here and discuss as a group what words or phrases you will use for cloud and glory. Look up glory and Yahweh in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated these words in another book of the Bible, use the same words that you have used there.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 8:12–21
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 8:12–21 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 8:12–21 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 8:12–21 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous passage, Yahweh saw everything that King Solomon and the people of Israel did. When the priests placed the ark of the covenant in the Most Holy Place, Yahweh responded when he filled the temple with a thick cloud. In this passage, King Solomon speaks to Yahweh in a prayer and then addresses the people of Israel in response to Yahweh's presence in the temple.

Stop here and review the photo of King Solomon's Temple. Pause this audio here.

This passage is almost exactly the same as 2 Chronicles 6:1–11 that you may have translated previously.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: If you have already translated 2 Chronicles 6:1–11, listen to your translation of that passage. Pause this audio here.

King Solomon begins his prayer when Solomon says words that Yahweh spoke previously. In the book of Leviticus, Yahweh spoke to Moses and told Moses not to allow his brother Aaron to go into the Most Holy Place. Yahweh also said that Yahweh would come in a dark cloud into the Most Holy Place over the ark of the covenant. You will remember that Yahweh did the same thing in the tabernacle when Moses was leading the Israelites in the wilderness. This was a sign that Yahweh was pleased and was present in the tabernacle. King Solomon recognizes this, and King Solomon tells Yahweh that King Solomon has built a magnificent temple for Yahweh to live in forever.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Remember that the people of Israel likely knew the story of Moses and Yahweh's presence in the tabernacle. How do you think people felt when they saw Yahweh come in a cloud like this? How would you feel if you saw this yourself instead of only hearing about it? Pause this audio here.

After King Solomon prays to Yahweh, King Solomon then turns away from the temple and faces the assembly, or the group of the people of Israel. Assemblies are large groups of people who gather together for a common reason. King Solomon then blesses the people. Again, King Solomon does the same act that Moses did at the tabernacle. King Solomon sees all that has happened, and King Solomon honors Yahweh and then speaks Yahweh's blessings over the people of Israel.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Think about the most important or famous person in your country. This could be a king or a leader. How would you feel if this person recognized you and spoke kind words to you? In your culture, how do leaders bless or honor other people? Pause this audio here.

King Solomon begins his prayer by praising Yahweh, the God of Israel. King Solomon is reminding the Israelites that they are Yahweh's special people. Yahweh is the Israelites' God! King Solomon says he praises Yahweh because Yahweh has fulfilled Yahweh's promises through Yahweh's hands, or Yahweh's power. Yahweh fulfilled his promise to Solomon's father, King David, that Solomon would be king and build the temple. King Solomon likely wants to remind the people that Yahweh is faithful and trustworthy.

In the next section, King Solomon speaks words that Yahweh has already spoken. King Solomon quotes Yahweh when Solomon says, "Since the day I brought my people Israel out of Egypt, I have not chosen a city in any tribe of Israel for people to build a temple where my people can worship me, but I have chosen David to rule my people Israel."

In this part of the passage, King Solomon likely shares what Yahweh said to King David to explain why King David did not build the temple sooner. Yahweh wanted King David to rule over the people of Israel, but Yahweh did not give King David permission or instructions to build a temple.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Imagine that you are King David. How would you feel if you were king and you wanted to build a temple, but Yahweh would not let you? Describe your feelings and thoughts. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, who is allowed to build or create holy places, like temples? Who chooses when it is the right time or place to make these holy places? Pause this audio here.

King Solomon [then quotes Yahweh again to remind the people of Israel why King Solomon built the temple.]{.mark} Yahweh did not let King David build the temple, even though Yahweh loved King David and King David wanted to build the temple to honor Yahweh's name. However, Yahweh did have a plan. Yahweh told King David that King David's son, King Solomon, would build the Temple. When Solomon repeats Yahweh's words, King Solomon reminds the people of Israel that Yahweh is faithful and is in control of all things, including time.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story of a time when you thought someone would choose you for a special role, but the person chose someone else instead. Share with the group how you felt. Pause this audio here.

After King Solomon reminds the people of what Yahweh said, King Solomon then confirms that what Yahweh said has happened. Yahweh has kept all of Yahweh's promises. Solomon is king and has built the temple for Yahweh, just as Yahweh promised. Yahweh promised that King Solomon would sit on the throne and rule over Israel. A throne is a special seat for a king that shows the king's importance and power. Again, King Solomon says that he has built the temple for "Yahweh, the God of Israel."

Stop here and look at a picture of a throne. Pause this audio here.

[To finish his reminder to the people, King Solomon explains that King Solomon has built a place where people can put the ark of the covenant. In the ark are the stone tablets. These tablets are a reminder of Yahweh's covenant with the people of Israel after Yahweh led the people of Israel out of Egypt. King Solomon reminds the people of Israel that Yahweh made promises to their ancestors. Yahweh will keep those promises just as Yahweh kept his promises to David.]{.mark}

Stop here and look at a picture of the ark of the covenant. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and look at a picture of the stone tablets. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, how do you pass information down through generations so that people will know what happened during their ancestors' lives? How do you keep a record of all that has happened?

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 8:12–21 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has two scenes.

In the first scene: King Solomon faces the temple and speaks to Yahweh.

In the second scene: King Solomon turns around and blesses the people of Israel. King Solomon reminds the people of how Yahweh keeps his promises.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Storyteller
  • King Solomon
  • Yahweh
  • People of Israel
  • And King David

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In this entire passage, King Solomon is the only one who speaks. King Solomon speaks to Yahweh and to the people of Israel. King Solomon's speech has several parts. King Solomon praises Yahweh. King Solomon also tells why he built the temple instead of his father, King David. King Solomon shows the people that Yahweh has fulfilled Yahweh's promises. King Solomon's speech honors Yahweh and reminds people of who Yahweh is and that they should also honor Yahweh.

In the first scene, King Solomon faces the temple and speaks to Yahweh. King Solomon reminds Yahweh, and everyone who listens, that Yahweh previously said that he would live in a dark cloud. You will remember that Yahweh told Moses many years before that Yahweh would live in the dark cloud. King Solomon then offers the temple to Yahweh so that Yahweh can have a place to live in forever.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: It sounds like King Solomon speaks about Yahweh when Solomon says that Yahweh said he would live in a dark cloud. Then King Solomon speaks directly to Yahweh. How will you show the shift in who King Solomon is talking to? It is possible that King Solomon is talking directly to Yahweh from the beginning of this passage, and you can translate it in that way if you wish. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your language, how will you translate this part so that people will understand that King Solomon is repeating words that Yahweh had already said many years earlier? Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, King Solomon turns and speaks to the people of Israel. The storyteller says that the people are standing as an assembly, so King Solomon is likely talking to people who are purposely listening to Solomon. The storyteller says that King Solomon blesses the people.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, what types of blessings do people speak over other people? Think of examples of how people might speak words to comfort people or to remind them of how Yahweh blesses them. Pause this audio here.

In this section, King Solomon talks about both what Yahweh does now and what Yahweh has said and done in the past. King Solomon starts by praising "Yahweh, the God of Israel" for helping King Solomon do what Yahweh told King David that King Solomon would do through Yahweh's hands or power. The storyteller is not saying that Yahweh's actual hand built the temple. But the storyteller is likely saying that Yahweh made it possible for King Solomon and the people to complete the temple with Yahweh's power.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: When you tell a story in your language, how do you show that something happens now and that something happened in the past? What words would you use to help someone understand when something happened? Pause this audio here.

Solomon says that Yahweh used his own mouth to speak to King David. We see that Yahweh directly acted and spoke to King David and to King Solomon several times about Yahweh's plans. Yahweh did not give a specific place for anyone to build a temple for him in King David's time. Yahweh said that David would not build a temple for "his name," or Yahweh's name, which means a temple for Yahweh and where Yahweh would be present. But Yahweh did bless King David and chose King David to rule over the people of Israel.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your language, how do you show that a person is quoting someone else instead of using their own words? How can you show when King Solomon is quoting Yahweh and when King Solomon is using Solomon's own words? Pause this audio here.

King Solomon explains that his father, King David, "had it in his heart" to build a temple for Yahweh. In the original language, people use the heart and mind to describe the places where they think, feel, and process information. King David really wanted to build a temple. Then King Solomon again quotes Yahweh's words that Yahweh spoke to King David. Yahweh told King David that Yahweh was pleased that King David wanted to build a temple for Yahweh. However, Yahweh told King David that King David would not be the one to build the temple. Instead, Yahweh chose King Solomon, King David's own "flesh and blood," to build the temple for Yahweh. In this case, flesh and blood means King David's son.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your language, how do you show that someone is related to someone else in their family? What are some other words or phrases you could use to show that someone is truly another person's son? Think about how you can translate "flesh and blood" to show this. Pause this audio here.

It is important to remember that King Solomon is not saying that he built the temple by himself. This means that King Solomon appointed people to build the temple and watched over the process.

King Solomon finishes this speech by making it clear to the people that Yahweh has answered and fulfilled the promises that Yahweh made to King David. King Solomon now sits on Israel's throne and rules over the people of Israel. King Solomon has also built the temple for Yahweh and has placed the ark of the covenant in the temple. King Solomon reminds the people that the stone tablets are in the ark of the covenant. The tablets represent the covenant that Yahweh made with Moses and the people of Israel when Yahweh rescued the people of Israel from Egypt.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Why do you think King Solomon repeats Yahweh's promises? What are some reasons why you might repeat stories or phrases? Why is this helpful? Pause this audio here.

Stop here and look at a picture of a throne. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and look at a picture of the ark of the covenant. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and look at a picture of the stone tablets. Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 8:12–21 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has two scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Storyteller
  • King Solomon
  • Yahweh
  • People of Israel
  • And King David

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

After King Solomon built a temple for Yahweh, King Solomon faced the temple and spoke to Yahweh. King Solomon said that in a previous time, Yahweh said he would dwell in a dark cloud. King Solomon then says that King Solomon has built a temple for Yahweh to live in forever. The temple has a dark cloud inside, and this shows that Yahweh accepted the temple.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I hope Yahweh approves of this temple! or
  • Look at how much better this temple is than a temporary tabernacle! or
  • I feel proud of what I have accomplished; or
  • I am so glad we have a permanent place to worship Yahweh!

Continue the drama.

After King Solomon spoke to Yahweh, King Solomon faced all of the people of Israel who were standing in front of him. King Solomon blessed the people.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the people of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I must be quiet and listen to King Solomon! or
  • I am excited to hear what King Solomon is going to say to us! or
  • Look at this beautiful temple!

Continue the drama.

King Solomon praised Yahweh by saying, "Praise be to Yahweh, the God of Israel!" King Solomon then told the people why he was praising Yahweh. King Solomon praised Yahweh because Yahweh fulfilled the promise that Yahweh made to King David.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I remember exactly what King Solomon is saying! or
  • I love it when King Solomon honors me! or
  • It makes me happy when King Solomon sees what I have done for Solomon!

Continue the drama.

King Solomon speaks about what Yahweh did when Solomon reminds the people of Israel of the time when Yahweh led the people of Israel out of Egypt. Yahweh did not choose a place anywhere in Israel for anyone to build a temple. Instead, Yahweh chose King Solomon's father, King David, to rule the people of Israel.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I want the people to see Yahweh's faithfulness! or
  • I am in awe of all Yahweh has done! or
  • I wonder why Yahweh chose me!

Continue the drama.

King Solomon then tells the people of Israel that his father, King David, wanted to build a temple for Yahweh. King Solomon then tells the people of Israel what Yahweh said to King David. Yahweh told King David that King David was right to want to build the temple for Yahweh. But Yahweh then told King David that King David could not be the person to build the temple. Instead, Yahweh said that King Solomon would build the temple.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I wish I could build the temple! or
  • I am thankful that my son will get to build the temple; or
  • I am sad that I will not get to see the temple.

Continue the drama.

King Solomon declares that Yahweh has fulfilled the promises that Yahweh made to King David. King Solomon now sits on the throne of Israel and has built the temple for Yahweh. King Solomon placed the ark of the covenant in the temple with the stone tablets in the ark to represent the covenant that Yahweh made when Yahweh led the people of Israel out of Egypt.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the people of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Wow! Look at all Yahweh has done! or
  • I feel like I can trust Yahweh when he speaks! or
  • I am glad that Yahweh approves of our king!

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 8:12–21 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

After Yahweh's presence fills the temple, King Solomon faces the temple and speaks to Yahweh. King Solomon says that Yahweh previously said that Yahweh would dwell or live in a dark cloud. Someone dwells in a place when they have settled in that place as their home. King Solomon says that he has built a magnificent temple for Yahweh where Yahweh can live forever. Use the same words or phrases for king, temple, and cloud as you used in previous passages. Look up king and temple in the Master Glossary for more information.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for dwell. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

After King Solomon speaks to Yahweh, King Solomon turns around and faces the assembly of Israel. People gather in an assembly when they gather all together for a specific purpose. King Solomon praises or blesses Yahweh, the God of Israel, and tells the people of Israel that Yahweh has done what Yahweh told King Solomon's father, King David, he would do. Look up Israel, God, Yahweh, and praise or bless in the Master Glossary for more information, and translate these ideas in the same way you have in previous passages.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for "assembly," or gathering of people. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

King Solomon says that King David had it in his heart to build a house for Yahweh. Yahweh told King David that Yahweh had not chosen any city in any tribe of Israel for anyone to build a temple. However, Yahweh did choose for King David to rule over the people of Israel. Refer to the Master Glossary for more information about heart, and use the same words or phrases for heart and tribe as you used in previous passages.

Yahweh said that King David's son, King David's own "flesh and blood," would build the temple.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for flesh and blood. If you have already translated this phrase in another book of the Bible, use the same phrase that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Finally, King Solomon reminds the people of Israel that King Solomon placed the ark of the covenant in the temple. The ark of the covenant contains the stone tablets that represent the covenant Yahweh made with the people's ancestors when Yahweh rescued the people of Israel's ancestors from Egypt. Use the same words or phrases for ark of the covenant and covenant as you used in previous passages. Look up "ark of the covenant" and covenant in the Master Glossary for more information.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 8:22–30
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 8:22–30 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 8:22–30 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 8:22–30 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this passage, King Solomon continues to pray to Yahweh in front of the people of Israel. This passage contains two sections of King Solomon's prayer. In the first part of King Solomon's prayer, King Solomon praises Yahweh for how Yahweh has kept and fulfilled Yahweh's covenant or promise to King Solomon's father, King David. In the second part of this prayer, King Solomon asks that Yahweh continue to listen to the Israelites.

As King Solomon prays to Yahweh, King Solomon stands facing the bronze temple altar. The people of Israel stand behind King Solomon, who stretches out his hands to heaven. This is the altar that is in front of the temple building.

Stop here and look at an image of King Solomon's Temple, including the altar. Pause this audio here.

King Solomon declares that Yahweh is the most powerful God in all the earth, in all of heaven, and in every place below the earth. This is important because other people groups outside of the people of Israel worshipped many gods during King Solomon's time. King Solomon honors Yahweh as the only God that the people of Israel worship.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, what types of worship or traditional practices do people perform? What kinds of gods do people in your area worship? How do people worship these gods? How do you show that you only worship Yahweh? Pause this audio here.

King Solomon describes Yahweh as the God who keeps Yahweh's "covenant of love" with the people of Israel. The people of Israel are Yahweh's servants who walk in or obey Yahweh's ways and commands. In this covenant, both Yahweh and the people of Israel promised to act in certain ways. Yahweh promised to love and show kindness to the people of Israel, and the people of Israel promised to obey Yahweh's commands.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, what types of agreements or covenants do leaders make with their people? Think about your family structure and community structure. How do people agree and then make sure that they keep the agreements? Pause this audio here.

King Solomon again declares that Yahweh has kept the promises that Yahweh made to King David. Yahweh has chosen King Solomon to be king after King David, and Yahweh has allowed King Solomon to build the temple.

Then King Solomon asks Yahweh to keep his promises in the future. King Solomon quotes Yahweh's words when King Solomon says that Yahweh promised King David that one of David's descendants would always sit on the throne of Israel, or be king over Israel. But David's descendants must obey Yahweh as David did. After King Solomon speaks about the original covenant, King Solomon asks Yahweh to be faithful to continue to keep the covenant and the words that Yahweh spoke to King David.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Think of a time when you wanted to encourage a trustworthy person to keep a promise that this person made. How did you remind this person that they should continue to keep this promise? Pause this audio here.

King Solomon then prays that Yahweh would pay attention to the prayers that the people of Israel would pray. King Solomon asks Yahweh if Yahweh would live on earth, even though the heavens cannot contain the power of Yahweh. The "heavens" is the place where God lives with his angels. King Solomon says that even the wonderful temple that King Solomon has built for Yahweh is still too small for such a powerful God! King Solomon knows that the temple cannot contain Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, where do people believe that gods live? Pause this audio here.

King Solomon does not expect Yahweh to contain himself in the temple. King Solomon knows that Yahweh is greater than any building a human could build. Instead, King Solomon describes what King Solomon hopes that Yahweh will do.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, how do you recognize Yahweh's ability to be both God over all things and also a God who listens to your prayers? Pause this audio here.

King Solomon asks Yahweh to pay attention to the prayers of Yahweh's people from the temple. King Solomon reminds Yahweh that Yahweh said that people would worship Yahweh's name in one place. This is the moment that King Solomon shows what he is actually praying for. King Solomon wants Yahweh's name present in the temple. In biblical times, a person's name showed that person's character. King Solomon wants Yahweh himself to be with Yahweh's people in the temple, even though Yahweh is too great for the temple.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, where do people go to worship deities? Does your culture have special types of buildings or structures for deities? Do people often speak to deities in these special places? Pause this audio here.

King Solomon then asks Yahweh to listen to King Solomon, Yahweh's servant, and to the Israelites, Yahweh's people. King Solomon asks Yahweh to hear the prayers from heaven, which is where Yahweh lives. King Solomon asks Yahweh to hear the prayers of the people and then forgive the people of Israel when they pray from the temple. King Solomon and the people of Israel see the temple as the place where Yahweh's people can come to Yahweh and where Yahweh meets his people.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 8:22–30 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has two scenes.

In the first scene: King Solomon stands in front of the bronze altar in the temple courtyard and thanks Yahweh for keeping Yahweh's promises.

In the second scene: King Solomon asks Yahweh to listen to Solomon's prayers and the prayers of Yahweh's people when they pray towards the temple.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Storyteller
  • King Solomon
  • People of Israel
  • King David
  • And Yahweh

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In this passage, Solomon begins his long prayer to Yahweh when Solomon dedicates the temple to Yahweh. Most of the characters in the story either watch King Solomon, or King Solomon speaks about these characters or to them. It is good for you to remember that the Israelites and Yahweh are both present as Solomon prays.

In the first scene, King Solomon begins a longer prayer to Yahweh. King Solomon faces the altar in the courtyard of the temple. The assembly, or special gathering, of Israelites stands behind King Solomon as King Solomon raises his hands to heaven to pray to Yahweh. This was a common way for people to pray during King Solomon's time.

Stop and discuss this question as a group: In your language, how do you show action or show specific ways that a person stands or moves? For example, how can you make sure the listener understands that the people were behind King Solomon? Pause this audio here.

King Solomon begins to pray and thank Yahweh. King Solomon recognizes that Yahweh is the God above all other gods when King Solomon says that there is no other god in the highest part of heaven or the lowest place under the earth or on earth that compares to Yahweh. In other words, King Solomon says that Yahweh is the greatest god of all gods anywhere. King Solomon then shows Yahweh as a personal and present God when King Solomon shows that Yahweh makes covenants with Yahweh's people and loyally loves his people, the Israelites. King Solomon also says that the people of Israel keep Yahweh's commands with all their heart. When someone does something "with all their heart," that person does that thing happily and well.

Stop and discuss this question as a group: King Solomon has changed from talking about Yahweh to talking to Yahweh. How will you make it clear that King Solomon is talking to Yahweh here? Pause this audio here.

King Solomon recognizes that Yahweh kept his earlier covenant with King David when Yahweh chose King Solomon to be king and allowed King Solomon to build the temple. Solomon says that Yahweh said the promise with his mouth, and Yahweh kept his promise with his hand, or with his power. King Solomon is saying that the Israelites can see that Yahweh kept his promise because Yahweh has made King Solomon, David's son, king of Israel, and King Solomon has built the house for Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss as a group: How will you translate that Yahweh said a promise with his mouth and kept the promise with his hand, or power? Pause this audio here.

King Solomon now asks Yahweh to keep another specific covenant that Yahweh made with King David. King Solomon quotes Yahweh when King Solomon refers to how Yahweh promised King David that Yahweh would continue to give the people of Israel a king from King David's family line. David's descendants must follow Yahweh's commands just as King David did. King Solomon wants Yahweh to continue this covenant with King Solomon.

Stop and discuss this question as a group: This section has a quote from Yahweh inside King Solomon's prayer. In your language, how can you translate this to show when Solomon speaks his own words and when Solomon quotes Yahweh? Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, King Solomon asks Yahweh to hear the prayers of King Solomon and Yahweh's people. First, King Solomon asks this question: "Will God really live on earth? The heavens cannot hold you, and neither can this temple!" Solomon means that he does not believe that Yahweh is supposed to live only in the temple on earth. King Solomon understands that if heaven cannot contain Yahweh, a temple that people built cannot contain Yahweh either. King Solomon is asking a question that he does not expect Yahweh to answer. Instead, Solomon is praising Yahweh for being so great that Yahweh cannot live in one place.

Stop and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate this section to show that King Solomon believes that Yahweh can live anywhere, and no one place is the only place Yahweh lives? Pause this audio here.

King Solomon begs Yahweh to respond to the prayers that King Solomon and the people of Israel pray now and will pray in the future. King Solomon asks Yahweh to open Yahweh's eyes and to give full attention to Yahweh's people's prayers and to what happens in the temple during the day and the night, or all the time. To open your eyes to something means to pay attention and watch what happens, just as a person with their eyes open can see and pay attention to something.

Stop and discuss this question as a group: In your language, how do you speak about the way that someone pays attention to something or someone? Pause this audio here.

King Solomon asks Yahweh to hear and listen to King Solomon and the people of Israel when they pray anytime during the day or night. King Solomon again quotes Yahweh when King Solomon says that Yahweh once promised that Yahweh's "Name would be there" in one place for people to worship Yahweh. King Solomon probably wants to show that Yahweh promises to be present in the temple to meet with the people. King Solomon finishes this part of the prayer when Solomon asks Yahweh to hear the supplications of Yahweh's people when they pray in the temple. Supplication is a way that people can pray to Yahweh. In this case, the people are strongly asking Yahweh to do something for them.

Stop and discuss this question as a group: In your language, how do you strongly ask God to do something for you? Pause this audio here.

You will notice that King Solomon speaks about himself in this section as if Solomon were speaking about someone else. King Solomon calls himself Yahweh's "servant."

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you show that Solomon is talking about himself when he refers to Yahweh's servant? Pause this audio here.

King Solomon asks Yahweh to forgive the people of Israel when Yahweh hears the prayers that the people pray "towards this place," the temple. If the Israelites were not near the temple, the Israelites could face the direction of the temple to pray, to show that they were praying to Yahweh. King Solomon expects that Yahweh will hear these prayers and Yahweh will forgive his people of their disobedience. Yahweh will answer these prayers from heaven, the place where Yahweh lives.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 8:22–30 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has two scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Storyteller
  • King Solomon
  • People of Israel
  • King David
  • And Yahweh

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses. The storyteller should share the drama.

Before King Solomon prayed to dedicate the temple to Yahweh, King Solomon turned to face the altar in the temple court. The people of Israel stood behind King Solomon as King Solomon spread his hands to heaven to pray to Yahweh.

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing the people of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I wonder what King Solomon is going to say? or
  • I feel like I can trust King Solomon to lead us! or
  • I need to focus on what King Solomon will pray for!

Continue the drama.

King Solomon began his prayer when Solomon praised Yahweh and recognized that no other god exists anywhere on earth, above the earth, or below the earth that can ever compare to Yahweh. King Solomon recognized that Yahweh is trustworthy to keep Yahweh's covenants with people who keep Yahweh's commands. King Solomon also recognized that Yahweh kept the covenant that Yahweh had made with King David when Yahweh chose Solomon to be king and allowed King Solomon to build the temple.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I love my servant, King Solomon! or
  • I feel honored by King Solomon! or
  • I remember the covenant I made with King David.

Continue the drama.

King Solomon then asked Yahweh to keep another covenant that Yahweh had made with King David. King Solomon asked Yahweh to always provide a new king from King David's family line to sit on Israel's throne. King Solomon also recognized that David's descendants must keep Yahweh's commands in order to continue to rule Israel. King Solomon asked Yahweh to fulfill this promise.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I hope Yahweh continues to be trustworthy! or
  • I wonder if Yahweh is hearing my prayers! or
  • I feel nervous that Yahweh will change his mind!

Continue the drama.

King Solomon again acknowledged how big and powerful Yahweh is. King Solomon asked, "But, will God really live on earth?" King Solomon recognized that heaven cannot contain Yahweh's power, and the temple is not supposed to contain Yahweh either. Instead, King Solomon asked Yahweh's name to rest in the temple as Yahweh had promised in another covenant to Moses. King Solomon asked Yahweh to open Yahweh's eyes to all that happens in the temple, both day and night. King Solomon wanted Yahweh to hear the prayers that both King Solomon and the people of Israel prayed in the temple. King Solomon asked Yahweh to hear the pleas of the people and to forgive the people of Israel when Yahweh hears their prayers.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Solomon is right! Nothing can contain me! or
  • I am honored to have my name in the temple! or
  • I will hear the people's prayers!

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 8:22–30 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

In this passage, King Solomon turns away from the assembly of Israel and faces the altar of Yahweh. In the temple, the altar is a large structure where priests make burnt animal sacrifices. King Solomon stretches out his hands to heaven and prays to Yahweh. Heaven is the place where Yahweh lives with his angels. Use the same words or phrases for assembly, altar, king, Yahweh, Israelites, and Israel as you have in previous passages. For more information, refer to these words in the Master Glossary.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for heaven. Look up heaven in the Master Glossary for more information. Pause this audio here.

King Solomon honors Yahweh as the only God among other gods. Use the same word or phrase for God as you used in previous passages. Look up God in the Master Glossary for more information.

Stop here and discuss as a group how you will say that there is no god like Yahweh. For more information about how to talk about false gods that are not Yahweh, refer to "gods" in the Master Glossary. Pause this audio here.

Solomon says that Yahweh kept his covenant of steadfast love with Yahweh's servants, who are Solomon, David, and the Israelites. This covenant is a commitment that requires both people to keep promises to each other. Use the same words or phrases for covenant, steadfast love, and servants as you have used in previous passages. Covenant, steadfast love, and servant are in the Master Glossary.

King Solomon reminds Yahweh that Yahweh promised that there would be a descendant of David on the throne of Israel forever. Use the same words or phrases for descendants and throne as you have used in previous passages. Descendants is in the Master Glossary.

King Solomon recognizes that King Solomon's temple cannot contain Yahweh! King Solomon knows that Yahweh's name is the most important part of the temple. Use the same words or phrases for temple and the name of Yahweh as you have used in previous passages. Temple is in the Master Glossary.

King Solomon asks Yahweh to hear the prayers and supplications of the people of Israel. When someone prays, that person is talking to God. Supplication is a type of prayer when people ask humbly, but very strongly, for Yahweh to hear them and answer them.

Stop here and discuss as a group what words or phrases you will use for supplication and pray. Look up prayer in the Master Glossary for more information about the different types of prayers to Yahweh. If you have already translated these types of prayers in another book of the Bible, you may be able to use the same words that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

King Solomon asks that Yahweh hear the people's prayers and then forgive the people. When Yahweh forgives people, Yahweh has mercy on them and does not punish them as they deserve.

Stop here and discuss as a group what words or phrases you will use for forgive. Forgive is in the Master Glossary.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 8:31–53
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 8:31–53 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 8:31–53 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 8:31–53 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this passage, King Solomon continues the prayer to Yahweh that Solomon began praying in the previous passage. In this section, King Solomon makes seven requests to Yahweh. King Solomon mostly talks to Yahweh about the whole nation of Israel. In each request, King Solomon gives examples of what may happen, and then King Solomon asks Yahweh to respond in a certain way.

First, King Solomon speaks to Yahweh about oaths or promises that people might make to one another because one person has done something wrong to someone else. In this case, both people must stand before the altar in front of the temple and allow Yahweh to judge who is telling the truth. King Solomon asks Yahweh to hear both people's arguments and then punish the guilty person and show who is innocent.

Stop here and look at an image of the temple and of an image of the altar in the temple courtyard. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, how do people decide who is telling the truth and who is lying? How do people decide if someone is guilty or innocent? Pause this audio here.

King Solomon knows how the people of Israel have disobeyed Yahweh before, and Yahweh has allowed enemy armies to conquer the people. In his second request, King Solomon asks Yahweh for something in the future. Solomon asks Yahweh to hear the people of Israel when they repent and turn back to Yahweh after an enemy army defeats the people of Israel because they disobeyed. King Solomon asks Yahweh to hear from heaven and listen to the people's prayers in the temple.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Think of a time when you prayed for a situation that had not happened yet. What did you pray, and why? Pause this audio here.

In King Solomon's third request, King Solomon speaks to Yahweh about drought. Drought can happen when there is no rain for a long period of time. Again, King Solomon seems to expect that drought will happen, and King Solomon also recognizes that Yahweh may bring the drought because of the people's sins. King Solomon asks Yahweh to listen and respond to the people of Israel when the people of Israel realize that they have sinned and turn back to Yahweh and pray. King Solomon prays that Yahweh would forgive the people of Israel. Then King Solomon asks that Yahweh would teach the people of Israel how to live in the right way. Finally, King Solomon then asks that Yahweh would end the drought and send rain on the land that Yahweh promised to the people of Israel as their inheritance.

Stop here and look at an image of the effects of drought. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and look at an image of land that is flourishing from rain. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Think about a time when your land had no rain for a long time. What happened to the plants? What happened to the animals and people? How did you feel and respond? Pause this audio here.

In King Solomon's fourth request, King Solomon speaks to Yahweh about any kind of disaster in the land. First, King Solomon talks about famine and "plague" or terrible sickness. Famine means that people have no food. Terrible sickness means that people have diseases, and that the diseases have spread to many people. King Solomon then lists specific events that could happen, such as hot and dry winds, moisture that rots crops, locusts or grasshoppers, and people who attack from another country. Locusts and grasshoppers are both insects that can easily destroy crops when they eat the crops quickly. A siege happens when an enemy surrounds a city and prevents the people in the city from trading or receiving any supplies or food. In time, the people in the city would have no choice but to leave their city and fight. Again, King Solomon knows the typical problems that his people will have.

Stop here and look at an image of a blighted plant. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and look at an image of a plant with mildew. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and look at images of grasshoppers and locusts. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Think about your community's history and even your family's history. Talk about a time when you or your community experienced a terrible sickness or drought. Share your experiences with the group. Pause this audio here.

The people of Israel must recognize the suffering they have "in their hearts" and turn to Yahweh. King Solomon says that the people will spread their hands to the temple to show that they know that God, who lives in the temple among the people of Israel, is the source of their help. The people must pray and strongly ask for Yahweh's forgiveness. King Solomon then simply asks Yahweh to do two things: Forgive and act. King Solomon recognizes that only Yahweh knows every person's motives and attitudes, and King Solomon asks Yahweh to choose how to respond to each person. King Solomon asks Yahweh to remind each person who Yahweh is and that they should honor Yahweh for as long as the people live in Yahweh's land that Yahweh has given to the people of Israel.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do you normally pray in your culture? What kinds of things do you normally pray for? Pause this audio here.

In King Solomon's fifth request, King Solomon speaks to Yahweh about foreigners who are not Israelites but who hear and believe in Yahweh. King Solomon asks Yahweh to hear the foreigner's prayers, because the foreigner has heard people speak about Yahweh's power and strength. King Solomon then boldly asks Yahweh to do whatever the foreigner asks. King Solomon believes that the foreigner can tell all people about the character of the God of Israel, so that all people, not just the Israelites, will fear Yahweh. Lastly, King Solomon wants all people to know that the temple bears Yahweh's name and Yahweh's name alone.
