Then people will know that the temple is the place where God is present and that they should worship God there.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Think about a time when you prayed for or were kind to foreigners who used to be your enemies. Share your story with the group. Pause this audio here.

In King Solomon's sixth request, King Solomon speaks to Yahweh and asks Yahweh to give victory to the people of Israel when Yahweh tells them to fight a war with enemies. King Solomon describes to Yahweh that the people will look toward the city that Yahweh has chosen for the people of Israel. The people will also look toward where the temple is as the people pray. When this happens, King Solomon asks Yahweh to hear the people's prayers and supplications, to make the Israelites strong, and to cause the Israelites to defeat their enemies.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, what do people do with their bodies when they pray? Pause this audio here.

Finally, King Solomon talks about the times when the Israelites will sin [and when enemy armies will force the Israelites to leave their own land and be slaves in a foreign land.]{.mark} Even though Yahweh is angry when the Israelites sin, King Solomon asks Yahweh to listen to the Israelites' prayer when they turn back to Yahweh and obey him. The Israelites must confess that they have sinned and decide to change before Yahweh will forgive the Israelites and save them. If the people then pray, King Solomon asks that Yahweh save the people of Israel from their captors and cause the enemies to show compassion to the Israelites! King Solomon also reminds Yahweh of how the people of Israel are Yahweh's chosen people, whom Yahweh rescued from their very difficult situation in Egypt, which he compares to a hot furnace!

Stop here and look at an image of a furnace. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, how do people show that they are truly sorry and show that they will act in a different way? Pause this audio here.

To close his prayer, King Solomon now asks for blessings and recognizes who Yahweh is and what Yahweh has done. King Solomon asks Yahweh to see and listen to all that the people of Israel pray to Yahweh and to hear what King Solomon prays. King Solomon reminds Yahweh that Yahweh chose the people of Israel out of all the other people to be Yahweh's inheritance. King Solomon reminds Yahweh that Yahweh declared that the Israelites were his own people since the time of Moses. King Solomon honors what Yahweh did for the people of Israel by calling him Adonai Yahweh as a sign of respect. King Solomon asks Yahweh to continue to listen to and answer the people of Israel.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 8:31–53 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has eight scenes. Even though King Solomon is praying during the whole scene, it may be easier to act out Solomon's separate requests as separate scenes.

In the first scene: King Solomon asks Yahweh to judge people when one person accuses another person of doing something wrong.

In the second scene: King Solomon asks Yahweh to save the Israelites from their enemies, to return the Israelites to the land Yahweh gave them, and to forgive the Israelites when they pray and turn back to Yahweh.

In the third scene: King Solomon asks Yahweh to forgive the Israelites when they pray and to send rain to end the drought.

In the fourth scene: King Solomon asks Yahweh to forgive the Israelites when they pray and to stop disease and disaster from happening.

In the fifth scene: King Solomon asks Yahweh to hear foreigners when they pray to Yahweh.

In the sixth scene: King Solomon asks Yahweh to give the Israelites victory when Yahweh sends the Israelites to fight enemies.

In the seventh scene: King Solomon asks Yahweh to hear and forgive the Israelites when they pray and to release them from captivity by their enemies.

In the eighth scene: King Solomon prays for Yahweh to see and listen to all of the prayers of King Solomon and Israel.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Storyteller
  • King Solomon
  • Yahweh
  • People of Israel—this group can act out any part of the drama with people of Israel
  • Two people at the altar
  • Enemies of Israel—this group can act out any part of the drama with enemies
  • Foreigners
  • All peoples of the earth
  • The Israelites' ancestors
  • And Moses

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In this passage, each scene represents a different part of King Solomon's prayer to Yahweh. Each scene seems to act as a shorter prayer within the larger prayer. King Solomon speaks to Yahweh about seven situations that will probably happen in Israel. These situations are things that Yahweh has talked about before. Yahweh has promised that Yahweh will act in the way that Solomon asks him to now. Just like the beginning of this prayer, Solomon asks Yahweh to do what Yahweh promised. King Solomon also asks Yahweh to show mercy on the people of Israel when they repent.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, how do you pray for things or situations that might happen in the future? Pause this audio here.

King Solomon seems to use a pattern when he prays. King Solomon recognizes that something will happen. King Solomon then mentions whether or not the people's sins have caused the thing to happen. King Solomon says that the people must be sorry and ask for Yahweh's forgiveness. Solomon asks that Yahweh hear the people and forgive the people. Lastly, Solomon asks that Yahweh give something to the people in a specific way. This is a common pattern for most scenes.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Think back through each section and notice the pattern. Notice how King Solomon uses a lot of the same phrases throughout the prayer. How can you show this pattern in your translation? You will probably want to translate these phrases in the same way throughout the prayer. Pause this audio here.

In the first scene, King Solomon speaks to Yahweh about what to do if someone accuses a neighbor of doing something against the person. In this situation, a neighbor can mean a friend or any other person. King Solomon says that both people will stand in front of the altar in the temple courtyard and take an oath, or a strong promise, that they are innocent. King Solomon then asks Yahweh to judge who is telling the truth. King Solomon asks Yahweh to punish the person who does something wrong and to also clear the innocent person of any judgment.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, how do people talk when they want to emphasize that they are telling the truth? What kinds of words or phrases do you have that might be the same as what King Solomon uses for "oath" in this passage? Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, King Solomon speaks to Yahweh about when the Israelites sin and when Yahweh allows enemies to defeat the Israelites. King Solomon uses the word "when," which means that King Solomon expects this situation to happen at some point. However, King Solomon asks Yahweh to hear when the Israelites pray and make supplication to Yahweh. You will remember that when people make supplication, people are very sincerely and humbly begging Yahweh to do something for them. King Solomon also asks Yahweh to forgive the Israelites and return the Israelites to the land that Yahweh gave to their ancestors.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your language, how can you describe supplication? Take time to review the words you used in the last passage for how the Israelites talk to Yahweh through supplication and prayer. Discuss which terms you can use in this passage. Pause this audio here.

In the third scene, King Solomon speaks to Yahweh about when the Israelites sin and when Yahweh "shuts up the heavens," which means that Yahweh stops rain from falling from the sky.

Stop here and discuss as a group how you will translate that Yahweh has "shut up the heavens," or stopped rain from falling from the sky? Pause this audio here.

When there is a drought, it does not rain for a very long time. A drought can make it difficult for people to grow crops and make food. Again, King Solomon recognizes that the people of Israel may sin against Yahweh and cause the drought. King Solomon asks Yahweh to hear the prayers of the people and to forgive the people. King Solomon also asks Yahweh to teach the people of Israel the right way to act and then to send rain down onto the land that Yahweh gave as an inheritance to the people of Israel. Many years before, Yahweh gave the land of Israel as a gift to the Israelites. This is not the same as an inheritance that a person receives after someone dies. Yahweh provides the land of Israel, this special place, for the Israelites because Yahweh loves and provides for the Israelites.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you describe that Yahweh has given the land to the people of Israel as an inheritance? Pause this audio here.

In the fourth scene, King Solomon begins to list different types of disasters that could happen to the people of Israel. King Solomon says that the people of Israel could experience famine or plague. Famine is when a large part of the population cannot grow crops and has no food. King Solomon lists different ways that this may happen, like blight, mildew, locusts, or grasshoppers, which all destroy plants that people eat. King Solomon also talks about when an enemy besieges or surrounds an Israelite city. Then the people inside would run out of food. King Solomon finishes the list by saying "whatever disaster or disease" and seems to make sure that he is listing every possible disaster. When these disasters happen, the people can pray to Yahweh, just as Yahweh said they can. The people of Israel must recognize the suffering they "hold in their hearts." This means that the Israelites recognize that they are suffering because they have sinned. Yahweh knows what is "in our hearts," or what we think or feel. The Israelites have sinned. The Israelites must ask God to forgive them and must stop sinning. King Solomon asks Yahweh to hear the prayers and forgive the people. King Solomon also asks Yahweh to deal with each person so that each person will always "fear," or honor, Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your language, what words do you have like "fear" that can have different meanings? How can you show that fear means to honor Yahweh in this passage? Pause this audio here.

In the fifth scene, King Solomon speaks to Yahweh about foreigners who may hear about Yahweh and travel to where the people of Israel live. King Solomon asks Yahweh to hear the prayers of the foreigners who believe and pray towards the temple. King Solomon knows that if Yahweh hears a foreigner's prayer and answers the prayer, the people of Israel and all people will see what Yahweh does and how Yahweh answers, and will know who Yahweh is and fear him. King Solomon wants everyone to know that the temple only exists for people to honor and praise Yahweh's name. When King Solomon talks about Yahweh's strength and power, Solomon says that Yahweh's name is great, Yahweh has a mighty hand, and Yahweh has an outstretched arm. This means that Yahweh is strong and powerful and deserves the highest honor.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In this passage, King Solomon says that the temple exists for Yahweh's name. In your culture, how do names show the character and reputation of a person? How can you translate this passage to show why Yahweh's name and reputation were important in the temple? How will you translate that Yahweh has a mighty hand and outstretched arm, or that Yahweh has great power? Pause this audio here.

In the sixth scene, King Solomon asks Yahweh to give the people of Israel victory when Yahweh sends the people of Israel to fight against other nations. The people of Israel must turn in the direction of the city where Yahweh is, and the people must pray towards the temple. King Solomon asks Yahweh to hear the prayers and pleas and to cause the Israelites to win!

In the seventh scene, King Solomon speaks to Yahweh about captivity. King Solomon recognizes that the people of Israel will sin. King Solomon also says that Yahweh will allow other nations to capture the people of Israel and keep them in captivity. This is the punishment that Yahweh promised if the Israelites continued to sin against Yahweh. However, King Solomon asks Yahweh to see and hear when the people of Israel turn back to Yahweh with their heart and soul and pray for Yahweh's forgiveness. King Solomon uses the words "sin," "wrong," and "evil" to show how much they realize that they have acted in horrible ways against Yahweh. King Solomon knows that the people must show that they need forgiveness and must ask Yahweh with sincere prayers to forgive them. King Solomon asks Yahweh then to forgive the people and to bring the people back to the land that Yahweh gave as inheritance to the people of Israel when they left Egypt. King Solomon compares Egypt to an iron-smelting furnace! An iron-smelting furnace is a chamber that melts iron with very high heat and pressure. In this case, the furnace is an image that represents the suffering that Israel endured in Egypt.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: This passage speaks about a person's heart and soul and how a person makes decisions. In your language, how can you translate "heart and soul" to show how a person changes their behavior and decides to act differently? Pause this audio here.

In the eighth scene, King Solomon finishes this long series of requests by asking Yahweh to open his eyes to King Solomon's and the Israelites' prayers. Throughout the prayer, King Solomon uses different body parts, like Yahweh's eyes or outstretched hand, to describe Yahweh. This does not mean that Yahweh has the body of a human. King Solomon uses this special language to help the Israelites understand Yahweh's power and actions. King Solomon seems to give a reason why Yahweh should listen to the people of Israel. King Solomon says that Yahweh chose the people of Israel out of every other nation and called the people of Israel "Yahweh's inheritance." King Solomon emphasizes this inheritance through the prayer as Solomon continues to refer to the Israelites as Yahweh's people. King Solomon reminds Yahweh that Yahweh said this to Moses as Yahweh led the ancestors of the people of Israel out of Egypt.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Throughout the prayer, King Solomon refers to the Israelites as Yahweh's people. In your culture, how do you include ways that God has shown his faithfulness in your stories and prayers? How can you show God's faithfulness when you share stories? Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 8:31–53 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has eight scenes. Even though King Solomon prays a prayer for the whole scene, it may be easier to act out the separate requests as separate scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Storyteller
  • King Solomon
  • Yahweh
  • People of Israel—this group can act out any part of the drama with people of Israel
  • Two people at the Altar
  • Enemies of Israel—this group can act out any part of the drama with enemies
  • Foreigners
  • All peoples of the Earth
  • The Israelites' ancestors
  • And Moses

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses. The storyteller should share the drama.

First, King Solomon prays to Yahweh about oaths that people swear when they disagree. King Solomon says that when a person brings harm to another person, the two people stand in front of the temple altar and make an oath and promise that they have not done a harmful act. King Solomon then asks Yahweh to judge the two people and to condemn the guilty person and show the innocence of the person who has not done a harmful act.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the guilty person, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am afraid of what Yahweh will do! or
  • I feel anxious that everyone will know what I did! or
  • I should not have done this sin!

Continue the drama.

Second, King Solomon prays to Yahweh about when the Israelites sin and when Yahweh allows enemies to defeat the Israelites. When the Israelites turn back to Yahweh and praise Yahweh sincerely, King Solomon asks that Yahweh listen. Then Yahweh can forgive the people and bring the people of Israel back to the land that Yahweh promised to the people of Israel.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Please hear my prayer, Yahweh! or
  • I have faith that Yahweh will hear me! or
  • I hope the people of Israel remember this prayer.

Continue the drama.

Third, King Solomon prays to Yahweh about drought in the land. King Solomon knows that Yahweh can make the rain stop when the people of Israel do not follow Yahweh. When drought happens in this way, King Solomon asks Yahweh to hear the people of Israel when the people pray to Yahweh and no longer sin. King Solomon also asks Yahweh to teach the people the right way to live and to send rain down on the land that Yahweh gave to the people of Israel as an inheritance.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am sad when my people sin against me! or
  • King Solomon is very wise; or
  • I want to listen to my people when they pray.

Continue the drama.

Fourth, King Solomon prays to Yahweh about famine and plagues. King Solomon recognizes the different ways that famine can happen. King Solomon knows that the people of Israel will sin and that Yahweh will then allow blights, mildew, locusts, and grasshoppers to destroy their crops and enemies to besiege their cities. King Solomon asks Yahweh to hear the people when they are in the middle of any disaster or disease and begin to pray. King Solomon asks Yahweh to see the people as they spread their hands towards the temple and pray to Yahweh for forgiveness. King Solomon then asks Yahweh to forgive the people and to deal with each person because of what they think in their hearts. Yahweh knows what people think and how to treat each person so that each person will always fear and honor Yahweh.

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing the Israelites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" A person might answer things like:

  • We are going to lose all of our crops! or
  • I am scared of what is going to happen next! or
  • Why is this happening to us?

Continue the drama.

Fifth, King Solomon prays to Yahweh about how to treat foreigners who travel to Israel because they have heard about Yahweh. King Solomon recognizes that the foreigners will likely arrive because the foreigners have learned about Yahweh's great name, mighty hand, and outstretched arm! King Solomon then asks that Yahweh would hear the foreigner's prayer and give the foreigner whatever they ask for. King Solomon believes that all people will see what Yahweh does, will honor Yahweh, and will know that people honor Yahweh in the temple.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the foreigner, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am so grateful to know Yahweh! or
  • I trust that Yahweh will answer! or
  • I must tell other people about Yahweh!

Continue the drama.

Sixth, King Solomon prays to Yahweh for victory for the people of Israel when Yahweh sends the Israelites to fight with their enemies in different places. King Solomon asks Yahweh to cause the Israelites to win when the people pray towards the city and temple.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the people of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We need Yahweh's help! or
  • We must remember to pray to ask Yahweh for help; or
  • I feel afraid of going to war!

Continue the drama.

Seventh, King Solomon speaks to Yahweh about possible captivity in the future. King Solomon realizes that the people of Israel may sin against Yahweh, and then enemies may capture the people. King Solomon asks Yahweh to hear the people as they pray from the different lands and ask for forgiveness. King Solomon asks Yahweh to forgive the people and to allow the captors to show mercy and release the people of Israel. King Solomon reminds Yahweh that the people of Israel are Yahweh's inheritance that Yahweh led out of Egypt.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I pray my people will never suffer in captivity again! or
  • I hope Yahweh frees us in the future! or
  • Please hear my prayers, Yahweh!

Continue the drama.

King Solomon finishes this series of smaller prayers by asking Yahweh to open Yahweh's eyes to the people of Israel and hear their prayers. King Solomon also asks Yahweh to hear King Solomon's pleas. King Solomon again reminds Yahweh that Yahweh chose the people of Israel out of all other nations to be Yahweh's inheritance. King Solomon refers back to when Yahweh spoke through Moses and led the people of Israel out of Egypt.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I hope the people of Israel follow Yahweh! or
  • I hope Yahweh answers! or
  • I feel at peace after praying to Yahweh.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 8:31–53 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

King Solomon begins to pray for neighbors, or people, who must take oaths before the altar in the temple. When two people disagree, those people must stand before the altar and take an oath to say that they are innocent. For more information on altar, temple, and oath, refer to the Master Glossary, and use the same words or phrases as you have in previous passages.

Stop here and discuss as a group what words or phrases you will use for neighbor. In this case, a neighbor can mean any person you interact with. Refer to the Master Glossary for more information about neighbor. Pause this audio here.

King Solomon asks Yahweh to then judge Yahweh's servants and condemn the guilty person and vindicate the innocent person. In this passage, a servant is someone who is devoted to Yahweh. You will remember that when someone judges another person, they decide if that person is guilty or innocent. To condemn a person means to punish a person. To vindicate means to say that someone is innocent. For more information on judge, servants, and guilty, refer to the Master Glossary, and use the same words or phrases as you have in previous passages.

King Solomon then speaks to Yahweh about future moments when the people of Israel might sin against Yahweh and another army defeats the Israelites. King Solomon asks Yahweh to hear the people when they pray for forgiveness and praise or thank Yahweh. King Solomon says that the people will pray in supplication in the temple. You will remember that supplication means to pray sincerely and humbly. In other words, the people of Israel need to show that they mean what they are praying. Then King Solomon asks Yahweh to forgive the people's sins. Sin is when a person does anything that goes against what Yahweh asks them to do. Use the same word or phrase for pray, forgive, supplication, and praise as you did in previous passages. Look up pray, forgive, and praise in the Master Glossary for more information.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for sin. Look up sin in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

King Solomon then says that Yahweh shuts up the heavens so that it does not rain**,** which means that Yahweh brings drought. King Solomon talks about "heavens" to mean the sky from which rain falls, and not the place where Yahweh lives.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for heaven. If you have already translated heaven to mean "sky" in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Look up heaven in the Master Glossary for more information. Pause this audio here.

King Solomon asks Yahweh to hear the prayers of the people and to send rain to the land that Yahweh gave to the people of Israel as their inheritance. In this passage, King Solomon also says that Yahweh calls the Israelites his inheritance, or treasure. This means that the land belongs to the Israelites because Yahweh has given it to them, and that the people of Israel belong to Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss as a group what words or phrases you will use for inheritance. If you have already translated this idea in another book of the Bible, use the same words that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

King Solomon then speaks to Yahweh about famine and plague. Famine happens when people experience a widespread loss of crops and food. Plague happens when diseases, illnesses, or disasters spread to many people. King Solomon also lists other things that cause famine, such as blight, mildew, grasshoppers, locusts, and a siege on a city. Blight is when hot and dry winds blow and destroy crops. Mildew is when plants become too damp and rot. To besiege a city means that people from another land surround a city so that people cannot leave the city or receive supplies from outside the city walls.

Stop here and look at the photos again of a land that has experienced blight or mildew. Look at photos of grasshoppers and locusts. Discuss as a group what words or phrases you will use for famine, plague, disease, blight, mildew, grasshoppers, locusts, and siege. Refer to the Master Glossary for more information about siege. If you have already translated these words in another book of the Bible, use the same words that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Yahweh knows people's hearts, and Yahweh wants everyone to fear Yahweh. You will remember that a person's heart is the way that people in the Bible talked about where a person's thoughts, feelings, and decisions happen. When someone fears Yahweh, they honor and respect Yahweh very much. Use the same word or phrase for heart as you have used previously, and refer to heart in the Master Glossary for more information.

Stop here and discuss as a group how you will translate that people should fear Yahweh. For more information, refer to "fear of God" in the Master Glossary. Pause this audio here.

King Solomon wants Yahweh to protect the land that Yahweh gave to the ancestors of the people of Israel. Ancestors are people in a person's family who are older than that person or lived before that person. Use the same word or phrase for ancestors as you used in previous passages.

King Solomon then speaks to Yahweh about the foreigner who might hear about Yahweh and travel to where the people of Israel live. King Solomon asks Yahweh to hear the foreigner when the foreigner prays towards the temple.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for foreigner. Look up foreigner in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same words that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

King Solomon then speaks to Yahweh about the people of Israel going to war. King Solomon asks Yahweh to bring victory to the people of Israel when they pray to Yahweh in the city and temple that honors Yahweh. Use the same word or phrase for Yahweh as you used in previous passages. Look up Yahweh in the Master Glossary for more information.

King Solomon then prays to Yahweh about captivity. King Solomon again knows that the people will likely sin and that captors, or enemies who capture the people, will take the people and hold them captive in another land.

Stop here and discuss as a group what words or phrases you will use for captive and captor. Pause this audio here.

King Solomon asks Yahweh to hear the people when they repent or turn away from their sins and return to follow Yahweh. The people stop acting in wrong and wicked ways.

Stop here and discuss as a group what words or phrases you will use for repent and wicked. Look up repent and wicked in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated these words in another book of the Bible, use the same words that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

King Solomon says that the people turn back to Yahweh with their "heart and soul," which means they show that they are completely devoted to Yahweh. Use the same words or phrases for "heart and soul" as you used in 1 Kings 2:4, and refer to the Master Glossary for more information about soul.

King Solomon prays that Yahweh will cause the captors to show mercy and that the people will leave other lands the way the people left Egypt. When a person shows mercy to another person, they act kindly toward them.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for mercy. Look up mercy in the Master Glossary for more information. Pause this audio here.

King Solomon compares Egypt to an iron-smelting furnace.

Stop here and look at the picture of an iron-smelting furnace again as a group. What words will you use to translate this? Pause this audio here.

King Solomon finishes his prayer by asking Yahweh to open Yahweh's eyes and ears to all that King Solomon and the people of Israel are praying. King Solomon uses the special name of Adonai Yahweh to show how much he honors Yahweh. Adonai means master. Use the same title for Adonai Yahweh here as you used in 1 Kings 2:26, and refer to Adonai in the Master Glossary for more information.

King Solomon reminds Yahweh that Yahweh chose Israel over all other nations to hold Yahweh's inheritance. Use the same word or phrase for nations as you used in previous passages.

Stop here and discuss as a group what words or phrases you will use for Adonai. Look up Adonai in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you used there.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 8:54–61
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 8:54–61 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 8:54–61 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 8:54–61 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the last passage, King Solomon prayed to Yahweh. Solomon asked Yahweh to help the people of Israel whenever the Israelites ask for help. In this passage, Solomon blesses the Israelites.

While Solomon prayed in the last story, Solomon first stood, then knelt in front of Yahweh's altar. At the beginning of this story, Solomon stands up to bless the Israelites. A blessing is similar to a prayer. Solomon asks that Yahweh do good things for the people of Israel. Solomon wants the close relationship between Yahweh and the Israelites to continue.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do you bless people in your culture? Why do you bless one another? How is a blessing different than a prayer? Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: When Solomon prays, Solomon stands with his arms raised, and then Solomon kneels. What do you do with your body when you pray? When Solomon blesses the people, Solomon stands and speaks loudly. What do you do with your body when you bless someone? Pause this audio here.

Solomon first "blesses" or worships Yahweh. Solomon thanks and praises Yahweh because Yahweh has caused the Israelites to have rest from war. Because of Yahweh, the Israelites do not have to fight their enemies but can live peacefully. Long ago, Yahweh promised the Israelites' ancestors that Yahweh would bring the Israelites to a place where the Israelites could live peacefully. Yahweh told this promise to Moses, Yahweh's servant. Then Moses told the Israelites what Yahweh had said. Yahweh made other promises to the Israelites as well, and Yahweh has kept every promise Yahweh made.

Solomon asks that Yahweh continue to be with the Israelites, just like Yahweh was with the Israelites' ancestors. Solomon also asks that Yahweh help the Israelites love and focus on Yahweh. Then the Israelites can live in a way that pleases Yahweh and obey Yahweh's commands. Remember that Yahweh also gave Moses and the Israelites' ancestors a set of instructions that showed the Israelites how to live in the right way. Solomon wants Yahweh to help the Israelites live in this good and right way.

Solomon also asks that Yahweh always remember what Solomon prayed today. Solomon is probably not worried that Yahweh will forget what Solomon said. Solomon wants Yahweh to pay attention to what the Israelites need and help the Israelites in every situation, just like Solomon asked when Solomon prayed. When other people groups see how Yahweh helps the Israelites, then those people will understand that Yahweh is the only true God. People will then know that all the false gods that other people worship are not real, like Yahweh is.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story about a time when God did something amazing in your community. How did people react? What did people say about God? Pause this audio here.

Finally, Solomon asks that the Israelites be completely faithful to Yahweh, their God. Right now, the Israelites honor and obey Yahweh. The Israelites have built the temple, offered sacrifices, and focused on Yahweh. The Israelites obey Yahweh's statutes and commands, so this is a beautiful and joyful time! Solomon says, "May you Israelites always act like this!"

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story about a time when someone blessed someone else during a celebration, like at a wedding. What did the person say in this blessing?

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 8:54–61 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has three scenes.

In the first scene: Solomon finishes his prayer and then blesses the Israelites. First, Solomon praises Yahweh and asks that Yahweh never leave the Israelites.

In the second scene: Solomon asks that the people follow Yahweh faithfully.

In the third scene: Solomon asks that Yahweh act justly to help Solomon and the Israelites, so that all people will know that Yahweh is truly God. The Israelites should be faithful in response to Yahweh.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Solomon
  • Yahweh, the Israelites' God
  • The Israelites
  • Moses, Yahweh's servant
  • The Israelites' ancestors
  • And all the people on earth

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

Remember that when Solomon began to pray a few passages ago, Solomon stood in front of the altar in the temple courtyard. Solomon lifted his hands towards God in heaven. In the first scene in this passage, the storyteller tells us that at some point during his prayer, Solomon knelt or bowed down on his knees. Now Solomon has finished his prayer, so Solomon stands up again. The storyteller uses two words to describe how Solomon prayed. These words mean about the same thing and are the same two words Solomon himself used in Solomon's prayer. The first word is a general word for prayer, while the second word is a special kind of prayer that means to ask Yahweh for help.

As Solomon stands now, Solomon speaks loudly and blesses all the people of Israel who had gathered at the temple. Solomon's blessing is like a prayer, because Solomon asks that Yahweh do certain things. But instead of talking to God, Solomon talks to the Israelites about God. Solomon says, "May Yahweh do these things, and may you, the Israelites, do these things."

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: When you bless someone, who do you speak to? Who do you ask to give good things to the person you bless? Discuss how you will show that Solomon's blessing is different than Solomon's prayer. Pause this audio here.

To begin his blessing, Solomon says, "Bless Yahweh!" To bless Yahweh means to worship and praise Yahweh, so some translations say "Praise Yahweh" here. Solomon both praises Yahweh and urges the Israelites to praise Yahweh too. Solomon praises Yahweh because Yahweh has given rest to the Israelites, which means that Yahweh has caused the Israelites to live safely and peacefully. The Israelites do not have to fear that enemies will attack.

This is exactly what Yahweh promised to the Israelites' ancestors many years ago. Yahweh made many promises to the Israelites, which Yahweh told to Moses, Yahweh's servant. Moses then told the Israelites' ancestors what Yahweh promised. Solomon says that not one word of Yahweh's promises has failed. This means that Yahweh kept every promise that Yahweh made.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do you talk about the way that someone does all that they promise to do? Discuss how you want to translate the way that Solomon says that Yahweh has kept all of his promises. Pause this audio here.

Yahweh has constantly been with and helped the Israelites' ancestors. Solomon asks that Yahweh continue to be with the Israelites now. Notice how Solomon says this in three different ways. Solomon asks that Yahweh be with the Israelites, that Yahweh never leave the Israelites, and that Yahweh never abandon the Israelites. Solomon uses these different ways to say the same thing to show how strongly Solomon wants this.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your language, how do you emphasize something that is important to you? When are some times when you would say what you want in a few different ways? Discuss how you will translate what Solomon says here. Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, Solomon asks that Yahweh "incline" or turn the people's hearts to Yahweh. Remember that people used the heart to represent the part of a person where a person feels, thinks, and makes decisions. When someone's heart is "for" someone, that person loves and is completely loyal to that person. So Solomon wants Yahweh to help the Israelites love and be loyal to Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your language, how do you talk about the way that someone deeply loves and is devoted and loyal to someone? Discuss how you want to translate the way Solomon wants Yahweh to turn the Israelites' hearts to Yahweh. Pause this audio here.

If the Israelites love Yahweh, the Israelites will want to walk in obedience. Remember that "to walk" means to live or behave. Solomon wants the Israelites to obey Yahweh and live as Yahweh wants. Solomon wants the Israelites to obey Yahweh's commands, ordinances, and statutes. Remember that people often use these words together to talk about all of Yahweh's commands. These are the religious laws that Yahweh gave to the Israelites' ancestors for all the Israelites to obey.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: If you want, review or remind each other how you have translated Yahweh's commands, ordinances, and statutes earlier in this book. Pause this audio here.

In the third scene, Solomon asks that Solomon's words be near to Yahweh. Solomon wants Yahweh to remember or pay attention to what Solomon has prayed in the last few passages. It is easy to remember or pay attention to something that is near you! Remember that Solomon prayed for things that would happen in the future, such as when the Israelites fight their enemies. When those things happen in the future, Solomon wants Yahweh to remember what Solomon asked today. Solomon wants Yahweh to remember Solomon's prayer during both the day and the night, which is another way to say "always."

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your language, how do you talk about the way someone does something and never stops doing that thing? How would you urge someone to remember what you said? Discuss how you want to translate the way Solomon asks that Yahweh always remember Solomon's prayer. Pause this audio here.

Solomon asks that Yahweh would act justly towards Solomon and the Israelites. Some translations say that Yahweh would uphold the cause of Solomon and the Israelites. Remember that Solomon also prayed this in the last passage. Solomon wants Yahweh to treat the Israelites fairly and help them. Solomon asks this respectfully and calls himself Yahweh's servant. Solomon wants Yahweh to help the Israelites so that other people can see what Yahweh is like. Then all the people groups all over the world will understand that Yahweh is truly God and that there is no other God.

Finally, Solomon wants the Israelites to fully commit their hearts to Yahweh. Solomon means that the Israelites should be completely faithful to Yahweh. Notice how Solomon emphasizes that Yahweh is the Israelites' God. The Israelites should be faithful to their own God! The Israelites should obey Yahweh's commands, just like the Israelites do now. The Israelites must live in a way that pleases Yahweh. When Solomon says this blessing, Solomon both asks Yahweh that this would happen, but also asks the people to continue to act in this good way.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Think about some times when you would bless people who are about to do something, such as at a wedding. Perhaps you would say something to the new husband and wife like, "May you be good and faithful to each other." When you say something like this, how do you expect the new husband and wife to respond? What do you want those people to do? What are some other blessings where you ask both God and people to do good things? Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 8:54–61 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has three scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Solomon
  • Yahweh, the Israelites' God
  • The Israelites
  • Moses, Yahweh's servant
  • The Israelites' ancestors
  • And all the people on earth

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

While Solomon prayed in the last story, Solomon knelt in front of Yahweh's altar and raised his hands to heaven. Then Solomon finished his prayer to Yahweh to ask for Yahweh's help. Then Solomon stood up in front of Yahweh's altar. As Solomon stood, Solomon loudly blessed all the people of Israel who had gathered there. Solomon said, "Praise Yahweh! Just as Yahweh promised, Yahweh has given his people, the Israelites, rest from war. Through his servant Moses, Yahweh gave many good promises. Yahweh has kept all of his promises."

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am so thankful for all the good things Yahweh promised to do for us. Yahweh has done everything he said he would do! or
  • I am so happy! We have finished this beautiful temple. We have a good land where we live peacefully. Yahweh has caused all this to happen! or
  • I want the Israelites to remember that Yahweh deserves all praise for the good things we have.

Continue the drama.

Then Solomon said, "May Yahweh our God be with us as Yahweh was with our ancestors. May Yahweh never leave us or abandon us. May Yahweh help us to love Yahweh, so that we live as Yahweh wants. May we follow the commands, decrees, and laws for justice that Yahweh gave to our ancestors."

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am glad that the Israelites remember the good instructions that I have given them. These instructions will help the Israelites live peacefully with each other and have a good relationship with me; or
  • I love the Israelites and want to have a good relationship with them. I want the Israelites to love me and be faithful to me; or
  • Solomon honors me as he prays and blesses the people. Solomon leads the Israelites well, and I am pleased with Solomon.

Continue the drama.

Then Solomon said, "May Yahweh always remember these prayers I have prayed. May Yahweh act justly to help me, his servant, and his people, the Israelites. May Yahweh help us with whatever we need each day. Then all the nations of the earth will know that Yahweh is truly God, and that there are no other gods. May you Israelites be completely faithful to Yahweh, our God. May you obey Yahweh's decrees and commands, just as you do today."

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I help the Israelites because I love the Israelites, but also so that the other nations will see who I am. I want all people everywhere to know that I am truly God; or
  • I listen when people pray and remember what they ask; or
  • I will continue to keep my promises and take care of the Israelites.

Ask the person playing the Israelites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am so thankful that Yahweh is our God. Yahweh is powerful and has given many good things to us; or
  • I am honored that Solomon blesses all of us. Solomon is a good king, and I am glad that Solomon leads us; or
  • I have seen and heard many amazing things today. Solomon has told and reminded us what Yahweh is like. I want to obey Yahweh because Yahweh is such a good God.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 8:54–61 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

At the beginning of this passage, Solomon finishes his prayer and supplications. These two words can both refer to the way a person speaks to God. The first word is a general word for prayer, while the second means the way people ask Yahweh for help. For more information on prayer, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same words or phrases for prayer and supplication as you used in previous passages.

While Solomon prayed, Solomon knelt or bowed down. A person can bow down when they kneel. Sometimes, a person who kneels also puts their head to the ground. For more information on kneel or bow down, refer to bow in the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for kneel or bow down as you used in previous passages.

Solomon also lifted his hands towards the heavens. Heaven or the heavens can mean both the sky and the place where God lives. In this case, Solomon seems to be lifting his hands toward God, who lives in heaven. For more information on heaven, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for heaven as you used in previous passages.

Then Solomon stands in front of Yahweh's altar. An altar is a place where people make offerings or sacrifices to God. This is the bronze altar in the temple courtyard. Yahweh is God's personal name. For more information on altar and Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for Yahweh's altar as you used in previous passages.

Solomon blesses the assembly of the people of Israel. You will remember that people form an assembly when they gather together for a special purpose. Use the same word or phrase for assembly as you did in previous passages. When a human speaks a blessing over another person, they pray that God will do good things for this other person. Solomon also blesses Yahweh. When a person blesses God, it means that they worship God and thank God for his goodness and blessings. So some translations say "Praise Yahweh" instead of "Bless Yahweh." For more information on bless or "praise," refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for the way people bless each other, and for the way people bless God, as you used in previous passages.

Solomon says that Yahweh has given his people rest, which means that there is no more war or evil things happening around them. Use the same word or phrase for rest as you used in 1 Kings 5:4.

Solomon talks about Yahweh's people, Israel. Here, the word Israel means the same as Israelites, or as "people of Israel." For more information on Israel, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for Israel as you used in previous passages.

Solomon calls Moses Yahweh's servant. Solomon also calls himself Yahweh's servant. This is a respectful way to refer to yourself. For more information on servant, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for servant as you used in previous passages.

Yahweh is the Israelites' God. For more information on God, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for God as you used in previous passages.

Solomon asks that Yahweh be with the Israelites like Yahweh was with the Israelites' "fathers," which here means ancestors. Use the same word or phrase for ancestors or fathers as you used in previous passages.

Solomon wants all the people groups of the earth to know Yahweh. A people group is a group of people who speak a common language and have a common culture. Although the storyteller does not say "nations of people," you may use the idea of a nation in your translation if you would like.

Stop here and discuss as a group how you will talk about the peoples or "nations" of the earth. If necessary, refer to nation in the Master Glossary for more information. Pause this audio here.

Solomon asks that Yahweh turn the Israelites' hearts to Yahweh. People use the "heart" to mean the place where a person thinks, feels, and decides things. So Solomon wants Yahweh to cause the Israelites to love and be loyal to Yahweh. Solomon also asks that the Israelites fully commit their hearts to Yahweh. Solomon wants the Israelites to be completely faithful to Yahweh. For more information on heart, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for heart as you used in previous passages.

Solomon wants the Israelites to obey Yahweh's commands, ordinances, and statutes. A command is a law, a rule, or an instruction. A statute may refer to a special required task or instructions for living in society. When Solomon says commands, ordinances, and statutes, Solomon means the set of instructions that God has given the people of Israel. Sometimes people also call this the "law." You may choose to summarize these three words and just talk about Yahweh's commands to his people. For more information on commands, ordinances, statutes, and law, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same words or phrases for commands, ordinances, and statutes as you used in previous passages.

Solomon asks that Yahweh would give justice to the Israelites or treat the Israelites in a just way. Justice is when someone treats someone else fairly according to the law. For more information on justice, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for justice as you used in previous passages.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 8:62–66
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 8:62–66 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 8:62–66 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 8:62–66 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the last passage, King Solomon blessed the Israelites because Solomon wanted the good relationship between Yahweh and the Israelites to continue. In this passage, Solomon and the Israelites continue to celebrate Yahweh at the new temple. Solomon and the Israelites offer many sacrifices to Yahweh and celebrate a festival. At the end of two weeks, all the Israelites go home happy.

This passage begins right after Solomon blessed the Israelites in the last passage. Solomon and the Israelites offer many sacrifices to Yahweh in Yahweh's presence. Remember that the temple is where Yahweh said Yahweh's presence would be. Solomon and the Israelites give peace offerings to Yahweh there at the temple. People would give peace or fellowship offerings to show that they wanted to have a good relationship with Yahweh and to celebrate their relationship with Yahweh. People would completely burn the fat and certain parts of the animal to show that they gave those parts to God. Then the people and the priests would cook and eat the rest of the meat. For the peace offerings, Solomon and the Israelites give 22,000 cattle, which may include both male and female animals as well as adult and young animals. Cattle are animals like bulls and cows.

Stop here and look at a picture of a bull and a cow as a group. Pause this audio here.

Solomon and the Israelites also give 120,000 sheep and goats. The cattle, sheep, and goats are all for the peace offerings. Solomon and the Israelites give these offerings to dedicate or use the temple for the first time.

Stop here and look at a picture of a sheep and a goat as a group. Pause this audio here.

On the same day that Solomon and the Israelites dedicate the temple, Solomon also consecrates part of the temple. Solomon makes the middle part of the temple courtyard "holy" or acceptable for worship. This part of the courtyard is probably the court of the priests or the inner court.

Stop here and look at a diagram of the temple as a group. Find the inner court. Pause this audio here.

Solomon makes this area holy because the Israelites need more space to offer their sacrifices. The Israelites bring so many sacrifices that the priests cannot put all of the sacrifices on the bronze sacrifice altar. The bronze altar is too small! So the priests probably make some temporary altars in the holy space in the courtyard for the different kinds of offerings.

Stop here and look at a picture of the bronze altar as a group. In your culture, what kind of special places do people have to offer gifts to gods? When are some times when people would make temporary or extra places to offer gifts? How do people make these special places ready for people to use? Pause this audio here.

There are the burnt offerings, which are animals that people cut up and burn on the altar. There are also the grain offerings, which are grain that people would mix together with sweet-smelling spices and oil. The priests would also burn this grain mixture on an altar. The priests would also burn the fat of the peace offerings on the altar. The fat pieces included the inner organs and the fat on the organs.

In this way, King Solomon, together with all the people of Israel, celebrates the festival. We know from the time of year that this was the Festival of Tabernacles. Remember that this was the festival where the Israelites remembered how God took care of the Israelites while they traveled and lived in tents instead of in permanent houses. This was a yearly festival when the Israelites would all gather at the place of worship for the celebration. The Israelites come from all over the land of Israel. Israelites come from as far north as Lebo Hamath or the entrance of Hamath. This place could be a trail through the mountains or a city about 290 kilometers north of Jerusalem. This was the northern edge of the land Yahweh promised to the Israelites.

Stop here and look at a map of Israel as a group. Find Lebo Hamath. Pause this audio here.

Israelites also come from as far south as the "Wadi" or Brook of Egypt. A wadi is a valley or deep gully that is usually dry in the dry season but has rushing water in the rainy season. This is probably the Wadi-el-Arish, which is about 150 kilometers southwest of Jerusalem. This was the southern edge of the land Yahweh promised to the Israelites.

Stop here and look at a map of Israel as a group. Find the Wadi-el-Arish. Look also at a picture of a wadi. Pause this audio here.

The Israelites celebrate for two weeks. The Israelites probably dedicate the temple for the first seven days. Then the Israelites celebrate the Festival of Tabernacles for the other seven days. At the end of that time, King Solomon sends all the Israelites back to their homes. The Israelites first bless King Solomon. In this time, people often blessed each other when they said goodbye. Then the Israelites travel home. The Israelites are happy because of what Yahweh has done for David and the Israelites. Remember that Yahweh promised David that David's son would build the temple. Solomon has built the beautiful temple, just like Yahweh promised!

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, how do you say goodbye to one another? How would you respectfully say goodbye to a leader? What kind of special greetings or goodbyes do you say at celebration times?

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 8:62–66 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has four scenes.

In the first scene: King Solomon and the Israelites offer many animals as peace offerings to dedicate the temple.

In the second scene: King Solomon makes part of the temple courtyard holy for all of the offerings.

In the third scene: King Solomon and the Israelites celebrate for 14 days.

In the fourth scene: At the end of the celebration, King Solomon sends the Israelites to their homes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • King Solomon
  • The Israelites
  • Yahweh
  • David, Yahweh's servant
  • And the priests

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, King Solomon and the Israelites offer sacrifices before Yahweh. Remember that when someone offers a sacrifice "before Yahweh," it might mean both that they offer the sacrifice to Yahweh and in Yahweh's presence, which is at the temple. In the next sentence, the storyteller says that Solomon offers many animals as peace offerings. The storyteller probably means that both King Solomon and all the Israelites offer these animals, since the storyteller then says that this is how King Solomon and the Israelites dedicate the temple.

King Solomon and the Israelites offer 22,000 cattle and 120,000 sheep and goats as peace or fellowship offerings. Although the storyteller does not mention the priests here, the priests and Levites probably sacrifice the animals. The people would help prepare the animals, but certain parts of the job were for the priests. The storyteller says that Solomon does things like offer sacrifices and dedicate the temple. Solomon probably had people to help him do these things, but Solomon was king and was in charge. Although the storyteller does not mention the priests in this passage, we know from the previous stories that the priests were there too.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story about a leader who did many things in your community. When are some times when you would mention all the people who helped the leader, and some times when you would just talk about how the leader led? Pause this audio here.

King Solomon and the Israelites offer the cattle, sheep, and goats to dedicate the temple. Here, to dedicate means to use something for the first time.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story about a time when your community finished a new building that had a specific purpose, like a church or a school. How did you celebrate that you could use the building for that purpose for the first time? How would you talk about the way that people celebrate that they can use something for the first time? Pause this audio here.

The second scene happens on the same day. King Solomon consecrates part of the temple courtyard. To consecrate something means to make it holy or acceptable for worship. Solomon consecrates this part of the temple courtyard to make more space for the sacrifices and offerings. Normally, the priests would offer sacrifices on the bronze altar. However, the altar is too small for all the sacrifices. So Solomon consecrates part of the temple courtyard. Solomon probably does a ritual to prepare that part of the courtyard, and then priests probably set up some temporary altars there.

The storyteller says that Solomon then offers the burnt offerings, grain offerings, and fat pieces from the peace offerings in that part of the courtyard. It is possible that Solomon helped with the offerings, but the priests and Levites probably did most of the work, as that was their job.

In the third scene, the storyteller reminds us that Solomon and the Israelites also celebrate the festival at this time. Remember that although the storyteller does not say the name of the festival, we know that this is the Festival of Tabernacles because of the time of year that this story happens. This was a normal gathering time for the Israelites, so Israelites had gathered from all over Israel. The storyteller says that people came even from the north and south borders, to show that people came from all over Israel.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Where are the borders of your people's land? What places in your land are farthest from where you live? In your language, how do you emphasize that people come from everywhere, even the farthest places? Pause this audio here.

The storyteller also tells us how long Solomon and the Israelites celebrate at the temple. The storyteller says the Israelites celebrate for seven days and seven days more, 14 days in all. This probably means that the Israelites dedicate the temple for seven days, then celebrate the festival for seven more days. Some translations just say the Israelites celebrate for seven days, not 14, but it is more likely that the Israelites celebrated for 14 days. Notice how the storyteller says that the Israelites celebrate the feast before "Yahweh our God." The storyteller's God is Yahweh, too!

Stop here and discuss as a group how you want to translate the way the storyteller describes how long Solomon and the Israelites celebrated. How would you talk about a celebration that happens in two parts? How would you talk about the total time of the celebration? Pause this audio here.

The fourth scene happens at the end of the festival. The storyteller calls this the eighth day, which is probably the eighth day from the beginning of the Festival of Tabernacles. So this is at the end of the 14 days. King Solomon sends the Israelites back to their homes. First, the Israelites bless King Solomon. This may mean that the Israelites thank Solomon or ask God for good things for Solomon. This is a normal way to say goodbye.

Then the people travel home. The people are joyful and glad in their hearts, which is another way to say that the people are happy. The Israelites are happy because of what Yahweh has done for Yahweh's servant David and Yahweh's people, the Israelites. The Israelites probably think about how Yahweh did what Yahweh promised David and caused the Israelites to build the beautiful temple.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story about a time when you celebrated something good with your community. How did you feel after you celebrated? Discuss how you want to translate about the way the Israelites were deeply happy and satisfied because of the good things Yahweh has done. Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 8:62–66 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has four scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • King Solomon
  • The Israelites
  • Yahweh
  • David, Yahweh's servant
  • And the priests

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Then King Solomon and all the people of Israel gave sacrifices to Yahweh. These sacrifices were peace offerings to Yahweh. The peace offerings were 22,000 cattle and 120,000 sheep and goats. So this was how King Solomon and all the Israelites dedicated the temple of Yahweh.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the Israelites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I respect Yahweh so much that I want to honor Yahweh through my offerings; or
  • I want to have a good relationship with Yahweh and will show this with my peace offering; or
  • This is a wonderful time for our people! We have worked together to build the temple, and now we celebrate Yahweh together as a people.

Continue the drama.

On that same day, King Solomon made part of the courtyard holy. This was the center area of the courtyard in front of the house of Yahweh. There, King Solomon offered burnt offerings, grain offerings, and the fat pieces from the peace offerings. Solomon sacrificed in the courtyard because the bronze altar that stood before Yahweh was not large enough. The altar was too small to hold the burnt sacrifices, the grain offerings, and the fat pieces from the peace offerings.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am amazed! The Israelites have brought so many offerings that we do not have enough space! I am so glad that the Israelites want to honor and celebrate Yahweh like this! or
  • I am thankful that Yahweh has treated us so kindly. I want to show how thankful I am; or
  • We have offered many sacrifices all day, and I am tired. But I am tired in a good way, because we have celebrated Yahweh and worked to honor Yahweh.

Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am so pleased that my people show that they want a good relationship with me. That is what I want too! or
  • The Israelites honor me like they promised they would. I also will protect and care for them as I promised I would; or
  • I love to see when my people are happy and celebrate together! I feel happy when I see them happy like this!

Continue the drama.

In this way, King Solomon, together with all the people of Israel, celebrated the festival. There was a huge crowd of people. The people came from all over Israel, from Lebo Hamath in the north to the wadi at the border of Egypt in the south. The Israelites feasted before Yahweh our God for a week. Then the Israelites feasted for another week. So in total, the Israelites feasted for 14 days.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the Israelites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am amazed that so many of our people have gathered here! or
  • I am so happy! I have seen friends and family who live in other parts of Israel. I have met new people, and we have celebrated together; or
  • I will never forget this celebration. I will tell stories to my children about how good and joyful this time is.

Continue the drama.

The day after, King Solomon sent the people to their homes. The people asked God to bless Solomon. Then the people went back to their homes. The people were very glad about all the good things Yahweh had done for David, Yahweh's servant and for the Israelites, Yahweh's people.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the Israelites, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am so happy and thankful for what Yahweh has done that I want to talk about Yahweh! I will remind everyone of what Yahweh has done! I will tell stories to my children and the people who come visit me; or
  • Though the celebration is over, I do not feel sad. This has been a good time for our people and I hope we will celebrate Yahweh again in the future; or
  • I have a long journey to travel home, but I am so glad I came to celebrate at the temple. I feel connected with my people. We have seen and reminded each other how amazing and good our God is.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 8:62–66 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

King Solomon and all the people of Israel offer sacrifices to Yahweh. For more information on king, Israel, and Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same words or phrases for king, Israel, and Yahweh as you used in previous passages.

A sacrifice is a gift to God. For more information on sacrifice, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for sacrifice as you used in previous passages.

Solomon and the Israelites offer three kinds of sacrifices. The first kind of sacrifice is a peace or fellowship offering. People gave peace offerings to show that they wanted to fellowship with Yahweh and to celebrate their relationship with Yahweh. People would burn certain fatty parts of an animal to give it to Yahweh, and then cook and eat the rest of the meat. For more information on "peace or fellowship offering," refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for peace offering as you used in previous passages.

The second kind of sacrifice is a burnt offering. When a person brings God a burnt offering, he kills an animal and burns this animal completely on an altar. For more information on burnt offering, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for burnt offering as you used in previous passages.

The third kind of sacrifice is a grain offering. A grain offering was when people brought gifts of grain, like wheat flour, which they sometimes mixed with oil, to Yahweh as an act of worship. The priest would burn the offering on the altar to symbolically give it to God.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for grain offering. Look up grain offering in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Solomon gives cattle, sheep, and goats for the peace offerings. This group of cattle may include male and female cattle, as well as young and adult bulls and cows. Use the same words or phrases for cattle, sheep, and goats as you used in previous passages.

Solomon and the Israelites dedicate the temple when they give these offerings. Here, to dedicate means to open or use something for the first time. To dedicate the temple means to worship God in the temple for the first time.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for dedicate. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

The temple is the place where people worship Yahweh. The temple's courtyard is an open area with a wall around it. This is where the bronze altar is. For more information on temple, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for temple and courtyard as you used in previous passages.

Stop here and look at the diagram of the temple again as a group as needed. Find the inner court. Pause this audio here.

Solomon consecrates part of the temple courtyard. When you consecrate something, you set that thing apart to do a special task for God. This thing is now holy and belongs to God.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for consecrate. Look up consecrate in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Normally, the priests offer the sacrifices on the bronze altar. People combine copper and tin to make bronze. The bronze altar is a place where people make offerings or sacrifices to God in the temple courtyard. For more information on bronze and altar, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same words or phrases for bronze and altar as you used in previous passages.

Solomon and the Israelites celebrate the festival. This is the Festival of Tabernacles. During the festival, the Israelites lived in booths or temporary shelters. This helped the Israelites to remember how God took care of the Israelites while they traveled in the wilderness and lived in tents instead of in permanent houses. For more information on the Festival of Tabernacles, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for festival as you used in previous passages.

A big assembly of people comes to celebrate the festival. An assembly is a special group of people who get together for a certain purpose, like a religious meeting. Use the same word or phrase for assembly as you used in previous passages.
