Israelites came from as far south as the Wadi of Egypt. A wadi is a valley or deep gully that is usually dry in the dry season but has rushing water in the rainy season.

Stop here and look at a picture of a wadi. Discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for wadi, or dry river valley. Use the same word or phrase for wadi as you used in previous books of the Bible. Pause this audio here.

Yahweh is the Israelites' God. For more information on God, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for God as you used in previous passages.

The Israelites bless Solomon and then travel home. When a human blesses another person, they pray that God will do good things for this other person. For more information on bless, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for bless as you used in previous passages.

As the Israelites travel, they are joyful and glad in their hearts. People use the word heart to mean the place where people think, feel, and decide things. Joyful and glad in your heart is another way to say that you are happy and content, or satisfied.

Stop here and discuss as a group how you will say that the Israelites were joyful and glad in their hearts. Pause this audio here.

The Israelites are happy because of the good things Yahweh has done for Yahweh's servant David and the Israelites. For more information on servant and Israelites, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same words or phrases for servant and Israelites as you used in previous passages.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

resource.label: Shepherd Guiding Sheep into the Sheepfold
Shepherd Guiding Sheep into the Sheepfold

title: 1 Kings 9:1–9
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 9:1–9 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 9:1–9 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 9:1–9 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the last story, Solomon and the Israelites dedicated the temple and celebrated a festival. Now, in this story, Yahweh speaks to Solomon in a dream. Yahweh commands Solomon to be faithful to Yahweh. Yahweh warns that if Solomon and his descendants are not faithful and if they worship other gods, then Yahweh will punish the Israelites.

This story begins after Solomon finishes all his building projects. This includes the temple and the palace, or the king's house. Yahweh appears to Solomon in a dream, just like Yahweh did at Gibeon, before Solomon built the temple.

Stop here and look at a map of Israel as a group. Find the town of Gibeon and the city of Jerusalem, where the temple and the king's palace are. Pause this audio here.

Yahweh tells Solomon that Yahweh heard what Solomon prayed when Solomon finished the temple. Yahweh is about to make an agreement with Solomon, where Yahweh will do certain things if Solomon and the Israelites do certain things. Yahweh assures Solomon that Yahweh accepts the temple. Yahweh makes this temple holy because Yahweh is present there. Yahweh promises that people will always be able to worship Yahweh at the temple. Yahweh also promises to always pay loving attention to the temple, just like Solomon asked.

Yahweh instructs Solomon to do certain things, too. Yahweh wants Solomon to be completely faithful to Yahweh. Solomon must live fairly and sincerely, just like David did. Solomon must obey Yahweh's commands, statutes, and ordinances. If Solomon does, then Yahweh will cause Solomon's descendants to rule over the people of Israel forever. Yahweh also promised this to David. Remember that David had already told Solomon about this promise that Yahweh made to David. Yahweh reminds Solomon of what Solomon already knows: Yahweh's promises and how Solomon should act.

But Yahweh also warns Solomon about what will happen if Solomon and Solomon's descendants reject Yahweh. Solomon and his descendants may choose to reject Yahweh and Yahweh's commands. Solomon and his descendants may choose to worship false gods as if they were the true God. If Solomon and his descendants act like this, then Yahweh will force the Israelites to leave the land of Israel, which Yahweh gave to the Israelites. Solomon and his descendants, who will be kings, are responsible for the whole nation. The way a king acts affects all of the people in the kingdom, not just the king. People will follow their king and do what the king does. And if a king is not faithful to Yahweh, Yahweh will punish the Israelites.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, how does the way a leader acts affect their people? Tell a story about a time when a leader acted in a bad way. What bad things happened to the people because of how the leader acted? How did people follow the leader's bad example? Pause this audio here.

If Solomon and his descendants disobey Yahweh, Yahweh will reject the temple, even though Yahweh made the temple holy with his presence. Then people everywhere will think that Israel is shameful and will mock Israel. The temple will no longer be a beautiful building but a pile of rocks, probably because enemies will destroy the temple. When other people pass by the temple, those people will look at the ruins and be amazed. Those people will make sounds like hisses to show how they feel shocked. Other people will no longer have respect for Israel, but scorn or treat Israel with contempt. Even other people groups would know that these disasters happened because the people of Israel were not faithful to Yahweh, but instead worshipped other gods.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Discuss what other people groups you respect, then discuss what people groups you do not respect. Why do you respect or not respect another group? How would you feel if other people groups did not respect your people group? Pause this audio here.

Yahweh's message is a promise and warning. Solomon must be faithful to Yahweh. If Solomon and his descendants are faithful, Yahweh will be with the Israelites just like Yahweh promised. If Solomon and his descendants are not faithful, Yahweh will punish the Israelites just as Yahweh promised.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Discuss the way a parent tells a child what will happen because of the way a child acts. Why does a parent tell the child about the good and bad things that will happen because of the way the child acts?

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 9:1–9 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has four scenes.

In the first scene: Solomon finishes the temple, Solomon's palace, and all his other building plans.

In the second scene: Yahweh appears to Solomon in a dream. Yahweh accepts the temple and promises to be present at the temple.

In the third scene: Yahweh reminds Solomon that Solomon must be completely faithful to Yahweh. If Solomon is faithful, then one of Solomon's descendants will always be king of the people of Israel.

In the fourth scene: Yahweh warns Solomon what will happen if Solomon and his descendants reject Yahweh. Yahweh will punish the Israelites, and even other people groups will talk about how the Israelites were not faithful to Yahweh.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Solomon
  • Yahweh
  • David
  • Solomon's descendants
  • The Israelites
  • And other people

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

The first scene begins some time after Solomon dedicated the temple. Solomon has achieved all he wanted to do, which probably means that Solomon has finished his building projects. Remember that when the storyteller says that Solomon built all these buildings, the storyteller means that Solomon led other people to build these buildings. The storyteller does not say exactly how many years pass between the last story and this story. The storyteller simply explains that Solomon has finished these building projects. Some people think that this story happens 13 years after Solomon finished the temple, as we know from an earlier story that it took people 13 years to build Solomon's palace.

In the second scene, the storyteller says that Yahweh appears to Solomon a second time, as Yahweh had appeared to Solomon at the town of Gibeon. Remember that the first time Yahweh appeared to Solomon was in a dream at Gibeon. So here, Yahweh appears to Solomon in a dream again. We do not know where Solomon is during this second dream.

The rest of the passage is what Yahweh says to Solomon. Yahweh says that Yahweh has heard Solomon's prayer and supplication. These are the same two words for prayer that the storyteller has used in the last few stories. Yahweh explains that Yahweh has answered Solomon's prayer, because Yahweh has consecrated the temple. Yahweh makes the temple holy when Yahweh puts his name at the temple. When Yahweh puts his name somewhere, that means that Yahweh's presence is at that place. The temple is now a place where people can worship and honor Yahweh.

Yahweh also answers Solomon's prayer to always be with the Israelites. Yahweh promises that Yahweh's eyes and heart will always be at the temple. Yahweh does not have a body as people do, but in this time, people would sometimes use a body part to represent a person as a whole. When Yahweh says his eyes will be at the temple, Yahweh means Yahweh will pay attention to the temple and the people in it, just like you pay attention to something you look at. When Yahweh says his heart will be at the temple, Yahweh means he will act in a loving way, because Yahweh feels love in his heart.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Discuss whether you ever use a phrase like "my eyes and heart will be there" in your language. Discuss how you want to translate the way Yahweh talks about his eyes and heart. Since these parts represent Yahweh as a whole, you could also choose to say that Yahweh himself will pay attention and show love. Pause this audio here.

In the third scene, Yahweh talks about what will happen if Solomon is faithful to Yahweh. Solomon must walk before Yahweh, or live and serve Yahweh, with integrity and uprightness of heart. Remember that people used the heart to talk about the place where people feel, think, and make decisions. When someone has "integrity and uprightness of heart," they act in a faithful, sincere, and honest way.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story about someone who was so completely faithful to someone that they acted sincerely and focused all their attention on that person. How would you describe that person? How do you talk about the way that people act faithfully or sincerely? Pause this audio here.

Solomon must also observe, or obey, Yahweh's commands, statutes, and ordinances. These are the instructions for right living that Yahweh gave to the Israelites. If Solomon is faithful to Yahweh and obeys Yahweh, Yahweh will establish Solomon's throne. People used a throne, or a king's special seat, to represent the way a king rules. Yahweh means that Yahweh will cause Solomon's descendants to rule over the people of Israel forever. This is the same thing Yahweh promised to David. Yahweh then repeats what Yahweh promised to David. David will always have an heir or descendant on the throne of Israel, who will rule over the people of Israel.

Stop here, and if helpful, review how David talked about Yahweh's promise in 1 Kings 2:4. Discuss how you want to translate Yahweh's promise to Solomon here. Discuss a family line of leaders in your people's history. How do you talk about the way that the family and their descendants continued to rule or lead? Pause this audio here.

In the fourth scene, Yahweh talks about what will happen if Solomon and Solomon's descendants are not faithful. Solomon and his descendants may choose to turn away from or reject Yahweh and not obey Yahweh's commands. Solomon and his descendants may choose to serve and worship other gods instead of Yahweh. If Solomon and his descendants act in this way, Yahweh will cut off the Israelites from Israel, which is the land Yahweh gave to the Israelites. To cut off means to punish or separate. So Yahweh will force the Israelites to leave their land.

Yahweh will also reject the temple. In the original language, people would also talk about the way that a man divorces and sends away, or rejects, his wife. People showed strong emotions and grief when they talked about someone rejecting a person or a place. This is the temple that Yahweh consecrated for his name, or made holy so Yahweh could live there. Yet if the Israelites are not faithful to Yahweh, then Yahweh will reject and get rid of the temple like someone gets rid of something they hate.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your language, how do you talk about the way that someone rejects something that they once loved? Discuss how you want to translate the way Yahweh says he will reject the temple. Pause this audio here.

Yahweh says Israel will become a proverb or byword. Both of these words here mean taunts or mocking comments. Yahweh means that people everywhere will ridicule and mock Israel because those people will not respect Israel.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your language, what words do you have that mean mocking taunts or negative comments? How do you talk about the way that people think another nation is weak or shameful? Pause this audio here.

The temple will become a heap of ruins, which means that the temple will no longer be a beautiful building but only a pile of rocks. When other people pass by, those people will feel shocked, horrified, and amazed. The people will make sounds to show that they are shocked and feel scorn for Israel.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What noises do you make or words do you say to show that something shocks or disgusts you? How do you talk about those noises or words? Discuss how you want to translate the way that people are shocked and show that they are shocked. Pause this audio here.

Yahweh then describes a conversation that people will have about Israel if Yahweh punishes Israel. Some people will ask why Yahweh forced the Israelites to leave and treated the temple like this. Other people will explain that Yahweh caused this disaster because the Israelites abandoned Yahweh, or were not loyal to Yahweh. People will say that even though Yahweh brought the Israelites' ancestors from Egypt to the good land of Israel, the Israelites chose other gods. The Israelites embraced, or became deeply loyal to, those false gods. The Israelites worshiped and served those false gods. Everyone will know that Yahweh caused this disaster, or evil thing, on the Israelites because of the way the Israelites acted.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 9:1–9 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has four scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Solomon
  • Yahweh
  • David
  • Solomon's descendants
  • The Israelites
  • And other people

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Solomon completed the temple for Yahweh as well as Solomon's own house. Solomon also finished all his other building plans. Then Yahweh appeared to Solomon a second time, in the same way Yahweh had appeared to Solomon at Gibeon. And Yahweh said to Solomon: "I heard your prayer. I know what you have asked me, so I have made this temple holy. You built this temple, and I made it holy because I will be present there. I will always be present at the temple and pay loving attention to it."

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am amazed and honored that Yahweh speaks to me again! or
  • I am so glad that Yahweh accepts the temple we built; or
  • I am thankful that Yahweh listens to me when I pray.

Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am happy that the Israelites built the temple as I asked; or
  • I want to have a good relationship with my people. That is why I asked the Israelites to build the temple. Now the Israelites have a place where they can interact with me and keep that good relationship; or
  • I care about the Israelites and Solomon. I will pay attention to them and respond with love when they speak to me at the temple.

Continue the drama.

Then Yahweh said, "As for you, Solomon, you must be faithful to me and live with complete and true sincerity. David, your father, lived in this way, and you must do the same. You must obey my commands and all the statutes and ordinances I give you. If you do all this, then I will always cause a man from your family line to rule over the people of Israel. I promised your father, David, that one of David's descendants would always be king of the people of Israel."

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I want Solomon to lead the Israelites well. If Solomon lives in the right way, which I have told the Israelites, then Solomon will be a good king; or
  • I will help Solomon live in the right way. Solomon must rely on me; or
  • I loved David, and I love Solomon. I want to care for and protect their family.

Continue the drama.

Then Yahweh said, "But if you or your descendants ever reject me and do not obey the commands and statutes I have given you and begin to serve and worship other gods, then I will remove the people of Israel from the land I have given them. I will reject this temple that I made holy for myself. Then all the other peoples will speak scornfully and mockingly of Israel. This temple will become a pile of stones. Everyone who sees it will be astonished. Those people will look at the temple with scorn and ask, 'Why did Yahweh do this terrible thing to this land and to his house?' Other people will answer, 'Because the people of Israel have rejected Yahweh their God, who long ago led their ancestors from Egypt. Instead, the Israelites began to worship and bow down before different gods. That is the reason why Yahweh punished the Israelites so harshly.'"

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing other people, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am shocked and horrified at what happened to the Israelites; or
  • The Israelites are a weak and shameful people! The Israelites did not worship their own God correctly! The Israelites could not even stay in their own land! or
  • If this is how Israel's God treats people who disobey him, then Israel's God must be powerful and worthy of respect.

Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I have heard this warning before, but it still shocks me. I hope my descendants remain faithful to Yahweh. I do not want these horrible things to happen to our people; or
  • Yahweh has made me wise. I am sure it will be easy to continue to be faithful to Yahweh; or
  • I am not worried about this warning. I serve Yahweh now, and I am sure I will continue to serve Yahweh in the future.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 9:1–9 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

This story begins after Solomon has finished Yahweh's temple and the palace, or the king's house. The temple is the place where people worship God. Yahweh is God's personal name. For more information on temple and Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same words or phrases for temple, Yahweh, and the king's palace as you used in previous passages.

Yahweh has heard Solomon's prayer and supplications. These two words can both refer to the way a person speaks to God. When someone prays a supplication, they are specifically asking God to do something for them. For more information on prayer, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same words or phrases for prayer and supplication as you used in previous passages.

Yahweh has consecrated the temple. When you consecrate something, you set that thing apart to do a special task for God. This thing is now holy and belongs to God. For more information on consecrate, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for consecrate as you used in previous passages.

Yahweh says his eyes and his heart will be at the temple. People use "heart" to mean the place where a person thinks, feels, and decides things. Yahweh means that Yahweh will always be present at the temple and pay loving attention to the temple. For more information on heart, refer to the Master Glossary.

Stop here and discuss as a group how you will say that Yahweh's eyes and heart will be at the temple. Pause this audio here.

Solomon must obey Yahweh's commandments, statutes, and ordinances. These are all the instructions that Yahweh has given to the people of Israel. Sometimes people also call these the law. For more information on commandments, statutes, and ordinances, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same words or phrases for commandments, statutes, and ordinances as you used in previous passages. Remember that you may summarize these three types of laws and just say that Solomon must obey Yahweh's instructions or laws.

Yahweh says that if Solomon is faithful to Yahweh, Yahweh will establish the throne of Solomon's kingdom over Israel. A kingdom is the territory or the people that a king rules. Israel here means the people of Israel. You will remember that people often use a throne or a king's special seat to represent the way a king rules. Yahweh means that a person from Solomon's family line will always be king over the Israelites. For more information on kingdom and Israel, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same words or phrases for kingdom and Israel as you used in previous passages.

Solomon and his descendants must always be faithful to Yahweh. A person's descendants are his children, grandchildren, and all future generations from the same family line. For more information on descendant, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for descendant as you used in previous passages.

If the Israelites worship and serve other false gods, Yahweh will punish the Israelites. In the original language, to worship literally means to bow down, but most translations use a more general word for worship. Sometimes people worship other spirit beings or idols as if they have power. When people worship those false gods, they give honor to those gods and serve those false gods as if they were the real, true God.

Stop here and discuss what word or phrase you will use for worship. For more information on worship, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for worship as you used in previous passages. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for gods or false gods. Look up gods in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Other people will know that the Israelites did not worship Yahweh, the Israelites' God, as the Israelites should. This is the more general word for Yahweh. For more information on God, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for God as you used in previous passages.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 9:10–25
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 9:10–25 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 9:10–25 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 9:10–25 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous story, Yahweh appeared to Solomon and promised him that if Solomon was faithful to Yahweh, then Solomon's descendants would reign over Israel forever. But if Solomon or his descendants turned away from Yahweh and worshiped other gods, Yahweh would remove the people of Israel from their land and reject the temple that Solomon had built for Yahweh. In this story, the storyteller tells us more about Solomon's building projects.

If you have already translated the book of 2 Chronicles, then you have already translated one version of this story. It might be helpful to go back and listen to how you translated that story. If the book of 2 Chronicles is available in your language, stop here and listen to 2 Chronicles chapter 8. Pause this audio here.

It took King Solomon 20 years to finish building the house of Yahweh and the king's house, or royal palace. In a previous story, the storyteller said that it took seven years to finish building the temple and 13 years to finish building the king's house. During that time, Solomon received wood and gold from Hiram, the king of Tyre. In return, Solomon gave Hiram 20 towns in a part of Israel called Galilee. This means that those towns became part of Hiram's territory, and Hiram was king over the people who lived there.

Stop here and look at a map that shows the location of Israel, including the location of Galilee and the city of Tyre. Pause this audio here.

Galilee was in the northern part of Israel. It was the closest part of Israel to Tyre. When King Hiram came to see the towns, he was not pleased with them. This is why Hiram gave them the name "Cabul." Cabul means "worthless" or "nothing."

King Solomon used forced labor, or slaves, for his building projects. This was very common in the ancient world. The people Solomon used as slaves were the descendants of the Canaanites that the Israelites had not been able to remove from the land. The laws that Yahweh gave to Israel through Moses did not allow Solomon to enslave Israelites to do forced labor, so Solomon made Israelites his soldiers, officers, and commanders.

Stop here and discuss these questions: How common is forced labor or slavery in your area? If it is not common now, was it ever common in the past? Which people were slaves? What do you think about the fact that King Solomon had slaves? Pause this audio here.

In this story, Solomon used forced labor to build the house of Yahweh, the king's house, and something called "the Millo." Bible scholars do not agree on what "the Millo" means. For this reason, some versions of the Bible translate this word to sound like the word in the original language and do not try to explain what it means. But some Bible scholars think the word means "terraces" or "landfill." A terrace is a flat area that people build on, or into, a hill.

Stop here and look at a picture of a terrace. Pause this audio here.

A landfill is an area where someone uses dirt or other materials to fill in a gap in the ground.

King Solomon also used forced labor to build a wall around Jerusalem and to build three cities that people called Hazor, Megiddo, and Gezer.

Stop here and look at a map that shows the location of the cities of Hazor, Megiddo, and Gezer. Continue to look at this map during the following discussion. Pause this audio here.

In those days, people commonly built walls around large cities. The walls provided protection from anyone who might try to attack a city. The cities of Hazor, Megiddo, and Gezer existed before Solomon became king. Hazor was in the far north near the border. Megiddo was located on a trade route. Gezer was near Jerusalem and also located on important trade routes. Earlier, the pharaoh of Egypt had destroyed Gezer and killed the Canaanites who lived there, so Solomon had to rebuild it. We do not know if Solomon had to rebuild Hazor and Megiddo as well, or if he just made them much bigger and stronger.

Solomon married Pharaoh's daughter. Pharaoh gave the city of Gezer to his daughter as a dowry, or wedding gift.

Stop here and discuss these questions: In your culture, when a woman gets married, what kinds of gifts do her parents usually give her? Do the bride and groom know ahead of time what she will receive from her parents, or is it a surprise? Pause this audio here.

King Solomon built other cities called Lower Beth Horon, Baalath, and Tamar. This last city is sometimes called "Tadmor." Lower Beth Horon is located near Jerusalem. Tamar is located south of Jerusalem near the wilderness of Judah. Bible scholars do not agree on where Baalath was located.

Solomon built cities to store extra goods in. These cities are called storage cities or supply centers. A king might store things like grain, oil, or wine in a storage city. Or the king might use a city to store materials for battle, like weapons and things that protect soldiers in battle. Solomon also built cities to house his army, and cities for his chariots and cavalry. A chariot is a kind of cart that horses pull. People used chariots to fight battles. Normally, at least two soldiers rode in a chariot. One soldier controlled the horses while the other soldier attacked his opponents. But some chariots could hold more than two soldiers. A soldier who rides in a chariot is called a "charioteer."

Stop here and look at a picture of a chariot and charioteers. Pause this audio here.

Cavalry is a word for soldiers who ride horses. Riding on a horse gave a soldier many advantages. Horses are much faster than people, so the rider could easily catch up to opposing soldiers and kill them. Because the rider is up higher on the horse, he can see farther than the people on the ground. A soldier on a horse might shoot arrows at his opponents, or he might ride closer to them and try to stab them with his sword.

Before Solomon finished building the King's house, Solomon's wife, the daughter of Pharaoh, was living in the City of David. Most Bible scholars believe this means that Pharaoh's daughter was living in an older part of Jerusalem, where King David had lived, and that Solomon built his palace in a different part of Jerusalem. When Pharaoh's daughter moved into the king's palace, she just moved from one part of Jerusalem to another.

The storyteller finishes this story by telling us that Solomon regularly came to the temple and made offerings to Yahweh. This reminds us that, at least for a time, Solomon did everything Yahweh required him to do.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 9:10–25 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has three scenes.

In the first scene: After King Solomon finishes building the temple of Yahweh and his own palace, Solomon gives Hiram, king of Tyre, 20 cities in the region of Galilee. But Hiram does not like these cities.

In the second scene: The storyteller tells us about the cities that King Solomon rebuilds or fortifies throughout his territory. King Solomon uses the remaining Canaanite people as forced labor for his building projects, but Solomon assigns Israelites to work as his officials, commanders, and soldiers.

In the third scene: After King Solomon's building projects are completed, Solomon moves the daughter of Pharaoh, his wife, from the City of David into the palace in Jerusalem. Solomon also makes regular offerings at the temple of Yahweh.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Solomon, Hiram, Pharaoh, and Pharaoh's daughter
  • The Amorite, Hittite, Perizzite, Hivite, and Jebusite people
  • Solomon's Israelite soldiers, government officials, commanders, captains, charioteers, and horsemen
  • And Yahweh

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, at the end of 20 years, King Solomon had finished building the temple of Yahweh and his own palace. Solomon built the palace, the temple, and other buildings using cedar and cypress logs, which he received from King Hiram of Tyre. These are the same two kinds of wood that you already translated in chapter five of 1 Kings. It might be helpful to go back and listen to 1 Kings chapter five to remind yourselves of how you decided to translate the words for these two kinds of trees.

Stop here and listen to 1 Kings chapter five, specifically the part when Solomon sends a message to King Hiram to ask for cedar and cypress wood, and Hiram agrees to send the wood. Pause this audio here.

Solomon gave Hiram 20 cities in the region of Galilee. This means that those cities now became part of Hiram's kingdom, and Hiram was their king instead of Solomon. Most Bible scholars think that Solomon did this as a form of payment for all the wood and gold that Hiram had sent to Solomon for his building projects. But when Hiram came from Tyre to see the cities that Solomon gave him, Hiram was not pleased with the cities. Hiram did not think that the cities were very valuable to him. Hiram told his feelings to Solomon using a question that he did not expect Solomon to answer. Hiram said, "What are these cities that you have given me, my brother?" This question means, "These cities that you have given me are not good" or "I do not like these cities that you have given me."

When Hiram calls Solomon "my brother," it does not mean that Hiram and Solomon are related to each other. It was common at that time for kings who were allies to address each other like this. It was a way that those kings could show that they saw each other as equals and that they were on friendly terms with each other.

Stop here and discuss this question: Under what circumstances might you call someone who is not a relative by the words "brother," "sister," "cousin," "aunt," "uncle," "mother," or "father"? If it is unnatural or unclear in your language for Hiram to call Solomon "my brother," what other word could you use to show that they had a friendly relationship as equals? Pause this audio here.

Because Hiram did not like these cities, he gave the region an insulting name. The word Cabul in the original language sounds like the word for "worthless" or "like nothing." Since your hearers most likely will not understand what the word Cabul means, you might need to say something like "Hiram called that area Cabul, which means 'worthless.'" The storyteller also says that this area is called Cabul "to this day." This phrase means that when the storyteller wrote this information down, the Israelites still called that area by the name Cabul.

At the end of this scene, the storyteller says, "And Hiram sent the king more than 4,000 kilograms of gold." Bible scholars do not agree on what this sentence means. Some scholars think it is a summary statement, meaning that over the course of 20 years, Hiram sent Solomon 120 talents, or a total of 4,000 kilograms, of gold. Other scholars think that Hiram sent Solomon more than 4,000 kilograms of gold as payment for the 20 cities in Galilee.

In the Book of Chronicles, the storyteller shares the same story but with a small difference. The Book of Chronicles says King Hiram gave some towns to King Solomon. This version of the story says Solomon gave some towns to Hiram. Bible scholars suggest two possible explanations: maybe the two kings traded land, or maybe Solomon let Hiram rule some towns for a while and later took them back. Either way, the two kings likely exchanged land to settle the cost of the supplies Hiram gave Solomon for his building projects.

Stop here and describe a situation in which two people in your culture might exchange property in order to settle an agreement. Under what circumstances might they do this? What kind of items might they exchange? Do they exchange the property permanently, or can they return the property? Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, the storyteller tells the events out of order. First, he tells us that Solomon used forced labor to build the temple of Yahweh, the king's house, the Millo, the wall of Jerusalem, and the cities of Hazor, Megiddo, and Gezer. Then, the storyteller explains why Solomon needed to rebuild Gezer: some time before, the Egyptian army had attacked the city, burned it down, and killed all the Canaanites who were living there. Next, the storyteller tells us about more cities that Solomon built up throughout his kingdom. Then the storyteller jumps back in time again in order to tell us about the descendants of the Amorites, Hittites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites who were still living in the land of Israel. Solomon made these people into slaves, while the Israelites served as his officers, commanders, and officials.

In your language, it might not be possible to tell a story out of order like this. Even if it is possible, it might be too confusing for your hearers to hear the story told in this way. If this is the case, you may choose to rearrange the events of the story so that they are in the order in which they happened.

Stop here and discuss whether you will tell this story in the order that the events occurred, or if you will tell the story in the order the storyteller records the events in the Bible. Pause this audio here.

This is the order in which the events of this story occurred:

First, the Israelites did not completely remove all the Canaanites from the land. When Joshua led the Israelites into the land, the Israelites allowed some Canaanites to continue living there. So when Solomon became king, there were still some descendants of the Amorites, Hittites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites who lived in Israel. King Solomon made these people his slaves, and he appointed Israelites as their supervisors.

Next, King Solomon began to build the temple of Yahweh in the fourth year of his reign. Solomon used forced labor to build it. We do not know exactly when Pharaoh destroyed the city of Gezer, but we do know it was before his daughter married Solomon. We also do not know exactly when Solomon married Pharaoh's daughter, but it was probably before Solomon finished building the temple. So this is the most likely order of events: Solomon began to build the temple of Yahweh, then Pharaoh attacked and destroyed the city of Gezer, then Solomon married Pharaoh's daughter, and she received Gezer as a wedding gift.

Finally, Solomon finished building the temple of Yahweh in the 11th year of his reign. Then Solomon began to build the king's house, the Millo, the wall around Jerusalem, and all of the other cities the storyteller mentions in this story.

The Canaanites were the people who lived in the Promised Land before the Israelites. The Canaanites were all descendants of a man named Canaan. Yahweh told the Israelites to drive all of the Canaanites out of the land, but the Israelites did not completely drive them out. Some groups of Canaanites still lived in parts of the Israelite territory, including in the city of Gezer.

After Pharaoh, the king of Egypt, burned down Gezer and killed all the Canaanites who lived there, he gave the city to his daughter as a wedding gift. This means that even though the king of Egypt was the one who conquered the city, he gave it to the Israelites. After Solomon rebuilt the city, the people who lived there would need to grow crops, raise livestock, and make various products that were necessary to live. It is possible that Pharaoh's daughter might have received a share of these products, or the people of the city might have paid a tax to her.

Stop here and discuss what would be the best way in your language to say that Pharaoh gave a city to his daughter as a wedding gift. Pause this audio here.

It was against the law that Moses gave to the Israelites for the Israelites to do forced labor. If Solomon used Israelites for his building projects, he would have to hire them as regular workers or use them for a short amount of time. So Solomon forced the Canaanites to do the hard physical labor. You might have heard a few different ways to say this when you listened to different versions of this story. Some translations say that Solomon conscripted or drafted the forced labor. Other translations say that Solomon imposed, raised, or levied forced labor, while yet others simply say that King Solomon used forced labor to build all these structures. Each of these is just a different way to say that King Solomon forced the non-Israelites in his territory to do this hard physical work, and that they did not have the option to say no.

Stop here and discuss this question: What are some ways in your language that you can say that a leader forced someone to work, and that person did not have the option to say no or to change jobs? Pause this audio here.

At this point in scene two, the storyteller says that the Amorites, Hittites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites are still slaves in Israel "to this day." This statement means that those people were enslaved in Israel at the time that the storyteller wrote this story down. It does not mean that they are slaves in Israel right now.

This scene contains the first mention of the Millo. Bible scholars do not agree on what this term means. The Millo is a man-made structure, but Bible scholars are not sure what it is or where it was located. Some scholars think the Millo might be a large set of terraces that was located on the east side of Jerusalem. A terrace is a flat surface just outside a building. People usually build it with supports underneath. People often build a terrace on a hill or slope so that people have a flat place to walk or sit.

Stop here and look at a picture of a terrace. Pause this audio here.

Other scholars think the Millo is related to the word for "landfill," meaning a place that had to be filled in with earth in order to build something else on it. Jerusalem has many hills and valleys, so it is possible that Solomon had to fill in some places with earth in order to build the structures he wanted. Because there is so much uncertainty about this structure, many translations of the Bible simply call this place "the Millo" and do not try to explain what it is. It is acceptable to translate this word as "terrace" or "landfill," or to call it "the Millo."

Solomon built up a city near Jerusalem called Lower Beth Horon. There was another city about two kilometers away called Upper Beth Horon. Lower Beth Horon was at the bottom of a valley, while Upper Beth Horon was at the top of the valley. The city that is called Tamar or Tadmor is in the wilderness, or desert. A wilderness is a place without much water, trees, or plant life. It is very difficult to grow crops in the wilderness, and people usually do not live there.

Stop here and discuss this question: In your language, how would you describe an area of land that does not have much water, trees, or plant life? Pause this audio here.

Solomon built storage cities, or supply centers, all through the territory that he controlled. A king might store things like grain, oil, salt, or wine in a storage city. The king stored these things for use in the event of famine or shortage. The king might also use a city to store supplies for battle, like weapons and items that soldiers use to protect themselves. If the country went to war, these supplies would help the people to be better prepared than their enemy.

Stop here and discuss these questions: In your country, how do people store excess goods? Where do people store the goods? Who controls the goods? How does the military or national government store its battle supplies? Pause this audio here.

Near the end of scene two, the storyteller uses six different words and phrases to describe the roles that the Israelites filled for Solomon's building projects. The first phrase is "men of war," which means men who fight in wars. It can also be translated as "soldiers" or "fighting men."

Stop here and try to think of as many words as you can in your language that have this meaning. Pause this audio here.

The second word is "servants." This word can mean many things, from a household helper to a government official to a military commander. It describes anyone who does something for someone else. A servant is also usually of a lower social or political rank than the person he or she serves. In this case, these servants were most likely some sort of government officials.

Stop here and discuss how you might call these servants who served King Solomon as government officials. Pause this audio here.

The third word is for a person who is a leader of a group of people. You might have heard this word translated as "commanders" or "officers" who lead groups of men in the army.

Stop here and try to think of as many words as you can in your language that have this meaning. Pause this audio here.

The fourth word is a word for someone who probably works directly for the king in the army, in special fighting units. You may have heard this word translated as "captain."

Stop here and try to think of as many words as you can in your language that have this meaning. Pause this audio here.

The fifth phrase means "commanders of chariots." If an army has many chariots, it needs someone to tell the charioteers what to do in the battle. The commander of chariots probably rode in a chariot himself, but his job was to tell the other charioteers where to go and what to do. A commander of chariots would be a mid to high-ranking military official.

Stop here and discuss how to translate "commanders of chariots" in your language. Pause this audio here.

The sixth word is a word for men who work with horses. This might be soldiers who ride on horses or in chariots. You might have heard this word translated as "cavalry," "horsemen," or "charioteers."

Stop here and try to think of as many words as you can in your language that have this meaning. Pause this audio here.

The total number of Israelites who worked as supervisors for Solomon's building projects was 550 men.

In the third scene, as soon as the king's house is complete, Solomon moves Pharaoh's daughter from her temporary house in the City of David into the king's house that he built for her. Then Solomon begins to construct the Millo. After the temple of Yahweh was complete, Solomon went to the temple three times per year to make burnt offerings and peace offerings on the altar of the temple. Solomon also burned incense on the altar of incense. These rituals were important parts of the way the Israelites worshipped Yahweh. Incense is a substance that creates a lot of smoke and smells good when people burn it. There were many different kinds of incense in Israel, but priests had to use a special type of incense in the temple of Yahweh. The storyteller probably includes this statement to let the audience know that, for many years, Solomon faithfully worshipped Yahweh.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 9:10–25 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has three scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Solomon, Hiram, Pharaoh, and Pharaoh's daughter
  • The Amorite, Hittite, Perizzite, Hivite, and Jebusite people
  • Solomon's Israelite soldiers, government officials, commanders, captains, charioteers, and horsemen
  • And Yahweh

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

It took Solomon 20 years to build the temple of Yahweh and the king's house. King Hiram of Tyre had provided Solomon with all the cedar and cypress wood and all the gold he wanted for this work. After it was finished, King Solomon gave Hiram 20 towns in the region of Galilee. Hiram went to see these towns, and Hiram did not like them. Hiram said to Solomon, "My brother, these towns you have given me are not good." And Hiram called that area "Cabul," which means "good for nothing," and people still call it that name today. And Hiram had sent Solomon over 4,000 kilograms of gold.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King Hiram, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel angry at Solomon for giving me such awful cities; or
  • Maybe Solomon is not as honest and trustworthy as I thought he was; or
  • I feel cheated because, after all I did to help Solomon, Solomon gave me these worthless cities.

Ask the person playing King Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel satisfied that I have completed such wonderful building projects; or
  • I feel sad that I gave up Israelite territory; or
  • I feel frustrated that Hiram is not happy with the towns I gave him.

Continue the drama.

This is the account of the forced labor that King Solomon raised to build the house of Yahweh, the king's house, the Millo, the wall of Jerusalem, and the cities of Hazor, Megiddo, and Gezer. Pharaoh, the king of Egypt, had attacked and captured Gezer. Pharaoh killed the Canaanite people who lived there and burned down the city. Then Pharaoh gave the city to his daughter as a wedding gift. So Solomon rebuilt the city of Gezer. Solomon also built up the towns of Lower Beth Horon, Baalath, and Tamar in the wilderness within his land. Solomon built storage cities and cities where he could keep his chariots and horses. Solomon built everything he desired in Jerusalem and Lebanon, and throughout his entire kingdom.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel proud that I am able to build so many cities in my country; or
  • I feel confident that all the supplies I have stored up for my people will keep us secure; or
  • I feel strong because I have such a big army and so many horses.

Ask the person playing Pharaoh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am glad that I was able to make a marriage alliance with Solomon; or
  • I feel resentful that a bunch of slaves escaped from my country and have grown into a powerful nation; or
  • I do not trust Solomon. Therefore, I will not give my daughter a very good wedding gift.

Ask the person playing Pharaoh's daughter, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am angry that my father made me marry this Israelite and leave Egypt; or
  • I feel honored to be a queen in Israel; or
  • I am glad that I have an entire town to generate income and goods for me.

Continue the drama.

For his forced labor, Solomon used the descendants of the Canaanites whom the Israelites had not killed when they took possession of their land. These included Amorites, Hittites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites, and their descendants are still slaves to this day. But Solomon did not make slaves of the Israelites. The Israelites served as his soldiers, government officials, officers, captains, commanders of chariots, and horsemen. There were 550 officials in charge of the forced labor working on Solomon's building projects.

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing the Amorite, Hittite, Perizzite, Hivite, and Jebusite people, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • I am angry that my people are now slaves; or
  • I feel hopeless. First, our people lost our land, and now we are forced to build temples and palaces for our enemies; or
  • I hate the Israelites. I wish I could get away from them.

Ask the people playing the Israelite soldiers, officers, commanders, and charioteers, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel happy and secure because our army is so big and strong; or
  • I feel hopeful that our country has a bright future because of all the things that King Solomon has done; or
  • I am excited to see the beautiful new cities and storehouses that King Solomon has built!

Ask the person playing King Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel happy that I have made my country so strong and wealthy; or
  • I am glad that I can put these Canaanites to good use; or
  • I am pleased with the work my people have done.

Continue the drama.

Then Solomon moved his wife, Pharaoh's daughter, from the City of David to the new house he had built for her. Then he built the Millo. Three times each year, Solomon made burnt offerings and peace offerings on the altar that he had built for Yahweh. Solomon also burned incense to Yahweh. And so Solomon finished the work of building the house of Yahweh.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the Pharaoh's daughter, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel happy and comfortable in this beautiful palace Solomon built for me; or
  • I miss Egypt, and I wish I could visit my home; or
  • This place is nice, but not as beautiful as the palaces back in Egypt.

Ask the person playing King Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I hope that Yahweh is pleased with the temple and the offerings we are making to him; or
  • I hope that my wife is happy with the palace I have built for her; or
  • I look forward to a time when I can rest now that all these building projects are done.

Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you thinking or feeling?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am happy because Solomon built a beautiful temple; or
  • I am pleased that Solomon and the people are worshipping me as I told them to; or
  • I am glad that Solomon was wise enough to build storage cities and cities for the chariots and horses. These provisions help keep my people safe.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 9:10–25 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

At the end of 20 years, after Solomon had finished building the temple of Yahweh and the palace, or king's house, and Hiram the King of Tyre had supplied Solomon with cedar and cypress wood and all the gold he wanted, King Solomon gave Hiram 20 cities in the land of Galilee. Cities are usually settlements with walls around them. Translate cities the same way you did in previous passages.

Be sure to translate Yahweh, Temple of Yahweh, and palace the same way you have in previous passages. See the Master Glossary for more information on temple and Yahweh.

You translated cedar and cypress wood in a previous passage.

Stop here and listen to 1 Kings chapter five, specifically the part when Solomon sends a message to King Hiram to ask for cedar and cypress wood, and Hiram agrees to send the wood. Be sure to translate these words the same way here as you did there. Pause this audio here.

Galilee is a region in the northern part of Israel.

Stop here and discuss what word you will use for Galilee. See the Master Glossary for more information on Galilee. If you have already translated Galilee in another book of the Bible, make sure to translate it the same way here as you did there. Pause this audio here.

Hiram had sent King Solomon 120 talents of gold. A talent is around 34 kilograms, so this is around 4,000 kilograms of gold total.

Stop here and discuss as a group how you will talk about 120 talents. For more information about talent, refer to the Master Glossary. Pause this audio here.

This is the account of the forced labor that King Solomon conscripted to build the house of Yahweh, the palace, the Millo, the wall of Jerusalem, and the cities of Hazor, Megiddo, and Gezer.

Forced labor is a situation where a person in power, usually a king or a chief, forces other people to work for him, and those people are not free to say no. Different translations use different words to describe the process of a leader forcing people to work against their will, or they might just say, "King Solomon used forced labor."

Stop here and decide how you will say that King Solomon used forced labor to build the house of Yahweh and his other building projects. Pause this audio here.

Bible scholars are not sure what "the Millo" is. Many Bible translation teams have chosen to find a word that sounds similar to the word in the original language, rather than trying to describe what "the Millo" is. However, a few versions of the Bible translate this word as "terraces," "supporting terraces," or "landfill." Any of these translation choices is acceptable.

Stop here and decide whether you will translate "the Millo" to sound like the original language, or if you will translate it to mean "terrace" or "landfill." If you have already translated the Millo in 1 Chronicles 11:8, translate it in the same way here. Pause this audio here.

Hazor, Megiddo, and Gezer are all names of cities.

If you need to remember how you translated these names, stop here and listen to Joshua chapter 11 to remember how to translate Hazor, listen to 1 Kings chapter 4 to remember how to translate Megiddo, and listen to the last story in Judges chapter 1 to remember how to translate Gezer. Pause this audio here.

Pharaoh, the king of Egypt, had attacked and captured Gezer. Pharaoh killed the Canaanite population and burned down the city. Then Pharaoh gave the city to his daughter as a wedding gift when she married Solomon. So Solomon rebuilt the city of Gezer.

The word "Pharaoh" is a special word for the king of Egypt. Be sure to translate Pharaoh the same way you did in previous passages. See the Master Glossary for more information on Pharaoh.

The Canaanites were the descendants of a man named Canaan. The Canaanites lived in the land of Israel before the Israelites lived there.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for Canaanite. Look up Canaan in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Solomon also built up the towns of Lower Beth Horon, Baalath, and Tamar in the wilderness within his land. Solomon built storage cities and cities where he could keep his chariots and horses. A storage city was a place where the leaders of a country could store extra goods, like food, clothing, or weapons.

Stop here and decide how you will translate storage city. Pause this audio here.

For his forced labor, Solomon used the descendants of the Canaanites whom the Israelites had not killed when they took possession of their land. These included Amorites, Hittites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites, and their descendants are still slaves to this day. But Solomon did not make slaves of the Israelites. The Israelites served as Solomon's fighting men, government officials, commanders, captains, commanders of chariots, and horsemen. There were 550 officials in charge of the forced labor working on Solomon's building projects.

Stop here and review and decide what words you will use to translate fighting men, government officials, army commanders, captains, commanders of chariots, and horsemen. Pause this audio here.

Then Solomon moved his wife, Pharaoh's daughter, from the City of David to the new house he had built for her. Then Solomon built the Millo. Three times each year, Solomon made burnt offerings and peace offerings on the altar that he had built for Yahweh. Solomon also burned incense to Yahweh. And so Solomon finished the work of building the house of Yahweh. Be sure to translate "City of David" the same way you did in previous stories.

Be sure to translate burnt offering, peace or fellowship offering, and altar the same way as you did in previous passages. See the Master Glossary for more information on burnt offering, peace or fellowship offering, and altar.

Incense is a word for a substance that smells good when people burn it. Israelites used incense for religious purposes. Be sure to translate incense the same way as you did in previous passages.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 9:26–10:13
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 9:26–10:13 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 9:26–10:13 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 9:26–10:13 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous story, King Solomon built cities throughout the land of Israel. Solomon made storage cities and cities for his chariots and horsemen. In this story, Solomon's ships bring back gold, almugwood, and precious stones. The Queen of Sheba comes to visit Solomon in Jerusalem and brings him gifts. When the Queen of Sheba goes home, Solomon sends a large number of gifts with her.

In this story, the storyteller tells the events of the story out of order. First, he tells us that Solomon built ships in a city called Ezion Geber. These ships sailed to Ophir and brought back gold. Next, the storyteller tells us about the Queen of Sheba's visit to Solomon. Then the storyteller jumps back in time to add that Solomon's ships had not only brought back gold from Ophir, but also high-quality wood and precious stones. At the end of the story, Solomon gave the Queen of Sheba everything she had asked for, and then the queen went back to her own country.

A ship is a large boat that sails on the ocean. The boat had to be large enough to sail over large ocean waves and carry many people and a large amount of supplies for many days on the ocean.

Stop here and look at a picture of a ship on the ocean. Pause this audio here.

Solomon built ships in a city called Ezion Geber, near Elath in the land of Edom. Edom is the land where the descendants of Esau lived. King Solomon controlled this land. This area was south of Israel on the coast of the Red Sea.

Stop here and look at a map of Israel and Edom that shows the location of Ezion Geber and Elath. Pause this audio here.

King Hiram sent his experienced sailors to help Solomon's men with the ships. A sailor is a person who knows how to sail a ship. Ships on the ocean were very big and needed many sailors to do different jobs required to sail the ship. Solomon's ships sailed to Ophir and brought back over 14,000 kilograms of gold, almugwood, and precious stones. Bible scholars are not sure where Ophir was located.

Kings often hosted the leaders of other countries in the palace. One king might send his officials to another king's palace to make an agreement, end a conflict, or show respect. Solomon probably hosted leaders from many other countries during his 40 years as king, but there was something special about the Queen of Sheba's visit. The Queen of Sheba heard not only about Solomon's great riches and wisdom, but also about Solomon's God, Yahweh. A queen could be the wife of a king, but a queen could also rule a country by herself. In Bible times, some queens had a lot of power, while other queens did not. Bible scholars are not sure where Sheba was located. Some scholars think Sheba was located in Arabia, while other scholars think it was located in northeast Africa.

The Queen of Sheba visited Solomon because she wanted to ask him questions to see how wise Solomon really was. When the Queen of Sheba talked with Solomon, he answered all her questions and showed her that he was indeed very wise.

Stop here and discuss these questions: Tell a story in your language about someone who tests the wisdom or knowledge of another person. How does this person test the other person? Do they use riddles, stories, questions, or something else? If you do not know any stories like this, do you know any riddles or questions that people use to show whether a person is wise and learned? If so, say them out loud. Pause this audio here.

The Queen of Sheba brought gifts for Solomon. It was common at that time for kings and queens to give each other gifts to show respect for each other. It was also a way for the king or queen to show how wealthy he or she was. The Queen of Sheba brought gold, spices, and precious stones. The queen brought these things on animals that people called camels. A camel is a large animal that is common in places where the weather is hot and dry. Camels can survive for a long time without drinking water. Camels are also easy for people to train and can carry a lot of weight, so people in this part of the world often used camels to carry goods while traveling.

Stop here and look at a picture of a camel next to a person. Pause this audio here.

King Solomon also gave gifts to the Queen of Sheba. This passage does not tell us what Solomon gave her, but it does say that Solomon gave her everything she desired and asked for.

The Queen of Sheba was very impressed by Solomon, his palace, and everything in it. The Queen of Sheba said that, although she had heard very good things about Solomon, what she saw in person was even better than what people had reported to her. The storyteller says that when the Queen of Sheba saw everything in Solomon's palace, she was so amazed that it left her breathless.

Stop here and tell a story about someone who saw something incredible and felt amazed. Pause this audio here.

Next, the Queen of Sheba blessed Solomon and his officials, and she praised Yahweh who made Solomon king. Then the queen left and returned to her own country.

Stop here and discuss these questions: In your culture, when a person is successful, to whom do they give credit for their success? To whom do others give credit for that person's success?

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 9:26–10:13 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

If you tell this story in the same order as it occurs in 1 Kings, there are four scenes.

In the first scene: Solomon builds ships at Ezion Geber. The ships sail to Ophir and bring back gold.

In the second scene: The Queen of Sheba visits Solomon, brings him gifts, and tests him with questions to see how wise Solomon really is.

In the third scene: The storyteller tells us that Solomon's ships also brought back almugwood and precious stones. Solomon used the wood to build supports for the temple of Yahweh and the king's house, and also harps and lyres for the musicians.

In the fourth scene: King Solomon gives the Queen of Sheba everything she asks for, and then the queen goes back to her own country.

If you decide to tell this story in the order that the events happened, then scenes one and three are combined, while scenes two and four are combined.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Solomon
  • Hiram
  • Hiram's sailors
  • Solomon's men
  • The Queen of Sheba
  • The Queen of Sheba's servants
  • Solomon's officials
  • Palace servants
  • Cupbearers
  • And musicians

If you have already translated the book of 2 Chronicles, then you have already translated one version of this story. It might be helpful to go back and listen to that story now to remember how you translated it.

Stop here and listen to the first part of 2 Chronicles chapter 9, if it is available in your language. Pause this audio here.

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In this story, the storyteller tells the events of the story out of order. In your language, it might not be possible to tell a story out of order like this. Even if it is possible, it might be too confusing for your hearers to hear the story in this way. If this is the case, you may choose to rearrange the events of the story so that they are in the order in which they happened.

Stop here and discuss whether you will tell this story in the order that the events happened, or if you will tell the story in the order that the storyteller tells the events in the Bible. Pause this audio here.

If you choose to tell the events of the story in the order in which they happened, this is the order you would use:

First, Solomon built a fleet of ships in Ezion Geber. King Hiram sent his sailors to sail to Ophir in these ships with Solomon's men. The men brought back gold, almugwood, and precious stones. Next, Solomon used the almugwood to build the temple of Yahweh and the royal palace, and also to make musical instruments.

Then, the Queen of Sheba visited Solomon. The queen brought gifts to Solomon and tested him with hard questions. Solomon answered all of the queen's questions, and she realized that Solomon was truly very wise. When the Queen of Sheba saw that Jerusalem, the temple of Yahweh, and the palace were very beautiful, she was amazed. The queen said that even though she heard wonderful reports about Solomon and Yahweh, the reports were not half as good as what she saw when she went there. So the Queen of Sheba praised Solomon and Yahweh. Solomon gave the queen everything she wanted, then she went back to her own country.

In the first scene, Solomon built ships in Ezion Geber, near Elath in the land of Edom. When Solomon built his ships, he did not do the work himself. Rather, Solomon hired workers to build the ships for him. Ezion Geber was located on the coast of a large, narrow body of water. You might have heard different names for this body of water when you listened to three different versions of the story. Some versions call it the Red Sea, others call it the Reed Sea, and some call it the Gulf of Aqaba. "Red Sea" and "Reed Sea" are two different ways to translate the meaning of the name in the original language. "The Gulf of Aqaba" is a modern-day name of this body of water. Any of these translation choices is acceptable.

A group of many ships is called a fleet of ships. If your language has a special word for a group of ships, you may use that word here. If not, you can say "Solomon built many ships in Ezion Geber."

Stop here and discuss how you could say "a fleet of ships" in your language. Pause this audio here.

A sailor is a person who knows how to sail a ship. A large ship required many sailors to do different tasks to control the ship. In the original language, the storyteller calls these sailors "men who know the sea."

Stop here and discuss how you will translate "men who know the sea" into your language. Pause this audio here.

Hiram's sailors and Solomon's servants sailed to Ophir and brought back 420 talents, or over 14,000 kilograms, of gold. Ophir is the name of a place. Bible scholars do not know for sure where Ophir was located.

In the second scene, the Queen of Sheba heard that King Solomon was very famous. A queen was usually the wife of a king, but sometimes a queen was the ruler of a country. A queen was usually the highest-status female in the country. If a queen was the wife of a king, then only the king had a higher status than the queen did.

Stop here and decide what word you will use for "queen." Pause this audio here.

The Queen of Sheba heard that Solomon was famous. When someone is famous, it means that even people in faraway places know all about that person. A person might be famous because people admire them for things like intelligence, strength, kindness, or wisdom. Or a person might be famous because of things they have done, like fighting in battles or becoming rich. People far away from Israel heard stories about Solomon's wealth and wisdom and were impressed. When people thought about Solomon, they probably thought he was a good king and a good person.

Stop here and talk about someone in your culture who is famous. Why is that person famous? What words or phrases do you use when you talk about that person? Which of these words could you use to describe how famous Solomon was? Pause this audio here.

Solomon was famous because of his wealth and wisdom, but he was also famous because of "the name of Yahweh." The name of Yahweh represents Yahweh's character—his power, love, and all the things Yahweh has ever done. The name of Yahweh also represents the presence of Yahweh. Solomon's fame was because of all the things that Yahweh did for Solomon. Yahweh blessed Solomon and gave him the things he needed to build the temple, palace, and other buildings. Yahweh gave Solomon what Solomon needed to make Israel strong and secure. Also, Solomon's success made Yahweh look good. When other people saw Solomon's achievements and wisdom, they would think, "Yahweh is a very good God."
