Stop here and discuss how you might translate the phrase "concerning the name of Yahweh" into your language. Pause this audio here.

The purpose of the Queen of Sheba's visit was to test Solomon's wisdom with hard questions. The queen wanted to find out for herself if Solomon really was as wise as everyone said he was. The queen chose questions that would be difficult to answer because the answers needed good understanding or insight.

Stop here and discuss how you will say in your language that the Queen of Sheba tested Solomon's wisdom with difficult questions. Pause this audio here.

The Queen of Sheba did not travel by herself. The queen brought along a large number of servants and attendants. The queen probably also brought soldiers to protect her from attacks on the way. You might have heard different words for this group of people when you listened to three different versions of this story. There are many different ways to say that many people traveled with the Queen of Sheba. This was a special group of people who traveled with the queen to serve her and protect her during her journey.

Stop here and discuss how you could say in your language that many people traveled with the Queen of Sheba. Pause this audio here.

The Queen of Sheba brought many gifts to Solomon. The queen used camels to carry these goods. A camel is an animal that lives in hot, dry areas. A camel can travel for a long time without drinking water. Camels are very useful animals when people travel through dry areas.

Stop here and look again at a picture of a camel. If your language does not have a word for "camel," discuss how you could translate the word "camel" into your language. Pause this audio here.

The Queen of Sheba brought 120 talents, or about 4,000 kilograms, of gold, precious stones, and spices as a gift for Solomon. These spices were not the kind of spices people used to cook food. These were sweet-smelling substances that people used for perfume, incense, or to prepare a body for burial.

Stop here and discuss this question: What words can you think of in your language for these kinds of spices? Pause this audio here.

The Queen of Sheba asked Solomon hard questions and talked with him about many things. The queen spoke to Solomon about all the things that were in her mind or heart.

In the original language, the heart is the source of all feelings and thoughts. What is in a person's heart shows who that person truly is.

Stop here and discuss these questions: In your culture, what part of the body do you say is the source of all feelings and thoughts? What sayings do you have about a person's feelings and thoughts coming from this part of the body? How could you translate the phrase "she spoke to him about all that was in her heart" into your language? Pause this audio here.

King Solomon told the Queen of Sheba everything she wanted to know. Different Bible translations say this in different ways. Some say "nothing was too difficult for him to explain to her" or "he gave her accurate answers to every question." Other translations might say "there was nothing hidden from the king which he did not explain to her." These are different ways to say the same thing—that Solomon answered all of the queen's questions, and none of the questions were too hard for Solomon.

After the Queen of Sheba talked with Solomon, she realized that Solomon truly was very wise. Some translations say that the Queen of Sheba saw Solomon's wisdom. This might sound awkward in your language, because no one can actually see wisdom. In the original language, to see something can also mean to experience something, know something, or understand something. So this is a way to say that the Queen of Sheba realized that Solomon was very wise.

Stop here and discuss how you will say "the Queen of Sheba had seen all the wisdom of Solomon" in your language. Pause this audio here.

The Queen of Sheba saw Solomon's palace and thought it was grand and beautiful. The queen was impressed by the quality, quantity, and variety of food that Solomon served to his guests and officials. The queen was also impressed by "the seating of his officials." Bible scholars are not sure what this phrase means. This phrase might mean that Solomon had many officials and that they all ate luxurious meals in Solomon's palace. This would show that Solomon was very rich, because the king had to pay for all those people to eat in his palace. It might mean that Solomon provided very beautiful places for his officials to live. This would also show that Solomon was rich and generous to the people who worked for him. It might also mean that the offices and rooms where Solomon's officials worked were very beautiful.

The Queen of Sheba was impressed by the clothing of Solomon's servants. One type of servant was a cupbearer. The cupbearer was a high-ranking official in the king's court. The cupbearer's main job was to taste the king's drink before the king did to make sure it was not poisoned. But the cupbearer could have other jobs as well.

The Queen of Sheba was impressed by the sacrifices that Solomon made at the temple of Yahweh. Some translations include the idea that Solomon had to go up a stairway in order to go up to the temple and make the sacrifices.

After the Queen of Sheba saw all the beauty of Solomon's palace, the storyteller says that "there was no spirit left in her." In the original language, the word for spirit is also the word for breath. To have no breath left means the person is stunned or shocked by what they have just experienced. In this case, the Queen of Sheba was shocked in a good way. Everything the queen saw and heard, from King Solomon's wisdom to the beauty of his palace, was amazing to her. The queen had expected to see good things in Solomon's palace, but everything was much better than she thought it would be. You might have heard the storyteller say that "she was overwhelmed" or "she was breathless and amazed."

Stop here and recall the story you told before about someone who saw something incredible and felt amazed. What words or phrases do you use to describe how that person felt? Pause this audio here.

The Queen of Sheba told Solomon that she had heard reports of his wisdom and the things Solomon had done, but that she did not believe them.

The Queen of Sheba did not believe the reports of Solomon's wisdom and achievements until she came and saw it with her own eyes. This is a common way to say that the Queen of Sheba found out for herself that all the reports were true. In this case, the Queen of Sheba saw Solomon's palace, heard his wisdom, ate his food, and saw all the riches that Solomon had.

After the Queen of Sheba talked to Solomon and saw his palace in person, the queen told Solomon that people had not told her even half of what Solomon had done. This means that, although the queen heard many good things about Solomon, those things were not enough to fully describe how wise Solomon was and how wealthy and beautiful Jerusalem was. The report the queen heard did not have nearly enough information to describe how wonderful Solomon's palace was and how much wisdom Solomon had.

Stop here and discuss how you will describe what the Queen of Sheba meant when she said that people had not told her even half of what Solomon had done. Pause this audio here.

When you listened to three different versions of this story, you might have heard the Queen of Sheba say, "How happy your people must be!" or you might have heard her say, "How happy your wives must be!" The word for "people" and the word for "wives" are very similar in the original language, and Bible scholars do not agree on which one is meant here. We recommend that you base your translation on whichever word is used in the Bible that your local church uses.

When the Queen of Sheba says that Solomon's people or wives must be happy, she means that they are very blessed or fortunate because of all the good things Solomon has done for them. The queen means that these people have good lives, with everything they need to be prosperous and secure. These people do not have to worry about their safety or their basic needs, and they can be proud of their people and their country. This would make them feel happy most of the time.

Stop here and discuss how you might translate "How happy your people must be" into your language. Pause this audio here.

Now, the Queen of Sheba says, "Blessed be Yahweh your God." This statement has a different meaning than when the Queen of Sheba said that Solomon's people were blessed. This statement is a way to praise or worship Yahweh. This statement means that Yahweh is a very good God and deserves to be praised because of that goodness.

The Queen of Sheba says that Yahweh set Solomon on the throne of Israel. This is just a way to say that Yahweh made Solomon king of Israel. Only a king can sit on the throne, so if Yahweh seated Solomon on the throne, that means that Yahweh made Solomon the king. Finally, the Queen of Sheba says that Yahweh made Solomon king over Israel because Yahweh loves Israel forever and wants Solomon to do justice and righteousness.

In ancient Israel, the king was the highest human authority in the land. The king was responsible for everything that happened in his country. The people could come to the king if they needed an impartial judge to help with a conflict, solve a problem, or right a wrong. The king was supposed to protect those who were poor and weak and punish those who did wrong. The king was also supposed to always follow Yahweh's law and do what was right. This is what "justice and righteousness" means.

Stop here and discuss these questions: In your country, or in your people group, who is responsible to help people resolve conflict, solve problems, or fix bad situations? How do you describe the role that these people fill? Pause this audio here.

In scene three, the storyteller goes back in time. The storyteller tells us that Solomon's ships that sailed to Ophir and brought back gold also brought back precious stones and almugwood. Bible scholars are not sure exactly what kind of wood this was, but it was good for constructing large buildings like the temple of Yahweh and the royal palace. Most translations of the Bible call it "almugwood." The word "almug" sounds like the word for these trees in the original language. But a few translations call it "sandalwood" or "juniper wood." Sandalwood is a kind of tree that grows in Asia. It is one of the most expensive kinds of wood in the world.

Stop here and look at a picture of a sandalwood tree. Pause this audio here.

Juniper trees grow all over the world. Some of them are very strong wood that is useful for building.

Stop here and look at pictures of a sandalwood tree and a juniper tree. Pause this audio here.

When you listened to three different versions of the passage, you might have heard that "the king used the almugwood to make supports for the house of Yahweh." Or you might have heard that the king made railings for the house of Yahweh. Or you might have heard that the king made steps for the house of Yahweh. Bible scholars are not sure what kind of structure Solomon made from the almugwood, so any of these translation choices is acceptable.

The Israelites also used almugwood to make musical instruments. King Solomon used the wood to make harps and lyres for his musicians. A harp is a musical instrument that people make of wood. A harp uses strings to make sound. A harp is usually shaped like a triangle. A lyre also uses strings to make sound, but the people arrange the strings differently on the wood. A lyre is usually shaped more like a rectangle or oval.

Stop here and look at a picture of a harp and a lyre. What kind of stringed instruments do you have in your country? Are any of those instruments like a harp or a lyre? Pause this audio here.

Solomon's ships brought back precious stones. These are stones that are rare and very beautiful. Because these stones are rare and beautiful, people will pay a lot of money for them. Solomon used precious stones to decorate the temple of Yahweh. The priests' garments also contained precious stones. Women liked to use precious stones to make jewelry.

Stop here and discuss these questions: What precious stones do your people value? What do you use the stones for? Pause this audio here.

In scene four, Solomon gave the Queen of Sheba gifts. It was the normal custom for kings and queens to give each other gifts as a sign of respect and also to show how rich they were. This is what the storyteller calls the "royal generosity." But King Solomon gave the Queen of Sheba even more than the usual amount of gifts. Solomon gave the queen whatever she requested. Then the Queen of Sheba returned to her own land with her servants.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 9:26–10:13 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has four scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Solomon
  • Hiram
  • Hiram's sailors
  • Solomon's men
  • The Queen of Sheba
  • The Queen of Sheba's servants
  • Solomon's officials
  • Palace servants
  • Cupbearers
  • And musicians

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

King Solomon built a fleet of ships at Ezion Geber, near Elath in the land of Edom, along the shore of the Red Sea. King Hiram sent crews of sailors to sail the ships with Solomon's men. The men sailed to Ophir and brought back to Solomon more than 4,000 kilograms of gold.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am so happy to have these wonderful ships; or
  • I am glad that the trip to Ophir was successful; or
  • I am grateful to Hiram for sending his sailors to teach my men how to sail the ships.

Ask the people playing Solomon's men, "What are you feeling or thinking?" They might answer things like:

  • I feel safe and secure now that our people have these ships; or
  • I have never seen so much gold in my life; or
  • I am proud to serve a king who is as strong and wise as King Solomon.

Ask the people playing Hiram's men, "What are you feeling or thinking?" They might answer things like:

  • I wish we did not have to work so hard only to give all the goods to Solomon; or
  • We have an important alliance with Solomon's people, so we should work together with them; or
  • If King Hiram wants us to train Solomon's men to sail, then I will do my duty and train them.

When the Queen of Sheba heard that Solomon was famous because of the name of Yahweh, she came to test him with difficult questions. The queen arrived in Jerusalem with a large group of attendants and a great caravan of camels loaded with spices, large quantities of gold, and precious stones. When the queen met with Solomon, she talked with him about everything that was in her heart. Solomon had answers for all of the queen's questions. Nothing was too hard for the king to explain to the Queen of Sheba. When the Queen of Sheba realized how wise Solomon was, and when she saw the palace he had built, the excellent food on his tables, the seating of his officials, the service of his waiters and their excellent clothing, his cupbearers, and the sacrifices Solomon made at the temple of Yahweh, she was completely amazed.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am glad that this important woman has come to visit me; or
  • I am pleased that the Queen of Sheba is impressed with my palace; or
  • I enjoy sharing wisdom with others.

Ask the person playing the Queen of Sheba, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am impressed with this man's wisdom; or
  • I feel happy and honored to have the chance to talk with such an insightful person; or
  • It feels good to know someone who can answer these tricky questions; or
  • I have never seen a person or a place like this before!

The Queen of Sheba said to the king, "The report that I heard in my country about your achievements and your wisdom is true. But I did not believe these things until I came and saw them myself. In fact, not even half was told to me! You are much more wise and rich than anyone told me! How happy your people must be! How happy your officials must be, who continually stand in your presence and hear your wisdom! Blessed be Yahweh your God, who has delighted in you and placed you on the throne of Israel. Because of Yahweh's eternal love for Israel, he has made you king to do what is right and just."

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am very pleased that my country and I have made such a good impression on this woman; or
  • I like the Queen of Sheba. Perhaps we can be allies with her country; or
  • I am glad that the Queen of Sheba is giving glory to Yahweh, because he is the one who made all of this possible.

Ask the person playing the Queen of Sheba, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Solomon's God has blessed him abundantly; or
  • I want my country to be as rich and as beautiful as Israel; or
  • I cannot say enough good things about Solomon and his achievements.

Ask the people playing King Solomon's officials, palace staff, and cupbearers, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • I feel proud to serve such a great king in such a great nation; or
  • I am amazed that our food and clothes are even better than those in Sheba; or
  • I am pleased that this foreigner is so impressed with our country.

And also, the ships of Hiram, which brought gold from Ophir, brought in from Ophir a very great amount of almugwood and precious stones. The king made the almugwood into supports for the temple of Yahweh and for the royal palace, and he also made lyres and harps for the musicians. It was the best almugwood that anyone ever brought into Israel. No one has seen anything like it ever again.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am very pleased to have all this almugwood for the temple of Yahweh and my palace; or
  • These ships are very good for our country. Perhaps we should build more; or
  • I feel humbled that I can provide all these beautiful things for my people.

Ask the people playing the musicians, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am so happy to have such a beautiful new harp to play; or
  • With all these new harps and lyres, our musicians will be truly excellent; or
  • I feel proud to serve such a great king as Solomon.

King Solomon gave the Queen of Sheba everything she asked for, even more than all the royal bounty that he had generously given her. Then the queen and her servants returned to the land of Sheba.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am glad that I was able to give the Queen of Sheba so many generous gifts; or
  • I hope the Queen of Sheba sees Israel as an important and powerful country; or
  • Thank you, Yahweh, for blessing me with wisdom that impresses the leaders of other countries.

Ask the person playing the Queen of Sheba, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel pleased to bring all these amazing gifts back to my country; or
  • This was a very successful trip; or
  • How will I ever explain to my people all the wonderful things I saw in Solomon's country?

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 9:26–10:13 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

If you have already translated the book of 2 Chronicles, then you have already translated one version of this story. It might be helpful to go back and listen to that story now to remember how you translated it.

Stop here and listen to the first part of 2 Chronicles chapter 9, if it is available in your language. Pause this audio here.

King Solomon built a fleet of ships at Ezion Geber, near Elath in the land of Edom, along the shore of the Red Sea. Use the same word you have used previously for king, and remember that king is in the Master Glossary.

If necessary, stop here and listen to Deuteronomy chapter 2, if it is available in your language, to remember how you translated Ezion Geber and Elath. Pause this audio here.

If necessary, stop here and listen to the beginning of Genesis chapter 36, if it is available in your language, to remember how you translated the name Edom. Pause this audio here.

If necessary, stop here and decide what word or phrase you will use for ship. Pause this audio here.

King Hiram sent crews of sailors to sail the ships with Solomon's men. The men sailed to Ophir and brought back to Solomon 420 talents, or more than 14,000 kilograms, of gold. If you use the word for talent, use the same word that you have used previously, and refer to talent in the Master Glossary for more information.

Stop here and decide what word you will use to translate sailor. Pause this audio here.

When the Queen of Sheba heard that Solomon was famous because of the name of Yahweh, she came to test Solomon with difficult questions. Be sure to translate Yahweh the same way you did in previous passages. See the Master Glossary for more information on Yahweh.

Stop here and decide how you will translate queen and famous. See the Master Glossary for more information on a queen. Pause this audio here.

The Queen of Sheba arrived in Jerusalem with a large group of attendants and a great caravan of camels loaded with spices, large quantities of gold, and precious stones. When the queen met with Solomon, she talked with him about everything that was in her heart. Solomon had answers for all of the queen's questions. Nothing was too hard for the king to explain to her. When the Queen of Sheba realized how wise Solomon was, and when she saw the palace he had built, the excellent food on his tables, the seating of his officials, the service of his waiters and their excellent clothing, his cupbearers, and the sacrifices Solomon made at the temple of Yahweh, she was completely amazed. Remember to translate palace, or king's house, and temple the same way you did in previous passages. See the Master Glossary for more information on temple and camel.

If necessary, stop here and listen again to Genesis chapter 40 to remember how you translated cupbearer. Pause this audio here.

The Queen of Sheba said to the king, "The report that I heard in my country about your achievements and your wisdom is true. But I did not believe these things until I came and saw them myself. In fact, not even half was told to me! You are much more wise and rich than anyone told me!" Be sure to translate wisdom the same way that you have in previous passages. See the Master Glossary for more information on wisdom.

The Queen of Sheba also said, "How happy your people must be! How happy your officials must be, who continually stand in your presence and hear your wisdom! Blessed be Yahweh your God, who has delighted in you and placed you on the throne of Israel." In this case, to say "Blessed be Yahweh" is a way to worship Yahweh. Be sure to translate "throne" the same way you did in previous passages. Be sure to translate "Bless, or praise Yahweh" the same way you have in previous passages. See the Master Glossary for more information on bless and praise. Also, be sure to translate God the same way you have in previous passages. See the Master Glossary for more information on God and Israel.

The queen also said, "Because of Yahweh's eternal love for Israel, he has made you king to do what is right and just."

Stop here and discuss how you will translate that the king does what is right and just. Be sure to translate the ideas of "justice" and "righteousness," or doing what is right and fair, the same way that you did in previous passages. See the Master Glossary for more information on justice and righteousness. Pause this audio here.

And also, the ships of Hiram, which brought gold from Ophir, brought in from Ophir a very great amount of almugwood and precious stones.

Stop here and decide how you will translate almugwood into your language. Will you use a word for an existing kind of wood from your language, or will you translate it to sound like the word in the original language? If necessary, you can look again at the picture of a sandalwood tree and a juniper tree to help decide what you will call this kind of wood. Pause this audio here.

The king made the almugwood into supports for the temple of Yahweh and for the palace, and he also made lyres and harps for the musicians. It was the best almugwood that people ever brought into Israel, and nothing like it has ever been seen again.

Stop here and decide what word you will use for lyre and what word you will use for harp. Look again at pictures of a harp and a lyre if you need to. Pause this audio here.

King Solomon gave the Queen of Sheba everything she asked for, even more than all the royal bounty that he had generously given her. Royal bounty means all the gifts that a king usually gave to another king or queen who visited his palace.

Stop here and decide how you will translate royal bounty into your language. Pause this audio here.

Then the Queen of Sheba and her servants returned to the land of Sheba.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 10:14–29
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 10:14–29 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 10:14–29 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 10:14–29 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous story, the storyteller told us about the time the Queen of Sheba visited King Solomon. In this story, the storyteller tells us more about how rich Solomon was. The storyteller includes a description of the beautiful throne that Solomon built inside the palace, the gifts he received from visitors, and the number of chariots and horses that Solomon owned.

In Bible times, people considered kings to be powerful and important if they were very rich. This story shows us that King Solomon was extremely rich. However, Yahweh had told the Israelites that their king was not supposed to own many horses, store large amounts of silver and gold, or marry a large number of wives. The kings of Israel were supposed to be different from the kings of the people around them. When Solomon built up a large army of horses and a large amount of silver and gold, Solomon disobeyed Yahweh.

Solomon received 666 talents of gold, which was almost 23,000 kilograms of gold each year. This is a very large amount of gold. In addition, Solomon received money and goods through taxes on his own people and from people in the lands around Israel.

Stop here and discuss these questions: How does the government of your country get money? Do you have to pay taxes? What sorts of things do you have to pay taxes on? Pause this audio here.

In this story, the storyteller describes some of the ways Solomon used his gold. Solomon used some of the gold to make large and small shields. A shield is a piece of equipment that soldiers used for defense in battle. A shield could protect a soldier from arrows, swords, or other objects.

Stop here and look at a picture of a soldier with a shield. Pause this audio here.

Gold is a very soft metal and is not good to use as a shield. So Solomon's soldiers probably did not use these shields in battle. Solomon probably made these shields as decoration for the House of the Forest of Lebanon. This house was a large building in Jerusalem. Bible scholars believe that Solomon occasionally took the shields out of the House of the Forest of Lebanon and used them during ceremonies. This would have shown the people how rich their king was.

Solomon used gold and ivory to make a large, beautiful throne in his palace. A throne is a special seat that only the king can sit on. Ivory is white and comes from the tusks of elephants.

Stop here and look at a picture of an elephant. Note the size and color of the tusks. Pause this audio here.

Ivory is very beautiful and easy to carve. People used ivory to make jewelry and art. In this case, Solomon used the ivory to decorate his throne. It was a very large throne, with six steps leading up to it. The lions next to the armrests and on the steps were not real lions, but statues of lions. A lion is a large, wild cat that was common in Israel during Solomon's time. Lions are very strong and can easily kill a human. Because of this, the Israelites feared lions more than any other animal in the land. Hardly any other animal can kill a lion, so all the animals are afraid of lions too. Because the lion is so strong and every animal is afraid of a lion, many people call lions "the king of the animals."

Bible scholars think the lion statues around Solomon's throne were probably covered in gold.

Stop here and look at a picture that shows what Solomon's throne might have looked like. Pause this audio here.

Solomon had so much gold that he was able to make everyday items, like cups, plates, forks, and spoons, out of gold. Solomon had so much gold that the Israelites considered silver relatively worthless, even though most people usually consider silver to be valuable. The storyteller says that during Solomon's time, silver was as common as stones in Jerusalem. People built Jerusalem on hills that are very rocky, and Jerusalem stone is famous because it is a very good material to build with. So stones were extremely common in Jerusalem. Sycamore trees were common in the lowlands of Judah. Cedar trees were not common in Israel, and people had to import them from Tyre and other areas further north. Cedar was expensive, but Solomon was so rich that he was able to buy a large amount of cedar wood for his building projects.

The storyteller says that Solomon had a fleet of ships that sailed on the ocean with Hiram's fleet. These ships brought goods and animals from faraway countries. It is likely that this fleet of ships was in addition to the ships stationed at Ezion Geber in the far south of Israel. Hiram was the King of Tyre, and the city of Tyre was located north of Israel on the coast of the Mediterranean Sea. Israel also had a coastline on the Mediterranean Sea, so it makes the most sense for the ships of Solomon and Hiram to sail from this location.

Stop here and look at a map of Israel and the kingdom of Tyre. Note that both kingdoms have a coast on the Mediterranean Sea. Pause this audio here.

Although Solomon was very rich, he was also famous for his wisdom. The storyteller says that people from all over the world came to Jerusalem to visit Solomon and listen to his wisdom, and when they came, they brought Solomon gifts. This is another way that Solomon became rich.

Solomon also owned many chariots and horses. This meant that Solomon had a powerful army. Solomon stationed the chariots in Jerusalem and in other special cities throughout Israel. Solomon purchased horses and chariots from Egypt and another place called Kue. Most Bible scholars believe Kue was located northwest of Israel in the modern-day country of Turkey. Solomon's men not only bought horses and chariots for Israel, but also sold them to the kings of the Hittites and the Arameans.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 10:14–29 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has five scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller tells us how much gold Solomon received each year and describes the shields Solomon made from that gold.

In the second scene: The storyteller describes the throne that Solomon built in the palace.

In the third scene: The storyteller describes how rich Solomon was and the items that Solomon's trading ships brought to him.

In the fourth scene: The storyteller tells us that many people came to visit Solomon in order to hear his wisdom, and the people brought marvelous gifts with them.

In the fifth scene: The storyteller tells us about Solomon's chariots and horses.

The characters in this passage are:

  • King Solomon
  • Traders, merchants, kings of Arabia, and governors of territories
  • People who came to Jerusalem to hear Solomon's wisdom
  • Solomon's sailors and Hiram's sailors
  • And Solomon's horsemen and charioteers

If you have already translated the Book of 2 Chronicles, then you have already translated a version of this story. It might help to go back and listen to that story now. Stop here and listen to the second part of 2 Chronicles chapter 9, after the story of the visit of the Queen of Sheba. Pause this audio here.

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, the storyteller says that Solomon received 666 talents, or almost 23,000 kilograms, of gold each year. Every year, the rulers of the countries around Israel paid tribute to Solomon. A less powerful ruler pays tribute to a more powerful ruler. Because of this payment, the more powerful ruler agrees not to attack the country of the less powerful ruler.

King Solomon did not just get rich from gold—he also made money from people who did business in his land. If someone wanted to buy or sell things in Israel, they had to pay a tax to the king. Even if traders were just passing through the country, they had to pay a fee to travel there. All this money went to Solomon and helped make Solomon even richer.

The Arabian kings were the rulers of the lands to the southeast of Israel. The storyteller said that their tribute was not included in the 23,000 kilograms of gold that Solomon received each year.

Stop here and look at a map of Israel and Arabia. What name does your language have for the area called Arabia? Pause this audio here.

The phrase "governors of the land" or "governors of the territories" probably refers to the governors of different areas inside Israel. A governor managed part of the country for the king. For example, in Israel, there was one governor who was responsible for managing the territory that belonged to each tribe. One job of a governor was to collect a tax from the people and give it to the king.

Stop here and discuss these questions: In your country, what do you call the people who are responsible for managing different areas of the country? Could you use any of these words to translate the word "governor"? If not, what words could you use for a person who is responsible for managing one part of a country? Pause this audio here.

King Solomon hired artists to make his gold shields. Solomon did not make the shields himself. The artists made large shields and small shields. Most Bible scholars believe that people made the shields of a strong yet lightweight material like wood and then overlaid that material with gold. The larger shields required about 600 shekels, or about seven kilograms of gold, and the smaller shields required three minas, or about two kilograms of gold. Gold is a soft metal and is a poor material to use to make shields. Bible scholars believe that soldiers did not use these shields in battle, but that Solomon put them in the House of the Forest of Lebanon as decoration.

The House of the Forest of Lebanon was the largest building in Solomon's palace. Solomon built this house from cedar that came from Lebanon. This house might have been a gathering place for large groups of people, or it might have been a place to store weapons.

In the second scene, Solomon hires men to build a throne that is unlike any other throne in the world. Solomon's throne was on a platform with six steps leading up to it. When you listened to three different versions of this passage, some of them might have said that Solomon made the throne from ivory and then overlaid it with the finest gold. Most Bible scholars think it was unlikely that Solomon would have covered ivory with gold, because ivory was expensive and also very beautiful. Most Bible scholars believe that Solomon built the throne, overlaid it with gold, and then placed ivory decorations on certain parts of the throne.

Stop here and look at a picture of some ivory objects and decorations. Pause this audio here.

When you listened to three different versions of this passage, you might have heard some versions say that the top of the throne was round, while a few other versions might have said that there was a bull's head at the top of the throne. Most Bible scholars believe that the top of the throne was round because that was a very common way to make a throne at that time. We recommend that you translate this phrase to say that the top of the throne was round.

Each step that led up to Solomon's throne had a figure of a lion on each side, and there was a figure of a lion next to each armrest on the throne. The tribe of Judah used the figure of a lion as a symbol of their tribe. Solomon was from the tribe of Judah.

Stop here and look at a picture of a lion. Pause this audio here.

In scene three, the storyteller tells us that people made all of Solomon's drinking cups from gold and that people made all of the utensils in the House of the Forest of Lebanon from pure gold. This shows that Solomon had so much gold that he could afford to use it for everyday items like cups and utensils.

Stop here and look at a picture of a gold drinking cup. Pause this audio here.

Silver is another valuable metal that people commonly used in Bible times to make coins, jewelry, utensils, or other items. In this scene, the storyteller tells us that silver was considered as nothing during Solomon's days. This is a way to say that silver had no value, or was worthless, during Solomon's reign as king. Solomon was so rich during his reign that even ordinary Israelites thought that silver was not worth very much.

Solomon had a fleet of ships called "the ships of Tarshish." Tarshish was a city located on the far western end of the Mediterranean Sea. Israel was located on the far eastern end of the Mediterranean Sea.

Stop here and look at a map of the Mediterranean Sea that shows the location of Israel and the approximate location of Tarshish. Pause this audio here.

A ship would have to sail about 1,600 kilometers to go from Israel to Tarshish. So this phrase tells us that Solomon's fleet of ships sailed in the Mediterranean Sea and could sail very long distances. Some versions might have called these ships "merchant ships" or "trading ships" because traders used them to take goods to other countries and to bring goods back home. Solomon's ships brought back gold, silver, ivory, and animals. Bible scholars are not certain what kind of animals these were. Some versions of this story say that they were "apes and peacocks," while other versions say they were "apes and baboons" or "apes and monkeys."

Monkeys are small to medium-sized, four-legged animals that have hands similar to human hands. Monkeys also have tails.

Stop here and look at a picture of a monkey. Pause this audio here.

Apes are large monkeys. Some apes can be larger than humans. Apes do not have tails.

Stop here and look at a picture of an ape. Pause this audio here.

A baboon is a specific type of medium-sized monkey.

Stop here and look at a picture of a baboon. Pause this audio here.

A peacock is a kind of bird. Peacocks have very colorful, beautiful tail feathers. People use these feathers for decorations. Women can even wear peacock feathers on their hats or clothing.

Stop here and look at a picture of a peacock. Pause this audio here.

If your language does not have a word for any of these animals, you can provide a description. For example, instead of "peacocks," you could say "exotic birds" or "birds with brightly colored feathers."

In scene four, the storyteller reminds us that Solomon was the richest and wisest king of his time. Everyone wanted to come see Solomon and hear his wise sayings. The storyteller says that God put this wisdom in Solomon's heart. In the Israelites' culture, people considered the heart to be the place where a person's thoughts, feelings, and wisdom came from.

Stop here and discuss this question: In your culture, what part of the body do you consider thoughts, feelings, and wisdom to come from? Pause this audio here.

Each person who came to see Solomon brought a gift. Some people brought silver and gold. Other people brought clothes, weapons, or spices. Some people brought horses and mules. A mule is an animal that is very similar to a horse, but mules can work harder and are easier to handle than horses. When a male donkey mates with a female horse, the baby will be a mule.

Stop here and look at a picture of a mule. Pause this audio here.

In the fifth scene, the storyteller describes Solomon's chariots and horses. Solomon had 1,400 chariots. When you listened to three different versions of this passage, some might have said that Solomon had 12,000 horses, while others said that Solomon had 12,000 horsemen. A horseman is a person who knows how to handle horses. Bible scholars do not agree on whether the original language means that Solomon had 12,000 horses or that Solomon had 12,000 horsemen. Some scholars think that translating the word as "horses" makes more sense in this story than translating it as "horsemen," but either choice is acceptable.

Solomon kept some of his horses, chariots, and charioteers in the chariot cities he had built all over Israel. The chariot cities had special buildings to keep the horses, chariots, and horsemen in. Solomon also kept some of his chariots in Jerusalem to protect the capital city. Solomon bought horses from Egypt and from a place called Kue. Bible scholars are not certain exactly where Kue was. However, scholars know that during Solomon's time, people who lived in an area in southeastern Turkey known as Cilicia had a reputation for breeding excellent horses. When you listened to three different versions of this passage, you might have heard that Solomon bought horses from Egypt and Kue, or that Solomon bought horses from Egypt and Cilicia.

Stop here and look at a map that shows the locations of Israel and Cilicia. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller says that the price of a chariot was about 600 shekels, or seven kilograms of silver, and the price of a horse was about 150 shekels, or two kilograms of silver. The storyteller adds that Solomon's men sold chariots and horses to the kings of the Hittite people group and the kings of the Aramean people group.

In the middle of this scene, the storyteller mentions again that silver was so common in Israel that it was as common as stones. The storyteller also claims that cedars were as common as sycamore trees in the lowlands. The Israelites had to import good-quality cedar from Lebanon, and it was expensive. In contrast, sycamore trees were very common in an area people called Shephelah, which was in the foothills of Judah. Both of these statements mean that Solomon was so rich that he could buy a lot of expensive things.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 10:14–29 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has five scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • King Solomon
  • Traders, merchants, kings of Arabia, and governors of territories
  • Solomon's sailors and Hiram's sailors
  • People who came to Jerusalem to hear Solomon's wisdom
  • And Solomon's horsemen and charioteers

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Every year, King Solomon received around 23,000 kilograms of gold. This was in addition to the taxes that merchants paid to Solomon, the profits from traders, and the money Solomon received from all the kings of Arabia and the governors of the country. King Solomon made 200 large shields and covered each one with about seven kilograms of hammered gold. And Solomon made 300 small shields and overlaid each one with about two kilograms of hammered gold. The king placed these shields in the House of the Forest of Lebanon.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • It is so nice to have the resources to make all these beautiful things; or
  • All this gold will make our country more secure; or
  • I feel powerful because I have so much wealth.

Ask the people playing the traders and merchants, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • I wish we did not have to pay these taxes and fees; or
  • I wonder if I can find a way around paying these taxes and fees; or
  • I do not mind paying these taxes because the king uses them to make our country strong and secure.

Ask the people playing the kings of Arabia, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I do not like it that I have to pay tribute to Solomon; or
  • If we do not pay the tribute, I wonder if our army is strong enough to fight against Solomon's army; or
  • I feel sad that we are losing our gold to a foreigner.

Ask the people playing the governors of the country, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am happy that our country is so prosperous; or
  • I do not like to pay taxes when the king wastes the money to make golden shields; or
  • Did Yahweh not say that our king should not collect a lot of gold?

Continue the drama.

Then the king made a great throne that people inlaid with ivory and overlaid with the finest gold. The throne had six steps leading up to it, and the top of the throne was rounded at the back. There were armrests at either side of the seat, and a lion stood next to each armrest. Twelve lions stood on the steps, one at each end of each step. Nothing like this throne had ever been made for any other kingdom.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am so proud of this beautiful throne that my workers have made! or
  • I feel strong and powerful when I sit on this throne; or
  • I hope other kings will come visit me and be impressed by my throne.

Continue the drama.

All of King Solomon's cups were made of gold, and all of the utensils in the House of the Forest of Lebanon were made of pure gold. Nothing was made of silver, for silver had very little value in Solomon's day. For the king had a fleet of trading ships at sea with the ships of King Hiram. Once every three years, the ships came back bringing gold, silver, ivory, apes, and peacocks.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am glad that I have so much gold, silver, and other valuable goods; or
  • Our partnership with Hiram has been very profitable; or
  • I wish my father, David, could have seen these days when Israel is so rich and powerful.

Ask the people playing Solomon and Hiram's sailors, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • I really enjoy sailing to Tarshish and bringing back so many good things to the king; or
  • I am glad that our country has so much gold; or
  • I wonder why the king needs these apes and peacocks?

Continue the drama.

So King Solomon became greater than all the kings of the earth in riches and in wisdom. People from all over the earth came to consult Solomon and hear the wisdom that God put in his heart. Year after year, everyone who visited Solomon brought him gifts of silver and gold, clothing, weapons, spices, horses, and mules.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am glad that my wisdom can help so many people; or
  • I am thankful that Yahweh gave me the gift of wisdom; or
  • I am amazed by all the gifts the people are bringing to me.

Ask the people playing the people who came to hear Solomon's wisdom, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • I feel honored that the great King Solomon is willing to see me; or
  • I am amazed by how wise Solomon is; or
  • It was definitely worth the trip and the cost to come see such a wise king!

Continue the drama.

Solomon built up a force of 1,400 chariots and 12,000 horsemen. Solomon stationed these chariots and horsemen in the chariot cities and with the king in Jerusalem. The king made silver as common as stones in Jerusalem, and he made cedar as common as sycamore trees in the lowlands of the foothills of Judah. Solomon's horses came from Egypt and from Cilicia, and the king's merchants bought them from Cilicia at the standard price. They bought a chariot from Egypt for about seven kilograms of silver, and a horse for about two kilograms of silver. And they also exported them to the kings of the Hittites and the Arameans.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am pleased that our army has grown so large; or
  • I hope that these chariots and horsemen will keep our nation safe for many years to come; or
  • I want Israel to be the most powerful nation on earth.

Ask the people playing Solomon's horsemen and charioteers, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • Finally, our country has an army as powerful as Egypt; or
  • I feel proud to serve in such a strong army; or
  • I am grateful to King Solomon for providing such good chariots and horses for our army.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 10:14–29 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

If you have already translated the Book of 2 Chronicles, then you have already translated a version of this story. It might help to go back and listen to that story now. Stop here and listen to the second part of 2 Chronicles chapter 9, after the story of the visit of the Queen of Sheba. Pause this audio here.

Every year, King Solomon received around 666 talents, or 23,000 kilograms of gold. This was in addition to the taxes paid by merchants, the profits from traders, and what Solomon received from all the kings of Arabia and the governors of the country. Be sure to translate king the same way as you did in previous stories. See the Master Glossary for more information on king. If you use the word for talent rather than a modern-day unit of measurement, translate it in the same way that you did previously, and refer to the Master Glossary if you need more information.

Stop here and discuss the following question. If your language does not have a name for the place called "Arabia," then decide how you will translate this name into your language. Also, decide how you will translate "governors" into your language. A governor is an official who manages one part of a country on behalf of the king. Pause this audio here.

King Solomon made 200 large shields and covered each one with about 600 shekels, or seven kilograms of hammered gold. And Solomon made 300 small shields and overlaid each one with about three minas, or two kilograms, of hammered gold. A shekel is a unit of weight, around 11 grams. The king placed these shields in the House of the Forest of Lebanon.

Stop here and look at a picture of a shield as a group. Decide how you will translate "shield" into your language. Be sure to translate "House of the Forest of Lebanon" the same way you did in previous stories. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss how you would like to translate shekels and minas as a group. You may use the modern-day kilogram unit of measurement if you would like, which would be seven kilograms of gold in each of the large shields and two kilograms of gold in each of the small shields. For more information about "shekel," refer to the Master Glossary. Pause this audio here.

Then the king made a great throne that people inlaid with ivory and overlaid with the finest gold. Be sure to translate "throne" the same way you did in previous passages.

Stop here and look again at the photo of the elephant with ivory tusks. Decide how you will translate "ivory" into your language. Pause this audio here.

The throne had six steps leading up to it, and the top of the throne was rounded at the back. There were armrests at either side of the seat, and a lion stood next to each armrest. Twelve lions stood on the steps, one at each end of each step. Nothing like it had ever been made for any other kingdom. Be sure to use the same word for kingdom as you did in previous passages. See the Master Glossary for more information on kingdom.

Stop here and look again at the photo of the lion if needed. If your language does not have a word for lion, decide how you will describe a lion for your hearers. Pause this audio here.

All of King Solomon's cups were made of gold, and all of the utensils in the House of the Forest of Lebanon were made of pure gold. Nothing was made of silver, for silver had very little value in Solomon's day. For the king had a fleet of trading ships at sea with the ships of King Hiram. Once every three years, the ships came back bringing gold, silver, ivory, apes, and peacocks.

Stop here and look at photos of apes, peacocks, baboons, and monkeys if needed. Decide how you will translate "apes" and "peacocks" into your language. You may also use the words "baboons" and "monkeys." Pause this audio here.

So King Solomon became greater than all the kings of the earth in riches and in wisdom. People from all over the earth came to consult Solomon and hear the wisdom that God put in his heart. Year after year, everyone who visited Solomon brought him gifts of silver and gold, clothing, weapons, spices, horses, and mules. Be sure to use the same words for wisdom, heart, and God as you did in previous passages. See the Master Glossary for more information on wisdom, heart, and God.

Stop here and look at the photo of a mule again if needed. Decide what word you will use for "mule." Pause this audio here.

Solomon built up a force of 1,400 chariots and 12,000 horsemen. Solomon stationed them in the chariot cities and with the king in Jerusalem. The king made silver as common as stones in Jerusalem, and the king made cedar as common as sycamore trees in the lowlands of the foothills of Judah. Be sure to use the same words for chariots and chariot cities as you used before. Solomon's horses came from Egypt and from Cilicia, and the king's merchants bought them from Cilicia at the standard price.

Stop here and look again at a map of Israel and Cilicia. Note the location of Cilicia. Decide how you will translate "Cilicia" into your language. Pause this audio here.

People bought a chariot from Egypt for about 600 shekels, or seven kilograms of silver, and a horse for about 150 shekels, or two kilograms of silver. And people also exported them to the kings of the Hittites and the Arameans. Be sure to translate Hittites and Arameans the same way you did in previous passages.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

acai.preferred_label: House of the Forest of Lebanon

title: 1 Kings 11:1–13
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 11:1–13 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 11:1–13 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 11:1–13 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous story, the storyteller described more about King Solomon's great wealth, wisdom, and military power. In this story, King Solomon marries many foreign women, and when Solomon becomes old, he begins to worship the gods that his wives worship. This makes Yahweh angry. So Yahweh tells Solomon that, starting with Solomon's son, Solomon's descendants will no longer rule over all 12 tribes of Israel, but only over one.

When Yahweh led the Israelites into Canaan, Yahweh told the Israelites many times not to marry any Canaanites. Yahweh told the Israelites this because Yahweh knew that if an Israelite married a Canaanite, the Canaanite would pressure the Israelite to worship the Canaanites' false gods. Then the Israelite might begin to worship those false gods instead of Yahweh. Moses and Joshua also warned the Israelites not to marry Canaanites, because then the Israelites would certainly worship the Canaanites' gods. Sadly, this is exactly what happened to King Solomon. Solomon married many foreign women, and those women wanted to worship their own gods. Solomon built places for his foreign wives to worship their gods, and eventually Solomon began to worship those gods too.

Stop here and tell a story about someone who faced pressure to do something wrong and eventually did it. What did other people say or do to that person to pressure that person? How long did that person resist the pressure before that person decided to do the wrong thing? What happened afterwards? Pause this audio here.

At this time in history, a king often had many wives. When a king had many wives, it showed that the king was rich and powerful. Solomon probably married many of these women in order to make agreements with the leaders of the Hittites, Moabites, Ammonites, Edomites, and Sidonians. While the Moabites, Ammonites, Edomites, and Sidonians had their own countries, the Hittites were scattered around the area in small groups.

Stop here and look at a map that shows the location of Israel, Moab, Ammon, Edom, and Sidon. Pause this audio here.

When Solomon made these marriage agreements with the leaders of the Hittites, Moabites, Ammonites, Edomites, and Sidonians, it showed that the leaders of those countries were afraid of Solomon's power and wanted to be on good terms with Solomon.

Stop here and discuss this question: In the area where you live, how do leaders show that they are rich and powerful? Pause this audio here.

Solomon had 700 wives and 300 concubines, or wives who were of lesser status. This is a very large number of wives. Solomon definitely broke Yahweh's command that the king should not have many wives.

Stop here and discuss this question: In your area, how many wives is it appropriate for a leader to marry? Pause this audio here.

Solomon's wives turned his heart away from Yahweh, which means that Solomon did not love Yahweh as much as he once did. Because Solomon did not completely love Yahweh anymore, Solomon worshipped other gods. Yahweh told Solomon not to worship these false gods, but Solomon disobeyed Yahweh and worshipped false gods anyway.

Stop here and tell a story about someone who disobeyed a command of someone in authority. Why did that person disobey the command? What happened after? What were the consequences? Pause this audio here.

Yahweh was very angry and confronted Solomon about this. Yahweh said that Solomon had broken the special agreement Yahweh made with the Israelites and did not follow Yahweh's laws. Yahweh had every right to punish Solomon for this. Yahweh could even have chosen not to allow Solomon to be king of Israel anymore because Solomon had failed as a spiritual leader of Israel. But Yahweh also remembered that Yahweh had promised King David that someone from David's family would always be king of Israel. So Yahweh said that Yahweh would not take the entire kingdom of Israel away from Solomon. Instead, Yahweh would allow 10 of the 12 tribes of Israel to follow another leader, while Solomon's son would be king over just one tribe. This was a punishment because Solomon worshipped other gods, but Yahweh still kept his promise to David by letting someone from David's family remain king.

Stop here and tell a story about someone who showed kindness or mercy to someone because of a relationship with that person's parent. What might have happened if that person had not had a relationship with that person's parent? Pause this audio here.

Yahweh also said that Yahweh would allow Solomon's son to rule over one tribe of Israel "for the sake of Jerusalem." Yahweh chose Jerusalem as the special city where Yahweh's presence would live forever and where Yahweh's people would worship Yahweh. Yahweh commanded the Israelites to build Yahweh's temple in Jerusalem and to offer sacrifices only at that location. The descendants of David who ruled over Israel had a special responsibility to protect the temple, encourage faithful worship of Yahweh, and set a good spiritual example for the people of Israel.

Yahweh always keeps his promises. Yahweh promised that someone from David's family would always be king of Israel. Yahweh also promised that Yahweh's special presence would always stay in Jerusalem. Because Yahweh made both of these promises, David's family had to keep ruling from Jerusalem, where the temple was located.

Stop here and discuss these questions: Is there a place that is special to your people group? If so, why is it special to you? What do people say about this place? How would your people feel if you could not go to this place?

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 11:1–13 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has two scenes.

In the first scene: King Solomon's foreign wives lead him to worship other gods besides Yahweh.

In the second scene: Yahweh tells Solomon that the consequences of Solomon's idolatry will be that Solomon's descendants will lose control over all the tribes of Israel except for one.

The characters in this passage are:

  • King Solomon
  • Pharaoh's daughter
  • Solomon's other foreign wives
  • Solomon's concubines
  • And Yahweh

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, the storyteller described how Solomon came to sin against Yahweh by worshipping foreign gods. Solomon loved and married many foreign women. In a previous story, we heard that Solomon married Pharaoh's daughter. When Solomon did that, he disobeyed Yahweh. The storyteller revealed that Solomon loved a large number of women from the pagan nations around Israel. Solomon's wives were Hittite women, as well as women from Moab, Ammon, Edom, and Sidon. Sadly, Solomon disobeyed Yahweh over and over.

The storyteller quoted Yahweh's law, which Moses wrote down in one of his books: "You shall not marry them, nor shall they intermarry with you." Yahweh gave this command twice, in a slightly different way each time, to emphasize how important it was.

Stop here and discuss this question: In your language, how do you emphasize something that is really important? How can you translate Yahweh's command so that your hearers know it is very important? Pause this audio here.

Yahweh commanded the Israelites not to marry foreigners because the foreigners would turn the Israelites' hearts away from Yahweh. The Israelites believed that the heart was the place where a person's feelings come from—feelings like love, happiness, anger, or sadness. If your heart is turned toward someone, it means you love that person. But if your heart is turned away from someone, it means you do not love that person. Yahweh warned the Israelites that if they married foreigners, their hearts would not love Yahweh anymore. In the original language, this phrase suggests that Solomon's heart used to be perfect in the way he loved Yahweh, but that Solomon's heart was not perfect anymore.

Stop here and discuss these questions: What words or sayings do you have in your language to describe a situation where one person used to love another person but does not love them anymore? Do any of these sayings make you think that something that was perfect has been lost? Pause this audio here.

Yahweh said that if the Israelites married foreigners, the foreigners would "surely" turn the Israelites' hearts away from Yahweh. Yahweh is saying that it is certain that the Israelites' hearts would turn away from Yahweh. Yahweh wanted to emphasize this commandment even more.

Stop here and talk about words in your language that mean something will definitely happen. Which of these words might go best here? Pause this audio here.

Solomon clung to his foreign wives in love. This means that Solomon gave all of his love and loyalty to his wives and refused to change. In this case, when Solomon gave his love and loyalty to his wives, Solomon also gave his love and loyalty to their gods. In the original language, the storyteller says Solomon loved his wives so much that it was like his skin being attached to his body. Just like you cannot take off your skin without dying, Solomon could not let go of his foreign wives or their gods.

Stop here and discuss these questions: What words do you have in your language to describe a situation where one person loves another person so much that they become attached to that person the way your skin is attached to your body? Which of these words might work well here? Pause this audio here.

Solomon had 700 royal wives and 300 concubines. "Royal" means that the woman was a member of the family with the highest social status. In a country that had a king, to be royal meant that a person was a member of the king's family. Some of Solomon's wives might have been from the highest-status family in their own country, or some of them might have become royal just by marrying Solomon.

Stop here and discuss these questions: What special words do you have in your language to talk about people of the highest social status? Which of these words has a similar meaning to "royal"? Pause this audio here.

A concubine was a servant woman who had sexual relations with her master. A concubine was of lower status than a wife, but a concubine still had important legal rights. If a concubine gave birth to children, people considered those children to be the children of the man and his wife.

Stop here and discuss this question: In your culture, is there a role or a status similar to a concubine? Is it possible for a man to have a woman who is not his wife but whom it is socially acceptable for him to have children with? Pause this audio here.

After Solomon married these foreign women, Yahweh's words came true. Solomon's wives "turned his heart away," and Solomon "went after" their gods. This means that Solomon began to give his love and loyalty to the gods that his wives worshipped, instead of giving it to Yahweh only.

In this part of the story, when the storyteller talks about Milcom, Chemosh, and Molech, the storyteller uses a word that has a very negative meaning in the original language. It was a word for something so bad that it made people feel really upset or disgusted. It was something that was so bad that people wanted to stay far away from it. Furthermore, If a person went near the thing that was an abomination, that person would also become filthy and disgusting. Even worse, that person would also have to face Yahweh's judgment. When you read three different versions of this story, you probably heard this word translated in different ways. Some translations might have said something like, "Milcom, the abomination of the Ammonites." Others might have said something like, "Milcom, the disgusting god of the Ammonites." All of these are different ways to try to communicate that Yahweh thought these false gods were filthy and terrible, and that the Israelites should avoid them completely, because if the Israelites went near those gods and worshipped them, the Israelites would also become filthy and terrible.

Stop here and discuss these questions: What words do you have in your language for something that is filthy and that you should avoid completely? Which of these words could you use for the gods of the pagan people around Israel? Pause this audio here.

Ashtoreth, Milcom, Chemosh, and Molech were the names of the gods that Solomon's wives worshipped. We recommend that you translate these names to sound like the names in the Bible your local church uses. You might find that some Bibles use the name Molech instead of Milcom. This is because some Bible scholars think that Molech and Milcom were two different names for the same god. In order to make this clear, some Bibles use the name Molech whenever the storyteller mentions this god. But other Bible scholars think that Molech and Milcom were two different gods. We recommend that you listen to the version of the Bible that your local church uses and use the names that version uses.

Next, the storyteller reminds us that worshipping idols is evil in the sight of Yahweh. This means that Yahweh thinks that it is evil for people to worship idols. Solomon still followed Yahweh partially, which means that Solomon followed some of Yahweh's commands. But Solomon broke Yahweh's command not to worship other gods.

Solomon built places for his foreign wives to worship their gods. Solomon probably did not build these places with his own hands, but rather provided workers, money, and materials to build these places. Solomon should not have allowed anyone to build places of worship for foreign gods. When you listened to three different versions of this passage, you might have heard these places called "high places" or "pagan shrines". These are different ways to describe a place where people worship false gods.

To worship these false gods, Solomon's wives burned incense and made sacrifices.

In the second scene, Yahweh is extremely angry with Solomon.

Stop here and discuss these questions: What words or phrases do you use in your language to describe when a person is extremely angry? How might you translate that Yahweh was very angry with Solomon? Pause this audio here.

Yahweh gave a message to Solomon. The storyteller does not tell us if Yahweh appeared to Solomon directly or if Yahweh gave the message to someone else to tell Solomon. Yahweh told Solomon that Yahweh would punish Solomon.

When Solomon worshipped other gods, Solomon broke Yahweh's covenant and Yahweh's statutes. Yahweh clearly told Solomon that this is why Yahweh would punish Solomon. Yahweh said that Yahweh would tear the kingdom away from Solomon and give it to someone else. This statement shows that Yahweh is the one who decides which man gets to be the king of Israel.

Yahweh describes the kingdom of Israel almost like an object that Yahweh gave Solomon to hold. Now, because Solomon did not do what was right, Yahweh will take or tear away the kingdom from Solomon. When people "tear" something, it is like they are tearing cloth into pieces. This word is important because later, someone in Israel will actually tear a piece of cloth into 12 pieces to show that the kingdom of Israel will be divided.

Stop here and find a piece of cloth that will be easy to tear apart. Have two people stand in front of the group and hold the piece of cloth. One person will represent Solomon, while the second person will represent Yahweh. Have the person who represents Yahweh tear the piece of cloth in two. Now, the person who represents Solomon will have one piece, and the person who represents Yahweh will have the second piece. What words do you use to describe this action? Yahweh is going to tear the kingdom away from Solomon's descendants like you tore this piece of cloth. How will you translate this action into your language? Pause this audio here.

Some people might get confused because first Yahweh says, "I will tear the kingdom away from you" when Yahweh is speaking to Solomon, but later Yahweh says he will not do it while Solomon is still alive. Instead, Yahweh will do it when Solomon's son is king. Yahweh means that Yahweh will take the kingdom away from Solomon's family. Back then, people believed that a man's children were a big part of his reputation and honor. So even though Solomon would not lose the kingdom himself, it probably made Solomon very sad to know that his descendants would rule over a smaller, poorer, and weaker kingdom. That made Solomon's family seem less important, and that would have felt like a punishment for Solomon, too.

Yahweh also said that Yahweh would not divide the kingdom while Solomon was still alive. Yahweh said this was because of Solomon's father, David. That means Yahweh cared so much about David that Yahweh showed kindness to Solomon, too. The special promises Yahweh made to David also passed down to David's son, Solomon. Yahweh would punish Solomon for Solomon's sin, but the punishment would not be as bad as it could have been.

Yahweh said he would give the kingdom to one of Solomon's servants. Solomon had many servants. Actually, everyone in Israel served the king in some way! But Yahweh did not tell Solomon which servant would get the kingdom. Yahweh probably did not mean a household servant who cooked Solomon's food or cleaned his house. Yahweh probably meant someone important, like a government leader or a military officer.

Stop here and discuss these questions: How have you translated the word "servant" in previous passages? Can this word refer to a government official, military officer, or other leader in the kingdom? Pause this audio here.

Yahweh told Solomon that Yahweh would allow Solomon to be the king of one tribe of Israel. Yahweh also said that this was for the sake of Solomon's father, David.

"For the sake of your father, David," means because of the promise Yahweh made to David. For the sake of Jerusalem means that Yahweh had chosen Jerusalem to be the place where Yahweh's presence would live forever. It was important to Yahweh to make sure that Jerusalem remained under the control of a descendant of David.
