Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 11:1–13 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has two scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • King Solomon
  • Pharaoh's daughter
  • Solomon's other foreign wives
  • Solomon's concubines
  • And Yahweh

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

But King Solomon loved many foreign women, in addition to Pharaoh's daughter—women from Moab, Ammon, Edom, Sidon, and Hittite women. Solomon loved these women even though they came from nations about whom Yahweh had said to the Israelites, "You must not marry them, or allow them to marry your people, because they will definitely persuade you to worship their gods." These were the kind of women that Solomon clung to in love. Solomon had 700 royal wives and 300 concubines. Solomon's wives turned his heart away from Yahweh. As a result, when Solomon was old, Solomon's wives convinced him to worship other gods. Solomon's heart was not loyal to Yahweh his God, as the heart of Solomon's father, David, had been.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • My wives want to worship their gods. I have to give my wives what they want to keep them happy; or
  • These are the women I love. Surely it is okay to make them feel happier and more at home by having places to worship their gods; or
  • I married these women to make Israel secure. Maybe this is just what a king has to do; or
  • I kind of enjoy these different forms of worship.

Ask the people playing Solomon's wives, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • I feel relieved. I heard that Solomon was very devoted to his God, but maybe Solomon is not so devoted after all; or
  • I feel grateful. Solomon has treated me well and has taken good care of me; or
  • I feel happy that I can worship the gods of my ancestors and that I do not have to worship only Solomon's God; or
  • It seems that Solomon has become more relaxed in his old age.

Continue the drama.

Solomon worshipped Ashtoreth, the goddess of the Sidonians, and Milcom, the abomination of the Ammonites. Solomon did what was evil and did not live the good and faithful life that Yahweh wanted, like Solomon's father, David, had done. On the hill that is east of Jerusalem, Solomon built a place for his wives to worship Chemosh, the abomination of Moab. Solomon also built a place for his wives to worship Molech, the abomination of the Ammonites. Solomon did this for all his foreign wives, who burned incense and made sacrifices to their gods.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I built an altar for this wife's gods, so I guess it is only fair to build altars for these other wives' gods; or
  • I brought peace and security through these marriage alliances. I think it is wise to keep my wives happy; or
  • I feel anxious about what my legacy will be. I built the temple for Yahweh, but now there are so many altars to other gods in Israel; or
  • I know Yahweh said not to worship other gods, but I love this wife so much, and I do not want to disappoint her.

Ask the people playing Solomon's wives, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • I feel honored! Solomon is the wisest king in the world, and Solomon built an altar for my gods. I must go there often to show Solomon how much I appreciate it; or
  • When I see King Solomon worshipping my gods, my heart fills with pride; or
  • I hear that Yahweh told Solomon to worship Yahweh only. I wonder if this is going to cause a problem? or
  • These ignorant Israelites call my gods detestable. I hope they will learn from the example that their king is setting

Continue the drama.

So Yahweh was very angry at Solomon, because Solomon's heart had turned away from Yahweh, even though Yahweh had appeared to Solomon twice. Yahweh had commanded Solomon about this very matter and told Solomon not to follow other gods. But Solomon did not obey what Yahweh commanded. Therefore, Yahweh said to Solomon, "Because you have done this, and you have not obeyed my covenant and my statutes, as I told you to, I promise that I will tear the kingdom away from you and give it to one of your servants. But I will not do this while you are alive, for the sake of your father David; I will tear the kingdom away from your son. But I will not tear the entire kingdom away from him. I will allow your son to rule over one tribe of Israel, in keeping with the promise that I made to your father David, and for the sake of Jerusalem, which I have chosen."

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel very sad. Yahweh is right, I disobeyed Yahweh and did evil; or
  • I regret that I ever married these women and followed their gods; or
  • I feel so guilty. My son will lose most of the kingdom, and it is all my fault; or
  • I should have known better. Yahweh appeared to me twice, but I did not listen to Yahweh, and I worshipped other gods.

Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am very angry. I gave Solomon more than any other Israelite, yet Solomon did not listen to me and he worshipped detestable idols; or
  • I am disappointed. I gave Solomon great wisdom, and yet Solomon allowed his wives to lead him astray; or
  • I deeply desire for my people to know me, but Solomon has made that very difficult by making all these altars and worshipping his wives' gods; or
  • Although Solomon failed, I will be faithful to my covenant and my promises. This is not the end.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 11:1–13 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

But King Solomon loved many foreign women, in addition to Pharaoh's daughter—women from Moab, Ammon, Edom, Sidon, and Hittite women. For more information on king and Pharaoh, refer to the Master Glossary. Be sure to translate king, Pharaoh, Moab, Ammon, Edom, Sidon, and Hittite the same way you did in previous passages.

Solomon loved his wives even though they came from nations about whom Yahweh had said to the Israelites, "You must not marry them, or allow them to marry your people, because they will definitely persuade you to worship their gods." For more information on nations, Yahweh, Israelites, worship and gods, refer to the Master Glossary. Be sure to translate these words the same way you did in previous passages.

These were the kind of women that Solomon clung to in love.

Stop here and decide how you will translate the phrase "clung to in love." Pause this audio here.

Solomon had 700 royal wives and 300 concubines. A royal wife is a wife who comes from the family with the highest social status in the country. A concubine is a man's legal wife, but she is of lower status than a royal wife.

Stop here and decide how you will translate the terms "royal wife" and "concubine." If you have already translated these words in another book of the Bible, use the same words that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Solomon's wives turned his heart away from Yahweh.

Stop here and decide how you will translate the statement "turned his heart away." If you use the word for heart in your translation, refer to the Master Glossary for more information about heart. Pause this audio here.

As a result, when Solomon was old, Solomon's wives convinced him to worship other gods. Solomon's heart was not loyal to Yahweh his God, as the heart of his father David had been. Use the same word for God as you have used in previous passages, and refer to God in the Master Glossary for more information.

Solomon worshipped Ashtoreth, the goddess of the Sidonians and Milcom, the abomination of the Ammonites. We recommend that you translate the names Ashtoreth and Milcom so that they sound like the names that are in the Bible your local church uses. The storyteller called both Ashtoreth and Milcom an abomination. Abomination means something that is so filthy and disgusting that people want to stay very far away from it. If a person comes close to something that is an abomination, that person might also become filthy and disgusting. And then that person would have to face Yahweh's judgment.

Stop here and decide how you will translate the word "abomination." Pause this audio here.

Solomon did what is evil and did not live the good and faithful life that Yahweh wanted, as his father, David, had done. On the hill that is east of Jerusalem, Solomon built a high place for his wives to worship Chemosh, the abomination of Moab. You will remember that a high place is a special place where people can worship a god closer to where they live. People often built high places on hills or higher ground, but not always. Be sure to translate evil and high places the same way you did in previous passages. See the Master Glossary for more information on evil. We recommend that you translate the names Chemosh and Molech so that they sound like the names that are in the Bible your local church uses.

Solomon did this for all his foreign wives, who burned incense and made sacrifices to their gods. Be sure to translate burned incense and sacrifices the same way you did in previous passages. See the Master Glossary for more information on sacrifice.

Therefore, Yahweh said to Solomon, "Because you have done this, and you have not obeyed my covenant and my statutes, as I told you to, I promise that I will tear the kingdom away from you and give it to one of your servants." Be sure to translate covenant, statutes, kingdom, and servant the same way you did in previous passages. See the Master Glossary for more information on covenant, statutes, kingdom, and servant.

Stop here and decide how you will translate the phrase "tear the kingdom away from you."

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 11:14–25
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 11:14–25 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 11:14–25 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 11:14–25 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous story, King Solomon disobeyed Yahweh and worshipped other gods. So Yahweh decided that after Solomon's death, Solomon's descendants would only reign over one tribe of Israel. In this story, we hear about two men who caused trouble for Solomon: Hadad the Edomite and Rezon of Zobah.

Most of Solomon's time as king was peaceful and secure. But toward the end of Solomon's life, Yahweh causes two men, Hadad and Rezon, to gain enough power to cause some trouble in Israel. In the first part of this story, the storyteller tells a story from the time when King David was still alive. David and his military commander, Joab, attacked the country of Edom and killed all the men, except for a small group of servants who escaped with Hadad. Hadad was just a child when this happened. Hadad was from the royal line of Edom, which means that one of Hadad's relatives was the king of Edom. This group of servants took Hadad from Edom to a region called Midian, then to the region of Paran, and then to Egypt.

Stop here and look at a map of Israel, Edom, Midian, and Egypt. Note the location of Edom, Midian, Paran, and Egypt. Pause this audio here.

In Egypt, Pharaoh decided to be friends with Hadad and take care of him. Pharaoh gave Hadad and Hadad's father's servants a place to live and food to eat.

Stop here and discuss these questions: In your country, how do people treat the royal family or the leaders of neighboring countries? For what reasons might your leaders show kindness and friendship to the leaders of other countries and their families? Pause this audio here.

When Hadad grew up, he married the sister of Pharaoh's wife. Hadad and his wife had a child named Genubath, and Genubath grew up in Pharaoh's household. Hadad and his family seemed to have a good life in Egypt. But sometime after David and Joab both died, Hadad decided to go back to Edom. The storyteller does not mention what Hadad does after he comes back to Edom. The storyteller only says that the things Hadad does are evil. It is possible that the storyteller assumed that his audience already knew about the things that Hadad did, and the storyteller only needed to remind his audience about those things.

Stop here and discuss these questions: Name someone that everyone you know thinks is a bad person. It could be a historical figure, a political leader, a military leader, or someone in the community. Why does everyone think this person is a bad person? When you hear this person's name, how do you feel? Pause this audio here.

Edom was southeast of Israel. The next part of the story is about Rezon, who was from Zobah, a city north of Israel. Together, these two men were able to make trouble for Solomon on opposite sides of his kingdom.

Stop here and look at a map that shows the location of Israel, Edom, and Zobah. Pause this audio here.

Next, the storyteller tells another story from the time when King David was still alive. The storyteller does this to explain where Rezon of Zobah came from. Rezon served Hadadezer, king of Zobah. While King David was alive, David fought and defeated Hadadezer. At some point in time, Rezon left his master Hadadezer. Then Rezon became the leader of a group of men. Rezon and this group of men traveled around the area north of Israel. The men attacked other people and stole people's property.

Eventually, Rezon and his group of followers went to Damascus, a city in the country of Aram, and Rezon became the king there. When you listened to three different versions of this story, you might have heard the storyteller call this area "Aram," or you might have heard the storyteller call this area "Syria." "Aram" was the name that the Israelites called this area. "Syria" is the modern-day name of the same region.

Stop here and look at a map of Israel and Aram that shows the location of Damascus. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller calls Rezon an adversary, or enemy, of Israel. This probably means that Rezon did things like attack Israelite towns, hurt Israelite people, and steal things from the Israelites.

Hadad and Rezon both caused trouble for Israel while Solomon was king. In other stories, the storyteller describes Solomon's reign as peaceful and prosperous. Therefore, it is likely that these problems happened near the end of Solomon's life, after Solomon began to worship foreign gods instead of only worshipping Yahweh.

Stop here and tell a story about a time when one group of people attacked another group of people. Why did the first group attack the second group? What happened to the people in the second group, especially the children in that group? After the conflict was over, what did the people in the second group think about the people who attacked them? As they grew up, what did the children in the second group think about the people who attacked them?

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 11:14–25 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has three scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller tells us that after King David attacked Edom, Hadad the Edomite escaped and went to live in Egypt. When Hadad grew up, he married the sister of the Queen of Egypt and had a son.

In the second scene: Hadad hears that King David and Joab are dead and decides to go back to Edom.

In the third scene: Rezon, the son of Eliada, was the leader of a band of thieves, and then he became the king of Aram. Rezon hates the Israelites and constantly causes trouble for them. Hadad the Edomite also causes trouble for Israel.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Yahweh
  • King Solomon
  • Joab, the commander of David's army
  • King David
  • The men of Edom
  • Hadad the Edomite
  • Servants of Hadad's father
  • Men of Paran
  • Pharaoh
  • Queen Tahpenes
  • Hadad's wife
  • Genubath, son of Hadad
  • Rezon, son of Eliada
  • Hadadezer, king of Zobah
  • Rezon's followers
  • And the Israelites

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, Yahweh raises up an adversary against Solomon. This statement makes it clear that Yahweh was in control of these events and caused Hadad to become Solomon's adversary. "Adversary" is another word for enemy. An adversary means someone who opposes another person. An adversary might try to harm another person or stop that person from doing what he or she wants to do.

Stop here and discuss these questions: Think about the kind of person in your culture who opposes another person, tries to harm that person, or tries to stop that person from doing what he wants to do. How do you describe this kind of person? What kind of words do you use for this kind of person? Pause this audio here.

To tell Hadad's story, the storyteller must talk about things that happened before Solomon became king of Israel. The storyteller reminds us that many years before Solomon became king, King David's army fought against the army of Edom and won. After that, David's army commander, Joab, stayed in Edom and killed almost every man in Edom. Joab stayed in Edom for six months. When you listened to three different translations, you might have heard some of them say, "Joab and all of Israel stayed there six months." This means that Joab and the Israelite army stayed in Edom for six months. It does not mean that every single Israelite went to Edom. Joab and the army killed all the men in Edom, and they also buried the Israelite soldiers who died in battle.

The Israelites' custom was to bury the body of a person who had died. Usually, the Israelites buried dead bodies in caves in the hillsides, instead of putting them in a hole in the ground.

Stop here and discuss these questions: In your culture, what do you do with a person's body after that person dies? What do your people think about burying someone's body in a tomb or in the ground? Pause this audio here.

Hadad and his father's servants were the only men who escaped from David's army. The word "servants" could mean that these men worked in Hadad's father's household, or it could mean that these men were government officials. First, Hadad and his father's servants went to Midian, which is south of Edom. Then they went to a region called Paran. This route actually took them closer to Israelite territory, but they had to go that way in order to get around the Red Sea and go toward Egypt.

Stop here and look at a map of Israel, Midian, Edom, Paran, and Egypt. Pause this audio here.

In Paran, some other men joined this group, and then the entire group went to Egypt. Hadad was a young boy when all this happened. The phrase in the original language is not very specific, but it suggests that Hadad was probably around seven or eight years old.

When Hadad arrived in Egypt, Pharaoh gave Hadad and his helpers a place to live, their own land, and food. It is possible that Pharaoh was kind to Hadad because Hadad was from the royal family of Edom. When the storyteller says that Hadad found great favor with Pharaoh, it means that Pharaoh liked Hadad and was willing to help him.

Stop here and discuss these questions: In your language, how do you say that a leader likes another person and does good things for that person? How will you talk about the fact that Pharaoh liked Hadad and gave Hadad good things? Pause this audio here.

When Hadad was old enough to get married, he married the sister of Pharaoh's wife. This made Hadad a member of Pharaoh's family. The storyteller says that Pharaoh gave Hadad the sister of his wife in marriage. This was a common way to say that Pharaoh arranged the marriage between Hadad and the Queen's sister.

Stop here and discuss these questions: In your culture, do men and women choose their own spouse, or does someone else choose for them? If someone else chooses for them, how do you talk about this situation? Pause this audio here.

It was a great honor for Hadad to marry the sister of Pharaoh's wife. Hadad's wife gave birth to a son named Genubath. Genubath grew up in the household of Pharaoh, which means that he spent a lot of time with Pharaoh's family members.

In scene two, the storyteller talks about what happened at the time that King David and Joab both died. The storyteller says that King David rested with his fathers. This was a common way to say that King David died. Hadad was still living in Egypt, but when he heard that David and Joab were dead, Hadad wanted to go back to Edom. Hadad asked Pharaoh for permission to leave Egypt and go back to his childhood home.

Pharaoh asked Hadad, "What do you lack here with me, that you want to go back to your own country?" Pharaoh might mean that he had given Hadad everything Hadad needed to have a good life in Egypt. Or, Pharaoh might really want to know what he could give Hadad to make Hadad stay in Egypt. Either way, it seems that Pharaoh liked Hadad and did not want Hadad to leave.

Here, it is acceptable to translate this statement as a regular question or as a question that does not expect an answer.

Stop here and decide if you will translate this as a question that does not expect an answer, or if you will translate it as a regular question. Pause this audio here.

Hadad told Pharaoh that Hadad lacked nothing. This means that Hadad had everything he wanted. But Hadad asked Pharaoh to let Hadad go back to his home country anyway. The storyteller does not tell us the rest of the story. Because the storyteller calls Hadad "an adversary of Solomon," and later in this story the storyteller says that Hadad did evil against Israel, Hadad probably did go back to Edom. From there, Hadad was able to make trouble for Solomon.

In scene three, God raised up another enemy against Solomon. The man's name was Rezon, son of Eliada. Rezon's lord was King Hadadezer of Zobah. At some point in time, Rezon ran away from his lord, Hadadezer. The city of Zobah was located north of Israel.

Stop here and look again at a map that shows Israel and Zobah. Pause this audio here.

If you have already translated the book of 2 Samuel, then you have already translated the story in which King David defeats King Hadadezer. It might be helpful to listen to that story to remember how you translated the names Hadadezer, Zobah, and Damascus.

Stop here and listen to the beginning of 2 Samuel chapter 8, when King David defeats Hadadezer. Pay close attention to how you translated the names Hadadezer, Zobah, and Damascus. Pause this audio here.

After David's army defeated Hadadezer's army, Rezon escaped. The storyteller does not tell us where Rezon went. The storyteller does say that Rezon gathered men to himself. This means that Rezon convinced men to join his group, and Rezon was the leader of the group. You might have heard the storyteller describe this group with many different words. This group traveled around and attacked people, stole things, and they might have even attacked King David's army.

Stop here and discuss this question: In the area where you live, what do you call a group of people that travels around to attack people and steal things? Which of these words or phrases could you use here? Pause this audio here.

When you listened to three different versions of this story, you might have heard the storyteller use different words or phrases to say that Rezon hated Israel very much. In the original language, this word also means that Rezon did not want Solomon to be the king over Zobah anymore. Instead, Rezon wanted to fight against Solomon and not obey Solomon.

Stop here and discuss these questions: How do you say in your language that someone "hates someone very much"? How could you say that they hate a leader so much that they fight against the leader? Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 11:14–25 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has three scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

The characters in this passage are:

  • Yahweh
  • King Solomon
  • Joab, the commander of David's army
  • King David
  • The men of Edom
  • Hadad the Edomite
  • Servants of Hadad's father
  • Men of Paran
  • Pharaoh
  • Queen Tahpenes
  • Hadad's wife
  • Genubath, son of Hadad
  • Rezon, son of Eliada
  • Hadadezer, king of Zobah
  • Rezon's followers
  • And the Israelites

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

So Yahweh caused Hadad, a member of the royal family of Edom, to become an enemy of Solomon. Long before this, when King David defeated the army of Edom, Joab, the commander of his army, stayed in Edom to bury the dead Israelite soldiers. Joab and the Israelite army remained in Edom for six months. During that time, they killed every male in Edom except for Hadad and some of his father's servants, who escaped to Egypt. This happened when Hadad was a young boy. Hadad and his father's servants left Midian and went to Paran, where some other men joined them. Then they all went to Egypt.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Hadad, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel scared. My family is dead, I cannot go home, and I do not know what is going to happen to me; or
  • I feel sad. I will never see my mother or father again, and I do not know if I will ever see my homeland again; or
  • I hate David and Joab. David and Joab killed my entire family.

Ask the people playing the servants of Hadad's father, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We feel very sad. Our king is dead, many of our people are dead, and our homeland belongs to the Israelites. What will happen to us? or
  • I am so angry that the Israelites did this to us. Our people are related. They should not treat us this way! or
  • Perhaps if we can save Hadad, then our people might be able to survive; or
  • If we can make it to Egypt, maybe we will be safe there.

Continue the drama.

Hadad and his father's servants went to Pharaoh, the king of Egypt, who gave them some land and a house and provided them with food. Hadad found great favor with Pharaoh. When Hadad grew up, Pharaoh arranged for Hadad to marry the sister of Queen Tahpenes, the sister of Pharaoh's own wife. Hadad's wife gave birth to a son, Genubath, and he grew up in the palace along with the sons of Pharaoh.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Hadad, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am relieved. I finally have a safe place to live; or
  • I am very grateful to Pharaoh, because he spared my life and took care of me and my men; or
  • I love my wife and my son, but I wish they could see what my country was like before the Israelites destroyed it; or
  • If I ever get a chance to get revenge on the Israelites, I will take it!

Ask the person playing Pharaoh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am happy that Hadad is now a member of my family; or
  • It was a good idea to treat Hadad well. Now Hadad owes me and will do whatever I ask; or
  • I hope that the Israelites are not angry that I protected Hadad.

Continue the drama.

When Hadad heard that King David and Joab were both dead, Hadad said to Pharaoh, "Let me go back to my own country."

"Why?" Pharaoh asked Hadad. "What do you lack here in Egypt, that you suddenly want to go back home?"

"Nothing," Hadad answered. "But please let me go anyway." So Hadad went back to his homeland.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Hadad, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am grateful to Pharaoh, but Pharaoh must understand that I miss my own country; or
  • I love my family, but I feel sad that my son has never seen my homeland; or
  • This is my chance to get revenge. If I cannot get revenge against David, I will get revenge against his son; or
  • It is dangerous to go back to Edom, but it means so much to me, so I will take the risk.

Ask the person playing the Pharaoh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I like Hadad. I do not want Hadad to leave. I might never see Hadad again; or
  • I am confused. I gave Hadad a good life and even brought him into my own family. Why does Hadad want to go back to a destroyed country? or
  • Why would Hadad take his wife and son away from a good life here and take them back to Israelite territory? or
  • I understand Hadad's desire to return to his homeland. I would never want to live outside of Egypt, my homeland.

Continue the drama.

God also caused Rezon, son of Eliada, to become an enemy of Solomon. Rezon had run away from his lord, Hadadezer, king of Zobah, and had become the leader of a gang of rebels. After David killed all the men in Hadadezer's army, Rezon and his men escaped to Damascus, and Rezon became king. Rezon was an enemy of Israel for the rest of Solomon's life, in addition to the evil that Hadad did. And Rezon hated Israel and continued to reign in Aram.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Rezon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I hate the Israelites. King David killed my king and stole my homeland. I will never forgive the Israelites for that; or
  • I feel proud that I was able to survive when King Hadadezer did not. Now I am the one with the power; or
  • I will never let the Israelites go unchallenged. I will make sure they remember me as the one who fought back.

Ask the person playing King Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am afraid. Hadad and Rezon are causing problems. I am worried my enemies might take some of my land; or
  • I am worried. If things are like this while I am still alive, what will it be like when my son becomes king? or
  • I feel guilty. I know this is happening because I disobeyed Yahweh; or
  • I once brought peace and prosperity to my people, but now they are paying for my sins.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 11:14–25 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

So Yahweh caused Hadad, a member of the royal family of Edom, to become an enemy of Solomon. The name Hadad means "loud noise" or "thunder." See the Master Glossary for more information on Yahweh.

Stop here and decide how you will translate the name Hadad. Will you translate the name according to what it means or according to the way it sounds? Pause this audio here.

Long before this, when King David defeated the army of Edom, Joab, the commander of David's army, was in Edom burying the dead soldiers. Joab and the Israelite army remained in Edom for six months. During that time, they killed every male in Edom except for Hadad and some of his father's servants, who escaped to Egypt. Be sure to translate king, David, commander, Israelite, and servant the same way you did in previous passages. See the Master Glossary for more information on king, David, Israelite, and servant.

All this happened when Hadad was a young boy. Hadad and these men left Midian and went to Paran, where some other men joined them. Then they all went to Egypt. Be sure to translate the names Edom, Egypt, and Midian the same way that you did in previous passages.

Hadad and the men went to Pharaoh, the king of Egypt, who gave them some land and a house and provided them with food. Hadad found great favor with Pharaoh. When Hadad grew up, Pharaoh arranged for Hadad to marry the sister of Queen Tahpenes, the sister of Pharaoh's own wife. Tahpenes is an Egyptian name. Be sure to translate Pharaoh and queen the same way you did in previous stories. See the Master Glossary for more information about Pharaoh and "queen."

Hadad's wife gave birth to a son, Genubath, and he was raised in Pharaoh's palace, along with the sons of Pharaoh. The word "palace" means a king's house. Be sure to use the same word for palace, or king's house, that you used in previous stories.

God also caused Rezon, son of Eliada, to become an enemy of Solomon. Be sure to translate God the same way you have in previous passages. Note that this is the word for God and not the name Yahweh. See the Master Glossary for more information on God.

Rezon had run away from his lord, Hadadezer, king of Zobah, and had become the leader of a gang of rebels, a group of people who attacked other people and stole things from them. After David killed all the men in Hadadezer's army, Rezon and his men escaped to Damascus, and Rezon became king. Be sure to translate Hadadezer, Zobah, and Damascus the same way you did in 2 Samuel chapter 8. A lord is a master of someone who is lower status.

Stop here and decide as a group how you will translate "master" or "lord." Be sure to translate lord the same way you did in previous books of the Bible. See the Master Glossary for more information on lord. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and decide how you will translate the phrase "gang of rebels." Pause this audio here.

Rezon was an enemy of Israel for the rest of Solomon's life, in addition to the evil that Hadad did. And Rezon hated Israel and continued to rule in Aram, or Syria. Be sure to use the same word for Israel and evil that you used in previous stories. See the Master Glossary for more information about Israel and evil.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 11:26–43
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 11:26–43 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 11:26–43 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 11:26–43 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous story, the storyteller told us about two men who caused problems during Solomon's reign: Hadad the Edomite and Rezon of Zobah. In this story, the prophet Ahijah goes to Jeroboam, son of Nebat, an Israelite from the tribe of Ephraim. Ahijah tells Jeroboam that after Solomon dies, Jeroboam will become the king over 10 tribes of Israel.

Remember that when King Solomon became old, he disobeyed Yahweh and worshipped other gods besides Yahweh. Because of this, Yahweh told Solomon that after Solomon died, Yahweh would divide the kingdom, and Solomon's son would only rule over one tribe. Yahweh chooses Jeroboam, son of Nebat, to be the king over the other 10 tribes. Jeroboam was from the tribe of Ephraim, from a town called Zeredah. The territory of Ephraim was in the center of Israel. Ephraim's territory had a lot of forests and good farmland. Zeredah was about 40 kilometers northwest of Jerusalem.

Stop here and look at a map of Israel that shows the location of the territory of Ephraim, the town of Zeredah, and the location of Jerusalem. Pause this audio here.

Jeroboam is a young man who works for Solomon. Jeroboam probably works on one of Solomon's construction projects, such as building the Millo or rebuilding the walls of Jerusalem. Remember, the Millo was a structure that Solomon built in Jerusalem, but no one is sure exactly what it was. The Millo might have been a terrace, a wall, or a landfill. Solomon sees that Jeroboam is strong and a very good worker. So Solomon gives Jeroboam a better job. Jeroboam becomes the boss of all the workers from his own tribe and also the workers from the tribe of Manasseh. The people of both these tribes were descendants of Joseph.

One day, Jeroboam has to go outside of Jerusalem. A man named Ahijah finds Jeroboam while Jeroboam is walking on the road, and when nobody else is around. Ahijah is a prophet whom Yahweh gave a special message to tell Jeroboam. Ahijah does not just tell Jeroboam the message, but also shows it in a way Jeroboam can see and remember. Ahijah is wearing a new cloak. A cloak is a piece of clothing that people wear over everyday clothing. A cloak is useful to keep a person warm when the weather is cold. A cloak can also help keep a person dry if it rains.

Stop here and look at a picture of a person wearing a cloak. Pause this audio here.

Ahijah takes off his cloak and tears it into 12 pieces. These pieces represent the 12 tribes of Israel. Then Ahijah tells Jeroboam to take 10 of the pieces of the cloak. Each piece represents one tribe of Israel.

Stop here and find a piece of cloth that will be easy to tear. Choose someone from the group to represent Ahijah. That person should tear the piece of cloth into 12 pieces. Then choose someone from the group to represent Jeroboam. The person who represents Ahijah should give the person who plays Jeroboam 10 pieces of cloth. Pause this audio here.

This action shows that Jeroboam will become the leader of 10 tribes. When Jeroboam holds the pieces, Jeroboam has a way to remember the promise Yahweh had made to him.

Some people might get confused because there are 12 tribes of Israel, and Solomon's son will rule over one tribe while Jeroboam will rule over 10 tribes. Ten plus one adds up to 11, which seems to leave one tribe out. But there is an easy explanation. Solomon was from the tribe of Judah, so people expected the tribe of Judah to follow Solomon's son. So when Yahweh says that Solomon's son will rule over one tribe, it really means "your tribe plus one more tribe." Jerusalem is located in the territory that belongs to the tribe of Benjamin, so in order for Solomon's descendants to rule in Jerusalem, they also have to rule over the tribe of Benjamin.

Through the prophet Ahijah, Yahweh tells Jeroboam that some things will definitely happen. Yahweh also tells Jeroboam some things that will happen only if Jeroboam obeys Yahweh. Yahweh says that Jeroboam will definitely become the king of 10 tribes of Israel. Solomon is still king for now, but after Solomon dies, Solomon's son will only get to rule over one tribe of Israel. This was a punishment because Solomon had started to worship other gods.

Yahweh says that Solomon's son will get to rule over one tribe of Israel so that David will always have a lamp before Yahweh in Jerusalem. Yahweh uses creative language here to describe the descendants of David. The lamp represents the kings of Israel who are descended from King David. A lamp gives off light, and in the Bible, light represents hope and things that are good. As long as a descendant of David is the king of Israel, this means that Yahweh has kept his promises to David and the people of Israel. Therefore, the people can have hope.

Stop here and find a lamp, a flashlight, or a candle. If possible, have the entire team go inside a room or a closet that has no windows. Turn on the lamp or flashlight, or light the candle in the dark room. Then discuss these questions: In your culture, what does light symbolize? After you have finished this discussion, turn the light off or blow out the candle and discuss what darkness symbolizes in your culture. When you are done, turn the light back on and come out of the dark room. Pause this audio here.

Yahweh also tells Jeroboam, "You will rule over all that your heart wants." This means that Jeroboam will rule over all of the territory that he wants. This is another one of the things that Yahweh tells Jeroboam will definitely happen.

But some of Yahweh's promises have conditions. Yahweh says if Jeroboam obeys Yahweh and does what is right, then Yahweh will always be with Jeroboam to help him. And if Jeroboam obeys Yahweh's commands, Jeroboam's descendants will be kings too and will rule over the 10 tribes.

Stop here and tell a story about someone who received a promise that had conditions. Did the person fulfill the conditions and get the promise? Or did the person fail to fulfill the conditions? Pause this audio here.

Solomon must hear about what Ahijah said to Jeroboam, because Solomon tries to kill Jeroboam. However, Solomon is not able to kill Jeroboam. Then Jeroboam goes to Egypt and stays there until Solomon dies.

Stop here and look at a map of Israel and Egypt. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller finishes this story by telling us some final details about Solomon's life. Solomon ruled over Israel for 40 years. People wrote other books that talked about other things Solomon did. These books are lost now. The people bury Solomon in Jerusalem, the city of his father, David, and Solomon's son Rehoboam becomes the next king.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 11:26–43 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has three scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller tells us that Jeroboam, son of Nebat, was a capable officer who served King Solomon.

In the second scene: Ahijah the prophet gives Jeroboam a message from Yahweh. Yahweh tells Jeroboam that Yahweh will make Jeroboam king of Israel.

In the third scene: King Solomon tries to kill Jeroboam, but Jeroboam escapes to Egypt. Then Solomon dies, and his son Rehoboam becomes king.

The characters in this passage are:

  • King Solomon
  • Jeroboam
  • The labor force of the house of Joseph
  • Ahijah, from Shiloh
  • The Israelites
  • Shishak, king of Egypt
  • Rehoboam
  • And Yahweh

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, Jeroboam, son of Nebat, rebels against King Solomon. To rebel means to fight against a ruler or government because you do not want to follow their rules anymore. When you listened to three different versions of this story, you might have heard the storyteller say things like "Jeroboam raised a hand against the king" or "Jeroboam turned against King Solomon." These are different ways to say that Jeroboam rebelled against King Solomon.

Stop here and describe a situation where some people decided that they did not want to follow their ruler or leader anymore and began to oppose that leader. How do you describe what those people did? Pause this audio here.

Now the storyteller tells us the story of Jeroboam to help explain how Jeroboam began to rebel against King Solomon. Jeroboam was part of the tribe of Ephraim, and Jeroboam came from the town of Zeredah. Jeroboam's mother was a widow named Zeruah. Jeroboam was a servant of Solomon. Remember that in the Bible, the word "servant" might mean a domestic servant, such as a person who gets paid to cook, clean, or work in the house, but "servant" can also mean a government official or any person who helps the king in some way. Most Bible scholars believe Jeroboam probably worked on one of Solomon's building projects, like the Millo. The Millo was a structure that Solomon built in Jerusalem, but Bible scholars are not sure what the Millo was. Some scholars think the Millo might have been supporting terraces, while others think it might have been a place where people filled in land. Earlier in 1 Kings, you translated a story in which the storyteller mentioned the Millo. You should translate this word here the same way you translated it there. If you need to go back and listen to that story again, listen to the middle part of 1 Kings chapter 9, where the storyteller talks about Solomon's building projects.

Stop here if necessary and listen to 1 Kings chapter 9 in your language. Pause this audio here.

Solomon also built a wall around Jerusalem. In those days, it was very common for people to build walls around large, important cities. Since Jerusalem was the capital city of Israel, Jerusalem was the most important city in Israel. When this story happened, either the wall of Jerusalem was not complete yet, or a part of the wall had broken down, and Solomon wanted his workers to fix it.

When you listened to three different versions of this story, you might have heard the storyteller describe Jeroboam with different words. The storyteller wants us to understand that Jeroboam was very strong and smart and did his work very well.

Stop here and discuss this question: In your language, how do you describe someone who is strong and smart and does their work well? Pause this audio here.

Solomon realized that Jeroboam worked hard until he got things done, and that when Jeroboam encountered a problem, Jeroboam came up with clever ways to fix that problem.

Stop here and discuss these questions: How do you describe a person who works hard until he gets things done, and who comes up with clever ways to fix a problem? Which of these words could you use to describe Jeroboam? Pause this audio here.

Because Jeroboam was such a good worker, Solomon made Jeroboam the leader of all the workers from the house of Joseph. "House of Joseph" means the tribes of Ephraim and Manasseh. The people in these two tribes were descendants of Joseph, the son of Jacob. When you listened to three different versions of this story, you might have heard the storyteller say that Solomon forced these workers to work for him, like they were slaves. In an earlier story, the storyteller said that King Solomon did not make any Israelites into slaves. So make sure that the word you choose for "forced labor" does not mean that these Israelites were slaves. A good choice might be to say that these Israelites were the people who worked hard for Solomon in building projects.

Stop here and discuss how you will say that these Israelites were the people who worked hard for Solomon in his building projects. Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, Jeroboam travels outside Jerusalem. The storyteller does not tell us where Jeroboam is going. While Jeroboam is on the road outside Jerusalem, Jeroboam encounters Ahijah, who is a prophet. Ahijah is a Shilonite, which means he is from the town of Shiloh. Shiloh is also in the territory of Ephraim, about 30 kilometers north of Jerusalem. When Jeroboam sees Ahijah, Ahijah is wearing a new cloak.

Stop here and look again at a picture of someone wearing a cloak. Pause this audio here.

Ahijah removes the cloak and tears it into 12 pieces. Then Ahijah tells Jeroboam to take 10 pieces. In this way, Jeroboam can see a picture of what Yahweh is about to do. Then Ahijah tells Jeroboam that Ahijah has a message for Jeroboam directly from Yahweh, the God of Israel.

Stop here and tell a story where one person speaks and quotes the words that another person said. How do you describe a situation where one person tells another person what a third person said? When you tell this story, how do you show who is saying the words? Pause this audio here.

In this message to Jeroboam, Yahweh says some similar things to what Yahweh said in the message Yahweh gave to Solomon earlier. It might be helpful for you to go back and listen to how you translated that message.

Stop here and listen to the beginning of 1 Kings chapter 11. Pay close attention to how you translated Yahweh's speech to Solomon. Pause this audio here.

First, Yahweh tells Jeroboam some things that will definitely happen. Yahweh begins this statement with a special word that means "Look" or "Pay close attention." This word means that what comes after is very important.

Stop here and discuss this question: What words or phrases does your language use to tell the listener that something very important is coming next? Pause this audio here.

Yahweh says that Yahweh is about to tear the kingdom from the hand of Solomon and give 10 of the tribes to Jeroboam. This means that Yahweh will cause Jeroboam to become the new king over 10 tribes of Israel.

Yahweh tells Jeroboam that Solomon's descendants will continue to rule over one tribe of Israelites, because Yahweh had promised David that one of David's descendants would always rule as king in Jerusalem. Jeroboam was not a descendant of David, so Jeroboam could not rule as king in Jerusalem.

Next, Yahweh explains that Yahweh will tear the kingdom away from Solomon because Solomon worships the gods of other nations. Although Yahweh has been talking about Solomon worshipping other gods, Yahweh tells Jeroboam that "they" have turned away from Yahweh and worshipped other gods, and that "they" have not followed Yahweh's commands and statutes, or instructions. This means that Solomon led other Israelites to disobey Yahweh, too.

When you listened to three different versions of this story, you might have heard different ways to say that the people had intentionally stopped worshipping Yahweh and had started worshipping other gods. Yahweh says that the people had abandoned Yahweh completely.

Stop here and tell a story about a time when a leader did something wrong and bad things happened to them. What was the impact on that leader? What was the impact on the people around the leader? How much did the leader's actions affect the ordinary people? Pause this audio here.

Yahweh continues his message to Jeroboam and says, "They have not walked in my ways, they have not done what is right in my eyes, and they have not kept my statutes and ordinances." Yahweh actually says the same thing in three different ways. This shows that this matter was extremely important to Yahweh.

"They have not walked in my ways" means that the people have not lived the way that Yahweh wanted them to live. "They have not done what is right in my eyes" means that the people did not do the things that Yahweh considered right. Instead, the people did things that Yahweh considered wrong. "They have not kept my statutes and ordinances" means that the people have disobeyed Yahweh's rules.

Next, Yahweh says that Yahweh will not take the entire kingdom of Israel away from Solomon's "hands," or power. Yahweh also will not take the kingdom away from Solomon while Solomon is still alive. After Solomon dies, while Solomon's son is king, Jeroboam will become king of 10 tribes of Israelites. Solomon's son will be the king of just one tribe of Israelites. Yahweh will allow Solomon's son to be king of one tribe because of the promise Yahweh made to David.

In this scene, Yahweh calls David "my servant" three times. In this case, "servant" simply means a person who does something for a person of higher status. Yahweh might also call David "my servant" to emphasize the fact that David almost always did exactly what Yahweh wanted David to do. David was not perfect, but David loved Yahweh and wanted to obey Yahweh all the time.

Yahweh says, "David will always have a lamp before me in Jerusalem." The lamp is a symbol. The lamp stands for David's family. The lamp means Yahweh will keep his promise that someone from David's family would always be king in Jerusalem.

Stop here and look at a photo of a lamp. Try to think of any sayings or poems in your language where one object represents another object. Tell each other some of these sayings or poems. Then decide how you want to translate the statement, "David will always have a lamp before me." Pause this audio here.

Jerusalem is the place that Yahweh has chosen for "his name" to live among Yahweh's people. In Israelite culture, Yahweh's name often means Yahweh's presence. Yahweh chose to send Yahweh's presence to the temple in Jerusalem, which was the only place where the people of Israel could worship Yahweh.

Yahweh promises Jeroboam again that Jeroboam will sit on the throne of Israel. This means that Jeroboam will be king of the Israelites. And in addition, Yahweh says that Jeroboam will rule over all Jeroboam's heart desires. Remember, the Israelites believed that all of a person's feelings and desires come from the person's heart. This statement means that Yahweh will allow Jeroboam to rule over all the territory that Jeroboam wants to rule over.

Stop here and discuss this question: What sayings do you have that mean a person will get everything he wants? Which of these could you use here? Pause this audio here.

The next part of Yahweh's message to Jeroboam comes with a condition. This part of the promise will only happen if Jeroboam obeys Yahweh. Yahweh promises to establish Jeroboam's house. This means that Jeroboam's descendants will also be kings of Israel, but only if Jeroboam listens to Yahweh and does what is right in Yahweh's eyes. This means that Jeroboam must obey everything Yahweh says.

At the end of Yahweh's message, Yahweh says that he will do this to punish the descendants of David, but that the punishment will not last forever.

In the third scene, Solomon tries to have someone kill Jeroboam. Somehow, Solomon finds out about Ahijah's message to Jeroboam, and Solomon does not like it. But Jeroboam arises and runs away to Egypt. This means that Jeroboam quickly leaves Israel. It does not mean that Jeroboam is lying down or sitting down when Jeroboam finds out that Solomon wants someone to kill Jeroboam. Jeroboam stays in Egypt until Solomon dies.

At the end of this scene, King Solomon dies. The storyteller uses specific words to give his hearers specific information about the time that Solomon ruled as king. First, the storyteller mentions that someone else wrote another book about King Solomon. This book was called the Book of the History of Solomon. The storyteller introduces this information using a question that does not expect an answer. This question means, "Someone wrote down everything that Solomon did and all his wisdom in the Book of the History of Solomon." Nobody knows where this book is now.

At this time in Israel, people did not have books made of paper as we do now. People wrote important information on papyrus, parchment, or animal skins. Then the people rolled these items up into a scroll. Scrolls could easily become damaged, so the people kept scrolls in a safe place.

Stop here and look at a picture of a scroll. Pause this audio here.

Next, the storyteller says that Solomon ruled Israel from Jerusalem for 40 years. Then Solomon died, and the people buried Solomon in the City of David with his father. People sometimes call Jerusalem the "City of David." Finally, Solomon's son Rehoboam became the next king.

In the rest of the Book of Kings, whenever the storyteller ends his stories about a particular king, the storyteller will tell us the same information. The storyteller will name one or more books that contain stories about that king. Then the storyteller will say how long the king ruled and the name of the king's capital city. Then the storyteller will say that the king rested with his fathers, or died, and the storyteller will say where the people buried that king. Finally, the storyteller will say who became the next king.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 11:26–43 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has three scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • King Solomon
  • Jeroboam
  • The labor force of the house of Joseph
  • Ahijah, from Shiloh
  • The Israelites
  • Shishak, king of Egypt
  • Rehoboam
  • And Yahweh

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Another man who rebelled against King Solomon was one of Solomon's officials, Jeroboam, son of Nebat. Jeroboam was from a town called Zeredah in Ephraim. Jeroboam's mother was a widow named Zeruah. This is the story of how Jeroboam rebelled against the king: Solomon built the Millo and repaired damage to the walls of Jerusalem, the city of his father, David. Jeroboam was a very capable man, and Solomon noticed that Jeroboam was a very hard worker. So Solomon made Jeroboam the official over all the labor force from the tribes of Ephraim and Manasseh, the sons of Joseph.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing King Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am pleased with Jeroboam. Jeroboam is a very good worker; or
  • With Jeroboam in charge, I do not need to worry about those workers from Ephraim and Manasseh; or
  • I feel fortunate to have such a capable worker as Jeroboam to take care of Jerusalem.

Ask the person playing Jeroboam, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am grateful that King Solomon recognized my hard work and rewarded me; or
  • I feel proud to be the boss, not just over other workers from the tribe of Ephraim, but from Manasseh too! or
  • I have King Solomon's favor. If I work hard and act smart, I could become even more important!

One day, when Jeroboam went out of Jerusalem, the prophet Ahijah from Shiloh met Jeroboam on the road when no one else was around. Ahijah was wearing a new cloak. Then Ahijah took hold of the new cloak and tore it into 12 pieces. Ahijah said to Jeroboam, "Take 10 pieces for yourself. This is what Yahweh, the God of Israel, says to you: 'Watch, I am going to tear the kingdom away from Solomon, and I will give 10 tribes to you. But Solomon will continue to have one tribe for the sake of my servant David, and for the sake of Jerusalem, the city I have chosen out of all the tribes of Israel. I am doing this because Solomon and the Israelites have turned away from me and worshipped Ashtoreth, the goddess of the Sidonians, Chemosh, the god of the Moabites, and Milcom, the god of the Ammonites. The Israelites have not followed my way, they have not done what is right in my eyes, and they have not obeyed my statutes and ordinances, as Solomon's father, David, did.'"

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing Jeroboam, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am nervous. I do not know how to be a king! or
  • I am afraid. If Solomon hears about this, he might try to hurt me; or
  • I am surprised. I thought Solomon had been a very good king. The country is rich and powerful; or
  • I feel proud because soon, 10 tribes of Israel will have to obey me!

Ask the person playing Ahijah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel sad that Yahweh will split the kingdom in two. Our people will not be united anymore; or
  • I am angry at Solomon. Solomon knew he should not worship foreign gods; or
  • I do not want to deliver this sad message, but I must obey Yahweh.

Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am very sad that Solomon disobeyed me and is worshipping other gods; or
  • Solomon has led my people astray, and now they are worshipping false gods. I must act to show that people must not break my commandments; or
  • Solomon must face consequences for his actions, but I will keep my promise to Solomon's father, David.

Ahijah continued to tell Jeroboam what Yahweh said: "However, I will not take the entire kingdom away from Solomon. I made Solomon king for his entire life for the sake of my servant David. I chose David because he kept my commands and statutes. But I will tear the kingdom away from Solomon's son and make you ruler over 10 tribes. I will give one tribe to Solomon's son, so that the descendants of my servant David will continue to reign in Jerusalem. David's descendants will shine like a lamp in Jerusalem, which is the city I have chosen to be the place for my name. And I will put you on the throne of Israel, and you will rule over everything you want. If you listen to me, follow my rules, and do what I consider right, and if you obey my statutes and commands, like my servant David did, then I will always be with you. I will allow your descendants to rule over Israel forever, as I did for David. Because of Solomon's sin, I will punish the descendants of David, but the punishment will not last forever."

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing Jeroboam, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Ahijah said that Solomon will still be the king until Solomon dies. What am I supposed to do in the meantime? or
  • I feel sad that Yahweh will soon divide my country; or
  • I feel excited. I will reign over more people than Solomon's son will!

Ask the person playing Ahijah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am nervous about what Solomon will do if he finds out about this message from Yahweh. Solomon could get angry and kill me; or
  • I hope Jeroboam will be a good and wise king; or
  • I am afraid of what is going to happen. This could be very bad for a lot of people.

Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am sad that Solomon disobeyed me and now worships idols; or
  • Even though Solomon sinned, his descendants are still from the royal line of David. They are still the ones I chose to rule in Jerusalem; or
  • I am giving Jeroboam a chance to be a good king and receive many blessings.

Solomon tried to have people kill Jeroboam, but Jeroboam ran away and went to Shishak, the pharaoh of Egypt.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am so angry. I trusted Jeroboam and made him one of my top officials, but Jeroboam has betrayed me; or
  • This is happening because of my sin. It is all my fault; or
  • Maybe I can fix this. Maybe if someone kills Jeroboam, my son will keep the entire kingdom. How can I make that happen?

Ask the person playing Jeroboam, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • This is what I was afraid of. Solomon thinks I am a traitor. I have to escape! or
  • I am grateful that Pharaoh Shishak has been kind to me. I will remember his kindness when I am king; or
  • I feel eager to be the next king of Israel.

And Jeroboam stayed in Egypt until Solomon died. The rest of the events of Solomon's reign as king, including all his deeds and his wisdom, are in the Book of the History of Solomon. King Solomon ruled in Jerusalem over all Israel for 40 years. Then Solomon died, and the people buried his body in the City of David, the city named for his father. And Solomon's son Rehoboam became the next king.

Ask the person playing Rehoboam, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am worried because my father told me that Yahweh will take the kingdom away from me; or
  • I am angry that Yahweh chose Jeroboam to be king over Israel, and I will only rule over one tribe; or
  • I feel sad. My father and grandfather built this wealthy, strong kingdom, but I am going to lose most of it!

Ask the person playing Jeroboam, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel relieved. Now that Solomon is dead, I can go back home; or
  • I am happy. Now Yahweh will fulfill his promise, and I will be the king! or
  • Yahweh chose me for a reason. I will be ready to lead the people when the time comes.

Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel sad about Solomon. I gave Solomon wisdom and riches, but he still chose to turn away from me and worship idols; or
  • If Solomon had obeyed me, I could have given his son a strong kingdom. But now I am going to divide the kingdom and take it away from Rehoboam; or
  • It makes me sad to divide the kingdom of Israel, but it is necessary to show the people that they must obey my commands.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 11:26–43 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Another man who rebelled against King Solomon was one of his officials, Jeroboam, son of Nebat. Jeroboam's mother was a widow, a woman whose husband had died. Be sure to use the same word for widow and king that you used in previous stories. See the Master Glossary for more information on king.

This is the story of how Jeroboam rebelled against the king: Solomon had built the Millo and repaired the damage to the walls of Jerusalem, the city of his father, David. Jeroboam was a very capable man, and Solomon noticed that Jeroboam was a very hard worker. So Solomon made Jeroboam the official over all the labor force from the tribes of Ephraim and Manasseh, the sons of Joseph. Be sure to translate Millo, Jerusalem, Ephraim, Manasseh, and Joseph the same way you did in previous passages.

One day, when Jeroboam went out of Jerusalem, the prophet Ahijah from Shiloh met Jeroboam on the road when no one else was around. See the Master Glossary for more information on prophet and translate this word in the same way you have previously. Be sure to translate Shiloh the same way you did in 1 Kings 2:27.

Ahijah was wearing a new cloak. Then Ahijah took hold of the cloak and tore it into 12 pieces.

Stop here and look at a picture of a cloak. Decide as a group what word you will use for cloak. See cloak in the Master Glossary for more information. Pause this audio here.

Ahijah said to Jeroboam, "Take 10 pieces for yourself. This is what Yahweh, the God of Israel, says to you: Watch, I am going to tear the kingdom away from Solomon, and I will give 10 tribes to you." Be sure to translate Yahweh, God, Israel, kingdom, and tribe the same way you did in previous passages. See the Master Glossary for more information on Yahweh, God, Israel, and kingdom.

Ahijah continued, "But Solomon will continue to have one tribe for the sake of my servant David, and for the sake of Jerusalem, the city I have chosen out of all the tribes of Israel." When Yahweh says that David was Yahweh's servant, Yahweh does not mean that David was a domestic servant who cooked and cleaned. Yahweh means that David did what Yahweh wanted David to do. Be sure to use the same word for David, Jerusalem, and Israel that you used in previous stories. See the Master Glossary for more information on David, servant, Jerusalem, and Israel. Use the same word for servant as you have in previous stories when it means someone who does what Yahweh wants them to do.

Ahijah continued, "I am doing this because they have turned away from me and worshipped Ashtoreth, the goddess of the Sidonians, Chemosh, the god of the Moabites, and Milcom, the god of the Ammonites. They have not followed my ways, they have not done what is right in my eyes, and they have not obeyed my statutes and ordinances, as Solomon's father David did." Be sure to use the same words you used in previous stories for worship, god, female god or goddess, statutes, and ordinances. See the Master Glossary for more information on worship, god, statutes, and ordinances. Remember that when storytellers use words like "statutes and ordinances" together, they are talking about all of Yahweh's instructions or laws.

Ahijah continued to tell Jeroboam that Yahweh would not take the entire kingdom away from Solomon's "hands" or power. Use the same words for taking something out of someone's hands or power.

Yahweh said that he chose David because David kept Yahweh's statutes and commands.

See the Master Glossary for statutes and commands, and remember that you can also just say "Yahweh's instructions."

Yahweh wanted David's descendants to reign like a lamp shining in Jerusalem. In that time, lamps were bowl-shaped, and people filled them with oil. People put a wick, or a long string of cloth, in the oil and lit it.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for lamp. Look at the photo of the lamp again if needed. Pause this audio here.

Ahijah continued, "And I will put you on the throne of Israel, and you will rule over everything your heart desires." Be sure to use the same word you used in previous stories for throne and heart. See the Master Glossary for more information on heart.

Stop here and decide how you will translate the phrase "everything your heart desires." Pause this audio here.

Yahweh promised to build a lasting house, or dynasty, for Jeroboam if Jeroboam followed Yahweh. In this case, Jeroboam's house refers to his descendants who would rule as king after him.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for "house" or dynasty. If you use the word for house, you may refer to the Master Glossary for household if needed. Pause this audio here.

Ahijah continued, "Because of Solomon's sin, I will punish the sons, or descendants, of David, but the punishment will not last forever." The storyteller calls the descendants of David "sons" in the original language, but these sons refer to all of David's descendants. Use the same word or phrase you have used in previous passages, and refer to descendants in the Master Glossary if necessary.

Solomon tried to have Jeroboam killed, but Jeroboam ran away and went to Shishak, the pharaoh of Egypt. Be sure to use the same words that you used in previous stories for pharaoh and Egypt. See the Master Glossary for more information about pharaoh if needed.

Jeroboam stayed in Egypt until Solomon died. The rest of the events of Solomon's reign as king, including all Solomon's deeds and his wisdom, are in the Book of the History of Solomon. King Solomon ruled in Jerusalem over all Israel for 40 years. Then Solomon died and was buried in the City of David, the city named for his father. Be sure to use the same words for wisdom and "City of David" as you used in previous stories.

Stop here and look at a photo of a scroll again if needed. Decide as a group how you will talk about the title "The Book of the History of Solomon." For more information, refer to "scroll" in the Master Glossary.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 12:1–15
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 12:1–15 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 12:1–15 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 12:1–15 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous passage, King Solomon died, and Solomon's son, Rehoboam, became the new king. Solomon's temple was in Jerusalem, the capital city of Israel, and Rehoboam also lived in Jerusalem. Now, the northern tribes of Israel gather in Shechem in the north of Israel to recognize Rehoboam as their new king. Jeroboam, who had led a rebellion against Solomon, now leads the northern tribes to ask Rehoboam to give them less work and make their lives easier. Rehoboam asks two different groups of advisers how he should answer the people of Israel, and Rehoboam decides to listen to the advice of his younger advisers instead of the advice of his older advisers.

King David and David's son, King Solomon, ruled over all of Israel. It is likely there were problems between the northern and southern tribes of Israel even before Rehoboam. But during the rule of King Rehoboam, the tribes of Israel divide into two parts: one in the north and one in the south. This passage is the start of a new section in the book of 1 Kings, and a new part of the history of the people of Israel as they lived with a divided kingdom. This passage is similar to 2 Chronicles 10:1–7 and 2 Chronicles 10:8–19.
