If you have already translated the two similar passages in 2 Chronicles, you should translate this passage the same way as the passages in 2 Chronicles in most places, except where there are some small differences in the passages.

Stop here and listen to your translations of 2 Chronicles 10:1–7 and 2 Chronicles 10:8–19 now, if your team has already translated these similar passages in 2 Chronicles. Pause this audio here.

As the story begins, Rehoboam goes to Shechem, which is an important religious city in the north of Israel in the land of the tribe of Ephraim. Shechem was the first religious center of the 12 tribes of Israel when the Israelites first came into the land of Canaan. The northern tribes of Israel had already gathered in Shechem to renew their loyalty to David's grandson, Rehoboam, as their new king. These tribes are: [Reuben, Simeon, Issachar, Zebulun, Dan, Naphtali, Gad, Asher, Ephraim, and Manasseh.]{.mark} However, some people think that because the northern tribes did not travel to Jerusalem to make Rehoboam their king, this shows that these tribes already planned to leave Rehoboam and make their own kingdom.

Stop here and look at a map that includes Shechem as a group. Pause this audio here.

Also, stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about a time your community, village, city, or nation chose a new leader. How did your community choose this leader? What did you like or dislike about the new leader? Pause this audio here.

Jeroboam, the son of Nebat, is another important person in this passage. We know from a previous story that Jeroboam was from the tribe of Ephraim and that Jeroboam was a servant of King Solomon in the past. Yahweh had promised to give Jeroboam control of 10 of the tribes as a punishment because Solomon worshipped false gods. King Solomon tried to kill Jeroboam, so Jeroboam ran away to live in Egypt. Now, in this passage, Jeroboam hears the news that King Solomon has died and that Solomon's son, Rehoboam, is the new king in Israel. The storyteller gives background information that Jeroboam was living in exile in Egypt while King Solomon was still alive.

Stop here and look at a map that includes Egypt as a group. Pause this audio here.

In the next part of the story, Jeroboam and the northern tribes of Israel ask Rehoboam to lighten their heavy load of work. The people say that Rehoboam's father, Solomon, put a heavy yoke on them, and they want Rehoboam to make their yoke, or burden, lighter. A yoke is a wooden bar that joins together the necks of two cows or oxen so they can work together. King Solomon forced the people to build Solomon's temple, his palace, and other buildings. It is likely that the people also had to pay heavy taxes to Solomon.

Stop here and look at a picture of a yoke as a group. Pause this audio here.

Jeroboam and the people tell Rehoboam they will be loyal to Rehoboam if Rehoboam makes their lives easier. The people do not ask Rehoboam to take away their burdens completely, but to make their burdens less than their burdens were during the rule of Rehoboam's father, Solomon. It was common during Rehoboam's time for a new king to do what the people requested in exchange for their loyalty to him as their new king.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Think about your previous discussion about a time when your community chose a new leader. Describe how your new leader was different from your previous leader. What did the new leader do or say to make the people like him? Pause this audio here.

Rehoboam tells Jeroboam and the people to leave for three days, probably so that Rehoboam can think about how to answer the people's request to lighten their heavy load of work. Rehoboam does not pray to Yahweh or talk to a prophet about what he should do. Instead, Rehoboam talks with two different groups of advisers during these three days. First, Rehoboam consults his older advisers who served his father, King Solomon. These older, experienced advisers tell Rehoboam to be a servant to the people today, and in the future, the people will always be Rehoboam's servants. A servant is someone who works for their master. In this passage, the advisers want Rehoboam to act like a servant to his people and speak kindly to the people in order to keep the loyalty of the northern tribes. Some people think the older advisers want Rehoboam to be kind to the people now, and then later, Rehoboam will be free to be harsh to the people.

Rehoboam does not listen to the advice of Rehoboam's older advisers, which is surprising because people in Israel respected the advice of older advisers. Instead, Rehoboam asks his younger advisers what they recommend Rehoboam should do. It is likely that Rehoboam already plans to follow the advice of the younger advisers before Rehoboam hears their answer. We know from a later passage that Rehoboam was 41 years old when he became king, and these younger advisers are the same age as Rehoboam. These young men grew up with Rehoboam, and they do not have the same years of experience and wisdom that Rehoboam's older advisers had.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Who do you talk to for advice in your community? Talk about a time when you asked a few different people for advice about the same situation. How was the advice different? How did you choose which advice to follow? Pause this audio here.

The younger advisers tell Rehoboam to be even harsher than Rehoboam's father, King Solomon. The younger advisers want Rehoboam to show the people that he is very strong and that Rehoboam's easiest demands will be worse than the hardest work Solomon made the people do. The younger advisers use a proverb, or saying, that Rehoboam's little finger is thicker than his father Solomon's loins, or waist. Some people think the little finger is a polite way to refer to the male sexual organ.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell some proverbs, or wise sayings, in your language. Practice explaining what each proverb means. Pause this audio here.

The younger advisers also say that Rehoboam should make the yoke even heavier for the people. The men say Solomon punished, or disciplined, the people with whips, which means Solomon was harsh on the people. A whip has a long handle, which people often make of wood, and a flexible cord that a punisher uses to hit a person.

Stop here and look at a picture of a whip as a group. Pause this audio here.

The advisers say that Rehoboam should punish the people with whips that sting like scorpions.

A scorpion is an insect in Israel that has a poisonous sting. This could mean that Rehoboam should punish the people more than Solomon. Or it could mean that Rehoboam will punish the people with whips that have pieces of sharp metal in them, which will cut into a person's body like the point of a scorpion's sting. If the tribes of Israel do not listen to King Rehoboam, then King Rehoboam will painfully punish the people.

Stop here and look at a picture of a scorpion and a picture of a whip with metal at the end of the cord. Pause this audio here.

Jeroboam and the people come back to Rehoboam after three days, and King Rehoboam chooses to follow the advice of the younger advisers. King Rehoboam speaks harshly, or unkindly, with the people, and Rehoboam rejects the advice of the older advisers. This also shows that King Rehoboam rejects the wisdom of his father, King Solomon, whom people knew was very wise. If Rehoboam had listened to his people, Israel would have had an opportunity to remain one unified nation, but because King Rehoboam did not listen to his people, the nation of Israel later divided into two parts. Rehoboam adds to people's oppression. This is similar to what Pharaoh, the king of Egypt, did when Pharaoh made the Israelites work harder instead of making their burdens lighter.

In the final part of this story, the storyteller tells us that the events in this story are really from Yahweh. The reason Yahweh allowed these things to happen was because these things fulfilled the promise that Yahweh made through Ahijah the prophet in a previous passage. Remember that the prophet Ahijah was from Shiloh, and Ahijah had told Jeroboam that Yahweh would give Jeroboam 10 of the tribes of Israel. Remember that in the prophecy, Ahijah said this was a punishment on Solomon because Solomon stopped following Yahweh and followed the gods of the other nations around him.

We see that Rehoboam acted foolishly when he listened to bad advice, but the ultimate reason that the kingdom of Israel split is because Yahweh made it happen. Yahweh is in control of everything.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 12:1–15 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has seven scenes.

In the first scene: The northern tribes of Israel gather in the city of Shechem to make Rehoboam their new king.

In the second scene: The northern tribes of Israel send for Jeroboam to come back from his exile in Egypt. Jeroboam comes back to Israel.

In the third scene: Jeroboam and the people ask Rehoboam to lighten their load. Rehoboam tells the people to come back to Rehoboam in three days.

In the fourth scene: Rehoboam asks the older advisers for advice. The older advisers tell Rehoboam to answer the people with kind words.

In the fifth scene: Rehoboam rejects the advice of the older advisers. Instead, Rehoboam asks his younger advisers for advice. The younger advisers tell Rehoboam to be harsh to the people.

In the sixth scene: Jeroboam and the people come back to Rehoboam after three days. Rehoboam speaks harshly to the people. Rehoboam says he will be even harsher than his father Solomon was to them.

In the seventh scene: King Rehoboam does not listen to the people. Yahweh causes this to happen in order to fulfill the promise that Yahweh made to Jeroboam through the prophet Ahijah.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Rehoboam
  • The northern tribes of Israel
  • Jeroboam, the son of Nebat
  • Older advisers who served King Solomon in the past
  • Younger advisers who are Rehoboam's age
  • Yahweh
  • And the prophet Ahijah, the Shilonite

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

Remember that before this passage, King Solomon died, and now King Solomon's son, Rehoboam, is the new king of Israel. This passage is the start of a new section in the book of 1 Kings. In this passage, and in the following passages, we will see that because of King Rehoboam's choices, Israel divides into a northern kingdom and a southern kingdom. Remember that this passage is similar to 2 Chronicles 10:1–7 and 2 Chronicles 10:8–19, and it is best to translate this passage similarly to these passages in 2 Chronicles.

In the first scene, the northern tribes of Israel gather in Shechem, a city in the north of Israel. Shechem is 65 kilometers north of Jerusalem, between Mount Ebal and Mount Gerizim. Shechem is in the land of the tribes of Ephraim and Manasseh. Rehoboam also goes to Shechem because the northern tribes want to recognize Rehoboam as their new king.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What is the most natural order to translate this scene of the passage?

  • All Israel gathered in Shechem to make Rehoboam their king, so then Rehoboam went to Shechem too; or
  • Rehoboam went to Shechem because all Israel had gathered in Shechem to make Rehoboam their king.

Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, Jeroboam hears the news that King Solomon died and that Rehoboam is the new king of Israel. Remember that the storyteller gives background information that Jeroboam was living in exile in Egypt because King Solomon tried to kill Jeroboam while King Solomon was still alive.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story that includes some background information about one of the characters. Pay attention to how you introduce the background information about the character. How will you translate this background information about Jeroboam? It might be better to say first that Jeroboam fled to Egypt, and then later Jeroboam heard the news. Pause this audio here.

It is hard to know when Jeroboam comes to Shechem. In the original language, it might mean that when Jeroboam hears the news, Jeroboam remains in Egypt, or it might mean that when Jeroboam hears the news, Jeroboam returns to Shechem from Egypt. Or it might mean that Jeroboam had been living in Egypt in exile since he came there to escape King Solomon. In a similar passage in 2 Chronicles, the storyteller says that Jeroboam returns from Egypt. Now, the northern tribes send someone to Egypt to tell Jeroboam to come back home to Israel, which Jeroboam does.

Remember that it is not clear if Jeroboam at first remains in Egypt after he hears the news or if Jeroboam immediately returns from Egypt after he hears the news.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate this part of the passage? Remember that in the similar passage in 2 Chronicles, the storyteller says that Jeroboam returns from Egypt. Pause this audio here.

Jeroboam returns from Egypt to Israel. Remember that it may be that some people go to Egypt to get Jeroboam, or that Jeroboam has already come back to Israel. Most translations do not say that Jeroboam comes back to Israel, but we know from the next scene that Jeroboam is in Shechem now. In any case, the Israelites have asked Jeroboam to come with them to talk to King Rehoboam.

In the third scene, Jeroboam and the people of Israel talk to King Rehoboam. Jeroboam and the people remind Rehoboam that Solomon, Rehoboam's father, gave the people a heavy yoke of work. Remember that people put a yoke on two animals to help the animals work together. During the time of Rehoboam, people used a yoke as special language to talk about heavy burdens of work. Remember also that the people do not have a physical yoke on their necks. Solomon gave the people a lot of work, and Solomon made the people pay a lot of taxes to Solomon.

Stop here and look at a picture of a yoke again as a group if needed. Pause this audio here.

Also, stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about hard work in your culture. How will you translate this part of the passage about the heavy yoke of work that King Solomon put on the people of Israel? It is probably best to translate this in the same way that you did in the 2 Chronicles passage. Pause this audio here.

Now, the people want Rehoboam to lessen their harsh labor and heavy yoke of work. Some people think that "harsh labor" and "heavy yoke" are two ways to say the same thing about the hard work that Solomon forced the people to do. Other people think the heavy yoke means the heavy taxes that the king made the people pay, in addition to the harsh labor. The people say that if Rehoboam lessens their work, the people will serve Rehoboam as their king. The people want Rehoboam to be a different kind of king than Solomon was.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate "harsh labor" and "heavy yoke" in this passage? Also, how will you show the contrast between the hard work that Solomon made the people do in the past and the lighter load of work that the people want Rehoboam to give to them now? A few translation examples are:

  • Now, if you lighten the demands he made and do not make us work as hard, we will serve you; or
  • Lighten the harsh labor demands and heavy taxes that your father imposed on us. Then we will be your loyal subjects; or
  • But if you make our work easier, we will serve you and do whatever you ask.

Pause this audio here.

Rehoboam tells Jeroboam and the people to leave for three days. Rehoboam probably wants three days to think about how to answer the request of the people. The people agree and leave for three days. It may be more natural to translate this part of the scene with the exact words that Rehoboam said to the people, or with a description of what Rehoboam said to the people. Some translation examples include: "Rehoboam told the people, 'Go away for three days and then come back,'" or "Rehoboam told the people to come back in three days to hear his answer."

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story that includes some talking between the characters. Do you say the exact words that the characters say to each other, or do you tell a description of what the characters said? What is the best way to translate this part of the passage where Rehoboam tells the people to come back in three days? Pause this audio here.

In the fourth and fifth scenes, Rehoboam asks two different groups for advice about how to answer the request of the people.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story about someone who asked a group of people for advice. Pay attention to how you talk about asking for advice. How will you translate this part of the passage where Rehoboam asks two groups of people for advice? Pause this audio here.

In the fourth scene, Rehoboam talks with the older advisers who were alive during the time of Solomon. In the original language, it says these advisers stood before Solomon, which means they gave Solomon advice about what to do. Other ways to translate "older advisers" could be "elders" or "older men who advised Solomon." Rehoboam asks the older advisers, "How do you advise me to answer these people?" Again, it may be better to translate this as a description of what Rehoboam said: "Rehoboam asks the older advisers how they think Rehoboam should respond to the people."

The older advisers tell Rehoboam that Rehoboam should serve the people and that Rehoboam should speak good words to the people. The older advisers want Rehoboam to be kind to the people and lessen their harsh load of work. The older advisers say that if Rehoboam is kind to the people, then the people will always serve Rehoboam.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Think about a time when you or someone you know gave conditions to someone else in order to get the person to do something. For example, a father might tell his child that if his child helps with the father's work, the father will give the child a gift. What is the best way to translate this part of the passage about the way that the older advisers tell Rehoboam that Rehoboam must act so that the people will always serve Rehoboam? Some translation choices include:

  • If you are a servant to the people today, and if you give the people a good answer, then the people will always serve you; or
  • Serve the people and give the people a good answer today. Then the people will always serve you.

Is it best to include the exact words that the older advisers told Rehoboam to say, or to say, "The older advisers told Rehoboam that he should help the people and give the people a good answer, and then the people will serve Rehoboam." Pause this audio here.

In the fifth scene, Rehoboam rejects, or refuses, the advice of the older advisers. Instead, Rehoboam asks the younger advisers what to do. In the original language, the storyteller calls these younger advisers "young boys" or "children" because these advisers are inexperienced. But these younger advisers are actually grown men like Rehoboam. It may be more natural in your language if you include the information about what the people asked Rehoboam to do before Rehoboam asks the younger advisers for advice.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you show the contrast between the advice of the older advisers and what Rehoboam decides to do? Would it be better in your language to translate the words that Rehoboam asked the younger advisers or a description of what Rehoboam said to the younger advisers? What should Rehoboam say first and last? Here are some translation examples:

  • Rehoboam asked the younger advisers, "What is your advice? What should we tell these people?" The people say, "Lighten the load that your father, King Solomon, gave to us!" or
  • Rehoboam asked the younger advisers to give Rehoboam advice about how to answer the people. Rehoboam told the younger advisers that the people had complained about the work that King Solomon had given the people. The people wanted Rehoboam to give them less work; or
  • Rehoboam said to the younger advisers, "The people asked me to lighten the load of work that my father put on them. What answer do you advise me to give to the people?"

Pause this audio here.

The younger advisers repeat what the people said to Rehoboam. Again, you can include the exact words that the people said to Rehoboam, or a description of the words. It might be good to do the same thing here that you did earlier when Rehoboam asked the younger advisers for advice.

The younger advisers tell Rehoboam a proverb, or wise saying, to show that Rehoboam should act even harsher than Rehoboam's father, Solomon. The younger advisers say that Rehoboam's little finger is thicker than his father Solomon's "loins" or waist. Remember that the proverb means that the easiest work that Rehoboam requires will be worse than the hardest things that Solomon required. Some people think that "little finger" means the male sexual organ.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate this proverb? It might be best to translate the meaning of the proverb, as in: "I will be much harsher than my father was!" Pause this audio here.

The younger advisers give two different examples of how Rehoboam will be harsher than his father, Solomon. The advisers say that Solomon gave a heavy yoke of work to the people. Rehoboam should make the yoke even heavier for the people. The men also say that Solomon punished the people with ordinary whips, but Rehoboam should punish the people with whips that sting like scorpions. Remember, this probably means that Rehoboam will punish the people even more than Solomon did. In each example, the younger advisers emphasize the difference between how Solomon acted in the past and how the younger advisers think Rehoboam should act now.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate this part of the passage to show the contrast between the work that Solomon gave to the people and the work that Rehoboam will give to the people? Pause this audio here.

In the sixth scene, Jeroboam and the people come back to Rehoboam after three days. Remember that this is when Rehoboam told Jeroboam and the people to come back. Rehoboam speaks harshly to the people. Rehoboam rejects the advice of the older advisers and instead, Rehoboam follows the advice of the younger advisers. Rehoboam does not repeat the proverb again, but Rehoboam does repeat the two examples that the younger advisers gave to him about how Rehoboam will make life even harder for the people.

In the final scene, the storyteller gives more information about the real reason for all of the events in this passage. This comment from the storyteller is not a part of the main action of the story. Yahweh caused these things to happen, meaning Yahweh is the one controlling everything, including the unhappiness of the people of Israel and Rehoboam's choice not to listen to the people. Remember that in a previous story, Yahweh spoke through the prophet Ahijah to Jeroboam. Ahijah told Jeroboam that Yahweh would give 10 of the tribes of Israel to Jeroboam.

Now, in this passage, we see that Yahweh will fulfill the promise to Jeroboam because Rehoboam did not listen to the people of Israel. Remember that we will learn in later passages about how the tribes of Israel split into two kingdoms, with Rehoboam leading the southern kingdom and Jeroboam leading the northern kingdom.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story that includes a statement about why the event in the story happened. How will you translate the end of this passage to show how Yahweh fulfilled his "word," or did what he said he would do? Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 12:1–15 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has seven scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Rehoboam
  • All the people of Israel
  • Jeroboam, the son of Nebat
  • Older advisers who served King Solomon in the past
  • Younger advisers who are Rehoboam's age
  • Yahweh
  • And the prophet Ahijah, the Shilonite

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

The people of Israel traveled to Shechem to make Rehoboam their king. So, Rehoboam went to Shechem too.

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing the people of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We are not sure if we can trust Rehoboam as our new king. We need to talk with Rehoboam before we decide to make him our king; or
  • It is our duty to follow Rehoboam as our new king. Rehoboam's father, Solomon, was our king before, and now we must follow his son, Rehoboam; or
  • We hope that Rehoboam will be kinder to us than his father, Solomon, was.

Continue the drama.

In the past, King Solomon tried to kill Jeroboam, so Jeroboam fled to live in Egypt. Jeroboam has been living in exile in Egypt since then. Now, Jeroboam hears the news that Rehoboam is the new king of Israel. The people of Israel ask Jeroboam to help them. At some point, Jeroboam comes back to Israel.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Jeroboam, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am happy to finally come back to my home in Israel; or
  • I was afraid that Solomon would kill me, but now it is safe for me to come home because I am not afraid of this new king, Rehoboam; or
  • I wonder why the people of Israel want me to come back to Israel now. Could this be part of the prophecy that the prophet Ahijah made about me?

Continue the drama.

Jeroboam and the people of Israel talk to King Rehoboam. The people say, "Your father, King Solomon, made us work very hard. But if you make our work easier, we will serve you and do whatever you ask." Rehoboam tells the people to come back in three days to hear Rehoboam's answer to their request. So, the people leave for three days.

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing the people of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We think that our request is reasonable for King Rehoboam to lessen our heavy work. We are not asking Rehoboam to take away our work, but to lessen it; or
  • We hope that Rehoboam listens to our request because we cannot keep working this hard and paying this many taxes as we did for his father, King Solomon; or
  • This is our chance to finally get the king to listen to us! We will be loyal to this new king if he gives us something in return; or
  • We are hopeful that the king will think carefully about our request during these three days.

Also, ask the person playing Rehoboam, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Why are these people coming to talk to me like this? The people should make me king and then let me go back home to Jerusalem. The people should be grateful that I traveled here to Shechem to see them; or
  • I need to be careful how I answer the people because I want their loyalty and support. I know that the people worked very hard for my father, King Solomon, but I also enjoy the life that I have as king in Jerusalem, and I do not want the people to stop working or paying taxes to me; or
  • I am not sure what to do yet, so I will take a few days to think about my answer and ask some advisers for advice about what to do.

Continue the drama.

King Rehoboam talks with the older advisers who served his father, King Solomon, when King Solomon was alive. Rehoboam asks the older advisers, "What answer do you think I should give to the people?" The older advisers tell Rehoboam, "If you are a servant for the people today, and if you give the people a good answer, then the people will always serve you."

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing the older advisers, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We think that Rehoboam should say anything he can to make the people happy, so the people will support Rehoboam as their new king. It does not matter if Rehoboam plans to keep his promises to the people or not. In the future, Rehoboam can be harsher with the people, but today, Rehoboam should speak kindly to the people; or
  • We think it is important to carefully listen to the people and speak kindly to the people. Rehoboam needs the support of the northern tribes of Israel. Rehoboam should not do anything now that would make these people of Israel mad at Rehoboam. Otherwise, the people may not support Rehoboam as their new king; or
  • We are honored that the new king came to ask us for advice first. We have the most experience, and we know how to handle the requests of the different tribes of Israel.

Continue the drama.

But Rehoboam rejects the advice of the older advisers. Instead, Rehoboam asks the younger advisers what to do. These younger advisers also served Rehoboam, and they were the same age as Rehoboam. Rehoboam asks the younger advisers, "What should I answer the people who asked me to give them less work than my father gave to them?"

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Rehoboam, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I do not want the people to think I am weak. If I follow the advice of the older advisers, people will say I am weaker than my father, King Solomon; or
  • I prefer to see what the advisers of my own age think about this situation. I trust the people my own age more than the older advisers that my father had; or
  • The people of Israel seem to complain a lot. I do not think that my father made the people work very hard; or
  • I hope that my younger advisers give me better advice than the older advisers gave me. I think I will follow the advice of the younger advisers, even before I know what their advice is.

Continue the drama.

The younger advisers tell Rehoboam that Rehoboam should reject the request of the people. The people complained that Rehoboam's father, Solomon, made them work very hard. The people wanted Rehoboam to give them less work. The younger advisers say that Rehoboam should be even harsher than Solomon was. The younger advisers say that Rehoboam should tell the people, "My little finger is thicker than my father's waist!" The younger advisers also think Rehoboam should make the people's work even harder. Solomon punished the people with ordinary whips, but Rehoboam should punish the people with whips that sting like scorpions!

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing the younger advisers, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • Rehoboam should listen to our advice because Rehoboam knows us, and we know him. We have the best ideas for how Rehoboam can be a strong king of Israel; or
  • We think it would be a bad idea to follow the advice of the older advisers. Those old men do not know the new way of doing things. Rehoboam will show them what a real king looks like, one who is even tougher than King Solomon was; or
  • These people of Israel need to know that they cannot come and talk to King Rehoboam like this. These people should respect the new king and not ask him for things. Rehoboam will be even stronger and harsher than his father was!

Continue the drama.

Jeroboam and the people come back to Rehoboam on the third day. This is just as King Rehoboam told them to do. Rehoboam talks harshly to the people. Rehoboam rejects the advice of the older advisers and instead, Rehoboam follows the advice of the younger advisers. Rehoboam tells the people, "My father made you work hard, but I will make you work even harder! My father punished you with ordinary whips, but I will punish you with whips that sting like scorpions!"

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Rehoboam, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I like the advice of my younger advisers better than the advice of my older advisers. I am the king, so I can decide what I want to do; or
  • I am ready to be an even harsher and more demanding king than my father was! or
  • These people need to respect me. They should never have asked me to lessen their heavy load of work, because now they will need to work even harder.

Also, ask the people playing the people of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We are very disappointed that Rehoboam did not listen to us; or
  • We are shocked that Rehoboam plans to be even harsher than his father was; or
  • We do not plan to serve Rehoboam as our king now. We gave Rehoboam a chance to be kind to us in return for our loyalty. Rehoboam did not take the chance, and now we know that we are not welcome in the kingdom of Rehoboam. We must start to make other plans with Jeroboam as our leader.

Continue the drama.

Rehoboam refused to listen to the people of Israel. Yahweh caused this to happen in order to fulfill the promise that Ahijah the Shilonite spoke to Jeroboam, the son of Nebat.

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing the people of Israel who are listening to this story generations after it happened, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We wish that Rehoboam had chosen to listen to the people of Israel. Maybe then there would never have been a divided kingdom of Israel; or
  • We trust that Yahweh's ways are always good, even though we do not understand why Yahweh makes things happen; or
  • We remember the promise that Ahijah the Shilonite made to Jeroboam in a previous story, and now we see that Yahweh keeps the promises he makes.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 12:1–15 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Rehoboam became the new king of the Israelites in the previous passage. David was the first king of Israel, and David's son, Solomon, was the second king of Israel. Rehoboam is Solomon's son. We will learn in a later passage that Rehoboam was the son of an Ammonite woman whose name was Naamah. We also know that Rehoboam's father, Solomon, wrote many proverbs to teach his sons about wisdom. In this passage, we learn that Rehoboam does not follow the wisdom of his father, Solomon.

In this passage, "Israel" and "Israelites" refer to the northern tribes of Israel. These northern tribes of Israel come together in an assembly to talk to King Rehoboam at Shechem in the north of Israel. An assembly is when people gather together for a specific purpose. Translate assembly in the same way as you did in previous passages. Shechem later becomes the first capital of the northern kingdom of Israel. Translate Israel and Israelites the same as you have in previous passages. For more information on Israel and Israelites, refer to Israel and Israelites in the Master Glossary.

Jeroboam returns from his exile in Egypt. Egypt is the land where the Israelites lived in slavery for 400 years before Yahweh delivered them during the time of Moses and Aaron. Jeroboam was in Egypt because, in the past, King Solomon tried to kill Jeroboam. A king is someone who rules over a city or territory. Translate Egypt and king the same as you have in previous passages. For more information on king, refer to the Master Glossary.

The people of Israel ask King Rehoboam to lighten their heavy yoke of work that King Solomon gave to them. A yoke is a wooden collar with two openings that people use to tie two oxen or two other animals together. In the Bible, sometimes a yoke means slavery or hard work.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate yoke in this passage? Remember your discussion from step three about how to translate this part of the passage. Will you use the word yoke here, or translate it as heavy work, harsh demands, or something else? Pause this audio here.

King Rehoboam first asks the elders, or the older advisers, for advice on how to answer the people. Elders are people with authority in the community who are usually leaders of their families. These elders gave advice or wisdom to King Solomon while Solomon was alive.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate elders in this passage? You may want to translate elders the same as in previous passages, but these elders may have a more specific job to advise the king. For more information on elders, refer to the Master Glossary. Pause this audio here.

The older advisers tell King Rehoboam that if Rehoboam is a servant to the people today, then the people will always be the king's servants in the future. A servant is someone who does work for their master. In this case, the advisors are telling the king to serve, or please, his people. Translate servant the same as you have in previous passages. For more information on servant, refer to the Master Glossary.

Rehoboam also asks the young men Rehoboam grew up with for advice. Other ways to translate this could be younger advisers, young advisers, or younger officials.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate young men in this passage? Pause this audio here.

Rehoboam says that he will punish the people with whips that sting like scorpions.

Stop here and look at a photo of a whip that has metal at the end of the cord and a photo of a scorpion. What words or phrases will you use for whips and scorpions? Pause this audio here.

The events in this passage were from Yahweh. Yahweh is the personal name for God. Translate Yahweh the same as you have in previous passages. For more information on Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary.

Yahweh caused these events to happen in order to fulfill the word that Yahweh spoke to Jeroboam through Ahijah the Shilonite. The word that Yahweh spoke to Jeroboam was a promise from Yahweh. Translate "word" the same as you have in previous passages. For more information on word, refer to the Master Glossary.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 12:16–24
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 12:16–24 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 12:16–24 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 12:16–24 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous passage, the northern tribes of Israel asked King Rehoboam to lessen their heavy load of work. King Rehoboam refused to listen to the people's request. Instead, King Rehoboam said he would make the people work even harder. Now, we hear how the people react. The people rebel against King Rehoboam and make Jeroboam their new king instead. King Rehoboam plans to fight against the people.

Remember that 2 Chronicles 10:8–19 and 2 Chronicles 11:1–12 are very similar passages to this passage.

Stop here and listen to your translations of 2 Chronicles 10:8–19 and 2 Chronicles 11:1–12,

if you have already translated those passages. Pause this audio here.

As the story begins, the northern tribes of Israel realize that Rehoboam will not listen to their request to lessen their heavy load of work. The people tell King Rehoboam that the people have no place in the kingdom of David now. In the original translation, the people ask King Rehoboam, "What part do we have in David, and what part do we have in the son of Jesse?" The people do not want Rehoboam to answer their question. The people know that David was Rehoboam's grandfather, the son of Jesse, and a descendant of the tribe of Judah. David and David's family have nothing to do with the people in the north of Israel.

The northern tribes of Israel also say that they should all go home to their tents. A tent is a temporary home. Tents are another way to talk about home. The people want the descendants of David to take care of themselves and their own people, but the northern tribes of Israel do not want Rehoboam to rule over them anymore. The northern tribes no longer feel like they are a part of the people of Israel.

Stop here and look at a picture of a tent. Pause this audio here.

The northern Israelites go home. However, King Rehoboam still rules over those Israelites who were originally from the northern tribes but who now live in the cities and towns of Judah in the south of Israel. We know that Rehoboam also rules over all the other people in Judah as well.

Stop here and look as a group at a map that shows the kingdoms of Israel and Judah. Pause this audio here.

Rehoboam sends out Adoniram, the man who was in charge of forcing the people to build buildings for the kings of Israel. Rehoboam probably sends Adoniram because Rehoboam wants the people of Israel to know that Rehoboam will be harsh with the people and increase their heavy yoke of work. But the Israelites do not listen to Adoniram, and the people kill Adoniram by throwing stones at him. The people probably do this to show Rehoboam that the people do not respect Rehoboam as their king and will not do any more building projects for Rehoboam.

Rehoboam quickly gets in his chariot and rides away to Jerusalem, the capital of Israel. This means that Rehoboam was in the northern part of Israel before this, and maybe Rehoboam went with Adoniram. A chariot is a two-wheeled cart that horses pull in battle, but sometimes kings rode on chariots, too.

Stop here and look at a picture of a chariot and a map that includes Jerusalem. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller says that "Israel," meaning the northern tribes of Israel, has been rebelling against the family of David since the time this story happened. The northern tribes of Israel refuse to let a descendant of David be their king. The storyteller told these stories long after the stories happened, so the people of Israel had been rebelling for a long time.

The people of the northern tribes of Israel hear that Jeroboam is back in Israel. Remember that Jeroboam is the person who used to be a servant to Rehoboam's father, King Solomon. Jeroboam has been in exile in Egypt because King Solomon tried to kill Jeroboam. Now, the people ask Jeroboam to come meet with them. At their meeting, the people make Jeroboam the king over all of northern Israel. Only the tribe of Judah remains loyal to Rehoboam, the descendant of David. We know from a previous passage that the prophet Ahijah said that Jeroboam would be the leader of 10 of the tribes of Israel. The smaller tribe of Benjamin later becomes part of the tribe of Judah, so this is likely why the storyteller says here that only the tribe of Judah remains loyal to David's family.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about two rival leaders in your village, city, or country. How did each of the two leaders try to make people like them better than the other leader? Pause this audio here.

Rehoboam arrives in Jerusalem, the capital city of Israel. Remember that Rehoboam ran away on his chariot earlier in this passage. Rehoboam is ready for a war against the rebellious northern tribes of Israel who refuse to recognize Rehoboam as their king. Rehoboam chooses 180,000 fighting men from the tribes of Judah and Benjamin to go with him to the north. King Rehoboam wants to reunite the divided kingdom, with Rehoboam as the king over all of the tribes of Israel. We know that the tribe of Benjamin later becomes part of the tribe of Judah, and that is probably why the storyteller said earlier that only the tribe of Judah is loyal to Rehoboam, even though here we see that the tribe of Benjamin is willing to fight for Rehoboam.

But Shemaiah, the man of God, hears a word from God. God tells Shemaiah to give a message to Rehoboam and to the people of Judah and Benjamin, and to the rest of the people. The rest of the people might mean the people from the northern tribes who live in Judah. The message says that Rehoboam and the people of the southern kingdom of Judah should not fight against their brothers or relatives, the other Israelites. Instead, Yahweh tells the people to go home because Yahweh caused this thing to happen. You will remember that in an earlier story, the prophet Ahijah had said that Yahweh would take away 10 of the tribes of Israel from Rehoboam.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about a time when two people or groups of people wanted to fight each other, but someone else convinced the people or groups of people not to fight each other. How did the person convince the other people not to fight? Pause this audio here.

The people listen to the words of Yahweh that Shemaiah, the man of God, tells them. The people all go back to their homes, just as Yahweh ordered them to do.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 12:16–24 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has six scenes.

In the first scene: The people say that they have no place in the kingdom of David now. The people go home to their tents. Rehoboam still rules over the people from the northern tribes of Israel who now live in the land of the tribe of Judah.

In the second scene: Rehoboam sends Adoniram, the man in charge of forcing the people to build buildings. The people kill Adoniram with stones. Rehoboam escapes and flees in his chariot to Jerusalem.

In the third scene: The storyteller reminds us that at the time he tells this story, the northern tribes of Israel are still rebelling against the family of David.

In the fourth scene: The Israelites have an assembly, and they make Jeroboam their new king. Only the tribe of Judah remains loyal to Rehoboam, the descendant of David.

In the fifth scene: Rehoboam chooses 180,000 fighting men from the tribes of Judah and Benjamin. Rehoboam prepares for war against the northern tribes of Israel.

In the sixth scene: Yahweh sends a message to Shemaiah, the man of God, to tell Rehoboam and the people of Judah and Benjamin that they must not fight against the other Israelites. All of the people go home, and they do not fight against the other Israelites.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The people of the northern tribes of Israel who live in the north of Israel
  • King Rehoboam
  • People from the northern tribes of Israel who live in the cities of Judah
  • Adoniram
  • Jeroboam
  • Tribe of Judah
  • Tribe of Benjamin
  • 180,000 fighting men from the tribes of Judah and Benjamin
  • Shemaiah, the man of God
  • And Yahweh

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

Remember that in the previous passage, King Rehoboam refused to listen to the people's request to lessen their heavy load of work. Remember also that this passage is very similar to the passages in 2 Chronicles 10:8–19 and 2 Chronicles 11:1–12.

Stop here and listen again to your translation of these similar passages if you have already translated those passages. Pause this audio here.

In the first scene, the northern tribes of Israel tell Rehoboam that they have no place in the kingdom of David. The people ask, "What part do we have in David, and what part do we have in the son of Jesse?" Remember that the people do not want Rehoboam to answer this question, but they want to show Rehoboam that they are not a part of David's family, so Rehoboam cannot rule over them.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Would it be best to translate this as a question or as a statement? Another way to translate this part of the passage is, "We have nothing to do with King David, and we have nothing to do with this grandson of Jesse." It is probably best to translate this in the same way that you did in the 2 Chronicles passage. Pause this audio here.

The people of Israel also say they should go back to their tents, which means their homes. Then, the people go home. Remember that the northern tribes of Israel do not accept a descendant of David as their king, but Rehoboam does rule over some of the people from the northern tribes who live in the land of the tribe of Judah in the south of Israel.

Stop here and look again as a group at a map that includes Judah, if needed. How will you talk about the tents that people want to go back to? Remember that this does not necessarily mean that the people lived in temporary cloth shelters. "Tents" was a way to say "their homes." Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, Rehoboam sends Adoniram to the people. Remember that Adoniram was in charge of forcing people to build buildings for Israel. Adoniram supervises the workers who work hard for King Solomon. The people of Israel kill Adoniram by throwing stones at Adoniram. Rehoboam escapes and quickly rides away on his chariot to Jerusalem.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you show that Rehoboam escapes quickly on his chariot? Other ways to translate this part of the passage could be:

  • King Rehoboam quickly climbs into his chariot and rides away to Jerusalem.
  • King Rehoboam jumps into his chariot. Then, King Rehoboam quickly drives to safety in Jerusalem.

Pause this audio here.

In the third scene, the storyteller says that the northern tribes of Israel have been in rebellion against the house, or family, of David from that time until now. This information from the storyteller is not in the main action of the story. We know that events in this passage are very important for the rest of the story of the divided kingdom of Israel because this passage is when the kingdom of Israel splits into two kingdoms.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story that includes some background information from the storyteller that is not in the main action of the story. How will you include this background information in this passage? Pause this audio here.

In the fourth scene, the northern tribes of Israel hear that Jeroboam is back in Israel. Remember that Jeroboam had been in Egypt in exile. It may be helpful to include in your translation that Jeroboam returns to Israel from Egypt. The northern tribes of Israel send someone to bring Jeroboam to an "assembly" or meeting. The people make Jeroboam their new king over all of northern Israel. Some people think this part is hard to understand because it seems like Jeroboam is meeting the people for the first time. But in the last story, the storyteller said that Jeroboam and the people asked Rehoboam to make their hard work easier. Maybe Jeroboam only talked to some of the people before, and now Jeroboam is meeting with all of the people.

Remember that "Israel" means the northern tribes of Israel. Remember that this meeting happens in the north of Israel, where the northern tribes live, and the land of the tribe of Judah is in the south of Israel. The tribe of Judah is the only tribe that remains loyal to the family of David. Remember that Rehoboam is a descendant of King David.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a map of Israel and the land of the tribe of Judah. Pause this audio here.

In the fifth scene, Rehoboam arrives in Jerusalem. Rehoboam gathers together all the men of the tribes of Judah and Benjamin. In the original language, "gathers together" means that Rehoboam calls together the men to fight. Rehoboam chooses 180,000 fighting men to make war against the northern tribes.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate "fighting men" in this passage? Pause this audio here.

Rehoboam wants to fight against the northern tribes of Israel to take back the northern land for his own kingdom. Remember that the kingdom of Israel was united under King Solomon, Rehoboam's father, but now there are two separate kingdoms. King Rehoboam is the king of Judah in the south, and King Jeroboam is the king of Israel in the north. Remember that now "Israel" means the northern tribes of Israel, and not the united kingdom of Israel.

The sixth scene begins with a strong contrast between King Rehoboam's plans to reunite the kingdom of Israel and Yahweh's plans to divide the kingdom of Israel. Yahweh sends a message to a prophet to tell King Rehoboam not to attack the northern kingdom.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you show the contrast between Rehoboam's plans and the message that Yahweh sent to the prophet, Shemaiah? Pause this audio here.

In the sixth scene, Shemaiah, the man of God, hears a word from God. In other words, God speaks to Shemaiah, the man of God. First, the storyteller says who Shemaiah should tell the message to. Yahweh tells Shemaiah to deliver this message to Rehoboam, to the people of Judah and Benjamin, and to all the other people. Then, the storyteller says the message that Shemaiah should say. Shemaiah says, "This is what Yahweh says to do. Do not go fight against your brothers, the Israelites. Go home, all of you, because I caused all of this to happen." You can translate brothers as "relatives" or "your own people."

In the final part of this passage, the people of the northern tribes of Israel listen to the message from Yahweh that Shemaiah tells them. The people all obey Yahweh and return to their homes.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 12:16–24 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has six scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The people of the northern tribes of Israel who live in the north of Israel
  • King Rehoboam
  • People from the northern tribes of Israel who live in the cities of Judah
  • Adoniram
  • Jeroboam
  • Tribe of Judah
  • Tribe of Benjamin
  • 180,000 fighting men from the tribes of Judah and Benjamin
  • Shemaiah, the man of God
  • And Yahweh

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

The people from the northern tribes of Israel see that King Rehoboam will not listen to them and will not make their work easier. The people tell the king, "We have nothing to do with King David, and we have nothing to do with this grandson of Jesse. We should all go home to our tents. David's descendant, Rehoboam, can take care of David's family." So the northern tribes of Israel go to their homes. But Rehoboam still rules over the Israelites from the northern tribes who now live in the land of the tribe of Judah.

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing the northern tribes of Israel who live in the north of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We are frustrated that Rehoboam refused to listen to us. We were not asking for too much. Rehoboam should have listened to us! or
  • Now, Rehoboam will see that he is not our king, and we will not follow him. Rehoboam can take care of his own people, and we are not a part of those people; or
  • Rehoboam will regret his choice not to make our work easier. Rehoboam will see that we have a plan to get a new king.

Also, ask the people playing the northern tribes of Israel who live in the cities of Judah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We do not like Rehoboam, but we live here in the cities of Judah, so he is our king; or
  • We have homes and families here in Judah, so it would be difficult to move back to the north of Israel, where we are from; or
  • We wish that we lived in the north of Israel, so we did not have to follow Rehoboam as our king. Rehoboam did not listen to our people in the north, and we are angry.

Also, ask the person playing Rehoboam, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • These northern tribes are a problem. These people need to learn to listen to me; or
  • I am happy that at least everyone left and went back to their homes. Maybe they are ready to do the hard work for me; or
  • I am worried that the northern tribes think I am not their king. Maybe I should have listened to the advice of the older advisers and agreed to make the people's work easier?

Continue the drama.

King Rehoboam sends Adoniram to the people. Adoniram is the manager of the laborers. The people kill Adoniram by throwing stones at Adoniram. King Rehoboam quickly climbs into his chariot and flees to Jerusalem. Israel has been rebelling against the family of David ever since this story happened.

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing the northern tribes of Israel who live in the north of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We killed Adoniram to show Rehoboam that we refuse to do any more work for Rehoboam! or
  • We are ready to go to war against Rehoboam because we refuse to follow Rehoboam as our king; or
  • We are happy that Rehoboam is afraid of us now. Rehoboam had a chance to listen to our requests, but Rehoboam did not listen to us.

Also, ask the person playing Rehoboam, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am afraid that the people will kill me in the same way that they just killed Adoniram. I hope that the people do not catch me on my way back to Jerusalem; or
  • I am shocked that the people killed Adoniram! The people should be afraid of me and be willing to build buildings for me, their king; or
  • I need to gather some warriors to fight these terrible people in the north! These people will be sorry for killing Adoniram and for not respecting me as their king!

Continue the drama.

The northern tribes of Israel hear that Jeroboam is back in Israel. The northern tribes of Israel invite Jeroboam to a meeting of the people of Israel. The people of Israel make Jeroboam the king of Israel. The only tribe that is loyal to the family of David is the tribe of Judah.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Jeroboam, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am happy to be the new king for the people of Israel; or
  • This is happening just like the prophet Ahijah told me it would happen; or
  • My kingdom has many more people than Rehoboam's kingdom. Rehoboam should have listened to our request to make our work easier.

Also, ask the person playing Rehoboam, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I cannot believe that the people want to have Jeroboam as their king and not me; or
  • I am glad that at least the tribe of Judah is still loyal to me as their king; or
  • Now I am angry, and I will punish the northern tribes of Israel for their betrayal.

Continue the drama.

Rehoboam arrives in Jerusalem. Rehoboam gathers together all the men of Judah and Benjamin. Rehoboam chooses 180,000 fighting men to go to war with him against the northern tribes of Israel. Rehoboam, the son of Solomon, wants to restore the kingdom to himself so that Rehoboam will rule over all the tribes of Israel again.

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing the 180,000 fighting men, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We are ready to fight for the true king of Israel, King Rehoboam; or
  • We are angry at the northern tribes of Israel because they are rebelling against King Rehoboam; or
  • We are glad that we are loyal to King Rehoboam, the son of King Solomon, who was the son of King David. We hope that King Rehoboam wins the fight against the northern tribes of Israel.

Continue the drama.

Shemaiah, a man of God, hears a message from God. God says to Shemaiah, "Tell this to Rehoboam, the son of Solomon, the king of Judah. Say this also to all the people of Judah and Benjamin, and to all the other men there. This is what Yahweh says to do: 'Do not go fight against your brothers, the Israelites. Go home, all of you, because I caused all of this to happen.'" The people obey the words of Yahweh. The people return to their homes as Yahweh told them to do.

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing Rehoboam and the men of the tribes of Judah and Benjamin, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We are surprised that Yahweh told us to go home and not to fight to restore the kingdom of Israel; or
  • We are confused about why Yahweh wants us not to fight against Jeroboam, but we are ready to obey Yahweh's commands; or
  • We do not understand why Yahweh said that Yahweh caused these things to happen.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 12:16–24 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

In this passage, Israel and Israelites refer to the northern tribes of Israel. Translate Israel and Israelites the same as you have in previous passages. For more information on Israel and Israelites, refer to Israel and Israelites in the Master Glossary.

The people of the northern tribes of Israel realize that King Rehoboam will not listen to them. Translate king the same as you have in previous passages. For more information on king, refer to the Master Glossary.

The people of Israel say that they have nothing to do with David, the son of Jesse. The people also say that David should take care of his own house. David was the first king of Israel. The house of David means the descendants of David and the dynasty of kings that descend from David. Translate "house of David" the same as you have in previous passages. For more information on "house of David," refer to the Master Glossary.

Rehoboam rules over the people from the northern tribes of Israel who now live in the cities of Judah. A city is a settlement of people with a wall around it. Judah is one of the tribes of Israel, and people often called the two tribes in the south that still followed Rehoboam as their king "Judah." Translate city and Judah the same as you have in previous passages. For more information on Judah, refer to the Master Glossary.

Rehoboam escaped in his chariot to Jerusalem. A chariot is a two-wheeled cart that horses pull. Jerusalem is the capital city of Israel, and now the capital city of the land of Judah, where Rehoboam is the king. Translate chariot and Jerusalem the same as you have in previous passages.

The people of northern Israel called Jeroboam to an assembly, or a special gathering of people that made decisions. Translate "assembly" the same as you have in previous passages.

Rehoboam calls together the whole house, or family, of the tribes of Judah and Benjamin. Benjamin was another tribe of Israel. Translate Benjamin the same as you have in previous passages. A "house" in this case means the descendants or family line of a person. The "house of Judah" is the descendants and tribe of Judah. Translate house in the same way you have before, when it means the descendants of someone. Refer to household in the Master Glossary for more information.

Rehoboam gathers together 180,000 fighting men to fight against the northern tribes of Israel. Rehoboam wants to reunite the divided kingdom. A "kingdom" is the territory or the people that a king rules. Translate kingdom the same as you have in previous passages. For more information on kingdom, refer to the Master Glossary.

The man of God, Shemaiah, hears a word of God to give to Rehoboam and the people. In the Old Testament, sometimes God spoke through his prophets, or people who received messages from God. In this passage, the storyteller calls the person who receives a message from God a "man of God."

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate "word of God" and "man of God" in this passage? Check how you translated this in the 2 Chronicles 11:1–12 passage. Remember that in the 2 Chronicles passage, the storyteller says "word of Yahweh" instead of "word of God," but they mean the same thing. For more information on "word of God," refer to "word" in the Master Glossary. For more information on "man of God," refer to "prophet" in the Master Glossary. Pause this audio here.

Shemaiah says to Rehoboam and the people that Yahweh does not want the people to attack their brothers, the other Israelites. Yahweh is the personal name of God. Translate Yahweh the same as you have in previous passages. For more information on Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 12:25–33
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 12:25–33 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 12:25–33 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 12:25–33 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous story, the people of the northern tribes of Israel chose Jeroboam as their new king of the northern kingdom of Israel. Now, Jeroboam strengthens Israel's military power when he builds up some cities in Israel to defend Israel from any enemies. Jeroboam also creates a new way to worship in Israel when he makes two places for people to worship in the kingdom of Israel: one place at Bethel in the south and one place at Dan in the north.

In the first part of the story, Jeroboam builds up the cities of Shechem and Peniel to protect his new kingdom from enemy attacks. Jeroboam makes the city of Shechem more secure, probably by building strong walls around the city. Remember that the northern tribes of Israel first made Jeroboam their king at Shechem. Jeroboam lives and rules from Shechem, his new capital city. Shechem is in the hill country of Ephraim, one of the 12 tribes of Israel. Hill country is a place with a lot of hills or raised areas of land.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a map that includes the city of Shechem and the land of the tribe of Ephraim. Also, look at a picture of hill country as a group. Pause this audio here.

Jeroboam also builds up the city of Peniel, a city on the east side of the Jordan River. It is possible that Jeroboam builds up Peniel in order to protect the area on the east side of the Jordan River from the enemies in that area.

Stop here and look at a map that includes the city of Peniel as a group. Pause this audio here.

In the second part of the story, Jeroboam decides that he should make an easier, more convenient way for the people to worship Yahweh. In the law of Moses, Yahweh said the people must travel to Jerusalem three times a year to offer sacrifices at the temple, or "house," of Yahweh. Jeroboam worries that if the people keep going to Jerusalem to worship Yahweh, the people will become loyal again to their former lord, or master, King Rehoboam of Judah. Remember that King Rehoboam is from the house, or family, of David. Jeroboam also worries that the people will kill Jeroboam. Jeroboam wants to protect his new kingdom.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about a time when you had a new leader in your village, town, city, community, or country. What new plans or practices did your new leader start? Why do you think your new leader started these new practices? Pause this audio here.

Jeroboam asks for advice, probably from wise men who helped Jeroboam make important decisions. Then, Jeroboam makes two golden calves. A calf is a young, male bull or ox. A bull represented strength and fertility. Many people in the region made images of bulls to represent their gods.

Stop here and look at a picture of a golden calf as a group. Pause this audio here.

Also, stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story about a time when you saw someone worshipping an idol. What did the people who worshipped the idol do? What did the idol look like? How did people make the idol? Pause this audio here.

People have different opinions about the purpose of the golden calves. Some people think that Jeroboam wants the people of Israel to worship the golden calves as their new gods instead of worshipping Yahweh. People who worshipped other gods had idols to help them visualize and worship those gods, and it was unusual that the Israelites had no idols to worship. Other people believe that Jeroboam still wants the people of Israel to worship Yahweh, but closer to their homes, so they will remain loyal to Jeroboam. These people think that the two golden calves in Bethel and Dan might be like a footstool for Yahweh to stand on. Maybe Jeroboam wants Yahweh to leave the resting place of the ark of the covenant in the temple of Yahweh in Jerusalem to come to Bethel and Dan for a new resting place.

Jeroboam tells the people of the northern tribes of Israel that it is too difficult for them to travel to Jerusalem to worship. Jeroboam gives the people the two golden calves and says that these are the gods who brought the Israelites out of Egypt. You will remember that the Israelites were slaves in Egypt for 400 years. After Yahweh brought the Israelites out of Egypt, Aaron made a golden calf for the Israelites. Jeroboam even says the same thing that Aaron told the people: "These are the gods that brought you out of Egypt!"

Stop here and look at a map of Egypt and Israel as a group. Pause this audio here.

Jeroboam puts one golden calf in Bethel, and Jeroboam puts the other golden calf in Dan. Bethel and Dan are both cities in the northern kingdom of Israel. Jeroboam wants the people of the northern kingdom of Israel to go to Bethel or Dan to worship instead of traveling all the way to Jerusalem to worship Yahweh in his temple, or house. Jeroboam does not trust Yahweh to keep Jeroboam in power as the king. You will remember that Yahweh promised Jeroboam a lasting kingdom if Jeroboam followed Yahweh.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a map of Bethel, Dan, and Jerusalem. Pause this audio here.

You will remember that the people of Israel sinned against Yahweh when they worshiped the golden calf that Aaron made in the past. Now, in this passage, the people also sin against Yahweh when they worship the golden calves that Jeroboam makes. We do not know exactly why Jeroboam makes the golden calves, but we do know that the golden calves cause the Israelites to sin. Sin means that the people disobey Yahweh because Yahweh said the people of Israel should not worship any idols.

Jeroboam also builds houses of worship on high places, which probably means Jeroboam builds walls around the altars on the high places. High places are often on hills. Yahweh's presence was not in these houses on high places because Yahweh's presence was only in the temple in Jerusalem. Jeroboam also chooses priests to serve in these houses of worship. Jeroboam chooses priests from all sorts of people, and not from the Levites. Levites are from the tribe of Levi, and in the law of Moses, Yahweh said that only Levites could be priests for Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Where do people in your community go to worship your god or gods? Describe the place or the building where you go to worship. Pause this audio here.

Also, stop here and discuss this question as a group: Describe the leaders in your place of worship. How do you choose these leaders? Think about what would happen if a different person started leading your religious meetings in your community, and not the person or people that your community normally chooses. Pause this audio here.

Jeroboam also changes the day for the "festival" or feast. In the law of Moses, Yahweh said that the Israelites should celebrate the Festival of Tabernacles on the fifteenth day of the seventh month, but Jeroboam changes the festival to the fifteenth day of the eighth month. This festival is one of three festivals the Israelites celebrate each year. During the Festival of Tabernacles, the Israelites live in temporary shelters in Jerusalem to remember the time when the Israelites wandered in the desert after they left Egypt. The Festival of Tabernacles is during the time of the harvest in Israel. Some people think that the harvest happens a month later in the northern part of Israel than in the southern part of Israel, and these people think that is the reason why Jeroboam changes the day for the festival to the eighth month.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Discuss some important religious days in your community. What do you do to celebrate each important religious day in your community? Why are those the days for your religious celebrations? Pause this audio here.

Finally, to start his new festival on the fifteenth day of the eighth month, Jeroboam himself offers sacrifices on the altar that Jeroboam had made in Bethel. An altar is a platform where a person makes a sacrifice. Remember that according to the law of Moses, only priests can offer sacrifices.

Stop here and look at a picture of an altar as a group.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 12:25–33 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has three scenes.

In the first scene: Jeroboam builds up Shechem and Peniel.

In the second scene: Jeroboam makes two golden calves for the people to worship because Jeroboam is worried the people will return to follow King Rehoboam. Jeroboam puts one golden calf in Bethel, and Jeroboam puts the other golden calf in Dan.

In the third scene: Jeroboam makes new places to worship, and Jeroboam chooses new priests who are not Levites. Jeroboam also chooses a new day of worship, and Jeroboam makes the first sacrifices himself on this new day of worship.

The characters in this passage are:

  • King Jeroboam
  • People of the northern tribes of Israel
  • King Rehoboam of Judah
  • King Jeroboam's advisers
  • And the new priests whom King Jeroboam chooses

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the previous story, the storyteller talked a lot about Rehoboam and the southern kingdom of Judah.
