Rehoboam prepared to attack the northern kingdom of Israel, but a man of God, Shemaiah, told Rehoboam and all the people of Judah and Benjamin to go home. Remember that the people all listened to the man of God, and they all went home.

Now, the storyteller talks about the northern kingdom of Israel. Remember that the people made Jeroboam their new king in Shechem. In this passage, Jeroboam strengthens two of his cities to defend his new kingdom of Israel. Jeroboam also makes new religious rules for the people of the northern kingdom of Israel.

As the first scene begins, Jeroboam builds up the city of Shechem. Shechem is 65 kilometers north of Jerusalem. Shechem is in the hill country of the tribe of Ephraim. Remember that "hill country" is an area of land with a lot of hills. People had already built Shechem, but Rehoboam makes Shechem stronger to protect against enemy attacks. Jeroboam does this by building walls around the city. Jeroboam does not build up the city himself because Jeroboam is the king. Jeroboam tells other people to build up the city.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a map that includes the city of Shechem and the land of the tribe of Ephraim, if needed. Also, look at a picture of hill country as a group if needed. Pause this audio here.

Also, stop here and discuss this question as a group: Think about ways that you protect your cities and villages from an enemy attack. In this case, Jeroboam wants to build strong walls around the city of Shechem. How will you describe the way that the people build up or protect their cities from an enemy? Remember that the storyteller in 2 Chronicles says that Rehoboam builds up some cities in the southern kingdom of Judah. Translate "build up" in the same way in this passage as you did in 2 Chronicles 11:1–12. Pause this audio here.

Jeroboam also lives in Shechem, which means that Jeroboam rules from the city of Shechem. Jeroboam probably chooses Shechem as his new capital city of the northern kingdom of Israel because Shechem is between two mountains, Mount Ebal and Mount Gerizim. Jeroboam probably thinks that Jeroboam will be able to more easily defend Shechem from enemies because Shechem is in the mountains.

Jeroboam also builds up, or strengthens, the city of Peniel. Peniel is a city 8 kilometers east of the Jordan River. Another way to say Peniel is "Penuel." Jeroboam might travel to visit Peniel, or it is more likely that Jeroboam moves from Shechem to live in Peniel. Shechem remains the capital city of Israel. Peniel is in a good location to defend Jeroboam's new kingdom against Jeroboam's enemies. People often traveled from the east to the west, or west to east, along the road near Peniel.

In the second and third scenes, the storyteller talks about the new religious practices that Jeroboam starts in the northern kingdom of Israel. Jeroboam chooses new places to worship. Jeroboam also chooses different priests and a new day to worship. You will remember from the law of Moses that Yahweh had very specific rules for where to worship Yahweh, who could serve as a priest, and on which days the people of Israel should have special festivals to worship Yahweh. Jeroboam changes all of these things in Jeroboam's new kingdom.

In the second scene, Jeroboam changes where the people of the northern kingdom of Israel should go to worship. Jeroboam thinks to himself that the people might become loyal to King Rehoboam again if the people keep going up to Jerusalem to worship Yahweh. Jerusalem was on higher ground than the other areas in Israel, so people often said they traveled "up" to Jerusalem. In the original language, the storyteller says that Jeroboam "says this in his heart." This probably means that Jeroboam thinks these things, but Jeroboam does not say these things out loud. Jeroboam is worried the people of his new kingdom will stop being loyal to him. In the original language, the storyteller says that Jeroboam thinks the heart of the people will return to King Rehoboam. "Heart of the people" means the loyalty of the people, or who the people follow as their king. Jeroboam is also worried that the people will kill him.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a map of Jerusalem and the northern kingdom of Israel. Pause this audio here.

Also, stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story where one of the characters in the story has a problem, and this character says something to themselves or thinks something about the problem that they have. How will you translate this part of the passage that Jeroboam thinks something to himself? Pause this audio here.

King Jeroboam asks for advice about what to do. King Jeroboam makes two male calves out of gold. Jeroboam probably does not make the golden calves himself. Maybe Jeroboam had workers pour liquid metal into molds to make the calves, or maybe Jeroboam had workers make wooden statues and then cover those statues with a thin layer of gold. Remember that Jeroboam tells the people that it is too much for them to travel up to Jerusalem. Jeroboam tells the people, "These are your gods, people of Israel, that brought you up from Egypt!" Jeroboam probably says "up from Egypt" because the land of Egypt was on lower ground than the highlands of Israel. Remember that Aaron told the people of Israel the same thing when Aaron made the golden calf after Yahweh brought the Israelites out of Egypt.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a map of Egypt and Israel again if needed. Pause this audio here.

Jeroboam puts one golden calf in Bethel at the southern end of his kingdom, and Jeroboam puts the other golden calf in Dan at the northern end of his kingdom. Bethel is 16 kilometers north of Jerusalem, and Bethel is where Jacob saw Yahweh many years before this. Bethel is in the land of the tribe of Benjamin. It is possible that Jeroboam hopes Yahweh will show himself to the people of the northern kingdom of Israel in the same way that Yahweh revealed himself to Jacob at Bethel. Dan is on the northern boundary of the northern kingdom of Israel. In the past, the people of the tribe of Dan made an altar in Dan. Dan is about 40 kilometers north of the Sea of Galilee.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a map of Bethel, Dan, and Jerusalem again if needed. Pause this audio here.

In the final part of the second scene, the storyteller says what happened because of what Jeroboam did. The storyteller says that Jeroboam caused the people of the northern kingdom of Israel to sin because of the two golden calves in Bethel and Dan. Some people went to Bethel to worship the golden calf in Bethel, and some people went to Dan to worship the golden calf in Dan.

In the third scene, Jeroboam builds new shrines, or houses of worship, on high places. This probably means that Jeroboam builds walls around the altars already on the high places in the kingdom of northern Israel. These are not houses where people live, but places for people to come worship. High places are often on hills, but not all high places are on hills. High places are places where people built altars or shrines to worship their gods. Remember that Yahweh said only to worship Yahweh in the temple in Jerusalem, and not in any other place. It is likely that Jeroboam also put a house or shrine in Bethel and Dan. Jeroboam probably put a golden calf and an altar inside each house of worship in Bethel and in Dan.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Remember your discussion from the previous step about your place of worship. How will you translate "high places" in this passage? Pause this audio here.

Jeroboam also chooses new priests from among all the people. "From among all the people" means that Jeroboam chose priests from all different tribes of Israel, and not just from among the Levites. Remember that Yahweh said that only Levites can be priests for Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate this part of the passage to show that Jeroboam chooses priests from all different kinds of people, and not just from the Levites? Pause this audio here.

We know from a similar passage in 2 Chronicles 11:13–17 that Jeroboam says the Levites cannot serve as priests in the northern kingdom, and so the priests and other Levites leave their homes and lands to go to Judah and Jerusalem. Some of these Levites lived in the northern kingdom of Israel in the past, but now they move to the southern kingdom of Judah so they can continue serving Yahweh.

Stop here and, as a group, listen to your translation of 2 Chronicles 11:13–17, if you have already translated this passage. Pause this audio here.

It is possible that Jeroboam does not trust the Levites anymore because they serve Yahweh in Jerusalem, and Jeroboam wants new priests who will be loyal to Jeroboam. It is also possible that the Levites do not want to help the Israelites worship the golden calves and other high places that Jeroboam sets up, so Jeroboam needs to find other priests who are willing to follow Jeroboam's new religious changes.

Jeroboam makes a new day for the religious festival of shelters. Remember that Jeroboam changes the day of the festival from the fifteenth day of the seventh month to the fifteenth day of the eighth month. The festival was a time to celebrate that Yahweh provided for the Israelites in the desert after Yahweh brought the Israelites out of Egypt.

Jeroboam also sacrifices animals on the altar in Bethel on the fifteenth day of the eighth month. Remember that Yahweh said only priests can make sacrifices. The storyteller includes some information that shows the storyteller does not approve of Jeroboam's choice. The storyteller says that Jeroboam chooses the month himself, and not because Yahweh told Jeroboam to choose this day for a new festival.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you show that Jeroboam chooses this month by himself? Pause this audio here.

The storyteller says three times that Jeroboam "goes up" to the altar. Sometimes people built an altar on a platform, so people had to go up on the platform to go to the altar. The storyteller probably repeats this because the storyteller wants to remind people how awful it is that Jeroboam himself offered sacrifices. All of the Israelites knew that only priests could offer sacrifices, so this is offensive and against Yahweh's law.

In later passages, we will see that most of the later kings of the northern kingdom of Israel follow the example of Jeroboam, the first king of the northern kingdom of Israel. Most of the later kings turn the people of Israel away from Yahweh in the same way that Jeroboam does in this passage.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 12:25–33 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has three scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • King Jeroboam
  • People of the northern tribes of Israel
  • King Rehoboam of Judah
  • King Jeroboam's advisers
  • And the new priests whom King Jeroboam chooses

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Jeroboam builds up the city of Shechem to make Shechem strong against enemy attacks. Shechem is in the hill country of the land of the tribe of Ephraim. Jeroboam lives in Shechem. Later, Jeroboam moves to the city of Peniel. Jeroboam also builds up the city of Peniel and makes Peniel strong against enemy attacks.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Jeroboam, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am worried that people from other nations around me will attack me and take my new kingdom from me. Then I could lose everything! or
  • I hope that I can protect my kingdom from enemy attacks. I might need to build up more cities in my kingdom to protect my kingdom; or
  • These new walls around Shechem and Peniel will protect me and my kingdom.

Continue the drama.

Jeroboam thinks the people of his kingdom might start to follow the family of David again. If the people go to Jerusalem to make sacrifices at the temple of Yahweh, then the people might decide to become loyal to King Rehoboam of Judah again. King Rehoboam was the master of the people in the past, and Jeroboam worries that the people of the northern kingdom will serve King Rehoboam again. Jeroboam also worries that the people of Jeroboam's northern kingdom will kill Jeroboam.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Jeroboam, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I do not trust Yahweh to help me keep my kingdom. I know that Yahweh promised me a kingdom that will not end, but I do not know if Yahweh will keep his promise; or
  • I need to do anything I can to keep the loyalty of the people of the northern kingdom of Israel. I need to think creatively and make a plan so that I preserve my kingdom; or
  • I am nervous that the people will kill me just like they killed Adoniram, King Rehoboam's representative. I need to please people so that they will not want to kill me.

Also, ask the people playing the people of the northern tribes of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We like King Jeroboam more than King Rehoboam because King Jeroboam will help us more than King Rehoboam. We do not want to build any more buildings for King Rehoboam; or
  • We like the idea that we do not need to travel all the way to Jerusalem to make sacrifices to Yahweh. King Jeroboam seems like a wise king; or
  • We do not think that King Jeroboam is right about what would happen if we went to Jerusalem to make sacrifices to Yahweh. We do not like King Rehoboam, and we will not change our minds about King Rehoboam; or
  • I am nervous about what will happen if we do not go to Jerusalem to worship Yahweh.

Continue the drama.

King Jeroboam asks for advice on what to do. Then, King Jeroboam makes two golden calves. King Jeroboam tells the people of the northern tribes of Israel, "It is too far for you to travel to Jerusalem. People of Israel, these two golden calves are your gods who brought you out of Egypt." Jeroboam puts one golden calf in Bethel, and Jeroboam puts the other golden calf in Dan. But the people of the northern tribes of Israel sin, and they worship the golden calves. The people even go to Dan to worship the golden calf in Dan.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Jeroboam, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I do not think there is a problem with these two golden calves. All of the other nations around Israel have idols, and Israel should have some idols to worship, too; or
  • I want the people to continue to worship Yahweh, but I do not want the people to travel to Jerusalem to worship Yahweh. These two golden calves will help me keep the loyalty of the people of the northern tribes of Israel because now the people can worship Yahweh in Bethel and in Dan; or
  • Maybe Yahweh will see these two golden calves, and Yahweh will come to stand on these two golden calves. Yahweh showed himself to Jacob in Bethel, so maybe Yahweh will come to live in Bethel and Dan now; or
  • Aaron made a golden calf for the people after the people came out of Egypt, and I am doing the same thing now.

Also, ask the people playing the people of the northern tribes of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We are shocked that King Jeroboam made these two golden calves for us to worship! We know that the law of Moses says we should only worship Yahweh, and we should not worship any idols; or
  • These idols seem so beautiful, and it is much easier to go to Bethel or Dan than to travel to Jerusalem to worship Yahweh. It is easier to worship these idols; or
  • We trust King Jeroboam. King Jeroboam told us to worship these idols, and King Jeroboam takes good care of us. We will worship these idols because we do not want to go to Jerusalem to see King Rehoboam. King Rehoboam does not care about us.

Continue the drama.

Jeroboam also builds houses of worship on high places in the northern kingdom of Israel. Jeroboam chooses priests from among all the ordinary people, and Jeroboam does not choose priests from the tribe of Levi. Jeroboam makes a festival on the fifteenth day of the eighth month. This festival is similar to the festival in Judah. Jeroboam offers sacrifices on the altar in Bethel to the golden calf. Jeroboam also chooses priests at Bethel for the high places of worship. Jeroboam himself starts the new festival on the fifteenth day of the eighth month when Jeroboam offers sacrifices on the altar that Jeroboam built at Bethel. Jeroboam chooses this day himself.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Jeroboam, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I want my new kingdom to have new ways of doing things. We have a new day for our own festival, new priests that I chose myself, and new places to worship; or
  • I think that anyone should be able to be a priest now. I know that in the law of Moses, Yahweh says that a priest must be a Levite, but it does not matter if a person is a Levite or not; or
  • I am the king, so I can make the sacrifices myself if I want to. All of the Levites who would tell me that I am not allowed to do this are already gone, so I can do whatever I want to do in my own kingdom.

Also, ask the people playing the people of the northern tribes of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We are happy that King Jeroboam has made some changes to the difficult religion that Yahweh gave to us. It is easier to worship here in Bethel and Dan and in the houses of worship on high places. Now we are more like the other nations around Israel; or
  • Our new festival is similar to the festival of tabernacles in Judah. We have our festival a month later because our harvest happens a month later than the harvest in Judah; or
  • We do not mind that King Jeroboam chose new priests for us. These priests are less strict about how we follow the law of Yahweh, and we prefer to worship however and wherever we want.

Also, ask the people playing the new priests that King Jeroboam chooses, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We are glad that King Jeroboam chose us as new priests, even though we are not Levites. It is an honor to be a priest; or
  • We will do anything that King Jeroboam wants us to do, even if it means letting the people worship idols; or
  • We like the two golden calves that King Jeroboam made for us. King Jeroboam had a good idea to keep the people of the northern tribes here in the northern kingdom of Israel. Now King Jeroboam will not lose the loyalty of the people.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 12:25–33 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

King Jeroboam builds up the city of Shechem. Shechem is in the hill country of the land of the tribe of Ephraim. Ephraim and Manasseh were both sons of Joseph, and Ephraim is one of the half-tribes of the tribe of Joseph. The land of the tribe of Ephraim is in the northern kingdom of Israel. Hill country is land that has many hills or raised areas of land. Translate Ephraim, city, and hill country the same as you have in previous passages.

King Jeroboam thinks to himself that Jeroboam's northern kingdom might go back to the house, or family, of David. David was the second king of Israel, and King Rehoboam is David's grandson. Jeroboam thinks the people might return to serve their former lord, or master, King Rehoboam of Judah. A lord is someone who has authority over other people, and a king is someone who rules over a city or a territory. A kingdom is the people or the territory that a king rules. In this passage, "kingdom" means the people of the northern kingdom. Judah is the name of the southern kingdom of the tribes of Judah and Benjamin. Translate kingdom, house of David, lord, king, and Judah the same as you have in previous passages. For more information on kingdom, "house of David," lord, king, and Judah, refer to the Master Glossary.

King Jeroboam is worried that if the people of the northern kingdom of Israel continue to go up to Jerusalem to offer sacrifices in the temple of Yahweh, the people will not be loyal to King Jeroboam anymore. Jerusalem was the capital city of the unified nation of Israel, and now, in this passage, Jerusalem is the capital city of the southern kingdom of Judah. King Rehoboam lives and rules in Jerusalem. Yahweh told the priests of Israel to offer animal sacrifices in the temple, or house, of Yahweh. The sacrifices are gifts to Yahweh. Yahweh is God's personal name. Translate Jerusalem, sacrifices, temple, and Yahweh the same as you have in previous passages. For more information about sacrifices, temple, and Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary.

King Jeroboam makes two golden calves. King Jeroboam tells the people of Israel that these golden calves are the gods of the people of Israel. King Jeroboam also tells the people that these gods brought the people out of Egypt. People often made statues or idols to represent their gods. Translate gods, Israel, and Egypt the same as you have in previous passages. For more information on gods and Israel, refer to the Master Glossary.

The people sin when the people worship the two golden calves that King Jeroboam puts in Bethel and in Dan. To "sin" means to disobey Yahweh. In Yahweh's laws, Yahweh said not to worship any idols. But in this passage, the people of Israel worship two golden calves. "Worship" means that the people of Israel honor these two golden calves and thank the golden calves for rescuing the people of Israel from slavery in the land of Egypt. Translate sin and worship the same as you have in previous passages. For more information on sin and worship, refer to the Master Glossary.

King Jeroboam also makes houses of worship on high places. High places are often on hills, or raised areas of land, but not all "high places" are in the hills. King Jeroboam puts altars in the houses of worship on high places. Translate high places the same as you have in previous passages.

King Jeroboam also chooses priests from all different kinds of people, and not just from among the Levites. Priests are people who serve in the temple of Yahweh. Levites are people who belong to the tribe of Levi, one of the 12 tribes of Israel. In the law of Moses, Yahweh said that only Levites could become priests for Yahweh. Translate priests and Levites the same as you have in previous passages. For more information on priests and Levites, refer to the Master Glossary.

King Jeroboam also chooses a new day for the festival. We know that this festival is called the Festival of Tabernacles. In the law of Moses, Yahweh tells the Israelites to celebrate the Festival of Tabernacles at the end of the harvest season, which was on the fifteenth day of the seventh month. The people live in temporary shelters for eight days during the Festival of Tabernacles. Yahweh also told the people they must come to Jerusalem for this festival. Earlier in the book of 1 Kings, King Solomon dedicated the temple in Jerusalem during the Festival of Tabernacles. Your team may choose to say the name of this festival, the "Festival of Tabernacles." If you do call it the Festival of Tabernacles, translate it the same as you have in previous passages, and you can refer to "Festival of Tabernacles" in the Master Glossary. Your team may choose to translate the festival as a more general word, like "festival."

King Jeroboam offers sacrifices himself on the altar that King Jeroboam had built in Bethel. An altar is a place where people burn sacrifices to God. Translate altar the same as you have in previous passages. For more information on altar, refer to the Master Glossary.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 13:1–10
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 13:1–10 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 13:1–10 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 13:1–10 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous story, we learned how King Jeroboam strengthened his military power and his religious power. King Jeroboam made golden calves, started a new festival, chose new priests for his new high places, and offered sacrifices on the altar in Bethel. Now, in this passage, as King Jeroboam stands near the altar in Bethel to make a sacrifice, a man of God speaks against the altar that King Jeroboam made in Bethel. The man of God also makes a prophecy about the altar, and the man of God gives a sign that the prophecy will happen.

As the story begins, a man of God, or prophet, leaves Judah to come to Bethel. Judah is the southern kingdom, and Bethel is a city in the northern kingdom of Israel.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a map of both Judah and Israel. Find the city of Bethel on the map. Pause this audio here.

King Jeroboam stands by the altar in Bethel, which is where King Jeroboam put one of the golden calves that he made. King Jeroboam is ready to make a sacrifice on the altar at the start of the new festival that King Jeroboam made. Remember that only priests of Yahweh, who are Levites, should make sacrifices to Yahweh, and they should only make sacrifices in Jerusalem and not in any other place. Yahweh had also told people to celebrate this festival on a certain day each year, but King Jeroboam picked a different day to celebrate.

Suddenly, the man of God, or prophet, arrives in Bethel. Yahweh had sent the man of God from the southern kingdom of Judah to Bethel in the northern kingdom to share a message with King Jeroboam. It is important to remember that Yahweh is still the God of all the people of Israel, including the people of the northern kingdom of Israel.

The man of God loudly speaks out against the altar at Bethel with the authority of Yahweh. An altar is a platform where a person makes a sacrifice. The man of God speaks against the altar because Yahweh does not want the people of Israel to sacrifice at a different altar than the altar in the temple in Jerusalem.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a picture of an altar. Pause this audio here.

The man of God says that the "house," or family, of David will have a son named Josiah. We know from later stories in the book of 2 Kings that King Josiah would rule the southern kingdom of Judah about 300 years after the time of King Jeroboam. The man of God says that Josiah will sacrifice priests of the high places on this altar. This is shocking! Also, people will burn human bones on the altar, which is also shocking. If people burn human bones on an altar, the altar becomes unclean, and no one can use the altar to make sacrifices again.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What does it mean when people in your culture burn human bones? What happens to the bones after people burn the bones? Pause this audio here.

That same day, the man of God also gives a sign, or proof, that what he said about Josiah and the altar is true. Prophets often did a special sign to help people believe what they said about the future. The sign is that the altar will split apart, and the fatty ashes from the altar will pour out. It is not clear if people will break apart the altar or if an earthquake will break apart the altar. The fatty ashes are the burnt wood mixed with the fat of the animals that people sacrificed on the altar. In the law of Moses, Yahweh says the people should take these fatty ashes and put them in a ritually clean place. When the altar splits and the fatty ashes spill out, this shows that Yahweh does not like the sacrifices that the people make on this altar.

When King Jeroboam hears what the man of God says against the altar, King Jeroboam points with his hand at the man and tells some people to capture the man. Then, suddenly, King Jeroboam's hand becomes stiff so that King Jeroboam cannot pull it back. This shows that Yahweh is not happy with King Jeroboam.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Describe a person you know or someone you have seen who has a paralyzed arm or leg. Pause this audio here.

Also, stop here and discuss this question as a group: What gesture do you make in your culture if you want to tell someone else to do an action? Pause this audio here.

Then, the altar splits apart, and the fatty ashes pour out. It is not clear when this sign happens, but it does happen exactly how the man of God said it would happen. Many people believe the altar splits apart and the fatty ashes pour out on the same day that the man of God speaks with King Jeroboam. Other people think this part of the passage tells about the time 300 years in the future, when King Josiah tears down the altar. Either way, when the storyteller tells this story in 1 Kings, there was no longer any altar at Bethel because King Josiah had already torn down the altar.

King Jeroboam asks the man of God to pray to Yahweh so that Yahweh will heal King Jeroboam's hand. The man of God prays to Yahweh, and Yahweh heals King Jeroboam's hand so that King Jeroboam can use his hand again. When Yahweh restores Jeroboam's hand, this shows that the man of God speaks with the authority of Yahweh because Yahweh listens to the man of God's prayers. This also shows that the prophecy that the man of God made about King Josiah and the altar is true.

Then, King Jeroboam asks the man of God to come and eat a meal with King Jeroboam. King Jeroboam also wants to give the man of God a reward, which is probably to thank the man of God for healing King Jeroboam's arm. Some people think that King Jeroboam invites the man of God to eat with King Jeroboam in order to take away the judgment that the man of God had made about King Jeroboam's altar at Bethel.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about a time when you gave a reward to someone you know. What reward did you give them? How did you choose the reward? Why did you give that person a reward? Pause this audio here.

But the man of God strongly refuses to go eat with King Jeroboam. King Jeroboam was probably very surprised. To eat with someone honored that person, and it may have also shown other people that you agreed with that person. The man of God says that even if King Jeroboam gives the man of God half of King Jeroboam's house, or kingdom, the man of God will not even eat bread with King Jeroboam. During the time of King Jeroboam, people said bread to mean any kind of food. The man of God also says he will not even drink water with King Jeroboam. The man of God says that Yahweh commanded him not to eat bread or drink water, and also not to return by the same way that the man of God came to Bethel.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about a time when someone refused to eat a meal with you or someone you know. How did you feel when the person refused the invitation to eat with you? Pause this audio here.

If the man of God eats with King Jeroboam, people in the northern kingdom of Israel may think that Yahweh and the man of God approve of all the religious changes that King Jeroboam made in the northern kingdom of Israel. But we know that the man of God spoke against the altar, and that Yahweh does not approve of the altar and the things that King Jeroboam is doing in the northern kingdom. It is important that the man of God obeys what Yahweh tells him to do.

The man of God obeys Yahweh. The man of God does not eat and drink with King Jeroboam. The man of God also goes back to Judah on a different road than the way the man of God came to Bethel at the beginning of this passage. It is likely that the man of God traveled on the main road on his way from Jerusalem to Bethel, and now the man of God probably travels through fields and on smaller paths from Bethel back to Jerusalem.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 13:1–10 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has four scenes.

In the first scene: Yahweh sends the man of God from Judah to Bethel. The man of God speaks against the altar at Bethel. The man of God makes a prophecy about King Josiah, and that people will destroy the altar in the future.

In the second scene: King Jeroboam reaches out his hand and tells his men to arrest the man of God. King Jeroboam's hand becomes paralyzed. The altar breaks open, and the ashes spill out, just as the man of God said they would.

In the third scene: King Jeroboam asks the man of God to pray to Yahweh to heal King Jeroboam's paralyzed hand. The man of God prays to Yahweh, and Yahweh heals King Jeroboam's hand.

In the fourth scene: King Jeroboam asks the man of God to stay and eat with King Jeroboam. The man of God refuses to stay and eat with King Jeroboam. The man of God leaves Bethel and returns to Jerusalem in a different way than the man of God came to Bethel.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The man of God
  • King Jeroboam
  • King Josiah
  • Priests of the altar of Bethel
  • And Yahweh

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

Remember that this current story continues the action from the previous story. In the previous story, King Jeroboam made changes to the religious practices of the northern kingdom of Israel. Remember that King Jeroboam made two golden calves and put them at Bethel and Dan, chose new priests, chose a new day for a festival, and made sacrifices on the new altar in Bethel.

As the first scene of this story begins, the man of God leaves Judah to come to Bethel. King Jeroboam is standing next to the altar at Bethel. King Jeroboam is ready to make a sacrifice on the altar at Bethel. Then, suddenly, a man of God comes to the altar at Bethel. A man of God is someone who speaks a message from Yahweh. Yahweh sends this man of God from the southern kingdom of Judah to the city of Bethel in the northern kingdom. The city of Jerusalem in Judah in the south, and the city of Bethel in the north, are connected by a main road.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a map of Bethel and Judah again, if needed. Pause this audio here.

The man of God suddenly comes to Bethel, while Jeroboam is standing next to the altar in Bethel. Remember that an altar is a platform where a person makes a sacrifice. It may be better to change the order of this part of the passage. For example: "While Jeroboam was standing by the altar in Bethel to make a sacrifice, suddenly a man of God came up to Jeroboam. Yahweh had sent this man from Judah to talk to Jeroboam."

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What is the best way to translate this part of the first scene to show that the man of God comes unexpectedly and suddenly to Bethel with a message from Yahweh? Pause this audio here.

The man of God "cries out" in a loud voice by the word or authority of Yahweh. This does not mean that he wept. Rather, it means that Yahweh commanded or told the man of God to speak this message to King Jeroboam. When we hear that the man of God spoke by the "word" of Yahweh, we know that means that he speaks with the authority of Yahweh. The man of God also says, "This is what Yahweh says," when the man of God gives the message from Yahweh to King Jeroboam.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate this part of the story that the man of God is telling King Jeroboam a message from Yahweh, with the authority of Yahweh? Pause this audio here.

There are three parts to the message that the man of God speaks from Yahweh. First, the man of God speaks against the altar at Bethel. It is unusual to hear someone speak to an altar, but the man of God speaks against this altar to show that Yahweh does not approve of this altar. Some people think that when the man of God speaks against the altar, the man of God is also speaking against the golden calf and the new date for the festival that King Jeroboam chose. Other people think that the man of God only speaks against the altar because this shows that Yahweh's main concern is that the altar exists at all.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story where someone speaks against an object. How will you translate the part where the man of God speaks against the altar? Pause this audio here.

Next, the man of God also makes a prediction, or prophecy, about the future. Before his prediction, the man of God says something in the original language to focus the attention of King Jeroboam and all the people listening to the man of God. The man of God says David's family, or David's "house," will have a son whom people will name Josiah. The man of God says that right now, priests make sacrifices on this altar, but in the future, Josiah will sacrifice priests on the high places of this same altar in Bethel! People will also burn human bones on this same altar!

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about how you get people's attention when you speak to a crowd. How will you translate this part of the man of God's prophecy to show that the man of God wants to focus the attention of King Jeroboam and the others listening to the man of God's message from Yahweh? Pause this audio here.

The final thing the man of God says is a sign, or proof, that what the man of God says is true. The man of God says this sign from Yahweh on the same day as the first part of the message. The sign is that the altar will break apart, and the fatty ashes will pour out. Remember that it is not clear if the man of God means that the altar will break apart and the fatty ashes will pour out on the same day that the man of God is speaking with King Jeroboam, or if this sign will happen sometime in the future.

In the second scene, King Jeroboam hears what the man of God says against the altar, and King Jeroboam stretches out his hand, or arm, from the altar, probably to point at the man of God. It is possible that King Jeroboam points with his hand across the altar to the man of God. King Jeroboam tells his men to capture the man of God. Then, King Jeroboam's hand becomes paralyzed, stiff, and rigid, and King Jeroboam cannot pull back his hand.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate this part of the scene where King Jeroboam tells the men to capture the man of God, and then King Jeroboam's hand becomes paralyzed? Some ideas include:

  • King Jeroboam stretches his hand over the altar and points at the man of God. King Jeroboam tells his men, "Capture that man!" But the hand King Jeroboam uses to point at the man of God becomes stiff and dry, and King Jeroboam cannot move his hand; or
  • King Jeroboam raises his arm from the altar, and King Jeroboam tells his men to take the man of God captive. But King Jeroboam's arm becomes dry, and King Jeroboam cannot put his arm back at his side.

Pause this audio here.

It is difficult to know when the next part of the second scene happens. Remember that some people think that the same day that the man of God speaks with King Jeroboam, the altar also breaks apart, and the fatty ashes pour out. Other people think this part of the scene happens 300 years in the future, when King Josiah breaks apart the altar and pours out the fatty ashes. Remember that in the previous story, King Jeroboam built this altar in Bethel and made the golden calf for this altar, but the storyteller might be showing what happens when someone destroys the altar many years later. Remember that regardless of when this happens, it does happen before the original listeners to this story heard the storyteller tell this story. It also happens exactly as the man of God says that the sign would happen.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate the second scene where the sign happens? Will you make it clear that it happens on the same day that King Jeroboam is speaking with the man of God, or will you translate it to mean that the sign happens much later when King Josiah destroys the altar? Here are some example translations:

  • Also, the altar split apart, and the fatty ashes poured out. This sign happened just as the man of God said, when the man of God told the sign with the authority of Yahweh; or
  • Much later, people tore down the altar, and the fatty ashes spilled out. This happened just as the man of God had said with Yahweh's authority.

Pause this audio here.

In both the third and fourth scenes, King Jeroboam asks the man of God something. In the third scene, the man of God does what King Jeroboam asks the man of God to do. In the fourth scene, the man of God refuses to do what King Jeroboam asks the man of God to do.

In the third scene, King Jeroboam asks the man of God to pray to Yahweh to heal King Jeroboam's hand. In the original language, King Jeroboam asks the man of God to "plead with the face of Yahweh." This might mean that King Jeroboam wants the man of God to try and smooth out Yahweh's angry face for what happened to King Jeroboam's hand.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Act out how a person's face looks when they are angry. Describe how the person's face looks crinkled and not smooth. Pause this audio here.

Also, stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about a time when a friend of yours was angry with you for some reason, and you asked another person to talk with your friend on your behalf. How will you translate this part of the scene where King Jeroboam asks the man of God to pray to Yahweh to heal King Jeroboam's hand? Pause this audio here.

The man of God prays to Yahweh. In the original language, the storyteller says again that the man of God pleads with the face of Yahweh. Translate "plead with the face of Yahweh" the same as you did earlier in this scene. After the man of God prays to Yahweh, Yahweh heals King Jeroboam's hand. King Jeroboam can use his hand again! In the original language, the storyteller says that King Jeroboam's hand is restored to him, and King Jeroboam's hand becomes like it was before his hand was paralyzed.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your language, what is the best way to clearly translate this part of the scene when Yahweh heals King Jeroboam's hand? It may be better to simply say, "Yahweh heals King Jeroboam's hand," and not also say that King Jeroboam's hand becomes like it was before. Pause this audio here.

In the fourth scene, King Jeroboam asks the man of God to come and eat a meal with King Jeroboam. King Jeroboam also wants to give the man of God a reward, probably to thank the man of God for praying to heal King Jeroboam's paralyzed hand. We are not sure what this reward was, but it might have been money or some other valuable thing.

However, the man of God does not agree to go eat a meal with King Jeroboam. In the original language, the storyteller says, "But the man of God says no to King Jeroboam." Remember that King Jeroboam does not expect the man of God to refuse this invitation to eat with King Jeroboam. The original listeners of the story also probably would not expect the man of God to refuse the invitation to eat a meal with King Jeroboam.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate the man of God's response to show that his response surprises people? Pause this audio here.

Instead, the man of God tells King Jeroboam that the man of God will not go with King Jeroboam, even if King Jeroboam offers the man of God half of King Jeroboam's house. Half of King Jeroboam's house means half of King Jeroboam's kingdom, wealth, or possessions. The man of God also says that he will not even eat simple bread or drink water with King Jeroboam.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What is the best order to translate the man of God's response to King Jeroboam? Here are some translation ideas:

  • Even if you offer me half of your wealth, I will not go with you. I also will not eat or drink anything here; or
  • I will not go eat bread or drink water with you, even if you give me half of everything in your house.

Pause this audio here.

The man of God says the reason that the man of God cannot eat with King Jeroboam is because of Yahweh's command. In the original language, the storyteller says that "Yahweh commanded the man of God by the word of Yahweh." This means that Yahweh told the man of God what to do with Yahweh's authority. The man of God should obey the commands of Yahweh. Yahweh told the man of God not to eat bread or drink water, and not to return in the same way that the man of God came to Bethel.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Would it be best to translate the exact words that Yahweh said to the man of God, or to describe what Yahweh said to the man of God? For example:

  • Yahweh gave me this order: "Do not eat bread, do not drink water, and do not return the same way that you came"; or
  • Yahweh commanded me not to eat any food or drink anything, and to go home by a different way.

Pause this audio here.

In the final part of this scene, the man of God does exactly what Yahweh told the man of God to do. Even though the storyteller does not say anything in this last part of the passage about whether or not the man of God eats with King Jeroboam, we know from earlier in the scene that the man of God already refused to eat with King Jeroboam. So, the man of God obeys Yahweh's command not to eat bread or drink water in that place, which is Bethel.

The man of God also obeys Yahweh's command to return to Judah by a different road than the road the man of God came to Bethel. It is not clear why Yahweh tells the man of God to go home a different way than the way he came, but it is possible that Yahweh wants the man of God not to be near the people of the northern kingdom of Israel. The storyteller says the same information in two different ways, probably to emphasize that the man of God obeyed Yahweh's command and did not return the same way he had come to Bethel. First, the storyteller says that the man of God went another way. Then the storyteller says that the man of God did not go back the same way he had come to Bethel.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Would it be better to say first that the man of God went another way, or to say first that the man of God did not go back the same way that he had come to Bethel? For example:

  • The man of God went another way. The man of God did not go back the same way that he had come to Bethel; or
  • The man of God did not travel back on the same road that he had taken to Bethel. The man of God went home a different way.

Pause this audio here.

The man of God obeys Yahweh's commands, which is in contrast to the way King Jeroboam does not obey Yahweh. Remember that in the previous story, King Jeroboam made new ways to worship Yahweh that are not according to Yahweh's commands. It is important to notice that the man of God is obedient to all of Yahweh's commands.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 13:1–10 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has four scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The man of God
  • King Jeroboam
  • King Josiah
  • Priests of the altar of Bethel
  • And Yahweh

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Yahweh sends the man of God from Judah to the city of Bethel to talk to Jeroboam. While Jeroboam is standing by the altar in Bethel to make a sacrifice, the man of God comes to Jeroboam. The man of God speaks in a loud voice against the altar at Bethel. The man of God speaks with the authority of Yahweh.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the man of God, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am shocked to see King Jeroboam next to this altar in Bethel! King Jeroboam knows he is not a priest and cannot offer sacrifices! King Jeroboam also knows that priests should only make sacrifices at the temple in Jerusalem; or
  • I am prepared to obey the commands of Yahweh. I came here to Bethel to share the message that Yahweh told me to say to King Jeroboam; or
  • I know that Yahweh will protect me from King Jeroboam, so I can speak confidently to King Jeroboam with the message that Yahweh gave to me. Yahweh himself gave me the authority to say these things, so I am not afraid of King Jeroboam.

Also, ask the person playing King Jeroboam, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am angry that this man is interrupting my new festival! I am preparing to offer a sacrifice. This man came here uninvited. This man is causing a problem! or
  • I hope that the people of the northern tribes of Israel do not listen to anything this man says. I want the people in the northern kingdom to come to this altar at Bethel to worship, and I do not want this man to change their minds about that; or
  • I do not care what this man says to me or who he is. I will send this man away because I am the king here!

Continue the drama.

The man of God says, "Altar, listen to Yahweh! Yahweh says to you that David's family will have a son. People will name this son Josiah. Priests offer sacrifices on you, altar, but one day, Josiah will sacrifice these priests on you! On this altar, people will burn the bones of people!" The man of God also gives a sign from Yahweh that same day. The altar will break apart, and the fatty ashes will pour out.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King Jeroboam, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • This man does not know what he is talking about. Why is this man talking to an altar? Why does this man think he is speaking with the authority of Yahweh? or
  • I am offended that this man thinks that one day someone named Josiah will sacrifice the priests of this altar on this same altar! That will never happen! or
  • This altar is good, and I am angry that this man thinks one day people will burn human bones on this altar. If that happened, no one would be able to use this altar again. That will never happen! or
  • I have no idea what this man is saying about a sign from Yahweh. No one will break this altar, and the ashes from the sacrifices will not pour out.

Also, ask the person playing the man of God, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am glad that someday, Josiah will break this altar apart. Yahweh does not approve of this altar or the sacrifices that people make on this altar; or
  • I am happy to give this message to King Jeroboam. Jeroboam is not following Yahweh, and Jeroboam needs to know that someday, Yahweh will judge him for not obeying Yahweh; or
  • I wish that Yahweh would come and break this altar apart today. It is terrible that King Jeroboam is making sacrifices here!

Continue the drama.

King Jeroboam hears what the man of God cries out against the altar in Bethel. King Jeroboam stretches his hand over the altar and points at the man of God. King Jeroboam tells his men, "Capture that man!" But his hand that he pointed at the man of God becomes stiff and dry, and King Jeroboam cannot move his hand. Also, the altar split apart, and the fatty ashes poured out. This sign happened just as the man of God said, when the man of God told the sign with the authority of Yahweh.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King Jeroboam, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am angry that this man dares to speak boldly at my new altar. I want to punish this man! or
  • I am shocked that my hand will not move! Why did this happen to me? or
  • I wonder if Yahweh punished me by paralyzing my hand. I am worried that Yahweh might not approve of my new altar here in Bethel.

Continue the drama.

King Jeroboam asks the man of God, "Please pray to Yahweh your God so that I can use my hand again." So, the man of God prays to Yahweh, and Yahweh heals King Jeroboam's hand. The king's hand is healthy again!

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King Jeroboam, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I know that Yahweh will listen to this man more than Yahweh will listen to me, so I want this man to pray to Yahweh to heal my hand; or
  • I hope that Yahweh can heal my hand. I need to be able to use my hand! or
  • Maybe if this man can pray to Yahweh and my hand becomes better, I will listen more closely to what this man is saying to me.

Continue the drama.

King Jeroboam also asks the man of God, "Come home with me to eat a meal with me. I will also give you a reward." But the man of God tells King Jeroboam, "Even if you offer me half of your wealth, I will not go with you. I also will not eat or drink anything here. Yahweh gave me this order, 'Do not eat bread, do not drink water, and do not return the same way that you came.'" The man of God did not travel back on the same road that he had taken to Bethel. The man of God went home a different way.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King Jeroboam, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am deeply offended that this man refused to come eat with me! I wanted to reward this man for healing my hand, and now this man refuses to eat with me! or
  • I wanted this man to eat a meal with me because then I would feel that Yahweh is not so angry with me anymore. I want all of the people of the northern kingdom to think that Yahweh actually approves of this new altar at Bethel; or
  • I am embarrassed that this man refused my invitation. What will the people in the northern kingdom of Israel think when they hear that someone refused the king's invitation to eat with the king?

Also, ask the person playing the man of God, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Yahweh gave me strict commands not to eat or drink at all here, and not to go home the same way that I came to Bethel. I am going to obey all of Yahweh's commands; or
  • I do not want to eat with King Jeroboam! King Jeroboam does not follow Yahweh, and I do not want King Jeroboam to think that I approve of him and his new practices; or
  • I wish Yahweh had not healed King Jeroboam's hand. I think that King Jeroboam deserves punishment from Yahweh; or
  • I want to leave Bethel as soon as possible. I delivered the message from Yahweh, and now I want to return home.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 13:1–10 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

A man of God comes from Judah to Bethel by the word of Yahweh. In the Old Testament, people sometimes call a prophet a "man of God." Yahweh sends prophets with messages for the people. Judah is the southern kingdom of the nation of Israel that includes the land of the tribes of Benjamin and Judah. "By the word of Yahweh" means that this man of God comes with the authority of Yahweh with a message from Yahweh. Yahweh is God's personal name. Translate "man of God," Judah, word, Yahweh, and God the same as you have in previous passages. For more information on "man of God," refer to prophet in the Master Glossary. For more information on Judah, word, Yahweh, and God, refer to the Master Glossary.

King Jeroboam is standing by the altar to make an offering. An altar is a place where people make offerings or sacrifices to God. An offering is a gift people give to God. Translate altar and offering the same as you have in previous passages. For more information on altar and offering, refer to the Master Glossary.

The man of God says that the house of David will have a son, or a male child. The house of David means the descendants of David, the second king of Israel. One day in the future, one of David's descendants will have a son whom people will name Josiah. Translate son, which really means descendant, and "house of David," the same as you have in previous passages. For more information on son, descendant, and "house of David," refer to the Master Glossary.

Josiah will sacrifice the priests of the high places on the altar in Bethel. In the law of Moses, Yahweh told the Israelites how to sacrifice animals, food, oil, and grain. Priests are the only people who should make sacrifices. High places are places where people made sacrifices to idols or other gods. High places are often on hills, or raised areas of land, but not all "high places" are in the hills. Translate sacrifice, priests, and high places the same as you have in previous passages. For more information on sacrifice and priests, refer to the Master Glossary.

The man of God also says that Yahweh gives a sign, or proof, that what the man of God says is true. The sign is that the altar will break apart, and the fatty ashes will pour out.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate "sign" in this passage? Translate sign the same as you have in other parts of the Old Testament. For more information on sign, refer to the Master Glossary. Pause this audio here.

King Jeroboam hears what the man of God says against the altar. Translate king the same as you have in previous passages. For more information on king, refer to the Master Glossary.

King Jeroboam asks the man of God to pray for King Jeroboam. King Jeroboam asks the man of God to pray a special kind of prayer to Yahweh, where a person prays earnestly to Yahweh for something. Use the same word or phrase for pray as you have used previously, and refer to pray in the Master Glossary for more information.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate the idea that King Jeroboam wants the man of God to plead with Yahweh to heal King Jeroboam's hand? Pause this audio here.

The man of God prays to Yahweh, and Yahweh heals King Jeroboam's paralyzed hand. Then King Jeroboam asks the man of God to come eat a meal with King Jeroboam. King Jeroboam also says he will give a reward to the man of God. Remember that we are not sure what this reward was, but it could have been money or some other valuable thing.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What is the best way to translate "reward" in this passage? Remember that King Jeroboam probably wants to thank the man of God with this reward because the man of God prayed to heal King Jeroboam's paralyzed hand. Translate reward the same as you have in other parts of the Old Testament.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 13:11–22
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 13:11–22 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 13:11–22 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 13:11–22 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous story, a man of God spoke against the altar in Bethel. The man of God refused to eat with King Jeroboam because Yahweh told the man of God not to eat. The story ended when the man of God left by a different road than the way he came to Bethel. Now, an old prophet hears about what the man of God did in Bethel, and the old prophet follows the man of God. The old prophet invites the man of God to come eat and drink with the old prophet. We will see if the man of God continues to obey the commands of Yahweh. This is the first part of a two-part story.

As the story begins, an old prophet lives in Bethel. We do not know the name of this old prophet. Remember that Bethel is a city near the southern border of the kingdom of Israel. King Jeroboam had made an altar and put a golden calf in Bethel for the people of Israel to go and worship at Bethel. It is possible that the old prophet is loyal to King Jeroboam's new religious practices.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a map of Bethel, the northern kingdom of Israel, and the southern kingdom of Judah. Pause this audio here.

The sons of the old prophet come to their father to tell their father some important news about what happened in Bethel that day. It is possible that the old prophet stayed home and did not go to the altar in Bethel because he was an old man. The sons tell their father everything that the man of God from Judah did that day, and everything the man of God said to King Jeroboam. Remember that the man of God spoke about the destruction of the altar, prayed to Yahweh to heal King Jeroboam's hand, and then refused to stay and eat a meal with King Jeroboam because Yahweh had commanded the man of God not to eat or drink anything.

After the old prophet hears about what happened in Bethel that day, the old prophet decides he wants to follow the man of God. The old prophet asks his sons which road the man of God took from Bethel back to Judah, and the sons of the old prophet show their father the right road. The old prophet tells his sons to put a saddle on his donkey. A donkey is a common animal that people rode on during the time of the Old Testament. A saddle is a seat or cloth that people put on an animal to make it more comfortable to ride on that animal. The sons put the saddle on the donkey, and the old prophet gets on the donkey and follows after the man of God.

Stop here and, as a group, look at pictures of a donkey and a saddle. Pause this audio here.

Also, stop here and discuss this question as a group: What animals do people ride in your community, if any? Do you use a saddle or a seat, or something else to ride on them? Pause this audio here.

The old prophet finds the man of God sitting under an oak or terebinth tree. Maybe the man of God is resting under the shade of this tree. Oak trees and terebinth trees can grow as tall as 9 meters high.

Stop here and look at a picture of an oak tree and a terebinth tree as a group. Pause this audio here.

The old prophet asks the man of God if he truly is the man of God from Judah, and the man of God says that he is. The old prophet invites the man of God to come to the old prophet's home to eat together. The man of God refuses the invitation to eat anything in Bethel because of Yahweh's commands not to eat or drink in Bethel.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Remember your discussion in the previous passage about a time when someone refused an invitation to come eat with you. Talk about how you feel when someone refuses an invitation to eat with you. Pause this audio here.

The old prophet invites the man of God to come to his house again, but this time, the old prophet lies to the man of God. The old prophet says he is also a prophet and that an angel, or messenger, of Yahweh told the old prophet to bring the man of God back to his house to eat with the old prophet. Remember that an angel is a supernatural being and not a person. The old prophet is not telling the truth because no angel of Yahweh told the old prophet to bring the man of God back with him. It is not clear why the old prophet lies to the man of God, but maybe the old prophet wants to be close to someone who heard from Yahweh, or maybe the old prophet wants to learn more about the man of God.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about a time that someone lied to you, and at first you believed the lie, and then later you learned it was a lie. How did you feel when you found out it was a lie? Why do you think you believed the lie? Pause this audio here.

The man of God believes the old prophet's lie. The man of God goes back to Bethel with the old prophet. The man of God eats and drinks with the old prophet in the old prophet's house. Remember that Yahweh commanded the man of God not to eat or drink anything in Bethel, but the man of God disobeys Yahweh's command.

While the old prophet and the man of God are eating together at a table, Yahweh speaks to the old prophet. This time, the word from Yahweh is true and not a lie. People put food on the table, and then they sat next to the table to eat the food.

Stop here and look at a picture of a table as a group. Pause this audio here.

The old prophet tells Yahweh's message to the man of God. Yahweh says that the man of God did not obey Yahweh's command because the man of God ate food and drank water in Bethel, and Yahweh had told the man of God not to do this. If a person disobeys Yahweh's commands, Yahweh can punish that person. The punishment for the man of God is that people will not bury the body of the man of God in the tomb of his fathers, or ancestors. Israelites cut tombs for their families into the rocky hillsides, and they put dead bodies in these tombs. It was shameful and humiliating if a family did not bury their dead relative in their family tomb.

Stop here and look at a photo of a tomb. Pause this audio here.

Also, stop here and discuss this question as a group: After someone dies, where do you put that person's body? What happens if someone dies when they are far from their home?

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 13:11–22 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has four scenes.

In the first scene: The sons of an old prophet tell the old prophet about what the man of God did in Bethel. The old prophet follows after the man of God.

In the second scene: The old prophet finds the man of God sitting under an oak tree. The old prophet invites the man of God to the old prophet's house to eat and drink, but the man of God refuses to come.

In the third scene: The old prophet lies to the man of God and says that an angel from Yahweh told the old prophet to bring the man of God back to the old prophet's house to eat and drink. The man of God goes with the old prophet to eat and drink.

In the fourth scene: Yahweh speaks through the old prophet and tells the man of God that the man of God disobeyed Yahweh's commands. Yahweh's message also says that people will not bury the man of God in his family tomb.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The old prophet
  • The sons of the old prophet
  • The man of God
  • And Yahweh

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

This story continues the previous story. Remember that in the previous story, the man of God spoke against the altar in Bethel. Also, remember that King Jeroboam invited the man of God to eat with King Jeroboam. The man of God obeyed the commands of Yahweh not to eat or drink in Bethel, and also to return home to Judah by a different way. Now, in this story, an old prophet also invites the man of God to eat and drink with the old prophet. This is the first part of a two-part story.

As the first scene begins, the sons of an old prophet in Bethel come to talk to their father, the old prophet. In the original language, the storyteller says that one son comes to tell his father everything that the man of God did and said that day in Bethel. It is possible that one son first tells the old prophet about the man of God, and then the other son or sons also speak with their father.

The old prophet asks his sons, "Which road did the man of God take?" The old prophet wants to know if his sons can show him where to follow the man of God. Then, the sons show the old prophet the way that the man of God went. In the original language, the storyteller uses words that show that the sons had seen the way the man went or that the sons now show their father the way. Then, the old prophet tells his sons, "Put my saddle on my donkey for me." Remember that a saddle is a seat or cloth that people use on a donkey to make it more comfortable to ride the donkey. The sons prepare the donkey, and the old prophet gets on the donkey. The old prophet follows after the man of God.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a picture of a donkey and a saddle again, if needed. Pause this audio here.

Also, stop here and discuss this question as a group: Would it be better to translate the exact words that the old prophet says to his sons, or to summarize what the old prophet says? For example:

  • The old prophet asks his sons which road the man of God took; or
  • The old prophet asks his sons, "Which road did the man of God take?" or
  • The old prophet tells his sons to put the saddle on the donkey for the old prophet; or
  • The old prophet tells his sons, "Put my saddle on my donkey for me."

Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, the old prophet finds the man of God sitting under an oak tree. Remember that oak trees are often very tall, and it is likely the man of God was resting under the oak tree. It is possible to translate oak tree as "a big tree."

Stop here and, as a group, look at a picture of an oak tree and a terebinth tree again, if needed. Pause this audio here.

Also, stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate the name for this tree in this passage? Pause this audio here.

The old prophet talks with the man of God. In this scene, the old prophet asks the man of God two questions, and the man of God answers both of the questions. It might be better to translate the exact questions that the old prophet asks the man of God, and the exact responses that the man of God gives. Or it may be more natural to translate a summary of the conversation. First, the old prophet wants to know if the man he is speaking with really is the man of God from Judah.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What is the best way to translate the conversation between the old prophet and the man of God? Here are some ideas for translation:

  • The old prophet asks the man of God if he is the prophet from Judah. The man of God says that he is the prophet from Judah; or
  • The old prophet asks the man of God, "Are you the man of God who came from Judah?"

The man of God says, "I am."

Pause this audio here.

Next, the old prophet asks the man of God to come to the old prophet's house to eat and drink together. Again, it may be better to translate the exact words that the old prophet says to the man of God, or to translate a summary of what the old prophet says.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What is the best way to translate the old prophet's invitation to the man of God? Here are some ideas for translation:

  • The old prophet invites the man of God to come to the old prophet's house to eat together; or
  • The old prophet says to the man of God, "Please come to my house and eat with me."

Pause this audio here.

The old prophet's invitation is similar to the invitation that King Jeroboam made to the man of God in the previous passage. Similar to the previous passage, the man of God again refuses the invitation to eat anything in Bethel because the word of Yahweh commanded the man of God not to eat food, drink water, or return by the same way that he came to Bethel.

In the third scene, the old prophet again invites the man of God to his house to eat together. The old prophet says that an angel of Yahweh told the old prophet to bring the man of God back to eat at the old prophet's house. It is important to notice that the old prophet's message does not include the words "this is what Yahweh says," which is usually in a message from Yahweh. The storyteller includes the information that the old prophet lies to the man of God. The man of God does not know that the old prophet is lying.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you show that the old prophet lies to the man of God? It might sound more natural to say, "But the old prophet is lying to the man of God," or that the man of God does not expect that the old prophet would lie to him. Pause this audio here.

The man of God believes what the old prophet says to him, so the man of God goes back to Bethel to eat and drink at the old prophet's house. Remember that the man of God knows Yahweh told him not to eat or drink anything in Bethel.

In the fourth scene, while the old prophet and the man of God are sitting at the table and eating together, the old prophet hears a real message from Yahweh. This time, the old prophet says, "This is what Yahweh says." The old prophet says that the man of God disobeyed the word of Yahweh. Then, the old prophet says the same message from Yahweh in a negative way, that the man of God did not keep the command that Yahweh gave to the man of God. Then, the old prophet says what the man of God did: that the man of God ate and drank in Bethel, even though Yahweh said not to eat and drink in Bethel. The actions of the man of God are in contrast to the command of the word of Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate the message from Yahweh to the man of God to show it is a real message from Yahweh? Would it be better to say the message in a negative way first or second? How will you show that the man of God disobeyed the command of Yahweh when the man of God ate and drank in Bethel? Pause this audio here.

The final part of the message is the punishment that Yahweh gives the man of God for disobeying Yahweh's command. The punishment is that people will not bury the dead body of the man of God in the tomb of the man of God's family. People dug a hole in a hillside to make a tomb. Then, the people put the bodies on stone shelves inside the tomb.

Stop here and look at a photo of a tomb. Pause this audio here.

Also, stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate the ideas of dead body, bury, and family tomb? Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 13:11–22 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has four scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The old prophet
