  • The sons of the old prophet
  • The man of God
  • And Yahweh

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

An old prophet lives in Bethel. The sons of the old prophet come to the old prophet. The sons tell their father about what the man of God did in Bethel, and everything that the man of God said to King Jeroboam. The old prophet asks his sons, "Which way did the man of God go?" The sons show their father the way the man of God from Judah went. Then, the old prophet asks the sons to saddle his donkey for him to ride. The sons saddle the donkey, and the old prophet gets on the donkey. The old prophet rides the donkey to follow after the man of God.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the old prophet, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I wish I were there in Bethel to see what the man of God did, and to hear what the man of God said to King Jeroboam; or
  • I am shocked that the man of God said those things to King Jeroboam! I wonder if the man of God really had a true message from Yahweh; or
  • I want to follow this man of God so I can speak with him. I need to tell this man of God that he cannot speak to King Jeroboam this way because it is King Jeroboam's choice to make these changes to the altar and priests in Bethel; or
  • I want to talk to the man of God because I want to learn from him. If this man of God has a real message from Yahweh, then I want to be around him!

Also, ask the people playing the sons of the old prophet, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We are shocked by what this man of God did and said today at the altar in Bethel! or
  • We know that our father will want to know about what happened today at the altar in Bethel. We are excited to share this important news with him; or
  • We wish that we could go with our father to meet this man of God. We want to hear from the man of God, too.

Continue the drama.

The old prophet finds the man of God sitting under an oak or terebinth tree. The old prophet asks the man of God, "Are you the man of God from Judah?" The man of God says, "Yes, I am." The old prophet says, "Come home to eat with me." But the man of God says, "I cannot go back to Bethel with you, and I cannot eat or drink in Bethel because Yahweh commanded me not to eat or drink here, and not to return by the same way that I came here."

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the man of God, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am tired from my journey, so I am resting under this oak tree in the shade; or
  • I am hungry and thirsty. I would like to go eat a meal with this man, but I must obey the commands of Yahweh; or
  • I am ready to go home to Judah. This was a difficult message to give to King Jeroboam in Bethel, and I am trying to have enough strength to make the journey home; or
  • I do not understand why Yahweh commanded me not to eat or drink in Bethel, but I must obey Yahweh's commands.

Also, ask the person playing the old prophet, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am offended that this man of God will not come with me to eat and drink! or
  • I am surprised that this man of God refused King Jeroboam's invitation and also my invitation. Surely this man of God is hungry and thirsty from his journey; or
  • I wonder how I might convince the man of God to come to my house. I want to speak with him and learn more about him.

Continue the drama.

The old prophet tells the man of God, "I am also a prophet like you are. An angel gave me this message from Yahweh that I should bring you back to my house so you can eat and drink." But the old prophet is lying to the man of God. Then, the man of God returns to Bethel to eat and drink with the old prophet.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the old prophet, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • This command of Yahweh not to eat or drink in Bethel seems unfair. I think the man of God should be able to eat and drink here with me if he is hungry and thirsty! or
  • I hope that the man of God will come back to eat with me in my house. I want the man of God to say that the new practices of King Jeroboam are okay to do; or
  • I want to follow Yahweh in the right way, and I feel bad that I did not say anything against the new practices that King Jeroboam made in the northern kingdom of Israel. I want to talk to this man of God about these new practices of King Jeroboam.

Also, ask the person playing the man of God, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I know that Yahweh commanded me not to eat or drink here in Bethel, but I think Yahweh will be gracious to me if I go with this old prophet to eat and drink; or
  • I am very tired, hungry, and thirsty, and it would be so good to rest and eat with this kind old prophet; or
  • I do not think I am disobeying Yahweh's commands because this old prophet seems to have a new command from Yahweh that says I can eat and drink here.

Continue the drama.

While the old prophet and the man of God are eating together, Yahweh sends a message to the old prophet who brought the man of God back to Bethel. The old prophet tells the man of God this message. The old prophet says, "This is Yahweh's message for you. You disobeyed what Yahweh told you to do! You did not obey the command that Yahweh your God gave to you. You came back to Bethel to eat and drink, and I told you not to eat and drink here in Bethel. Your punishment is that people will not put your body in your family tomb."

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the old prophet, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am surprised that Yahweh chose to send a message through me! or
  • I feel guilty because I brought this man of God back to Bethel by lying to him, and now there is a terrible message from Yahweh for this man of God; or
  • I hope that these things in Yahweh's message do not happen anytime soon.

Also, ask the person playing the man of God, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I regret that I came back here to Bethel to eat and drink with this old prophet; or
  • I feel very sad that I broke Yahweh's command not to eat and drink in Bethel; or
  • I feel betrayed by this old prophet because now I do not think he had a special message from an angel of Yahweh who told him I could go back to Bethel with him; or
  • I hope that I do not die for a long time. It is very painful not to be buried in my family's tomb. I hope that I can make this journey back to Judah safely, but I know this punishment might happen on my journey back to Judah.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 13:11–22 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

An old prophet lives in Bethel. A prophet is a person who gives messages from Yahweh to the people. The sons of the prophet tell the prophet what the man of God did in Bethel. Sons are the male children of their father. In the Old Testament, people also called a prophet a "man of God." The sons also tell their father what the man of God said to the king. A king is someone who rules over a city or a territory. Translate prophet, son, man of God, and king the same as you have in previous passages. For more information on prophet, son, and king, refer to the Master Glossary. For more information on "man of God," refer to prophet in the Master Glossary.

The man of God is from Judah. Judah is the southern kingdom of the nation of Israel that includes the land of the tribes of Benjamin and Judah. Translate Judah the same as you have in previous passages. For more information on Judah, refer to the Master Glossary.

The old prophet tells his sons to saddle his donkey for him. A donkey is an animal that is big enough to carry heavy loads or people. A saddle is a seat or cloth that people use on a donkey to make it more comfortable to ride the donkey. Translate donkey the same as you have in previous passages. For more information on donkeys, refer to the Master Glossary.

Stop here and look at a picture of a donkey and a saddle again as a group if needed. Pause this audio here.

Also, stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate "saddle my donkey" in this passage? It may be best to translate that the old prophet tells his sons to prepare his donkey so he can ride it, and not include the word "saddle" if this is a difficult idea to translate. Pause this audio here.

The man of God tells the old prophet that the man of God cannot eat or drink with the old prophet because the word of Yahweh told the man of God not to eat or drink anything.

"The word of Yahweh" means a message from Yahweh to the man of God not to eat or drink anything in Bethel. Yahweh is God's personal name. Translate "word of Yahweh" the same as you have in previous passages. For more information on "word of Yahweh," refer to word and Yahweh in the Master Glossary. For more information on God, refer to the Master Glossary.

The old prophet tells the man of God that an angel gave a message to the old prophet by the word of Yahweh. The old prophet wants the man of God to think that the old prophet has a message with the authority of Yahweh. An angel is a messenger from Yahweh. Be careful not to confuse angel with prophet. A prophet is a human, but an angel is a supernatural being.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate "angel" in this passage? For more information on angels, refer to the Master Glossary. Pause this audio here.

The old prophet receives a message from Yahweh for the man of God. The old prophet says that people will not bury the body of the man of God in the tomb of his fathers, or ancestors. A tomb is a place where people bury dead bodies. For more information on tomb, refer to the Master Glossary.

Stop here and look at a photo of a tomb. Pause this audio here.

Also, stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate "tomb" in this passage?

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 13:23–34
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 13:23–34 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 13:23–34 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 13:23–34 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous story, the man of God went to the house of the old prophet to eat with the old prophet, even though Yahweh had commanded the man of God not to eat in the city of Bethel. Then, the old prophet heard a message from Yahweh for the man of God. The message from Yahweh said that people will not bury the man of God in his family tomb because the man of God disobeyed Yahweh's command. Now, the man of God leaves the house of the old prophet to travel from Bethel back to Judah. This is the second part of a two-part story.

The man of God finishes his meal, and then the old prophet puts a saddle on a donkey for the man of God to ride on from the city of Bethel to Judah. It is unclear if the donkey belongs to the man of God or to the old prophet, but it is possible that the old prophet lends one of his own donkeys for the man of God to use. Remember that a donkey was a common animal that people rode on during the time of the Old Testament. A saddle is a seat or cloth that people put on an animal to make it more comfortable to ride. Bethel is the city where the old prophet lives in the northern kingdom of Israel, and Judah is the southern kingdom.

Stop here and, as a group, look at pictures of a donkey and a saddle. Also, look at a map of Bethel, the northern kingdom of Israel, and the southern kingdom of Judah as a group, if needed. Pause this audio here.

While the man of God is traveling on the donkey, a lion attacks the man of God and kills him. A lion is a dangerous wild animal. The body of the man of God lies on the road, and it is very surprising that the lion does not eat the body of the man of God or attack the donkey, and that the donkey does not run away from the lion. It is clear that Yahweh caused the lion to attack the man of God, and that the man of God did not die by accident!

Stop here and look at a picture of a lion as a group. Pause this audio here.

Also, stop here and discuss this question as a group: What dangerous animals do you have in your community? What do those animals do to the body of a person that they kill? Tell a story about a time when a dangerous animal injured you or someone you know. Pause this audio here.

Some people are also traveling on the same road and pass by the body of the man of God, with the lion standing next to the body. This is very surprising to these people! These people go to the city of Bethel, and they tell people in Bethel about the lion and the dead body.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about a time you heard surprising news about an event in your town, city, or community. Who told you the news? How fast did you hear the news after the event happened? Pause this audio here.

The old prophet also hears about the lion and the dead body, and the old prophet knows that the dead person is the man of God. The old prophet says that Yahweh gave the man of God to the lion, exactly how Yahweh warned the man of God. During the time of the man of God, if someone did not keep their promise or "oath," people believed that the person's god or gods would send a lion to eat that person. The old prophet knows that Yahweh punished the man of God for disobeying Yahweh's commands.

The old prophet tells his sons to put a saddle on his donkey for him, and the sons prepare the donkey. The old prophet rides the donkey to the place where the body of the man of God is lying on the road and where the lion and the donkey are standing next to the body. The old prophet sees that the lion did not eat the dead body or attack the donkey. Yahweh prevented the lion from eating the donkey or the dead body of the man of God. Yahweh also prevented the donkey from running away. This shows that Yahweh controlled both the lion and the donkey, and Yahweh is the one who punished the man of God.

The old prophet puts the dead body of the man of God on the donkey, and the old prophet brings the dead body back to Bethel. The old prophet mourns for the man of God, and he puts the body of the man of God into his own family tomb. It is possible that the sons of the old prophet or other people also mourned the man of God. In the time of the old prophet, to "mourn" meant to show grief by weeping and crying loudly. The old prophet says, "Oh my brother! How sad!" because the old prophet feels very sad that the man of God died.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do you mourn when someone dies in your community? Pause this audio here.

The old prophet tells his sons, "When I die, bury me in the same tomb as this man of God from Judah." When people buried bodies during the time of the old prophet, they put the bodies on stone shelves inside a tomb they dug out of the hillside. Then, later, the people put the bones in a pit under the shelves and reused the tomb. Bones from different bodies would mix together so that it would be impossible to tell the bones apart from the different bodies. It is possible that the old prophet wants his own body to be close to the bones of the man of God in order to honor the man of God, or so that the old prophet's bones are close to someone who heard a true word from Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Remember your discussion from the previous passage about how and where you bury someone's body when they die. Where do you bury the dead bodies of people from the same family? Pause this audio here.

The old prophet also honors the man of God when he says that he knows the prophecy, or "word from Yahweh," that the man of God spoke will come true. The man of God spoke against the altar in Bethel and against the temples in the high places of the towns of Samaria. In this story, Samaria means the area of the 10 northern tribes of Israel. Yahweh will definitely keep his promise to destroy the altar in Bethel someday because Yahweh punished the man of God for not obeying the command of Yahweh. This is probably the most important message of this story because the storyteller shows that even if Yahweh's prophet fails to obey Yahweh, Yahweh's word will never fail.

In the final part of the story, the storyteller tells us King Jeroboam's reaction to all of the things in this story and in the previous stories. People might think that King Jeroboam will change his mind after the man of God warns him, and after a lion kills that same man of God for not obeying the command of Yahweh. But King Jeroboam continues to do evil.

King Jeroboam continues to choose anyone he wants to be priests for the high places that King Jeroboam makes. Remember that Yahweh said in the law of Moses that only Levites should be priests. This is the sin of the house, or family, of Jeroboam. The storyteller says that because of Jeroboam's sin, Yahweh will later destroy Jeroboam's family.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 13:23–34 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has five scenes.

In the first scene: The man of God leaves on a donkey. A lion attacks and kills the man of God. The lion does not eat the body of the man of God or the donkey.

In the second scene: Some people see the body of the man of God lying in the road, and the lion and donkey standing next to the body. The old prophet hears about the death of the man of God, and the old prophet knows that Yahweh caused the man of God to die.

In the third scene: The old prophet goes to get the body of the man of God. The old prophet brings the body back to Bethel to bury the body. The old prophet mourns the man of God.

In the fourth scene: The old prophet tells his sons that he wants his sons to bury his own body with the man of God. The old prophet says that the message the man of God spoke against the altar at Bethel will happen exactly as the man of God says it will happen.

In the fifth scene: Jeroboam still did not change his evil ways. Jeroboam still chose priests from among the people, which became a great sin. In the future, Yahweh will destroy Jeroboam's family because of Jeroboam's sin.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The old prophet
  • The sons of the old prophet
  • The man of God
  • Yahweh
  • People who pass by on the road
  • King Jeroboam
  • And the priests whom King Jeroboam chooses

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

Remember that this is the second part of a two-part story. In the previous story, the old prophet invited the man of God to eat with the old prophet in Bethel. The old prophet lied to the man of God, and the man of God went to eat with the old prophet. At the end of the story, the old prophet hears a message from Yahweh that people will not bury the man of God in the tomb of his family. Now, the man of God leaves the old prophet's house, and the man of God starts to travel back to Judah.

In the first scene, the man of God finishes eating and drinking with the old prophet. Then, the old prophet, who had brought the man of God back to Bethel, saddles a donkey for the man of God to ride. We do not know if the donkey belongs to the old prophet or to the man of God. It may be best to be unclear in your language about whether the donkey belongs to the man of God or to the old prophet.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a picture of a donkey and a saddle again, if needed. Pause this audio here.

The man of God travels on the donkey. Then, a lion attacks and kills the man of God, and the body of the man of God lies in the road. Remember that the lion does not eat the body of the man of God, which is surprising. The lion also does not attack or eat the donkey. Remember that this shows that Yahweh is the one who sent the lion to attack the man of God, and Yahweh prevented the lion from attacking the donkey or eating the body of the man of God.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you show that the lion unexpectedly attacked the man of God? How will you show that it was surprising that the lion did not eat the body of the man of God or attack the donkey? Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, some people pass by the body of the man of God with the lion standing next to the body. In the original language, the storyteller shows that the people are surprised that the lion does not eat the dead body on the road. The donkey is probably also there, but the storyteller does not say that the people see the donkey in this part of the passage.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you show that the people are surprised that the lion is standing next to the dead body, and the lion is not eating the dead body? Pause this audio here.

The people go back to the city of Bethel and tell people in Bethel about what they saw on the road. The old prophet also hears about the dead body and the lion, and the old prophet says the dead person must be the man of God who disobeyed Yahweh. In the original language, the old prophet says that "the man of God rebelled against the mouth of Yahweh," which means the man of God did not do what Yahweh said to do. The old prophet also says that Yahweh gave the man of God to the lion, and the lion tore the body of the man of God, or broke the bones of the body of the man of God. Remember that this happened exactly as Yahweh warned the man of God in the message in the first part of this story.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate the idea that Yahweh gave the man of God to the lion? Other translation ideas include, "Yahweh caused the lion to attack the man of God," or "Yahweh sent a lion to attack the man of God." Pause this audio here.

In the third scene, the old prophet tells his sons to saddle a donkey for him. The sons prepare the donkey, and the old prophet rides the donkey to see the dead body of the man of God. The old prophet also sees the lion and the donkey standing next to the body. Then, the old prophet puts the body on the donkey. We do not know if the old prophet puts the body on the donkey that the man of God had been riding, or if the old prophet puts the body on his own donkey that is with the old prophet. Then, the old prophet goes back to Bethel to bury the body in the old prophet's own family tomb. Remember that this fulfills the message from Yahweh that people will not bury the man of God in his own family tomb.

The old prophet mourns for the man of God, which probably includes weeping and crying. The old prophet says, "Oh my brother! How sad!" This does not mean that the old prophet is literally the brother of the man of God. "Brother" is a term of affection and respect that one Israelite would often use to address another Israelite.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you show the grief and sadness of the old prophet? Pause this audio here.

In the fourth scene, the old prophet tells his sons to bury him in the same tomb as the man of God from Judah. Remember that people buried several bodies in the same family tomb. The old prophet says the reason he wants people to bury his body in the same tomb as the man of God is that the old prophet knows that the prophecy of the man of God will come true. Remember the prophecy that the man of God spoke against the altar in a previous story. The old prophet now knows that prophecy is true, or came from Yahweh, because Yahweh has punished the man of God for not following Yahweh's instructions. However, we are not sure whether the altar of Bethel broke apart on that same day or whether the prophecy came true later, when King Josiah became king. The old prophet also says that the man of God spoke against the high places in the towns of Samaria. Samaria probably means the area of the 10 northern tribes of Israel, and not the city of Samaria, because people had not built Samaria yet when the storyteller is telling this story.

In the fifth scene, the storyteller tells the reaction of King Jeroboam. King Jeroboam was not in this story, but King Jeroboam was in the previous passage when the man of God came to speak against the altar in Bethel. Remember that people might expect that King Jeroboam would stop doing evil things after Yahweh sent a man of God to warn King Jeroboam, and even after Yahweh sent a lion to kill the same man of God for disobeying Yahweh's commands. But King Jeroboam still continues to do evil things.

The main way King Jeroboam does evil things is by choosing priests for the high places himself, without asking Yahweh. King Jeroboam chooses anyone he wants to be a priest. In the original language, the storyteller says that King Jeroboam "fills the hand of anyone who wants to become a priest." This could mean that King Jeroboam puts gifts in the hands of new priests, but it is not clear.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What is the best way to translate the idea that King Jeroboam chooses or appoints anyone he wants to be a priest? Pause this audio here.

The storyteller also says that King Jeroboam sins when he chooses priests according to his own ideas. When King Jeroboam sins, he causes Yahweh to destroy his "house," or descendants. In the original language, the storyteller says that someone, probably Yahweh, will cut off Jeroboam's family from the face of the earth. This means that no descendants of Jeroboam will live on the earth anymore. Yahweh will punish Jeroboam because Yahweh does not want anyone to change the way people worship Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate the idea that someone, probably Yahweh, will completely destroy King Jeroboam's family in the future? Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 13:23–34 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has five scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The old prophet
  • The sons of the old prophet
  • The man of God
  • Yahweh
  • People who pass by on the road
  • King Jeroboam
  • And the priests whom King Jeroboam chooses

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

After the man of God finishes eating and drinking, the old prophet saddles a donkey for the man of God to ride.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing the old prophet, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel guilty that I lied to the man of God; or
  • I want to honor the man of God by saddling this donkey myself. I do not want to ask my sons to saddle the donkey for my visitor; or
  • I will give the man of God my own donkey so that the man of God will have an easier journey back to Judah. Maybe Yahweh will be merciful to me, and to the man of God. I feel responsible that the man of God disobeyed the command of Yahweh because the man of God believed my lie to come and eat with me at my house; or
  • I hope that the man of God arrives home safely in Judah.

The man of God continues on his journey back to Judah. Then suddenly, a lion comes and attacks the man of God. The lion kills the man of God, and the man of God falls off his donkey. The body of the man of God lies in the road, and the lion and the donkey stand next to the body. The lion does not attack the donkey, and the lion does not eat the body of the man of God. Some people pass by the body in the road, with the lion next to the body. These people go to Bethel and tell other people about the body and the lion. Bethel is where the old prophet also lives.

Pause the drama.

Ask the people playing the people who pass by on the road, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We are shocked that this dead body is lying in the road, and the lion is not eating it! This is another miracle! or
  • We wonder who this dead person is lying on the road; or
  • We do not want to touch this dead body, but if we tell everyone in Bethel about this dead body, maybe the relatives of this dead person will come and get the body.

The old prophet hears the news that there is a dead body in the road, and a lion standing next to the dead body. The old prophet says, "This dead man is the man of God who disobeyed Yahweh's command. Yahweh gave this man of God to the lion, and the lion attacked him and killed him. This happened just as Yahweh said it would happen."

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing the old prophet, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I know that this dead man is the man of God that I saw recently; or
  • I feel guilty that I caused the death of the man of God; or
  • I am disappointed that the man of God did not arrive home safely.

The old prophet tells his sons to saddle a donkey for him, and the sons saddle a donkey for the old prophet. Then the old prophet rides the donkey to the place where the dead body of the man of God is. The donkey and the lion are standing next to the dead body, and the lion has not eaten the body of the man of God or attacked the donkey. The old prophet picks up the dead body and puts it on a donkey. Then the old prophet goes back to Bethel. The old prophet puts the dead body of the man of God in his tomb, and the old prophet mourns for the man of God. The old prophet says, "Oh my brother, my brother!"

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing the old prophet, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am really sad that the man of God died; or
  • I feel ashamed because I lied to the man of God to convince him to come back to Bethel with me; or
  • I am honored to bury this man of God in my own grave.

After the old prophet buries the body of the man of God, the old prophet tells his sons, "After I die, bury me in this same tomb with the dead body of the man of God. Put my bones next to his bones. Everything that Yahweh told this man of God to say against the altar in Bethel will come true. Also, everything that Yahweh told this man of God to say against the high places in the towns of Samaria will come true."

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing the old prophet, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I want my body to be near the body of the man of God because I want to honor the man of God; or
  • This man of God spoke the truth about the altar in Bethel, and I want my bones to be with a true prophet of Yahweh after I die; or
  • I am old, and I know that I will die soon. I want my sons to know that I want to be buried near this man of God.

Also, ask the people playing the sons of the old prophet, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We feel guilty that we told our father about the man of God. Maybe the man of God would not have died if we had not told our father what happened in Bethel; or
  • We are confused about what happened. Why did our father lie to the man of God to get the man of God to come back to Bethel to eat and drink with him? or
  • We know that to obey Yahweh is very important. We need to listen to what Yahweh says to do and not listen to messages from anyone else.

After all this happens, King Jeroboam still does not stop doing evil things. King Jeroboam continues to choose priests from the common people to serve at the high places. This is the sin of the family of Jeroboam. Eventually, Yahweh destroys Jeroboam's family.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing King Jeroboam, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I do not care what this man of God did or what he said to me. I want to choose my own priests and make my own way to worship Yahweh; or
  • I am surprised to hear that the man of God died on the way back to Judah. Maybe that means that the message from the man of God was not important for me; or
  • I think that Yahweh should let anyone be a priest who wants to be a priest.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 13:23–34 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

The old prophet and the man of God finish eating bread together. Remember that in this story, bread means food in general.

Stop here and decide how you will talk about food, or bread, in this story. Pause this audio here.

The old prophet saddles a donkey for the man of God. The man of God is also a prophet. A prophet is a person who gives messages from Yahweh to the people. A donkey is an animal that is big enough to carry heavy loads or people. A saddle is a seat or cloth that people use on a donkey to make it more comfortable to ride the donkey. Translate man of God, prophet, and donkey the same as you have in previous passages. For more information on prophet and donkey, refer to the Master Glossary. Also, be sure to translate "saddle the donkey" the same as you did in the previous passage.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a picture of a donkey and a saddle again, if needed. Pause this audio here.

The man of God continues on his way back to Judah. Then, a lion attacks and kills the man of God. A lion is a dangerous wild animal. Translate lion the same as you have in previous passages.

Some people see the dead body of the man of God in the road, and the lion and the donkey standing next to the dead body. These people go back to the city of Bethel to tell other people about what they saw on the road. A city is larger than a village, and it has walls around it. Translate "city" the same as you have in previous passages.

The old prophet says that the message that the man of God spoke against the altar in Bethel will come true. The man of God spoke this message by the word of Yahweh, which means the man of God spoke with the authority of Yahweh. Yahweh is the personal name of God. An altar is a place where people make offerings or sacrifices to God. Translate "by the word of Yahweh" and "altar" the same as you have in previous passages. For more information on word, Yahweh, and altar, refer to the Master Glossary.

The man of God also spoke against the high places of Samaria. At high places, people made sacrifices to idols or other gods. People also made shrines, or houses for the idols. High places were often on hills, or raised areas of land, but not all "high places" were in the hills. Samaria is a city in the north of Israel. In this passage, Samaria probably means the area of the 10 northern tribes of Israel, and not the city of Samaria, because people had not built the city of Samaria yet when the storyteller is telling this story. Translate high places the same as you have in previous passages.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate "Samaria" in this passage? For more information on Samaria, refer to the Master Glossary. Pause this audio here.

The old prophet puts the body of the man of God in the old prophet's family "grave," or tomb. Translate tomb the same as you did in the previous passage, and refer to tomb in the Master Glossary for more information.

King Jeroboam does not change his evil ways. Evil is the opposite of good. Jeroboam does evil when he chooses priests in his own way for his high places. Priests make sacrifices to Yahweh, and only Levites should become priests for Yahweh. This is the sin of the house, or family, of Jeroboam. Sin is when people disobey Yahweh's laws. In this passage, Jeroboam sins when he does not obey Yahweh's laws about who can become a priest for Yahweh. Translate evil, priests, and sin the same as you have in previous passages. For more information on evil, priests, and sin, refer to the Master Glossary.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 14:1–11
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 14:1–11 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 14:1–11 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 14:1–11 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the last story, the storyteller told how King Jeroboam of Israel continued to disobey Yahweh even after Yahweh sent a prophet to warn Jeroboam. Instead of worshiping Yahweh in the way Yahweh commanded, Jeroboam chose common people to serve the false gods as priests at the shrines. So the storyteller said that because of Jeroboam's great sin, Yahweh would destroy Jeroboam and take the kingdom away from Jeroboam.

The storyteller begins this story when he says that Jeroboam's son Abijah becomes very sick. We do not know exactly when this story happens, but the storyteller says in this story that the prophet Ahijah is now a blind old man. You will remember from an earlier story that Ahijah was the prophet who prophesied that Jeroboam would become king over 10 of the tribes of Israel, so a number of years have probably passed since the last story happened.

King Jeroboam gives his wife some instructions. First, Jeroboam tells his wife to disguise herself. This may mean to make herself look like someone other than the king's wife, or perhaps to cover her face so no one will recognize her. Then Jeroboam tells his wife to travel to see the prophet Ahijah at Shiloh. We know that King Jeroboam lived at Tirzah, which was a city about 10 kilometers northeast of the city of Shechem. We also know that Shiloh was north of Bethel, along the road that people would travel between Shechem in the north and Jerusalem in the south.

Stop here as a group and look at a map of Israel. Find Tirzah and Shiloh on the map. Pause this audio here.

Jeroboam also tells his wife to take with her 10 loaves of bread, some cakes, which are a type of food that people press flat, and a jar of honey. In Israel at this time, people who went to see a prophet would usually take a gift to give to that prophet. This gift that Jeroboam's wife will take is a small gift, like a common person in Israel could afford, so no one will guess because of the gift that she is a king's wife. Finally, Jeroboam tells his wife that when she arrives in Shiloh, she must ask Ahijah what will happen to her son.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, who do people talk to if they need spiritual advice or want to know what might happen in the future? How do people pay for this kind of advice? Pause this audio here.

So Jeroboam's wife travels to Ahijah's home in Shiloh just as Jeroboam told her to do. The storyteller says that Ahijah is now an old man, and Ahijah can no longer see. Although the storyteller does not say this, it should be easy for Jeroboam's wife to deceive Ahijah since Ahijah is blind. But before Jeroboam's wife arrives, Yahweh tells Ahijah that Jeroboam's wife is coming and that she will pretend to be someone else. Yahweh also tells Ahijah that Jeroboam's wife will ask Ahijah about her son, because her son is very sick. And Yahweh tells Ahijah that Ahijah must tell Jeroboam's wife all that Yahweh tells Ahijah to say.

When Jeroboam's wife arrives, she pretends to be someone other than the king's wife. But as soon as Ahijah hears Jeroboam's wife walk into the house, Ahijah speaks and says, "Come in, Jeroboam's wife. Why do you pretend to be someone else?" Then Ahijah tells Jeroboam's wife that Ahijah has bad news to tell her and that she must tell Jeroboam this message from Yahweh, the God of Israel: First, Ahijah says Yahweh reminds Jeroboam that Jeroboam was a common person like everybody else, but that it was Yahweh who made Jeroboam king. Yahweh reminds Jeroboam how Yahweh took the kingdom away from the ancestors of David and gave it to Jeroboam. Although Ahijah does not say this, you will remember that Yahweh took the kingdom away from David's ancestors because those ancestors did not obey Yahweh as David did. However, instead of obeying Yahweh's commands and serving Yahweh as David did, Jeroboam did even more evil than the other kings who lived before Jeroboam. Jeroboam has made idols out of metal and has served false gods instead of Yahweh. This has made Yahweh very angry, because Jeroboam has rejected Yahweh and treated Yahweh like Yahweh is worthless.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story about a new leader who replaced a leader whom people did not like. How was the new leader? Was he or she better or worse than the old leader, and how did people feel about the new leader? Pause this audio here.

Because of the evil things that Jeroboam has done, Yahweh says he will destroy Jeroboam's family. Yahweh will kill every man and boy in Jeroboam's family. Just like people burn up animal manure in the fire so that nothing is left, Yahweh will completely destroy Jeroboam's family so that nothing is left. Dogs will eat the bodies of Jeroboam's family members who die in cities or towns, and birds will eat the bodies of the family members who die in the countryside or places outside the towns and cities. This is the message that Yahweh sends to Jeroboam.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 14:1–11 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has three scenes.

In the first scene: King Jeroboam's son becomes sick, so Jeroboam tells his wife to go ask the prophet Ahijah what will happen to the boy. Jeroboam tells his wife to pretend she is someone else so Ahijah will not know she is the king's wife.

In the second scene: God tells Ahijah that Jeroboam's wife is coming to see Ahijah. Yahweh also tells Ahijah what Ahijah must say to Jeroboam's wife so that she can then go tell Jeroboam.

In the third scene: Ahijah tells Jeroboam's wife that Yahweh is very angry with Jeroboam. Yahweh made Jeroboam king, but instead of worshiping Yahweh, Jeroboam has done evil and made the people of Israel turn away from Yahweh. So, all of Jeroboam's male descendants will die, and Jeroboam's family will no longer lead the kingdom.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Jeroboam
  • Jeroboam's son, Abijah
  • Jeroboam's wife
  • Ahijah the prophet
  • Yahweh
  • People of Israel
  • Jeroboam's male descendants
  • Dogs
  • And birds of the air

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

The storyteller begins and says, "At that time Jeroboam's son Abijah became very sick." We are not sure exactly what time the storyteller means. However, it may be that a few years have passed since the stories in the last passage, because the storyteller is about to mention that the prophet Ahijah, whom we learned about in an earlier story, is now old and blind.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, how do you talk about the way time passes over many, many years? When you tell a story that happened a long time ago, how do you tell people approximately when that story happened without being specific? Pause this audio here.

Although the storyteller does not use the word "king," we know this is the same King Jeroboam from the last story. Jeroboam gives his wife several instructions. First, Jeroboam says, "Arise." This does not necessarily mean that Jeroboam's wife is sitting down, but just that she should get ready to take action. Next, Jeroboam tells his wife to disguise herself so that no one will know that she is Jeroboam's wife. Jeroboam probably means that his wife should put on clothing that common people wear instead of the rich clothing that Jeroboam's wife would normally wear as a queen. Jeroboam may also mean that Jeroboam's wife should cover her head and part of her face with a cloth, since women often dressed this way in this time.

Stop here as a group and look at a picture of a woman who has covered her head and part of her face with a cloth, like a woman might have done in ancient Israel. Pause this audio here.

Next, Jeroboam tells his wife to go to Shiloh, where Ahijah the prophet is. Jeroboam says this is the same Ahijah who once told Jeroboam that Jeroboam would become the next king. Jeroboam also tells his wife to take some specific things with her. First, Jeroboam's wife must take 10 loaves, which means the kind of bread that rises and becomes soft when people cook it. Second, Jeroboam's wife must take some cakes. Jeroboam does not say which type of cakes Jeroboam means. In Jeroboam's time, although cakes could refer to a type of flat or non-rising bread that people cooked with oil or honey, cakes could also mean dried fruit like raisins or dates that people would mash together into small flat disks. Third, Jeroboam says to take a jar full of honey or a similar sweet liquid. The storyteller does not say that Jeroboam tells his wife what to do with these items, but we know that at this time, people always took a gift with them when they visited a prophet or spiritual leader like Ahijah.

Stop here as a group and look at pictures of loaves of bread, flat cakes of raisins and dates, and a jar with honey. Pause this audio here.

Finally, Jeroboam tells his wife to go to Ahijah and ask what will happen to Abijah, the child. In the original language, the word that people translate as "child" could refer to either a young man or to a baby only a few months old. So we are not sure how old Abijah was at the time of this story.

Jeroboam's wife follows Jeroboam's instructions and goes to Shiloh. However, before Jeroboam's wife arrives, the storyteller tells us about Ahijah. First, the storyteller gives some background information that Ahijah cannot see because Ahijah's eyes are dim from age. In the original language, this may mean not only that Ahijah is blind and unable to see, but also that Ahijah's eyes do not move and that he stares straight ahead.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story about someone who is old and blind. How do you describe how this person looks or behaves? Pause this audio here.

The storyteller says that Yahweh speaks to Ahijah and says that Jeroboam's wife is coming and will ask about her son, who is sick. Yahweh also tells Ahijah what Ahijah must say to Jeroboam's wife. However, instead of repeating Yahweh's actual words, the storyteller says that Yahweh says, "Thus and thus you shall say to her." "Thus and thus" is special storyteller language. This is a way to quote someone or show that the person spoke without saying that person's actual words.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: When you tell a story in your language, how do you show that someone said something without saying that person's actual words? Why might you do this? Pause this audio here.

When Jeroboam's wife arrives, Ahijah hears the sound of her feet coming in at the door. The sound of someone's feet is the sound we hear when someone's feet hit or scrape against the ground when that person walks. Ahijah says, "Come in, wife of Jeroboam. Why do you try to look like someone else?" This is not a question Ahijah expects Jeroboam's wife to answer. Instead, Ahijah asks this question to show that Ahijah already knows who Jeroboam's wife is. Then Ahijah says that Yahweh has "sent" Ahijah to Jeroboam's wife with very bad news. Ahijah has not left his house, but Ahijah means that Yahweh himself gave Ahijah this bad news.

Now Ahijah quotes the exact words that Yahweh told Ahijah to say. First, Ahijah says, "I, Yahweh, exalted you from among the people and appointed you ruler over my people Israel." To exalt means to raise or lift up, so here Yahweh is saying that Jeroboam did not become a king because Jeroboam was special or powerful, but because Yahweh chose Jeroboam.

Next, Ahijah quotes, "I tore the kingdom of Israel away from the family of David and let you rule instead." To tear away means to remove something by force in a way that damages that thing, like a person who tears a piece of fabric.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Remind each other of how you talk about what happens when a person tears a piece of fabric. If possible, have someone in the group tear some fabric while the group watches. Then talk about what you saw, felt, or heard. Pause this audio here.

Ahijah continues and says, "But you are not like my servant David, who kept my commands and followed me with all his heart. David only did what pleased me." You will remember that for the Israelites, the heart represented the place where a person's thoughts, feelings, and decisions happen. So Yahweh is saying that David thought about Yahweh and wanted to do what Yahweh said was right, but Jeroboam has ignored Yahweh and instead does what is evil. In fact, Ahijah quotes, "You have done more evil than all who lived before you. You have made for yourself other gods from metal, and you have provoked me to anger by rejecting me." To provoke means to cause someone to have a strong feeling of grief, anger, or wrath.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Tell a story about someone who did or said something that caused another person to feel very angry. Pause this audio here.

Yahweh also says that Jeroboam has cast Yahweh behind Jeroboam's back. This is special language that shows how Jeroboam has totally abandoned or rejected Yahweh.

Ahijah continues to quote Yahweh and says, "Because of this, I am going to bring disaster on the house of Jeroboam." Here, Yahweh begins to talk about Jeroboam as if Jeroboam were another person, but Yahweh is still speaking to Jeroboam. In ancient Israel, people commonly talked in this formal way when they made an important promise. "I will cut off from Jeroboam every last male in Israel—slave or free." Here, to cut off means to kill, and the word "slave" probably does not refer to actual slaves, but rather to young men or boys who were still under their father's authority. Some people think these words mean that literally every male in Israel will die, but we know this does not happen. Instead, it seems more likely that it only means that every male in Jeroboam's household will die.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story about a man's two sons—one young son who is not yet old enough to get married and who still lives in his father's house, and one older son who is married and lives in his own house with his own family. Pay attention to the words you use to describe each son. Pause this audio here.

Ahijah quotes: "I will burn the house of Jeroboam as people burn manure. It will all be gone." In that culture, people often mixed animal manure with straw and allowed it to dry so that people could then use it as a slow-burning fuel for cooking. So this may be what Yahweh means. However, in the original language, these words could also mean to sweep away dung. Either way, Yahweh shows how offensive Jeroboam and Jeroboam's family are to Yahweh by comparing them to animal dung. "Dogs will eat the people of Jeroboam's family who die in a city, and the birds of the air will eat those who die in the country." In that culture, it was common for dogs to roam the streets without owners. These dogs would be very wild and hungry and would often eat dead things. Also, in the original language, "birds of the air" refers to the kind of birds that eat meat or dead things. These things would be very shameful to the Israelites, because in Israelite culture, people honored someone when they buried them. So this was one of the worst punishments people could imagine.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story about someone who received a very shameful punishment for something that person did wrong. Pay attention to the words you use to describe this shame. Also, pay attention to the way your audience responds when you talk about this shame. Pause this audio here.

Finally, Ahijah quotes, "Yahweh has spoken!" With these final words, Yahweh shows that Yahweh will do exactly what Yahweh has just promised, and this is certain because Yahweh always does what Yahweh says.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 14:1–11 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has three scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Jeroboam
  • Jeroboam's son, Abijah
  • Jeroboam's wife
  • Ahijah the prophet
  • Yahweh
  • People of Israel
  • Jeroboam's male descendants
  • Dogs
  • And birds of the air

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

At a certain time, Jeroboam's son Abijah gets very sick. So Jeroboam says to his wife, "Go and change clothes so that no one will see that you are the wife of King Jeroboam. Then go to the city of Shiloh, where you will find Ahijah the prophet, who told me that I would become king over Israel. Take 10 loaves of bread, and also some cakes and a jar of honey, and go see Ahijah. Ask Ahijah if your child will get well again. Ahijah will be able to tell you."

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Jeroboam, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am worried about our son Abijah, but I hope Ahijah will say our son will get well. Then I will not feel worried anymore; or
  • The gods and idols I serve cannot tell me if my son will get well, but I remember that Yahweh can tell Ahijah things like that; or
  • I need Ahijah's help, but because I have disobeyed Yahweh, I am afraid Ahijah will get angry if I ask. Maybe Ahijah will not know it is my son if my wife pretends to be someone else.

Continue the drama.

But before Jeroboam's wife arrives, Yahweh speaks and tells Ahijah that Jeroboam's wife is coming and that she will pretend to be someone else. Then Yahweh tells Ahijah a message that Ahijah must speak to Jeroboam's wife from Yahweh.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am very angry! I made Jeroboam king so that Israel would worship me, but instead, Jeroboam worships false gods and metal idols; or
  • Jeroboam cannot trick Yahweh, the God of Israel. I know all of the evil things Jeroboam is thinking and doing.

Ask the person playing Ahijah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Jeroboam's wife will probably be surprised and afraid when I say I know who she is and why she came. But she will be even more afraid when I tell her what Yahweh said; or
  • Jeroboam probably thinks he is very clever and can easily trick an old, blind man like me. But Jeroboam forgets that I serve Yahweh, and Yahweh sees everything.

Continue the drama.

When Jeroboam's wife arrives, and Ahijah hears her coming, Ahijah says, "Come in, wife of Jeroboam. Why are you trying to hide who you are?" Then Ahijah tells Jeroboam's wife that Ahijah has a terrible message from Yahweh that she must tell Jeroboam. "I, Yahweh, raised you up from among the ordinary people to be the king of my people Israel. I even took David's kingdom away from David's descendants and gave it to you. But you are not like David, who served me, Yahweh. David obeyed my commands and lived to serve me and do what pleases me. The rulers of Israel before you did some evil things, but you have done much worse. You have made me furious by making metal images of false gods and abandoning me, Yahweh. So listen, Jeroboam! I am about to destroy your family! I will make sure that every single man and boy in your family dies. I will throw them all away as if they were dung. Dogs will eat the dead bodies of your relatives in the towns, and birds will eat the bodies of your relatives in the field. I, Yahweh, have declared this will happen!"

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Jeroboam's wife, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am terrified! When I came, I was only afraid for our son Abijah. But now this prophet says that every one of our male family members will die too! or
  • I do not know much about Yahweh because we worship other gods. But Yahweh must be very powerful to tell Ahijah all of these things, even before I came.

Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am usually very patient and give the Israelites many chances to change and obey me. But Jeroboam has gone too far!

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 14:1–11 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

When Jeroboam's son Abijah becomes sick, Jeroboam tells Jeroboam's wife to disguise herself and go to Shiloh. To disguise yourself means to change your normal appearance or cover yourself so that people will not recognize you.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for disguise. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

You will remember that Shiloh was once a very important city for the Israelites. Before King David moved the ark of the covenant to Jerusalem, and Solomon later built the temple, people went to Shiloh, where the tabernacle was, to worship Yahweh. Use the same word or phrase for Shiloh as you used in previous passages.

Jeroboam tells his wife that in Shiloh, she must go to see the prophet Ahijah. A prophet is a person who gives messages from God to the people. This message can be an instruction from God about something that needs to be done, or it can be a message that tells about something that is going to happen in the future. But most of the time, these messages were warnings to people to stop disobeying God. Use the same word or phrase for prophet as you used in previous passages. For more information on prophet, refer to the Master Glossary.

Yahweh tells Ahijah that Jeroboam's wife is coming to see Ahijah. God is the general name people use to talk about the most powerful spiritual being who created everything, but Yahweh is a name that God gives himself. When people hear the name Yahweh, they are reminded especially of the covenant, the special promise that God had made with the Israelites at the time of Moses. In this passage, Yahweh reminds Jeroboam's wife that Yahweh is the God of Israel. Use the same words or phrases for Yahweh and for God as you used in previous passages. For more information on Yahweh and God, refer to the Master Glossary.

Ahijah begins to give Yahweh's message to Jeroboam's wife so that she can later tell it to Jeroboam. First, Yahweh says that Yahweh exalted Jeroboam among the people. To exalt means to put someone in a place of high honor or to raise that person above others in power or position.

Stop here and discuss as a group how you will talk about Yahweh exalting, or raising up, Jeroboam to be king. Pause this audio here.

A kingdom is the territory or the people that a king rules. Use the same word for kingdom as you used in previous passages. For more information on kingdom, refer to the Master Glossary.

The house of David refers not to a building, but to the people who belong to David. You will remember that David was an important king in Israel's history. Here, "house of David" refers specifically to the descendants of King David. Use the same word for "house of David" as you used in previous passages. For more information on "house of David," refer to the Master Glossary.

Yahweh says that David was Yahweh's servant who kept Yahweh's commandments. A servant is a person who does work for someone else, and a commandment is a law, a rule, or an instruction. David was Yahweh's servant because he did the things Yahweh told him to do. God gives commandments to people to show people how to live well, in a good relationship with God and with others. Use the same words for servant and commandment as you used in previous passages. For more information on servant and commandment, refer to the Master Glossary.

Yahweh says that David followed Yahweh with all of David's heart and did what was right in Yahweh's eyes. You will remember that in the Bible, the heart is special language that means the place where a person's thoughts, feelings, and decisions happen. And to do right in someone's eyes is special language that means to do what pleases that person. Use the same words or phrases for heart and doing what is right in the eyes of Yahweh as you used in previous passages. For more information on heart, refer to the Master Glossary.

Yahweh says that Jeroboam has done evil. Evil is the opposite of good. A person who is evil willingly does bad things that hurt other people.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for evil. Look up evil in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Yahweh says that Jeroboam has made gods and metal images. These are not the true God, Yahweh, the most powerful spiritual being. Instead, these are other spirit beings or false gods that people worship as if they have power. Use the same word or phrase for god as you used in previous passages. For more information on god, refer to the Master Glossary.

Sometimes people make statues or metal images to represent these false gods. Here, Jeroboam probably did not make these gold or silver idols himself but instead ordered craftsmen to make the images.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for metal images. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. For more information, look up idols in the Master Glossary. Although this is a slightly different word in the original language, some translations use the same word for idols here, so you may choose to do the same. Pause this audio here.

Yahweh says he will destroy, or bring disaster to, Jeroboam's household. For more information, look up household in the Master Glossary. This is the same concept as "house of David" earlier in this passage. Use the same word or phrase for house or household when it means someone's descendants as you used in previous passages.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 14:12–20
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 14:12–20 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 14:12–20 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 14:12–20 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the last story, King Jeroboam's son got sick, so Jeroboam sent his wife to the prophet Ahijah to find out what would happen to the child. Even though Jeroboam's wife pretended to be someone else, Yahweh had already told Ahijah who she was and what to say. So Ahijah spoke Yahweh's message to Jeroboam's wife so she could take the message to Jeroboam. In that message, Yahweh said he was very angry with Jeroboam because Jeroboam had led Israel to worship idols instead of Yahweh. Yahweh also said he would kill Jeroboam's male descendants, and that wild animals would eat the bodies. Then Jeroboam's family would no longer lead Israel. This was Yahweh's message to Jeroboam.

Now, in this story, Ahijah continues to speak to Jeroboam's wife, but instead of speaking Yahweh's words, Ahijah now speaks Ahijah's own words. First, Ahijah tells Jeroboam's wife that she must go back to her home. You will remember that Ahijah's home is in the city of Shiloh, but Jeroboam and Jeroboam's wife live in the city of Tirzah, which is north of Shiloh.

Stop here as a group and look at a map of Israel. Find Shiloh and Tirzah on the map. Pause this audio here.

Before Jeroboam's wife goes back home, Ahijah tells Jeroboam's wife what will happen. As soon as Jeroboam's wife enters the city, the child Abijah will die. Then all the people of Israel will mourn, or weep and cry, because Abijah has died, and people will bury Abijah's body. But Abijah is the only one in Jeroboam's family who people will bury in this respectful way. This is because Abijah is the only person in Jeroboam's family who has done anything to make Yahweh happy. You will remember from the last story that Yahweh said all the males in Jeroboam's family would die, and that no one would bury their bodies. Instead, wild animals would eat their bodies. So Abijah is the only one in Jeroboam's family who will not suffer that great shame.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: When someone in your community dies, what do you do with that person's body? How do you show honor and respect for that person? How do you show your sadness? Pause this audio here.

Abijah says that after that, Yahweh will raise up another king who will replace the family of Jeroboam as ruler over Israel. We will not hear more about this king until a later story, but we know that Ahijah is talking about Baasha, the man who will later kill King Nadab, Jeroboam's son. Then Baasha will make himself the new king of Israel. You will remember that at this time, the land was divided into two kingdoms: the kingdom of Israel in the north and the kingdom of Judah in the south. And so another person will replace Jeroboam's family as the king of the northern kingdom of Israel, just as Yahweh promised.

Now Ahijah uses special language to describe how Yahweh will punish Israel. First, Ahijah says that Yahweh will strike Israel like a person shakes a reed or a special type of long, thick grass that grows in the water. Yahweh will uproot Israel from the land Yahweh gave their ancestors. This means that Yahweh will force the people to go somewhere else. And finally, Ahijah says that Yahweh will scatter the people of Israel beyond the Euphrates. This means that Yahweh will force the people to go to a land east of Israel, across the Euphrates River. Although Ahijah does not give any more details in this story, this probably refers to the story we find in 2 Kings chapter 17, where the Assyrians will conquer the northern kingdom of Israel and take the people to Assyria.

Stop here as a group and look at a map of Israel and the countries that surround Israel. Find the Euphrates River and the land east of it. Pause this audio here.

Ahijah says that Yahweh will do these things to Israel because the people made Yahweh very angry when they made idols of the goddess Asherah. Asherah was the goddess of fertility whom the Canaanite people who lived in the land before the Israelites worshiped. And so Yahweh will give up or abandon the people of Israel because Jeroboam sinned and caused the people to sin.

So Jeroboam's wife left Ahijah and traveled back to Tirzah. And as soon as Jeroboam's wife entered the house, the boy Abijah died. And people buried Abijah, and all the people of Israel mourned and cried, just like Yahweh said would happen.

The storyteller says we can learn more about everything else Jeroboam did, including the battles Jeroboam fought and how Jeroboam ruled as king, in the book called "The History of the Kings of Israel." However, we know that this book no longer exists.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, how do you remember and share stories or information about things that happened long ago? What happens if people lose or forget this information? Pause this audio here.

Finally, the storyteller says that Jeroboam ruled as king for 22 years. Then Jeroboam died, and Jeroboam's son Nadab became the next king.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 14:12–20 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has four scenes.

In the first scene: Ahijah tells Jeroboam's wife to go home. Then Abijah will die, and people will bury Abijah's body. But people will not bury anyone else from Jeroboam's family when they die. Abijah is the only person in Jeroboam's family who pleases Yahweh, and so only Abijah will receive a proper burial.

In the second scene: Ahijah says that Yahweh will raise up another person as king. Then Yahweh will punish the people of Israel for the sins Jeroboam caused them to commit. Yahweh will do this by forcing the Israelites to live in a foreign land.

In the third scene: Jeroboam's wife goes home, and as soon as she gets there, Abijah dies. Then people mourn for Abijah and bury his body, just as Ahijah said.

In the fourth scene: The storyteller says Jeroboam reigned for 22 years before he died, and then Nadab, Jeroboam's son, became king.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Ahijah
  • Jeroboam's wife
  • Abijah, Jeroboam's son
  • All of Israel
  • Jeroboam
  • Yahweh, the God of Israel
  • Jeroboam's "house" or family
  • Israel's fathers, or ancestors
  • Asherah
  • And Nadab

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, Ahijah tells Jeroboam's wife to get up and go to her house. In the original language, "get up" often means to begin some action, especially the action of traveling. It does not mean that Jeroboam's wife was sitting or lying down. You will remember that Jeroboam's house is in another city, Tirzah, so Jeroboam's wife will have to travel on the road to get home. However, before Jeroboam's wife leaves, Ahijah tells her what will happen.

Ahijah says that as soon as Jeroboam's wife sets foot in the city, the child will die. To set foot in a place means to walk into that place. Then all of Israel, or all of the people who live in the northern kingdom of Israel, where Jeroboam is king, will mourn for Abijah and bury Abijah's body. To mourn means to cry and show great sadness, and to bury someone's body. In ancient Israel, people often buried a body in a mountain cave or in a special "tomb" or room that people had carved into a large rock.

Stop here and discuss as a group: How do you talk about the way a person walks into a place? Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss how you will talk about the way many people will mourn and bury Abijah's body in your translation. Can you think of a time in your community where a large number of people mourned someone's death? If so, what was it like? What words do you use to describe the way people felt and acted? Pause this audio here.

Ahijah says that Abijah is the only person who "belongs to Jeroboam" whom people will bury. To belong to Jeroboam means to be a family member or descendant of Jeroboam. Ahijah says that Abijah will receive burial because Abijah is the only one in Jeroboam's family in whom Yahweh, the God of Israel, has found anything good. This means that Abijah has done something to make Yahweh happy, although Ahijah does not say what Abijah did.

Next, Ahijah says that Yahweh will raise up a king over Israel who will cut off the house of Jeroboam, and that this is already beginning to happen today. To raise up a king means that Yahweh will choose someone who is not yet powerful or important and make that person king.
