This person will cut off the house of Jeroboam, which means he will destroy Jeroboam's family and take away their power to rule Israel. This is the same violent destruction that Yahweh promised Jeroboam's family would experience in the previous story. When Abijah says this is already beginning to happen today, Abijah means that it will happen very soon.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story to warn people about something that will happen very soon, but do not say exactly when that thing will happen. Pay attention to the words you use to describe when that thing will happen. Pause this audio here.

Ahijah now uses special language to describe how Yahweh will punish Israel. First, Ahijah says that Yahweh will strike Israel. This means that Yahweh will attack or punish Israel in a violent way. Second, Ahijah says that when Yahweh does this, Israel will shake as a reed sways in the water. A reed is a type of large grass that grows in a river. As water flows in a river, it pushes against these reeds and makes them sway back and forth. Ahijah also says that Yahweh will uproot Israel from this good land that Yahweh gave to their fathers or ancestors. To uproot a plant means to pull the plant out of the ground by force or in a violent way. So in that same way, Yahweh will violently force the Israelites to leave their homeland. Then Yahweh will scatter the Israelites beyond the Euphrates River. To scatter means to throw things in different directions so that those things are apart from each other in different places. In the same way, Yahweh will force the Israelites to leave their land and go east, over the Euphrates River, to a foreign country where they will live among people from another country instead of just with other Israelites.

Stop here as a group and look at a picture of a reed swaying in the water. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story about a person who does a certain task. For example, the person might build a house, plant a garden, or cook a meal. As you talk about the way the person does that task, use special language to describe how that action is like another thing or action, like Ahijah does in this story. Pause this audio here.

Yahweh will punish the Israelites in this way because they made Yahweh feel very angry when they made Asherah poles. What Yahweh feels here is the same feeling you discussed in the last story when people made gods for themselves out of metal. Asherah was a Canaanite goddess, and Asherah poles were wooden statues or idols that people made to worship Asherah. So the people made Yahweh very angry when they made idols and worshipped false gods. Yahweh will give Israel up because Jeroboam committed these sins and because Jeroboam caused Israel to also commit these sins. To give Israel up means that Yahweh will abandon or leave Israel. Yahweh will no longer be with the Israelites and protect the Israelites from their enemies.

Then Jeroboam's wife stood up, left Ahijah's house, and went back to the town of Tirzah, where she lived. And as soon as Jeroboam's wife stepped over the threshold of her house, the boy, Abijah, died. A threshold is the bottom part of a doorway. Then people buried Abijah, and all of the people of Israel mourned, or cried and acted very sad, for Abijah, just as Yahweh had said through Yahweh's servant Ahijah.

Now, the storyteller says that people wrote about the rest of Jeroboam's acts, or the things Jeroboam did, including the wars Jeroboam fought and how Jeroboam reigned or made decisions as king, in the book called "The History of the Kings of Israel." This means that people wrote down these stories and information in a book about the different kings of Israel so people would not forget the history.

Jeroboam reigned as king of Israel for 22 years, and then Jeroboam rested with his fathers or ancestors. This does not mean that Jeroboam went to sleep or took a nap. Instead, this is a nice way to say that Jeroboam died. And then Nadab, Jeroboam's son, reigned in Jeroboam's place. This means that Nadab became the next king of Israel.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your language, what is a nice way to say that a person dies? How will you translate that Jeroboam "rested with his fathers" so that people will understand it means Jeroboam died? Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 14:12–20 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has four scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Ahijah
  • Jeroboam's wife
  • Abijah, Jeroboam's son
  • All of Israel
  • Jeroboam
  • Yahweh, the God of Israel
  • Jeroboam's "house" or family
  • Israel's fathers, or ancestors
  • Asherah
  • And Nadab

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

In the first scene, Ahijah says to Jeroboam's wife, "Now, Jeroboam's wife, you must go home. And when you step into your town, your son will die. All the people of Israel will mourn, and the people will bury Abijah's body with honor. Abijah will be the only person in Jeroboam's family to receive burial like this because Abijah is the only one in Jeroboam's family who has done anything to please Yahweh, the God of Israel."

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Jeroboam's wife, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I do not want to go home now, because I do not want Abijah to die! or
  • I feel sad and hopeless, but I am grateful that at least Abijah will not die in a shameful way.

Ask the person playing Ahijah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I know this news must be very sad and painful to Abijah's mother; or
  • I do not like to give people bad news like this, but I serve Yahweh, and this is what Yahweh told me to say; or
  • This is very sad and difficult news, but Jeroboam and the Israelites caused this to happen.

Continue the drama.

Ahijah says, "Yahweh will choose a new king of Israel. That king will destroy Jeroboam's family. This is starting today and will keep happening! Yahweh will attack Israel until they shake like grass in a river. Yahweh will pull up the people of Israel out of this good land that Yahweh gave to their ancestors long ago. And Yahweh will scatter the Israelites in different directions and make the Israelites live in the land on the other side of the Euphrates River to the east of Israel. Yahweh will abandon Israel because Jeroboam sinned and caused the Israelites to sin too."

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • The Israelites have made me furious by worshiping these idols instead of worshiping me! or
  • I rescued the Israelites from being slaves in Egypt so that they could worship me, but now the Israelites are acting like slaves to the false gods and idols; or
  • The Israelites are not sorry for the evil way they act, but they will be sorry when I punish them. Then maybe the Israelites will finally choose to do what is right.

Continue the drama.

Then Jeroboam's wife leaves Ahijah's house and travels back to her own house in Tirzah. And as soon as Jeroboam's wife enters the house, Abijah dies. Then, people bury Abijah's body, and they cry loudly and act very sad that Abijah has died. These things happen just as Yahweh told Ahijah, who told Jeroboam's wife.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Jeroboam's wife, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I hoped that Ahijah was wrong and that my son would not really die; or
  • I feel devastated that Abijah has died, but now I am even more afraid, because that must mean that everything else Ahijah said will also be true.

Ask the person playing Jeroboam, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • When my wife told me the message Ahijah gave from Yahweh, I felt afraid; or
  • I am not sure that what Ahijah said is really true. Ahijah is very old, so maybe Ahijah is confused; or
  • I know Abijah died just like Ahijah said, but Abijah was very sick, so that does not prove that Yahweh did it; or
  • Maybe if I keep worshiping these idols, the idols can protect me from the bad things Ahijah said.

Continue the drama.

Now the storyteller says, "Everything else King Jeroboam did, including the battles Jeroboam fought and the details of how Jeroboam ruled the people, is all recorded in the book called The History of the Kings of Israel. And Jeroboam ruled for 22 years, and then Jeroboam died, and people buried him close by the bodies of his ancestors. And Jeroboam's son Nadab became the next king."

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Nadab, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • My father ruled over Israel for a long time, so he must have been a good king. I want to be just like my father; or
  • I remember how the prophet Ahijah said Israel's old God, Yahweh, would punish my father. But my father was king for a long time, so maybe Ahijah was wrong; or
  • The gods my father worshiped seemed to help my father be king for a long time, so I will worship them too.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 14:12–20 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

The prophet Ahijah is a servant of Yahweh. You will remember that a prophet is a person who gives messages from God to the people, and a servant is a person who does work for someone else. In this case, Ahijah is a servant of Yahweh because he does the things Yahweh tells him to do. Use the same words or phrases for prophet and servant when they are used in similar situations as you used in previous passages. Prophet and servant are in the Master Glossary.

Ahijah refers to Abijah as the child. In the original language, this word can refer to either a young man or a baby only a few months old, so we are not sure how old Abijah was at the time of this story. In your translation, if possible, translate this in a way that also does not say how old Abijah is in this story. If you do not have a general word for this in your language, you could just say "your son."

Abijah will die. Then people will mourn and bury Abijah. To mourn means to wail or cry loudly. In ancient Israel, people usually buried the body of someone important, like a king's son, in a tomb, which is a special cave or room that people had carved into a large rock. Use the same words or phrases for mourn and bury as you used in previous passages.

Abijah is the only person in the house of Jeroboam who has done anything to please Yahweh, the God of Israel. In the Bible, people talked about everyone who lived in the same house as a "household" or "house." So the house of Jeroboam meant all of the people who lived in Jeroboam's house. But people could also use the word household, or house, to mean a larger group of related people. So here, the house of Jeroboam meant all of Jeroboam's ancestors or family who came from Jeroboam. Use the same word or phrase for "house of Jeroboam" as you used in previous passages. For more information on household, see the Master Glossary.

Ahijah uses the special phrase "Yahweh, the God of Israel" to talk about God. God is the general name people use to talk about the most powerful spiritual being who created everything, and Yahweh is a name God gives himself. When people hear the name Yahweh, they are reminded especially of the covenant, the special promise that God had made with the Israelites at the time of Moses. The name Israel comes from Abraham's grandson, Israel. Abraham was the first ancestor of the Israelites. Abraham's grandson was named Jacob, but God later gave Jacob the name "Israel." People call all of Jacob's descendants "Israelites." Use the same words or phrases for God, Yahweh, and Israel as you used in previous passages. For more information on God, Yahweh, and Israel, see the Master Glossary.

Yahweh will raise up a new king over Israel. Use the same word or phrase for king as you have previously, and refer to king in the Master Glossary for more information.

Ahijah says Yahweh will uproot the Israelites from this good land that Yahweh gave their fathers. This is special language that means God will take the people away from this good and prosperous land, or force the people to leave the land.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for uproot. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Yahweh gave this land to the Israelites' fathers. This does not mean these people's literal fathers, but rather their ancestors or forefathers. Use the same word or phrase for fathers as you used in previous passages.

Yahweh will make the Israelites live on the other side of the Euphrates. In the original language, this word simply means "river," but we know this refers to the Euphrates River. The Euphrates is a very large river to the east of Israel that was an important source of water for Babylon, a powerful enemy of Israel. Use the same word or phrase for Euphrates as you used in previous passages.

The people of Israel made Asherah poles. Asherah is the name for the female goddess that many people of the nations surrounding Judah worshiped. The male god's name is Baal.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for Asherah poles. For more information about Asherah, refer to Baal in the Master Glossary. Pause this audio here.

Yahweh will give up Israel because of the sins that Jeroboam did. When someone sins, they disobey God. Use the same word or phrase for sin as you have used previously, and refer to sin in the Master Glossary for more information.

When Jeroboam's wife went home, as soon as she came to the threshold of the house, Abijah died. A threshold is the bottom part of a doorway or opening in the wall of the house where people enter.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for threshold. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Jeroboam reigned for 22 years before he died. To reign means to be the king and to have the authority and power to rule the country. Use the same word or phrase for reigned as you used in previous passages.

Someone wrote down everything that Jeroboam did in the "Book of the History of the Kings of Israel." We do not have this book anymore. In that time, people wrote histories on long sheets of parchment or animal skin that they called scrolls. Use the same word or phrase for scroll, or book, as you have used previously, and refer to the Master Glossary for more information about scroll.

Stop here and, if needed, look at a picture of a scroll.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 14:21–31
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 14:21–31 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 14:21–31 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 14:21–31 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the last story, we heard about Jeroboam, who was king over the northern kingdom of Israel. And now, in this story, we will hear about Rehoboam, who was king over the southern kingdom of Judah at the same time.

Stop here and listen: If you have already translated 2 Chronicles 12:1–16, which contains the same information as this story, listen to your translation of that passage as a group. Pause this audio here.

Rehoboam was Solomon's son. You will remember that King Solomon was the second king of Israel before Israel divided into two kingdoms. Rehoboam was 41 years old when he became king of the southern kingdom of Judah, and Rehoboam continued to be the king for 17 years. Rehoboam lived in Jerusalem, where Rehoboam's grandfather, King David, had built King David's palace and where King Solomon had built Yahweh's temple. The storyteller reminds us that Yahweh specifically chose the city of Jerusalem out of all the tribes of Israel as the place where people would come to worship Yahweh.

Stop here as a group and look at a map of Israel. Find the northern kingdom of Israel and the southern kingdom of Judah. Also, find the city of Jerusalem in Judah. Pause this audio here.

Rehoboam's mother was Naamah. You will remember that King Solomon had a great number of wives, and many of those wives were not Israelites. Naamah was from the Ammonite people. The Ammonites were the descendants of Lot, Abraham's nephew. The Ammonites lived in the land east of Judah.

Stop here as a group and look at a map of Israel again. Find the land of Ammon, east of Judah. Pause this audio here.

When Rehoboam was king, the people of Judah did very bad things that Yahweh had said not to do. This made Yahweh feel very jealous. The storyteller says that during Rehoboam's reign, the people of Judah sinned even more than their ancestors had sinned.

The people of Judah made high places, or shrines, in different places throughout the kingdom where people could worship other gods. The people also set up pillars, or stones, to worship the god Baal, and the people also set up idols to worship the goddess Asherah. Baal and Asherah were gods that people worshiped in other countries where people were enemies of Israel and Yahweh. The storyteller says the people of Judah set up these idols on every high hill and under every green tree. These are the kind of places where the Israelites' enemies worshiped idols, and so the Israelites did the same.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, what are some gods or spirits that people worship? What kinds of things do people do to worship, and where do people do those things? Pause this audio here.

Male cult prostitutes were also in Judah during this time. Cult prostitutes were people who would do shameful sexual acts to honor or worship these pagan gods. These prostitutes may have included both men and women. The storyteller reminds us that the people who lived in this land before the Israelites had done all of these evil things. And now the Israelites are acting just like those people.

Now the storyteller tells a specific story about what happened because of the evil things people did in Judah. In the fifth year that Rehoboam was king, Shishak, the king of Egypt, came up from Egypt to attack Jerusalem. We hear this same story in 2 Chronicles 12:1–16, where we learn that when Shishak came, Yahweh sent a message to Rehoboam to say that because Judah rejected Yahweh, Yahweh would now reject Judah and allow Shishak to destroy Jerusalem. However, when Rehoboam and the other leaders of Judah heard this, they were very humble and sorry, so Yahweh said he would not allow Shishak to destroy Jerusalem. Instead, Shishak took all of the treasures, or valuable things, from Yahweh's temple and from the king's palace. These things even included the gold shields that Solomon had made. And so King Rehoboam had people make new shields of bronze, which is far less expensive than gold. Then Rehoboam gave these shields to the officers of the guard, who guarded the door of the king's house. And whenever the king went to the house of Yahweh, the temple, the guards carried the shields with them and then brought the shields back to the guardroom.

Stop here and look at a map that shows both Egypt and Israel. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller says that all of the things that Rehoboam did when Rehoboam was king are in the "Book of the History of the Kings of Judah." This means that someone wrote all of this information down so people would not forget. However, we know that unfortunately, these writings no longer exist, so we cannot read these things to learn more about Rehoboam. However, the storyteller does remind us that Rehoboam was always at war with Jeroboam, king of the northern kingdom of Israel.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story about some people who are enemies and always seem to be fighting with each other over a long period of time. Pause this audio here.

Finally, Rehoboam died, and people buried Rehoboam's body near Rehoboam's ancestors in the old part of Jerusalem that people called the City of David. Once again, the storyteller reminds us that Rehoboam's mother was Naamah the Ammonite. And then Reboboam's son Abijah became the next king of Judah.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 14:21–31 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has four scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller introduces King Rehoboam. We learn who Rehoboam's parents were, and how long Rehoboam reigned in Jerusalem.

In the second scene: We learn about the evil things that the people of Judah did while Reboam was king, including how the people worshiped idols and practiced prostitution, just like the people groups Yahweh forced out of the land when the Israelites came here.

In the third scene: King Shishak of Egypt attacked Jerusalem and took all of the treasures from Yahweh's temple and the king's palace, including the gold shields Solomon had made. So King Rehoboam made bronze shields instead.

In the fourth scene: King Rehoboam and King Jeroboam were always at war while Rehoboam was king. Then Rehoboam died, and Abijah became the next king.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Rehoboam, the king of Judah
  • Solomon, Rehoboam's father
  • Yahweh
  • The tribes of Israel
  • Naamah, Rehoboam's mother, who is an Ammonite
  • The people of Judah
  • The nations, or people groups, who lived in this land before Yahweh forced them out
  • Shishak, the king of Egypt
  • Jeroboam, the king of the northern kingdom of Israel
  • And Abijah, Rehoboam's son

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

You will remember that in the last story, the storyteller told about Jeroboam's reign as king of the northern kingdom of Israel. In the first scene of this story, the storyteller uses a special word in the original language to show that he will now talk about a different place where things happened at the same time as the last story.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Tell a story where people do things at the same time but in different places. Tell what happens in one place, and then tell what happens in the other place. Pay attention to the words you use to show where and when these things happen. Pause this audio here.

Rehoboam was Solomon's son. Sometimes in the Bible, the word son can mean a more distant descendant from many generations before, but here it means that Rehoboam was literally one of King Solomon's sons. Rehoboam became the king of Judah when Rehoboam was 41 years old, and Rehoboam reigned, or acted as the king, in Jerusalem for 17 years. The storyteller says that Yahweh chose Jerusalem out of all the cities in all the tribes of Israel as the place to put Yahweh's name. You will remember that in that culture, a person's name represented their character or their self. So when Yahweh chose this as the place to put Yahweh's name, it means that this was the place where Yahweh's house, or temple, was and where people would come to worship Yahweh. Rehoboam's mother's name was Naamah the Ammonite. This means that Naamah came from the country of Ammon, where people were not Israelites.

Stop here as a group and discuss how you will talk in your translation about the way Yahweh chose Jerusalem as the place to "put his name." Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Tell a story about a person whose parent comes from another people group or place. How do you talk about that person and the place where their parent comes from? Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, the storyteller explains how "Judah," or the people of Judah, did evil in the sight of Yahweh when Rehoboam was king. This is special language that means Yahweh saw or knew what the people did, and that Yahweh judged those actions and said the actions were evil. The people did such great sins that it made Yahweh feel very jealous, much like a husband feels jealous if his wife is unfaithful to him with another man. The storyteller says that the people did more sinful things than all of their "fathers," or ancestors, before them. In fact, the people behaved just like the people of the northern kingdom of Israel, which we heard about in the last story.

Stop here as a group and discuss how you will talk in your translation about the way people "did evil in the sight of Yahweh." Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story about a person who became very jealous because of how their husband or wife acted unfaithfully toward that person. Pay attention to the words you use to describe how that person feels. Pause this audio here.

The people of Judah made high places, or shrines. These were places where people would worship other gods. The people also set up pillars and "Asherah." Pillars refer to stones that people would stand upright, like a monument or special marker. Sometimes in Israel, people would use stones in this way to mark where they buried a person's body or to remind people of an important event that happened at that place. But here, people used these stones to worship Baal, a foreign god. People also set up Asherah. Asherah is a foreign goddess, and people would often set up wooden poles to represent this goddess. The people set up these pillars and Asherah on every high hill and under every green tree. The other people groups around Israel who worshipped these gods believed that high mountains and trees were sacred places for their gods.

There were also male shrine prostitutes in the land of Judah. In the original language, we are not sure if these were only men or if both men and women were shrine prostitutes. Either way, these shrine prostitutes were people who did sexual acts as part of the people's worship of these gods. So people who lived in Judah did the same "detestable," or disgusting, things as the people Yahweh forced out of the land long ago when the Israelites arrived.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What is a culturally appropriate way you can talk about the evil sexual things people did at these shrines as part of their worship of these gods? What words will you use to describe this in your translation? Pause this audio here.

In the third scene, the storyteller tells a specific story of an event that happened when Rehoboam was king. We hear this same story in 2 Chronicles 12:1–16. This story happened in the fifth year after Rehoboam first became king. In that year, Shishak, the king of Egypt at that time, came up to attack Israel. You will remember that Shishak's army came from Egypt in the south, all the way up to Judah in the north, to attack the city of Jerusalem. So this is why the storyteller says they "came up." Then Shishak took away the treasures of the temple, or "house" of Yahweh, and the king's house. This means that Shishak's army stole all of the expensive things from the temple and the palace in Jerusalem. The storyteller says Shishak took away every valuable thing, including all the gold shields that Solomon had made. You will remember that this does not mean Solomon made these shields himself, but probably that Solomon commanded craftsmen to make these shields. So Rehoboam made, or had other people make, a new set of shields from bronze to replace the gold shields. Then Rehoboam assigned, or gave, these shields to the captains of the men who guarded the door of the king's palace. And whenever the king went to Yahweh's temple, the guards also went and carried the shields. And when the king left the temple, the guards took the shields back to the room where the guards stored the shields.

In the last scene, the storyteller asks a question he does not expect anyone to answer. The storyteller says, "Now the rest of the acts of Rehoboam, and all that Rehoboam did, are they not written in the Book of the History of the Kings of Judah?" The storyteller uses this question to tell us that we can read more about Rehoboam and what people did in Judah during Rehoboam's reign. You will remember that the storyteller has mentioned similar history books in previous stories in 1 Kings. However, these books are not part of the Bible, and unfortunately, they no longer exist.

The storyteller reminds us that Rehoboam and Jeroboam were continually fighting. This means that King Rehoboam's armies and King Jeroboam's armies fought many wars while Rehoboam was king.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your translation, how will you describe how Rehoboam and Jeroboam were always fighting? If needed, refer back to the story you told in step two to see how you talked about this. Pause this audio here.

Then Rehoboam slept with his fathers, which means Rehoboam died, and people buried Rehoboam in the City of David. To bury a dead body means to put that person's body into a place in the ground where no one can see it. In ancient Israel, people usually buried the body of a king in a tomb, which is a special cave or room that people carved into a large rock. The storyteller reminds us once again that Rehoboam's mother's name was Naamah the Ammonite. And finally, Abijah reigned as king after Rehoboam.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 14:21–31 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has four scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Rehoboam, the king of Judah
  • Solomon, Rehoboam's father
  • Yahweh
  • The tribes of Israel
  • Naamah, Rehoboam's mother, who is an Ammonite
  • The people of Judah
  • The nations, or people groups, who lived in this land before Yahweh forced them out
  • Shishak, the king of Egypt
  • Jeroboam, the king of the northern kingdom of Israel
  • And Abijah, Rehoboam's son

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Meanwhile, in Judah, Solomon's son Rehoboam was king. Rehoboam was 41 years old when he started to rule, and Rehoboam was king for 17 years in Jerusalem. Jerusalem was the city that Yahweh had chosen from all the cities in all of the tribes of Israel to be a place where people would worship Yahweh's name. Rehoboam's mother's name was Naamah, and Naamah was from the people of Ammon.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Solomon, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I married women from other countries and worshiped other gods besides Yahweh. But my father David always served Yahweh, and I built Yahweh's temple, so I hope my son will worship Yahweh too.

Ask the person playing Naamah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Even though I live in Israel now, I still love my family and the place I was born, so I serve the gods of my people; or
  • I love my husband, but I am not an Israelite, and I do not know the Israelites' God. So I will teach my son about the gods of my people and how to serve them, instead.

Continue the drama.

The people of Judah did things that Yahweh said were evil. The people committed such great sins that they caused Yahweh to become even more jealous than Yahweh was when their ancestors had sinned. The people of Judah made for themselves various local places of worship. On every high hill and under every green tree, the people set up stones to worship Baal and poles to worship Asherah. And some people in Judah even did very shameful sexual things to worship these idols. The people who lived in this land before the Israelites came had done these evil things, and God had taken the land away from them. But now the people of Judah were doing these same evil things.

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing the people of Judah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We still have Yahweh's temple in Jerusalem, but we want to have other gods too; or
  • All of the other nations around us have more than one god, and we want to be like other people. Besides, maybe if Yahweh does not give us what we want, we can ask another god; or
  • Yahweh has a bunch of rules about the right way to worship, and only special people can serve in the temple. But anyone can worship Baal and Asherah, and we can worship however we want.

Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am very upset that people are worshiping Baal and Asherah instead of me! or
  • I am disgusted by the way people mistreat each other and even their own bodies when they worship these idols; or
  • I feel as jealous as a husband whose wife is unfaithful with another man!

Continue the drama.

After Rehoboam had been king for almost five years, the Egyptian king Shishak and his army fought against Jerusalem. Shishak's men stole all the valuable things from the house of Yahweh in Jerusalem. The men also took all of the valuable things from the king's house. Shishak took everything away, including the shields that Solomon had made from gold. So, King Rehoboam had people make shields out of bronze instead. Rehoboam gave these shields to the captains of the royal guards. When the king went to the temple of Yahweh, the guards went with the king and held the shields, and when the king left the temple, the guards took the shields back to the room where they stored the shields.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Shishak, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I have a huge army, so I could have killed everyone in Jerusalem. But instead, I will just take all of their valuable things; or
  • I remember hearing how the Israelites' God killed many people in Egypt and helped the Israelites to escape. But the Israelites' God does not seem to be helping them now.

Ask the person playing Rehoboam, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Shishak took a lot of very valuable and important things, but at least Shishak did not kill us; or
  • These bronze shields are not as impressive as the gold shields my father made, but hopefully, people will still be impressed when they see all of these guards who protect me.

Continue the drama.

All of the things that happened during Rehoboam's reign, and all that Rehoboam did, are written in the records of the kings who ruled in Judah. The armies of Rehoboam and Jeroboam were always fighting with each other. Then Rehoboam died, and people buried Rehoboam beside his ancestors in the City of David. Rehoboam's mother's name was Naamah, who was from the people of Ammon. And Rehoboam's son Abijah became the next king.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Rehoboam, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I did a lot of fighting while I was king. I feel very tired; or
  • I feel discouraged because I was never able to take back the northern kingdom of Israel that Jeroboam stole from me; or
  • Although I was born in Israel, my mother came from Ammon, so I wanted to honor my mother's gods as well as Yahweh.

Ask the person playing Abijah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • My father was king for a long time, and I hope I can be king for a long time too; or
  • I know my father worshiped idols of the gods from other people groups, so I will worship those idols too; or
  • My father always fought with King Jeroboam, so I will fight Jeroboam too.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 14:21–31 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Rehoboam reigned in Judah for 17 years. To reign means to rule or act as the king. Judah was the southern kingdom in the land of Israel during this time. Use the same words or phrases for reigned and for Judah as you used in previous passages. For more information about Judah, refer to the Master Glossary.

Out of all the tribes of Israel, Yahweh chose the city of Jerusalem to put his name there. You will remember that Abraham's grandson was Jacob, and God later gave Jacob the name Israel. From that time onwards, all Jacob's descendants are called Israelites. Jacob had 12 sons, and therefore the Israelites were divided into 12 tribes. Each of the tribes had its own land in Israel, and Judah was one of these tribes. Use the same word or phrase for "tribes of Israel" as you used in previous passages. For more information on Israel, refer to the Master Glossary.

Yahweh is God's personal name. Use the same word or phrase for Yahweh as you used in previous passages. For more information on Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary.

Jerusalem is the most important city for the Jews, or Israelites, and Jerusalem is in the land where Judah's tribe lived. Jerusalem was where Yahweh's temple was and also where the king's palace, or special house, was located. Use the same word or phrase for Jerusalem as you used in previous passages. For more information on Jerusalem, refer to the Master Glossary.

The people of Judah did what was evil in the sight of Yahweh. Evil is the opposite of good. A person who is evil willingly does bad things that hurt other people. When people stop obeying God, those people begin to do evil actions. Use the same word or phrase for "evil" as you used in previous passages. For more information on evil, refer to the Master Glossary.

The people of Judah committed even more sins than their fathers had done. In the Bible, sometimes "father" means a person's literal father, but other times it can mean a male ancestor. Here, the storyteller is saying that Judah sinned even more than their ancestors sinned. Use the same word or phrase for fathers, when you are talking about ancestors, as you used in previous passages. Use the same word or phrase for sin as you have used previously, and refer to sin in the Master Glossary for more information.

The people of Judah set up high places on every hill and under every tree. You will remember that high places are local places of worship. People often made these altars and shrines on hills or high places, but not always. Use the same word or phrase for high places as you have used in previous passages.

The people set up Asherah poles. Asherah is the name for the female goddess that many people of the nations that surrounded Judah worshiped. The male god's name is Baal. For more information about Asherah, refer to Baal in the Master Glossary. Use the same word for Asherah as you did in the previous story.

The people of Judah did all the same abominable things as the people of the other nations that Yahweh had driven out of the land. Abominable means disgusting, horrible, or shocking. These are things that God hates, or very strongly dislikes, and people should strongly dislike these things too.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for abominable. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Nations means the other countries or people groups who are separate from the Israelites and who do not worship Yahweh. For more information about nations, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for nations as you used in previous passages.

Shishak, the king of Egypt, attacked Jerusalem. A king is someone who rules over a city or territory, like a region or a country. People called the kings of Egypt "pharaohs." Use the same word or phrase for kings as you used in previous passages.

Shishak took treasures from the temple and the palace. Translate temple and king's house, or palace, the same way you have in previous passages. Remember that sometimes people translate the temple as "house of Yahweh." Refer to "temple" in the Master Glossary for more information.

Shishak took the shields of gold, so Rehoboam made shields of bronze to replace those shields. A shield is something that soldiers use to protect themselves from an enemy's attack. People usually made shields with a wooden frame and covered that frame with animal hide or leather. Shields covered with metal were not common because, although this kind of shield provided better protection, it was also heavier and difficult to carry. Gold is a very expensive metal that is yellow in color and does not tarnish. Bronze is also a yellow metal, but it is less expensive and does tarnish. Use the same words or phrases for shields, gold, and bronze as you used in previous passages, and for more information about bronze, refer to the Master Glossary.

Stop here as a group and look at a picture of a shield that people have covered in metal. Pause this audio here.

Everything else that happened while Rehoboam was king is in the "Book of the History of the Kings of Israel." In Bible times, people did not make books the same way we make them today. Instead, this was probably a scroll, which is a type of ancient book where people wrote everything on one very long piece of paper or animal skin and then rolled that paper or skin around a stick so it was easy to carry and store away. Use the same word for book or scroll as you used in previous passages, and refer to scroll in the Master Glossary for more information.

At the end of the story, the storyteller says that Rehoboam slept with his fathers and they buried Rehoboam in the City of David. You will remember that the City of David was the older part of the city of Jerusalem that existed when David was king. Use the same words or phrases for "buried" and for "City of David" as you used in previous passages.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 15:1–8
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 15:1–8 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 15:1–8 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 15:1–8 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the last story, we heard about Rehoboam, king of Judah, Solomon's son. The people of Judah did many evil things while Rehoboam was king. People built shrines, worshiped other gods, and did shameful sexual acts as part of that worship, just like pagan people. Then the king of Egypt attacked Jerusalem and took the treasures from Yahweh's temple and from King Rehoboam's house, including the gold shields. So Rehoboam replaced those shields with shields that people made from bronze, a less expensive metal. The storyteller also said that King Rehoboam fought constantly with King Jeroboam of Israel while Rehoboam was king. Then Rehoboam died, and Rehoboam's son Abijah became the new king of Judah.

You will remember that during this time, the Promised Land that God had given the Israelites was divided into two kingdoms: the kingdom of Israel in the north and the kingdom of Judah in the south. Each of these kingdoms had its own king. So, whenever the storyteller introduces a new king, the storyteller always tells us how many years it has been since the king of the other kingdom became king. This way, we can figure out when each king ruled. So the storyteller begins by telling us that Abijah became king of Judah, the southern kingdom, in the same year that Jeroboam had already been king of the northern kingdom of Israel for 18 years. Many people think this was about the year 913 B.C. The storyteller says that Abijah reigned in Jerusalem, the capital city of Judah, for three years. The storyteller also says Abijah's mother was Maacah, the daughter of Abishalom. We are not sure, but Maacah's father may be the same person as King David's son Absalom.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story about an important leader who lived a long time ago. Tell when the person became the leader, how long the person was a leader, who the person's family was, or any other important information. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller says that Abijah sinned just like Abijah's father, Rehoboam, had sinned. Although Abijah's ancestor, King David, had been fully loyal to Yahweh, Abijah was not like David. In an earlier story, in 1 Kings 11:36, we heard how Yahweh used the idea of a lamp to show how David would always have a descendant on the throne after David. Now, in this story, the storyteller returns to this idea of a lamp to give Judah hope that Yahweh will give Abijah a son who will once again make Jerusalem strong.

Stop here as a group and look at a picture of a lamp like people who lived at this time might have used. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller reminds us that Yahweh did this because David had always done what pleased Yahweh, except in the matter of Uriah the Hittite. You will remember this story from 2 Samuel 11, where King David secretly had sex with a married woman named Bathsheba, whose husband was Uriah. Then, when Bathsheba became pregnant, David was afraid of what would happen when Uriah found out, so David sent Uriah into battle so that Uriah would die while fighting. Later, David felt very sorry that David had done these sins. Yahweh forgave David, but Yahweh also punished David by causing David and Bathsheba's baby to die.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story about a very good leader who did a very bad thing one time. What were the consequences of the one bad thing the leader did? Pause this audio here.

The storyteller again reminds us that Rehoboam and Jeroboam constantly fought battles, so this means that Judah was at war with Israel all of Abijah's lifetime. The storyteller also says that we can learn more about all the things Abijah did when Abijah was king if we find the book called "The History of the Kings of Judah." We know, however, that this book unfortunately no longer exists. The storyteller also says that when Abijah became king, Abijah continued to fight with Jeroboam, just like Rehoboam had.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story about two countries, people groups, or families that continued to fight with each other for a very long time. Tell how that fighting continued from one generation to the next generation. Pause this audio here.

Finally, Abijah died, and people buried Abijah in the old part of Jerusalem that people call the City of David. And Abijah's son Asa became the next king.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 15:1–8 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has three scenes.

In the first scene: Abijah was king of Judah for three years during the time that Jeroboam was king of Israel. Abijah sinned like Abijah's father had sinned and was not true to Yahweh. But because Abijah's ancestor, King David, had obeyed Yahweh, except in the case of Uriah the Hittite, Yahweh gave Abijah a son to rule as king after Abijah.

In the second scene: During Abijah's lifetime, the army of Judah continued to fight with the army of Israel. People wrote down all the important things that Abijah did in The Book of the History of the Kings of Judah.

In the third scene: Abijah died, and Abijah's son Asa became the next king.

The characters in this passage are:

  • King Jeroboam of Israel
  • Nebat, Jeroboam's father
  • King Abijah of Judah
  • Abijah's mother, Maacah, the daughter of Abishalom
  • Yahweh, Abijah's God
  • King David
  • Uriah the Hittite
  • Rehoboam
  • And King Asa

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, we learn that Jeroboam, who was the son of Nebat, had ruled Israel for 18 years as king when Abijah became king of Judah. In the original language, this story uses the name "Abijam," but this is the same king as "Abijah" that we hear about in 1 Chronicles and 2 Chronicles. So you can use the same name here if you would like.

Abijah reigned for three years in Jerusalem. To reign means to be the king and to have the authority and power to rule the kingdom. You will remember that Jerusalem was not only the capital city of Judah but also the city where King Solomon had built the temple for Yahweh. So Jerusalem was a very important city for all of the Israelites.

The storyteller says that Abijah's mother was Maacah, Abishalom's daughter. However, we hear in 2 Chronicles 11 that Maacah is Absalom's daughter. Although we are not sure, it may be that Abishalom is the same person as Absalom, and if this is the same Absalom as King David's son, then Maacah would be King David's granddaughter. You will remember that King Abijah was King David's great-grandson. Sometimes in the Bible, storytellers use the word "mother" to mean grandmother and "daughter" to mean granddaughter. However, we also hear in 2 Chronicles 13:2 that Abijah's mother was "Micaiah, the daughter of Uriel." Although we are not sure exactly why these passages are different, you should translate this passage exactly as the storyteller told it here and say that Abijah's mother was Maacah, Abishalom's daughter.

Abijah walked in all the sins that Abijah's father did before him. This means that Abijah imitated his father, or did the same sinful actions that Abijah saw his father, Rehoboam, do. Abijah's heart was not fully devoted to Yahweh like the heart of David, Abijah's father. You will remember that for the Israelites, the heart was the place where a person's thoughts, feelings, and desires lived, so the storyteller means that Abijah did not love Yahweh, did not want to listen to Yahweh, and did not want to obey Yahweh like David did. You will also remember that Rehoboam was Abijah's literal father, so when the storyteller refers to David as Abijah's "father," this means ancestor or forefather.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story about a person who behaves in the same bad way that their father or ancestors behaved. Now tell a story about a person who behaves in the same good way that their father behaved. Pay attention to the words you use to describe this person. Pause this audio here.

Even though Abijah acted like Rehoboam and not like David, the storyteller says that for David's sake, Yahweh gave Abijah a lamp in Jerusalem, and Yahweh did this by raising up a son after Abijah to make Jerusalem strong. Here, the storyteller uses the special language of a lamp to show how people continue to remember David because of David's family, just like the fire in a lamp continues to burn and does not go out.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story about the way people continue to remember a person who has died because of how that person's family continues. What happens if that person's family does not continue? Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss as a group how you will say that Yahweh gave Abijah a lamp in Jerusalem. Pause this audio here.

Yahweh did these things for Abijah because David did what was right in the eyes of Yahweh and did not turn aside from anything that Yahweh commanded all the days of his life, except in the case of Uriah, the Hittite. You will remember that to do what is right in someone's eyes is special language that means to do what pleases that person. To not "turn aside" means not to disobey or to do things that are different from what Yahweh says to do or not do. A person who turns aside is like a person who begins to walk in a different direction from the direction someone has told them to walk. And "all the days of his life" means every day that David was alive. So King David always pleased Yahweh except for what David did to Uriah.

Stop here as a group and discuss how you will say that David did what was right in Yahweh's eyes and did not turn aside from Yahweh's commands all the days of David's life. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller says that there was war between Rehoboam and Jeroboam all the days of Abijah's life. This means that even when Abijah was a small child, the kingdoms of Judah and Israel were constantly having battles. The storyteller also says that the things that happened when Abijah reigned, or when Abijah grew older and became king himself, are in the book called "The History of the Kings of Judah." This is the same book where people recorded the reigns of the other kings of Judah. And the armies of Abijah and Jeroboam continued to fight one another.

Finally, the storyteller says that Abijah slept with his fathers and that people buried Abijah in the City of David. "Slept with his fathers" is special language that is a nice way to say that Abijah died and that people buried Abijah's body in the same way they buried kings in previous passages. Then Asa, Abijah's son, reigned in Abijah's place. This means that Asa became the next king of Judah.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story about how someone died and what people did with that person's body. Pay attention to the words you use to talk about these things. Then, as a group, talk about how you will translate this part of the story so people will understand. Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 15:1–8 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has three scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • King Jeroboam of Israel
  • Nebat, Jeroboam's father
  • King Abijah of Judah
  • Abijah's mother, Maacah, the daughter of Abishalom
  • Yahweh, Abijah's God
  • King David
  • Uriah the Hittite
  • Rehoboam
  • And King Asa

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Jeroboam, the son of Nebat, ruled Israel for 18 years. Then Abijah started to rule over Judah. Abijah ruled in Jerusalem for three years. Abijah's mother was Maacah, Abishalom's daughter. Abijah sinned just as Abijah's father had sinned. Abijah's heart was not true to Yahweh, his God, like Abijah's ancestor David. However, because of the promise Yahweh made to Abijah's ancestor David, Yahweh let Abijah's son continue to reign in Jerusalem.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing David, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am ashamed that people still think about the terrible sin I committed against Yahweh and Uriah; or
  • I am grateful that Yahweh continues to keep Yahweh's promise to me; or
  • I am ashamed that my descendants do not worship and obey Yahweh as I did.

Ask the person playing Abijah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I remember that my ancestor, King David, served Yahweh, but my father served other gods; or
  • I want to be like my father, so I will keep serving these gods instead of Yahweh; or
  • Things seem to be working out okay for me as king, so I guess these gods that I worship are happy with me.

Continue the drama.

During Abijah's lifetime, the armies of Judah and the armies of Israel continued to fight each other. People wrote down all the things that happened while Abijah ruled and all Abijah did in the book people called The History of the Kings of Judah. And the war between Abijah and Jeroboam continued.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Jeroboam, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I have fought with Rehoboam for many years, and I will fight with Abijah now; or
  • I am tired of fighting, but I refuse to let Judah have control.

Ask the person playing Abijah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I must be an important king for people to write all of those stories about me. I hope people will always remember me as a strong king; or
  • For all of those years, my father never let Jeroboam take control of Judah. Maybe I can finally beat Jeroboam; or
  • I hope I will be king for at least as many years as my father.

Continue the drama.

And Abijah died, and people placed Abijah's body in a tomb in Jerusalem, in the City of David. And Abijah's son Asa became the next king of Judah.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Abijah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • My father was king for many years, so I am surprised I was king for only three! or
  • I thought the gods my father served would help me, but I did not stay king for long; or
  • I hope my son will be a strong king and rule for many years.

Ask the person playing Asa, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • My father did not live very long as king, so I hope I live a lot longer! or
  • My father said these gods would help him be a strong king, but that did not seem to work; or
  • Maybe I should serve Israel's first God, Yahweh, instead of these other gods. People say that Yahweh is a strong God and that Yahweh always protected the Israelites in the past.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 15:1–8 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

The storyteller says that it was in the 18th year of King Jeroboam that Abijah began to reign over Judah. You will remember that Jeroboam was king of the northern kingdom of Israel, and Abijah was king of the southern kingdom of Judah. A king is someone who rules over a city or territory, like a region or a country, and to reign means to be the king and to have the authority and power to rule the country. Use the same words or phrases for king and for reign as you used in previous passages. For more information on king, refer to the Master Glossary.

Abijah's mother was Maacah, the daughter of Abishalom. Use the same words or phrases for mother and for daughter as you used in previous passages.

Abijah sinned just like Abijah's father had sinned. Sin is an act of rebellion against God. Anytime a person disobeys God's laws, that person sins. Use the same word or phrase for sin as you used in previous passages. For more information on sin, refer to the Master Glossary.

Because of the promise Yahweh made to David, Yahweh, David's God, gave Abijah a lamp in Jerusalem by raising up a son after Abijah and establishing Jerusalem, or making Jerusalem strong. Use the same words for Yahweh and God as you have used in previous passages, and refer to Yahweh and God in the Master Glossary for more information. A lamp is special language that refers to the way a person's descendants remind people of that person's life and the good things that person did, even after the person dies. Use the same word or phrase for "a lamp" as you used in previous passages.

Stop here as a group, if needed, and look again at a picture of a lamp like people who lived at this time might have used. Pause this audio here.

"Raising up a son" means that God allowed Abijah's family line to continue by giving Abijah a son who would become the next king. This would help Jerusalem to remain secure and stable, or established, even after Abijah died.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for raising up a son and establishing Jerusalem. For more information about "son," refer to the Master Glossary. If you have already translated these words in another book of the Bible, use the same words that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

David had done what was right in the "eyes of Yahweh." Use the same words or phrases for doing what is right "in the eyes of Yahweh" as you have used in previous passages.

Someone wrote down what Abijah did in the scroll or "Book of the History of the Kings of Judah." Use the same title for this book as you used in the previous passage. For more information about scroll, refer to the Master Glossary.

Finally, Abijah slept with his fathers, and they buried Abijah in the City of David. Use the same words or phrases for "slept with his fathers," "buried," and "City of David" as you used in previous passages.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 15:9–24
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 15:9–24 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 15:9–24 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 15:9–24 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous story, Abijah became king of the southern kingdom of Judah and ruled for three years in Jerusalem. Abijah followed the sins of his father and did not obey Yahweh his God with his whole heart as King David did. Still, Yahweh allowed Abijah to keep the kingdom because of Yahweh's promise to David. Abijah fought against Jeroboam, the king of the northern kingdom of Israel. Then Abijah died, and his son Asa became king.

The storyteller introduces the rule of King Asa of Judah. When Jeroboam has been king over Israel for 20 years, Asa becomes king over Judah. Asa rules in Jerusalem for 41 years, from around the year 911 to 870 before Jesus. Jerusalem was the capital of Judah, from where the kings of Judah ruled. The storyteller says that Asa's mother's name is Maacah. Maacah's father is Abishalom. However, we learn from the previous passage that Maacah is the mother of Abijah, Asa's father. Therefore, Maacah must be Asa's grandmother. We are not sure, but Maacah's father may be the same person as King David's son Absalom. Therefore, it is likely that Abishalom and Absalom are the same person.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a map of Israel and Judah. Find Jerusalem, the capital of Judah. Pause this audio here.

King Asa does what Yahweh thinks is right, just like his ancestor King David does. Bible storytellers often judge the kings of Judah and Israel by comparing them to King David's reign. Asa is one of the few kings of Judah who gets a good report when the storyteller compares him to David.

King Asa chases away all the male cult prostitutes who live in the land of Judah. Cult prostitutes are men or women who work in temples where people worship false gods. Cult prostitutes have sex as part of pagan religious ceremonies. King Asa also removes all the horrible idols that the kings of Judah made before King Asa ruled. People use the idols to worship false gods like Baal and Asherah, just as many surrounding nations do. King Asa destroys these objects because King Asa wants the people of Judah to worship only Yahweh.

King Asa removes his grandmother Maacah from being queen mother because Maacah made a horrible idol to worship the false goddess Asherah. We do not know for sure if being queen mother was a real job with duties or if it was just a title. But queen mothers were often important and had a lot of power in Judah's religion and politics. Maacah probably helped rule when Asa was a young king. Asherah was a goddess of fertility. King Asa cuts down the idol Maacah made and burns the pieces at the Kidron Brook in the Kidron Valley.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a map of Israel and Judah. Find the Kidron Brook near Jerusalem. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What role does the queen mother or the mother of the leaders have to help rule your people group, especially if the leader is young? Talk about the role other family members of the leader may have and how those people can influence the leader's reign. Pause this audio here.

However, King Asa does not remove all the shrines on the hilltops where people worship other gods. The people of Judah often worship Yahweh at shrines on hilltops, but this kind of worship is wrong. Yahweh wants the people of Judah to worship only Yahweh and to worship Yahweh only at the temple in Jerusalem. Even so, King Asa remains completely faithful to Yahweh all the days of King Asa's life. This does not mean that King Asa never does anything wrong. This means that King Asa worships Yahweh only and does not worship idols.

King Asa puts holy gifts into Yahweh's temple. These gifts were silver, gold, and other treasures that King Asa and his father Abijah had promised to give to Yahweh. People gave holy gifts when they made a promise to God or when God answered their prayers. King Asa and King Abijah likely gave treasures from the wars they fought.

The armies of Asa, king of Judah, and Baasha, king of Israel, are at war throughout their reigns. King Asa of Judah and King Baasha of Israel are enemies, and people have been fighting between the two kingdoms for a long time. The wars between the northern and southern kingdoms are border conflicts in which one or the other king tries to make the border, and therefore his kingdom, larger.

One day, King Baasha of Israel goes against Judah. Baasha captures the city of Ramah, about 8 kilometres north of Jerusalem. Ramah city is on the main road that connects Jerusalem to Shechem and other places. Baasha makes the walls of Ramah stronger and puts many soldiers there. Baasha does this to stop people from going in and out of Judah, where King Asa rules. By controlling Ramah, Baasha can affect Judah's communication, trade, and security, since Baasha now controls the key road into Judah. The storyteller tells the same story in 2 Chronicles 16.

Stop here and, as a group, find the city of Ramah on the map. What kinds of cities do you have that are important for your trade, communication, and security? Pause this audio here.

Then King Asa responds to Baasha's capture of Ramah city. Asa takes the silver and gold that remain in the treasuries in Yahweh's temple and in King Asa's palace. King Asa gives the silver and gold to some of his men and sends it to Ben-Hadad. Ben-Hadad is the son of Tabrimmon, who is the son of Hezion. Ben-Hadad is the king of Syria and rules from Damascus, the capital city of Syria. King Asa tells Ben-Hadad that they should make a covenant, or a serious agreement to help each other in wars, just like the covenant that the father of Asa made with the father of Ben-Hadad. Asa mentions the generous gift of silver and gold that Asa is sending to Ben-Hadad. King Asa asks Ben-Hadad to break his covenant or serious agreement with Baasha, the king of Israel, and to fight against Baasha so that Baasha will leave King Asa's land. To break an agreement means to cancel it or to ignore it.

Stop here and, as a group, locate Syria and the city of Damascus on the map. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and, as a group, talk about a war your people group was involved in some time ago. Who started the war, and why did they start it? What happened during the war? How did the war end? Pause this audio here.

King Ben-Hadad agrees to King Asa's plan and does more than King Asa asks. King Ben-Hadad sends commanders and soldiers to attack towns in the northern kingdom of Israel. The army captures the cities of Ijon, Dan, and Abel Beth Maacah. The army also captures the land of Kinnereth, including the city of Kinnereth, the lake of Kinnereth, and the land south of the city. These cities stand on an important international road that King Baasha needs for trade, travel, and defence. King Ben-Hadad's army also captures the land of the tribe of Naphtali.

Stop here and, as a group, find the cities of Ijon, Dan, Abel Beth Maacah, the land of Kinnereth, and the land of the tribe of Naphtali on the map. Pause this audio here.

As soon as King Baasha of Israel hears this news, he stops making the city of Ramah stronger, abandons his work there, and goes back to live in the city of Tirzah. Tirzah was the capital of the northern kingdom of Israel at that time. Baasha rules Israel from there. Then King Asa sends a message to all Judah. King Asa requires everyone to carry away from Ramah the stones and wood that Baasha had been using there to make it stronger. The men of Judah use these materials to make the cities of Geba and Mizpah stronger. Both cities are in important places in the territory of Benjamin, on the northern border of Judah.

Stop here and, as a group, find the cities of Tirzah, Geba, and Mizpah on the map. Pause this audio here.

Now the storyteller sums up and judges the rule of King Asa. The storyteller says that people record the other events of Asa's reign in the book called "History of the Kings of Judah." The people write about the good things Asa did and about how Asa made several cities in his land stronger. This book records the actions of the kings of Judah during their reigns. However, when Asa grows old, Asa develops a disease in his feet. Asa's foot disease shows Yahweh disapproves of Asa and contrasts with the earlier time when Yahweh blessed Asa. We do not know for sure why Yahweh was not pleased with Asa.

Then Asa dies and goes to be with his ancestors. People bury Asa in the city of his ancestor David, where people buried Asa's other ancestors. The City of David is part of Jerusalem and becomes the burial place for the kings of Judah. And Asa's son Jehoshaphat becomes king of Judah in Asa's place.

Stop here and, as a group, locate the City of David on a map of Jerusalem.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 15:9–24 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has 11 scenes.

In the first scene: When Jeroboam has been king over Israel for 20 years, Asa becomes king over Judah. Asa rules in Jerusalem for 41 years.

In the second scene: King Asa does what Yahweh thinks is right, just like his ancestor King David did. King Asa chases away all the male cult prostitutes who live in the land of Judah. King Asa also removes all the horrible idols that the kings of Judah had made before King Asa.

In the third scene: King Asa removes his grandmother Maacah from being queen mother because Maacah made a horrible idol to worship the false goddess Asherah. King Asa cuts down the idol Maacah made and burns the pieces at the Kidron Brook in the Kidron Valley.

In the fourth scene: The storyteller says that despite the good things that Asa does, King Asa does not remove all the shrines on the hilltops where people worship other gods. Even so, King Asa remains completely faithful to Yahweh all the days of King Asa's life.

In the fifth scene: King Asa puts holy gifts into Yahweh's temple. These gifts are silver, gold, and other treasures that King Asa and his father Abijah had promised to give to Yahweh.

In the sixth scene: The armies of Asa, king of Judah, and Baasha, king of Israel, are at war throughout their reigns. Baasha captures Ramah city and makes it stronger to stop people from going in and out of Judah, where King Asa rules.

In the seventh scene: Then King Asa takes the silver and gold that remain in the treasuries in Yahweh's temple and in King Asa's palace. King Asa sends some of his men with the silver and gold to Ben-Hadad, the king of Syria, who rules from Damascus. King Asa sends a message to Ben-Hadad.
