Asa suggests that Asa and Ben-Hadad make a covenant, or a serious agreement, to help each other in wars, just like the agreement the father of Asa made with the father of Ben-Hadad. King Asa also asks Ben-Hadad to break, or cancel, his agreement with King Baasha of Israel. Asa wants Ben-Hadad to fight against King Baasha so that Baasha will leave the land of King Asa.

In the eighth scene: King Ben-Hadad agrees to King Asa's plan, and King Ben-Hadad sends commanders and soldiers to attack towns in the northern kingdom of Israel. The army captures the cities of Ijon, Dan, and Abel Beth Maacah. The army also captures the land of Kinnereth, including the city of Kinnereth, the lake of Kinnereth, and the land south of the city. King Ben-Hadad's army also captures the land of the tribe of Naphtali.

In the ninth scene: King Baasha of Israel hears the news that Ben-Hadad has captured cities in northern Israel. King Baasha stops making Ramah stronger and abandons the work there. King Baasha goes back to live in the city of Tirzah. Then King Asa sends a message to all Judah. King Asa requires everyone to carry away from Ramah the stones and the wood that King Baasha used to make Ramah stronger. The men of Judah use these materials to make the cities of Geba and Mizpah stronger.

In the tenth scene: Now the storyteller sums up and judges the reign of King Jehoshaphat. The storyteller says that people record the other events during Asa's reign in the book called "History of the Kings of Judah." The people write about Asa's successes and accomplishments and about how Asa strengthens several cities in his land. However, when Asa is old, Asa develops a disease in his feet.

In the eleventh scene: Then Asa dies and goes to be with his ancestors. People bury Asa in the city of his ancestor, David, where people buried Asa's other ancestors. And Asa's son Jehoshaphat becomes king of Judah in his place.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Jeroboam, king of Israel
  • Asa, king of Judah
  • Maacah, Asa's grandmother
  • Yahweh
  • Male cult prostitutes
  • Asa's servants, or men
  • Ben-Hadad, king of Syria
  • Ben-Hadad's commanders and soldiers
  • All the men and women of Judah
  • And Jehoshaphat, son of Asa

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

The storyteller introduces the rule of King Asa of Judah. The storyteller follows the normal way to talk about a king's rule and says how long King Asa ruled and gives the name of Asa's mother. When Jeroboam has been king over Israel for 20 years, Asa becomes king over Judah. Asa rules in Jerusalem for 41 years. The storyteller says that Asa's mother's name is Maacah, and she is the daughter of Abishalom. However, we know from the previous passage that Maacah is the mother of Abijah, Asa's father. Therefore, Maacah must be Asa's grandmother. Some Bible translators refer to Maacah as Asa's mother and some as Asa's grandmother in this passage. We are not sure, but Maacah's father may be the same person as King David's son Absalom. Therefore, Abishalom and Absalom are likely the same person.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a map of Israel and Judah. Find Jerusalem, the capital of Judah. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and, as a group, decide whether you are going to say that Maacah was Asa's mother or grandmother. You should translate the name of Maacah's father, Abishalom, exactly as the storyteller told it. Pause this audio here.

King Asa does what is right in the sight of Yahweh. This means Asa does what Yahweh considers right, just like David, Asa's father, did. "Father" means ancestor here, not biological father.

King Asa drives out all the male cult prostitutes who live in the land of Judah. Cult prostitutes may be male or female. King Asa also removes all the idols that his "fathers," or ancestors, made. These idols include stones, poles, and altars that the people use to worship Canaanite false gods, mainly Baal and Asherah. Asa does not do these actions himself, but commands his officials, priests, and military officers to remove the male cult prostitutes and tear down the idols. The storyteller uses a word for idols that, in the original language, has a strong negative meaning and can also mean "dung." Some translators call these idols "very dirty idols." The word fathers here means ancestors—the kings of Judah before King Asa.

Stop here and, as a group, talk about the words in your language that mean "an idol." How do these words differ? Which one will suit this passage best? Pause this audio here.

King Asa removes his grandmother Maacah from being queen mother because Maacah makes a horrible image to worship the false goddess Asherah. The word "removes" in the original language means something very strong, like when people remove a ruler from power. The storyteller uses a special word for "image" that appears only one other time in the Bible, in 2 Chronicles 15:16. People do not know the exact meaning of this word, but many think it means something like a "horror." Some translators call it an "obscene image." The image of Asherah is a wooden pole. King Asa cuts down the idol that Maacah makes and burns the pieces in the Kidron Valley. King Asa likely gives the order, and his workers destroy the idol.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a map of Israel and Judah. Find the Kidron Brook near Jerusalem. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller now uses contrast language to show that Asa did do one thing that was bad: King Asa did not remove all the shrines on the hilltops where people worship other gods. Even so, King Asa fully commits his heart to Yahweh. This means that King Asa remains completely faithful to Yahweh all the days of King Asa's life.

King Asa puts holy gifts into Yahweh's temple. These gifts were silver, gold, and other treasures that King Asa and his father Abijah had promised to give to Yahweh.

The armies of Asa, king of Judah, and Baasha, king of Israel, are at war throughout their reigns. People have been fighting between the kingdom of Judah and the kingdom of Israel for a long time.

Stop here and, as a group, discuss the different words for "war" in your language. Which word for war would work best here? Pause this audio here.

Now the storyteller introduces one specific battle to show the nature of the fight between Israel and Judah. Baasha builds up the city of Ramah, which is about 8 kilometres north of Jerusalem. The city of Ramah already exists. Baasha captures Ramah and makes it stronger. Baasha strengthens the city walls and places many soldiers in Ramah. Now the storyteller explains why Baasha makes Ramah city stronger. Baasha does this to stop people from going in and out of Judah, where King Asa rules.

Stop here and, as a group, find the city of Ramah on the map. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller starts the next sentence with a word that shows these events happen next or happen because of what Baasha did. Then King Asa takes the silver and gold that remain in the treasuries in Yahweh's temple and in King Asa's palace. Asa likely does not take the gold himself but asks his officials to do that. King Asa gives the silver and gold to some of his servants and sends it to Ben-Hadad. Ben-Hadad is the son of Tabrimmon, who is the son of Hezion. Ben-Hadad is the king of Syria and rules from Damascus, the capital city of Syria. The silver and gold are not coins, but lumps of metal or sacred objects from the temple, such as bowls, cups, or decorations. People did not use coins at that time. King Asa tells his men to tell Ben-Hadad to make a covenant, or a serious agreement to help each other in wars, just like the agreement the father of Asa made with the father of Ben-Hadad. The storyteller uses a word to draw Ben-Hadad's attention to the generous gift of silver and gold that Asa is sending to Ben-Hadad. King Asa also asks Ben-Hadad to break, or cancel, his agreement with King Baasha of Israel and fight against King Baasha so that Baasha will leave the land of King Asa.

Stop here and, as a group, locate Syria and the city of Damascus on the map. Pause this audio here.

King Ben-Hadad agrees to King Asa's plan and does more than King Asa asks. King Ben-Hadad sends commanders and soldiers to attack towns in the northern kingdom of Israel. The army captures the cities of Ijon, Dan, and Abel Beth Maacah. People called the city of Abel Beth Maacah "Abel Maim" and "Abel" in other parts of the Bible. The army also captures the land of Kinnereth, including the city of Kinnereth, the lake of Kinnereth, and the land south of the city. Lake Kinnereth is another name for the Lake of Galilee. The cities of Ijon, Dan, Abel Beth Maacah, and Kinnereth are on an important international road that King Baasha needs for trade, travel, and defence. King Ben-Hadad's army also captures the land of the tribe of Naphtali.

Stop here and, as a group, find the cities of Ijon, Dan, Abel Beth Maacah, the land of Kinnereth, and the land of the tribe of Naphtali on the map. Pause this audio here.

As soon as King Baasha of Israel hears this news, King Baasha stops making Ramah stronger and abandons the work there. King Baasha goes back to live in the city of Tirzah. Then King Asa sends a message to all Judah. The words for "sends a message" in the original language mean that King Asa causes everyone to hear him. King Asa requires everyone to carry away from Ramah the stones and the wood that King Baasha used to fortify Ramah. The men of Judah use these materials to make the cities of Geba and Mizpah stronger.

Stop here and, as a group, find the cities of Tirzah, Geba, and Mizpah on the map. Pause this audio here.

Now the storyteller sums up and judges the reign of King Asa. The storyteller says that people record the other events during Asa's reign in the book called "History of the Kings of Judah." The people write about the good things that Asa does and about how Asa strengthens several cities in his land. This book records the actions of the kings of Judah during their reigns. Everything the storyteller mentions so far about Asa's reign shows that Yahweh is pleased with Asa. Now the storyteller talks about something that shows Yahweh's disapproval of Asa. The storyteller uses words that contrast the earlier time of blessing with the new time of disapproval. However, when Asa is old, Asa develops a disease in his feet.

Then Asa dies and sleeps with his fathers, which means Asa goes to be with his ancestors. People bury Asa in the City of David, Asa's father, where people bury Asa's other fathers. In all these cases, the word "father" means ancestor. Then Asa's son Jehoshaphat becomes king of Judah in his place.

Stop here and, as a group, locate the City of David on a map of Jerusalem. Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 15:9–24 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has 11 scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Jeroboam, king of Israel
  • Asa, king of Judah
  • Maacah, Asa's grandmother
  • Yahweh
  • Male cult prostitutes
  • Asa's servants, or men
  • Ben-Hadad, king of Syria
  • Ben-Hadad's commanders and soldiers
  • All the men and women of Judah
  • And Jehoshaphat, son of Asa

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

When Jeroboam has been king over Israel for 20 years, Asa becomes king over Judah. Asa rules in Jerusalem for 41 years. Asa's mother's name is Maacah. Maacah's father's name is Abishalom.

King Asa does what Yahweh thinks is right, just like his ancestor King David did. King Asa drives out all the male cult prostitutes who live in the land of Judah. King Asa also removes all the filthy idols that the kings of Judah had made before King Asa.

King Asa removes his grandmother Maacah from being queen mother because Maacah made a horrible idol to worship the false goddess Asherah. King Asa cuts down the idol Maacah made and burns the pieces at the Kidron Brook in the Kidron Valley.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King Asa, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am trying my best to act in a way that pleases Yahweh, my God; or
  • I feel angry that the cult prostitutes and idols are still in Judah. I will get rid of them all; or
  • I feel shame that my own grandmother, the queen mother, made a horrible idol to worship the false goddess Asherah. My grandmother will no longer be queen mother.

Continue the drama.

However, King Asa does not remove all the shrines on the hilltops where people worship other gods. Even so, King Asa remains completely faithful to Yahweh all the days of King Asa's life.

King Asa puts holy gifts into Yahweh's temple. These gifts are silver, gold, and other treasures that King Asa and his father Abijah had promised to give to Yahweh.

The armies of Asa, king of Judah, and Baasha, king of Israel, are at war throughout their reigns. Baasha captures Ramah city and makes it stronger to stop people from going in and out of Judah, where King Asa rules.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King Baasha, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel threatened. I am worried that Judah is becoming a powerful country because Yahweh is blessing them; or
  • I feel angry at Judah. We are enemies, and I want to keep control over the border between our countries; or
  • I feel confident and powerful. Because we captured and strengthened Rama, people will not be able to travel in and out of Judah. This will weaken Judah and protect my kingdom.

Continue the drama.

Then King Asa takes the silver and gold that remain in the treasuries in Yahweh's temple and in King Asa's palace. King Asa sends some of his men with the silver and gold to Ben-Hadad, the king of Syria, who rules from Damascus. King Asa sends a message to Ben-Hadad. Asa suggests that Asa and Ben-Hadad make a covenant, or a serious agreement to help each other in wars, just like the agreement the father of Asa made with the father of Ben-Hadad. Asa draws Ben-Hadad's attention to the generous gift of silver and gold that Asa is sending to Ben-Hadad. King Asa asks Ben-Hadad to break, or cancel, his agreement with King Baasha of Israel. Asa wants Ben-Hadad to fight against King Baasha so that Baasha will leave the land of King Asa.

King Ben-Hadad agrees to King Asa's plan, and King Ben-Hadad sends commanders and soldiers to attack towns in the northern kingdom of Israel. The army captures the cities of Ijon, Dan, and Abel Beth Maacah. The army also captures the land of Kinnereth, including the city of Kinnereth, the lake of Kinnereth, and the land south of the city. King Ben-Hadad's army also captures the land of the tribe of Naphtali.

King Baasha of Israel hears the news that Ben-Hadad has captured cities in northern Israel. King Baasha stops fortifying Ramah and abandons the work there. King Baasha goes back to live in the city of Tirzah. Then King Asa sends a message to all Judah. King Asa requires everyone, without exception, to carry away from Ramah the stones and the wood that King Baasha used to fortify Ramah. The men of Judah use these materials to make the cities of Geba and Mizpah stronger.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King Ben-Hadad of Syria, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel happy because King Asa of Judah came to me to ask for help. Asa gave me silver and gold; or
  • I feel strong because I am going to attack Israel. This shows how powerful Syria is; or
  • I feel in control. I captured important cities in northern Israel, and as a result, King Baasha abandoned Ramah city. King Asa is pleased.

Continue the drama.

Now the storyteller sums up and evaluates the reign of King Asa. The storyteller says that people record the other events during Asa's reign in the book called "History of the Kings of Judah." The people write about Asa's successes and accomplishments and about how Asa strengthens several cities in his land. However, when Asa is old, Asa develops a disease in his feet.

Then Asa dies and goes to be with his ancestors. People bury Asa in the city of his ancestor, David, where people buried Asa's other ancestors. And Asa's son Jehoshaphat becomes king of Judah in his place.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel happy with King Asa because he did what is right, and Asa obeyed me; or
  • I feel sad about King Baasha because he did evil and did not obey me; or
  • I feel disappointed that Asa did not ask me for help to defeat Baasha. Asa made an agreement with the foreign king Ben-Hadad instead.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 15:9–24 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

When Jeroboam has been king over Israel for 20 years, Asa becomes king over Judah. Use the same words or phrases for king, Israel, and Judah as you used in previous passages. For more information on king, Israel, and Judah, refer to the Master Glossary.

King Asa does what is right in the sight of Yahweh. This means Asa does what Yahweh considers right, just like David, Asa's father, did. Translate "does what is right in the sight of Yahweh" like you did in previous passages.

Yahweh is God's personal name. Use the same name for Yahweh as you used in previous passages. For more information on Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary.

Father means ancestor here, not biological father. Use the same word for "father" when it means ancestor as you used in previous passages.

King Asa chases away all the male cult prostitutes who live in the land of Judah. King Asa also removes all the horrible idols that the kings of Judah had made before King Asa. Use the same words or phrases for male cult prostitute and idol as you used in previous passages. For more information on idol, refer to the Master Glossary.

Stop here and, as a group, discuss whether you will describe the idol as horrible or dirty. Pause this audio here.

King Asa removes his grandmother Maacah from being queen mother because Maacah makes a horrible image to worship the false goddess Asherah.

Stop here and, as a group, discuss how you will translate horrible image. If you already translated 2 Chronicles 15:16, check how you translated "horrible image" there. Pause this audio here.

However, King Asa does not remove all the shrines on the hilltops where people worship other gods. Use the same words or phrases for shrines on hilltops as you used in previous passages.

Even so, King Asa fully commits his heart to Yahweh. Use the same word or phrase for heart as you used in previous passages. For more information on heart, refer to the Master Glossary.

King Asa responds to Baasha's capture of Ramah city. Asa takes the silver and gold that remain in the treasuries in Yahweh's temple and in King Asa's palace. King Asa gives the silver and gold to some of his servants and sends it to Ben-Hadad, King of Syria. King Asa tells Ben-Hadad that they should make a covenant, or a serious agreement to help each other in wars, just like the covenant that the father of Asa made with the father of Ben-Hadad. Use the same words or phrases for temple, servants, and covenant as you used in previous passages. For more information on temple, servants, and covenant, refer to the Master Glossary.

People record the other events of Asa's reign in the book called "History of the Kings of Judah."

Translate "book, or scroll, called the History of the Kings of Judah" as you did before. Scroll is in the Master Glossary.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 15:25–32
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 15:25–31 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 15:25–31 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 15:25–31 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

The last few stories have been about the kings of Judah. The storyteller now begins a section about the kings of Israel. This is an unstable time in Israel. While King Asa rules in Judah, five men will replace each other as king in Israel, and most of these men will die in a violent way. The first king is Nadab. While Nadab is king, Baasha kills Nadab in order to take Nadab's place as king.

Remember that Jeroboam was the first king of Israel after Israel and Judah became two kingdoms. When Jeroboam died, Jeroboam's son, Nadab, became the next king of Israel. At this time, Asa is in his second year as king of Judah. Nadab rules Israel for two years, and Nadab acts in a way that displeases Yahweh. Nadab sins as Jeroboam had sinned. Jeroboam had led the Israelites in such a way that the Israelites willingly chose to sin as well. One way Jeroboam sinned was that he worshiped a golden statue of a calf.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: As an activity, tell a story about something that happened in your people group's history. Then tell a story about something that happened at the same time with another people group or in another area. Pay attention to the way you show that these two stories happen at similar times. Pause this audio here.

In his second year as king, Nadab attempts to capture the Philistine city of Gibbethon. However, Baasha, an Israelite man who is the son of a man named Ahijah, plans to kill Nadab. So while Nadab and the Israelite army surround Gibbethon, Baasha kills Nadab.

Baasha kills Nadab and becomes the next king of Israel while Asa is still king of Judah. Once Baasha is king, Baasha immediately kills all of Jeroboam's family. Baasha probably kills Jeroboam's family to make sure that no one will try to take revenge on Baasha or try to take the throne from Baasha. This happens just as Yahweh said it would happen. Remember that Yahweh had told Ahijah, the prophet from Shiloh, to tell Jeroboam that Jeroboam's family would die because of how Jeroboam sinned. Yahweh, Israel's God, was angry because of the way Jeroboam had sinned. Not only had Jeroboam sinned, but Jeroboam had also caused the Israelites to sin as well.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story about a time in your history or in the history of a nearby country when someone violently took over as leader. How did the new leader make sure that no one would try to stop the new leader? What do people think about a leader who takes over and rules like this? Pause this audio here.

Everything else Nadab did as king is in the book or scroll that has the history of the kings of Israel. This story shows that Yahweh does what Yahweh says he will do. If people sin, Yahweh will punish those people.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 15:25–31 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has four scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller formally introduces Nadab and how Nadab rules.

In the second scene: Baasha kills Nadab and becomes king.

In the third scene: Baasha kills Jeroboam's family, just like Yahweh said would happen.

In the fourth scene: The storyteller formally ends Nadab's story.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Nadab
  • Jeroboam
  • Asa, king of Judah
  • Yahweh
  • The people of Israel, including the army
  • Baasha, Ahijah's son
  • Jeroboam's family
  • And Yahweh's prophet, Ahijah

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, the storyteller uses the same formal language to introduce Nadab, the king of Israel, as the storyteller did for Asa, Abijah, and Rehoboam. The storyteller explains who Nadab's father is, who rules in Judah when Nadab becomes king, how long Nadab rules, and how Nadab rules.

Stop here and review how you translated the introduction to the kings of Judah. Pause this audio here.

When the storyteller says that Nadab rules for two years, the storyteller means that Nadab rules during two different years. Nadab becomes king in the second year that Asa is king in Judah, and dies in the third year that Asa is king of Judah. In this time, people would often count parts of years as whole years. It is possible that Nadab became king at the very beginning of one year and died at the very end of the next year, and so may have ruled for almost two years. It is also possible that Nadab reigned for fewer than two full years.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Imagine that something begins during one year and ends during the next year, but you are not sure exactly when during the year it began or ended. In your language, how would you talk about that amount of time? Pause this audio here.

Nadab does evil in the eyes of Yahweh, which means that Nadab does what Yahweh says is evil. The storyteller has used this phrase before when he told the stories of Rehoboam and Solomon. The storyteller will continue to use this phrase for each of the next few kings, so you will want to translate this phrase in the same way throughout all of the stories.

Stop here and review how you translated the phrase "did evil in Yahweh's eyes" or "acted in an evil way." Pause this audio here.

Nadab follows his father Jeroboam's example and sins, like someone follows another person on a path.

The second scene is about how Baasha kills Nadab. In Nadab's second year as king, Nadab tries to take over the Philistine town of Gibbethon. All Israel, which is all of Nadab's army, goes with Nadab and besieges Gibbethon. This is the way an army surrounds a town in order to defeat the people in the town and take control of the town. Gibbethon is probably about 20 kilometers east of the Mediterranean Sea on the western edge of the Judean hills. One of the Israelite tribes did not conquer this town like they should have when the Israelites first arrived in this land.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a map of Israel and Philistia. Find the town of Gibbethon. Pause this audio here.

Baasha plots against or makes plans to harm Nadab. Baasha's father is Ahijah, who is a different man from Ahijah the prophet. Baasha is from the house or tribe of Issachar. While Nadab is outside Gibbethon, Baasha strikes Nadab down. To strike someone down means to kill that person, since a person who stands will fall to the ground if someone kills him. The storyteller then explains that Baasha killed Nadab and became king in place of Nadab during Asa's third year as king of Judah.

The third scene happens right after Baasha becomes king. Baasha immediately kills Jeroboam's family. Baasha probably does not do this all himself, but instead has other men help Baasha do this. The storyteller says three times, in three different ways, that Baasha kills Jeroboam's family. The storyteller says that Baasha kills Jeroboam's family and destroys Jeroboam's family. The storyteller also says that there is no one left in Jeroboam's family who breathes. No one in Jeroboam's family breathes because all of Jeroboam's family is dead. The storyteller talks about what Baasha does three times to emphasize that Baasha definitely kills everyone in Jeroboam's family. Yahweh had given Ahijah the prophet a word or message about how this would happen, and it did happen just like Yahweh said it would happen. The way Jeroboam acted had angered Yahweh. The storyteller reminds us that Yahweh is Israel's God, whom the Israelites should have worshiped. But the Israelites did not worship Yahweh during Jeroboam's time as king.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your language, how do you emphasize that something really happened, especially something terrible? What different phrases do you have for the way that someone kills someone else? Pause this audio here.

In the fourth scene, the storyteller again uses the same formal language to finish Nadab's story as the storyteller used for Abijah and Rehoboam's stories. The storyteller has just included some parts of Nadab's story here, but everything else Nadab did as king is in another book. In the original language, this statement is a question, but the storyteller just means to emphasize that this is really true.

Stop here and review how you translated the end of the stories about the kings of Judah. The storyteller tells the end of stories about the kings of Israel in the same way. Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 15:25–31 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has four scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Nadab
  • Jeroboam
  • Asa, king of Judah
  • Yahweh
  • The people of Israel, including the army
  • Baasha, Ahijah's son
  • Jeroboam's family
  • And Yahweh's prophet, Ahijah

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Jeroboam's son Nadab began to rule over Israel during Asa's second year as king of Judah. Nadab was king of Israel for two years. Nadab acted in an evil way that did not please Yahweh. Nadab sinned just like his father, Jeroboam, had sinned. Jeroboam had caused the people of Israel to sin, and Nadab sinned in the same way.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Nadab, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I respect my father and act in the same way that he lived; or
  • I did not learn about Yahweh as I grew up, and I do not care about Yahweh enough to learn about him now; or
  • I love that I am king and I can do anything I want!

Continue the drama.

Baasha, the son of Ahijah from the tribe of Issachar, made plans to get rid of Nadab. Baasha killed Nadab at Gibbethon, which is a Philistine town. At that time, Nadab and his army had surrounded the town of Gibbethon in order to capture it. Baasha killed Nadab in the third year of Asa, king of Judah. Then Baasha ruled as king.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Baasha, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I do not like Nadab and how Nadab rules. I would be a better king than Nadab; or
  • Our king does not have to come from a special family. I remember that Jeroboam became king of Israel even though he was not from Solomon's family; or
  • I am happy that my plan worked and that I am king. Now I can do whatever I want.

Continue the drama.

As soon as Baasha began to rule, Baasha killed everyone in Jeroboam's family. No one in Jeroboam's family survived. Baasha killed them all. This is exactly what Yahweh had said would happen through his servant Ahijah of Shiloh. This happened because Jeroboam sinned and because Jeroboam caused Israel to sin. Jeroboam had angered Yahweh, the God of Israel.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I know what will happen because Jeroboam sinned. Because Jeroboam did not lead the Israelites to honor me and live in a good way, the Israelites will act in evil ways. Baasha will act in an evil way and kill Jeroboam's family. I am sad that the Israelites hurt each other when they sin; or
  • I still care for the Israelites, even when they act in an evil way. I will warn Jeroboam about what will happen; or
  • I told the Israelites that the way they sin will affect their children and grandchildren.

Continue the drama.

The other things that happened while Nadab was king, and all that Nadab did, are in the book that has the history of the kings of Israel.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the people of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel nervous that our new king killed our old king. I am worried that our new king will continue to act in a violent way; or
  • I was surprised when I heard that Baasha killed Nadab and all of Jeroboam's family. ; or
  • My parents followed the example of Jeroboam and acted as he did. Our king is our leader, so I will act as he does.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 15:25–31 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Nadab, the son of Jeroboam, becomes king of Israel. For more information on son and king, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same words or phrases for son and king as you used in previous passages.

Here, Israel means the northern kingdom. When the storyteller says that all Israel is at Gibbethon, the storyteller means the whole army of people who live in the northern kingdom of Israel. For more information on Israel, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for Israel as you used in previous passages.

Asa is king of Judah while Nadab rules in Israel. Judah is the southern kingdom. Use the same word or phrase for Judah as you used in previous passages, and refer to Judah in the Master Glossary if needed.

Nadab acts in an evil way in the eyes of Yahweh. Evil is the opposite of good. For more information on evil, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same words or phrases for evil and acting "in the eyes of Yahweh" as you used in previous passages.

The name Yahweh is a very personal name for God that shows the close relationship that God had with the people of Israel. For more information on Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for Yahweh as you used in previous passages.

Nadab sins like Jeroboam sinned. Sin is an act of rebellion against God. Anytime that a person disobeys God's laws, that person sins. For more information on sin, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for sin as you used in previous passages.

Nadab "besieges" or attacks Gibbethon with a siege. A city usually had thick walls to keep attackers out of the city. The attackers would surround the city so that the people inside could not come out to get food, water, and other supplies. The people inside the city would have to either starve, surrender, or fight the enemy to escape. For more information on siege, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for siege as you used in previous passages.

Baasha, who is from the house of Issachar, kills everyone in the house of Jeroboam. People sometimes used the word household, or house, to mean a large group of people who did not live in the same house, but who had something in common. So the house of Jeroboam refers not to a building but to the people who are in Jeroboam's family. This includes all of Jeroboam's descendants and probably most of his relatives as well. The house of Issachar is the tribe of Issachar, who are all Issachar's descendants. For more information on house, refer to "household" in the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for house when the storyteller uses it to mean a large family group as you used in previous passages.

Ahijah the prophet received the word of Yahweh about what would happen to Jeroboam's family. The word of Yahweh means what Yahweh tells people. For more information on the word of Yahweh, refer to "word" in the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for the "word of Yahweh" as you used in previous passages.

Ahijah is Yahweh's servant or prophet. A servant is a person who serves someone else. People would often call prophets Yahweh's servants, and the storyteller calls Ahijah Yahweh's servant here. A prophet is a person who gives messages from God to the people. For more information on servant or prophet, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for servant as you used in previous passages.

Jeroboam had provoked, or caused, Yahweh to be angry. Use the same word or phrase for provoked as you have in previous passages. Yahweh is Israel's God. For more information on God, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for God as you used in previous passages.

Nadab's story is also in the book or scroll that tells the history of the kings of Israel. A scroll is a long strip of material on which people would write down stories. Because those sheets were very long, people rolled them up around a stick. For more information on scroll, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for scroll or book as you used in previous passages.

Stop here and look at a picture of a scroll again as a group as needed.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 15:33–16:7
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 15:32–16:7 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 15:32–16:7 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 15:32–16:7 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the last story, Baasha killed King Nadab, and Baasha became king. This story is about how Baasha rules as king. Baasha sins, just like Nadab and Jeroboam had sinned. So Yahweh gives a prophet a message for Baasha. Because of how Baasha has acted, Yahweh will punish Baasha like Yahweh punished Jeroboam. All of Baasha's family will die.

Baasha, son of Ahijah, becomes king while Asa is in his third year as king of Judah. The whole time Baasha rules Israel, the Israelites fight with the people of Judah. Baasha rules for 24 years from the city of Tirzah. Remember that Jeroboam had chosen Shechem as his capital city, but now Baasha rules from Tirzah. Tirzah is about 11 kilometres northeast of Shechem. Baasha acts in a way that displeases Yahweh. Like Jeroboam, Baasha sins and leads the Israelites in such a way that the Israelites willingly choose to sin as well.

Stop here and look at a map of Israel as a group. Find the towns of Shechem and Tirzah. Pause this audio here.

Yahweh speaks to a prophet named Jehu, who is Hanani's son. Yahweh's message is for Baasha, and it is very similar to Yahweh's message for Jeroboam. Yahweh says that Baasha used to be a man without power or influence, but Yahweh chose Baasha to lead Israel. However, Baasha has not led well. Baasha has followed the example of Jeroboam and has caused the Israelites to sin. This has made Yahweh angry. Yahweh will completely destroy Baasha and Baasha's family. Someone will kill Baasha's family, just like Baasha killed Jeroboam's family. No matter where someone from Baasha's family dies, no one will bury that person's body. If someone from Baasha's family dies in a city, dogs will eat that person's dead body. If someone from Baasha's family dies outside the cities, birds that eat meat will eat that person's dead body.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your area, what are some animals that eat dead things? How do people feel about these animals? Pause this audio here.

It was very important during this time for someone to bury a dead person's body. It was very shameful for the dead person if no one buried that person's body. People often believed that if no one buried a dead person's body, this was a sign that God was unhappy with that person and wanted to punish that person.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, what is a respectful way to treat a dead body? What do people think about a body that no one buries? How would you feel if someone did not bury the body of someone you know? Pause this audio here.

Everything else Baasha did as king, including his actions as a military leader, is in the book or scroll that tells the history of the kings of Israel. Baasha dies, and people bury him in a place that is close to where they buried Baasha's ancestors in Tirzah. Baasha's son Elah then becomes king.

Yahweh spoke to Jehu, Yahweh's prophet, about Baasha because of the evil things Baasha had done. Like Jeroboam, Yahweh chose Baasha to lead Israel. Like Jeroboam, Yahweh gave Baasha the opportunity to honor Yahweh as Israel's true king when Baasha killed the previous king and began to rule in that king's place. Remember that Yahweh said that all of Jeroboam's male descendants will die. You will remember from the previous story that Baasha chose to kill Nadab and all of Jeroboam's descendants, both male and female. But then Baasha does not act any better than Nadab and Jeroboam. Baasha sins and does not honor Yahweh as Israel's true king. Yahweh still punished Jeroboam and caused Jeroboam's male descendants to die, just as Yahweh planned, despite the way that Baasha sinned. But because Baasha sinned, Yahweh will punish Baasha too.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story about a time when you saw how God did amazing things, even through people who did not worship or honor God.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 15:32–16:7 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has four scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller formally introduces Baasha and how Baasha rules.

In the second scene: Yahweh gives Jehu a message to tell Baasha. Yahweh says that because Baasha has sinned, Baasha and his entire family will die.

In the third scene: The storyteller formally ends Baasha's story.

In the fourth scene: The storyteller repeats that Yahweh gave Jehu this message because of the evil way Baasha acted.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Baasha, Ahijah's son
  • Asa, king of Judah
  • The people of Israel, the northern kingdom
  • Yahweh
  • Jeroboam, Nebat's son
  • Jeroboam's family
  • Jehu, Hanani's son
  • Baasha's family
  • And Elah, Baasha's son

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, the storyteller tells us that Baasha and the Israelites are at war with Asa and the people of Judah while both Baasha and Asa reign.

Though the storyteller has already talked about Baasha, the storyteller still uses the same formal language to introduce Baasha as the storyteller used for Nadab's story. In the first scene, the storyteller explains who Baasha's father is, who rules in Judah when Baasha becomes king, how long Baasha rules, and how Baasha rules. Remember that Baasha's father Ahijah is a different man from the prophet Ahijah in the last story.

Stop here and review how you translated the introduction to Nadab's story. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller has already told how Baasha fought with Asa in Asa's story and how Baasha became king during Nadab's story. The storyteller does not repeat those parts of Baasha's story. Here, the storyteller focuses on how Baasha sins and how Yahweh responds to Baasha.

Baasha becomes king of all Israel, which means all of the northern kingdom of Israel. Baasha rules from the city of Tirzah for 24 years.

Baasha does evil in the eyes of Yahweh, which means that Baasha does what Yahweh says is evil. The storyteller uses the same phrase that he used for Nadab. Baasha also follows Jeroboam's example and sins, like someone follows another person on a path.

Stop here and review how you translated the phrase "did evil in Yahweh's eyes" or "acted in an evil way." Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, Yahweh talks to Jehu, Yahweh's prophet. Once Yahweh finishes speaking, Jehu goes and repeats the message to Baasha. Sometimes Yahweh talks about "Baasha's family," and sometimes Yahweh speaks about "your family." But the whole time, Yahweh speaks to Baasha in this message.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your language, when are some times when you might change how you talk to a person, like Yahweh talks to Baasha here? If you do not talk like this in your language, you can follow the example of some translations that show Yahweh talking in just one way to Baasha. Pause this audio here.

Yahweh says that Yahweh "exalted" or lifted Baasha out of the dust. This is a poetic way to say that Baasha was not an important person until Yahweh made Baasha the ruler of Israel. Notice that Yahweh uses a different word for ruler than the word for king. Yahweh would often use this word for ruler or leader to mean someone Yahweh chooses to lead. This kind of ruler should understand that Yahweh is Israel's true king.

Stop here and discuss as a group how you will talk about how Yahweh lifts Baasha out of the dust and how Yahweh chooses Baasha to be ruler. Pause this audio here.

Notice how Yahweh calls Israel his people even though the Israelites sin. However, Yahweh is angry that his people sin, so Yahweh says Yahweh will burn or utterly sweep away Baasha and his house. This means Yahweh will completely destroy Baasha's household in the way that a fire or a flood completely destroys something. Baasha's family will die as Jeroboam's family did. Yahweh describes the way that no one will bury the dead bodies in the same way that Yahweh did in Yahweh's message to Jeroboam.

Stop here and review how you translated Yahweh's message to Jeroboam in 1 Kings 14:11, when Yahweh talked about how animals would eat the dead bodies. You should translate how Yahweh talks to Baasha in the same way here. Pause this audio here.

In the third scene, the storyteller uses formal language to finish Baasha's story. The storyteller has just included some parts of Baasha's story here, but everything else Baasha did as king is in another book. Notice this time that the storyteller says here that Baasha's achievements or mighty deeds are also in that other book. These mighty deeds are probably how Baasha fought and led during battles.

Stop here and review how you translated the end of Nadab's story. You will want to use the same phrases here, but include the storyteller's comment about Baasha's military achievements. In your language, how do you talk about a king's military achievements? Pause this audio here.

Baasha sleeps with his ancestors, which is a common polite phrase that means Baasha dies. People bury Baasha's body in Tirzah. Then Baasha's son, Elah, becomes king. These phrases are like the ones the storyteller used for the end of Asa's story, so you can translate this part in the same way you did earlier.

Stop here and review how you translated the end of Asa's story in 1 Kings 15:24. Pause this audio here.

In the fourth scene, the storyteller repeats why Yahweh sent this message to Jehu to emphasize how important the message was. The storyteller also gives some more information about one way Baasha sinned. Baasha made Yahweh angry not only because Baasha acted like Jeroboam but because Baasha killed Jeroboam's family. This seems surprising, since Yahweh said that Yahweh would punish Jeroboam's family in this way. However, Baasha killed not just the men, but the women as well. And Baasha did not kill Jeroboam's family in order to honor Yahweh. We can see this because Baasha sins as Jeroboam did. Baasha just killed Jeroboam's family because Baasha wanted to become king and continue to sin.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 15:32–16:7 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has four scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Baasha, Ahijah's son
  • Asa, king of Judah
  • The people of Israel, the northern kingdom
  • Yahweh
  • Jeroboam, Nebat's son
  • Jeroboam's family
  • Jehu, Hanani's son
  • Baasha's family
  • And Elah, Baasha's son

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

All the time that Asa ruled Judah and Baasha ruled Israel, their armies continued to fight each other. Ahijah's son Baasha began to rule over Israel during Asa's third year as king of Judah. Baasha ruled from Tirzah and was king of Israel for 24 years. Baasha acted in an evil way that did not please Yahweh. Baasha sinned just like Jeroboam had sinned. Jeroboam had caused the people of Israel to sin, and Baasha sinned in the same way.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Baasha, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Now that I am king, I can do whatever I want; or
  • I think that I act in the right way, so I will lead the Israelites to act in the same way; or
  • I am a better king than Nadab. Look how long I have been able to rule!

Continue the drama.

Then Yahweh gave this message to Jehu, son of Hanani, to give to Baasha: "I made you the leader of my people, the people of Israel, although you were nobody important. But you have lived in the same evil way as Jeroboam. You have caused my people Israel to sin. This has made me very angry! So I am going to destroy you, Baasha, and your family line. I brought to an end the family of Nebat's son Jeroboam. Now I will do the same to your family! If your family members die in the city, dogs will eat their bodies. If your family members die in the open fields, birds will eat their bodies."

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Jehu, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I felt angry when I saw how Baasha sinned and led the Israelites to sin. I wanted Baasha to stop! or
  • I am thankful that Yahweh will act in a just way and punish Baasha; or
  • I am nervous to give this message to the king. The king may be very angry when he hears this!

Continue the drama.

The other things that happened while Baasha was king, and all that Baasha did and all Baasha's mighty deeds, are in the book that tells the history of the kings of Israel. Baasha died, and people buried his body in Tirzah. Then Baasha's son Elah became king in Baasha's place.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Elah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am sad my father is dead; or
  • I heard the message that the prophet gave to my father. I am worried about what will happen while I am king; or
  • I am excited to be king and rule. Perhaps I will rule like my father did!

Continue the drama.

Yahweh gave this message to Baasha and Baasha's family through the prophet Jehu, son of Hanani, because Baasha acted in an evil way that did not please Yahweh. Baasha had made Yahweh very angry by the way Baasha acted. Baasha killed all of Jeroboam's family and then became just like Jeroboam!

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am sad and angry that Baasha chose to sin. I will punish Baasha for the bad way he has led Israel; or
  • Sometimes I work through one person to punish another person. However, the person who does my work must do it in the right way and for the right reason; or
  • I am sad that my people continue to sin. Yet I love my people and will not immediately punish them all. I will continue to give my people more opportunities to obey me.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 15:32–16:7 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Baasha becomes king of Israel. For more information on king, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for king as you used in previous passages.

Here, Israel means the northern kingdom. For more information on Israel, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for Israel as you used in previous passages.

Asa is the king of Judah, while Baasha rules in Israel. Judah is the southern kingdom. Use the same word or phrase for Judah as you used in previous passages.

Baasha acts in an evil way in the eyes of Yahweh. Evil is the opposite of good. For more information on evil, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for evil and "in the eyes of Yahweh" as you used in previous passages.

The name Yahweh is a very personal name for God that shows the close relationship that God had with the people of Israel. For more information on Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for Yahweh as you used in previous passages.

Baasha sins like Jeroboam sinned. Sin is an act of rebellion against God. Anytime that a person disobeys God's laws, that person sins. For more information on sin, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for sin as you used in previous passages.

Jehu received the word of Yahweh about what would happen to Baasha's family. The word of Yahweh means what Yahweh tells people. For more information on the word of Yahweh, refer to "word" in the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for the "word of Yahweh" as you used in previous passages.

Yahweh appointed Baasha as a ruler over Israel. To appoint means to assign a job or role to someone and give them authority to act in a certain way.

Yahweh appointed Baasha a ruler, or chosen leader. In the original language, this is not the same word as king. This means that Yahweh will still be the highest ruler, and this man will be second to Yahweh. Use the same word or phrase for ruler or chosen leader as you used in previous passages.

Everyone in the house of Baasha will die. People sometimes used the word household or house to mean a large group of people who did not live in the same house but who had something in common. So the house of Baasha is the people who are in Baasha's family. For more information on house, refer to "household" in the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for house as you used in previous passages.

Birds of the air or of the heavens will eat the dead bodies. Here, heaven means the visible sky, where birds fly. Some translations do not include this word since "birds of the heavens" is just another way to say "birds." For more information on heaven, refer to the Master Glossary. If you chose to use the word heavens in your translation, use the same word or phrase for heaven as you used in Yahweh's message to Jeroboam.

Baasha's story is also in the book or scroll that tells the history of the kings of Israel. A scroll is a long strip of material on which people would write down stories. Because those sheets were very long, people rolled them up around a stick. For more information, refer to "scroll" in the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for scroll or book as you used in previous passages.

Stop here and look at a picture of a scroll again as a group as needed. Pause this audio here.

Jehu is a prophet. A prophet is a person who gives messages from God to the people. For more information on prophet, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for prophet as you used in previous passages.

Baasha's actions also provoked, or caused, Yahweh to be angry. Use the same word or phrase for provoked as you have in previous passages.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 16:8–14
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 16:8–14 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 16:8–14 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 16:8–14 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

The last story was about how Baasha ruled as king of Israel. Because of how Baasha sinned, Yahweh said that all of Baasha's family would die. This story is about King Elah, Baasha's son. While Elah is king, a man named Zimri kills Elah in order to take Elah's place as king. Then Zimri kills all of Baasha's family, just like Yahweh said would happen.

Elah, son of Baasha, becomes king of Israel while Asa is in his 26th year as king of Judah. Elah rules for two years from the city of Tirzah. Remember that Tirzah is the capital city, or the city where the kings of Israel live and rule from.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a map of Israel that shows the city of Tirzah, as needed. Pause this audio here.

Elah has a military officer whose name is Zimri. Zimri is in charge of half of Elah's chariots. Chariots were wagon-like vehicles with two wheels. Horses pulled the chariots and two men rode in each chariot. One of these men drove the chariot while the other man fought as a warrior.

Stop here and look at a picture of a chariot as a group. Pause this audio here.

Zimri plans to kill King Elah. One day, Elah is in Tirzah at the house of Arza. Arza is in charge of Elah's palace, which is the house where the king lives. Arza probably takes care of the palace building, the people in the palace, and the king's property. Elah drinks alcohol with Arza and Elah becomes drunk.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, what kind of alcoholic drinks do people drink? At what times or in what ways is it appropriate to drink alcohol, and when is it not appropriate? What would people in your culture think about a leader who drinks alcohol until they are drunk? Pause this audio here.

Zimri comes into Arza's house and kills Elah. Then Zimri becomes king. Zimri does this in Asa's 27th year as king of Judah.

Zimri sits on the throne, or the special seat for a king. This is another way to say Zimri makes himself king. And once Zimri is king, Zimri then kills Baasha's family. Zimri does not kill the female members of Baasha's family, but Zimri does kill all of the male relatives. In this time, if someone killed another person, people expected the relatives of the dead person to take revenge on the killer. So in this way, Zimri makes sure that no one in Baasha's family can take revenge. Zimri also kills the old king's friends who might rebel against Zimri. Zimri kills everyone who might try to kill Zimri.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, what is the special seat or place where a king sits? What are some things that a man does to show that he has become king, such as wear certain clothes or live in a certain place? Pause this audio here.

This is exactly what Yahweh had said would happen because Baasha and his son Elah had both sinned. Baasha and Elah also caused Israel to sin and led the Israelites to worship idols or false gods. This made Yahweh very angry.

Everything else Elah did as king is in the book or scroll that has the history of the kings of Israel.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 16:8–14 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has four scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller formally introduces Elah.

In the second scene: Zimri kills Elah and becomes king.

In the third scene: Zimri kills Baasha's family and close friends, just like Yahweh said would happen.

In the fourth scene: The storyteller formally ends Elah's story.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Asa, king of Judah
  • Elah, Baasha's son
  • Zimri
  • Arza
  • Baasha
  • Baasha's male family members
  • Yahweh
  • Baasha's male friends
  • And the Israelites

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

Notice how Elah's story is very similar to Nadab's story earlier in the book of 1 Kings! Other men kill both Nadab and Elah in order to become king. Then the new king kills the old king's family, just like Yahweh said would happen. Notice how the storyteller tells Elah's story with some of the same statements as the storyteller used in Nadab's story. The storyteller also begins and ends with statements similar to those in the other kings' stories.

Stop here and, as a group, review the beginning and ending phrases from the other kings' stories, as needed. Pause this audio here.

In the first scene, the storyteller introduces Elah's time as king. When the storyteller says that Elah rules for two years, the storyteller means that Elah rules during two different years. Elah becomes king of Israel in the 26th year that Asa is king of Judah, and Elah dies in the 27th year that Asa is king of Judah. In this time, people would often count parts of years as whole years. Elah must have ruled for less than two complete years.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Remember that the storyteller counted parts of a year in the same way in Nadab's story. You will want to translate Elah's two years as king in the same way as you translated Nadab's time as king. Pause this audio here.

The second scene is about how Zimri kills Elah. Zimri is in charge of Elah's chariots. Zimri plots against or makes plans to harm Elah. In Elah's second year as king, Elah is with Arza, in Arza's house. Arza's house is in Tirzah, the capital city of Israel. Arza is in charge of the king's house.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your language, what do you call the place where a king lives and rules from? What do you call a person who takes care of the king's house and the people inside the king's house? Pause this audio here.

Elah drinks alcohol and becomes drunk, which makes it harder for Elah to defend himself. Zimri enters Arza's house, then strikes and kills Elah. To strike and to kill are two ways to say the same thing. This means Zimri hit Elah in order to kill Elah. It is not clear if Arza knew Zimri would do this. The storyteller explains that Zimri kills Elah and becomes king in place of Elah during Asa's 27th year as king of Judah.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your language, how would you say that someone hits another person in order to kill that person? Pause this audio here.

The third scene happens right after Zimri becomes king. Zimri immediately kills Baasha's "house," which is Baasha's family. The storyteller says Zimri kills Baasha's family as soon as Zimri sits on the throne. This phrase emphasizes two things. First, this shows Zimri chose to make himself king, just like Zimri chooses to sit on the throne. This phrase also emphasizes how quickly Zimri begins to kill Baasha's family. Zimri probably does not kill Baasha's family by himself, but instead has other men help Zimri do this.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your language, how do you emphasize that something happens very soon after another event? Pause this audio here.

Unlike Baasha, who killed both male and female members of Jeroboam's family, Zimri kills only the male members and relatives of Baasha's family. These relatives are kinsmen redeemers. Kinsmen is another word for relatives. In this time, people expected relatives to buy back or redeem land that another family member had sold. People also expected a relative, or "kinsman redeemer," to take revenge on someone who killed another family member. This is why Zimri kills these relatives, so that they will not kill Zimri. Zimri also kills any men who are friends of Baasha's family.

Stop here and discuss as a group what you want to call the kinsmen redeemers, or relatives that help or take revenge for their relative when someone kills their relative. Pause this audio here.

Yahweh had given Jehu the prophet a "word" or message about how this would happen to Baasha, and it did happen just as Yahweh said it would happen. The way Baasha and Elah acted had made Yahweh angry. The storyteller reminds us that Yahweh is Israel's God, whom the Israelites should have worshiped during Baasha and Elah's time as king. Instead, however, Baasha and Elah led the Israelites to worship idols or statues of false gods. These idols are worthless because these idols cannot do anything or help the Israelites.

In the fourth scene, the storyteller again uses the same formal language to finish Elah's story as the storyteller used for Nadab's story. The storyteller has just included some parts of Elah's story here, but everything else Elah did as king is in another book.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 16:8–14 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has four scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Asa, king of Judah
  • Elah, Baasha's son
  • Zimri
  • Arza
  • Baasha
  • Baasha's male family members
  • Yahweh
  • Baasha's male friends
  • And the Israelites

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

When Asa had been king of Judah for about 26 years, Baasha's son Elah became king of Israel. Elah ruled in the capital city of Tirzah for two years. Zimri was one of Elah's army officials who commanded half of Elah's chariots. Zimri made secret plans to kill Elah. Elah was in Tirzah at that time in Arza's house. Arza was in charge of Elah's palace. Elah had drunk a lot of alcohol. Zimri went into Arza's house and hit Elah so that Elah fell down and died. Then Zimri became king of Israel instead of Elah. At this time, Asa had been king in Judah for about 27 years.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Arza, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I like the king and want to spend time with the king. We will relax at my house and drink alcohol together; or
  • I was so surprised when Zimri came in and killed the king; or
  • I am nervous that Zimri might kill me, too, if I do not help Zimri.

Ask the person playing Elah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Since I am king, I can relax and do whatever I want to do. I want to drink alcohol and visit my friends; or
  • I am not worried about my army or my commanders. My men will surely obey me.

Continue the drama.

As soon as Zimri began to rule and sat on the throne, Zimri killed everybody in Baasha's family. Zimri did not allow a single male relative or friend to live. So Zimri destroyed the whole family of Baasha. All of this happened exactly as Yahweh had told Jehu the prophet.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Zimri, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am so glad my plan worked; or
  • I am worried that one of the men from Baasha's family might attack me and become king in my place. I will kill Baasha's family so that no one will try to do that; or
  • I heard that King Baasha received a message that all of Baasha's family would die. I should kill Elah and Baasha's family because Baasha's son, Elah, is supposed to die anyway.

Continue the drama.

Yahweh said this because of all the ways that Baasha and his son Elah had sinned. Baasha and Elah led the Israelites to sin. Baasha, Elah, and the Israelites worshiped useless statues and so made Yahweh, the God of Israel, very angry. The other things that happened while Elah was king, and all that he did, are in the book that has the history of the kings of Israel.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am sad that the Israelites continue to sin; or
  • I am angry that the kings continue to lead the Israelites to sin. The kings hurt the Israelites and themselves when they worship idols that cannot help them; or
  • I love the Israelites and will continue to give them more opportunities to obey me. However, when the Israelites sin, I will punish them.

Ask the person playing Asa, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I hear about the kings in Israel. I can see how the kings do not obey Yahweh, and Yahweh punishes them. I really should continue to honor and obey Yahweh; or
  • I feel sad for the Israelites in the northern kingdom. Those people do not have good kings to lead them; or
  • I am nervous when I see bad kings rule Israel. I am worried that those kings may attack us instead of live peacefully with us.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 16:8–14 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Elah becomes king of Israel. For more information on king, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for king as you used in previous passages.

Here, Israel means the northern kingdom. For more information on Israel, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for Israel as you used in previous passages.

Asa is the king of Judah, while Elah rules in Israel. Judah is the southern kingdom. Use the same word or phrase for Judah as you used in previous passages.

Zimri is Elah's servant or official who is in charge of half the chariots. A servant is someone who does work for someone else. Here, the storyteller uses the word servant to mean an official or military leader. Use the same word or phrase for a servant who is really an official as you used in previous passages. If you use the word for servant, you can refer to the Master Glossary for more information about the word servant.

Chariots were wagon-like vehicles with two wheels. Horses pulled the chariots, and two men, one driver and one warrior, rode the chariot into battle. The warrior would fight the enemy while standing in the chariot. Use the same word or phrase for chariot as you used in previous passages.

Azra was in charge of Elah's palace, which is the special place where a king lived. Use the same word or phrase for palace as you have used in previous passages.

Zimri sits on the throne. A throne is a special seat where a king sits. People would often make this special seat from precious materials and decorate the seat. Only a king would sit on a throne. Use the same word or phrase for throne as you used in previous passages.

Zimri kills everyone in the house of Baasha. The house of Baasha refers not to a building, but to the people who are in Baasha's family. Remember that here, this just means the male members of Baasha's family. For more information on house, refer to "household" in the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for house as you used in previous passages.

Zimri kills the male relatives and friends of Baasha. The storyteller uses a word for relatives that really means a kinsman redeemer. In this time, people expected relatives to buy back or redeem land that another family member had sold. People also expected a relative, or "kinsman redeemer," to take revenge on someone who killed another family member. Zimri wants to kill these "kinsmen redeemers" so that none of them will kill Zimri!

Stop here and discuss as a group what you will call these relatives who are really "redeemers," or "avengers" of Elah's death. For more information about this idea of redeeming something, refer to "redeem" in the Master Glossary. Pause this audio here.

Jehu the prophet received the word of Yahweh about what would happen to Baasha's family. The word of Yahweh means what Yahweh tells people.
