For more information on the word of Yahweh, refer to "word" in the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for the "word of Yahweh" as you used in previous passages.

The name Yahweh is a very personal name for God that shows the close relationship that God had with the people of Israel. For more information on Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for Yahweh as you used in previous passages.

Jehu is Yahweh's prophet. A prophet is a person who gives messages from God to the people. For more information on prophet, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for prophet as you used in previous passages.

Baasha and Elah had sinned. Sin is an act of rebellion against God. Anytime that a person disobeys God's laws, that person sins. For more information on sin, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for sin as you used in previous passages.

Yahweh is Israel's God. For more information on God, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for God as you used in previous passages.

Baasha and Elah led the Israelites to worship idols. An idol is an object that people make in order to worship a god. The object reminds the people about some characteristics of this god. For more information on idol, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for idol as you used in previous passages.

Baasha and Elah provoked, or caused, Yahweh to be angry. Use the same word or phrase for provoking Yahweh as you have in previous passages.

Elah's story is also in the book or scroll that tells the history of the kings of Israel. A scroll is a long strip of material on which people would write down stories. Because those sheets were very long, people rolled them up around a stick. For more information on scroll, refer to "scriptures" in the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for scroll or book, and for the name of this book, as you used in previous passages.

Stop here and look at a picture of a scroll again as a group as needed.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 16:15–20
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 16:15–20 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 16:15–20 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 16:15–20 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the last story, Zimri killed King Elah, and Zimri became king. This story is about Zimri's short time as king. The Israelite army does not accept Zimri as king and chooses another man, Omri, to be king. When Omri and the army attack the city of Tirzah, where the king lives, Zimri kills himself.

Zimri becomes king while Asa is in his 27th year as king of Judah. Zimri rules from the city of Tirzah for only seven days. When Zimri becomes king, the Israelite army is not at Tirzah. The Israelite army is at the Philistine city of Gibbethon. Remember that in Nadab's story, King Nadab and the Israelite army tried to capture Gibbethon. Nadab failed to capture Gibbethon, probably because Baasha killed Nadab during that time.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a map of Israel and Philistia. Find Gibbethon and Tirzah. Pause this audio here.

Someone tells the Israelite army that Zimri secretly planned against Elah and killed Elah. All the Israelites in the army decide they do not want Zimri to be their king. Instead, the Israelites in the army immediately declare that Omri will be their king. Omri is the commander of the whole Israelite army. Remember that Zimri was in charge of half of the chariots in Elah's army before Zimri became king. But Omri was a more important military leader than Zimri.

Then Omri leads the army of Israel away from Gibbethon and to Tirzah, where Zimri is. Omri and the Israelite army surround Tirzah and attack. When Zimri sees that Zimri's enemies have captured Tirzah, Zimri goes into the stronghold of the palace. In this time, a palace or a king's house would have many rooms and parts, like places to keep animals or places for servants to live. The stronghold was probably the strongest and most safe part of the king's house, where the king himself lived.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, what kind of rooms or sections does a king's house have? What part of the king's house would the king sleep in? Pause this audio here.

Zimri lights the stronghold rooms on fire while Zimri is in the stronghold, so that Zimri dies in the fire. This is not an honorable way to die in the Israelite culture. Zimri may have been scared of the way the Israelite army might hurt or dishonor Zimri when the Israelite army found Zimri. Zimri dies because Zimri had sinned and acted in a way that displeased Yahweh. Zimri followed Jeroboam's example and led the Israelites to sin. Zimri was a military leader before Zimri became king, so this probably means that Zimri sinned throughout his life and time as a military leader, not just during Zimri's short time as king.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, how do people expect a king to act when a king loses a battle? What do people think about a king who kills himself? Pause this audio here.

Everything else Zimri did as king, including the way Zimri planned to kill King Elah, is in the book or scroll that tells the history of the kings of Israel.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 16:15–20 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has four scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller formally introduces Zimri.

In the second scene: The Israelite army hears that Zimri is king, but the Israelite army declares that Omri will be king instead.

In the third scene: Omri and the Israelite army attack Tizrah. When the Israelite army captures the city, Zimri goes into the citadel and lights the building on fire to kill himself.

In the fourth scene: The storyteller formally ends Zimri's story.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Asa, king of Judah
  • Zimri
  • The Israelite army
  • Omri
  • Yahweh
  • Jeroboam
  • And the Israelites

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

Though the storyteller has already talked about Zimri in the last story, the storyteller still uses formal language to introduce Zimri as king. In the first scene, the storyteller explains who rules in Judah when Zimri becomes king and how long Zimri rules. Notice that the storyteller does not tell us who Zimri's father was, nor whether Zimri acted in a good or evil way here at the beginning of this passage.

The second scene tells how the Israelite army responds when the soldiers hear that Zimri has made himself king. While Elah was king, Elah had apparently sent the army to besiege the Philistine town of Gibbethon. Remember that this is the way an army surrounds a town in order to defeat the people in the town and take control of the town. Gibbethon is probably about 60 kilometers southwest of Tirzah. This is the same town that King Nadab tried to take over in an earlier story.

Once Zimri makes himself king, someone travels to the Israelite army. This person tells the Israelite army that Zimri has planned against and killed King Elah. The Israelite army does not want Zimri as their king, so these Israelites immediately declare that Omri is their king. Omri accepts the Israelite soldiers' decision. The storyteller says the Israelite soldiers chose Omri the same day the Israelites heard the news about Zimri. The storyteller says this to emphasize how quickly the Israelites chose Omri. Remember that in the previous story, the storyteller also emphasized how quickly Zimri killed Baasha's family. The storyteller wants us to understand how quickly everything happens during Zimri's very short time as king!

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: As an activity, tell a story about a time when you received some news and immediately began to do something because of that news. How do you emphasize how quickly you acted after you heard the news? Pause this audio here.

In the third scene, Omri leads the Israelites to Tirzah. Once Omri and the army arrive at Tirzah, the army surrounds Tirzah and attacks the city. There are probably some Israelites in the city of Tirzah who are loyal to Zimri and who try to defend Tirzah. However, the Israelites who fight for Omri win. When Zimri learns that the Israelites with Omri have captured the city, Zimri enters the palace fortress.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your language, what do you call the strongest, safest part of a palace? Pause this audio here.

Zimri then lights the palace on fire. People probably made parts of the palace, like the roof, with a material that burned, like wood. There were probably many other things in the palace that would burn well, like clothes, window coverings, and furniture. Zimri lights the palace on fire in order to kill himself. Zimri dies because Zimri had sinned. Zimri had done evil in the eyes of Yahweh, which means that Zimri did what Yahweh says is evil. Zimri also followed Jeroboam's example when Zimri sinned and caused Israel to sin.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your language, what is an appropriate way to say that a man killed himself? Pause this audio here.

In the fourth scene, the storyteller uses the same formal language as he has used before to finish Zimri's story. The storyteller has just included some parts of Zimri's story here, but everything else Zimri did as king, as well as how Zimri rebelled against the king, is in another book that has the kings' histories. Someone rebels when they resist and fight the government or a ruler.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story about a time when a person or a group of people rebelled and fought against a government or a ruler. In your language, what words or phrases do you have for the way people rebel against a ruler? Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 16:15–20 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has four scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Asa, king of Judah
  • Zimri
  • The Israelite army
  • Omri
  • Yahweh
  • Jeroboam
  • And the Israelites

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

When King Asa had ruled Judah for about 27 years, Zimri became king of Israel. Zimri ruled in the city of Tirzah for only seven days. At that time, the Israelite army was camped around the Philistine town of Gibbethon. The Israelite soldiers in the camp heard that Zimri had secretly planned against and killed King Elah. At once, there in the camp, the Israelites in the army announced that, instead of Zimri, their leader, Omri, was the new king of Israel.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the Israelite army, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I do not like Zimri. I would rather have Omri as our king. I trust Omri; or
  • I am angry that Zimri killed King Elah! I do not want a murderer to be our king! or
  • I was surprised and sad to hear that someone killed our king!

Ask the person playing Omri, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • If the Israelites want me to be their king, I should become king; or
  • I am surprised at how quickly things have changed! or
  • I have led the army well. I think I would be a good king and lead the Israelites well.

Continue the drama.

Then Omri led the army of Israel away from Gibbethon. The army went to Tirzah and began to attack it. When Zimri realized that his enemies had captured the city, Zimri went into the most safe area of the palace. Then Zimri set fire to the palace with Zimri inside. Zimri died in the fire. Zimri died because Zimri had sinned. Zimri had done what Yahweh said was evil. Zimri followed Jeroboam's example. Zimri led the Israelites to sin, like Jeroboam had done.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Zimri, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am surprised that the Israelite army attacked Tirzah! I thought I had made a good plan! I thought the Israelites would follow me! or
  • I am scared of what the Israelite army will do to me if they capture me. It is shameful to kill myself, but the Israelites may treat me in a worse way.

Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am sad that the Israelites fight against each other and kill each other; or
  • I am angry that the Israelites disobey me. The Israelites make their lives more difficult when they do not obey me! or
  • I have warned the Israelites what will happen if they continue to live in this violent and sinful way.

Continue the drama.

The other things that happened while Zimri was king, like how Zimri planned to kill King Elah, are in the book that tells the history of the kings of Israel.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 16:15–20 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Zimri becomes king of Israel. For more information on king, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for king as you used in previous passages.

Israel is the northern kingdom. For more information on Israel, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for Israel as you used in previous passages.

Zimri rules while Asa is king of Judah. Judah is the southern kingdom. Use the same word or phrase for Judah as you used in previous passages.

The Israelites attack the city of Tirzah with a siege. A city was a bigger settlement that usually had walls around it. A city usually had thick walls to keep attackers out of the city. The attackers would surround the city so that the people inside could not come out to get food, water, and other supplies. For more information on siege, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same words or phrases for siege and city as you used in previous passages.

Omri is the commander of the Israelite army. Here, this means that Omri is the military leader who leads the whole army. Use the same word or phrase for "military commander" as you used in previous passages.

Zimri goes into the "stronghold" of the palace. A stronghold, in this case, is a safe place that people have built to make it difficult for enemies to capture. Palaces often had an especially strong area inside that made it difficult for enemies to attack. Use the same word or phrase for palace as you have used previously.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for a man-made stronghold. For more information, refer to stronghold in the Master Glossary. Pause this audio here.

Zimri had sinned. Sin is an act of rebellion against God. Anytime that a person disobeys God's laws, that person sins. For more information on sin, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for sin as you used in previous passages.

Zimri acted in an evil way in the eyes of Yahweh. Evil is the opposite of good. For more information on evil, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for evil and doing evil in the eyes of Yahweh, as you used in previous passages.

The name Yahweh is a very personal name for God that showed the close relationship that God had with the people of Israel. For more information on Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for Yahweh as you used in previous passages.

Zimri's story is also in the book or scroll that tells the history of the kings of Israel. For more information, refer to scroll in the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for scroll or book, and for the name of this book, as you used in previous passages.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a picture of a scroll again as needed.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 16:21–28
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 16:21–28 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 16:21–28 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 16:21–28 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the last story, the army declared that Omri should be king, and Zimri killed himself. This story is about what happens after Zimri dies. Some Israelites want another man, Tibni, to be king. However, Omri's supporters defeat Tibni's supporters and Omri rules as king. While Omri is king, Omri sins even more than the other Israelite kings did.

This story begins right after Zimri dies. When Omri defeats Zimri, the Israelites do not all immediately support Omri. One group supports Omri while another group supports a man named Tibni, son of Ginath. Ginath could be Tibni's father's name or the place Tibni is from. The two groups of Israelites probably fight each other in battles for about four years. Omri's supporters are stronger than Tibni's and probably kill Tibni in a battle.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story about a time when your people group or a people group near you disagreed about who should rule next. How did the people who disagreed treat each other? When one person finally led the group, how did the group treat the other person who had tried to rule? Pause this audio here.

Omri becomes king of all the Israelites, including Tibni's supporters, while Asa is in his 31st year as king of Judah. Omri rules for a total of 12 years, including the 4 years when Omri and Tibni fought each other. For half of those 12 years, Omri lives and rules in the city of Tirzah. While Omri lives in Tirzah, Omri buys a hill from a person named Shemer for two talents of silver. A talent is a unit of measurement of about 34 kilograms.

Omri builds a town on this hill. This hill is a good place to build a city. This hill is easy to defend, and it is on a trading route. Omri names this town Samaria after Shemer, the name of the former owner of the hill. Omri moves to Samaria in Omri's seventh year as king and makes Samaria the new capital of Israel.

Stop here and look at a map of Israel as a group. Find Tirzah and Samaria. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How does a leader choose the capital city or the city the leader will rule from? Tell a story about a time when a leader picked a new capital city. What things or people did the leader have to move to the new city? What did people think about this change? Pause this audio here.

However, the whole time Omri is king, Omri acts in a way that displeases Yahweh. Omri sins even more than the kings who ruled before Omri. Omri follows Jeroboam's example and sins exactly like Jeroboam sinned. Omri leads the Israelites in such a way that the Israelites willingly choose to sin as well. Omri and the Israelites worship idols, which makes Yahweh very angry.

Everything else Omri did as king, including Omri's actions as a military leader, is in the book or scroll that has the history of the kings of Israel. Omri rests with his ancestors, which is a polite way to say Omri dies. This probably does not mean that people bury Omri near his ancestors because people bury Omri in Samaria. Since Samaria is a new town, people probably did not bury Omri's ancestors in Samaria in the past. Omri's son Ahab then becomes king.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 16:21–28 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has five scenes.

In the first scene: The Israelites divide into two groups. One group supports Tibni. The other group supports Omri. Omri's supporters defeat Tibni's supporters and kill Tibni. Omri becomes king.

In the second scene: The storyteller formally introduces Omri's time as king.

In the third scene: Omri buys a hill and builds the city of Samaria.

In the fourth scene: The storyteller talks about how Omri ruled as king.

In the fifth scene: The storyteller formally ends Omri's story.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The people of Israel, who divide into two groups
  • Tibni, son of Ginath
  • Omri
  • Shemer
  • Asa, king of Judah
  • Yahweh
  • Jeroboam, Nebat's son
  • And Ahab, Omri's son

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, the Israelites divide into two groups. It is not clear how many Israelites are in each group. The people who support Omri defeat the people who support Tibni and probably kill Tibni in a battle. Then Omri becomes king. Remember that Zimri died in the 27th year that Asa ruled in Judah, and that is when the Israelites began to fight. Omri becomes king in Asa's 31st year as king, so the Israelites probably fought for about 4 years.

In the second scene, though the storyteller has already said that Omri becomes king, the storyteller still uses formal language to introduce Omri's time as king. The storyteller explains who rules in Judah when Omri becomes king and how long Omri rules.

The storyteller says Omri rules for 12 years. This means that the army declares that Omri is king in Asa's 27th year, right after Zimri murdered Elah. Then Omri and Omri's group fight Tibni and Tibni's group until Asa's 31st year as king. This is when Tibni dies and Omri officially rules over all the Israelites. Then Omri dies in Asa's 38th year as king. These are the 12 years that the storyteller says Omri rules. For 6 of those 12 years, Omri lives in Tirzah, including the 4 years when Omri and Tibni fight each other. Then, for the other six years that Omri is king, Omri lives in Samaria.

Stop here and discuss as a group how you want to translate how long Omri ruled. You can draw or act out what Omri did during the 12 years between when Zimri died and when Omri died. Pause this audio here.

The third scene probably happens sometime during the last two years Omri lives in Tirzah. Omri buys a hill from a person named Shemer and builds a town on the hill. Omri probably orders other people to build the town. Omri names the town Samaria and moves to Samaria six years after Zimri died. Samaria is about 15 kilometers from Tirzah. Omri then rules for six more years in Samaria.

In the fourth scene, the storyteller describes how Omri rules as king. Omri does evil in the eyes of Yahweh, which means that Omri does what Yahweh says is evil. Omri sins even more than the previous kings did. Omri follows Jeroboam's example and sins. Omri leads the Israelites to worship idols or statues of false gods. These idols are worthless because these idols cannot do anything or help the Israelites. This idol worship makes Yahweh very angry. The storyteller reminds us that Yahweh is Israel's God, whom the Israelites should have worshiped.

In the fifth scene, the storyteller uses formal language to finish Omri's story. The storyteller has just included some parts of Omri's story here, but everything else Omri did as king is in another book. This is similar to what the storyteller said about other kings in past stories, but notice that this time the storyteller says here that Omri's achievements or mighty deeds are also in that other book. These mighty deeds are probably how Omri fought and led during battles.

Omri sleeps with his ancestors, which is a common polite phrase that means Omri dies. People bury Omri's body in Samaria. Then Omri's son, Ahab, becomes king. The storyteller ends Omri's story in the same way the storyteller ended Baasha's story.

Stop here and review how you translated the end of Baasha's story. You will probably want to translate the end of Omri's story in a similar way to how you translated the end of Baasha's story. Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 16:21–28 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has five scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The people of Israel, who divide into two groups
  • Tibni, son of Ginath
  • Omri
  • Shemer
  • Asa, king of Judah
  • Yahweh
  • Jeroboam, Nebat's son
  • And Ahab, Omri's son

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

After Zimri died, the people of Israel split into two groups. One group wanted Tibni, son of Ginath, to be their king. The other group wanted Omri to become king. But Omri's supporters defeated the supporters of Tibni. Omri's supporters probably killed Tibni, and then Omri became the king of Israel.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the Israelites who supported Tibni, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I do not like Omri. I think Tibni will be a better king; or
  • I am so surprised that Omri's group defeated us and killed Tibni! or
  • I am worried about how Omri will treat us now that Omri rules us all as king.

Ask the person playing the Israelites who supported Omri, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am happy that we defeated Tibni and his group; or
  • Omri won this fight against Tibni and the other Israelites. This shows that Omri is strong and should be king! or
  • I am glad that we have won and can go home now.

Continue the drama.

When Asa had been king of Judah for about 31 years, Omri became king of Israel. Omri ruled for 12 years. For the first six years, Omri ruled in Tirzah. During that time, Omri bought the hill of Samaria from a person named Shemer. Omri paid two talents of silver for the hill. When Omri built a city on the hill, Omri named the city Samaria after Shemer, who used to own the hill.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Omri, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I will build a new city so that whenever people enter the city, they will remember that I built the city. Then people will remember me as a great king! or
  • Since I am king, I am rich and can buy land to build a new city; or
  • This new city will be better than Tirzah. Zimri burned the old king's house, and I want to build a new one in this new city.

Continue the drama.

Omri did evil things that displeased Yahweh. Omri sinned more than any of the kings of Israel before him. Omri did all the bad things that Jeroboam, the son of Nebat, had done. Omri sinned in the same way that Jeroboam had caused the people of Israel to sin. The Israelites made Yahweh, the God of Israel, angry because they worshiped useless statues.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I give each new king a chance to honor me, and each time they do not. I knew this would happen, but this still makes me sad; or
  • I am angry that the Israelites dishonor me when they ignore me and worship idols; or
  • The Israelites fight each other and die because they do not live in the way I say they should.

Continue the drama.

Now, the rest of what took place during Omri's time as king, what Omri did and the power Omri showed, are in the book that has the history of the kings of Israel. Omri died, and people buried Omri's body in the city of Samaria. Omri's son Ahab then ruled as king.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 16:21–28 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

The people of Israel split into two factions or groups. Here, Israel means the northern kingdom. For more information on Israel, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for Israel as you used in previous passages.

Omri becomes king of Israel. For more information on king, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for king as you used in previous passages.

Asa is king of Judah while Omri rules in Israel. Judah is the southern kingdom. Use the same word or phrase for Judah as you used in previous passages.

Omri pays two talents of silver to Shemer to buy a hill. A talent is a unit of measurement. One talent is about 34 kilograms, so Omri probably pays around 68 kilograms of silver to Shemer. Use the same word or phrase for talent as you used in previous passages, and refer to talent in the Master Glossary for more information.

Omri acts in an evil way in the eyes of Yahweh. Evil is the opposite of good. For more information on evil, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for evil and acting in an evil way in the eyes of Yahweh as you used in previous passages.

Omri makes Samaria the new capital city of Israel. Use the same word or phrase for city as you used in previous passages.

The name Yahweh is a very personal name for God that showed the close relationship that God had with the people of Israel. For more information on Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for Yahweh as you used in previous passages.

Omri sins more than the other kings had sinned. Sin is an act of rebellion against God. Anytime that a person disobeys God's laws, that person sins. For more information on sin, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for sin as you used in previous passages.

Yahweh is Israel's God. For more information on God, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for God as you used in previous passages.

Omri led the Israelites to worship idols and provoked, or caused, Yahweh to become angry. An idol is an object that people make in order to worship a god. For more information on idol, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for idol and provoked Yahweh to be angry as you used in previous passages.

Omri's story is also in the book or scroll that tells the history of the kings of Israel. A scroll is a long strip of material on which people would write down stories. For more information, refer to scroll in the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for scroll or book, and the name of the book, as you used in previous passages.

Stop here and look at a picture of a scroll again as a group as needed.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 16:29–34
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 16:29–34 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 16:29–34 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 16:29–34 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

The last story was about how Omri ruled as king. This story is about King Ahab, Omri's son. Ahab becomes king while Asa is in his 38th year as king of Judah. Ahab rules for 22 years from the city of Samaria. Remember that Samaria is Israel's new capital city, which is the city where the kings rule from.

Ahab is even more evil than the kings who ruled before Ahab. Ahab does not think that it is a serious sin to act in the same evil way as Jeroboam. So Ahab sins like Jeroboam did, and then Ahab sins even more!

Ahab marries a woman named Jezebel, who is not an Israelite. Jezebel is the daughter of King Ethbaal, who ruled over the city of Sidon. Sidon was a city on the coast of the Mediterranean Sea, where the Phoenician people lived.

Stop here and look at a map of Israel and Phoenicia as a group. Find the cities of Sidon and Samaria. Pause this audio here.

Yahweh warned the Israelites not to marry people who were from other people groups. Those people from other people groups will try to convince the Israelites to worship other gods, like Baal. Baal was a male false god that the Canaanites worshiped. The Canaanites believed that Baal controlled the wind and rain and caused plants to grow.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, what would people think if someone from your people group married someone from another people group, who had different traditions and worshiped different gods? What would people think if a king or leader married someone from a different people group? Why might a king do this? Pause this audio here.

The people of Sidon probably worshiped Baal, and now Ahab does too. Ahab builds a temple with an altar for Baal in Samaria. Ahab also makes an Asherah pole. Asherah was a false goddess that the Canaanites worshiped. Asherah was also Baal's partner. Sometimes people would make a wooden pole to represent their Asherah goddess. The Israelites probably follow Ahab's example and worship these false gods too. All this makes Yahweh very angry. Ahab does more to make Yahweh angry than any of the previous kings of Israel had done.

Stop here and look at a picture of an altar and an Asherah pole as a group. Describe the items that people use to worship other gods in your area. How do those items look similar to or different from the altar and pole in this picture? Pause this audio here.

While Ahab is king, a man rebuilds the city of Jericho. Many years ago, when the Israelites first came to the land of Canaan, Yahweh helped the Israelites defeat the people of Jericho in an amazing way. Yahweh knocked down the walls of Jericho. The Israelite leader at that time was Joshua. Joshua warned the people of Israel never to rebuild the city of Jericho. Joshua said that Yahweh will place a curse on anyone who tries to rebuild Jericho, which means to build the walls of Jericho so that people can build houses inside. Joshua said that when someone starts to build the city, their oldest son will die. And when that person finishes building the city, their youngest child will die. This may mean that whoever rebuilds the city of Jericho will lose all of his children, from the oldest to the youngest.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, what do people believe about curses? When would be some times when a god might curse someone? When would someone curse whoever does a certain action? Pause this audio here.

The ruins of Jericho would remind the Israelites that Yahweh defeated the Canaanites and gave the land of Canaan to the Israelites. So, since Joshua's time, no one had rebuilt the city. Since Jericho was in Israel, people still lived in the area of Jericho, as we see in other books of the Bible. But until Ahab's time, no one rebuilt the walls of Jericho to make it a strong city again.

So now, while Ahab rules as king, a man from Bethel named Hiel wants to rebuild Jericho. Since Ahab is king, Ahab probably has to allow Hiel to do this. So this part of the story may show an example of how Ahab sins, because Ahab allows the Israelites to build the city that Yahweh said no one should rebuild.

To begin to build the walls, Hiel would first build the foundation, or the strong stones at the bottom of the wall that people put firmly in the ground. When Hiel sets up those stones, Hiel's oldest son, Abiram, dies. To finish the wall, Hiel would put gates or large doors in the wall. This would show that the city is finished and ready for people to live in. When Hiel puts the gates in the wall, Hiel's youngest son, Segub, dies. This happens exactly like Yahweh's curse, which Joshua spoke to the Israelites. This part of the story shows that even though the Israelites worship other gods during this time, Yahweh still rules over the Israelites, and that everything Yahweh says will happen, happens.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a picture of the foundation stones of a wall and a gate in a city wall. How do people build walls in your area? When someone builds a new town, what does that person build first? What is the last thing the person would build, which would show the town is ready for people to live in?

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 16:29–34 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has three scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller formally introduces Ahab's time as king.

In the second scene: Ahab marries Jezebel and sets up places to worship false gods.

In the third scene: Hiel rebuilds Jericho. When Hiel starts to build Jericho, his firstborn son dies. As Hiel finishes the gates of Jericho, his youngest son dies.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Ahab, Omri's son
  • Asa, king of Judah
  • The people of the kingdom of Israel
  • Yahweh
  • Jeroboam, Nebat's son
  • Jezebel, King Ethbaal's daughter
  • Hiel
  • Hiel's son Abiram
  • Hiel's son Segub
  • And Joshua, Nun's son

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the first scene, the storyteller uses formal phrases to introduce Ahab's time as king. The storyteller explains who rules in Judah when Ahab becomes king, how long Ahab rules, and how Ahab rules. Ahab does evil in the eyes of Yahweh, which means that Ahab does what Yahweh says is evil. Ahab does even more evil than the previous kings did. Ahab follows Jeroboam's example and sins.

Stop here and review the way you have translated the formal introduction to the kings of Israel in previous passages as needed. Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, Ahab marries Jezebel and worships false gods. The storyteller says that it was not enough for Ahab to sin like Jeroboam, so Ahab sins even more. Ahab thinks that it is not serious to sin like Jeroboam. The storyteller is emphasizing that Ahab wants to act in a sinful way, so Ahab chooses to sin even more than Jeroboam did. Ahab disobeys Yahweh when Ahab marries Jezebel, a woman who is not an Israelite, and when Ahab worships false gods.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your language, how would you emphasize that someone acts not only in a bad way like someone else, but also in another bad way? How would you talk about the way that someone thinks that the way they sin is not serious? Pause this audio here.

Jezebel's father rules over the people in the city of Sidon. Ahab builds a temple and altar to Baal in the city of Samaria. Ahab also makes an Asherah pole. Ahab probably has other people build these things.

Stop here and look at a map of Israel as a group. Find the cities of Sidon and Samaria. Pause this audio here.

Again, the storyteller says that Ahab did more to make Yahweh angry than the previous kings of Israel did. The storyteller shows how each king of Israel is worse than the one before him! The storyteller also emphasizes that Yahweh is Israel's God, whom Ahab and the Israelites should have worshiped.

In the third scene, Hiel of Bethel rebuilds Jericho. When Hiel starts to build and puts down the strong stones at the bottom of the walls, Hiel's oldest son, Abiram, dies. When Hiel finishes the city and puts on the gates, Hiel's youngest son, Segub, dies. The storyteller does not tell us how Hiel's sons die, but Hiel's sons die because of Yahweh's curse, which Joshua had told the Israelites about. The storyteller does not tell the story of how Joshua shared Yahweh's word or message with the Israelites because the Israelites would probably know this story already.

Stop here and look at a map of Israel as a group. Find the cities of Jericho and Bethel. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller does not give the formal ending to Ahab's story because in the next passages, the storyteller will tell stories about what happened while Ahab ruled as king.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 16:29–34 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has three scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Ahab, Omri's son
  • Asa, king of Judah
  • The people of the kingdom of Israel
  • Yahweh
  • Jeroboam, Nebat's son
  • Jezebel, King Ethbaal's daughter
  • Hiel
  • Hiel's son Abiram
  • Hiel's son Segub
  • And Joshua, Nun's son

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Ahab became king of Israel when Asa had ruled Judah for about 38 years. Ahab ruled Israel from the city of Samaria for about 22 years. Ahab, the son of Omri, did evil that displeased Yahweh more than any of the kings who ruled before Ahab.

Ahab did not think that it was a serious sin to act in the same evil way as Nebat's son Jeroboam. Ahab sinned even more than Jeroboam sinned and married Jezebel, who was the daughter of Ethbaal, king of the Sidonians. Then Ahab began to serve and worship the false god Baal. Ahab built a temple in Samaria to worship Baal and put an altar there to honor Baal. Ahab also made an Asherah pole. Ahab did more things to make Yahweh the God of Israel angry than any of the previous kings of Israel had done.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Jezebel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Israel is not like the land where I grew up. The people here live differently than how I live. I will ask my husband to do some of my people's traditions and build a temple like the ones in Sidon. Then this new land will not be so strange for me; or
  • I want my new husband to worship the same gods as I do. I do not want Ahab's God to tell Ahab to do something different than what my gods want me to do! or
  • I do not like the God of the Israelites. I did not learn about this God when I was young. I would rather worship the gods of my people.

Ask the person playing Ahab, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • This is a good marriage for our two people groups. Now our people will live in peace and trade with each other. I made a good decision to marry Jezebel; or
  • I want my wife to be comfortable. I will do some of the things that her people do, like worship these gods; or
  • I do not care about Yahweh and Yahweh's rules. I will live however I want to live.

Continue the drama.

While Ahab was king of Israel, a man from Bethel named Hiel rebuilt the city of Jericho. But when Hiel put the bottom stones for the wall in place, Hiel's oldest son, Abiram, died. And when Hiel finished the gates to the city, Hiel's youngest son, Segub, died. Many years earlier, Yahweh had told Joshua, the son of Nun, that this would happen.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Hiel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am so sad my sons are dead! or
  • When my oldest son died, I thought it was an accident or a coincidence. So I continued to build Jericho. But now my youngest son has died! This must have happened because of a curse! or
  • I wish I had not built Jericho.

Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I told the Israelites what would happen! I want the Israelites to listen to me! or
  • I am angry that Ahab does not honor me; or
  • I am sad that the Israelites hurt themselves and others when they sin.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 16:29–34 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Ahab becomes king of Israel. For more information on king, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for king as you used in previous passages.

Here, Israel means the northern kingdom. For more information on Israel, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for Israel as you used in previous passages.

Asa is king of Judah when Ahab becomes king in Israel. Judah is the southern kingdom. Use the same word or phrase for Judah as you used in previous passages.

Ahab acts in an evil way in the eyes of Yahweh. Evil is the opposite of good. For more information on evil, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for evil and acting in an evil way in the eyes of Yahweh, as you used in previous passages.

The name Yahweh was a very personal name for God. For more information on Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for Yahweh as you used in previous passages.

Ahab sins even more than Jeroboam sinned. Sin is an act of rebellion against God. For more information on sin, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for sin as you used in previous passages.

Ahab worships Baal. Baal was a male false god that the Canaanites worshiped. The Canaanites believed that Baal controlled the wind and the rain, and that Baal caused plants to grow. For more information on worship, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for worship as you used in previous passages.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for Baal. Look up Baal in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Ahab builds an altar for Baal. An altar is a place where people make offerings or sacrifices to a god. An altar is like a table or a platform that people make of earth or large stones. For more information on altar, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for altar as you used in previous passages.

This altar is in the temple for Baal. This temple is a building where people worship Baal, not the temple that Yahweh asked the Israelites to build for Yahweh. In the original language, people would call this building "Baal's house."

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for the temple, or house, of Baal. If you use a general word for a temple, refer to temple in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Ahab also makes an Asherah pole. Asherah, the partner of Baal, was the female false god that the Canaanites worshiped. Sometimes people would make a wooden pole to represent the Asherah goddess.

Stop here and, as a group, look at an Asherah pole again as needed. Discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for "Asherah pole." Use the same word or phrase for "Asherah pole" as you used in earlier stories in 1 Kings. Pause this audio here.

Ahab provokes, or causes, Yahweh, Israel's God, to become angry. Use the same word or phrase for provoke or cause as you have in previous passages. For more information on God, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for God as you used in previous passages.

Hiel builds the "foundation" of Jericho, which is the strong stones at the bottom of a wall. People would often dig holes and put these stones into the holes. These stones would make a very stable place for people to build a wall on top. Use the same word or phrase for foundation as you used in previous passages.

Stop here and look again at the picture of a foundation. Pause this audio here.

Joshua received the word of Yahweh about what would happen to the person who rebuilt Jericho. The "word of Yahweh" means what Yahweh tells people. For more information on the word of Yahweh, refer to word in the Master Glossary. Use the same word or phrase for the word of Yahweh as you used in previous passages.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 17:1–7
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 17:1–7 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 17:1–7 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 17:1–7 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

Here we meet Elijah the prophet for the first time. The name "Elijah" means "Yahweh is my God." Elijah is a Tishbite, from the village of Tishbe in Gilead, east of the Jordan River. King Ahab is the king of the Northern Kingdom of Israel and rules from the capital city, Samaria. Samaria is west of the Jordan River. It is likely that Elijah travelled from Gilead to Samaria to share Yahweh's words with King Ahab.

Stop here and look at a map of the regions of Gilead and Samaria. Find Gilead on the map, and then find the village of Tishbe, east of the Jordan River. Next, locate the city of Samaria, west of the Jordan River. Pause this audio here.

We know that Elijah must be a prophet because Yahweh has given a message to Elijah to share with King Ahab. First, Elijah identifies clearly who Elijah serves. Elijah is not serving King Ahab or King Ahab's "god," Baal. Elijah tells King Ahab that Yahweh, not Baal, is the God of Israel. Remember, you heard before that King Ahab built a temple and an altar for Baal in Samaria.

Elijah also tells King Ahab that Yahweh, the God of Israel, is a living God. By saying this, Elijah contrasts the living Yahweh with Baal, a god who is not alive. Finally, Elijah tells King Ahab that Yahweh has decided that there will be no dew or rain for several years. In Israel, the rainy season usually happens between October and March. During the dry season, the moisture in the air from the sea provides water for the land and the people in the form of heavy morning dew.

Stop here and look at a picture of morning dew. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What does "rainy season" look like in your country? How long does the rainy season last? Where do you get water if there is no rain? Pause this audio here.

[Yahweh gave Elijah the power to stop the dew and the rain because Elijah is a prophet of Yahweh. Yahweh wants to show that Yahweh is the true God of Israel, not Baal.]{.mark} Baal is a storm god and a fertility god, but Baal actually has no power to control the dew or the rain that helps the crops grow.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do we know our God is alive, powerful, and the only true God? How are the true God's actions the same or different from Baal's actions? Pause this audio here.

Notice that Elijah never mentions the name "Baal" when he shares Yahweh's words with King Ahab.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, how do you show someone that their actions are sinful or wrong? Do you tell that person directly, or do you simply share God's word with them, as Elijah does in this story? Pause this audio here.

After Elijah gives Yahweh's message to King Ahab, Yahweh warns Elijah to leave and hide in the Kerith Valley. We do not know where Elijah is leaving from, but we do know that Yahweh tells Elijah to go to the east side of the Jordan River.

Stop here and look at a map that shows the location of the Kerith Valley. Pause this audio here.

Yahweh tells Elijah to hide in the Kerith Valley. This area is a streambed or riverbed that is dry when there is no rain. However, the streambed or riverbed fills up with water during the rainy season.

Stop here and look at a picture of an area that is only full of water during the rainy season. Look at a picture of the area when there is water and another when there is no water. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss these questions as a group: What are some areas in your country that are sometimes dry and sometimes full of water? What does it look like when it is dry, and what does it look like when it has water? Pause this audio here.

Stop here and look at a picture of a brook or a stream. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What do you call flowing water that is smaller than a river in your language? Pause this audio here.

Yahweh tells Elijah that he can drink water from the brook and that ravens will bring Elijah bread and meat. A raven is a type of crow, a large black bird that people know for eating dead animals.

Stop here and look at a picture of a raven. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What do you call this type of bird in your language? Do any types of birds have special meaning in your culture? Pause this audio here.

We do not know what type of meat the ravens bring to Elijah.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story from your culture about a time when God provided food for someone in a miraculous way. Pause this audio here.

After a while, the brook dries up, and Elijah can no longer drink water from the brook. Yahweh did not promise Elijah that the brook would miraculously provide water forever. However, Yahweh shows that he clearly has power over the natural world. In later passages, Yahweh gives Elijah power to perform other miraculous events.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Remember that Yahweh has given Elijah the power to command the dew and the rain to come. Why do you think Elijah does not use that power at this time? Discuss a time when you did not go ahead and act or do something, even though you had the ability to do it. Why did you choose not to act?

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 17:1–7 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has four scenes.

In the first scene: Elijah tells King Ahab that Yahweh has told him there will be no dew or rain in the land for a few years. There will only be dew or rain if Elijah asks for it.

In the second scene: Yahweh tells Elijah to leave, go east of the Jordan River, and hide in an area near Kerith Brook. Yahweh promises to take care of Elijah. Yahweh tells Elijah that Elijah will be able to drink water from the brook and that ravens will bring him food.

In the third scene: Elijah obeys Yahweh and goes to Kerith Brook. Every morning and night, the ravens bring bread and meat to Elijah. Elijah drinks water from the brook.

In the fourth scene: After some time passes, the brook dries up because there has not been dew or rain for so long.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Elijah the Tishbite, a prophet from the village of Tishbe, in Gilead
  • Ahab, king of Israel, son of Omri
  • Yahweh, the God of Israel
  • And ravens

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

Remember, in the first scene, Elijah is speaking with King Ahab, maybe for the first time. King Ahab is one of the most wicked kings in Israel's history. Elijah emphasizes three things: one, that Elijah serves Yahweh; two, that Yahweh is a living God; and three, that Yahweh is the true God of Israel. Elijah says these things to make a strong promise that what Elijah says will happen, will actually happen.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do you make a strong promise or oath in your culture? Pause this audio here.

Elijah tells King Ahab that there will be no dew or rain for a few years, "except by my word." "Except by my word" means "only if I say it." Elijah is telling King Ahab that the only way it will rain is if Elijah says it will rain.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use to explain that Elijah tells King Ahab that it is Elijah who will say when the rain can come again. Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, God tells Elijah to hide from King Ahab. King Ahab is probably very angry about not having rain. Remember, we do not know where Elijah is leaving from, but we know that "the Word of Yahweh" came to Elijah again. This time God commands Elijah to go east of the Jordan, to the Kerith Valley, a dry river valley where there is a brook, or small river, of water when it rains. God also gives Elijah a message about the food and water that Yahweh will provide for Elijah.

Stop here and look at a map of the Kerith Valley area. Pause this audio here.

In the third scene, every morning and night, the ravens bring both bread and meat to Elijah. Some translations say the ravens brought bread and meat every morning and every night, and other translations say the ravens brought bread and meat every morning and bread and meat every night.

Stop here and discuss this question together as a group: What would be the most natural way in your language to explain that the ravens brought food at the same two times every day? Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss another question together as a group: What do you think it looked like for the ravens to bring bread and meat to Elijah? Pause this audio here.

In the fourth scene, we see that after some time passes, the brook has dried up and Elijah can no longer drink from it. We do not know exactly how much time passes before the brook dries up, but we know it is dry because there has been no rain for a while. Most likely, the period of time is more than a few days but less than one year.

Stop here and discuss this question together as a group: How does your language describe a period of time that is longer than a few days but less than a year? Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 17:1–7 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has four scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Elijah the Tishbite, a prophet from the village of Tishbe, in Gilead
  • Ahab, king of Israel, son of Omri
  • Yahweh, the God of Israel
  • And ravens

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Act out Elijah introducing himself to King Ahab. Remember, Elijah emphasizes three things: one, that Elijah serves Yahweh; two, that Yahweh is a living God; and three, that Yahweh is the true God of Israel. Elijah warns King Ahab that Yahweh has said that the dew and the rain will stop for a long time. Elijah also tells King Ahab that Yahweh has given Elijah the power to command the dew and the rain to come again. Act out this scene now.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Elijah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer something like:

  • King Ahab is such a wicked king. How can King Ahab deny that Yahweh is the God of Israel?

Ask the person playing King Ahab, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Who does this Elijah think he is? My god, Baal, is all-powerful. But what if Elijah is right? or
  • Elijah says he can command the dew or rain to come. Fine, I will just arrest Elijah and order him to make it happen!

Continue the drama.

Act out Yahweh speaking with Elijah. Yahweh instructs Elijah to leave and go to the brook in the Kerith Valley. Yahweh also explains how he will take care of Elijah, that Yahweh will send ravens to bring food to Elijah and that the brook will have water. Act out this scene now.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Elijah, you are truly my servant. I will protect you and take care of you; or
  • I know Elijah will have some tough times ahead, but I will always be with him.

Ask the person playing Elijah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am so thankful that Yahweh has a plan to keep me safe; or
  • I hope this will be a good hiding place. King Ahab looked really mad.

Continue the drama.

Act out Elijah travelling to Kerith Ravine, drinking from the brook in the ravine, and the ravens feeding him every morning and night.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Elijah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Yahweh is so good to provide food for me; or
  • It is very strange for wild birds to bring food to me.

Continue the drama.

Act out the brook drying up and Elijah not having water anymore.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Elijah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I wonder why Yahweh let the brook dry up? Maybe Yahweh wants me to move on; or
  • I am feeling a little worried, but I trust that Yahweh will take care of me.

Continue the drama.

Now practice all four scenes as a group and perform the scenes two times—the first time with no interruption and the second time with pauses to ask the characters questions.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 17:1–7 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

You have heard the words Yahweh, God, and Israel many times in 1 Kings. Use the same words or phrases for Yahweh, God, and Israel as you used in previous passages. For more information on Yahweh, God, or Israel, refer to the Master Glossary.

This is the first time that the storyteller mentions Elijah. Elijah is a prophet that God sent to warn Ahab about the consequences of Ahab's evil acts. A prophet is a person who gives messages from God to the people. The name "Elijah" means "Yahweh is my God."

Stop here and discuss as a group what name you will use for Elijah. Look up Elijah in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already used this name in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Elijah is called "the Tishbite from Tishbe in Gilead." A Tishbite is a person from the village of Tishbe. Gilead is a region that is on the east side of the Jordan River. You will need to decide if it is appropriate in your language to call Elijah "the Tishbite from Tishbe in Gilead," or if there is a more natural way to say this.

Stop here and discuss as a group what phrase you will use for "the Tishbite from Tishbe in Gilead." Pause this audio here.

Elijah travels east towards the Jordan River and crosses the Jordan River to get to the Kerith Valley. Remember that the sun rises in the direction of the east. Use the same word or phrase for east as you have used in previous passages in 1 Kings.

Use the same word for the Jordan River as you used in previous passages. For more information about the Jordan River, refer to the Master Glossary.

Remember, you learned that during Israel's dry season, the moisture in the air from the sea appears as a heavy morning dew. This dew provides water for the land and the people when there is no rain.

Stop here and look again at the photo of heavy morning dew, if needed. Discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for "dew." Pause this audio here.

God instructs Elijah to go east of the Jordan to a brook in the Kerith Valley. The name "Kerith" means "to cut" or "to cut off." The meaning of this name gives a hint about the future and some of the terrible things Ahab and his queen will do to the prophets of Israel. When it rains, there is a brook, or a small river, that flows through the valley.

Stop here and look again at the photos of the dry river valley and the brook that flows through it. Discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for the brook, or small river, that flows in the Kerith Valley when it rains. Use the same word for wadi, or dry river valley, that you used in 1 Kings 8:65. Pause this audio here.

The word of Yahweh comes to Elijah. Use the same word or phrase you used for "the word of Yahweh" in previous passages, and refer to word in the Master Glossary for more information.

Ravens feed Elijah, and they bring him both bread and meat every morning and every night.

Stop here and look at a photo of ravens again, if needed. Discuss as a group what word you will use for ravens. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Also, discuss what word or phrase you will use for "meat." We do not know what kind of meat this was.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 17:8–16
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 17:8–16 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 17:8–16 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 17:8–16 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous passage, we heard how Elijah, Yahweh's prophet, told King Ahab that there would be a long time of no rain or dew in Israel or Samaria. King Ahab was one of the most wicked kings in Israel's history. King Ahab did not obey Yahweh, but instead worshipped a false god named Baal. King Ahab believed in Baal as the god of fertility and rain. Yahweh protected Elijah from King Ahab and led Elijah to the Kerith Valley, which had a small brook, so that Elijah could have fresh water. Yahweh also sent ravens to bring food to Elijah every day. But then, the brook that Elijah had been drinking from dried up.

In this story, Yahweh commands Elijah to get up and go right away to Zarephath. Zarephath is a town between the two areas of Sidon and Tyre, near the Mediterranean Sea. Zarephath is located about 15 kilometers south of Sidon, and 14 kilometers north of Tyre, in the country of Phoenicia. Zarephath, just like Israel, has had no rain for a long time. It is interesting to note that Baal is a Phoenician false god. Yahweh makes it clear that Baal has no power, since Baal obviously cannot even make it rain in Baal's own home country.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a map that shows the town of Zarephath and where it is located between Sidon and Tyre, near the Mediterranean Sea. Trace Elijah's journey from Kerith Valley to Zarephath. Pause this audio here.

Yahweh sends Elijah to Zarephath and promises that a widow, or a woman whose husband has died, will provide food for Elijah.

Stop here and answer this question as a group: What is life like for a widow in your community? How do people recognize if a woman is a widow? Pause this audio here.

Elijah arrives at the town's entrance, a gate. In Elijah's time, many cities were surrounded by a wall to protect the city from attackers. There was usually one main way to enter and leave the city, through a gate. The gate was often a public gathering place.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Where do people in your community regularly gather to see each other and talk? Pause this audio here.

Elijah calls out to the woman from a distance and asks her to bring him some water to drink in a jar or a cup. This probably means that there was a shared, public source of water nearby. While the woman is on her way to get Elijah the cup of water, Elijah calls out to her again and asks her to please bring him a piece of bread. Elijah is not asking her for a lot of bread, just a piece.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a picture of a jar or a cup that people use to carry drinking water. Pause this audio here.

The woman responds to Elijah's request by declaring a strong oath, "As surely as Yahweh your God lives," and says that she does not have any bread to share with Elijah. When the woman says, "Yahweh your God," this shows that she recognizes that Elijah is from Israel and that she knows the God of Israel's name: Yahweh.
