Stop here and discuss these questions as a group: Why do you think the woman uses an oath here? What are some situations in your culture in which you would use an oath? How would you say that oath? Pause this audio here.

The woman explains to Elijah that all she has left for food is a handful of flour and a small amount of olive oil. Flour and olive oil are the main ingredients to make a flat, round type of bread. People make flour by grinding grain into a fine powder. People at this time stored flour in jars. People made the jars from clay. These jars were large enough to hold about two kilograms of flour.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a picture of a handful of flour and the container that people used to store the flour. What do you use to store flour? Pause this audio here.

The woman also says that she has olive oil in a jug. Olive oil is from the fruit of the olive tree, which people press and process to extract its oil. People would store olive oil in a jug, most likely a narrow container that they made of clay. The container was a special shape for pouring liquids, and was about 7.5 to 15 centimeters tall.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a picture of olive oil and a container that people used to store the oil. What do you use to store oil or liquids? Pause this audio here.

The woman explains to Elijah that she is gathering sticks to bring home and make a fire to cook a last meal for herself and her son. By saying, "that we may eat it and die," the woman shows that she does not expect to have any food left after this last meal. The woman has lost hope for survival and believes that she and her son will starve to death.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Share a story you know about a time when someone had lost all hope. How did this person speak and behave to show that they no longer hoped that good things would happen? Pause this audio here.

Elijah sees that the woman has lost all hope of survival for herself and her son. Elijah knows that Yahweh has sent Elijah to this particular woman so that she can provide him with food, and Elijah knows that Yahweh always keeps his promises. Knowing this, Elijah is able to give clear instructions to the woman, telling her what Yahweh has told Elijah. Elijah tells the woman to go ahead and make bread for Elijah first, and then she will be able to make more bread for herself and her son. How is this possible? Elijah explains that Yahweh has promised that the woman will not use up all the flour and the oil will not run dry until the next time it rains.

The woman obeys and finds that the word of Yahweh that Elijah has spoken to her is true! The jars of flour and oil miraculously fill up again every day, and the woman is able to continue to make bread for herself, her son, and Elijah every day.

Stop here and discuss these questions as a group: Share a story you know about a time when God provided for someone's needs in a miraculous way. How does that experience change the way you think about God? Pause this audio here.

This passage is important because Yahweh shows his power to meet the human need for food. Yahweh provides food in a miraculous way that is completely outside of the usual ways people grow and get food. Yahweh does not need to wait for rain or for grain to grow to make flour. Yahweh does not need to wait for olive trees to produce olives to make olive oil. The false gods like Baal have no power to do anything.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 17:8–16 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has four scenes.

In the first scene: The brook dries up, and Yahweh speaks to Elijah and instructs him to go to the city of Zarephath. Yahweh promises Elijah that in Zarephath, Elijah will meet a widow, or a woman whose husband has died. This woman will provide food for Elijah.

In the second scene: Elijah meets the widow at the town gate and asks her for water. As the woman goes away to get water for Elijah, he also asks for a piece of bread. The woman explains that she is almost out of food and will soon prepare her last meal for herself and her son. Elijah gives the woman instructions to go home and make bread for Elijah, and then make bread for herself and her son.

In the third scene: The woman follows Elijah's instructions and goes home to make bread for Elijah and also for herself and her son, and brings Elijah's bread back to him.

In the fourth scene: The woman goes home and continues to make bread for herself, her son, her household, and Elijah for many days, even though there is still no rain.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Yahweh
  • Elijah
  • The "widow," or the woman whose husband has died
  • And the widow's son and household

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

Remember, before the first scene, Elijah was staying in the Kerith Valley near a brook and drinking water from it. Now, in the first scene, the brook dries up, so Yahweh tells Elijah where to go next. Yahweh tells Elijah to get up and go right away to Zarephath, northeast of where Elijah is. When Yahweh says, "get up," it does not mean that Elijah is sitting down and needs to stand up. This is a way to tell someone that they must go somewhere right away. Zarephath is a town in the region of Sidon, very close to the Mediterranean Sea. Yahweh tells Elijah that Elijah will meet a woman there, and she will give Elijah food. The woman is a widow, which means her husband is dead.

Stop here and, if needed, look again at the map that shows how Elijah might travel from the Kerith Valley to Zarephath. Pause this audio here.

In the second scene, Elijah comes to the town gate at Zarephath and perhaps stops to rest there. Elijah sees a woman picking up sticks and calls out to her to bring Elijah some water.

Stop here and answer this question as a group: Elijah "calls out" to the woman, which lets us know that she is a short distance away from Elijah. What words do you use in your language to show that someone is near enough to hear but not close enough to have a conversation? Pause this audio here.

Elijah may recognize this woman as a widow based on her clothing, because in those days it was common for widows to wear certain colors to show their status as widows. Sometimes life could be very difficult for a widow because people often did not allow widows to work to support themselves. Widows might be dependent on family members and the community to support them.

Remember, when Elijah sees the woman at the gate, she is gathering small sticks of wood. The woman is gathering wood to bring home to make a cooking fire, and will cook a last meal for herself and her son to eat.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What do you use to build a fire? What different types of fires for different purposes do people make in your area? How do people make different types of fires? Pause this audio here.

In addition to carrying the sticks, the woman may also be carrying a small clay jar for water. This jar is what she uses to bring Elijah some water. Elijah's instructions to the woman are to "make a small loaf of bread for me from what you have and bring it to me, and then make something for yourself and your son."

Stop here and look at a picture of bread that people made from flour and olive oil. Discuss these questions as a group: What ingredients do you use to make bread in your community? What does the bread look like? How is the bread the same or different from the picture of bread you see? Pause this audio here.

In the third scene, the woman goes away from Elijah back to her home and makes the bread for Elijah using the flour in her jar and the olive oil in her jug. Then the woman finds she is able to make a second loaf of bread because the jar fills up with more flour and the jug fills up with more olive oil! We do not know if the woman brings the bread to Elijah and then goes home again and makes the second bread for herself and her son.

In the fourth scene, we see that the woman is able to continue to make bread for Elijah, herself, and her household every day. We do not know if anyone lived in the house with the woman besides her son. If there were other people in the house, we know there was enough flour and oil to make bread for all of them. We do not know how many days the miracle of the flour and oil continued, but we do know that this miracle lasted until the rain came again. The dry time lasted for several years. Yahweh's promise to the widow came true: Yahweh had said that the widow would not use up all of her flour and that the olive oil would not run dry, and that is indeed what happened. The widow never used up all the flour, and the jar of olive oil continued to fill up. The storyteller emphasizes that Yahweh's promise to the widow came true when the storyteller summarizes the story with the same words as the promise from Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your language, how do you show that the same thing continues to happen again and again for many days? Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 17:8–16 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has 4 scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Yahweh
  • Elijah
  • The "widow," or the woman whose husband has died
  • And the widow's son and household

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

For the first scene, act out Yahweh giving instructions to Elijah to go to Zarephath. Yahweh tells Elijah to "go at once," as soon as possible. Yahweh also describes how Yahweh will take care of Elijah through the widow. Act out this scene now.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Elijah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am glad Yahweh has a plan to take care of me; or
  • How will I know which woman to talk to in Zarephath?

Continue the drama.

For the second scene, act out the woman picking up sticks at Zarephath's town gate, and Elijah asking the woman first for water and then for bread. Remember, first Elijah calls out to the woman from a distance to ask for water. Then, while the woman is going away to get the water, Elijah calls out to her again and asks for a piece of bread. The woman swears an oath and says, "As surely as Yahweh your God lives," and explains that what Elijah is asking for is too much, because she only has enough to feed herself and her son one last meal. Then Elijah tells the woman that Yahweh told Elijah that her flour and oil will not run out until it rains again. Act out this scene now.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the woman in Zarephath whose husband has died, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am very sad because I know my son and I will die soon; or
  • Why does this man keep bothering me? Does he not see that I am poor and starving? or
  • I really hope what this man says will happen is true.

Ask the person playing Elijah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am excited to show this Phoenician woman, who does not know Yahweh, how powerful Yahweh is; or
  • I hope this woman follows my instructions and comes back with the bread.

Continue the drama.

For the third scene, act out the woman returning to her home and following Elijah's instructions. First, act out the woman making bread for Elijah, then making bread for herself and her son. Also, act out the woman bringing the bread back to Elijah. Act out this scene now

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the woman, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I really hope this man is not tricking me. This may be the only bread I can make; or
  • This is amazing! I know I used up all of the flour, but here is more! or
  • Elijah, your god, Yahweh, is very powerful! I have never seen Baal do anything like this!

Continue the drama.

For the fourth scene, act out the woman making bread again and again for herself, her son, and Elijah, while there is still no rain. Act out this scene now.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the woman, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • There was flour and oil yesterday, but will there still be flour and oil again today? or
  • Who is this god who can bring flour and oil out of nothing? or
  • I am so happy that my son is still alive and healthy because of how Yahweh provides for us.

Ask the person playing the son, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • My mother was so worried about having food before, and now she is not! or
  • I am so happy because my stomach is never empty!

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 17:8–16 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Use the same word or phrase for "word of Yahweh" that you have used in previous passages. For more information, refer to word in the Master Glossary.

You have heard the words Yahweh, God, and Israel many times in 1 Kings. Use the same words or phrases for Yahweh, God, and Israel as you used in previous passages. For more information on Yahweh, God, or Israel, refer to the Master Glossary.

Use the same name for Elijah as you used in previous passages.

Elijah meets a widow in Zarephath. This means a woman whose husband is dead. We do not know if the widow's husband died a long time ago or recently died. Use the same word or phrase for widow that you used in previous passages.

Use the same name for the region of Sidon as you used in previous passages. Sidon is a part of the country of Phoenicia, on the coast of the Mediterranean Sea.

Zarephath is a city located south of Sidon, in the country of Phoenicia, near the Mediterranean Sea. A city is a settlement that usually has a wall around it for protection. Use the same word for city here as you have used previously.

Stop here and discuss as a group what name you will use for Zarephath. If needed, look at the map again to see where Zarephath is located. Pause this audio here.

Elijah asks the woman for a piece of bread. Most likely, Elijah means a chunk of bread that someone pulled by hand from a full loaf of bread.

Stop here and look at a photo of bread again if needed. Discuss as a group what words you use for a full loaf of bread and for just a piece of bread torn off by hand. Pause this audio here.

Remember that a jar refers to a larger clay container that people used to store flour. A jug refers to a smaller container people used to store oil. Use the same word for jar and jug as you used in previous passages.

Stop here and look at a photo of a jar and a large clay container again, if needed. Pause this audio here.

The woman tells Elijah that she only has a handful of flour and a little olive oil left. We do not know the exact amount.

Stop here and look at photos of flour and olive oil again if needed. Discuss as a group what words you will use for a handful of flour and a little olive oil. Pause this audio here.

Elijah tells the woman that the jar of flour will not be used up and the jug of oil will not run dry. This means that the woman will continue to find flour and oil in the containers every time she goes to get flour and oil out of them!

Stop here and discuss as a group what words you will use to describe how the flour is not used up and how the oil does not run dry. Pause this audio here.

The woman makes bread for herself and her household or her family. If you decide you want to use a word that means something like household, continue to use the same word you used previously. Refer to the Master Glossary for more information about household.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 17:17–24
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 17:17–24 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 17:17–24 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 17:17–24 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the story before this one, we heard about the widow in the city of Zarephath. The woman is able to keep feeding herself, her son, her household, and Elijah from a jar of flour and a jug of olive oil that never runs out. There has been no rain in the land for a long time, but Yahweh continues to miraculously provide for Elijah's and the widow's needs. The widow is not from Israel but from the country of Phoenicia. The people in Phoenicia do not yet know Yahweh. They worship the false god, Baal.

Some time has passed, and we learn that Elijah is living in the upper room of the widow's house in Zarephath. The widow owns this house. The house has two levels or floors. The first floor is the main part of the house, and this is where the widow and her son eat and sleep. The second level of the house, the upper room, is where Elijah sleeps. We do not know for sure, but most likely there were stairs on the outside of the house that went up to the roof of the first level. There was a roof, but there may or may not have been walls.

Stop here and look at a picture of a house with two levels: a first level or floor and an upper room. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What do houses look like in your area? What did houses look like a long time ago in your area? How are they the same as or different from the house you see in the picture? Pause this audio here.

Now something very tragic happens: the widow's son becomes very sick and keeps getting more and more sick until finally he dies. Of course, the boy's mother is very sad, confused, and maybe even angry. The woman questions Elijah about why her son is dead. The woman asks Elijah if Yahweh is punishing her because of her past sins. We do not know what sin the woman is talking about.

Stop here and discuss these questions as a group: What stories do you know about someone who experiences a great loss or tragedy? What kinds of questions do you think that person might ask after this very sad thing happens? How might the questions be different for a person who believes in God and a person who does not believe in God? Pause this audio here.

After the woman questions Elijah and shares her grief, Elijah takes the boy up to the upper room and lays him on the bed there. Now Elijah shares his own grief and confusion with Yahweh. When Elijah says that he is the woman's guest, Elijah is reminding Yahweh that the woman herself is not an Israelite. The woman does not know Yahweh the same way the people of Israel should know Yahweh. With faith, Elijah begs Yahweh to bring life back to the dead boy. Yahweh responds to Elijah's faith, and Yahweh hears Elijah and does indeed bring life back to the boy.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Imagine you are a guest in someone's home, and while you are staying there, something awful happens. Your host does not know God. What would you say to your host? What would you say to God?

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 17:17–24 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has three scenes.

In the first scene: The woman's son becomes sick and dies. The woman asks Elijah if Yahweh is punishing her for her past sin. Elijah does not answer the woman's question—Elijah only asks her to give the boy's body to him.

In the second scene: Elijah takes the dead boy to the upper room of the house and lays the boy down on the bed. Elijah calls out to Yahweh his God, and asks why this tragedy happened. Elijah stretches himself out over the boy three times and prays to Yahweh to return the boy to life. Yahweh hears Elijah's prayer and gives life to the boy again.

In the third scene: Elijah brings the boy back down the stairs and gives the boy to his mother, the woman. The woman tells Elijah that now she sees that Elijah is truly a man of God and that the word of Yahweh is truth.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Elijah
  • The woman who owns the house—the widow
  • The woman's son, a young boy
  • And Yahweh

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

Remember, Elijah has been staying in the woman's house, where he sleeps on the second floor, in the upper room. We know that there is at least a bed in this room. The woman and her son sleep on the first or main floor of the house.

Stop here and, as a group, look again at a picture of what the woman's house probably looked like. Pause this audio here.

This first or main floor part of the house is where the boy gets sick and dies. The storyteller tells us that the boy stopped breathing. This is another way to say that the boy died.

Stop here and discuss this question: What are some ways that your culture talks about how someone dies, especially after an illness? How will you say that the boy was sick and finally stopped breathing? Pause this audio here.

The woman calls Elijah "Man of God," and she asks Elijah if the boy is dead because Yahweh is punishing her for her sin. The woman calls Elijah "Man of God" and asks, "What do you have against me?" in a way that shows that she blames Elijah for bringing this tragedy into her home. The woman is not really asking Elijah for an answer. The woman asks this question to show that she blames Elijah and his God for her son's death.

Stop here and discuss as a group what phrase you will use for "What do you have against me?"

If someone you helped does something wrong to you, what do you say to them or ask them? Pause this audio here.

Elijah only answers the woman with a command: "Give me your son." Elijah takes the boy up to the upper room of the house and lays him on the bed.

Stop here and look as a group at a picture of what a bed in Elijah's time probably looked like. Pause this audio here.

Elijah cries out to Yahweh with strong emotions and asks why Yahweh let this awful thing happen to the woman who helped Elijah. Even though Elijah is a visitor from another country, the woman has been kind and generous to let Elijah stay in her house and to feed him. "Cries out" here does not mean that Elijah was only weeping. "Cries out" means that Elijah cried loudly to or called out to Yahweh.

Then Elijah "stretches himself out" over the boy's body where it lies on the bed. We do not know exactly what this looks like. Maybe Elijah is showing his grief, or maybe Elijah thinks that putting his warm body close to the boy's lifeless body will help bring the boy to life again. While Elijah is stretching himself out over the dead boy, Elijah begs Yahweh to "let this boy's life return to him." It seems like the boy comes to life again pretty quickly, because we know Elijah stretches out on the body only three times.

Stop here and have two people in your group act like Elijah and the dead boy. Show what it might look like for Elijah to "stretch out" on the boy's body. After you act this out, discuss what words you will use to describe this action of stretching out on the body. Note that this should be two women or two men acting this out together. Pause this audio here.

When the boy comes alive again, Elijah picks the boy up, brings him down the stairs, and gives him to his mother. This is how we know that the boy must be young, maybe a child. As the woman receives her son back from Elijah, she says she does believe that Elijah is a man of God and that the word of Yahweh, or God's promises, that Elijah speaks are true.

The woman calls Elijah a "man of God" two times in this story. The first time the woman says "man of God" is in the first scene, right after her son dies. The woman questions Elijah about why her son is dead. The woman seems to blame Elijah, as God's representative, for her son's death. The second time the woman says "man of God" is right after Elijah gives her son back to her, alive.

Stop here and discuss these questions as a group: What is the difference between the first time the woman says "man of God" and the second time she says it? How do you think the woman's voice sounded when she said "man of God" each time? Pause this audio here.

The woman says to Elijah, "The word of Yahweh from your mouth is the truth." Now the woman understands clearly that Elijah speaks the words of the powerful and life-giving God of Israel. Nothing can stop God from doing what God wants to do: not even a lack of rain, sickness, or death. Yahweh's words are powerful and true.

Stop here and discuss how you will describe the truth of the "word of Yahweh" coming from Elijah's mouth. What is different about saying that Yahweh's words are true, rather than just saying that someone keeps a promise or tells the truth? Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 17:17–24 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has three scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Elijah
  • The woman who owns the house—the widow
  • The woman's son, a young boy
  • And Yahweh

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

In the first scene, the woman's son becomes very sick and keeps getting more sick until he dies. The woman questions Elijah about why this terrible thing has happened to her. The woman asks Elijah if it is because of her sins and if she is being punished. Remember, the woman calls Elijah a "man of God" here.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the woman, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I have been helping this "man of God" by letting him live with me and feeding him, and now this man's God is punishing me for my sin by killing my son? or
  • I should never have let this "man of God" stay with us. Look at what happened! or
  • I am so sad because my child is dead. I want to understand why!

Continue the drama.

In the second scene, Elijah takes the woman's son upstairs to his bed in the upper room. Elijah lays the boy down on the bed. Then Elijah cries out to Yahweh God, asking why God allowed the boy to die. Next, Elijah "stretches himself out" over the boy three times and begs Yahweh to let life return to the dead boy.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Elijah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I do not understand why Yahweh would give this woman flour and oil that never runs out, save us from starving, and then let her son become sick and die; or
  • Can I still trust Yahweh to do another miracle?

Continue the drama.

Yahweh hears Elijah, and the boy becomes alive again.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I love to answer the prayers from the people who have faith in me.

Ask the person playing Elijah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Praise you, Yahweh! You are amazing, and only you can bring a dead person back to life! or
  • Thank you for answering my prayer!

Ask the person playing the son, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer:

  • Wow, I feel great! I wonder why Elijah looks so happy to see me?

Continue the drama.

In the third scene, Elijah picks up the boy from the bed and brings him downstairs to his mother. The woman says again to Elijah that he is a "man of God" and that now she knows and believes that the word of Yahweh that Elijah speaks is the truth.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the woman, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Can I believe what I am seeing?! Is my child really alive again? or
  • I have never seen anyone dead come back to life. The words of Elijah's god are very powerful! or
  • How could I forget that this is the same Yahweh who keeps giving us flour and oil that never runs out?

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 17:17–24 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

When the woman's son dies, she addresses Elijah as "man of God." Earlier, the woman heard the word of Yahweh from Elijah, and she saw that her supply of flour and oil never ended. The woman clearly understands that Elijah has a relationship with a powerful god. Use the same phrase for "man of God" that you used in previous passages. People sometimes called prophets "men of God." For more information on "man of God," refer to prophet in the Master Glossary.

The woman asks Elijah why Yahweh is punishing her for her sin. When someone sins, they disobey God. Use the same word or phrase for sin as you have used in previous passages. For more information, refer to sin in the Master Glossary.

Remember, the upper room is a room on top of the main room of a house, and a person climbs up stairs to enter the upper room. Often, the stairs were on the outside of the house.

If necessary, stop here and look again as a group at a picture of an upper room. What word or phrase will you use to describe this upper part of a house? Pause this audio here.

Elijah calls Yahweh "Yahweh my God." Use the same phrase for "Yahweh my God" as you used in previous passages. For more information on Yahweh and God, see the Master Glossary.

Elijah calls out to God and asks why God brought this tragedy on the widow. "Tragedy" means the awful thing that happened, that the woman's beloved son died. Use the same word for "widow," or a woman whose husband is dead, as you used in previous passages.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for tragedy. Remember the story that you told in step two about someone who experienced an awful thing in their life. What word or phrase does your community use to describe when something awful happens to a person they love? Pause this audio here.

Elijah says that he prays to Yahweh and asks Yahweh to let life return to the boy. Use the same word or phrase for pray as you have used previously. For more information about pray, refer to the Master Glossary.

The widow says that now she knows that the word of Yahweh is true. Use the same phrase for "word of Yahweh" as you used in previous passages. For more information, see "Word" in the Master Glossary.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 18:1–15
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 18:1–15 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 18:1–15 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 18:1–15 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

This story is about the prophet Elijah meeting King Ahab after a long famine. A famine is when there is no rain for a long time, and it hurts crops and animals. Yahweh sent this famine as a punishment because King Ahab and Queen Jezebel led the people of Israel to worship false gods and hurt Yahweh's prophets.

In the last story, you will remember that Elijah was hiding in another country because Yahweh had told Elijah to hide during the famine. Yahweh took care of Elijah during the famine. Now, Yahweh tells Elijah that Yahweh will end the famine. Elijah must go tell King Ahab so that Ahab knows that Yahweh caused the famine and that Yahweh will also end it.

Elijah leaves the town of Zarephath to go to Samaria. Samaria was the capital city of Israel at that time. Sometimes people called the whole land of Israel "Samaria."

Stop here and look at the map that includes Zarephath and Samaria. Pause this audio here.

You will remember that Yahweh had promised there would be a famine. The famine had now lasted three years.

During the famine, there was little grass and water. Grass and water were both important for the king's horses and mules. So now, King Ahab and his palace manager, a man named Obadiah, go in different directions to search for grass near any streams for the animals. As palace manager, Obadiah helped take care of the king's house, land, and possessions.

Ahab and Obadiah hoped to find grass or water in springs or hidden valleys. These dry river valleys are small valleys that fill with water when it rains but stay dry during a drought.

Stop here and look at photos of a spring and a dry river valley. Discuss this question as a group: Talk about a time when there was no rain, and the plants died. Imagine what it would be like if there were no rain for three years. Pause this audio here.

Obadiah sees Elijah and bows down to show respect. When Elijah tells Obadiah to tell Ahab that Elijah is there, Obadiah says, "What have I done wrong? Are you trying to get me killed?"

You remember that during the famine, Ahab had searched for Elijah everywhere—even in other countries. Ahab made people promise they were not hiding Elijah. Obadiah was afraid that if he went to tell Ahab that Elijah was there, Yahweh might move Elijah somewhere else. Then Ahab would be angry and kill Obadiah.

Obadiah wanted Elijah to know he loved and followed Yahweh. Obadiah reminded Elijah that when Queen Jezebel tried to kill Yahweh's prophets, Obadiah hid 100 of them in two caves—50 prophets in each cave. Obadiah gave the prophets food and water to help them stay safe. Just like Yahweh sent ravens and a widow to care for Elijah, Obadiah cared for the prophets.

Stop here and look at a picture of a cave as a group. Pause this audio here.

We know Obadiah told the truth because other parts of the Bible say the same thing. The Bible says Obadiah truly believed in Yahweh.

Elijah made a promise to Obadiah. Elijah said, "As surely as Yahweh of Heaven's Armies lives, whom I serve, I will go to Ahab today."

"Yahweh of heaven's armies " means Yahweh is powerful and rules over many angels in heaven. Elijah says he stands in Yahweh's presence, meaning he is truly Yahweh's prophet. By making this strong promise, Elijah is saying he trusts Yahweh to take care of everything.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 18:1–15 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has three scenes.

In the first scene: Yahweh tells Elijah, "Go and show yourself to Ahab, and I will send rain on the land." It is the third year of the famine. Yahweh is ready to end the famine, and Elijah must deliver the message.

In the second scene: King Ahab and his palace administrator, Obadiah, are looking for any grass or water left in the land. Ahab and Obadiah hope to save the animals—the horses, mules, and other livestock. In this scene, the Bible also tells us something important about Obadiah: he truly followed Yahweh. Earlier in Obadiah's life, he had hidden 100 of Yahweh's prophets in caves when Queen Jezebel was trying to kill them.

In the third scene: Elijah appears before Obadiah. Obadiah is respectful but frightened. Obadiah is nervous that his own life will be in danger if Elijah disappears again, so Obadiah describes how he saved Yahweh's prophets. Elijah swears that he will show himself to Ahab.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Elijah
  • Yahweh
  • King Ahab
  • Obadiah
  • Jezebel
  • And the prophets of Yahweh

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

Long before this story happens, Jezebel, the queen, tried to kill all the prophets of Yahweh so that she could establish the prophets of her false gods in Israel.

In scene one, this happens after three years of no rain.

Stop and discuss with the team how you will start this passage so that it is clear that a long time has passed from the previous story. Pause the audio here.

Elijah is still in a foreign land, but Yahweh tells Elijah to return. We do not know how Yahweh spoke to Elijah, but it is clear that it was Yahweh who told Elijah to return.

Yahweh says that Yahweh will send rain and that Elijah must go and present himself to Ahab.

Stop and discuss how you will make it clear that Elijah is not just waiting around for Ahab to see him. Elijah is supposed to go to Ahab and talk with him. Pause the audio here.

Stop and show a picture of the landscape of Samaria. Pause the audio here.

Scene two takes place in Samaria. Ahab calls Obadiah to meet with him.

The storyteller gives us background about Obadiah. Obadiah fears, or respects and honors, Yahweh greatly. In the past, Obadiah hid 100 of Yahweh's prophets in caves and gave them bread and water. In this case, bread really means food in general.

Stop and discuss what word you will use for bread to make it clear that it is food. Pause the audio here.

Stop and discuss how to make it clear that when Obadiah hid the 100 prophets, this occurred long before Ahab and Obadiah are talking in this scene. Pause the audio here.

After that background, the storyteller tells us Ahab's plan. Ahab and Obadiah will split up and search the land for springs and valleys that have grass. Springs are streams of water that flow out of the ground. Valleys are low areas that are often dry when it has not rained, but during times of rain, there are streams of water. People are more likely to find grass near these water sources. Ahab goes one way to look for grass, and Obadiah goes the other.

In the third scene, Obadiah is walking and suddenly sees Elijah. Obadiah bows down, not to worship Elijah, but to show respect because Elijah is a prophet. Obadiah is not sure if it is really Elijah. In fact, Obadiah asks Elijah, "Is it you, my lord, or master, Elijah?" No one in Israel has seen Elijah for years. Elijah confirms that he is indeed Elijah. Elijah tells Obadiah to tell his lord, or master, the king, "Look! Elijah is here." Elijah's words mean, "I have found Elijah!" or "I have seen Elijah!" Obadiah gets worried that Yahweh's Spirit will take Elijah away while Obadiah is going to get Ahab. If Obadiah tells Ahab about Elijah and then Elijah disappears again, Ahab might kill Obadiah.

Obadiah says Ahab sent people to search for Elijah absolutely everywhere. Obadiah greatly emphasizes the certainty of the search by swearing, "as surely as Yahweh your God lives." Obadiah greatly emphasizes the wide scope of the search by saying, "There is not a nation or kingdom where Ahab has not sent someone to search for Elijah." King Ahab even made people in other nations swear oaths that Elijah was not in their land. Obadiah is telling what great effort the king has gone to in order to find Elijah so that Elijah will understand how angry Ahab would be if Obadiah said he found Eljiiah and then Elijah disappeared. Obadiah tells Elijah what the storyteller already told us: how Obadiah had risked his life to hide 100 prophets of Yahweh in caves and give them food and water to protect them from Jezebel. Obadiah wants Elijah to understand that Obadiah is faithful to Yahweh.

Elijah promises that he will not disappear. Elijah makes an oath that, as surely as Yahweh lives, Elijah will meet with Ahab today. Elijah is saying that just as it is true that Yahweh lives, it is true that Elijah will appear before Ahab today.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 18:1–15 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has three scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Elijah
  • Yahweh
  • King Ahab
  • Obadiah
  • Jezebel
  • And the prophets of Yahweh

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or t hinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

In scene one, it has been three years since the famine began. Yahweh tells Elijah to go to Ahab and that Yahweh will send rain on the land.

Stop the action. Ask the person playing Elijah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am ready to go back home to Israel, but I am afraid for my life; or
  • I am happy to hear this horrible famine will end.

Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am ready to bring rain, but the people are not ready to turn back to me.

Continue the drama.

Elijah responds to Yahweh by going to present himself to Ahab.

In scene two, the famine has been very severe in the capital of Samaria, so Ahab calls for his palace manager, Obadiah, to come to him. Obadiah is a devout follower of Yahweh. In the past, King Ahab's wife Jezebel put plans in place to kill off all of the prophets of Yahweh, but Obadiah took 100 of the prophets and hid them in two caves. Obadiah provided for the prophets by supplying them with food and water.

Stop the action. Ask the person playing Obadiah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer with things like:

  • I am afraid Jezebel may try to kill me, but I know that no one else has the resources to protect these prophets of Yahweh; or
  • At any moment, Jezebel could catch me and kill me.

Continue the drama.

Back to the present, Ahab tells Obadiah that Ahab and Obadiah will search all the land for grass to keep the horses and mules alive.

Stop the action. Ask the person playing Ahab, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer with things like:

  • I am a king over the land, but I cannot even keep my horses alive; or
  • I am so angry at that bad prophet, Elijah. I am a king, but instead of building a strong kingdom, I must go through all the land just looking for grass for animals.

Continue the drama.

So Ahab and Obadiah divide up where they will search and go in two different directions.

In scene three, Elijah walks up to Obadiah, who is walking alone. Obadiah recognizes Elijah, bows to the ground, and asks him if he is really Elijah. Elijah replies, "Yes. Go tell your master that Elijah is here." Then Obadiah asks, "What have I done wrong that you are handing me over to Ahab to be put to death?" Obadiah tells Elijah how the king searched throughout the land for Elijah and even forced kings from other nations to swear oaths that Elijah was not in their lands. Now, Elijah tells Obadiah to go to Ahab and say, "Elijah is here." Obadiah does not know if the Spirit of the Lord might carry off Elijah to protect Elijah while Obadiah would be left to face the anger of King Ahab, who would surely kill Obadiah if that happened. Obadiah tells Elijah that Obadiah has worshipped Yahweh ever since he was a youth. When Jezebel was killing all the prophets of Yahweh, Obadiah risked his life to hide and care for 100 prophets in two caves. Now, Elijah is telling Obadiah to go to the king and say, "Elijah is here." Ahab will surely kill Obadiah.

Stop the action. Ask the person playing Elijah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer with things like:

  • Obadiah has been faithful. I understand Obadiah's fear; or
  • Yahweh is at work in more people than I realized.

Ask the person playing Obadiah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer with things like:

  • I feel extreme fear; or
  • I feel frustrated. I have already risked everything. Will this cost me my life?

Continue the drama.

Elijah reassures Obadiah with an oath: "As Yahweh of Heaven's Armies lives, whom I serve, I will surely present myself to Ahab today."

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 18:1–15 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

This story is about the prophet Elijah coming to see King Ahab after a long period of famine.

The "word of Yahweh" comes to Elijah during the third year of the famine. Translate "word of Yahweh" in the same way you have in previous passages. For more information on word and Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary.

Yahweh told Elijah to go and show himself to King Ahab in Samaria. You will remember that Samaria was a city in northern Israel. Translate Samaria in the same way you have in previous passages. For more information on Samaria, refer to the Master Glossary.

King Ahab had been searching for Elijah, the prophet who predicted the famine. A famine is a time of severe hunger, often caused by a lack of rain. This famine had lasted for three years. Ahab and his palace manager, Obadiah, were looking for grass and water to save their animals, especially horses and mules. Obadiah was a palace manager who managed the royal lands and possessions.

Stop and discuss with the team how you will translate the term palace manager. Use the same word for palace, or king's house, that you have used in previous passages. Pause the audio here.

Even though he worked for an evil king, Obadiah followed Yahweh. Obadiah calls Elijah his "lord," and Elijah calls Ahab Obadiah's "lord." A lord is someone who is a master over other people, who has full authority, and who deserves for people to give him respect. In the Bible, we hear people address their king as lord and slaves call their master lord. Use the same word or phrase for "lord" or master as you have used in previous passages, and for more information on lord, refer to the Master Glossary.

Obadiah had hidden 100 of Yahweh's prophets in two caves while Jezebel was trying to kill them. Use the same word or phrase for prophets as you have used in previous passages. For more information about prophet, refer to the Master Glossary.

Ahab had told Obadiah to go look in all the springs and valleys for water. A spring is a place where fresh water comes up directly from the ground. A valley here is the same dry river valley from previous passages. You will remember that these dry river valleys are dry when it does not rain, and full of water when there is enough rain. Use the same word or phrase for these kinds of "wadis," or dry river valleys.

Stop here and look at a photo of a spring and a photo of a dry river valley. Discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for spring. Pause this audio here.

Obadiah feared Yahweh, which means that Obadiah worshipped and respected Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use to say that Obadiah feared Yahweh. For more information about fear of Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary.

When Obadiah saw Elijah, he bowed to the ground because he respected Elijah. Use the same word or phrase for bow as you used in previous passages. For more information about bow, refer to the Master Glossary.

Obadiah calls himself a servant of Elijah and asks what sin he has committed. A servant is a person who does work for someone else. Obadiah is not a slave or a servant who works in Elijah's household, but Obadiah respects Elijah's authority over him as a prophet of Yahweh. Use the same word or phrase for servant when it means something similar, and for more information on servant, refer to the Master Glossary.

Some versions translate "what sin have I committed" as "what have I done wrong?" A sin is an act of rebellion against Yahweh. Obadiah asks what he has done wrong against Yahweh or Elijah that would be reason to put Obadiah's life in danger to tell Ahab that Elijah has come back. Use the same word or phrase for sin here as you have used in previous passages. For more information on sin, refer to the Master Glossary.

Obadiah tells Elijah that as surely as Yahweh his God lives, King Ahab has searched every nation and kingdom for Elijah. A nation is a group of people who belong together, who live in the same country, and who have the same ruler. A kingdom is the territory that a king rules. Use the same words or phrases for God, nation, and kingdom as you have used in previous passages. For more information on God, nation, and kingdom, refer to the Master Glossary.

Obadiah says that he has worshiped Yahweh since he was young. Use the same word or phrase for worship as you have used in previous passages. For more information about worship, refer to the Master Glossary.

Obadiah is afraid that if he tells King Ahab that Elijah is there, the Spirit of Yahweh will take Elijah away and hide him. Then Ahab will be angry and might kill Obadiah for lying. In the Old Testament, the phrase "the Spirit of Yahweh" often means Yahweh's power and presence in action. It shows how Yahweh works in the world, like guiding people, giving them strength, or helping prophets speak Yahweh's message. In the New Testament, we hear about "the Spirit of Yahweh" or "the Holy Spirit." The Holy Spirit is one of the three persons of God—Father, Son (Jesus), and Holy Spirit. Although in this passage you should refer to God's Spirit as the Spirit of Yahweh, for more information, refer to the Holy Spirit in the Master Glossary.

When Obadiah meets Elijah, Elijah tells him to inform King Ahab that he is here. But Obadiah is afraid. Obadiah fears that if he tells Ahab and then Elijah disappears again, Ahab might kill Obadiah out of anger. Elijah promises with an oath, or strong promise, that he will surely meet Ahab that very day. The storyteller does not use the word for oath, but for more information about oath, you may refer to oath in the Master Glossary.

Elijah's oath uses a special name for Yahweh: "Yahweh of Heaven's Armies." Some versions translate this name for Yahweh as "Yahweh of Hosts" or "Yahweh Almighty." This name emphasizes that Yahweh is a mighty ruler who commands vast heavenly armies. Elijah is showing that he does not fear an earthly king like Ahab because Elijah serves the almighty Yahweh, who leads the armies of heaven. This title teaches us that Yahweh is powerful, sovereign, and able to act on behalf of Yahweh's people.

Stop here and discuss as a group what phrase you will use for Yahweh of Heaven's Armies. For more information, refer to "Yahweh of Hosts" in the Master Glossary.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 18:16–29
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 18:16–29 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 18:16–29 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 18:16–29 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

You will remember that King Ahab searched for Elijah during the three years of famine. After three years, Elijah came to King Ahab's palace manager, Obadiah, to tell him that Elijah was there to see him. At the beginning of this part of the story, Obadiah tells Ahab that he has found Elijah. Then Ahab goes to see Elijah.

Ahab calls Elijah a troublemaker of Israel. A troublemaker is someone who causes problems or ruin for someone else. Ahab is saying that Elijah has caused the great famine. But Elijah says Ahab and Ahab's father, Omri, who was king before Ahab, were the ones who caused the trouble for Israel. Ahab and Omri stopped following Yahweh's commands and turned instead to the false gods of Baal and Asherah.

You will remember that Ahab's wife, Queen Jezebel, was not an Israelite. Jezebel was a Canaanite. When Jezebel became queen, she brought the worship of Baal and Asherah to Israel and tried to kill all the prophets of Yahweh.

Baal was a false god that the Canaanite people had worshiped before the Israelites conquered the land and pushed out the Canaanites and their worship of Baal. Baal was the storm god whom the people thought had power over rain and fertility. Three years before this story, Elijah told Ahab that Yahweh would not allow it to rain and cause a great famine. The famine proved that the false god Baal had no power to bring rain.

Asherah was the female partner of Baal and another false god of the Canaanites. People set up wooden poles on which they may have carved images of Asherah in her honor.

Elijah called for King Ahab to invite all the people of Israel to Mount Carmel and bring the 450 prophets of Baal and the 400 prophets of Asherah. The story says the prophets ate at Jezebel's table. Jezebel probably did not have a table where the 850 false prophets ate. Jezebel supported the prophets of Baal and Asherah by providing them with food from the royal supplies.

Mount Carmel was a high mountain by the Mediterranean Sea in the northwest of Israel. Mount Carmel was near the border of the land where Jezebel grew up and worshiped Baal and Asherah.

Stop here and look at a map and a picture of Mount Carmel as a group. Pause this audio here.

Elijah, Ahab, the people of Israel, and the 850 prophets of Baal and Asherah gather on Mount Carmel. Elijah, the only prophet of Yahweh at Mount Carmel, confronts the people. Elijah asks how long the people will limp along with two different opinions, or jump between two different opinions. Elijah challenges the people to decide which one is the true God and follow that God. If Yahweh is the true God, follow him. If Baal is the true god, follow him. To follow God is to obey and worship him. But the people stay silent and do not answer Elijah.

Elijah proposes a contest between Baal and Yahweh to see who is the true god. Elijah and the prophets of Baal will prepare two different sacrifices of bulls. Elijah will let the prophets of Baal get the bulls for the sacrifice and let them choose which bull they want for the sacrifice. A bull is an adult male cattle and is one of the highest-quality animals for a sacrifice. Because the prophets of Baal will choose the animals, they will not be able to accuse Elijah of causing a problem so that Baal will not approve of the animal. Elijah and the group of prophets will each prepare the sacrifices in the same way. They cut the bulls into pieces and place them on wood, but will not set the wood on fire yet. The prophets of Baal will first call out to Baal to light the fire for the sacrifice. The story says that the prophets will call on the name of Baal. This is a way to say that they will pray to their god by name and ask him to answer. Then Elijah will get the opportunity to pray to Yahweh and ask Yahweh to light the other sacrifice. Since Baal is the storm god who has power over lightning and rain, the prophets of Baal must think it will be easy for Baal to use lightning to start the fire. The people agree that this is a good idea.

Stop and discuss how people make group decisions in your culture. The storyteller does not tell exactly how the people made the decision, but you should make clear that it was not just Elijah's decision. The people agreed that this was a good idea. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and look at photos of a bull and an altar of sacrifice. Discuss these questions: How do people offer sacrifices in your culture? If people in your culture do not make sacrifices, what are some things that people do to please or gain the attention of their god? Pause this audio here.

Baal's prophets try to get Baal's attention so that he will send down fire to light the sacrifice. Baal's prophets shout, but no one responds. Next, Baal's prophets dance or limp around the altar. No one responds to this ritual dance.

Elijah begins to taunt and mock Baal's prophets. Elijah suggests that Baal is busy or distracted in some way. Elijah gives at least four possibilities. Maybe Baal is meditating or deep in thought. Maybe Baal is busy. Maybe Baal is traveling somewhere. Maybe Baal is sleeping, and the prophets need to awaken him. Baal worshipers believed that Baal could go on a journey, fall asleep, or even die. But Elijah is simply taunting or mocking them by giving these suggestions.

The prophets of Baal are desperate. The prophets of Baal fear that they will lose their authority as prophets. To get Baal's attention, the prophets cut themselves using swords and lances. In some religions, people cut their skin with knives as an act of worship and mourning. [Yahweh forbade the Israelites from cutting their bodies in worship.]{.mark} The swords were probably small swords, like knives, that people used for stabbing. The lances were spears that had long shafts. People used lances in battle to thrust at the enemy rather than to throw like a spear or javelin. The prophets of Baal kept making marks on their bodies with the swords and lances until they began to bleed.

Stop here and look at photos of swords and lances like the prophets of Baal might have used in this ritual. Pause this audio here.

Baal still did not answer. [At noontime, the prophets of Baal started to shout louder and act wild and excited, hoping their god would answer them.]{.mark} By the time of the normal evening sacrifice that happens each day, Baal was still silent. Baal could not light the fire for the sacrifice.

Stop here and do this activity: Have one group member name an activity that happens in your culture or community at the same time every day. Then have the other group members respond with the time that the activity happens. Then have another group member name another activity, and have the group guess the time.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 18:16–29 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has four scenes.

In the first scene: Obadiah goes to tell Ahab that Obadiah found Elijah. Ahab goes to meet Elijah.

In the second scene: Ahab and Elijah accuse each other of causing the trouble of Israel. Elijah tells Ahab to gather all of Israel and the prophets of Baal to Mount Carmel.

In the third scene: Elijah rebukes the people of Israel for wavering between two gods. Elijah proposes a contest to see which is the true God—Yahweh or Baal.

In the fourth scene: All day, the prophets of Baal attempt to get Baal to light the fire for the sacrifice to prove that Baal is the true god. The prophets do many things, but Baal does not light the fire.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The prophet Elijah
  • Obadiah, the palace manager
  • King Ahab
  • 450 prophets of Baal and 400 prophets of Asherah
  • And the people of Israel

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

You will remember that in the last part of the story, just before this, Elijah promised Obadiah that he would present himself to Ahab. Now, in the first scene of this story, Obadiah leaves Elijah to find King Ahab to tell him about Elijah. Ahab then goes to meet Elijah. The story does not say exactly where this happens, but it seems that Elijah is in the same place that Obadiah had left him. Ahab had been looking for Elijah all over the place for several years.

In the second scene, King Ahab begins the conversation by asking a question that he does not expect Elijah to answer: "Is that you, you troubler of Israel?" Ahab already knows that it is Elijah. Ahab accuses Elijah of bringing trouble to Israel by announcing and causing the severe drought of no rain for several years.

Elijah replies that it is not Elijah who has brought trouble on Israel, but Ahab's father's family that has caused the trouble. Ahab's father was Omri, who was the king before Ahab. Elijah says that Omri's family has abandoned Yahweh's commands and followed the false god Baal. Ahab's wife, Jezebel, was supporting 450 prophets of Baal and 400 prophets of Asherah with the royal supplies. Elijah tells Ahab that it is both Ahab and Ahab's father's family who have abandoned Yahweh's commands and followed Baal.

Elijah calls for Ahab to summon all the people of Israel to meet Elijah on Mount Carmel, along with the 450 prophets of Baal and 400 prophets of Asherah.

In the third scene, Ahab sends "word," or a message, throughout all the people of Israel and gathers all the prophets to meet Elijah on Mount Carmel. The story does not tell us how many people gather there, but all of the people in the land know that they can come up on the mountain. The story is not clear whether all the prophets of Baal and Asherah come, but it is clear that Elijah asks for them all to be there. Elijah tells the people that Elijah is the only prophet of Yahweh who has come, and Baal has 450 prophets.

Stop here and look at photos of Mount Carmel. Describe a time when you were with a large group of people. How many people were gathered there? How did you feel in such a large crowd of people? Try to picture thousands of people gathered here for this contest. Pause the audio here.

Elijah first confronts the people and asks them, "How long will you go on limping between two opinions? If Yahweh is God, follow him, but if Baal is God, follow him." The question, "How long [will you go on limping between two opinions?" is special language to say, "How long will you try to follow both God and Baal at the same time?" This question means the people were not choosing one side, but were stuck in the middle, like someone who tries to walk on two different paths or uses crutches. Elijah is telling the people they need to make a clear choice and not keep switching back and forth.]{.mark}

Stop and discuss how groups in your culture try to decide between two different opinions when you are not sure. How do you talk about that? Pause this audio here.

However, the people do not answer Elijah's questions. It seems that the people have not yet decided. The Israelites have been worshiping Baal, Asherah, and sometimes, Yahweh. Queen Jezebel has been supporting the prophets of the Canaanite gods and trying to kill the prophets of Yahweh.

When the people do not answer Elijah, Elijah proposes a contest to see which is the true god. The people will get two bulls for sacrifices. The 450 prophets of Baal will first select one bull and prepare it for sacrifice. Elijah will prepare the other bull for sacrifice. [When people prepare a sacrifice, they make sure that the altar is ready and they have wood to burn the offering. The person then takes the animal or other things to be offered and kills a bull, cuts it into pieces, and puts the pieces on the wood. The person will not light the fire themselves, but will wait for the true God to send fire to burn up the sacrifice. People will do all these steps to show honor to God and ask for his answer.]{.mark}

The prophets of Baal will first get an opportunity to pray to Baal and ask him to light the fire of the sacrifice. Then Elijah will get an opportunity to call on Yahweh to light the fire of Elijah's sacrifice. Elijah says that the spiritual being who answers by fire is the true God. Then all the people say, "It is well spoken," which means they agree that this is a good and fair plan.

Stop and remember how you discussed how your culture comes to an agreement on a group decision. Discuss how you will translate that the people all agreed. Pause this audio here.

In the fourth scene, the contest begins when Elijah tells the prophets of Baal to choose a bull, prepare the bull, call on the name of their god, but do not light the fire. The prophets make all the preparations for the sacrifice. The prophets cut up the bull and put those pieces on the wood. The prophets do not light the fire. The prophets begin to call on the name of Baal.

From morning to noon, the prophets of Baal call loudly for Baal to answer them. But Baal does not answer. The prophets dance around the altar in a special ritual while they call on Baal. Still, Baal does not answer.

At noon, Elijah begins to taunt the prophets to shout louder. Elijah is encouraging the prophets and almost sounds like he agrees with them, but Elijah is only taunting and mocking them. Elijah tells the prophets that surely Baal is a god, but the problem could be that Baal is distracted and thinking about other things. Or maybe Baal is busy, or traveling, or sleeping. Baal just needs someone to wake him up. The prophets of Baal shout louder. To get Baal's attention, the prophets begin to cut themselves with swords and lances so that they bleed. The prophets receive no answer. As the hours continue to pass, the prophets begin to prophesy, or shout louder, and act in a wild and excited manner. When these prophets prophesy, it does not mean they are predicting the future or reminding people to keep the law of the covenant. When these prophets prophesy, or show that they speak to and hear from their god, it means they speak and move in a wild manner with much energy and move their hands.

By the time of the normal evening sacrifice, it has been all day, but Baal has not been able to light the fire of the sacrifice. According to Israel's law, the priests offered to Yahweh half of one of the grain offerings in the morning and the other half in the evening. Here, in this story, the reference to the time of the evening offering is simply a way to show the time of day, which was about three o'clock in the afternoon.

Stop here and discuss as a group how you will translate the idea of the evening offering so that people will understand that the storyteller is referring to the time of day. Pause the audio here.

The storyteller emphasizes that Baal does not respond by saying almost the same thing three times: There was no response. No one answered. No one paid attention.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 18:16–29 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has four scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The prophet Elijah
  • Obadiah, the palace manager
  • King Ahab
  • 450 prophets of Baal and 400 prophets of Asherah
  • And the people of Israel

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

After talking with the prophet Elijah, Ahab's palace manager, Obadiah, leaves Elijah to go tell king Ahab where he has found Elijah. When Ahab hears where Elijah is, Ahab goes to Elijah. When Ahab sees Elijah, Ahab asks, "Is that you, you troublemaker for Israel?"

Pause the drama. Ask the actor playing Elijah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The actor might answer things like:

  • I am afraid for my life because I have been hiding from Ahab. Jezebel has been trying to kill me because I am a prophet of Yahweh; or
  • I am angry at Ahab because he is blaming me for causing the trouble.

Continue the drama.

Elijah replies, "It is not me that has been causing the trouble for Israel. It is you and your father's family that has been causing trouble. You have abandoned the commands of Yahweh and started following Baal. Call together the people of Israel and gather the 450 prophets of Baal and the 400 prophets of Asherah that eat at Jezebel's table. Have everyone meet me on Mount Carmel."

Ahab notifies the people in all of Israel and gathers the prophets on top of Mount Carmel. Elijah addresses the people and asks them, "How long will you waver between two opinions? If Yahweh is God, follow him. But if Baal is God, follow him." But the people say nothing.

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing the people of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • There is only one prophet of Yahweh left and hundreds of prophets of Baal. But this one prophet sounds like he has a lot of authority; or
  • I feel guilty because Israelites have always worshipped Yahweh; or
  • I do not know what to think.

Continue the drama.

Elijah then says to the people, "I am the only one of Yahweh's prophets left, but Baal has 450 prophets. Here is what we will do. Get two bulls for sacrifices. Let the prophets of Baal choose one of the bulls for themselves and prepare the sacrifice by cutting the bull into pieces and putting it on the wood. I will prepare the other bull. No one will set fire to the sacrifices. The prophets of Baal will call on the name of Baal, and I will call on the name of Yahweh. The god who answers by setting fire to the sacrifices is the true God."

Then all the people say, "Your plan is good."

Elijah says to the prophets of Baal, "Since there are so many of you, you go first and prepare the bull for sacrifice. Call on the name of your god, but do not light the fire." The prophets of Baal prepare the bull and call on the name of Baal for hours and hours. From morning until noon they shout, "O Baal, answer us!" But there is no response. No one answers. So the prophets begin to dance around the altar of sacrifice.

When noon comes, Elijah taunts and mocks the prophets of Baal. Elijah says, "Shout louder. Surely Baal is a god! Maybe Baal is just thinking about something else. Or maybe Baal is busy. Or maybe Baal is traveling. Or maybe Baal's sleeping and someone needs to wake him up."

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing the prophets of Baal, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • I am getting very nervous. All the other prophets of Baal think Baal will light the fire, but I am not so sure now; or
  • That prophet Elijah is making me angry. Elijah is making fun of us; or
  • What do I have to do so that Baal will light the fire and prove that he is the true god?

Continue the drama.

When Elijah taunts the prophets, the prophets of Baal shout louder. The prophets cut themselves with swords and spears, as part of their rituals, even to the point that they begin to bleed. Even after the middle of the day passes, the prophets begin to pray in a wild and excited manner all the way until the time for the evening sacrifice. But Baal does not respond. No one answers. No one pays attention.

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing the people of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • I have never seen anything so crazy in my life; or
  • I cannot believe the storm god could not bring fire to light the sacrifice; or
  • I feel hopeless because there is still no rain. None of these gods can help.

Ask the person playing Ahab, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • This Elijah is causing all of these problems! or
  • If it is not Elijah's fault, then maybe it is my wife's fault for bringing in all of these prophets.

Ask the person playing Elijah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Wow, it felt great to give these prophets such a hard time. These prophets are miserable; or
  • Now it is time for Yahweh. I am a little anxious, but I know Yahweh will come through.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 18:16–29 in the easiest-to-understand translation.
