Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Ahab met Elijah and said, "Are you the troublemaker of Israel?" Translate Elijah and Israel in the same way you have in previous passages. For more information on Elijah and Israel, refer to the Master Glossary.

Elijah tells Ahab that Ahab and his father's family have abandoned Yahweh's commandments. A commandment is a law, a rule, or an instruction. God gives commandments to people to show them how to live well, in a good relationship with God and with others. When the Bible talks about God's commandments in this passage, this means all the instructions that God has given his people. Use the same words or phrases for Yahweh and for command that you have previously used in other passages. For more information on Yahweh and commandment, refer to the Master Glossary.

Elijah tells Ahab to summon the people of Israel, the 450 prophets of Baal, and the 400 prophets of Asherah. A prophet is a person who claims to speak messages from a god. Baal is a false god whom many people in Canaan worshiped. Asherah, Baal's partner, is a false goddess whom people in Canaan worshiped in Canaan. Use the same words or phrases for prophet and Baal as you have used previously. For more information on prophet and Baal, refer to the Master Glossary.

Elijah says, "If Yahweh is God, follow him. If Baal is god, follow him." In the Bible, "God" means the Creator of everything and the one true God. Yahweh made a special promise to Abraham and his family, the Israelites. Yahweh said not to worship any other gods. But some people worship spirits or idols that people made from wood or metal. These are false gods, like Baal. Use the same word or phrase for God and gods, or false gods, as you have used previously. For more information on God and gods, refer to the Master Glossary.

Elijah proposed that people prepare two sacrifices, one to Baal and one to Yahweh. The true God would be the one who lights the fire of the sacrifice without the help of people. [A sacrifice is a gift that people offer to God, usually by placing an animal or other gift on an altar and then burning it. People gave sacrifices to thank, worship, or ask forgiveness from God. Use the same word or phrase for sacrifice as you have used previously. For more information on sacrifice, refer to the Master Glossary.]{.mark}

Elijah tells the prophets to call on the name of their god, and then Elijah will call on the name of his God. To "call on the name of your God" is to talk to your God and trust that his name, or reputation, is good. The prophets of Baal cried out to Baal to reveal himself as true by answering them by lighting the fire of the sacrifice.

Stop here and discuss how to translate "call on the name of their god." Pause this audio here.

To get Baal's attention, the prophets of Baal cut themselves using swords and thrusting spears. Use the same word for swords as you have used in previous passages. In some ancient religions, it was the custom that people would cut their skin as a mark to mourn the dead and to worship idols. The swords were probably small swords, like knives, that people used for stabbing. The thrusting spears were long spears that had long shafts that people used in battle to thrust at the enemy rather than to throw like a spear or javelin. The prophets of Baal kept making marks with the swords and spears until they began to bleed.

Stop here and look at photos of swords and thrusting spears like the prophets of Baal might have used in this ritual. Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for "thrusting spear." If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

[The prophets of Baal tried to prophesy by calling on their god to answer with fire. The prophets shouted, danced, and even acted wildly or frenzily in hopes that their god would respond. The prophets kept this up for hours and got more and more excited, but Baal did not answer. Only Elijah, the prophet of the true God, gave a real message from Yahweh and saw God's power. So, to prophesy here means to speak or act as a messenger for a god, sometimes with loud or wild behavior, to ask for a sign or answer. For more information on "prophesy," refer to the Master Glossary.]{.mark}

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 18:30–40
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 18:30–40 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 18:30–40 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 18:30–40 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

You will remember that just before this story, Elijah told King Ahab to gather the people of Israel and the prophets of Baal at Mount Carmel. Elijah challenged the prophets of Baal to pray to Baal to reveal himself as the true God by lighting the fire of the bull sacrifice. The prophets prayed, shouted, danced, and cut themselves all day, but Baal did not answer them. Now it is Elijah's turn to pray to Yahweh.

Elijah tells the people, "Come to me." Elijah wants the people of Israel to pay close attention to how Elijah is preparing the sacrifice. First, Elijah repairs the altar of Yahweh. People used to make sacrifices to Yahweh on Mount Carmel. The altar was in bad condition because people had stopped making sacrifices to Yahweh. You will remember that Jezebel was trying to stop people from worshipping Yahweh in Israel so that the people would worship Baal instead.

Stop and look at a picture of an altar. Pause the audio here.

Elijah repairs the altar by using 12 stones, one for each of the 12 tribes of Israel. [The storyteller reminds us that many years before, Yahweh had changed Jacob's name to Israel. God spoke to Jacob and gave him the new name, "Israel." Jacob had 12 sons, and each son had many children. These families grew into the people we call the nation of Israel. Each family group from Jacob's sons became a tribe of Israel. So,]{.mark} Elijah took 12 stones to repair this altar to Yahweh, Israel's God.

Elijah did something unusual when Elijah dug a trench, or small ditch, around the altar. The trench was able to hold about two seahs of grain. A seah is an ancient measurement that held about 15 liters. Elijah did not put grain in the trenches. The measurement is to show how deep the ditch was around the altar. After Elijah dug the trench, Elijah prepared the bull by cutting the meat into pieces, just like the prophets of Baal had done for their sacrifice. Elijah put the meat on top of the wood, which was on top of the stone altar.

Then Elijah asked the people to prepare four large jars of water. Each of the jars probably had several liters of water. Elijah got the people to pour the water on top of the sacrifice so that the meat of the bull was very wet. The water made the wood and stones all very wet. A wet sacrifice would make it difficult for someone to light a fire on a wet surface.

Elijah told the people to pour four large jars of water on the sacrifice three times. [People had made these jars or pots from clay and shaped them to carry and store liquids. Each jar could hold a few liters of water, which is enough to fill a large bucket. People carried these jars by hand or on their shoulders. The jars were strong enough to hold heavy water, but not too big for a person to lift. People used jars like these every day for water, oil, or other liquids.]{.mark}

[Stop and look at a picture of a clay jar that can hold a few liters of liquid. Pause the audio here.]{.mark}

[The people poured so much water that everything was soaked. Water even filled the trench around the altar as it flowed down from the top of the sacrifice.]{.mark}

Stop here and have a group member tell a story about what it is like to build a fire when the wood is wet. What is it like to light a fire with wet firewood? Pause this audio here.

When Elijah prayed to Yahweh, Elijah called Yahweh the God of Abraham, Isaac, and "Israel," or Jacob. Abraham was Israel's grandfather, and Isaac was Israel's father. Abraham, Isaac, and Israel had all worshipped Yahweh. These three men were the famous ancestors of Israel.

Elijah prayed that Yahweh would answer Elijah's prayer by lighting the fire of the soaking-wet sacrifice. Elijah prayed that Yahweh would do this so the people of Israel would know that Yahweh is the one true God and that Yahweh was turning the hearts of the people of Israel back again. When people turn their hearts back to Yahweh, they commit themselves to stop worshipping Baal and to start worshipping Yahweh again.

Stop and t[ell a story about a time when people turned back or returned to an old way or belief. Pause this audio here.]{.mark}

Yahweh sent his fire and burned the sacrifice. Yahweh answered Elijah's prayer and burned the wet meat, the wet wood, and even the wet stones. Only an extraordinarily intense fire could burn up the stones. In Scripture, fire often symbolizes the presence of Yahweh or the judgment of Yahweh. Yahweh answered the prayer by sending fire when the storm god, Baal, could not.

The fire even licked up all the water in the trench around the altar. Another way to say this is that the fire ate up the water. This means that the fire completely dried up the water in the ditch.

Stop here and tell about a time when someone experienced a strong and intense fire. How quickly did the fire burn wood and other things? Does that fire seem stronger or less intense than the fire that burned up Elijah's sacrifice? Pause this audio here.

When all the people witnessed the fire burn up the sacrifice, the people threw themselves down on the ground to show their humility. The people shouted, "Yahweh, he is God. Yahweh, he is God." The people realized that, just like Elijah had said, Yahweh is truly God!

Then Elijah commanded the people of Israel to "seize," or capture, the prophets of Baal. The people captured all the prophets of Baal. Then Elijah took them down to the valley next to the mountain to kill them. Elijah was doing what the Israelite law in Deuteronomy 13 said to do to people who lead others to worship idols: the leaders should kill these prophets of Baal.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 18:30–40 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has two scenes.

In the first scene: Elijah repairs the altar, digs a trench around it, and prepares a sacrifice. Elijah tells some people to pour water on the altar and sacrifice.

In the second scene: Elijah prays to Yahweh, who answers by sending fire. The people of Israel praise Yahweh as God, and the people of Israel take the prophets of Baal down to the valley and kill them.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Elijah
  • The people of Israel
  • Ahab, the king of Israel, who we know is there, but who does not do anything in this part of the story
  • Yahweh
  • And the prophets of Baal

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In the previous passage, Elijah criticized King Ahab for abandoning Yahweh to worship false gods. Elijah instructed Ahab to gather the people of Israel and the prophets of Baal and Asherah on top of Mount Carmel to judge whether Yahweh or Baal is the true god. The prophets of Baal cried out all morning for the god Baal himself to light the fire of the sacrifice of the prophets of Baal.

The first scene overlaps a little with the end of the previous story about the prophets of Baal. The prophets of Baal cried out for Baal to answer them all the way until the time of the evening sacrifice to Yahweh, which took place around 3 p.m. Then, at the time of the evening sacrifice, around 3 in the afternoon, Elijah begins to pray to Yahweh.

Elijah takes a lot of time to repair an old altar with special stones, cut up the meat, dig a trench, and pour many jars of water onto the sacrifice, so Elijah has probably already started to prepare the altar before 3 in the afternoon. Here is how Elijah prepares the altar. Elijah prepares a separate altar of sacrifice from the ones for Baal. This includes the materials for the altar, the wood to burn the offering, and the bull for the sacrifice to Yahweh. Elijah begins by first asking the people of Israel to come closer to him. Elijah probably does not want the prophets of Baal to distract the people because the prophets are desperately crying out to Baal. Elijah wants the people to pay attention to the special way Elijah is preparing the sacrifice.

Elijah uses the same kind of young bull and wood that the prophets of Baal used for their sacrifice. But Elijah repairs an old, damaged altar to Yahweh. Elijah takes 12 stones to represent the 12 tribes of Israel to make the base of the altar. The storyteller interrupts the story about Elijah to tell the story about the 12 sons of Jacob and how Yahweh changed Jacob's name to Israel. The storyteller says that Yahweh's word came to Jacob. The word of Yahweh is what Yahweh said to Jacob. The word of Yahweh said that Jacob's new name was Israel.

Stop and tell a story from your culture that gives extra details from another story in the middle of the main story. Pay attention to how the storyteller changes from the main story to those extra details. Are there specific words or phrases that signal to the listener that this is extra information? Notice how the storyteller helps people understand what is happening. Pause this audio here.

Then, like the prophets of Baal, Elijah puts the wood on the altar and cuts up pieces of bull to place on top of the wood. Elijah digs a trench around the altar deep enough to hold many liters of water, but he does not put anything in the trench.

Elijah asks the people of Israel to prepare four large jars of water, which would each hold several liters of water. Then Elijah tells the people to pour the four large jars of water to soak the sacrifice and the wood. Then Elijah asks the people to do the same thing two more times. The storyteller repeats Elijah's command to pour the water, and then to do it again. This emphasizes that the people pour four jars of water three times. So altogether, this would be pouring 12 jars full of water on the sacrifice and wood. This was so much water that the water filled the trench.

Stop here and discuss how you will make it clear that while Elijah was preparing the altar, the prophets of Baal were continuing to cry out to Baal, but everything stopped at the time of the evening sacrifice when Elijah began his prayer. The easiest way to do this may be to emphasize the time of the evening sacrifice in both parts of the story. Pause the audio here.

The second scene starts at the time of the evening sacrifice, around 3 in the afternoon. The prophets of Baal had cried out to Baal all day until that time. Now it was Elijah's turn to pray to Yahweh. At this time, Elijah steps forward to the altar.

Elijah prays a short prayer. Elijah begins by calling on the name of Yahweh and calling Yahweh the God of the fathers, or ancestors, of Israel: Abraham, Isaac, and Israel. Then, as Elijah is asking Yahweh to light the fire, Elijah gives the reasons: Elijah wants people to know that Yahweh is the true God of the people of Israel, that Elijah is the servant of Yahweh, and that Elijah's actions are from Yahweh. Elijah twice asks Yahweh to answer Elijah's prayer. Then Elijah gives two more reasons: that the people of Israel will know that Yahweh is God and that Yahweh is turning the Israelites' hearts back from idols to Yahweh.

Stop and discuss how people in your community talk about turning back to something that was important or good. Remember the story in the previous step that you told about people turning back to an old belief. How did you talk about the people turning back and changing their hearts or minds? Pause the audio here.

The prophets of Baal have been praying all day with no answer, but Yahweh answers Elijah's prayer immediately. Yahweh sends a fire down that consumes, or burns up, the bull, the wood, and even the stones. Remember that all of these things are very wet. The fire even consumes, or dries up, all the water that had filled the ditch around the altar.

When the fire falls from heaven, the people immediately fall down on their faces to show humility. The people begin to repeat with loud voices, "Yahweh, he is God. Yahweh, he is God." Not only had Yahweh answered Elijah's prayer that Yahweh reveal himself as the true God, but Yahweh had also answered the prayer to turn the people away from Baal and back to Yahweh. [Make sure to say that Yahweh sent the fire; the fire was not an accident. The storyteller talks about fire falling and the people falling in the same way. This connection shows how both the fire and the people's response were powerful and immediate.]{.mark}

[Stop here and discuss how you can talk about the fire falling and the people falling. This helps show the connection between God's action and the people's reaction.]{.mark} [How will you show the connection between Yahweh's action and the people's reaction?]{.mark} Pause the audio here.

The fire even licked up all the water in the trench around the altar like an animal licks up water. Another way to say this is that the fire ate up the water. The meaning is that the fire completely dried up the water in the ditch.

Stop here and discuss as a group: How will you talk about the fire eating up or burning the bull, the wood, and the stones, and licking up the water? Pause this audio here.

Elijah commands the people of Israel to seize all the 450 prophets of Baal. Then Elijah takes the prophets down Mount Carmel into the Kishon Valley, where he slaughters the prophets. The Kishon Valley was a small farming valley on the northeast edge of the base of Mount Carmel.

Stop and look at a map that shows the Kishon Valley at the base of Mount Carmel. Pause this audio here.

We do not know why Elijah takes the prophets to the valley. When Elijah takes the prophets to the valley and kills the prophets, this probably means Elijah orders people to help him take the prophets to the valley and kill the prophets, not that Elijah kills every prophet himself. The storyteller talks about killing these prophets in the same way that people talk about killing animals by slitting their throats.

[Stop here and discuss as a group: How will you talk about Elijah and others killing the prophets of Baal in the same way that people kill animals by slitting their throats? Pause this audio here.]{.mark}

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 18:30–40 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has two scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Elijah
  • The people of Israel
  • Ahab, the king of Israel, who we know is there, but who does not do anything in this part of the story
  • Yahweh
  • And the prophets of Baal

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Elijah calls all the people to himself saying, "Come here to me." The Israelites come to Elijah and watch him repair an altar to Yahweh. To make the base of the altar, Elijah uses 12 stones to represent the 12 tribes of Israel. Yahweh had changed Jacob's name to Israel.

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing the people of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • Yes, the God of our ancestors is Yahweh; or
  • Yahweh is the God of our ancestors, but I feel uncertain if he is still our God. We have no rain; or
  • Look! Elijah uses 12 stones, just like there are 12 tribes in Israel. I remember stories of how Moses and Joshua made altars with 12 stones.

Continue the drama.

Elijah digs a trench around the altar. The trench is big enough to hold two measures of grain, which is about 15 to 30 liters. Elijah arranges the wood for the fire on top of the stone. Elijah cuts the meat into pieces and puts it on top of the wood. Then Elijah tells the people to fill four large jars of water and pour it on the offering of meat and on the wood. When the people pour the four jars of water on the sacrifice, Elijah tells the people to do it again. When the people refill the jars and do it again, Elijah tells them to do it a third time. At this point, the water is running down over the meat and wood and stones and filling up the ditch with water.

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing the people of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • This is not a normal sacrifice to Yahweh with all this water; or
  • It will be impossible for this sacrifice to catch fire. Why is Elijah doing this?

Continue the drama.

Then, at the time of the evening sacrifice, the prophet Elijah steps forward and prays, " Yahweh, the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Israel, let the people know that you are the God in Israel. Let the people know that I am your servant and have done all things at your command. Answer me, Yahweh, answer me, so these people will know that you, Yahweh, are God, and that you are turning their hearts back to you again."

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Elijah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am confident that Yahweh is God; or
  • I am a little confident but also nervous; or
  • I am eager for the people of Israel to turn back to God.

Continue the drama.

Then fire from Yahweh falls down from the sky and burns the sacrifice, the wood, the stones, and the soil, and burns up all the water in the trench.

When the people of Israel see this happen, they fall down on their faces, lie on the ground, and cry, "Yahweh, he is God! Yahweh, he is God!"

Pause the drama. Ask the people playing the people of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We are amazed! or
  • We feel afraid. We know that we have done wrong by allowing and participating in the worship of Baal.

Ask the person playing Elijah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am amazed! I knew that Yahweh would reveal himself, but I could never imagine anything like this; or
  • I feel justified because now it is clear that I am truly God's servant, helping Israel. It was not me who caused the famine. It was Ahab's family's fault; or
  • Praise Yahweh. Yahweh is God!

Ask the people playing the prophets of Baal, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The people might answer things like:

  • We are amazed! How could this happen? or
  • Everything we have been doing as prophets of Baal must have been a lie; or
  • Yahweh is the true God! or
  • We are terrified. People will accuse us of being false prophets.

Continue the drama.

Then Elijah commands the people of Israel, "Seize these prophets of Baal. Do not let any of them escape!" The people of Israel seize all the 450 prophets of Baal. Elijah has the people of Israel take the prophets of Baal down the mountain into the Kishon Valley where Elijah slaughters all the prophets of Baal.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 18:30–40 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Elijah rebuilt an altar to sacrifice to Yahweh. Use the same words for Elijah, altar, sacrifice, and Yahweh that you have used in previous passages. For more information on altar, sacrifice, and Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary.

[Elijah used 12 stones to build the altar, and each stone represented one of the sons of Jacob, or Israel. Yahweh gave Jacob a word, or a message, and told him that his name would be Israel.]{.mark} Use the same words for Israel and "word of Yahweh" that you have used in previous passages. For more information on word and Israel, refer to the Master Glossary.

Elijah dug a long hole, or trench, around the altar to Yahweh. The trench was able to hold about two seahs of grain. Normally, the altar did not have a trench like the one that Elijah dug around the altar. A trench is a long, narrow hole or ditch that people dig in the ground to drain water from land or bring water to crops. A seah is an ancient measurement of volume that may have held about 15 liters. Elijah did not put grain in the trench. The measurement is to show how deep the trench was around the altar.

Stop here and look at the photo of the altar with the trench around it again. Discuss as a group what words or phrases you will use for trench and seah. If you have already translated these words in another book of the Bible, use the same words that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

At the time of the sacrifice for the evening offering, Elijah approached the altar. An offering or sacrifice was a gift that people offered in a ritual to God, usually in the temple or on a special altar. People can make offerings for many reasons, including to give thanks, to be purified, or to ask forgiveness for sin. [The evening offering or sacrifice happened every day in the late afternoon, usually around 3 p.m. This was a regular time for the people to bring a sacrifice to Yahweh. Use the same word or phrase for offering as you have used in previous passages. For more information about offering, refer to the Master Glossary.]{.mark}

Elijah begins his prayer by talking to Yahweh, the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Israel. [The God of Abraham, Isaac, and Israel is Yahweh, the one true God that the ancestors of the Israelites worshipped. This name shows that Yahweh made promises to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob—who is also called Israel—and is faithful to their family. Use the same words for]{.mark} God, Abraham, Isaac, and Israel [as you have used in previous passages. For more information about God and Abraham, refer to the Master Glossary.]{.mark}

Elijah the prophet prayed that the people would know that Yahweh is God and that Yahweh is turning the people's hearts back to himself. Translate pray, heart, and prophet as you have previously. For more information on pray, heart, and prophet, refer to the Master Glossary.

After fire from heaven falls on Elijah's sacrifice to Yahweh, Elijah tells the people to seize the prophets of Baal. [Heaven can mean sky or the place where Yahweh lives. The fire came down from above, from the sky that people can see. Here, heaven does not mean the place where Yahweh lives.]{.mark} Translate heaven, when it means sky, and Baal as you have previously. For more information on heaven and Baal, refer to the Master Glossary.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 18:41–46
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 18:41–46 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 18:41–46 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 18:41–46 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

Just before this story, King Ahab and [the people of Israel gathered on Mount Carmel. Elijah challenged the prophets of Baal to prove whose god was real. Elijah prayed, and Yahweh sent fire from heaven to consume the sacrifice, which showed that Yahweh is the true God. Then Elijah killed all the prophets of Baal in the valley at the base of the mountain.]{.mark}

This story is about the end of the drought after three years of no rain.

At the beginning of this story, Elijah tells Ahab to go eat and drink. It had been a long day on Mount Carmel, and now Elijah says there is the sound of heavy rain. It is not raining on the mountain yet. Perhaps Elijah can hear thunder in the distance, but no clouds are in the sky.

Mount Carmel is in the northwest part of Israel on the coast of the Mediterranean Sea. Here, there is a great view of the sea from the top of the mountain. This is the perfect place to watch for rain coming from the west.

Stop and look at the map of Mount Carmel on the edge of the sea. Pause the audio here.

Elijah goes back up the mountain to kneel down and pray to Yahweh. Then Elijah tells his servant to look toward the sea for clouds. The servant says that there is nothing there. Elijah continues to ask the servant to go look. On the seventh time, the servant sees a cloud rising from the sea that looks like the size of a human hand. The servant probably does not mean that the cloud looks like a person's hand. The servant is probably saying that he thinks the cloud is about the same size as a person's hand. This shows the cloud is very far away. When the servant says, "rising from the sea," he does not mean that the cloud is coming out of the water. The servant is talking about how he sees the cloud coming out of the horizon in the west over the sea.

Stop and do this activity: Go outside and look at the sky. Have different people on your team take turns describing the clouds that you see. Pause this audio here.

Elijah tells the servant to go tell Ahab to hitch up his chariot. A chariot is a light, open, two-wheeled vehicle that one or more horses pull. People rode chariots in battle. Kings or leaders also used chariots to travel long distances. Ahab is not going off to war. It will take some time to get the horses ready and Ahab's chariot packed to leave for Jezreel. Elijah is telling Ahab to get ready to leave by hitching horses up to Ahab's chariot so Ahab can get to Jezreel before the rain stops Ahab.

Stop and look at a picture of a horse-drawn chariot. Pause the audio here.

The rain will make the road muddy. Ahab would have difficulty if Ahab had to drive the chariot along muddy roads. Elijah was not expecting a little rain, but a lot of rain.

Stop here and get someone to tell a story [or talk about a time when you knew a big storm was coming. Listen to how the storyteller describes the sky and the change of weather.]{.mark} Pause this audio here.

Ahab rides his chariot to Jezreel. Jezreel was a city located near Mount Gilboa, about 27 kilometers from Mount Carmel. Ahab's closest palace to Mount Carmel was in Jezreel. Ahab's winter home was in Jezreel when the capital of Samaria was too cold.

The phrase "Yahweh's hand was on Elijah" means that Yahweh's power helps Elijah. Elijah runs faster than Ahab's horse-driven chariot on the long 27-kilometer road to Jezreel. This is beyond a human's normal strength and speed, but Yahweh gives Elijah the strength.

Elijah tucks his cloak into his belt. Men in this culture usually wore long cloaks or robes that hung loose when they were not working. When a man needed to work or move quickly, he would tuck the robe under his belt. If Elijah did not do this, Elijah would probably trip and fall when he ran. It was a common phrase for people who wore robes in those days to speak of "tucking their robe in the belt" to get ready for a vigorous activity.

Stop and look at a picture of a cloak. Look at a picture of a man with his cloak tucked. Pause this audio here.

Stop and do this activity. [Have a team member take a big cloth or long robe and let it hang loose like a cloak. Try to run with it and see how hard it is. Then tuck the cloth into a belt and try running again. Talk about how much easier it is to move when the cloth is tucked in. This shows why Elijah tucked his robe before running.]{.mark}

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 18:41–46 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has four scenes.

In the first scene: Elijah tells King Ahab to go get something to eat and drink because rain is coming.

In the second scene: Elijah climbs back to the top of the mountain. While Elijah is kneeling down, he keeps telling his servant to look out over the sea for rain.

In the third scene: It begins to rain, and Ahab leaves in his chariot for Jezreel.

In the fourth scene: The power of God comes upon Elijah, and Elijah runs faster than Ahab's chariot to Jezreel.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The prophet Elijah
  • King Ahab
  • And Elijah's servant

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

When the first scene begins, Elijah and Ahab are at the bottom of Mount Carmel in the Kishon Valley. On top of the mountain, Yahweh had just sent fire from heaven, which proved that he was the true God. Elijah had also taken the prophets of Baal down the mountain to the Kishon Valley and slaughtered them. It is not clear whether the events in this new story take place on the same day or if it is the next day. Now, at the bottom of Mount Carmel, Elijah tells King Ahab to go and get something to eat and drink. [The king has just had a long day on the mountain. Like before, King Ahab just watches Elijah and follows what Elijah says to do.]{.mark} Elijah tells Ahab that heavy rain is about to come. It has not rained for three years, but Yahweh said that the rain would come again.

In the second scene, Elijah climbs back to the top of Mount Carmel to wait for the rain. When Elijah gets to the top, Elijah does not look for rain. Elijah kneels down and leans forward so that his head is between his knees. It is not clear why Elijah is kneeling, but it is possible that he is praying as he normally does or focusing very hard on praying to Yahweh. It is a position of humility.

[Stop and ask someone on the team to kneel like Elijah, with their head between their knees. This helps everyone see how Elijah showed humility and focus. While watching, talk about how people in your culture show deep thinking or respect with their bodies. This will help you find the right words for Elijah's actions in your translation.]{.mark} Pause the audio here.

Then Elijah tells his servant to go up to the part of the mountain where the servant can look out over the sea. Mount Carmel is on the edge of the Mediterranean Sea, where the rain clouds often move off the sea from the west over the land of Israel, east of the sea. The servant goes up and looks for rain clouds. Normally, the rain in Israel comes from rain clouds that form over the sea.

Stop here and look at a map of Mount Carmel and the Mediterranean Sea. Take a look at a photo of the sea as viewed from Mount Carmel. Pause the audio here.

The servant comes back and reports that there is nothing there. Elijah tells the servant to go back and look again. This happens seven times. The servant does not see anything until the seventh time, when the servant comes back and reports that he saw a cloud. The way the servant tells Elijah about the cloud shows that he is surprised, and the servant emphasizes that he sees something new he has not seen before. The servant says, "Look! I see a cloud as small as a man's hand rising from the sea." It is unclear whether the servant looked toward the sea seven times or whether Elijah told the servant to return and look seven times. If the servant went back seven times, then the servant looked toward the sea a total of eight times.

Elijah tells the servant to go tell Ahab to hitch the horses to his chariot. Elijah tells the servant to urge Ahab to go down to Jezreel before the rain stops Ahab. We are not sure whether Ahab is on the mountain at this point. So it is not clear whether Ahab is on the mountain and should go down the mountain or whether Ahab is at the base of the mountain and should go down to Jezreel. However, it is clear that Ahab should go to Jezreel before the rain and mud make Ahab's journey too difficult. The chariot wheels are heavy, and they will get stuck in mud if the ground is wet.

The third scene starts a few minutes after Elijah gave instructions to Ahab's servant. The storyteller starts by saying "in a little while," or "meanwhile." In a few minutes, the sky begins to get very dark with clouds. A heavy wind begins to blow. And when it begins to rain, Ahab leaves in his chariot for Jezreel.

Stop and tell a story about a huge storm that happened quickly. Pay attention to the words the storyteller uses to describe the sky and the change in weather. What does it look like in your area before a heavy storm comes, like the storm in this story? Use these words to describe the sky getting dark, the heavy wind beginning to blow, and the rain starting. Pause the audio here.

In the fourth scene, the hand or power of Yahweh comes on Elijah. In other words, Yahweh gives Elijah strength that is more than people normally have. Elijah tucks his cloak under his belt so that he can run.

[Stop here and discuss as a group: How will you translate that the power of Yahweh came on Elijah? Pause this audio here.]{.mark}

[Stop again and discuss as a group: Think about the activity where you ran with the cloth wrapped around you. How will you translate the idea that Elijah tucked up his robe to run easily? Pause the audio here.]{.mark}

Then Elijah runs so fast that he runs ahead of Ahab's horse-drawn chariot. Elijah runs from Mount Carmel and arrives in Jezreel ahead of Ahab.

Stop and look at a map from Mount Carmel and the Kidron Valley to Jezreel. Pause the audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 18:41–46 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has four scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The prophet Elijah
  • King Ahab
  • And Elijah's servant

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

The prophet Elijah says to King Ahab, "Go get something to eat and drink. There is a sound of heavy rain coming." So Ahab goes to eat and drink while Elijah climbs back up to the top of Mount Carmel.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing King Ahab, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am exhausted. What happened on this mountain was nothing like what I expected; or
  • I am starving. This drought is long, and I have no energy after climbing up and down the mountain. I am just ready to eat; or
  • I just have to do whatever this prophet of the Lord says.

Continue the drama.

When Elijah gets back to the top of the mountain, Elijah kneels down and leans over so that his head is between his knees. Elijah tells his servant to go look out over the sea for rain.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Elijah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am exhausted from going up and down the mountain and killing all of the prophets of Baal. But my work is not yet finished. Yahweh said he would bring rain; or
  • I am trying to pray, but I am so tired. I need Yahweh to give me strength to pray for rain.

Continue the drama.

Elijah's servant walks over to look for rain. The servant comes back and reports, "There is nothing there." Elijah tells the servant to go look again. This happens seven times.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Elijah's servant, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Surely there will be rain, but I do not see anything at all. How many times will the prophet keep telling me to go back out and look? or
  • Elijah is truly a man of God. Elijah has ridiculed Baal, who was powerless to bring rain. Elijah killed off all the prophets of Baal, and now he is praying for rain. Oh, how I hope that Yahweh brings rain back to our land.

Continue the drama.

On the seventh time, Elijah's servant reports that he sees a tiny rain cloud that looks as if it were the size of a human hand rising out of the sea. So Elijah tells the servant to go tell Ahab to hitch up his chariot with Ahab's horses to leave before the rain can stop Ahab. In a little while, the sky starts to grow very dark, and a strong wind begins to blow.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Ahab, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Rain is finally coming! or
  • I am so relieved. After three long years, the rain is back; or
  • Praise Yahweh for his power. Praise Yahweh for bringing rain.

Continue the drama.

Heavy rain pours down from the sky as Ahab rides in his chariot back to Jezreel. Then the power of Yahweh comes on Elijah so that Elijah tucks his cloak into his belt so that he can run. Elijah runs ahead of Ahab's chariot all the way to Jezreel.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 18:41–46 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause the audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Elijah tells Ahab to get something to eat and drink because rain will come soon. Elijah is the prophet of Yahweh. Ahab is the king of Israel. Translate Elijah and Ahab as you have previously.

Elijah urges Ahab to leave quickly in his chariot so that the strong rain does not stop Ahab from getting home. The word for rain is a general term for rainfall, but this is a heavy rainstorm that comes after three years of no rain.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for a heavy rain. Pause this audio here.

From the top of the mountain, Elijah's servant sees a small cloud. Your language may have many words for cloud, but the term used in this story is for a thick, dark rain cloud.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for a dark and heavy rain cloud. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Elijah tells his servant to go to the top of Mount Carmel and look out over the sea for rain. This sea is the Mediterranean Sea, which is a very large body of saltwater, much larger than a lake but not as big as the ocean. This sea is to the west of Israel, or the direction that the sun sets. Mount Carmel overlooks the sea. Translate servant and sea as you have in previous passages. For more information on servant, refer to the Master Glossary.

King Ahab rides back to Jezreel in his chariot. [A chariot is a small, light cart that horses pull and that carries people—especially kings and soldiers—when they are fighting in battles.]{.mark} Translate chariot as you have in previous passages.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a picture of a chariot if needed. Pause the audio here.

The power of Yahweh came on Elijah, and Elijah tucked in his robe, or "tucked in the lower part of his body," and ran. This means that Elijah tucked his robe into the belt, or the long piece of cloth that a person would wrap around their robe. Although the original story does not use the word for robe, you may want to say that Elijah tucked in his robe when he ran. If you use the word for robe, see the Master Glossary for robe and use the same word for robe that you have used previously. Yahweh is the name God gives himself. Translate Yahweh the same as you have in previous passages. For more information on Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary.

Stop here and, if needed, look again at a photo of a robe and of a man who tucks his robe into his belt. Discuss how you will talk about Elijah tucking in his robe so that he could run quickly.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 19:1–8
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 19:1–8 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 19:1–8 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 19:1–8 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

[In the previous stories, Elijah called the people together on Mount Carmel and challenged them to choose between Yahweh and Baal. Baal could not send fire, but when Elijah prayed, Yahweh sent fire from heaven, which showed Yahweh is the true God. Elijah then killed all the prophets of Baal. After this, Elijah prayed again, and Yahweh sent rain to end the three years of drought in Israel. Elijah told King Ahab to go ahead to Jezreel. Elijah then prayed and, with Yahweh's strength, ran ahead of Ahab's chariot to Jezreel.]{.mark}

[At the beginning of this story, King Ahab has just arrived in Jezreel and tells Jezebel everything that Elijah did. Ahab tells Jezebel that Elijah put all the prophets of Baal to death by the sword. In that culture, "by the sword" means that people killed them in a violent way, but it does not mean Elijah killed each one himself or that he used a real sword.]{.mark}

[Jezebel is very angry because she supported the prophets of Baal and tried to kill the prophets of Yahweh, but now Elijah has killed all the prophets of Baal. Jezebel sends a message to Elijah to say she will kill Elijah. Jezebel swears by or makes an oath to her gods, not by Yahweh, because she worships many gods. Jezebel says she will do to Elijah what he did to the prophets, but Jezebel only sends a messenger, not soldiers. People in that culture used this kind of sentence to make a strong promise. The words, "May the gods do to me, and more also," show an oath. It means the person is calling on their gods to punish them if they do not keep their promise.]{.mark}

[Stop and discuss as a group what a strong promise means in your culture. Discuss how someone might swear an oath to do something important or threatening. How do people show they are very serious when they make a promise? Pause the audio here.]{.mark}

[Elijah becomes afraid and runs away from Jezreel. Elijah leaves Israel and travels through Judah all the way to Beersheba.]{.mark}

Stop and look at a map of Elijah's journey from Jezreel to Beersheba and the wilderness. Pause the audio here.

We do not know why Elijah's servant only goes with Elijah as far as Beersheba, but Elijah leaves the servant there. Beersheba is a city in the southern kingdom of Judah, which had a different king than Ahab.

Elijah then walks alone into the wilderness, which is a dry, desert area with few trees or people. Elijah probably walks about 25 kilometers, or a day's journey, and sits down to rest under a broom tree. A broom tree is a large bush or small tree that grows in the desert. Some broom trees can grow up to 3.5 meters tall.

Stop and look at a photo of a broom tree. Pause this audio here.

People often used the wilderness near Beersheba to graze animals in winter. Long before Elijah, both Abraham and Isaac built altars to Yahweh in this area. Hagar wandered with Ishmael in the wilderness of Beersheba when Abraham sent them away. Like Abraham, Isaac, and Hagar, Elijah talks to Yahweh in the wilderness of Beersheba.

Under the broom tree, Elijah prays to Yahweh and says he has had enough and wants to die because his problems feel too great. Elijah says, "I am no better than my fathers or ancestors," which means Elijah feels he is not better off than those who have already died or that Elijah has not done better than the prophets before him. Elijah then lies down and falls asleep.

After some time, an angel touches Elijah and tells him to get up and eat and drink. The story does not say if Elijah sees the angel, but it does not seem like Elijah is afraid or surprised. An angel is a spiritual being that Yahweh created who brings messages or help from Yahweh. Sometimes angels look like people, and sometimes they look bright or frightening, but the storyteller does not describe the angel.

Elijah is very tired and wants to die, but Yahweh gives Elijah a jar of water and bread to help Elijah recover. Elijah finds a jar of water and bread beside his head. The bread is a flat, round loaf that someone baked on hot stones. This bread is not a sweet cake.

[Stop and look at a picture of a round, flat bread that someone baked on hot stones. Think about the basic food people eat in your area, even if it is not bread. Consider how people in your community would describe a basic food that gives strength and energy.]{.mark} Pause the audio here.

A jug of water was a small clay bottle about 7.5 to 15 centimeters tall. This means it was about as tall as a finger or hand. People normally used jars like this to hold water or other liquids.

Stop here and look at a jug that people used to hold water. Pause the audio here.

The angel tells Elijah to eat because Elijah's journey will be long and hard. The trip from Beersheba to Horeb is about 400 kilometers, and Elijah travels for 40 days and nights with the strength from the food God gives him.

Elijah walks all the way to Mount Horeb. People also call Mount Horeb "Mount Sinai," or "the mountain of God." You will remember this is the place where God appeared to Moses in the burning bush and gave the covenant to Israel after they left Egypt.

Stop here and look at a photo of Mount Horeb. Also, look at the map again, and this time note Elijah's journey from Beersheba to Mount Horeb.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 19:1–8 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has four scenes.

In the first scene: King Ahab tells Queen Jezebel everything that happened on Mount Carmel. So Jezebel sends a message to Elijah to warn Elijah that she is about to kill him.

In the second scene: Elijah runs to Beersheba and then to a place in the wilderness. Elijah prays to God to take Elijah's life. Then Elijah falls asleep under a broom tree.

In the third scene: An angel awakens Elijah and tells him to get up to eat and drink. Someone has provided bread and water for Elijah, so Elijah eats and drinks and then goes back to sleep.

In the fourth scene: The angel wakes up Elijah again and tells him to eat for the long journey. Elijah eats and drinks and then walks for 40 days and 40 nights until he gets to Mount Horeb.

The characters in this passage are:

  • King Ahab
  • Queen Jezebel
  • The prophet Elijah
  • Elijah's servant
  • And an angel of Yahweh

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

Just before the first scene, King Ahab has ridden his chariot through the rainstorm to his palace in Jezreel, a city in northern Israel. Now, in the first scene, as soon as Ahab arrives, he tells his wife Jezebel everything that happened with Elijah and the prophets of Baal. Yahweh had proven that the only true God was Yahweh and that Baal was a false god. Elijah and the people of Israel killed all the false prophets.

Elijah, who had run ahead of Ahab's chariot from Mount Carmel to Jezreel, is still in the city. Jezebel quickly sends a messenger to Elijah with a threat. Jezebel swears an oath that she will have her soldiers kill Elijah by the same time the next day, or within 24 hours. The way that people swore an oath was to say that their god should kill them if they did not do what they promised to do.

Stop and think about your discussion about making oaths. How will you translate the idea that Jezebel swore an oath? How will you translate this so people will understand that Jezebel is very serious about the promise she makes? Pau[se this audio here.]{.mark}

In the second scene, Elijah becomes afraid and immediately runs away to save his life. Elijah travels on foot from Jezreel, which is in the north of Israel, all the way south. Elijah crosses the entire land of Israel and then through Judah to Beersheba, a city on the southern border of Judah, about 160 kilometers from Jezreel. Elijah's servant is with Elijah during this long journey. When they reach Beersheba, Elijah leaves his servant there. Then Elijah walks alone for a whole day into the wilderness, a dry and empty area with very few trees or people.

Stop and look at a map of Elijah's journey from Jezreel to Beersheba and the wilderness. Notice where Elijah's servant was with him in the journey and where Elijah left the servant. Pause the audio here.

Elijah finally comes to rest at a broom tree there in the wilderness. Broom trees can be 3.5 meters tall, so this big shrub was probably taller than Elijah. Elijah sits down under the broom tree, which means Elijah sits down in the shade of the broom tree.

[Stop and look at a picture of a broom tree again, if needed. Talk with the team about how to say the name of this tree in your language. If your area does not have this kind of tree, you can describe the tree. Some ways to describe the tree are "tall desert shrub" or "bush that gives shade." Do not use a local tree name if it gives the wrong idea. Think about how the tree gave Elijah a place to rest and shade. Pause the audio here.]{.mark}

Elijah tells Yahweh that Elijah cannot experience anything else so difficult and asks Yahweh to take his life. Elijah feels that he does not have a better life than his ancestors, who are dead. So Elijah wants to die, too.

[Stop and share a story about someone in your community who felt very tired and wanted to give up. How did you describe this person's feelings? Discuss how you talk about someone ready to give up. Use these ideas to explain Elijah's feelings under the broom tree, when Elijah felt overwhelmed and asked God to take his life. Pause this audio here.]{.mark}

In the third scene, after Elijah has been sleeping for a short time, all of a sudden, an angel of Yahweh touches Elijah and tells him to get up and eat. In the original language, the storyteller uses a word to emphasize that something new and sudden has happened.

[Stop and have a team member tell a story about a time when something surprising happened, like something suddenly waking you from sleep. Listen for the words the person uses to show surprise or get attention. Discuss which words in your language signal that something unexpected is happening in a story. Pause the audio here.]{.mark}

Elijah looks around and is surprised to see flat bread and a jar of water by his head. Although you may have a different type of basic food in your culture, it is likely that this is really a flat bread that Elijah eats. After Elijah eats and drinks, and then Elijah lies down again and goes back to sleep.

Stop here and look again at the photo of the flat bread that someone has baked on hot stones. What will you call this in your translation? Pause this audio here.

In the fourth scene, the angel touches Elijah a second time and tells him to eat and drink more because Elijah has a long journey ahead. The angel wants Elijah to understand that Elijah will need to eat this food in order to have the strength he needs for the long journey. Elijah obeys, and the food and water give Elijah enough strength to walk for 40 days and 40 nights from the wilderness of Beersheba to Mount Horeb, about 400 kilometers away. The storyteller repeats that Elijah walks for both 40 days and 40 nights to show that this was a very long journey! Mount Horeb is also called Mount Sinai or the mountain of God. Many years before, when Yahweh had first rescued the Israelites from Egypt, God appeared to Moses at Mount Horeb and gave the Israelites his laws, which are the covenant agreement with his people.

Stop and look at a map of Israel with Beersheba and see where Elijah walked from Beersheba to Mount Horeb, or Mount Sinai. Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 19:1–8 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has four scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • King Ahab
  • Queen Jezebel
  • The prophet Elijah
  • Elijah's servant
  • And an angel of Yahweh

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Now, King Ahab tells his wife, Jezebel, everything that the prophet Elijah had done on Mount Carmel and how he had put all the prophets of Baal to death. So Jezebel sends a messenger to Elijah, who tells him, ["If I do not kill you in 24 hours like you killed the prophets of Baal, may the gods punish me severely."]{.mark}

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Jezebel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am furious at this troublemaker, Elijah, who has killed all my prophets; or
  • I am completely in shock. How could one prophet of Yahweh defeat 450 prophets of Baal? or
  • I must put an end to Elijah, but after what happened, I am afraid of Yahweh and the people. I will send a messenger to frighten Elijah away.

Ask the person playing Elijah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Everyone has turned to Yahweh except this wicked woman, Jezebel. Now this wicked woman will kill me; or
  • I am exhausted from everything that has been happening. Now, where is Yahweh to protect me?

Continue the drama.

Elijah is terrified and runs for his life all the way to Beersheba in the land of Judah. Elijah leaves his servant in the city of Beersheba and walks by himself all day into the wilderness. Elijah comes to a broom tree, sits down under it, and prays that he might die: "I have had enough of all of this, Yahweh. Take my life now. I am no better than my ancestors." Then Elijah lies down under the broom tree and falls asleep.

Ask the person playing Elijah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am done. I feel hopeless. I just want to die; or
  • I have no emotional or physical strength to do anything else at all; or
  • The life of a prophet is useless.

Continue the drama.

Suddenly, an angel of Yahweh awakens Elijah. The angel touches Elijah and says, "Get up and eat." Elijah looks around and sees that, by his head, there are some round cakes of bread that someone baked over hot coals. There is also a small jug of water. Elijah eats and drinks, and then lies down again.

Ask the person playing Elijah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • How did this hot bread and this jug of water get here in this desert place? or
  • Was that really an angel of Yahweh who touched me, spoke to me, and gave me this food? or
  • I do not know what happened, but I am starving and dying of thirst. Thanks be to Yahweh.

Continue the drama.

A second time, the angel of Yahweh touches Elijah to wake Elijah up. The angel says to Elijah, "Get up and eat, for the journey will be too much for you if you do not eat."

So Elijah gets up, eats the bread, and drinks the water. Elijah is strengthened by this food so that Elijah is able to walk 40 days and 40 nights.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Elijah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • The food the angel made for me was amazing; or
  • I do not know what Yahweh has planned for me next. I will go to Mount Horeb if that is what Yahweh tells me to do; or
  • Yahweh has again provided food for me in a very difficult time.

Continue the drama.

After 40 days and 40 nights, Elijah arrives at Mount Horeb, the mountain of God.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 19:1–8 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

King Ahab told his wife Jezebel all that Elijah had done and how Elijah had killed the prophets. The storyteller does not say that these are the prophets of Baal, but we know that they are the prophets of Baal from the previous stories. Translate Elijah and prophets in the same way you have in previous passages. For more information on prophets, refer to the Master Glossary.

Jezebel swears an oath by the gods. [An oath is a serious promise that people make in front of others. When people make an oath, they often call on God or gods to witness that oath and punish them if they break the promise.]{.mark} Jezebel worshipped false gods, like Baal and Asherah, gods of the Canaanite peoples. These are the false gods that Jezebel is calling on in her oath. Use the same word for gods as you have used previously, and refer to gods in the Master Glossary for more information. If you use the word "oath" in your translation, use the same word for oath that you used previously. If you need more information about oath, refer to the Master Glossary.

Elijah left his servant in Beersheba in the southern part of Judah. Translate servant and Judah as you have previously. For more information on servant and Judah, refer to the Master Glossary.

Elijah rests under a broom tree in the wilderness of Beersheba. A broom tree is the name for a kind of desert shrub that can grow as tall as 3.5 meters. People think of the broom tree as a small tree or a large bush.

Stop and look again at the photo of the broom tree. Discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for broom tree. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

[Wilderness is a wild, natural area with few people, often with rough land, trees, or desert, where people might go to be alone or find safety. Translate wilderness as you have previously. For more information on wilderness, refer to the Master Glossary.]{.mark}

[When Elijah is under the broom tree, he tells Yahweh that Elijah has had enough and wants to die. Yahweh is the name God gives himself. Elijah asks Yahweh to let him die. Elijah is talking to Yahweh and asking Yahweh for something, so some translations say that Elijah is praying here. If you use your word or phrase for praying in this story, translate "pray to ask Yahweh for something" in the same way you have previously, and refer to pray in the Master Glossary for more information. Translate Yahweh as you have previously. For more information on Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary.]{.mark}

[Elijah says he is no better than his fathers or ancestors. Elijah is talking about the people in Elijah's family who lived before him, like parents, grandparents, or even famous people from long ago, like Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. Elijah just means he is no better than those who died before him. Use the same word for fathers or ancestors as you have used previously.]{.mark}

[An angel of Yahweh comes to strengthen Elijah two times. An angel is a special spirit being that Yahweh created. Angels bring messages from Yahweh to people. Sometimes angels look like regular people, but other times they look bright or even scary. Be careful not to confuse the word for "angel" with "prophet." A prophet is a human who gives Yahweh's messages, but an angel is a spirit being from heave]{.mark}n. Translate angel as you have in previous passages. For more information on angel, refer to the Master Glossary.

Yahweh provides bread and water for Elijah.

Stop here and look at the photo of the flat bread that someone baked on hot stones again as a group. Look also at the jar for water. Review what words or phrases you decided to use for the flat bread and the jar of water.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 19:9–21
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 19:9–21 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 19:9–21 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 19:9–21 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

This story is about how Yahweh talks to the prophet Elijah on the mountain of God and tells him what to do next.

Earlier, Elijah ran away from Queen Jezebel because she wanted to kill Elijah for killing the prophets of the Canaanite god Baal. Elijah traveled across Israel and Judah into the wilderness, where he asked Yahweh to let him die. Instead, Yahweh let Elijah rest and sent an angel with bread and water, which gave Elijah strength for a 40-day journey to Mount Horeb. Mount Horeb is also called Mount Sinai, where Yahweh spoke to Moses many years before to give the covenant to the people of Israel.

Now, in this story, when Elijah gets to Mount Horeb, Elijah finds a cave to sleep in after his long trip. A cave is a natural hole in the mountain. People can use caves for protection from the weather or animals.

Stop and look at a photo of a cave. Pause the audio here.

Yahweh speaks his word to Elijah. Yahweh asks Elijah, "What are you doing here, Elijah?" Even though Yahweh already knows, Yahweh is starting a conversation so Elijah can share his thoughts and feelings.

Elijah answers that he has been very zealous for Yahweh, the God of heavenly armies. This name for God [means that Yahweh is the leader of a great army. "Yahweh" is God's personal name, and "hosts" refers to the heavenly armies or angels. This title shows Yahweh's power and authority over all spiritual and earthly forces.]{.mark}

Elijah has been very zealous by working hard and being very loyal to Yahweh. Elijah says he has done everything for Yahweh, but the people of Israel have broken their covenant with Yahweh, destroyed Yahweh's altars, and killed Yahweh's prophets. Elijah says that he himself is the only prophet left, and now people want to kill Elijah too. Elijah is telling Yahweh all of Elijah's problems, and Elijah feels like all of Elijah's hard work was for nothing.

Yahweh tells Elijah to go stand on the mountain because Yahweh is about to pass by. [This reminds us of when Yahweh showed Yahweh's glory to Moses on Mount Sinai, but passed by in a different way.]{.mark} This time, a strong wind comes, and rocks fall from the mountain, but Elijah knows Yahweh is not in the wind. Then there is an earthquake, which is when the ground shakes, but Elijah knows Yahweh is not in the earthquake either.

[Stop and have each team member share if they or people in their community have ever experienced an earthquake or heard stories about one. Tell a story of a time when you experienced very strong wind or an earthquake. Pause the audio here.]{.mark}

After the earthquake, a fire starts to burn. There are no details about the fire. Elijah knows that Yahweh is not in the fire. Then Elijah hears a gentle, quiet sound. Yahweh was not in the strong wind, the earthquake, or the fire. Instead, Elijah recognizes Yahweh's presence in the gentle, quiet sound. This may have been simply silence.

[Elijah covers his face with his robe because no one could look directly at Yahweh and live. Long ago, when Moses asked to see Yahweh's glory, Yahweh told Moses to hide in a crack of a rock. Yahweh covered Moses' face as Yahweh passed by. Even when Yahweh spoke from Mount Sinai, the people were afraid to look. Now Elijah, like Moses, covers his face in the presence of Yahweh. A voice asks Elijah again, "What are you doing here, Elijah?" and Elijah gives the same answer as before.]{.mark}

Then Yahweh tells Elijah to leave the mountain, go back the way he came, and head toward the wilderness near Damascus, which is in the land of Aram, or Syria.

Stop here and look at a map that shows where Elijah is at Mount Horeb and where Yahweh tells Elijah to go, near Damascus. The wilderness is the area outside of Damascus where few people live. Pause the audio here.

[Now, Yahweh wants Elijah to anoint a new king for a foreign country, a new king for Israel, and a new prophet.]{.mark} To anoint someone means to pour oil or rub ointment on that person's head to show that Yahweh has chosen them to do a special task.

Stop here and tell a story about a time when your community selected a new leader. How did you choose a new leader? How did people know that this was the new leader? Were there any kind of ceremonies? Pause the audio here.

Yahweh tells Elijah to anoint Hazael as the new king of Aram, which is also called Syria. Aram is a country next to Israel, and it is often Israel's enemy. Hazael is an officer who serves under the king of Aram, or Syria.

Yahweh tells Elijah to anoint Jehu, son of Nimshi, as the new king of Israel. Jehu was actually the grandson of Nimshi. In Hebrew culture, a son can refer to any male descendant. This King Jehu is not the same person as the prophet Jehu the storyteller mentioned in earlier stories.

Yahweh also tells Elijah to anoint Elisha, son of Shaphat, to take Elijah's place as prophet. Shaphat is Elisha's father. Abel Meholah is Elisha's hometown, but we do not know its exact location. Most scholars believe Abel Meholah was back in Israel, west of the Jordan River in the central Jordan Valley, about 60 kilometers south of Samaria.

Yahweh tells Elijah that Hazael, Jehu, and Elisha will do important tasks for Yahweh. These tasks include punishing people who are leading Israel to worship the false idol of Baal. When Yahweh says some will escape the sword of Hazael, Jehu, or Elisha, it means some people will escape being killed by these leaders. Yahweh will use Hazael, Jehu, and Elisha to punish those who worship false gods. But Yahweh will protect 7,000 people who have not worshipped Baal. In that culture, people worshipped an idol by kneeling down and kissing the idol. Seven thousand people have not done this but have stayed faithful to Yahweh. This shows Elijah that he is not alone and that Yahweh always keeps a group of faithful people.

After this, Elijah finds Elisha in his hometown of Abel Meholah, where Elisha is plowing a field with 12 pairs of oxen. A plow is a tool for digging soil to prepare it for farmers to plant seeds, and oxen are large cattle that people use for farming. Hired workers are using the other 11 pairs of oxen while Elisha is plowing with the 12th pair. Having 12 pairs of oxen means Elisha's family was likely wealthy.

Stop here and look at a picture of two oxen yoked to a plow. Pause the audio here.

Elijah puts his robe on Elisha to show that Yahweh is choosing Elisha as the next prophet after Elijah. Elijah's robe was a special outer garment that showed that Elijah was a prophet.
