Elisha asks to kiss his father and mother goodbye before he leaves. In that culture, kissing your parents goodbye was a normal, loving, and properly respectful way to say farewell.

Stop and discuss these questions about how your culture says goodbye to different people. How do children say goodbye to parents? How do people say goodbye when they leave for a long time? What special actions or words show respect when saying farewell? What is different about saying goodbye to others? Pause the audio here.

Elisha kills a pair of oxen and uses the yoke to build a fire to cook the meat of the oxen. By burning the yoke and cooking the oxen, Elisha shows that he is leaving his old life as a farmer completely behind. Elisha will not need these farming tools anymore because Elisha is becoming Elijah's helper.

Stop and discuss what tools are most valuable to farmers in your area.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 19:9–21 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has four scenes.

In the first scene: Yahweh talks with Elijah inside the cave at Mount Horeb.

In the second scene: Yahweh talks with Elijah outside the cave at Mount Horeb.

In the third scene: Elijah goes to the town where Elisha is plowing in the fields. Elijah calls Elisha to be a prophet.

In the fourth scene: Elisha slaughters the oxen he has been using to plow the fields and serves their meat to the people there to eat. Then Elisha leaves to follow Elijah as his servant.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The prophet Elijah
  • Yahweh
  • Elisha
  • And the people of Elisha's village, including Elisha's father and mother

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

Just before this story begins, an angel of Yahweh provided Elijah with bread and water. That meal gave Elijah strength to walk from the wilderness of Beersheba to Mount Horeb. The distance between these places is about 400 kilometers. Elijah walked for 40 days and 40 nights for this trip.

Stop and look at a map of Israel. Find Beersheba. Then find Mount Horeb. [Pause this audio here.]{.mark}

In the first scene, when Elijah arrives at Mount Horeb, he spends the night in a cave for shelter. If people in your language do not know about caves, you can say "a hole in the rocks" or something similar. Mount Horeb is also called Mount Sinai. The Israelites knew Mount Horeb as the mountain of God because it was at Mount Horeb that God appeared to Moses in the burning bush and gave the law to Israel after they left Egypt.

Suddenly, the word of Yahweh, or a message from Yahweh, comes to Elijah, and Yahweh asks him, "What are you doing here, Elijah?" The storyteller uses a word to show that something new and unexpected is happening.

[Stop and ask two people to tell about a time when something surprised them. Listen to the special words they use to show surprise. Talk about which words in your language best show big surprises. Pause the audio here.]{.mark}

Yahweh's question is not to get new information. Yahweh already knows what Elijah is doing. Yahweh asks so that Elijah can think about his answer and talk about what is happening.

Elijah answers that he has been zealous for Yahweh, the God of heavenly armies. [Yahweh is the name for the one true God. God of heavenly armies, or hosts, is a title that people use for God to show that God is the leader of many angels and armies in heaven. God has great power. God rules over all. People are zealous when they have strong feelings about protecting what belongs to Yahweh. A zealous person works hard to help others worship only Yahweh and feels upset when people follow false gods.]{.mark} A zealous person is absolutely loyal.

Stop here and, as a group, tell a story about people who have strong feelings about protecting other people. How do you describe these people? How will you talk about people who are zealous for Yahweh, the God of hosts? Pause this audio here.

But the people of Israel have broken their covenant, or special agreement, with God. The people have destroyed Yahweh's altars and killed all Yahweh's prophets with a sword, or in a violent way. Elijah says he is the only prophet of Yahweh left.

Yahweh tells Elijah to go out and stand on the mountain in the presence of Yahweh, because Yahweh is about to pass by.

In scene two, outside the cave, the storyteller uses a word to speak of something sudden and important as Yahweh is passing by. Outside the cave, a strong wind blows and breaks rocks off the mountain before Yahweh, but Yahweh is not in the wind. ["Before Yahweh" means Yahweh is there. Yahweh's power causes these things to happen. The rocks break because Yahweh is present. Everything happens in front of Yahweh.]{.mark} Then the ground shakes in an earthquake, but Yahweh is not in the earthquake. After that, a fire comes, but Yahweh is not in the fire either. [Yahweh's power causes the wind, the earthquake, and the fire. But Yahweh does not show himself in the wind, the earthquake, or the fire. Yahweh is near, but Yahweh does not speak through these things. Yahweh is present, but Yahweh chooses to speak in a quiet, gentle voice after these things happen.]{.mark}

Elijah hears a gentle, quiet sound like a whisper, a gentle breeze, a very quiet voice, or perhaps just the sound of quiet silence. When Elijah hears it, he covers his face with his robe and stands at the cave entrance.

[Stop and make these sounds: a leaf moving in the wind, a mother whispering to her sleeping baby, someone breathing very softly. Listen to each sound. Talk about words in your language for the quietest sounds you know. Choose which words best show Yahweh's quiet voice. Pause the audio here.]{.mark}

The voice asks Elijah again, "What are you doing here, Elijah?" This voice must be Yahweh since it is the same question that Yahweh asked before.

[Stop and talk about different words for "voice" in your language. Think about words you use for a human voice, an animal sound, thunder, wind, and spirits. Now think carefully: which word shows this is not a human body speaking, but Yahweh, who is spirit? Choose a word that helps people understand this is Yahweh's special voice speaking to Elijah. Pause the audio here.]{.mark}

Elijah gives the same answer. This time, Yahweh gives Elijah instructions. Yahweh tells Elijah to go back the way he came and stop in the wilderness near Damascus, which is the capital city of Aram. Aram is also called Syria. The wilderness of Damascus is the desert area south and east of the city.

Stop and look at a map that shows Israel, Judah, Aram, and Mount Horeb. Notice the route that Elijah would take from Mount Horeb to the wilderness outside Damascus in Aram. Pause the audio here.

When Elijah goes to the wilderness near Damascus, Elijah is to pour oil on Hazael's head to make Hazael the new king of Aram. Yahweh does not say where Elijah should anoint Jehu and Elisha, but those anointings happen somewhere else.

To anoint someone, a prophet would take special oil and pour it on the person's head. This oil would run down their head and show everyone that Yahweh chose this person for a special job. If your language has no special word for anointing, you can say "pour oil on the head" to show this special act.

Stop and do this activity: Show how someone would pour water, representing the oil, over a head. What words describe liquid flowing down on purpose? Pause the audio here.

Yahweh says he will use three people to punish those who worship Baal: Hazael, the new king of Syria, Jehu, the new king of Israel, and Elisha, the new prophet of Yahweh. Hazael will kill the idol worshipers. Jehu will kill the ones who escape the sword of Hazael. Elisha will kill the ones who escape the sword of Jehu. [The sword was a common weapon to destroy an enemy. To "escape the sword" means that you would escape so that your enemies would not kill you in battle. Yahweh is telling Elijah that Jehu, Hazael, and Elisha will kill the idol worshippers.]{.mark}

But Yahweh will protect 7,000 Israelites who have not bowed down to and kissed Baal. Bowing down to the idols of Baal and kissing the idols of Baal is to worship Baal and show allegiance and respect with your body.

[Stop and discuss as a team what actions people use to show deep respect to leaders in your area, such as bowing, kneeling, or other movements. Choose words that show how people gave their loyalty to Baal by bowing and kissing, while 7,000 people stayed faithful to Yahweh and refused to worship Baal with their bodies. Pause the audio here.]{.mark}

In scene three, Elijah leaves [Mount Horeb and travels back through Israel to find Elisha. Elisha is in his hometown, Abel Meholah, which is located in the Jordan Valley, north of Jericho.]{.mark}

[Stop and look at a map of Israel during the divided kingdom that shows Mount Horeb and Abel Meholah. Pause the audio here.]{.mark}

[Elisha is plowing a field with 12 pairs of oxen, which shows that Elisha's family is wealthy. Elijah walks up to Elisha in the field and throws his robe over him. Elijah's robe represents Elijah's role and authority as a prophet of Yahweh. This act means Elijah is choosing Elisha as the next prophet.]{.mark}

[Stop and talk about how leaders in your community choose someone for important work. What do the leaders do? What do the leaders give? Now, talk about how people will understand how Elijah puts his robe on Elisha. Choose words that show Elijah is picking Elisha as Yahweh's next prophet. Pause the audio here.]{.mark}

[Elisha is busy guiding one pair of oxen, so when Elijah leaves, Elisha has to stop his work and run after Elijah to show he accepts the call.]{.mark}

In scene four, [Elisha wants to say a proper farewell to his parents. In this culture, people kissed family members to say goodbye. In your language, what words show a loving, respectful farewell between children and parents? If people in your area do not kiss when they say goodbye, what words or actions show the same meaning? Choose words that clearly show this was a farewell gesture.]{.mark}

[Stop here and discuss how to translate this farewell scene so that everyone understands it shows respect between child and parents.]{.mark} If your culture does not kiss at goodbyes, discuss how you will make sure you clearly communicate that this is a farewell gesture. Pause the audio here.

[Elijah says, "What have I done to you?" This question can mean two things. It can mean, "Go back home if you want. I am not stopping you." It can also mean, "Go, but remember what I have done is important. Come back to me." Elijah wants Elisha to think about the choice to follow Elijah.]{.mark}

[Stop and discuss how people in your culture speak when you ask someone to make an important choice. Share examples from your own language. Decide if Elijah's words should sound like he is letting Elisha go back or asking Elisha to go, but to come back. Work as a group to choose the best words for your language. Pause the audio here.]{.mark}

[Elisha does several things to show he is leaving his old life behind. Elisha kills one pair of oxen and uses the wooden parts of the yoke and plowing equipment as firewood to cook the meat. Elisha gives the meat to the people in his village for a farewell meal. After this, Elisha leaves his home and family to follow Elijah and becomes his servant. Elisha is also becoming Elijah's student as he prepares to take Elijah's place as a prophet of Yahweh.]{.mark}

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 19:9–21 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has four scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The prophet Elijah
  • Yahweh
  • Elisha
  • And the people of Elisha's village, including his father and mother

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

When the prophet Elijah arrives at Mount Horeb, he goes into the cave and shelters there for the night. Suddenly, the word of Yahweh comes to Elijah: "What are you doing here, Elijah?"

Elijah replies to Yahweh, "I have been very zealous in my devotion to Yahweh, the God of Heavenly Armies. The people of Israel have rejected your covenant with them. The people have torn down the altars of sacrifice to you. And the people have put the prophets of Yahweh to death with the sword. I am the only one left who worships you, and now the people of Israel are trying to kill me, too."

Then Yahweh says to Elijah, "Go out of the cave and stand on the mountain in the presence of Yahweh."

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Elijah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • What does this mean to stand in the presence of Yahweh? or
  • I am too afraid of a holy God. There is no way I can stand in the presence of Yahweh; or
  • I will do whatever Yahweh says, but I am afraid that I have walked all the way out here to die.

Continue the drama.

Suddenly, a great wind blows. The wind is so powerful that it tears the rocks out of the mountain and shatters the rocks. But Yahweh is not in the wind. After the wind, there is an earthquake that shakes the ground, but Yahweh is not in the earthquake. After the earthquake, there is a fire, but Yahweh is not in the fire.

After the fire, there is a small, gentle whisper. When Elijah hears the whisper, Elijah pulls his robe over his face and goes out to stand at the entrance of the cave.

Then a voice says to Elijah, "What are you doing here, Elijah?"

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Elijah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am confused by all of these happenings; or
  • I am now certain that this is Yahweh speaking; or
  • Why is Yahweh asking me the same thing again? What does this mean?

Continue the drama.

Elijah replies to Yahweh, "I have been very zealous in my devotion to Yahweh, the God of Heavenly Armies. The people of Israel have rejected your covenant with them. The people have torn down the altars of sacrifice to you. And the people have put the prophets of Yahweh to death with the sword. I am the only one left who worships you, and now the people of Israel are trying to kill me, too."

Then Yahweh responds to Elijah, "Go back the way you came and then go out to the wilderness of Damascus. When you get to the wilderness, anoint Hazael as the new king of Aram. Also, you are to go anoint Jehu, son of Nimshi, as the new king of Israel. And you are to anoint Elisha, son of Shaphat from Abel Meholah, to become the prophet of Yahweh in your place. Jehu will kill any who escapes the sword of Hazael. Elisha will kill any who escapes the sword of Jehu. However, I will protect 7,000 people in Israel. These 7,000 are the ones who have not bowed their knees down to worship Baal nor have their mouths kissed his idols."

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Elijah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • There are 7,000 people who are following Yahweh and have never turned to Baal. I can hardly imagine this, but I am so happy that it is true; or
  • Yes! Finally, someone will take my place as prophet. Being a prophet is such a burden.

Continue the drama.

So Elijah leaves Mount Horeb to go find Elisha in his hometown. Elisha is plowing with 12 yokes of oxen. As Elisha is guiding the 12th pair of oxen, Elijah takes his robe and puts it on Elisha's shoulders.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Elisha, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Yes, this prophet Elijah is very strange to throw his robe on me while I am plowing. Does Elijah mean I am to be a prophet? or
  • I am excited to be a prophet. I knew Yahweh was preparing me to do something great for Yahweh; or
  • Elijah is leaving, but I am in the middle of plowing with all these oxen. What should I do? I had better act fast.

Continue the drama.

Elisha stops the oxen, runs up to Elijah, and says, "Let me go kiss my father and mother goodbye. Then I will come back to you."

Elijah says, "Go on back. What have I done to you?"

So Elisha leaves Elijah and goes back. Elisha takes one pair of oxen and slaughters the oxen. Elisha uses the plow for firewood to cook the meat. Elisha gives the meat to the people of the village, and the people all eat.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing the people of Elisha's village, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am enjoying this feast, but I am sad that Elisha is leaving; or
  • Young Elisha is now growing up to be the next prophet Elijah. What strange times!

Continue the drama.

Then Elisha leaves to follow Elijah and become his servant.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 19:9–21 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

When Elijah spends the night in the cave at Mount Horeb, the word of Yahweh, or a message from Yahweh, comes to Elijah. Yahweh is the name God gives himself. Translate Yahweh in the same way you have in previous passages. For more information on Yahweh and "word of Yahweh," refer to the Master Glossary.

Elijah tells Yahweh that Elijah has been very zealous for Yahweh, the God of Heavenly Armies. When someone is zealous, they are so passionately devoted to someone or something that they are jealous for that relationship to stay pure. Elijah is zealous for Yahweh, the God of hosts, Heavenly Armies. [This name for Yahweh means that Yahweh is God and leads powerful angels and spiritual beings in heaven, like a great king or commander of a huge army. This name shows that Yahweh is very powerful and rules over everything in heaven and on earth. Translate]{.mark} Yahweh, the God of Hosts, or heavenly armies, as you have previously. For more information on Yahweh, God of Hosts, refer to the Master Glossary.

Stop here and discuss how you will say that Elijah was zealous. For more information on zealous, refer to zeal in the Master Glossary. Pause this audio here.

Elijah says that the people of Israel, or the Israelites, have broken the covenant with Yahweh. A covenant is a solemn promise that people cannot break. Centuries before this story, Yahweh made a covenant with Israel on Mount Horeb and gave them the laws. Yahweh said that he would be with his people, and the people said that they would obey Yahweh's laws. Elijah says that the people broke the covenant because they did not keep the laws, but worshipped Baal and other gods instead. Translate the covenant, Israelites, and Israel, the same way you have previously. For more information on Israel, Israelites, and covenant, refer to the Master Glossary.

Elijah says the people of Israel tore down altars of worship to Yahweh. [An altar is a special table or platform that people make of earth or stones where people place and often burn gifts or sacrifices, like animals or food, to God. Translate altar as you have in previous passages. For more information on altar, refer to the Master Glossary.]{.mark}

[Elijah tells Yahweh that the Israelites put the prophets of Yahweh to death and that Elijah is the only one left. Translate prophet as you have in previous passages. For more information on prophet, refer to the Master Glossary.]{.mark}

When Elijah hears the gentle whisper, Elijah puts his robe over his face. Translate robe in the same way you have in previous passages, and refer to robe in the Master Glossary for more information.

[Yahweh tells Elijah to anoint new leaders. To anoint means to pour oil on someone's head. To pour oil on someone showed that God chose that person for a special job. The oil was a sign that Yahweh gave that person authority and power to lead. Use the same word or phrase for anoint as you used in previous passages. For more information on anoint, refer to the Master Glossary.]{.mark}

Yahweh says to anoint Jehu, the son of Nimshi, as the new king of Israel. A son is the male child of his parents, but in this case, Jehu was actually the grandson of Nimshi, so you may use the word for descendant if you would like. [For more information about son or descendant, refer to the Master Glossary.]{.mark}

Elisha leaves to follow Elijah and become his servant, or assistant. Elisha is Elijah's helper, but also a disciple who is learning to take on Elijah's role as prophet. It is both clearer and closer to the original language to say that Elisha set out to follow and serve, or assist, Elijah, rather than to say that Elisha is Elijah's servant.

Yahweh says he is protecting 7,000 people in Israel who have not bowed down to Baal. Baal is a false god of the Canaanite people. Translate Baal and bowed down as you have previously. For more information on Baal and bow, refer to the Master Glossary.

Yahweh tells Elijah to go to the wilderness of Damascus and anoint Hazael as the new king. Translate wilderness and king as you have previously. For more information on wilderness and king, refer to the Master Glossary.

Elijah finds Elisha plowing a field with a yoke of oxen. Translate yoke and oxen in the same way you have translated them in previous passages.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 20:1–12
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 20:1–12 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 20:1–12 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 20:1–12 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the last passage, Yahweh reminded the prophet Elijah that he was not alone. Yahweh told Elijah where to find Elisha. Elisha would become Elijah's assistant and eventually replace Elijah as Yahweh's prophet to Yahweh's people. Now, Israel's enemy Aram, or Syria, prepares once more to attack Israel.

Ben-Hadad, the king of Aram, or Syria, launches an attack against the capital city of Samaria in Israel. The things that happen in this story seem slightly different than the things that happened in history at that time. It is possible that the storyteller uses the two [kings' names as representatives of kings during this time. Because the storyteller does use the names Ahab and Ben-Hadad, it is best to keep the same names in your translation.]{.mark}

Stop here and, as a group, look at a map of Syria and the city of Samaria. Pause this audio here.

Ben-Hadad brought not only his entire army to Samaria but also 32 kings who were under his authority, along with their horses and chariots. A chariot is a vehicle with two wheels that horses pull, and soldiers often use chariots in battles.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a picture of horses and Syrian chariots. Pause this audio here.

The Aramean army besieged the city of Samaria. When an army besieged a city, the soldiers surrounded the city in order to cut off its supplies of food, water, and weapons. The people inside the city would eventually have to fight their way out or surrender. Once the Aramean army surrounded the city of Samaria, Ben-Hadad sent messengers to Ahab, the king of Israel. During this time, kings did not meet face to face in case their enemy decided to use the meeting as an opportunity to kill them. Instead, kings sent men back and forth to give messages on their behalf.

As a forceful way to get Ahab to submit to his demands, Ben-Hadad tells King Ahab that King Ahab's gold and silver belong to Ben-Hadad. The Aramean king also demands that King Ahab give him the best of his wives and children. It is unclear if "best" refers to women and children who were the most beautiful or the most intelligent. Regardless, the women and children would have been captives of war.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do leaders defend their land from enemies where you live? How do opposing forces come to an agreement to stop fighting over land? Pause this audio here.

King Ahab agrees to Ben-Hadad's demands because he knows it is the price he must pay to get the Aramean king to stop attacking Samaria. Ahab sends a messenger to Ben-Hadad to say that Ahab formally agrees to give Ben-Hadad his gold, silver, women, and children. Ahab calls Ben-Hadad "my Lord, the King" as a way to show that he will submit to him. King Ahab says that all he has belongs to Ben-Hadad. However, Ben-Hadad wants more. The Aramean king further insults Ahab by sending messengers to say that he will send his servants the next day to raid Ahab's palace and the houses of Ahab's officials and take whatever they want.

Once King Ahab understands that Ben-Hadad's goal is to completely humiliate him, Ahab calls all the elders, or tribal and family leaders, of Israel together for a meeting. King Ahab explains to the elders that he previously accepted the Aramean king's demands. However, Ben-Hadad is now asking for too much because he wants to take everything of value that Ahab has. The Israelite elders advise Ahab not to accept Ben-Hadad's demands. So Ahab sends messengers back to Ben-Hadad to say that though Ahab had agreed to give gold, silver, women, and children, Ahab now refuses to meet Ben-Hadad's new demands.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Ben-Hadad used fear and threats to try to make King Ahab give in. Tell a story of when someone or something forced your people to give up what was theirs. Discuss when it is wise to resist and when it is wise to submit. Pause this audio here.

Once Ben-Hadad hears Ahab's response, Ben-Hadad makes an oath, or promise, and says that he will completely destroy Samaria and make it only a few handfuls of dust. If he fails, then Ben-Hadad will accept the gods' punishment.

Ahab quickly sends back a reply to Ben-Hadad and says, "One who puts on his armor should not boast like one who takes it off." Armor is what soldiers wear to protect themselves in battle. In other words, Ahab tells Ben-Hadad that he should not be so quick to assume victory when he has not yet won the battle.

Stop here and look at a picture of armor as a group. Pause this audio here.

The Aramean king receives Ahab's message as he is drinking and celebrating with the other kings in his tent. Upset by Ahab's refusal, Ben-Hadad immediately orders his soldiers to attack the city of Samaria once more.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 20:1–12 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has six scenes.

In the first scene: The Aramean king Ben-Hadad attacks Samaria with all of his army, horses, chariots, and 32 other kings at his command.

In the second scene: Ben-Hadad sends messengers to Ahab and tells him to give Ben-Hadad all of his gold, silver, and his best women and children. Ahab sends a messenger back to Ben-Hadad to agree to his demands and says, "All I have is yours, my Lord, the king."

In the third scene: Ben-Hadad sends messengers to Ahab to say that not only will Ben-Hadad take Ahab's silver, gold, women, and children, but at this time tomorrow, Ben-Hadad will send his servants to take whatever valuables they want from Ahab's palace as well as from the houses of Ahab's officials.

In the fourth scene: Ahab calls the elders of Israel to Samaria to a meeting and tells them what has happened. The elders agree that Ahab should not meet Ben-Hadad's humiliating demands. Ahab sends messengers to Ben-Hadad to say he will not allow Ben-Hadad to raid his palace and his officials' homes.

In the fifth scene: Ben-Hadad receives Ahab's message and makes a promise to say that the gods will punish Ben-Hadad if he does not completely destroy Samaria and turn it into handfuls of dust. Ahab sends messengers back to Ben-Hadad and says that Ben-Hadad should not speak as one who already has victory.

In the sixth scene: Ben-Hadad receives Ahab's message while he is drinking with the other kings in his tent. Ben-Hadad is so upset by the message that he orders his soldiers to attack Samaria once again.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Ben-Hadad, the king of Syria
  • The Aramean army
  • 32 kings
  • Ahab, the king of Israel
  • Messengers
  • And the elders of Israel

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

Ben-Hadad gathers his army and 32 kings to go to Samaria, the capital of Israel, to surround and attack it. It is important to note that these kings were leaders or governors under Ben-Hadad's authority. Ben-Hadad was the king of Aram, otherwise known as Syria. Ben-Hadad's army included chariots, or war carts that horses pull.

It is important to note that the distance between the capital of Aram to Samaria is between 140 to 160 kilometers. This means it would take an army around five to eight days to arrive in Samaria.

Ben-Hadad tells Ahab that he owns all of Ahab's silver, gold, and his best wives and children. It is also important to note that Ben-Hadad is not asking for these things but making a statement that they already belong to Ben-Hadad.

King Ahab sends messengers and agrees to meet Ben-Hadad's demands. Ahab calls Ben-Hadad "my Lord, or master, the king" to show that he is willing to obey Ben-Hadad. Ahab agrees to the demands and says all he has belongs to Ben-Hadad.

Some time passes, though we are not sure how long it took Ben-Hadad to respond. The messengers return with another message from Ben-Hadad. In it, Ben-Hadad repeats his earlier claim of ownership over the gold, silver, wives, and children of Israel. But now, Ben-Hadad adds that by this time tomorrow, Ben-Hadad's servants will go to Ahab's palace and the houses of Ahab's officials and take whatever valuable things they want. These servants are most likely Ben-Hadad's military officers.

It is important to note that the messengers are giving exact quotes from their kings to each rival king. In the message, Ben-Hadad reminds Ahab of what he has already demanded and then makes an extra demand. Remember that the storyteller directly quotes each king's message, but it is really the messengers who are going back and forth and giving the messages to each king.

Stop here and discuss with your group: How do you tell a story where one character quotes someone who is quoting himself? How can the storyteller make it less confusing? Make up some examples or act them out to find a natural and clear way to express a messenger giving direct messages to someone else. Pause this audio here.

Ahab calls together the elders of the land. The elders were leaders of the tribes and families throughout the land who helped make community decisions for Israel. The meeting happens while the enemy army is surrounding the city of Samaria. Somehow, the elders can still reach Samaria. This could mean that the army was not directly at the gates but in the area around the city. It is also possible that Ahab only called together the leaders who were already in the city of Samaria.

Ahab tells the elders to see for themselves the demands of Ben-Hadad. Ahab also admits that he already agreed to Ben-Hadad's first request. The elders advise Ahab not to accept anything else Ben-Hadad demands.

Ahab tells the messengers what to say to Ben-Hadad: "Tell the king that I will agree to everything from the first message, but I will not accept the latest demand."

Stop here and remember what you discussed about telling a story with messengers who tell direct messages to someone else. Use that to make this part as natural and easy to understand as possible. Pause this audio here.

It is important to note that the storyteller does not repeat the two demands, but the audience will remember the two things that Ben-Hadad asked for. The first demand was that Ben-Hadad said he already owned all the silver, gold, wives, and children. The second demand was that Ben-Hadad's officers would come inside the city and take all the valuable things from both Ahab and Ahab's officials.

Then Ben-Hadad sends a response to Ahab. Ben-Hadad makes an oath, or promise, and says that the gods should punish Ben-Hadad severely if anything remains of Samaria. Ben-Hadad's army would leave so little, in fact, that there would not be enough dust for each of his men to take a handful.

It is important to note that we have already seen several people make these oaths, or strong promises, in previous stories. When people in that time made an oath, they often said that if they did not keep their promise, the gods should punish them. This was a way to say that they were serious about doing what they promised to do.

Stop here and discuss with your group: What expressions do you use in your culture to describe a situation where people completely destroy everything, and nothing remains? Pause this audio here.

Ahab uses special language to respond to Ben-Hadad. Ahab says, "The one who boasts should not be the one putting on his armor, but the one taking it off." Ahab is implying that someone who is just going into battle should not claim victory, but only the one who has already won and is taking off his armor has the right to boast.

Stop here and discuss with your group: How do people in your culture say that someone should not act like they have achieved something before they actually have? Pause this audio here.

Ben-Hadad receives Ahab's response when he is drinking alcohol with the other kings in a tent or temporary shelter. Soldiers often stayed in tents when they were fighting battles during wartime. Ben-Hadad was likely already celebrating with his men the fact that he thought he would win the victory over King Ahab's city.

Ben-Hadad is upset that Ahab rejects his terms, so Ben-Hadad orders his men to prepare to attack Samaria.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 20:1–12 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has six scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Ben-Hadad, the king of Syria
  • The Aramean army
  • 32 kings
  • Ahab, the king of Israel
  • Messengers
  • And the elders of Israel

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Act out the Aramean king Ben-Hadad attacking Samaria with all of his army, horses, chariots, and 32 other kings. Ben-Hadad sends messengers to Ahab. Ben-Hadad says he already owns all of Ahab's gold, silver, and best women and children.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Ahab, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am distressed. I must meet some of Ben-Hadad's demands, or he will not give up; or
  • I feel worried. Hopefully, a few treasures will be enough to make Ben-Hadad go away; or
  • I am afraid. Ben-Hadad's army is large. I hope this is all Ben-Hadad makes me pay.

Continue the drama.

Act out how Ahab sends messengers back to Ben-Hadad and agrees to his demands.

Act out how Ben-Hadad responds and says that not only will he take Ahab's silver, gold, women, and children, but at that time tomorrow, Ben-Hadad will send his servants to take whatever they want from Ahab's palace as well as from the houses of Ahab's officials.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Ahab, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am outraged. Now this is too much! I already accepted defeat; or
  • I feel hatred. War is one thing, but to push for such a humiliating defeat is just insulting; or
  • I am determined. I will call the elders, and we will find a way to fight. I cannot let the other nations think we are weak.

Continue the drama.

Act out how Ahab calls the elders of Israel to Samaria for a meeting and tells them what has happened. The elders agree that Ahab should not meet Ben-Hadad's humiliating demands. Ahab sends messengers to Ben-Hadad to say Ahab will not allow his palace and his officials' homes to be raided the next day.

Act out how Ben-Hadad receives Ahab's message and makes a promise to say the gods will punish Ben-Hadad if he does not completely destroy Samaria and turn it into handfuls of dust. Act out how Ahab sends messengers back to Ben-Hadad to say he should not speak as one who already has victory.

Act out how Ben-Hadad receives Ahab's message while he is drinking with the other kings in his tent. Ben-Hadad is so upset by the message that he orders his soldiers to attack Samaria once again.

Pause the drama. Ask the person playing Ben-Hadad, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel angry. How dare Ahab resist me? or
  • I feel insulted. There is no way Ahab can defeat me or my army; or
  • I will make Ahab pay for his words.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 20:1–12 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Ben-Hadad, the king of Aram, gathers his army and attacks Samaria. A king is someone who rules over a city or territory, like a country. Ben-Hadad ruled over Aram, which is the modern-day country of Syria. Use the same word or phrase for king as you used in previous passages. For more information on king, refer to the Master Glossary.

The Aramean army "besieges," or surrounds, the city of Samaria to force them to fight their way out or surrender. The Aramean army includes soldiers and "chariots," or war carts that horses pull. Be sure to translate besiege and chariot in the same way you have in previous passages.

After he attacks Samaria, Ben-Hadad sends a message to Ahab. Ben-Hadad uses messengers, or men who deliver Ben-Hadad's message to Ahab on his behalf. Be sure to use the same word for messenger as you have used in previous passages.

Ahab was the king of Israel. Here, Israel refers to the land where the Israelites lived. Specifically, Samaria was in the northern territory of Israel. Use the same word or phrase for Israel as you used in previous passages. For more information on Israel, refer to the Master Glossary.

Ben-Hadad demands all of Ahab's silver and gold as well as his best women and children. Ahab agrees to Ben-Hadad's demands. Ahab calls Ben-Hadad "my lord, or master, the king" to show that he will obey Ben-Hadad's authority. Ahab is admitting that Ben-Hadad has defeated Ahab and is master over Ahab. Use the same word or phrase for lord as you used in previous passages. For more information on lord, refer to the Master Glossary.

Ben-Hadad further demands that his servants, or officials, come the next day and raid Ahab's palace and the houses of Ahab's servants, or officials, to take whatever they want. Use the same word for palace, or king's house, that you have used in previous passages. Ben-Hadad calls both his officials and Ahab's officials "servants," or men who work for a king to carry out what the king asks them to do. Use the same word or phrase for servants as you have in previous passages when it means government officials. If you use the word for servant, refer to the Master Glossary for more information.

Ahab is outraged and summons the elders of Israel for a meeting. The elders were tribal and family leaders throughout the land of Israel who helped make community decisions. Use the same word or phrase for elder as you used in previous passages. For more information on elder, refer to the Master Glossary.

Ahab refers to himself as a servant and tells Ben-Hadad that he cannot meet Ben-Hadad's second demand. By calling himself a servant, Ahab admits that he is under the power of Ben-Hadad as his master, but Ahab refuses to give in to Ben-Hadad's second demand.

Ben-Hadad makes an oath, or promise, saying that the gods will punish Ben-Hadad if he does not completely destroy Samaria and turn it into a few handfuls of dust. Although the storyteller does not use the word for oath, if you use it in your translation, use the same word or phrase for oath that you have used previously and refer to oath in the Master Glossary for more information. When Ben-Hadad refers to gods, he is not referring to the true Creator God but rather to other spirit beings as if they have power. Use the same word or phrase for gods as you used in previous passages. For more information on gods, refer to the Master Glossary.

Ahab quickly sends back a reply to Ben-Hadad and says, "One who puts on his armor should not boast like one who takes it off." Armor is what soldiers wear to protect themselves in battle.

Stop here and look at a picture of armor as a group. What words or phrases will you use for armor? Pause this audio here.

Ben-Hadad is in a tent with the other kings, probably drinking alcohol. Use the same word or phrase for tents as you have used previously.

Ben-Hadad prepares to attack the city. Cities were large settlements that had walls surrounding them. Use the same word or phrase for city as you have previously.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 20:13–22
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 20:13–22 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 20:13–22 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 20:13–22 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the last story, Ben-Hadad, the king of Aram, attacked Samaria with his entire army, as well as 32 other kings. Ben-Hadad demanded that King Ahab give him all of Ahab's silver, gold, women, and children. King Ahab agreed and gave in, but when Ben-Hadad said his servants would also raid Ahab's palace and his officials' homes, King Ahab refused. Now, Ben-Hadad gets his soldiers ready to attack Samaria once again. You will remember that the modern-day name of Aram is Syria.

Stop here and look at a map of Aram and Samaria as a group. Pause this audio here.

While Ben-Hadad is preparing to attack Samaria, an unnamed prophet comes to Ahab, the King of Israel. As you remember, prophets are messengers of Yahweh who speak to people, especially kings, on Yahweh's behalf. The prophet asks Ahab if Ahab has seen the large numbers of the Aramean army. The prophet does not ask Ahab this because Ahab cannot see the army. The prophet wants to emphasize how large Ben-Hadad's army is and how hard it will be to defeat. The prophet tells King Ahab that Yahweh will give Ben-Hadad's army into Ahab's hand, or let Ahab's army win against Ben-Hadad's army. The prophet says Yahweh will do this so that Ahab will know that he is Yahweh, Israel's God, and that Yahweh is in control. You will remember that King Ahab and other Israelite kings during this time did not usually follow Yahweh as they should. Yahweh is yet again showing that he is the one true God of the Israelites.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story of a time when a spiritual messenger came to a leader during a time of trouble. How do community leaders recognize and respond to messages from the spirit world? Pause this audio here.

King Ahab asks the prophet who Yahweh will use to bring about such a victory. The prophet says Yahweh will bring victory through the young officers of the provincial governors. Governors were leaders of smaller territories within the land who were under the authority of the king. The officers were young, unmarried men who were part of a special elite guard. These men were under the authority of different governors who paid them. Ahab asks who will start the battle, and the prophet says that Ahab himself will lead the soldiers into battle against Ben-Hadad.

So Ahab quickly musters, or calls together, the military in order to fight. Ahab gathers 232 young officers of the governors as well as 7,000 Israelite foot soldiers. The Israelite army begins to march at noon, or when the sun is at its highest, with the 232 young officers marching out first. Meanwhile, Ben-Hadad is getting drunk in his tent with the 32 kings who are helping him.

Ben-Hadad had sent out scouts, or men who explore and return with information. The scouts alert Ben-Hadad that Israelite troops are approaching from Samaria. Ben-Hadad tells them that whether the Israelites have come out for peace or to fight, Ben-Hadad wants his men to take the Israelites alive and not kill them.

Meanwhile, Ahab's young officers march out toward the Aramean camp, and they begin to kill their Aramean enemies who are coming to fight them. They strike down each Aramean they come to. The Arameans quickly find that the Israelites are defeating them, so they begin to run away from Ahab's army. King Ben-Hadad gets on a horse and is able to escape the Israelite soldiers with some of his horsemen. King Ahab wins a great victory over the Arameans. Ahab most likely kills the horses and destroys the carts.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a picture of horses and chariots. How do groups celebrate victory over one another in your culture? Perhaps they celebrate victory even in competitions, sports, or wars. Pause this audio here.

When the battle is over, the same unnamed prophet comes to King Ahab. The prophet tells Ahab to prepare himself because Ben-Hadad, the King of Aram, will be back the next spring to attack once more. The prophet advises Ahab to rebuild his forces and strengthen his army so that he will be prepared for the next attack.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Though Ahab won a great victory, the prophet tells Ahab to prepare himself for another fight. Discuss how you will describe in your language a time when you knew a difficult task or fight was coming. How did you prepare for it?

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 20:13–22 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has five scenes.

In the first scene: Ben-Hadad is in his tent with the other kings, preparing his troops to attack Samaria. Meanwhile, Yahweh sends a prophet to King Ahab. The prophet asks Ahab if he has seen the Aramean army. The prophet tells Ahab that Yahweh will give Ahab victory over the Arameans so that Ahab will know that Yahweh is God. The prophet says the victory will come through the young officers. The prophet tells Ahab he must lead the battle against Ben-Hadad.

In the second scene: Ahab gathers together 232 young officers and 7,000 Israelite soldiers. The Israelite army then marches out of Samaria with the young officers going first.

In the third scene: Ben-Hadad's scouts return to the tent where Ben-Hadad is drinking and tell him that the Israelite army is approaching. Ben-Hadad says that whether the Israelites have come for war or peace, Ben-Hadad wants his men to take the Israelites alive.

In the fourth scene: The young Israelite officers defeat their Aramean enemies. The Arameans begin to run away. King Ahab defeats the horses and chariots, while King Ben-Hadad manages to escape on a horse with some of his horsemen. King Ahab has a great victory.

In the fifth scene: The prophet tells King Ahab to prepare himself because Ben-Hadad will be back to attack again in the spring.

The characters in this passage are:

  • King of Aram, Ben-Hadad
  • 32 kings
  • King Ahab
  • Yahweh
  • The unnamed prophet
  • The 232 young officers
  • The Israelite army
  • The Aramean army with horses and chariots
  • And Ben-Hadad's men who were sending Ben-Hadad messages

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

It is important to remember that Ben-Hadad is in his tent, drinking with the 32 other kings and preparing to attack Samaria once again. The way the storyteller starts the story makes sure that the audience is paying attention to the events that are about to happen.

Yahweh sends a prophet, or messenger of God, to King Ahab. The storyteller does not tell us the name of this prophet. The prophet starts the message by saying, "This is what Yahweh says...." This was a common way to introduce an announcement of God.

Yahweh asks this question through the prophet: "Have you seen all this great multitude?" Yahweh asks the question not because Yahweh is looking for an answer, but to draw attention to the number of people in the enemy's army. In this case, the word multitude can also mean a lot of loud noise and disorder.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How would you describe a big, disordered group of people? Pause this audio here.

The prophet says that Yahweh says, "I will give it into your hand this day, and you shall know that I am Yahweh." Yahweh emphasizes that it is Yahweh himself who will make King Ahab victorious over Ben-Hadad's army and that he is Yahweh, Israel's special God.

It is important to note that Yahweh does not say he will literally put the enemies inside the hand of King Ahab, but that Yahweh is going to submit Ben-Hadad's army to the power of King Ahab.

Yahweh is not implying that King Ahab does not know who Yahweh is. Instead, Yahweh is making it clear that Yahweh is in control of the situation.

Ahab asks the prophet who Yahweh will use to bring victory over the Arameans.

The prophet says Yahweh will use the servants or young officers of the governors of the districts. Yahweh explains that it is the young men whom the governors have as special guards who will fight and win.

Ahab asks the prophet, "Who shall begin the battle?" The prophet says Ahab himself will lead them. So Ahab gathers together the servants of the governors of the districts, and there are 232 men. Ahab also gathers together 7,000 Israelite soldiers.

The Israelite army marches out of Samaria at noon, with the 232 young officers going out first. At the same time, Ben-Hadad is outside the city, becoming drunk in the tent with the 32 kings who help him. The storyteller is saying that Ben-Hadad and the other 32 governors are drinking so much that they do not know what is happening.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do drunk people act in your culture? What is the most common way for people to get drunk in your culture? Pause this audio here.

When Ben-Hadad's men see the Israelite army approaching, they report what is happening to Ben-Hadad. It is important to note that these men were already out exploring around the city when they saw the Israelite army march out.

The men tell Ben-Hadad, "Men are coming out from Samaria." Ben-Hadad says, "If they have come out for peace, take them alive. Or if they have come out for war, take them alive." This is another way to say that Ben-Hadad wants his men to take the Israelites alive, regardless of the Israelites' reasons for coming. In either case, Ben-Hadad wants his army to capture the Israelites.

So the young officers from Israel fight and each strikes down his man. This is another way to say that the officers defeat each enemy they come to. The Arameans run away, and the army of Israel chases after them. But Ben-Hadad, the king of Aram, escapes on a horse with horsemen.

Ahab, the king of Israel, has a great victory over the horses and chariots. When the storyteller says the king of Israel struck a great blow, the storyteller is emphasizing the damage the Israelite army caused to the chariots and horses.

At the end of the battle, the prophet returns to Ahab and tells him to strengthen his position, because Ben-Hadad will return in the spring, or when the season changes. Spring is usually late March and late April. This was another way to say Ahab should strengthen and build his army since there is another battle yet to come.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 20:13–22 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has five scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • King of Aram, Ben-Hadad
  • 32 kings
  • King Ahab
  • Yahweh
  • The unnamed prophet
  • The 232 young officers
  • The Israelite army
  • The Aramean army with horses and chariots
  • And Ben-Hadad's men who were sending Ben-Hadad messages

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Act out Ben-Hadad in his tent with the other kings preparing his troops to attack Samaria. Yahweh sends a prophet to King Ahab. The prophet asks Ahab if he has seen the Aramean army. The prophet tells Ahab that Yahweh will give Ahab victory over the Arameans so that Ahab will know that Yahweh is Yahweh. The prophet says the victory will come through the young officers. The prophet tells Ahab he must lead the battle against Ben-Hadad.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing King Ahab, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am surprised. Why does Yahweh care about our people now? I did not ask for Yahweh's help; or
  • I do not have many choices. I will listen to this prophet if it will bring me victory; or
  • I feel frustrated. Out of all the gods I have called on, I did not expect Yahweh to be the one to help me.

Act out Ahab gathering 232 young officers and 7,000 Israelite soldiers. The Israelite army then marches out of Samaria with the young officers going first.

Act out Ben-Hadad's scouts returning to Ben-Hadad in the tent where he is drinking. The scouts tell Ben-Hadad that the Israelite army is approaching. Ben-Hadad says that, whether the Israelites have come for war or peace, Ben-Hadad wants his men to take the Israelites alive.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing King Ben-Hadad, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • This is all rather funny. What is Ahab doing now? or
  • Those Israelites have no chance. The Israelites need to accept that I have them surrounded; or
  • I am amused. Those soldiers look young! Maybe they are coming to give me what I demanded.

Act out the young officers defeating their Aramean enemies. The Arameans begin to run away. King Ahab defeats the horses and chariots, while King Ben-Hadad manages to escape on a horse with some of his horsemen. King Ahab has a great victory.

Act out the prophet coming to King Ahab and telling Ahab to prepare himself because Ben-Hadad will be back to attack again in the spring.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing King Ahab, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am victorious. I never would have believed we were capable of defeating the Arameans; or
  • I feel concerned. There is a lot to do if Ben-Hadad is going to come back with reinforcements; or
  • I am surprised Yahweh gave me victory. I did not expect Yahweh to help me!

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 20:13–22 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Yahweh sends a prophet to talk to King Ahab. A prophet is someone who gives messages from Yahweh to the people. Use the same word or phrase for prophet as you used in previous passages. For more information on prophet, refer to the Master Glossary.

The prophet speaks to Ahab, the king of Israel. A king is someone who rules over a city or territory, like a country. Ahab ruled over the nation of Israel, or the land where the Israelites lived. Use the same words or phrases for king and Israel as you used in previous passages. For more information on king or Israel, refer to the Master Glossary.

The prophet tells Ahab that Yahweh will give him victory so that Ahab will know that Yahweh is Yahweh. Yahweh is the personal name of God that shows the close relationship between God and Israel. Yahweh will give Ahab victory to show that Yahweh is the most powerful spiritual being who created everything. Use the same word or phrase for Yahweh as you used in previous passages. For more information on Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary.

The prophet says the junior officers under the provincial governors will lead the Israelite army into battle. The storyteller calls these men "servants," but we know that the junior officers were young, unmarried men who were a part of a special elite guard and under the authority of the provincial governors. The provincial governors were leaders of different areas of land in Israel and under the authority of the king of Israel.

Stop here and discuss how you will define junior officers and provincial governors in your language. Pause this audio here.

Ben-Hadad sent out men from his army to know what was happening around the city of Samaria. The men reported to Ben-Hadad that the Israelite army was marching out of the city.

Stop here and discuss how you will talk about these men from Ben-Hadad's army that he sent out to see what was happening. Pause this audio here.

The prophet says Yahweh will give the Syrian army into Ahab's hand, which is another way to say Yahweh will give Ahab power over the enemy army. Be sure to use the same word or phrase for "into your hand" as you have used in previous passages.

When Ben-Hadad hears that the Israelite army is approaching, Ben-Hadad says to take the Israelites alive, whether they have come for war or peace. If the Israelites had come for war, then that meant they were going to fight. But if the Israelites had come for peace, then that meant they were going to surrender. When people live in harmony with each other without fighting, they are living in peace. Use the same word or phrase for peace as you used in previous passages. For more information on peace, refer to the Master Glossary.

The Israelite army has a great victory over the Syrian soldiers and chariots. Chariots are war carts that horses pulled into battle. Be sure to use the same word for chariots as you have used in previous passages.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 20:23–34
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 20:23–34 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 20:23–34 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 20:23–34 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the last passage, Yahweh sent a prophet to King Ahab to tell him how to defeat the Aramean army that Ben-Hadad led. King Ahab and the Israelite army defeated Ben-Hadad at Samaria, and Ben-Hadad fled. But a prophet warned Ahab that Ben-Hadad would return and attack again in the spring.

After Ben-Hadad escaped back to Aram, or Syria, his servants, the officials who served in his government, told Ben-Hadad that they only lost the battle to Israel because Israel's gods were gods of the hills. Though Yahweh is the one true God of Israel, the Arameans believed the Israelites worshipped many gods. As you remember, the Arameans had attacked Samaria, the capital city of Israel, which was built on a large hill. The Arameans also used chariots, which work best on flat ground, not on rocky hills. The Arameans thought this was why the Israelites completely defeated the Arameans. Now, Ben-Hadad's officials believe that if they fight the Israelites on the "plains," or flat land with no mountains, this time, then they will win.

Stop here and look at a picture of a plain as a group. Pause this audio here.

The Aramean officials also advise Ben-Hadad to remove the regional kings who had helped him before and to replace them with new commanders. As you remember, these were the same 32 kings who had helped Ben-Hadad attack Samaria and who drank with Ben-Hadad in his tent. Then the officials tell Ben-Hadad he must rebuild his army as strong as before, with the same number of horses and chariots. King Ben-Hadad agrees with the officials' advice and begins to make preparations.

When spring arrives, or the time of the year when kings often go to war, King Ben-Hadad has rebuilt his Aramean army. Ben-Hadad's army marches to the city of Aphek in Aram to fight against Israel. Aphek is located between Damascus and the Valley of Jezreel.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a map of Aphek, Samaria, and Aram. Pause this audio here.

King Ahab also gathers his soldiers to fight at Aphek and gives them everything they need for battle. The two armies march out to meet each other and then camp across from each other on the plain. King Ahab divides the Israelites into two smaller groups. The storyteller tells us that the Israelite army looks small and unimpressive, like two small flocks of goats. In contrast, the Aramean army covers the whole countryside.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story about a time when a small army fought against a much larger army. Pause this audio here.

At this point, Yahweh sends the unnamed prophet, or man of God, to speak to King Ahab once again. The prophet gives Ahab the message from Yahweh, "The Arameans say that I am only a god of the hills, not the flat land. Because of this, I will cause you to conquer their large army, and in this way you and all your people will know that I am Yahweh." Yahweh tells Ahab he will deliver the Aramean army into Ahab's hands. This means that Yahweh will give Ahab power over the Arameans. Once again, Yahweh says Yahweh will do this so that Ahab and the Israelites will know that Yahweh is the one true God of Israel.

The two armies camp across from each other for seven days. Just like in the battle of Jericho, the Israelites enter into battle on the seventh day, and Yahweh gives them a huge victory. The Israelites kill 100,000 Aramean soldiers in one day, while the rest of the soldiers escape to the city of Aphek. But in the city, a wall collapses and kills 27,000 more Aramean soldiers. Meanwhile, King Ben-Hadad also runs away into the city and hides in an inner chamber or room.

While they hide with King Ben-hadad, Ben-Hadad's officials advise him to surrender to the king of Israel. The officials say that everyone knows that the kings of Israel are merciful, that they honor treaties and treat their enemies kindly. Ben-Hadad and his officials decide to wear "sackcloths," or rough pieces of cloth, and tie ropes around their heads before going to surrender to King Ahab. The officials hope that the king will spare their lives. At that time, people wore sackcloth to show that they were sad and humble. People put ropes around their heads to show that they were prisoners of someone.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a picture of someone wearing a sackcloth. Pause this audio here.

So Ben-Hadad wears a sackcloth and a rope around his head to meet King Ahab. Ben-Hadad waits behind while his officials go to talk to King Ahab. When the officials come before King Ahab, they say Ben-Hadad is the king's servant, and they beg Ahab to allow Ben-Hadad to live. King Ahab asks if Ben-Hadad is really still alive and seems surprised that he is not dead. Then King Ahab says the Aramean king is like a brother to Ahab. The officials listen carefully to King Ahab's words and try to decide if Ahab will treat Ben-Hadad kindly or not. When Ahab calls Ben-Hadad a brother, the officials are eager to believe him and agree that Ben-Hadad is like a brother to the king.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story of a time when someone who was an enemy became like friends or family. Pause this audio here.

Ahab asks the officials to bring Ben-Hadad to him. Then Ben-Hadad comes out of hiding and climbs into Ahab's chariot. Ben-Hadad promises to give back the cities his father took from Ahab's father. Ben-Hadad also tells Ahab that Ahab can open markets, or places to buy and sell goods, in his city of Damascus, just like Ben-Hadad has done in Samaria. By opening up markets for Israelite sellers in Damascus, Ahab would gain a new source of income. Ahab agrees to Ben-Hadad's covenant, or treaty, and decides to let the Aramean king return home free, as long as he keeps his promise.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 20:23–34 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has five scenes.

In the first scene: King Ben-Hadad's officials advise him that the Israelites only won because their gods are gods of the hills. The officials tell Ben-Hadad to replace the kings with officers, rebuild his army, and fight the Israelites again on flat ground. Then the Arameans will be able to defeat the Israelites. Ben-Hadad listens to the officials' advice and begins preparing his army once again.

In the second scene: The next spring, Ben-Hadad leads his new Aramean army and marches to the city of Aphek to fight the Israelites. King Ahab leads the Israelite army and prepares them to meet the Arameans on the plain. The two armies face each other across the plain. The Israelite army camps in two small groups, while the Arameans cover the countryside.

In the third scene: The man of God gives Ahab a message from Yahweh. The man of God says, "Because the Arameans think I am a god of the hills and not a god of the valleys, I will deliver this vast army into your hands, and you will know that I am Yahweh."

In the fourth scene: After the Israelites and Arameans have camped across from each other for seven days, the Israelite army attacks the Arameans on the seventh day and kills 100,000 men in one day. The Arameans run to the city, where a wall collapses and kills 27,000 more soldiers. Ben-Hadad also runs and hides in an inner room in the city. Ben-Hadad's officials advise him to surrender to the king of Israel so that Israel's king will spare Ben-Hadad's life. Ben-hadad and his officials dress in sackcloths and go to meet Ahab.

In the fifth scene: The officials plead to Ahab to spare Ben-Hadad's life. Ahab is surprised that Ben-Hadad is still alive and calls him his brother. Ahab invites Ben-Hadad into his chariot, and the two kings make a treaty. Ben-Hadad agrees to return captured cities and let Ahab open markets in Damascus if Ahab spares his life. Ben-Hadad goes free.

The characters in this passage are:

  • King Ben-Hadad's government officials
  • King Ben-Hadad
  • King Ahab
  • Man of God
  • Yahweh
  • Israelite army
  • And the Aramean army

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

It is important to remember that a prophet has already warned King Ahab that King Ben-Hadad will rebuild his army and attack again in the spring.

The servants of Ben-Hadad advised Ben-Hadad. Here, servants refer to the officials who served in the government of King Ben-Hadad of Aram.

The officials tell Ben-Hadad that Israel only won the battle because their gods are gods of the hills. The Arameans think that the Israelites worship many gods, when the Israelites only follow one God, Yahweh.

The officials advise Ben-Hadad to fight the Israelites on the plains, or in the valley between the mountains, so that Ben-Hadad's army will win next time. It is important to remember that plains or valleys refer to flat or level ground where there are no mountains.

The hills the officials are talking about are larger than small hills. Samaria, the capital of Israel, sits on a high hill, while Damascus, the Aramean capital, is on a plain. For this reason, the Aramean officials think that their own gods will have more power than the God of Israel in a battle on the plain. The word "plain" means flat land, but sometimes it is easier to say "land with no mountains." The Aramean chariots would also move more easily on flat ground.

The officials tell Ben-Hadad to replace the kings with military commanders. These are the same 32 kings who previously helped Ben-Hadad and stood around drinking with Ben-Hadad in his tent.

The officials also advise Ben-Hadad to rebuild his army just as strong as before—horse for horse and chariot for chariot. In other words, the officials want Ben-Hadad to gather a new army that has the same number of horses and chariots as the previous one so that the army will be just as strong as it was before. Ben-Hadad agrees and begins to rebuild.

After time passes, the next spring, or at the time when kings usually go to war, Ben-Hadad is once again ready to go to war with Israel. Ben-Hadad marches with the Aramean army from Damascus to the city of Aphek, still in Aram. Aphek is located between Damascus and the Valley of Jezreel.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a map that shows the route from Damascus to Aphek. Pause this audio here.

King Ahab listens to the prophet's advice and assembles and prepares his troops with provisions before going into battle. The Aramean army and the Israelite army arrive near Aphek and face each other across the plain.

The storyteller makes the Israelite army appear small and weak when he describes them as two flocks of goats, while the Aramean army appears large and powerful.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Why do you think the storyteller describes the Israelite army as two flocks of goats? How will you describe the way the two armies looked in your language? Pause this audio here.

The man of God, or the unnamed prophet from earlier, again gives King Ahab a message from Yahweh. Yahweh says, "The Arameans say that I am a god of the hills only and not the flat land. Because of this, I will cause you to conquer their large army, and in this way you and all your people will know that I am Yahweh."

It is important to note that Yahweh says Yahweh will bring victory to show Ahab and the Israelites who Yahweh is. He is Yahweh, and Yahweh is in control.

The two armies camp across from each other for seven days without attacking each other. The large plain where the armies are camped allows the soldiers in the two camps to see each other. But on the seventh day, the Israelites attack the Arameans and kill 100,000 soldiers. When the Arameans realize they are losing the battle, the Arameans run back toward the city of Aphek. But when the Arameans reach the city, a wall collapses and kills 27,000 more Aramean soldiers. We do not know why exactly the wall collapses.

At some point in the battle, King Ben-Hadad escapes to the city of Aphek and hides in an inner room. It is possible that this inner room is part of the inside of the city fortress. Ben-Hadad's officials hide with him and advise him to surrender to the king of Israel. The officials want Ben-Hadad to do this because everyone knows the Israelite kings are merciful and sometimes spare their enemies' lives.

So the officials and Ben-Hadad put on sackcloths and place ropes on their heads before going out of the city to meet the king of Israel. People wore sackcloth to show that they were sad and humble, while people put ropes on their heads to show that they were a prisoner of someone.

The officials approach King Ahab first because they are not sure how Ahab will react. The officials plead with Ahab to spare Ben-Hadad's life. King Ahab is surprised and asks if Ben-Hadad is still alive.

King Ahab then says that Ben-Hadad is Ahab's brother. Ahab does not mean that Ben-Hadad is his biological brother. Instead, because they had gone through so much as enemies, Ahab honors Ben-Hadad and feels that he is like a brother to Ahab.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Remember your story from the previous script about someone who was an enemy who became like a friend or like family. How would you describe this kind of reconciliation in your language? Pause this audio here.
