The officials take this as a good sign and are quick to pick up on Ahab's words. This means the officials are eager to believe that Ahab will treat Ben-Hadad kindly. The officials quickly agree with the king and repeat that Ben-Hadad is like a brother to Ahab.

King Ahab tells the officials to bring Ben-Hadad to Ahab. Ben-Hadad comes out of hiding and climbs into Ahab's chariot. The two kings make a covenant, or binding promise. Ben-Hadad agrees to return the captured cities to Ahab and to allow him to open markets in Damascus if Ahab lets him go free. It is important to note that Ben-Hadad had already established his own markets in the Israelite city of Samaria.

King Ahab allows Ben-Hadad to go home as part of their agreement.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 20:23–34 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has five scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • King Ben-Hadad's government officials
  • King Ben-Hadad
  • King Ahab
  • Man of God
  • Yahweh
  • Israelite army
  • And the Aramean army

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Act out how King Ben-Hadad's officials give him advice in Aram. The officials tell Ben-Hadad that the Israelites only won because their gods are gods of the hills. The officials say Ben-Hadad should replace the kings with officers, rebuild his army, and fight the Israelites again on flat ground. Then the Arameans will be able to defeat the Israelites. Ben-Hadad listens to the officials' advice and begins to prepare his army once again.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing Ben-Hadad, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • They are right! The Israelite gods will have no power against us if we fight the Israelites on flat ground. I will raise more chariots to destroy them! or
  • I am determined. We will be more organized this time. There is no way I will let the Israelites win again; or
  • I am hopeful our gods will help us on the plains.

Act out how Ben-Hadad leads his new Aramean army to fight the Israelites at the city of Aphek the next spring. King Ahab marches with the Israelite army and prepares them to meet the Aramean army on the plain. The two armies face each other across the plain. The Israelite army camps in two small groups while the Arameans cover the countryside.

Act out how the man of God gives Ahab a message from Yahweh. The man of God says, "Because the Arameans think I am a god of the hills and not a god of the valleys, I will deliver this vast army into your hands, and you will know that I am Yahweh."

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing Ahab, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • The prophet's advice worked last time. I will keep doing what he says; or
  • It is true what I heard. Yahweh is a jealous God who cares about his name among the nations; or
  • I will choose to trust Yahweh again for this battle.

Act out how the two armies camp across from each other for seven days. On the seventh day, the Israelite army attacks the Arameans and kills 100,000 men in one day. The Arameans run away to the city where a wall collapses and kills 27,000 more soldiers.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing the Israelite soldiers, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel excited. Our enemies cannot stand against us. Yahweh is with us! or
  • It is so clear Yahweh is fighting for us. I was nervous about our small numbers, but now our enemies are running away! or
  • I am proud to fight in Yahweh's army. I want to defend our people and keep the Arameans from coming back to Samaria.

Act out how Ben-Hadad runs away to the city and hides in an inner room. Ben-Hadad's officials advise him to surrender to the king of Israel because Israel's king is merciful. Ben-Hadad and his officials dress in sackcloth and go to meet Ahab.

The officials plead with Ahab to spare Ben-Hadad's life. Ahab is surprised that Ben-Hadad is still alive and calls Ben-Hadad his brother. Ahab invites Ben-Hadad into Ahab's chariot, and the two kings make a treaty. Ben-Hadad will return captured cities and allow Ahab to open markets in Damascus if Ahab spares Ben-Hadad's life. Ben-hadad goes free.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing Ben-Hadad's officials, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel relieved. I knew the king of Israel would not punish Ben-Hadad. The Israelite kings have too much compassion; or
  • I feel surprised. What fortune that the king saw Ben-Hadad as a brother! or
  • That could have gone much worse. At least we are still alive!

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 20:23–34 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

The Aramean servants, or officials, advise the King of Aram. Use the same word or phrase for government officials as you used in previous passages. A king is someone who rules over a city or territory, like a country. Ben-Hadad rules over the country of Syria, otherwise known as Aram. Use the same word or phrase for king as you used in previous passages. For more information on king, refer to the Master Glossary.

The officials tell Ben-Hadad that the Israelites only won because their gods are gods of the hills. Although there is only one God, Yahweh, sometimes people worship other spirit beings or idols as if they have power. The Syrians worshipped several different gods, and they assumed the Israelites did as well. Use the same word or phrase for gods as you used in previous passages. For more information on gods, refer to the Master Glossary.

The officials say that if Ben-Hadad fights the Israelites again, but on plains this time, then the Syrian army will win. A plain is an area of flat ground where there are no mountains.

Stop here and look at the photo of a plain again. Discuss with your group what word or phrase you will use for plains in your language. Pause this audio here.

The officials want Ben-Hadad to rebuild his army to be as strong as it was before, with just as many horses and chariots. As you remember, a chariot is a war cart that horses pulled. Use the same word for chariots as you used in previous passages.

When spring arrives, or at the time when kings go to war, Ben-Hadad once again goes to battle with Israel. Spring refers to a time of year between early April and early March when the weather becomes warmer after a cold time of year. The dry weather makes this a good time of year for kings to fight battles. Use the same word or phrase for spring as you used in previous passages.

When the officials refer to the gods of Israel, they are referring to the gods of the nation of Israel. Israel was the territory where the Israelites lived. Use the same word or phrase for Israel as you used in previous passages. For more information on Israel, refer to the Master Glossary.

The Israelite army looks like two small flocks of goats on the hillside.

Stop here and decide how you will describe the Israelite army. If you choose to describe them like flocks of goats, use the same word or phrase for goats as you have previously. Pause this audio here.

While Ahab is preparing for battle, the man of God appears to him. This man is probably the same prophet who came to Ahab earlier. Old Testament storytellers sometimes call a prophet a "man of God." In this case, the storyteller uses the title "man of God," but he is talking about the prophet. Use the same term for "man of God" as you have used previously. For more information on prophet, refer to the Master Glossary.

The man tells Ahab he has a message from Yahweh. Yahweh is the personal name of God that shows the close relationship between God and Israel. Yahweh tells Ahab that Yahweh will bring a great victory to show who Yahweh is. He is Yahweh, and Yahweh is in control. Use the same word or phrase for Yahweh as you used in previous passages. For more information on Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary.

The prophet tells Ahab that Yahweh will deliver his enemies into his hands, or into his power. In other words, Yahweh will give Ahab power and victory over the Syrians. Use the same word or phrase for "into your hands" as you used in previous passages.

The Israelites begin to defeat the Syrians, so many soldiers run away to the city of Aphek. A city is a large town where many people live. At this time, most cities had walls surrounding them to protect them from enemies. Use the same word or phrase for "city" as you used in previous passages.

When Ben-Hadad is hiding in the inner room, his officials tell Ben-Hadad to surrender, since everyone knew the kings of Israel were merciful. When someone is merciful, they show kindness, goodness, or compassion. The person keeps their promises and is loyal. In this case, King Ahab would be showing mercy, or loyal and steadfast love, if he allowed King Ben-Hadad to live. Some translations say that the kings of Israel show mercy, and some translations say that they show steadfast love.

Stop here and decide as a group what word or phrase you will use for mercy or steadfast love here. For more information on mercy or steadfast love, refer to the Master Glossary. Pause this audio here.

Ben-Hadad and his officials wear sackcloth and put ropes on their heads before going to meet Ahab. People wore sackcloth to show that they were sad and humble. Sackcloth is a very rough cloth, so it causes the person who uses it discomfort.

Stop here and, if needed, look again at the picture of someone who wears sackcloth. Discuss with your group what word or phrase you will use for sackcloth in your language. Pause this audio here.

The officials plead with Ahab to spare Ben-Hadad's life and call Ben-Hadad Ahab's servant. When the officials say that Ben-Hadad is Ahab's servant, they are saying Ben-Hadad is under the authority of Ahab and ready to serve him. Use the same word or phrase for servant as you used in previous passages. For more information on servant, refer to the Master Glossary.

In the end, Ahab makes a covenant, or treaty, with Ben-Hadad. Ben-Hadad will return captured cities and allow Ahab to open markets in Damascus as long as Ahab lets him go free. When people make a covenant, they make a solemn promise to each other that they cannot break. By making a covenant, people say that they want to have a good relationship with each other. Use the same word or phrase for covenant as you used in previous passages. For more information on covenant, refer to the Master Glossary.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step. Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage. Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene. Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 20:35–43
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 20:35–43 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 20:35–43 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 20:35–43 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the last passage, Yahweh gave King Ahab and the Israelites a great victory over Ben-Hadad's Syrian army. But when Ben-Hadad surrendered to Ahab, Ahab decided to make a treaty with Ben-Hadad and let him live. Now, Yahweh will send a prophet to tell Ahab what the consequences will be.

During this time, there were groups, or "brotherhoods," of prophets who were messengers of God. These men received messages from Yahweh and delivered the messages to the people. When the storyteller says "one of the sons of the prophets," it means the man belonged to a group of prophets. The man was not a prophet's biological son.

The prophet receives a word from Yahweh, or a message directly from Yahweh. The prophet acts on Yahweh's instructions and asks another prophet, a companion, to strike him. But the other prophet does not recognize that this is from Yahweh because it is a strange request, so he refuses to strike the first prophet.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, if one person asked another person to strike them, how might the second person react to the first person's request? Why would they react in this way? Pause this audio here.

The prophet tells the companion that by refusing to strike the prophet, the companion has disobeyed Yahweh. Then the prophet says that a lion will attack and kill the companion when he leaves. Just like the prophet said, when the companion walks away, a lion finds him and kills him. In this way, Yahweh does what Yahweh says he will do.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a picture of a Middle Eastern lion. The lion in this story was a sign from God. In your culture, how do animals carry spiritual messages or act as warnings? Pause this audio here.

The prophet tries again and politely asks another man to hit him. This time, the man agrees and strikes the prophet, which leaves the prophet with a wound. The injury makes the prophet look like he has just come from battle. The prophet disguises himself by pulling a bandage, a strip of cloth, over his eyes. Then the prophet goes and waits by the road for King Ahab to pass by.

When Ahab comes along, the prophet acts like a common soldier and calls out to the king. As you remember, the Israelite soldiers have just won a huge victory over the Aramean army. The prophet tells Ahab that he is Ahab's servant as a way to show respect and submission to the king. Then the prophet begins to tell a story that is not true. The prophet invents the story to teach the king a lesson from Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, how do storytellers or elders use stories to teach hard lessons indirectly, like this prophet? How can stories help you confront someone without being disrespectful? Pause this audio here.

The prophet tells the king that in the middle of the battle, a fellow soldier gave him a prisoner of war to guard. Most likely, this captured enemy would have become the soldier's slave. The soldier told the prophet that if the prisoner escaped for any reason, the prophet would have to give his life in place of the man's life. In other words, if the prisoner escaped, the prophet would have to take the man's place as a slave, or pay the soldier a talent of silver instead. This was about 34 kilograms, or 75 pounds of silver, which was far too high a price for a soldier to pay. The prophet says that while he was busy, the prisoner escaped. That is why the prophet is now in trouble.

King Ahab responds and says the prophet has judged himself and must now suffer the consequence he has already described. Then the prophet quickly removes the bandage from his face and reveals who he really is. King Ahab immediately recognizes the prophet.

The prophet gives the king a message from Yahweh: Ahab let a man go free who Yahweh commanded Ahab to destroy. Because of this, Yahweh says that Ahab will pay with his life, and the Israelite people will suffer because of what Ahab has done.

As you remember, ever since the Israelites began to conquer the Promised Land, Yahweh went before the Israelites as their leader and commanded them to completely destroy their enemies as an offering to Yahweh. This meant the Israelites were not to leave anyone alive. However, after Yahweh gave Ahab a great victory over Ben-Hadad and the Arameans, Ahab chose to leave Ben-Hadad alive. Now, because of his disobedience, Ahab will lose his life.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story of a time when someone in your culture ignored advice from elders, spirits, or tradition, and something bad happened as a result. Pause this audio here.

Ahab returned to his palace in Samaria, and Ahab felt bitter and angry. King Ahab heard a story about someone he thought was an irresponsible soldier and ended up pronouncing judgment on himself.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 20:35–43 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has four scenes.

In the first scene: A prophet receives a message from Yahweh. The prophet asks his fellow prophet to strike him. The man refuses. The prophet warns that because the man disobeyed Yahweh, a lion will kill him when he leaves. The man leaves, and later a lion attacks and kills the man.

In the second scene: The prophet politely asks another man to strike him. The man agrees and wounds the prophet. The prophet then covers his eyes with a bandage to disguise himself and waits by the road for the king.

In the third scene: King Ahab passes by, and the prophet calls out to the king. The prophet tells Ahab a story. The prophet says he was responsible for guarding a prisoner who escaped. The soldier who left the prisoner with the prophet said that if the prisoner escaped, the prophet must pay with his life or a talent of silver. King Ahab says that the prophet has judged himself. The prophet removes his bandage and reveals who he truly is. The prophet tells Ahab Yahweh's message, and says, "You have set free a man I had determined should die. Therefore, your life will pay for his life, and your people for his people."

In the fourth scene: King Ahab returns to his palace in Samaria. Ahab feels bitter and angry.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Yahweh
  • The prophet with a word from Yahweh
  • The fellow prophet
  • The lion
  • The man who strikes the prophet
  • And King Ahab

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

It is important to remember that Israel has just won a great battle against the Arameans. According to the rules Yahweh gave the Israelites for war, the Israelites were supposed to completely destroy their enemies as an offering to Yahweh, but Ahab was not a king who followed Yahweh. King Ahab let Ben-Hadad, the king of Aram, or Syria, live in order to have a better trade partnership with Ben-Hadad.

The word of Yahweh comes to one of the sons of the prophets. When Yahweh's word comes to someone, it means Yahweh speaks to that person and tells them what to do or say. Here, "son" does not mean biological son but shows that this man belonged to a group of prophets.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you talk about the group of prophets in your language? Pause this audio here.

The prophet asks another prophet to strike him. We assume that the prophet means to strike him with a weapon, though it is unclear how the prophet's companion was supposed to strike him. We later learn that this is a part of Yahweh's plan. But the fellow prophet refuses to hit the man.

The prophet tells the other prophet that by refusing to strike him, he has disobeyed Yahweh. As a consequence, the prophet predicts that a lion will kill the man when he leaves the prophet's presence.

When the man leaves the prophet, a lion finds the man and kills him, just as Yahweh had said through the prophet. Lions were common predators in the region. Lions hunted both people and animals. Yahweh uses the lion to bring his divine judgment, or punishment, on the man.

The prophet then finds another man. We are unsure if this man was another prophet or just another man that the prophet found. The prophet politely asks the man to strike the prophet. This man agrees and wounds the prophet. The prophet looks like a soldier injured in battle. This appearance allows the prophet to convincingly tell his story to King Ahab.

The prophet disguises himself by pulling a bandage over his eyes. Then the prophet goes to stand by the road to Samaria and waits for King Ahab to pass by. The prophet looks like a common, wounded soldier in Israel's army. King Ahab most likely travels in a chariot.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you describe the prophet's appearance in your language? Remember, the prophet had a cloth bandage over his eyes that covered his head wound. Pause this audio here.

When the king passes by, the prophet calls out loudly to get the king's attention and make him stop. It is important to understand that the prophet is not telling a true story to Ahab. The prophet uses this story to deliver Yahweh's message of judgment to Ahab.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Why do you think the prophet told an untrue story instead of directly accusing the king? Tell a local story that teaches an important lesson. Pause this audio here.

When the prophet says, "Your servant went into battle...," the prophet is referring to himself as a servant of the king to show that he submits to the king's authority.

As part of his story, the prophet says that another soldier gave him a captive or prisoner to watch. The soldier told the prophet that if the prisoner escaped, the prophet would have to pay with his life or a talent of silver. When the prophet says, "your life for his life," he means that the soldier would make the prophet the soldier's slave if the prisoner got away.

The prophet explains that while he was busy, the prisoner escaped and left the prophet in serious trouble. King Ahab responds and says the man has judged himself and must suffer the consequence he already described. Remember that the king does not know that this man is one of the prophets of Israel. This means the prophet would have to give control of his own life over to the soldier.

The prophet quickly removes his bandage, and King Ahab immediately recognizes the prophet. It is possible that Ahab knew this prophet personally, or that prophets at that time had a certain appearance, such as being bald, that made them easy to recognise.

The prophet says, "This is what Yahweh says..." to show that his message comes directly from Yahweh to Ahab. Yahweh tells Ahab that Ahab let a man live whom Yahweh had said must die. As you remember, Ahab should have killed Ben-Hadad, the defeated enemy king.

Now Yahweh says, "It will be Ahab's life for that man's life, and Ahab's people for that man's people." Because Ahab is the king of Israel, this means the Israelite people will suffer because of Ahab's disobedience.

Ahab returns to his palace in Samaria, feeling bitter and angry.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 20:35–43 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has four scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Yahweh
  • The prophet with a word from Yahweh
  • The fellow prophet
  • The lion
  • The man who strikes the prophet
  • And King Ahab

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Act out how the prophet receives a message from Yahweh. The prophet asks his fellow prophet to strike him with a weapon. The man refuses.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing the prophet's friend, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • This man is crazy. Why would Yahweh want me to hurt this man? or
  • I feel doubtful. I do not think this man has truly heard from Yahweh; or
  • I do not care if I am supposed to do this thing. I am not going to strike another person! I am a peaceful person.

Act out how the prophet says that a lion will kill the man because he did not obey Yahweh. The man leaves the prophet, and a lion attacks and kills the man.

Act out how the prophet politely asks another man to strike him. The man strikes the prophet and leaves him wounded. The prophet uses a bandage to disguise himself, then waits by the road for the king.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing the prophet, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • My head really hurts. I hope Ahab truly hears Yahweh's message; or
  • I am determined. I will give the king Yahweh's message, no matter the cost; or
  • I have a plan. I will let Ahab bring judgment on himself and then give him Yahweh's message.

Act out how King Ahab passes by as the prophet calls out loudly to get Ahab's attention. The prophet tells Ahab a story about being responsible for a prisoner who escaped. The prophet explains that the soldier who left the prophet in charge said he must pay with his life or a talent of silver if the prisoner got away. King Ahab says that the prophet has judged himself with his own words.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing Ahab, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • The fool. This man's case is obvious; or
  • I feel entertained. I do not know what this man expects me to say. This man has obviously been careless and deserves the consequences; or
  • I am annoyed. I do not know why this man stopped me for this.

Act out how the prophet removes his bandage and reveals who he truly is. The prophet says he has a message from Yahweh for Ahab. Yahweh says, "You have set free a man I had determined should die. Therefore, it is your life for his life, your people for his people."

Act out how King Ahab returns to his palace in Samaria and feels bitter and angry.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing Ahab, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am shocked. This God just helped me, and now he dares to bring judgment? or
  • I am angry. Yahweh asks too much. Kings must make alliances. I am at war! or
  • I feel guilty. I know I did not follow the rules of war Yahweh gave our people.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 20:35–43 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

The word of Yahweh came to a man. The "word of Yahweh" means everything that Yahweh tells people. Yahweh either speaks to the people directly or through people called prophets. In this case, Yahweh explained to the prophet what to do to bring a message of judgment on King Ahab. Use the same word or phrase for "word of Yahweh" as you used in previous passages. For more information on "word" and "Yahweh," refer to the Master Glossary.

The word of Yahweh comes to a son of the prophets. Here, "son" means that the man belonged to a group of prophets, not that he was someone's biological son. A prophet is someone who gives messages from Yahweh to the people. Use the same word or phrase for prophet as you used in previous passages. For more information on prophet, refer to the Master Glossary.

Stop here and discuss as a group: How will you talk about the group or sons of prophets in your language? Pause this audio here.

The prophet says a lion will kill the other prophet because he did not listen to Yahweh's voice. A lion is a large, powerful cat that hunts and eats other animals. Use the same word for lion as you used in previous passages.

Stop here and look at a picture of a lion as a group. Pause this audio here.

The wounded prophet stands by the road and waits for King Ahab. A king is someone who rules over a city or territory, like a country. King Ahab ruled over the nation of Israel. Use the same word or phrase for king as you used in previous passages. For more information on king, refer to the Master Glossary.

The prophet calls out to Ahab and says he is Ahab's servant. When the prophet calls himself a servant, the prophet is showing submission to the king and admitting that he is under Ahab's power. Use the same word or phrase for servant as you used in previous passages. For more information on servant, refer to the Master Glossary.

In the prophet's story, the soldier tells the prophet that if the prisoner escapes, then the prophet must become a slave in the prisoner's place or pay a talent of silver. A talent is about 34 kilograms of silver. Use the same word or phrase for talent as you used in previous passages. For more information about talent, see the Master Glossary.

Ahab was the king of Israel. Here, Israel refers to the territory, or land, where the Israelites lived. Use the same word or phrase for Israel as you used in previous passages. For more information on Israel, refer to the Master Glossary.

Ahab returns to his palace angry and sullen. A palace refers to the house of a king. Be sure to use the same word for palace as you used in previous passages.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 21:1–16
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 21:1–16 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 21:1–16 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 21:1–16 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the last passage, Yahweh sent a prophet disguised as a soldier to deliver a message to King Ahab. Yahweh told Ahab that because Ahab let the Syrian king, Ben-Hadad, live, Yahweh would take Ahab's life instead, and the Israelites would suffer because of it. King Ahab returned to his palace and felt bitter and angry with Yahweh.

Some time passed after the prophet gave Yahweh's message to King Ahab. Now, the king finds himself in another difficult situation. As you remember, King Ahab had a palace in Samaria, the capital city of Israel. But Ahab also had a second palace in the city of Jezreel. Jezreel was located north of Samaria and southwest of Lake Galilee, in the territory of Issachar.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a map of Samaria and Jezreel. Pause this audio here.

Naboth lived in Jezreel and owned a vineyard close to the palace of King Ahab. A vineyard is a type of garden where people grow grapes. Grapes are a soft, juicy fruit that grow on a tree called a grapevine.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a picture of a vineyard with grapes. Pause this audio here.

One day, King Ahab approaches Naboth and asks to buy Naboth's vineyard. Ahab says he wants to use the vineyard to plant different vegetables, or leafy plants. Ahab offers to give Naboth a better vineyard in exchange, or, if it seems good to Naboth, Ahab will pay Naboth the vineyard's value in silver. People used silver as money during that time.

Naboth strongly refuses the king's offer. Naboth says, "Yahweh forbid that I should give you the inheritance of my fathers." When Naboth says "Yahweh forbid," Naboth uses special language that puts a curse on himself from Yahweh if Naboth does something he is not supposed to do. This curse is a way to show Naboth's strong refusal and disgust towards the idea. For Naboth, the vineyard is Naboth's inheritance. According to the law of Moses, land was to stay with the descendants of the original owner. People could not sell land from one tribe to another. Each generation of Naboth's family had passed the vineyard down to the next. For Naboth, it is a matter of honor to keep what Naboth's ancestors have given him.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Why do you think Naboth felt so strongly about keeping the land? In your culture, what is something that has deep meaning or family history that you would never give away or exchange for any price? How would you feel if someone insisted on having that thing? Pause this audio here.

After Naboth rejects Ahab, Ahab returns to his palace sullen and angry. Ahab is so upset by Naboth's refusal that Ahab lies in bed, refuses to eat, and turns his face away so that he hides from everyone. Though Ahab is a king, Ahab begins to behave like a child who sulks. When Ahab's wife Jezebel finds out, Jezebel confronts Ahab and asks him why he is so upset that he will not eat.

Ahab tells Jezebel the story of Naboth and how Naboth refused to sell his vineyard. Jezebel asks Ahab, "Are you not the king of Israel?" not because she doubts Ahab's role, but to rebuke Ahab for not acting like a king. The queen commands Ahab to get up, eat, and let his heart be cheerful. In other words, Jezebel is telling Ahab that she will take care of his problem. When Jezebel says, "I will get you the vineyard," Jezebel emphasizes that she herself will make sure the vineyard becomes her husband's.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What kinds of roles do husbands and wives play when people make big decisions in your culture? How do husbands and wives influence each other's decisions? What do you see as a good or a harmful influence? Pause this audio here.

Jezebel then writes letters in her husband's name, which means she writes the letters and signs Ahab's name to give the letters the authority of the king. Jezebel also used Ahab's official seal. In those times, kings wore special rings that people had engraved with the king's name or their personal symbol in reverse. When someone pressed the ring into wax, it created a seal on the document and showed the king's official authorization. The person who received the letter could not open and read the letter or document unless they broke the wax seal. This confirmed that the document was authentic or that the document was really from the king. Such a ring was a sign of power and authority. Jezebel uses Ahab's seal and ring to make the letters official.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a picture of a wax seal and a royal signet ring. Pause this audio here.

Jezebel sends these official letters to the elders and nobles of Naboth's town, Jezreel. Elders were important family leaders, while nobles were wealthy landowners with certain rights that not all people had. Together, these two groups represented the city's leadership. In Jezebel's letter, she commands the city leaders to proclaim a fast and to place Naboth in a position of honor. When people fast, they intentionally deny themselves food for the specific purpose of prayer. In the case of a public fast, the community would come together when someone committed a crime that brought guilt on the whole city. The community fasted together to humble themselves before Yahweh. In this case, Jezebel's plan is to use the day of fasting as a public event so that everyone can see Naboth when the leaders accuse Naboth of blasphemy. This way, the people will believe Naboth is guilty and has sinned against Yahweh.

Jezebel orders the leaders to place two dishonest men on either side of Naboth to accuse him of two things Naboth did not do: blaspheming Yahweh and cursing the king. Both blasphemy and cursing the king were crimes that people punished with death. Jezebel instructs that after the two men have falsely accused Naboth, the leaders should stone Naboth to death. It is important to remember that, although Jezebel gives these orders, the leaders receive these instructions as if they come directly from King Ahab.

The city leaders believe King Ahab is giving them official orders, so the city leaders of Jezreel follow Jezebel's instructions exactly. The leaders proclaim a day of fasting, gather the people together, place Naboth in a position of honor, and seat two dishonest men beside Naboth. During the gathering, the two men falsely accuse Naboth of cursing both Yahweh and King Ahab. The people then take Naboth outside the city and stone Naboth until he dies. Afterwards, the city leaders send a message to Jezebel to report that Naboth is dead.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, what are some actions that people usually consider to be good or holy? How have you seen people misuse these good things to cover up wrong intentions? Pause this audio here.

When Jezebel hears that Naboth is dead, she tells Ahab to go and take possession of Naboth's vineyard, since Naboth is no longer alive. So Ahab listens to Jezebel and goes and takes the vineyard for himself.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 21:1–16 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has five scenes.

In the first scene: King Ahab approaches Naboth and asks to buy Naboth's vineyard so Ahab can plant vegetables. Ahab offers Naboth a better vineyard in exchange, or if Naboth prefers, Ahab will pay Naboth the vineyard's value in silver. Naboth strongly refuses the king and says it would insult Yahweh if Naboth sold his inheritance. King Ahab returns to his palace angry.

In the second scene: In his palace, King Ahab lies in bed and refuses to eat. Ahab will not talk to anyone. When Jezebel hears this, she goes to Ahab's room and asks why he is so upset. Ahab tells Jezebel that Naboth has refused to sell his vineyard. Jezebel asks if this is how Ahab acts as the king of Israel. Jezebel tells Ahab to get up and eat because Jezebel will show Ahab how to get Naboth's vineyard.

In the third scene: Jezebel writes letters in Ahab's name to the city leaders of Jezreel. In the letters, she commands the leaders to proclaim a day of fasting and to put Naboth in a place of honor. Jezebel instructs the leaders to place two dishonest men beside Naboth to accuse him of cursing Yahweh and the king. Jezebel orders them to take Naboth outside the city and stone him to death.

In the fourth scene: The city leaders of Jezreel receive the orders and follow all of the commands. The leaders proclaim a day of fasting, place Naboth in a seat of honor, and instruct two dishonest men to lie about Naboth. The two men accuse Naboth of cursing Yahweh and the king. The people take Naboth outside the city and stone him to death. The city leaders send word of Naboth's death to the king.

In the fifth scene: When Jezebel hears that Naboth is dead, she tells Ahab to go and take possession of the vineyard. Ahab immediately goes and seizes the vineyard.

The characters in this passage are:

  • King Ahab
  • Naboth
  • Jezebel
  • Elders and nobles of Jezreel
  • People of Jezreel
  • And the two dishonest men

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

It is important to remember that the prophet has already told Ahab that Yahweh is displeased with Ahab for letting Ben-Hadad live, and that eventually, Ahab will die for what he has done.

King Ahab's palace in Jezreel was close to the vineyard of Naboth. It is important to note that Ahab had two palaces, one palace in Samaria and one palace in Jezreel.

The storyteller calls Ahab the King of Samaria. Here, Samaria refers to the entire northern region of Israel and not just the capital city.

Ahab offers to buy Naboth's vineyard because Ahab wants to use it to grow vegetables. This means Ahab would remove the grapevines and plant something else instead. Ahab offers to give Naboth a better vineyard in exchange, or to pay Naboth in silver, if that seems good to Naboth. To say "if it seems good to you" is another way to say "if you prefer."

Naboth replies, "Yahweh forbid that I should give you the inheritance of my fathers," as a way to strongly refuse the king's offer. Naboth finds the offer so offensive that he calls down a curse on himself from Yahweh if he were to do such a thing. For Naboth, the vineyard is his inheritance, or passed down from his family, and it is a matter of honor and pride to keep it in his family.

King Ahab returns to his palace angry, sulks in his room, and refuses to eat bread. In the original language, "bread" refers to all food, so Ahab is refusing to eat anything at all.

When Jezebel hears that her husband will not eat, Jezebel comes to check on Ahab. Jezebel asks why Ahab is so upset. Ahab tells Jezebel what happened with Naboth.

Jezebel asks, "Are you not the king of Israel?" This is a way that Jezebel rebukes Ahab for his weak behaviour.

Stop here and discuss how you will talk about the way Jezebel rebukes Ahab in your language. Pause this audio here.

Jezebel thinks Ahab's actions are not those of a true king. Jezebel commands Ahab to get up, eat, and let his heart be cheerful. In the original language, "heart" means the part of a person that thinks, feels, and makes decisions. Jezebel uses this special language to tell Ahab not to worry but to be cheerful.

When Jezebel says, "I will get you the vineyard," Jezebel is emphasising that she herself will get it. Jezebel wants to show Ahab, through her actions, how she expects Ahab to act as king.

Jezebel writes letters in Ahab's name, which means Jezebel signs Ahab's name on the letters as if the letters came from Ahab. Jezebel also uses Ahab's official ring and seal so that the letters appear to be official messages from the king.

The message inside the letters looks like it comes from Ahab. In the letters, Jezebel commands the elders and nobles of Jezreel to proclaim a day of fasting, to place Naboth in a seat of honor, and to put two scoundrels beside Naboth who will falsely accuse Naboth of cursing Yahweh and the king. "Scoundrel" is another word for someone who is bad, evil, or not trustworthy.

Stop here and discuss with your group: Tell a story of a person whom the community did not trust because that person had a reputation for causing trouble. Pay attention to what words you use to describe this type of person. Pause this audio here.

Jezebel orders the leaders to stone Naboth to death. Jezebel sends the letters from Samaria to the city leaders of Jezreel.

The city leaders receive the letters and obey the instructions inside. The leaders proclaim a day of fasting and place Naboth in a seat of honor. The leaders secretly tell two dishonest men what to accuse Naboth of before the leaders seat the men beside Naboth. The two men sit beside Naboth and then falsely accuse Naboth of blaspheming, or cursing, Yahweh and the king in front of all the people. A person cursed God when they insulted God, dishonored God, or made God seem less powerful or important.

Stop here and discuss with your group: Tell a story of someone who said something really bad or disrespectful about their leaders. What happened to that person? Pause this audio here.

The two men, the leaders, and the people forcibly take Naboth outside the city and stone him to death. It is important to remember that the people were following the law of Moses. The law of Moses said that people should stone someone to death if that person blasphemed against Yahweh or said bad things about Yahweh.

The city leaders send a letter to Jezebel and let her know Naboth is dead. When Jezebel receives the news, she tells Ahab to get up and take possession of Naboth's vineyard because Naboth is dead. Jezebel does not want Ahab to physically stand up, but rather, Jezebel is telling Ahab to do something.

Ahab gets up and takes possession of Naboth's vineyard.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 21:1–16 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has five scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • King Ahab
  • Naboth
  • Jezebel
  • Elders and nobles of Jezreel
  • People of Jezreel
  • And the two dishonest men

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Act out how King Ahab approaches Naboth and asks to buy Naboth's vineyard so that Ahab can plant vegetables. Ahab says he will give Naboth a better vineyard, or if Naboth likes, Ahab will pay Naboth the value of the vineyard in silver. Naboth strongly refuses the king and says that to sell his inheritance would insult Yahweh. King Ahab returns to his palace angry and upset.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing Ahab, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am outraged. How dare Naboth refuse me, the king? I offered Naboth a very fair price; or
  • I feel confused. Why is Naboth so emotional about a vineyard? Money can buy Naboth a new vineyard; or
  • I am angry. I will find a way to get back at Naboth. I just do not know how yet.

Act out how King Ahab lies in bed in his palace and refuses to eat. Ahab will not talk to anyone. When Jezebel hears that Ahab refuses to eat, Jezebel goes to Ahab's room and asks why Ahab is so upset. Ahab tells Jezebel the story of Naboth and how Naboth refused to sell his vineyard. Jezebel asks if this is how Ahab behaves as the king of Israel. Jezebel tells Ahab to get up and eat because she will show Ahab how to get Naboth's vineyard.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing Jezebel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel disgusted. My husband is weak. I will show Ahab how a proper king should act; or
  • I am proud. I know how to get what I want. I will show my husband how to do the same; or
  • I feel frustrated. I do not understand why Ahab wants to act like a child instead of a king.

Act out how Jezebel writes letters using Ahab's name and seal. In the letters, Jezebel commands the city leaders of Jezreel to proclaim a day of fasting and to put Naboth in a place of honor. Jezebel instructs the leaders to seat two dishonest men beside Naboth to accuse Naboth of cursing Yahweh and the king. Jezebel orders the leaders to take Naboth outside the city and stone him to death.

Act out how the city leaders of Jezreel receive the king's instructions.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing the city leaders, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We feel confused. Why would Ahab want such a thing? Naboth is a good man; or
  • We are afraid. What choice do we have? That is the king's royal seal. We will die if we do not obey; or
  • We feel conflicted. We know it is wrong, but we do not want to bring punishment on ourselves or our families.

Act out how the city leaders follow the letter's instructions. The leaders proclaim a day of fasting, place Naboth in a seat of honor, and tell two dishonest men what to say about Naboth. The two men accuse Naboth of cursing Yahweh and the king. Then, the people take Naboth outside the city and stone him to death. The city leaders send a message to the king to report Naboth's death.

Act out how Jezebel hears that Naboth is dead. Jezebel tells Ahab the news and tells him to go take possession of the vineyard. Ahab immediately goes and takes over the vineyard.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing Ahab, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am satisfied. Jezebel may be bossy, but at least I got what I wanted; or
  • I am happy to let my wife kill Naboth for me. Maybe Yahweh will hold Jezebel responsible for Naboth's death; or
  • I feel clever. No one knows what we have done.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 21:1–16 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

Naboth owned a vineyard near the palace of Ahab. A vineyard is a garden where people grow grapes. Grapes are a soft, juicy fruit that grows on a tree called a grapevine. A vineyard usually has a wall around it so that wild animals or thieves cannot come into the garden. People made this wall out of rocks or thorny bushes.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for vineyard. Look up vineyard in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

King Ahab approaches Naboth and tells Naboth he wants to buy Naboth's vineyard to grow vegetables. A king is someone who rules over a city or territory, like a country. King Ahab ruled over the nation of Israel. Use the same word or phrase for king as you used in previous passages. For more information on king, refer to the Master Glossary.

King Ahab goes back to his palace sullen and angry. "Palace" refers to Ahab's large house. Use the same word for palace as you used in previous passages.

Naboth strongly refuses the king and says Yahweh forbade him to give away the inheritance of his fathers. "Yahweh forbid" is special language that means Naboth is pronouncing a curse on himself from Yahweh if Naboth does what he is not supposed to do. This curse is a way to show disgust for something the person wants nothing to do with. Yahweh is the personal name of God that shows the close relationship between God and Israel. Use the same word or phrase for Yahweh as you used in previous passages. For more information on Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary.

Naboth refuses to give away the vineyard because it was the inheritance of his fathers. Naboth's ancestors had passed the vineyard down from generation to generation until it was given to Naboth. The vineyard belonged to Naboth through family possession. Use the same word or phrase for inheritance as you have used in previous passages.

After Ahab sulks in his palace, Ahab's wife asks him why he is so upset. After Ahab explains, Jezebel asks if Ahab is not the king over Israel. Here, Israel refers to the territory, or land, where the Israelites lived in the northern kingdom. Use the same word or phrase for Israel as you used in previous passages. For more information on Israel, refer to the Master Glossary.

Jezebel uses the king's seal to send letters to the city elders and nobles in Jezreel. An elder refers to a family leader who helped make important community decisions. Nobles were wealthy landowners with certain rights that not all people had. Use the same word or phrase for elders as you used in previous passages. For more information on elders, refer to the Master Glossary.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for nobles. Pause this audio here.

Jezebel orders the city leaders to declare a day of fasting. When people fast, they do not eat or drink anything for a certain time. People fast for many reasons—because they feel sad, want to repent, or want to show humility to God. Most likely, this day of fasting was to be a special event where the people asked God for help or guidance. Fasting was a way to show God how serious the problem was. In this story, the problem was the false sin they claimed Naboth had done.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for fasting. Look up fasting in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

The two men accuse Naboth of cursing God and cursing the king. When a person curses God, insults God, dishonors God, or makes God seem less powerful or important, this is called blasphemy. The punishment for blasphemy was death. When you curse someone, you are wishing that something bad will happen to that person. Use the same words or phrases for God and for curse as you used in previous passages. For more information on God and curse, refer to the Master Glossary.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 21:17–29
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 21:17–29 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 21:17–29 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 21:17–29 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the last story, King Ahab was very upset when Naboth refused to sell Ahab his vineyard. Jezebel heard that King Ahab was so sad that he would not eat. So, Jezebel decided to fix the problem for Ahab. Jezebel made a plan to kill Naboth. Jezebel pretended to be King Ahab and sent letters to the leaders in Naboth's city. Jezebel told the leaders to stone Naboth to death, and they obeyed. Then Jezebel told Ahab to go and take possession of the vineyard. Ahab went right away.

After this, the word of Yahweh came to Elijah the Tishbite. As you remember, Elijah was a prophet from the town of Tishbe, in the region of Gilead. When we say "the word of Yahweh came to Elijah," it means Yahweh gave Elijah a message. This message was for King Ahab, who was acting badly. Yahweh tells Elijah to go and meet Ahab.

King Ahab was the ruler of Israel. Ahab ruled from the capital city called Samaria. But at this time, Ahab was in Naboth's vineyard in Jezreel. As you know, Ahab had just gone there to take possession of the vineyard after Jezebel made the leaders kill Naboth.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a map of Israel that includes Tishbe, Samaria, and Jezreel. Pause this audio here.

Yahweh tells Elijah to say, "This is what Yahweh says..." to introduce Yahweh's message to Ahab when Elijah speaks to Ahab. Elijah is to ask Ahab, "Have you killed and also taken someone's possession?" Yahweh is not really asking Ahab a question to find out the truth. Instead, Elijah's words are a way to point out clearly that Ahab has killed a man and taken control of his property, the vineyard. As you remember, a vineyard is a special garden where people grow grapes on vines.

Stop here and look at a picture of a vineyard as a group. Pause this audio here.

Elijah will then give a prophecy, which means a message from God about what will happen in the future. Elijah says that dogs will lick up Ahab's blood in the same place where dogs licked Naboth's blood after Naboth died. You will remember that Naboth died outside the city of Jezreel when the city leaders stoned Naboth to death. Elijah's prophecy will happen in parts. First, Ahab will die outside the city of Samaria, and dogs will lick Ahab's blood there. Later, Ahab's son Joram will also die, and people will leave his body on Naboth's land. The storyteller emphasizes that it will be Ahab's own blood that the dogs will lick up, which shows that Ahab will have the same punishment as Naboth.

Stop here and look at a picture of a dog as a group. Pause this audio here.

After Elijah hears Yahweh's message for Ahab, Elijah goes to Naboth's vineyard and delivers the message to Ahab directly. Ahab responds and says, "So you have found me, my enemy." Ahab's response is defensive when he calls Elijah his enemy. As you may remember, this is not the first time Elijah and Ahab have spoken. The last time Elijah and Ahab met, Yahweh sent fire from heaven and defeated the prophets of Baal. At that time, Ahab called Elijah the "troublemaker of Israel." Now Ahab goes even further and calls Elijah his personal enemy, because Elijah keeps bringing Yahweh's judgment to the wicked king.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, what happens when someone speaks the truth, even if it is hard to hear? How do people treat prophets or truth-tellers? What words do people use for someone who brings bad news or correction? Pause this audio here.

Elijah says he only came because Ahab chose to sell himself to do evil in Yahweh's eyes. In other words, Ahab gave himself fully to doing the things that Yahweh hates.

Then Elijah continues to tell Ahab Yahweh's message. Because of Ahab's wickedness, Yahweh says he will kill every male in Ahab's family, whether slave or free, in Israel. Yahweh says he will make Ahab's house, or family, like the family of Jeroboam, son of Nebat, and the family of Baasha, son of Ahijah. These two men, Jeroboam and Baasha, were kings who led the people to worship idols instead of Yahweh. Because of this, Yahweh allowed Jeroboam and Baasha's enemies to defeat them and kill all their descendants so their family lines would end. Yahweh says the same thing will happen to Ahab because Ahab has made Yahweh angry by causing the people of Israel to sin. Ahab did not force Israel to sin, but Ahab led Israel in a way that encouraged them to turn away from Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your culture, when a leader does wrong, how does it affect the people? How do people hold leaders responsible in your culture, and what happens to the people they lead when something goes wrong? Pause this audio here.

Yahweh also tells Ahab that Jezebel will die, and dogs will eat Jezebel's body near the walls of Jezreel. In fact, the dogs will eat every one of Ahab's relatives who die in the city, and birds will eat the bodies of his relatives who die in the countryside. These birds were most likely vultures, which are birds that eat animals that are already dead. For the Israelites, it was very shameful for dogs and birds to eat a person's dead body. Israelites saw dogs as unclean or impure animals, and they considered it shameful not to receive a proper burial. A decent burial was an important way to honor and respect the dead. You will remember that earlier, the unnamed prophet just said that Ahab would die, but now Elijah talks about what happens when Ahab dies.

The storyteller then begins to describe how evil Ahab really was. The storyteller says that no one else was as evil as Ahab, because Ahab gave himself completely to doing what Yahweh says is wrong. Jezebel persuaded Ahab to do evil, and Ahab chose to do things that were "vile," or disgusting, in Yahweh's eyes. Ahab worshiped idols, or false gods, instead of Yahweh. Ahab acted like the Amorites, the people who lived in the land before the Israelites conquered it.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How does your community talk about people who choose to do wrong over and over again? What words do people use to describe these kinds of people? Pause this audio here.

After explaining how evil Ahab was, the storyteller continues to tell his story.

When Ahab heard Elijah's message from Yahweh, Ahab tore his clothes, put on a sackcloth, and began to fast, or intentionally chose not to eat. Sackcloth was a rough, uncomfortable kind of cloth. In this culture, to tear your clothes and wear a sackcloth was a way to show that you were very sorry or deeply upset. Ahab even slept in a sackcloth and became quiet and sad. This was very different from how Ahab usually acted, because Ahab normally thought only about himself.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a picture of a person who wears a sackcloth. Pause this audio here.

Yahweh then speaks to Elijah and asks, "Have you noticed how Ahab has humbled himself before me?" The word "humbled" means that Ahab made himself low or chose to submit to someone else. In this case, Ahab submits to Yahweh and shows that Ahab is sorry for what he has done. Because of this, Yahweh decides to show mercy to Ahab. That means Yahweh chooses not to punish Ahab right away, even though Ahab deserves it. Instead, Yahweh says he will wait and bring the punishment or judgment on Ahab's family later on, after Ahab dies.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 21:17–29 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has five scenes.

In the first scene: Yahweh tells Elijah to go and find Ahab in Naboth's vineyard. Ahab is there because he took possession of the vineyard after Jezebel had Naboth killed. Yahweh tells Elijah to say, "This is what Yahweh says: Have you not murdered a man and taken his land? In the same place where dogs licked Naboth's blood, they will lick your blood too."

In the second scene: Elijah finds Ahab in Naboth's vineyard. Ahab says, "So you have found me, my enemy!" Elijah says he has come because Ahab keeps disobeying Yahweh. Then Elijah gives Ahab Yahweh's full message. Yahweh says Ahab, Jezebel, and all of Ahab's male descendants will die. This will happen because Ahab caused the people of Israel to sin.

In the third scene: The storyteller pauses his story to explain how Ahab was more evil than anyone else. Ahab gave himself fully to doing wrong. Jezebel helped Ahab do evil. Ahab followed idols like the Amorites, the people who lived in the land before the Israelites. Yahweh had chased those people out, but Ahab acted just like them. Then the storyteller returns to his story.

In the fourth scene: Ahab hears Yahweh's message and humbles himself. Ahab wears a rough cloth when he lies down to sleep, fasts, and begins to live in a quiet and humble way.

In the fifth scene: Yahweh speaks to Elijah again and asks him if he has seen how Ahab has humbled himself. Yahweh says he will delay punishing Ahab because Ahab has humbled himself.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Elijah
  • Yahweh
  • The storyteller
  • And Ahab

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

When the storyteller says that the word of Yahweh came to Elijah, the storyteller is using special language to mean that Yahweh gave Elijah the words to say to King Ahab.

Ahab was the king of Israel, and Ahab ruled from the city of Samaria. But at this time, Ahab was in the city of Jezreel, where Naboth's vineyard was. As you remember, Jezebel had people kill Naboth. Then Jezebel told Ahab to take the vineyard for himself. So Ahab went to take possession of it. Yahweh tells Elijah to go and meet Ahab there.

Yahweh tells Elijah to tell Ahab exactly what Yahweh says. As part of Yahweh's instructions, Yahweh tells Elijah to say, "This is what Yahweh says," as a way to introduce the message from Yahweh. Elijah has to say that these are Yahweh's own words. Then Yahweh continues to tell Elijah the rest of his message for Ahab.

Yahweh tells Elijah to ask Ahab on his behalf, "Have you killed and also taken possession?"' And also to say, "In the place where dogs licked up the blood of Naboth, dogs will lick up your blood."'

Yahweh is not asking the question because Yahweh wants an answer, but rather to emphasize what Ahab has done and to make Ahab aware of his own actions. The action of killing was against Naboth, and the action of taking possession refers to how Ahab took Naboth's vineyard. It is good to notice how Yahweh's message emphasizes that Ahab will die.

It is also important to note that the storyteller does not tell us that Elijah gave Yahweh's whole message to Ahab. But it is most likely that Elijah did say everything Yahweh told him to say. The storyteller also does not tell us that Elijah traveled to the vineyard. But we know Elijah had to go to the vineyard in Jezreel to be able to talk to Ahab. Perhaps the storyteller leaves out small steps in the story because they are already clear to the listener.

When Ahab sees Elijah in the vineyard, he asks Elijah, "Have you found me, my enemy?" Ahab is not really asking a question. Ahab already knows the answer. Ahab says these words because he did not expect Elijah to find him. Ahab knows he has done something wrong. Ahab calls Elijah "my enemy" because Elijah speaks for Yahweh, and Yahweh is against what Ahab has done.

Elijah answers, "I have found you, because you have sold yourself to do what is evil in the sight of the Lord." It is important to be aware that Elijah is stating two situations. Elijah agrees that he discovered the wrong Ahab did. Elijah is also stating that Ahab has done what is evil in the sight of Yahweh, or in other words, Ahab has disobeyed Yahweh.

Elijah answers, "I have found you because you have sold yourself to do evil in the sight of the Lord." Elijah is saying two things here. First, Elijah says that he has found Ahab doing wrong.

Second, Elijah says Ahab has chosen to do what is evil in God's sight. When Elijah says Ahab "sold yourself," he means that everything Ahab chooses to do is evil. Ahab seems to be committed to doing wrong things all the time.

When Elijah gives Yahweh's message, Elijah speaks as if Elijah is Yahweh. It is important to note that the storyteller is not suggesting that Elijah is Yahweh. The storyteller uses this way of speaking to bring attention to the words of Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss with your group: In your culture, how does someone deliver a message to emphasize who the sender of the message is? How would you give that message so that the receiver of the message would know who the message came from? Pause this audio here.

Yahweh tells Ahab through Elijah, "I will bring disaster upon you. I will utterly burn you up and will cut off from Ahab every male, whether they are a slave or a free man, in Israel." It is important to understand that Yahweh is talking about this same Ahab, even though the storyteller sometimes seems to be talking about someone else.

Yahweh says that Yahweh will make Ahab's house like the houses of Jeroboam and Baasha. Yahweh is not talking about the physical houses of Ahab, Jeroboam, and Baasha, but about their families or descendants.

It is important to remember that Jeroboam was an Israelite king who continued to do things that made Yahweh angry. Because of Jeroboam's sin, Yahweh allowed Baasha to kill Jeroboam and become king. Then Baasha killed all of Jeroboam's family, just as Yahweh said would happen. But Baasha followed the same evil path, leading Israel into more sin and causing Yahweh to be angry again. So Yahweh said that he would also destroy Baasha's family and descendants. When Yahweh compares Ahab to the two kings, Yahweh is warning Ahab that all of Ahab's descendants will die because of Ahab's sin.

Yahweh tells Ahab that Ahab has made Yahweh angry and made Israel to sin, or caused Israel to sin. It is important to notice that Ahab did not force the people of Israel to sin. Instead, Ahab led Israel in a way that made the people choose to sin on their own.

Yahweh also says Jezebel will die near the walls of Jezreel. As you remember, this was the city where the leaders followed the orders of Jezebel and stoned Naboth to death.

When Yahweh says, "Anyone belonging to Ahab who dies in the city the dogs will eat," Yahweh is talking about Ahab's male descendants.

After Elijah finishes giving Yahweh's message to Ahab, the storyteller stops the story for a moment and speaks directly to the listeners. The storyteller says that no king was as committed to doing evil, or disobeying Yahweh, as Ahab. This was because Ahab's wife, Jezebel, caused Ahab to worship idols, just like the Amorites did. The Amorites were people who lived in the land before Yahweh let the Israelites take it over. After describing how evil Ahab is, the storyteller returns to the story about Elijah and Ahab.

Ahab reacts to Yahweh's message by tearing his clothes, wearing sackcloth, and fasting. In Ahab's culture, all these actions showed that a person felt sorrow, humility, and regret.

Later, Yahweh speaks to Elijah again and asks if Elijah has noticed how Ahab has humbled himself. Yahweh is not really asking for information. Instead, Yahweh wants to emphasize the change in Ahab's behavior. Yahweh chooses to delay his punishment and not bring disaster during Ahab's lifetime, but to bring disaster on Ahab's son.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 21:17–29 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has five scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • Elijah
  • Yahweh
  • The storyteller
  • And Ahab

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

Act out how Yahweh gives a message to Elijah for King Ahab. Yahweh wants Elijah to go and find Ahab in Naboth's vineyard. Yahweh wants Elijah to confront Ahab about killing Naboth for his vineyard. Elijah needs to tell Ahab that Ahab will die and dogs will lick up his blood as punishment for what Ahab has done. Elijah obeys Yahweh and goes to find Ahab in Naboth's vineyard.

Act out how Elijah arrives at Naboth's vineyard and finds Ahab there taking possession of it. Ahab says, "So you have found me, my enemy!" Elijah tells Ahab he has come because Ahab continues to disobey Yahweh. Then Elijah speaks on Yahweh's behalf and gives Ahab Yahweh's whole message. Yahweh says Ahab, Jezebel, and all of Ahab's male descendants will die because Ahab caused Israel to sin.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing Ahab, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am shocked. I never imagined my actions could affect not just myself but all of my descendants! What have I done? or
  • I feel distressed. What am I going to do? How can I make this right again? or
  • I feel regret. I can not believe I have been caught again. Why did I listen to Jezebel?

Now, act out how the storyteller pauses his story to tell everyone how evil Ahab truly was. The storyteller says Jezebel caused Ahab to follow idols just as the Amorites used to before the Israelites drove them out of the land. Then the storyteller keeps telling his story about Elijah and Ahab.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing the storyteller, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Ahab was truly the worst out of all the Israelite kings; or
  • How could Ahab have let his wife rule in his place, lead Ahab to worship idols, and cause Ahab to allow murder and theft? or
  • Yahweh gave Ahab so many chances to change his evil ways, but unfortunately, Ahab never really followed Yahweh.

Act out how Ahab hears Yahweh's message and humbles himself. Ahab puts on a rough cloth and wears it even when he lies down to sleep. Ahab fasts and begins to live in a quiet and humble way.

Act out how Yahweh speaks to Elijah again and asks him if he has seen how Ahab humbled himself. Yahweh says he will delay his punishment because Ahab has humbled himself before Yahweh.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I see that Ahab regrets what he has done. I will show mercy and delay Ahab's punishment; or
  • I love Ahab as my creation, but I will not force him to follow me; or
  • I am all-knowing. I know the plans I have for all of my people.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 21:17–29 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

The word of Yahweh comes to Elijah. The word of God means everything that Yahweh tells people. Yahweh either speaks directly to the people or through people called prophets. In this case, Yahweh gave a message of judgment to Elijah the prophet for King Ahab. Use the same word or phrase for "word of Yahweh" as you used in previous passages. For more information on word, refer to the Master Glossary.

Yahweh tells Elijah to go and meet the king of Israel. A king is someone who rules over a city or territory, like a country. King Ahab ruled over the nation of Israel. Here, Israel refers to the territory, or land, where the Israelites lived in the northern kingdom. Use the same words or phrases for king and Israel as you used in previous passages. For more information on king and Israel, refer to the Master Glossary.

King Ahab ruled all of Israel from Samaria. In the Old Testament, Samaria was the capital city of the northern kingdom of Israel. One of Ahab's palaces was located in Samaria.
