Use the same word or phrase for Samaria as you used in previous passages. For more information on Samaria, refer to the Master Glossary.

Elijah found Ahab in Naboth's vineyard, where Ahab was claiming it as his own. A vineyard is a garden where people grow grapes. Grapes are a soft, juicy fruit that grows on a tree called a grapevine. Use the same word or phrase for vineyard as you used in previous passages. For more information on vineyard, refer to the Master Glossary.

Elijah begins his message by saying, "Yahweh says...." Yahweh is the personal name of God that shows the close relationship between God and Israel. Use the same word or phrase for Yahweh as you used in previous passages. For more information on Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary.

Ahab greets Elijah as his enemy, and Elijah says he only came because Ahab was doing evil in the eyes of Yahweh. When someone does evil, they willingly do bad things that hurt other people. When people stop obeying God, those people begin to do evil things. Use the same word or phrase for evil as you used in previous passages. For more information on evil, refer to the Master Glossary. Use the same words or phrase for doing evil "in the eyes of Yahweh" as you have used before.

Yahweh says he will bring disaster, or evil, on Ahab's descendants and that they will all die, whether they are free men or slaves. If a servant does not get paid for his work and does not have the freedom to leave his master when he wants, then the servant is called a slave. A slave is the property of a master. The master can sell the slave to someone else, just as he can sell his other property. Use the same word or phrase for slave as you used in previous passages. For more information on slave, refer to the Master Glossary.

Yahweh says he will make Ahab's house like the houses of Jeroboam and Baasha. Both of these kings disobeyed God, and all of their descendants were killed. Here, "house" refers to the household. Yahweh is talking about the family of Ahab, all of the people who lived in Ahab's house. Use the same word or phrase for house as you used in previous passages. For more information on household, refer to the Master Glossary.

Ahab has provoked, or caused, Yahweh to be angry. Use the same word or phrase for provoke, or cause, as you have used in previous passages.

Yahweh blames Ahab for causing Israel to sin, or disobey Yahweh. Sin is any act of rebellion against God. Anytime that a person disobeys God's laws, they sin. Use the same word or phrase for sin as you used in previous passages. For more information on sin, refer to the Master Glossary.

Yahweh says the dogs will eat the bodies of Ahab's descendants in the streets, and the birds of heaven will eat the bodies of his descendants in the fields. Here, heaven refers to the sky. Use the same word or phrase for heaven, meaning sky, as you used in previous passages. For more information on heaven, refer to the Master Glossary.

The storyteller tells how evil Ahab was for following idols like the Amorites did. Idols are objects that people make in order to worship a god. The object reminds the people of some characteristics of this god. People make an object, an image of a god, and worship that object as if it were that god. People could make such an object out of stone, wood, or metal. Use the same word or phrase for idol as you used in previous passages. For more information on idol, refer to the Master Glossary.

Yahweh gave the sons of Israel, or the Israelites, victory over the Amorites when the Israelites conquered the land. The sons of Israel is a way to say the "descendants of Israel," and it is the usual way to talk about the Israelite people. Use the same word or phrase for Israelites as you have used in previous passages. "Israelites" is in the Master Glossary.

When Ahab hears Yahweh's message, he puts on a sackcloth and fasts before Yahweh. When someone fasts, they intentionally do not eat or drink anything for a certain amount of time. People fasted for different reasons—because they were sad, wanted to repent, or show humility to God. Use the same words or phrases for sackcloth and fasting as you used in previous passages. For more information on fasting, refer to the Master Glossary.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 22:1–12
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 22:1–12 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 22:1–12 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 22:1–12 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous two stories, the storyteller told us about times when Yahweh's prophets told the king of Israel that the king was doing the wrong thing. In the first story, the storyteller does not name the prophet and the king of Israel. In the second story, the storyteller tells a story about Elijah the prophet and Ahab, the king of Israel. In this third story, it is possible that somebody took two stories from different times and mixed them together. It is possible that one storyteller took the story of Micaiah the prophet and put it with a story of Ahab, the king of Israel. You will notice that this story does not always name the king of Israel. Most people think that the king of Israel in this story is Ahab, but it is possible that parts of the story relate to a different king of Israel. In any case, it is clear that the storyteller wants to tell a story about Ahab.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What stories do you know that have changed over time? Why might people change stories or mix stories together? Pause this audio here.

We can also find this story in 2 Chronicles 18:1–11, where a different storyteller tells the story. There are a few differences between this story in 1 Kings and the other telling of the story in 2 Chronicles. The storyteller of 2 Chronicles names the king of Israel as Ahab.

Stop here and listen to your translation of 2 Chronicles 18:1–11 if possible. Pause this audio here.

This story probably happens less than three years after Ben-Hadad, the king of Aram, and Ahab, the king of Israel, made an agreement to stop fighting. You will remember that Syria is the modern-day name for Aram, and the Aramean people are the same people as the Syrian people. At the time of the storyteller, people counted part of a year as a full year. The people of Aram and the people of Israel do not fight for two years and a part of a third year. In the third year, Jehoshaphat, the king of Judah, goes from Jerusalem in Judah to the king of Israel in Samaria in Israel. Before Jehoshaphat visits the king of Israel, the king of Israel speaks to his officials. The king of Israel asks his officials a question that he does not expect them to answer about Ramoth, a city in Gilead. In the agreement between Ben-Hadad and Ahab, Ben-Hadad had agreed to give Ramoth back to Ahab. But Ben-Hadad did not give it back, and the king of Israel wants it. The king and the people of Israel are not doing anything to take the city back and make it part of Israel again.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a map of the kingdoms of Israel, Judah, and Aram and find the cities of Ramoth in Gilead and Samaria. Pause this audio here.

Jehoshaphat, king of Judah, visits the king of Israel in Samaria. We do not know why Jehoshaphat goes to Samaria. When Jehoshaphat is with the king of Israel, the king of Israel asks Jehoshaphat to fight with Israel against the people from Aram in Ramoth Gilead. The king of Israel wants the two armies to fight together against Ramoth Gilead. Jehoshaphat answers to say that everything that the king of Judah is and everything that belongs to Judah are the same or joined with everything that belongs to the king of Israel. The people of both kingdoms are like people of one kingdom, and the horses that Judah uses for fighting are like the horses in Israel. You may remember that a horse is a large animal. People ride on horses and use horses to pull war chariots.

Stop here and look at a picture of a horse as a group. Pause this audio here.

The two kingdoms have a close relationship; Jehoshaphat's son is married to Ahab's daughter.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: When a son from one family marries a daughter from another family, how does the relationship between the two families change? Talk about times when one family helped another family because the families were related by marriage. Pause this audio here.

Jehoshaphat says that he will help the king of Israel, but Jehoshaphat wants to follow Yahweh and know what Yahweh thinks about the plan to take back Ramoth Gilead.

The king of Israel calls together a large group of people who work as prophets. The prophets were people who said they spoke messages from God. It is possible that the prophets all served the king of Israel. The king of Israel asks the prophets if the king of Israel should fight or not fight Ramoth Gilead. All the prophets tell the king of Israel to go and fight because God will give the city to the king of Israel. The king of Israel will have control and power over Ramoth Gilead. The prophets do not use the personal name for God, Yahweh. The prophets use the name of Adonai in the original language. Adonai means "God who is the master and creator." Jehoshaphat does not trust the answers from the prophets. This might be because the prophets use a different and less personal name for God. It is also possible that Jehoshaphat might not trust the prophets because they all give the same answer.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about a time when someone said they spoke from God, but when you heard what the person said, you did not believe that person. Pause this audio here.

Jehoshaphat asks the king of Israel if there is a prophet of Yahweh to ask about the battle plans. Jehoshaphat does not know if any prophets of Yahweh still live in the kingdom of Israel. The king of Israel tells Jehoshaphat that there is one prophet whom the kings can use to talk to Yahweh. The king of Israel tells Jehoshaphat that the king of Israel does not like the one prophet of Yahweh. The one prophet of Yahweh prophesies or speaks from God about bad things for the king of Israel. The prophet is called Micaiah, who is the son of Imlah. Jehoshaphat tells the king of Israel that the king of Israel should not say that he hates a prophet of Yahweh. The king of Israel asks one of his officials to bring Micaiah to the kings.

The story moves to a different place. The two kings wear special clothes or robes that show they are kings. Special servants made the robes from fine material and decorated the robes with special signs and patterns.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What special clothes or decorations does your king or most important leader wear to show that they are the king? Pause this audio here.

The kings are sitting on their thrones, their special seats. The kings are at the threshing floor near the gate of the city of Samaria. The threshing floor is an open area with a flat, level, stone surface. People went to the threshing floor to beat or break the outer coating off the grains of wheat or other grains.

Stop here and look at a picture of people using a threshing floor to separate wheat grains from other parts of the plant. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What open spaces do your leaders use for special times when they are with your people? Are these spaces inside or outside of buildings, or inside or outside of the cities or towns? Pause this audio here.

The threshing floor is probably outside the city gate so that the wind can blow away the grain's light outer coating. Big walls surrounded the city of Samaria to protect the city. People used big gates in the city walls to go in and out of the city.

Stop here and look at a picture of a city gate in the wall of a city. Pause this audio here.

There is a lot of space for people at the threshing floor. You will remember that in David's time, two important events with the ark of the covenant happened on a threshing floor. The kings probably think the threshing floor is an important place to hear from God.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What special place or places do people go to in your city or the place where you live to hear from God? Pause this audio here.

The prophets are with the kings and are giving their messages and talking, or possibly dancing and shouting. One of the prophets is called Zedekiah and is the son of Kenaanah. Kenaanah is probably the father of Zedekiah. Zedekiah made horns from iron. Iron is a hard metal, and people use iron to make tools and weapons. Zedekiah probably shaped the iron to look like the horns on a bull, an adult male cow.

Stop here and look at a picture of a bull with horns and imagine the horns made from iron. Pause this audio here.

Sometimes bulls are dangerous. If a bull attacks a person, the bull hits a person with its horns and pushes its horns into the person's body. If a bull hurts a person with its horn, the person might die. Zedekiah probably put the iron horns on a helmet. Zedekiah tells the king of Israel that Yahweh said the king of Israel would use the horns to gore or make a big, bloody hole in the Arameans. The king would destroy or completely defeat the Arameans. Zedekiah probably gives the helmet to the king as a symbol of the words. You might remember that Zedekiah's words are very similar to the blessing Moses gave to the tribes descended from Joseph in Deuteronomy 33:17. But Zedekiah does not say that the king must be faithful to Yahweh like Moses said the tribes should be.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Listen to Deuteronomy 33:17 in your own language if you have translated it into your own language. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller tells us that all the other prophets at the threshing floor say the same thing as Zedekiah. All the prophets encourage the king of Israel to attack the Arameans at Ramoth Gilead and win the battle. The prophets say Yahweh will give Ramoth Gilead into the king of Israel's hand, or into the king's power.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 22:1–12 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has five scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller tells us that there was peace between Aram and Israel. In the third year of peace, Jehoshaphat visits the king of Israel.

In the second scene: Before Jehoshaphat arrives, the king of Israel reminds his officials that the king of Aram controls Ramoth Gilead.

In the third scene: The king of Israel asks Jehoshaphat to help take control of Ramoth Gilead. Jehoshaphat agrees to help fight the Arameans but asks the king of Israel to ask Yahweh what to do first.

In the fourth scene: The king of Israel asks about 400 prophets if Israel should fight Ramoth Gilead. The prophets say God will give control of Ramoth Gilead to the king of Israel. Jehoshaphat wants to consult one of Yahweh's prophets. The king of Israel does not like the prophet of Yahweh because the prophet of Yahweh does not say good things about the king of Israel. The prophet of Yahweh is called Micaiah. Jehoshaphat tells the king of Israel not to say bad things about Micaiah. The king of Israel asks officials to bring Micaiah.

In the fifth scene: As the kings wait for Micaiah to arrive, the two kings sit outside the city of Samaria with many prophets in front of them. One of the prophets, Zedekiah, has iron horns like a bull. Zedekiah says Yahweh says the kings will defeat the Arameans with the iron horns. The prophets make a loud noise and tell the kings to attack Aram at Ramoth Gilead because the kings will win the battle.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The king of Israel
  • Jehoshaphat, king of Judah
  • Officials of Israel
  • 400 prophets
  • And Zedekiah, son of Kenaanah

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

This story does not follow on directly from the previous story. This story is the beginning of another story about a prophet who tells the king of Israel that the king of Israel is not doing what Yahweh wants the king to do. After the war between Aram and Israel, the two countries did not fight for nearly three years. The peace did not last for exactly three years. The war stopped in the first year, there was no fighting in the second year, and in the third year, Jehoshaphat went to see the king of Israel. Jehoshaphat went down from Jerusalem, which was on top of a hill, to Samaria, which was lower down. Samaria was north of Jerusalem.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Talk about times you have travelled around your country, particularly from high to low areas. How will you translate Jehoshaphat going down from Jerusalem to Samaria? Pause this audio here.

Before Jehoshaphat arrives in Samaria, the king of Israel speaks to his officials. The officials are special servants who advise the king and help the king to make important decisions about Israel. The king asks the officials if the officials know Ramoth Gilead belongs to Israel. The king knows that the officials know the city belongs to Israel. The king does not expect the officials to answer him. The king asks the question to show his frustration that the king of Aram did not give Ramoth Gilead back to the king of Israel. The storyteller uses this scene to tell us that the king is thinking about attacking Ramoth Gilead to get control of the city back from the Arameans.

Stop here and look at a map that shows Israel and the cities of Samaria and Ramoth Gilead, if needed. Pause this audio here.

When Jehoshaphat arrives in Samaria, the king of Israel talks to Jehoshaphat. The king of Israel wants to know if Jehoshaphat will help the king of Israel fight the Arameans in Ramoth Gilead. Jehoshaphat tells the king of Israel that the king of Israel can use everything that belongs to Jehoshaphat. The king of Israel can use Jehoshaphat's people and horses in the same way as the king of Israel uses his own people and horses. In the same conversation with the king of Israel, Jehoshaphat says that the king of Israel should ask Yahweh for advice and wisdom before going to fight. The king of Israel listens to Jehoshaphat and calls for prophets. Prophets are people who bring messages from gods. You will remember from earlier stories that not all prophets bring messages from Yahweh. About 400 prophets come to the kings. The prophets were probably men and women. The king of Israel asks the prophets if the king should go to war against the people in Ramoth Gilead or not go to war. The prophets reply to the king of Israel and tell the king of Israel to attack. The prophets say that God will give Ramoth Gilead into the king's hand. The prophets use the word "hand" to symbolise power. The prophets say the king of Israel will have power over Ramoth Gilead. The prophets do not call God by his personal name but use a different name that means lord, master, and God.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How will you translate the different names for God to show the different ways that Jehoshaphat and the prophets talk about Yahweh or God? Pause this audio here.

Jehoshaphat listens to the prophets. Jehoshaphat does not think that the prophets tell the kings a message from Yahweh. Jehoshaphat knows that there were prophets of Yahweh in Samaria, but does not know if there is a prophet of Yahweh in Samaria at this time. Jehoshaphat wants to ask for advice from a prophet of Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do you ask someone politely if a person is in a town or village if you do not know if they are still there or if they have gone away? Pause this audio here.

The king of Israel tells Jehoshaphat about the one prophet of Yahweh who is in Israel. The king of Israel says that he hates the prophet and sees the prophet as an enemy. The prophet's messages for the king of Israel are never good. The king of Israel names the prophet as Micaiah, son of Imlah. Jehoshaphat tells the king of Israel that the king of Israel should not speak in that way. The king of Israel should not say bad things about Micaiah because he is a prophet of Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do you tell somebody not to say bad things about another person? Pause this audio here.

The king of Israel asked one of his officials to come to him and asked the official to bring Micaiah to the kings.

The story changes to a different scene. Many people think that a different storyteller told this part of the story about a different time, probably from the time of King Ahab. Somebody put the two stories together to try to make one story. While the kings are waiting for Micaiah to arrive, the two kings are now sitting together outside the gates of Samaria at an open space where the people of Samaria can thresh or beat their grain. The kings are wearing special clothes and sitting on special seats, called thrones. We do not know how much time has passed between the first time the prophets gathered and this time at the threshing floor. All the prophets make a lot of noise and dance and chant as they prophesy. One prophet is called Zedekiah. Zedekiah has made two horns from iron. Zedekiah says that Yahweh has a message. When Zedekiah says "you," it is only to one person, the king of Israel. As a bull gores with its horns, so will the king of Israel push or gore the men of Aram until the king destroys them.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a picture of a bull with horns, if needed. Pause this audio here.

At the threshing floor, in front of the kings, all the prophets are saying similar things. All the prophets tell the king of Israel to attack Ramoth Gilead and win. Yahweh will give Ramoth Gilead into the hand or the power of the king of Israel. The prophets use polite language and do not address the king directly. The prophets do not tell Jehoshaphat to attack.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 22:1–12 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has five scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • The king of Israel
  • Jehoshaphat, king of Judah
  • Officials of Israel
  • 400 prophets
  • And Zedekiah, son of Kenaanah

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

The storyteller tells us that there was peace between Aram and Israel. In the third year of peace, Jehoshaphat visits the king of Israel.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing the king of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I want Jehoshaphat to visit me so I can talk about plans for Israel; or
  • Peace is good, but Aram did not do everything they said they would do.

Ask the person playing Jehoshaphat, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I want to have a good relationship with the king of Israel; or
  • I wonder what the king of Israel is thinking and if he has plans?

Before Jehoshaphat arrives, the king of Israel reminds his officials that the king of Aram controls Ramoth Gilead.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing the king of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am frustrated and cross with Aram; or
  • I want Ramoth Gilead back. Ramoth Gilead should be part of Israel; or
  • It is time to do something to make Ramoth Gilead part of Israel again.

Ask the person playing the officials of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We are worried because our king is angry; or
  • We know Aram did not give back Ramoth Gilead when we made peace with the king of Aram; or
  • Will we go to war again?

The king of Israel asks Jehoshaphat to help take control of Ramoth Gilead. Jehoshaphat agrees to help fight the Arameans but asks the king of Israel to consult Yahweh.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing the king of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I want Jehoshaphat to help me get control of Ramoth Gilead and make it part of Israel again; or
  • I am happy that Jehoshaphat wants to help me; or
  • I have lots of prophets who will bring messages to me from God. I do not care which god they talk to.

Ask the person playing Jehoshaphat, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am happy to help the king of Israel because our families are connected; or
  • It is important to consult Yahweh. I do not want to fight if Yahweh does not tell us to fight; or
  • I am worried that the king of Israel does not care about Yahweh.

The king of Israel asks about 400 prophets if Israel should fight Ramoth Gilead. The prophets say God will give control of Ramoth Gilead to the king of Israel. Jehoshaphat wants to ask one of Yahweh's prophets. The king of Israel does not like the prophet of Yahweh because the prophet of Yahweh does not say good things about the king of Israel. The prophet of Yahweh is called Micaiah. Jehoshaphat tells the king of Israel not to say bad things about Micaiah. The king of Israel asks officials to bring Micaiah.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing the king of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am excited. All the prophets say that I should attack Ramoth Gilead. God is with me and will give me the victory; or
  • Why does Jehoshaphat care about Yahweh? Yahweh's prophet never says anything good to me. I do not want to talk to him; or
  • If I want Jehoshaphat to help me, I will need to get Micaiah to come and prophesy.

Ask the person playing the 400 prophets, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Yes, go and fight Ramoth Gilead; or
  • You, the king of Israel, are powerful. Of course, you will win; or
  • The king is very powerful. We want to tell the king something that will make him happy; or
  • Listen to all of us. We can tell you what God is saying.

Ask the person playing Jehoshaphat, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • There are so many prophets, but I do not think they speak messages from Yahweh; or
  • I do not trust these prophets; or
  • I must hear from Yahweh before I go with Israel and take my army to fight Aram; or
  • The king of Israel does not respect Micaiah, Yahweh's prophet. I do not like what the king of Israel says about Micaiah.

Ask the person playing the officials of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Quick, we need to go and find Micaiah; or
  • We wonder what Micaiah will say this time. We hope Micaiah does not make our king angry.

As the kings wait for Micaiah to arrive, the two kings sit outside the city of Samaria with many prophets in front of them. One of the prophets, Zedekiah, has iron horns like a bull. Zedekiah says Yahweh says the kings will defeat the Arameans with the iron horns. The prophets make a loud noise and tell the kings to attack Aram at Ramoth Gilead because the kings will win the battle.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing Jehoshaphat, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • What a noise and strange sight. Are these men speaking from Yahweh or something else? or
  • This man, Zedekiah, is sure that Israel will win the battle; or
  • I need to hear from a prophet who speaks messages from Yahweh.

Ask the person playing the king of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • All these prophets are saying the same thing. They must be right! or
  • I feel excited and very important. Zedekiah says that I will win if I fight at Ramoth Gilead; or
  • Look at the iron horns that Zedekiah wears! I will push back and hurt the Arameans like a bull pushes and hurts people.

Ask the person playing Zedekiah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Look at me! I know that Israel will be strong and dangerous like a bull; or
  • Listen to me and my message. It is time for Israel to take back Ramoth Gilead; or
  • The other prophets say the same as me. I feel powerful and excited.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 22:1–12 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

For three years, there was no war between Israel and Aram. Use the same words or phrases for war, Israel, and Aram as you used in previous passages. For more information on Israel, refer to the Master Glossary.

The king of Israel talked to his officials. Use the same word or phrase for king as you used in previous passages. For more information on king, refer to the Master Glossary. The officials are important people who serve the king and help run the country. The officials are not domestic servants. Use the same word or phrase for officials as you used in previous passages.

Jehoshaphat, king of Judah, said that his horses were Israel's horses. Use the same words or phrases for Judah and horses as you used in previous passages. For more information on Judah, refer to the Master Glossary.

Jehoshaphat told the king of Israel to seek counsel, or a "word," from Yahweh. Yahweh is the personal name for God. In this passage, the storyteller calls the God of Israel Yahweh. Be sure to translate Yahweh in the same way as you have in previous passages, and remember that Yahweh is in the Master Glossary. When Yahweh gives a word, he gives a message. Jehoshaphat wanted Yahweh to give Yahweh's word, or message, to them. Use the same word or phrase for word, or counsel, from Yahweh as you have used in previous passages, and refer to word in the Master Glossary for more information.

The king of Israel gathered the prophets. Use the same word or phrase for prophets as you used in previous passages. For more information on prophet, refer to the Master Glossary.

The prophets say that the "Lord," their word for the most powerful being, will give Aram to the king of Israel. In the original language, the prophets use the word "Adonai" for God. Use the same word or phrase for Lord as you used in previous passages. For more information on Lord and Adonai, refer to the Master Glossary.

God will give Ramoth Gilead into the king's hand or into the king's power. Use the same word or phrase for "into the king's hand," when it means into the king's power, as you used in previous passages.

The king of Israel says that Micaiah never prophesies anything good about the king. Use the same word or phrase for prophesies as you used in previous passages. For more information on prophesy, refer to the Master Glossary.

The kings are dressed in royal robes or clothes. Something is royal if a king or their close family uses the item. Use the same words or phrases for royal and robes as you used in previous passages. For more information on robes, refer to the Master Glossary.

The kings sit on their thrones or special seats. Use the same word or phrase for thrones as you used in previous passages.

The kings are at the threshing floor, the open space for people to separate grains from the outer parts of the plant.

Stop here and look at a picture of people using a threshing floor to separate wheat grains from other parts of the plant. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for threshing floor. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

The threshing floor is next to the entrance of the gate to the city. Use the same word or phrase for gate as you used in previous passages.

Zedekiah made iron horns. Zedekiah made from iron two horns like the horns on a bull. Use the same word or phrase for iron as you used in previous passages. The horns were not musical instruments.

Stop here and look at a picture of a bull with horns. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for horns. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Zedekiah tells the kings that Yahweh says the kings will gore or badly wound the Arameans with the horns. Use the same word or phrase for Arameans as you used in previous passages.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for gore. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 22:13–28
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 22:13–28 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 22:13–28 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 22:13–28 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

This passage follows on from the previous passage. The storyteller is telling us a story about the king of Israel and Jehoshaphat, the king of Judah, who consult prophets to find out if the kings and their fighting men should fight the Arameans at Ramoth Gilead. The kings and the prophets are at the city of Samaria in the kingdom of Israel. Syria is another name for Aram. Aramean people are the same people as Syrian people.

Stop here and look at a map of the kingdom of Israel and western Aram and find the cities of Samaria and Ramoth Gilead. Pause this audio here.

This story is made of two stories. Two different storytellers told stories about Micaiah and the kings at different times. One person put the two stories together. You will remember from the previous passage that one storyteller told a story about king Ahab of Israel. Another storyteller told a story about a king of Israel who was probably a different person. Many years later, another storyteller took the story from this book of 1 Kings and retold the story in the book of 2 Chronicles. There are a few differences between this story in 1 Kings and the other telling of the story in 2 Chronicles.

Stop here and listen to your translation of 2 Chronicles 18:9–27 in your own language, if possible. Pause this audio here.

At the beginning of this passage, the story continues from the time the kings are sitting at the threshing floor outside Samaria. Many people think that the second storyteller wrote this part of the story. A messenger, a person who carries a message, went to order Micaiah to come to the kings. The messenger speaks to Micaiah and tells Micaiah to pay special attention and know that all the prophets are saying the same thing. The prophets are all saying what the king of Israel wants to hear. The messenger tells Micaiah to give a message like the other prophets and say what the king wants to hear. Micaiah replies to the messenger and uses the words of a strong oath or promise. Micaiah promises to the messenger that Micaiah will only say what Yahweh wants Micaiah to say.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about a time when someone has told you to say something to keep another person happy. Maybe the person asked you to say something that was not true or to leave out some important information. Why did the person not want you to say the truth? Pause this audio here.

Micaiah comes to the king of Israel. The king of Israel asks Micaiah the same question that he asked to the 400 prophets. Micaiah answers with the same answer as the 400 prophets although Micaiah says Yahweh, not God, will give Ramoth Gilead to the king. Micaiah does not mean what he says. The king of Israel knew that Micaiah was not giving a true prophecy.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about a time when you heard someone talk, and you knew that the person did not mean what they said. The way they said it showed that they did not expect people to believe them. How did the person sound when they said the untrue thing? Pause this audio here.

The king of Israel is frustrated with Micaiah. The king rebukes Micaiah for not telling the truth. We do not know when or if Micaiah has told the king other things that are not true.

Micaiah answers the king of Israel's question with what Micaiah saw in a vision. We do not know when Micaiah saw this vision. Yahweh sends a vision to Micaiah to be a message for people. Micaiah saw the people of Israel spread out all over a hill. Micaiah uses special language to describe how the people look on the hill. Micaiah says the people look like a group of sheep who do not have a shepherd. Sheep live in open places with lots of grass and small plants. Normally, a shepherd works to keep sheep together to help protect the sheep and move them from one place to another. When no shepherd is with sheep, the sheep wander all over the place, do not stay together, and are not safe. Micaiah sees people, not sheep. The people who listen to Micaiah are used to seeing sheep on hills and know that it is wrong for a group of sheep to not have a shepherd.

Stop here and look at a picture of a group of sheep on a hillside and imagine the sheep all over the hillside instead of together in a group. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: If you see a group of people scattered over a large area, what might you compare those people to? How would you help someone understand what the group of people looked like? Pause this audio here.

Micaiah tells the king what Yahweh says to Micaiah. Yahweh says the people have no master, no person to tell them what to do and look after them. Yahweh says that each person is to go home in peace or safely and not fight.

The king of Israel then talks to Jehoshaphat, the king of Judah. The king of Israel uses a question that he does not expect Jehoshaphat to answer. The king of Israel asks, "Did I not tell you that Micaiah never prophesies anything good about me?" The king of Israel uses the question to say that Micaiah has done just what the king of Israel expected Micaiah to do.

Micaiah tells the king of Israel about another vision from Yahweh. Micaiah tells the king of Israel to hear a message from Yahweh. Micaiah talks about a vision. We do not know when Micaiah saw the vision. In the vision, Micaiah saw Yahweh sitting on a throne, a special seat for a king. Many heavenly beings stood around Yahweh on all sides of the throne. Yahweh asked the heavenly beings to say who will go to Ahab, the king of Israel, and try to persuade Ahab to attack Ramoth Gilead. Yahweh said that Ahab will die if Ahab attacks Ramoth Gilead. The heavenly beings gave different responses to Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about a time someone asked a group of people to do something that they did not want to do or would find it difficult to do. How did the group of people respond? What did the people say to each other or the person who asked the question? Pause this audio here.

In the vision, a "spirit," a heavenly being, stood in front of Yahweh and said it will entice the king of Israel. The spirit is a supernatural being who lives and serves Yahweh in heaven. The spirit is not the Holy Spirit or a demon. The spirit said it will be a deceiving spirit in the mouths of the king of Israel's prophets. Someone who deceives a person makes a person believe something that is not true. The spirit made the prophets believe what the prophets said. Yahweh knew that the spirit would persuade Ahab to attack Ramoth Gilead. Micaiah tells the king of Israel that Yahweh sent the spirit who made the prophets tell Ahab to attack Ramoth Gilead. Yahweh has decided and declared that Ahab will have a terrible end.

Zedekiah, the son of Kenaanah, is angry. Zedekiah slaps Micaiah on the face to insult Micaiah.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What ways do people use to insult other people in your community? Pause this audio here.

Zedekiah also uses a question that he does not expect Micaiah to answer. Zedekiah asks how or why the spirit of Yahweh has gone from Zedekiah to Micaiah. Zedekiah is not talking about the Holy Spirit or a demon. Zedekiah is talking about a spiritual being who carries the message from Yahweh. Zedekiah does not think that Micaiah has replaced Zedekiah as the main person to receive messages from Yahweh. Zedekiah thinks that he is better than Micaiah. Micaiah responds to Zedekiah and tells Zedekiah that Zedekiah will know the answer to his question on a day in the future. In the future, there would be a time when Zedekiah would hide in the most protected room inside a building or in the middle of the city. This may be when the Arameans defeat the Israelites or a time when Zedekiah hides from Israelites because Zedekiah gives false prophecies to the Israelites.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: If you want to hide from people who want to hurt you, where would you go to hide? Pause this audio here.

After Micaiah told the king a prophecy that the king did not like, the king of Israel gives an order to his official. The king of Israel tells the official to take Micaiah away and take him back to Amon and Joash. Amon was the governor or the most important official of the city of Samaria. We do not know if Joash was a son of the king. The king's son might be a special title for someone who is a royal official and responsible for safety in the city.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Who in your community is responsible to look after the safety of your people? What title or special name, if any, do they have? Pause this audio here.

The king of Israel commands Amon and Joash to put Micaiah in prison. A prison is a strong building. If an official puts a person in a prison, the person cannot leave the building. People will guard the person in the prison and there are locks or barriers on the door to stop the person from opening the door and leaving.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your community, what do you do with people who disobey the rules in your community or who might be a danger to other people in the community? Pause this audio here.

The king of Israel orders Amon and Joash to only feed Micaiah enough food, or bread, and water for Micaiah to stay alive. In Israel, bread that people make from wheat flour was not a special food.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What do you do with people who disobey your most important leader and say bad things about your most important leader? Pause this audio here.

The king wants the order to last until the time the king of Israel returns safely from attacking Ramoth Gilead.

Micaiah responds to the king of Israel's order to say that if the king comes back safely from the battle, Yahweh did not speak through Micaiah. Micaiah uses the outcome of the battle to show the people of Israel if Micaiah is a prophet of Yahweh or a false prophet. Micaiah then speaks to everyone who can hear him. Micaiah tells everybody to hear the warning from Yahweh.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 22:13–28 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has four scenes.

In the first scene: The messenger tells Micaiah to give the king the same prophecy as the other prophets. Micaiah says he will say what Yahweh tells him to say.

In the second scene: Micaiah says the false prophecy, but the king of Israel knows Micaiah is not giving a prophecy from Yahweh. Micaiah speaks about a vision from Yahweh where Micaiah sees Israelites who have lost their leader. The king of Israel is angry.

In the third scene: Micaiah speaks about another vision where Yahweh wants a spirit to persuade Ahab to attack Ramoth Gilead. Yahweh says Ahab will die if Ahab attacks Ramoth Gilead. A spirit agrees to deceive the king. Micaiah tells the king and everyone around the king that Yahweh put a spirit in the prophets to speak false prophecies. Zedekiah slaps and insults Micaiah. Micaiah says Zedekiah will understand one day in the future when Zedekiah hides away in an inner room.

In the fourth scene: The king of Israel orders people to take Micaiah back to Amon and Joash, and for Amon and Joash to put Micaiah in prison with basic food and water until the king of Israel returns from attacking Ramoth Gilead. Micaiah says if the king returns, Yahweh did not speak through Micaiah, and Micaiah warns everyone to listen to the prophecy.

The characters in this passage are:

  • A messenger
  • Micaiah
  • King of Israel
  • Yahweh
  • Heavenly beings
  • A spirit, one of the heavenly beings
  • Jehoshaphat, king of Judah
  • Zedekiah
  • And the king of Israel's official who takes Micaiah to Amon and Joash

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

This passage happens immediately after the events in the previous passage, when all the prophets began prophesying to the kings of Israel and Judah outside the gates of the city of Samaria. A messenger for the king of Israel went to get Micaiah and bring Micaiah to the kings. As the messenger and Micaiah are coming back to the kings, the messenger tries to give advice to Micaiah. The messenger uses special language to draw attention to the importance of what the messenger tells Micaiah. The messenger wants Micaiah to know that the words of the prophets are in unity, which means all the prophets say the same thing. The messenger wants Micaiah to say the same prophecy that the 400 prophets have said. The storyteller uses language that shows that Micaiah's response will not be what the messenger wants to hear. Micaiah will not agree with the messenger. Micaiah uses a strong promise to say that Micaiah will only say what Yahweh tells Micaiah to say. Micaiah says that he is sure Yahweh lives. In the same way that Micaiah is sure Yahweh lives, Micaiah is also sure that he will only say what Yahweh tells him to say.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What words or phrases do you use to make a strong promise or oath that you will do something? Pause this audio here.

Many people think that the first storyteller also wrote this part of the story. This is because this part follows on from the earlier part of the story when the king of Israel asked an official to bring Micaiah to the kings at the threshing floor. This part of the story tells us about when Micaiah came to the kings. The king of Israel asks Micaiah the same question he asked the 400 prophets. Micaiah tells the king to go up and attack and to be victorious, or win. Yahweh will give Ramoth Gilead into the king's hand or his power. Micaiah seems to say the opposite of what he means. Usually, a person talks like this to mock or insult someone. The king hears the mocking in Micaiah's voice and is frustrated with Micaiah.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do you use tone and body language to show that you are saying something that is not true or that you do not believe is correct? Try saying things to each other in this way. Pause this audio here.

The king uses the question, "How many times must I make you swear to only tell the truth?" to express how the king feels about Micaiah.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Have one person pretend to be frustrated with another person in the group and use questions to express how the first person feels about something the second person has done. An example might be for the person to say, "How many times do I need to ask you to put away your clothes?" or "Why do you always forget to do what I ask?" Notice if you use questions to show this frustration or if you show frustration in another way. Pause this audio here.

Micaiah then speaks about a vision from Yahweh. Micaiah saw the people of Israel scattered on the hills. The people are not together in one group but spread out. Micaiah says the people were like sheep without a shepherd. Yahweh told Micaiah that the people have no master or leader. Yahweh said to let each person go home in peace. When a person goes somewhere in peace, they do not fight, and they are safe.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How can you help people understand that Micaiah is describing a vision that Yahweh gave to Micaiah, compared to something that is actually happening in the story? Pause this audio here.

After Micaiah tells the king of Israel about the vision, the king of Israel speaks to Jehoshaphat, king of Judah. The king of Israel speaks about Micaiah. The king of Israel does not expect Jehoshaphat to answer the question, "Did I not tell you that he never prophesies anything good about me?"

Micaiah gives another prophecy. The second storyteller tells this part of the story for this passage. This part of the story originally followed on from when Zedekiah was prophesying to the kings on the threshing floor. The storyteller of 1 Kings now tells us this part of the story after Micaiah's first prophecy to the king of Israel. We do not know if the story of Micaiah giving this prophecy follows on from the story before, or if it is a completely different time Micaiah gave a prophecy to the king of Israel. Micaiah tells the people who listen to hear the word or the message from Yahweh. In Micaiah's vision, Yahweh sits on his throne. There are heavenly beings around Yahweh on the left and right of Yahweh. Micaiah describes the group of heavenly beings as a group that contains many, many beings. The heavenly beings serve Yahweh and do what Yahweh wants to be done. Yahweh asks all the heavenly beings if one of the beings will go to persuade Ahab to attack the people in Ramoth Gilead. Yahweh wants the heavenly being to persuade Ahab to do something by saying something that is not true. But in fact, Yahweh says Ahab will die if Ahab attacks Ramoth Gilead.

Micaiah uses special language to say the heavenly beings speak and that each being says a different thing. Each being says a different response to Yahweh.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: In your language, how would you describe it when lots of people talk at one time and give different responses to a question? Pause this audio here.

Then one of the beings, a spirit, moves to be in front of Yahweh. The spirit is a heavenly being, not the Holy Spirit or a demon. The spirit says that it will persuade Ahab to attack Ramoth Gilead. Yahweh asks the spirit how the spirit will entice Ahab. The spirit tells Yahweh that it will go from heaven to Ahab's prophets in Israel. In Israel, the spirit will make all Ahab's prophets say lies. Yahweh tells the spirit that the spirit will be successful and commands the spirit to go persuade Ahab.

Micaiah tells the king of Israel and the other people who listen to Micaiah that the spirit came to the prophets. The spirit made the prophets say untrue prophecies. Yahweh has decided that other nations will destroy and kill the king of Israel. Zedekiah, the son of Kenaanah, is one of the people listening to Micaiah. Zedekiah moves close to Micaiah and slaps or hits Micaiah in the face. A person slaps another person with an open hand, and it makes a loud noise when the hand hits the face.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What different words do you use when one person hits another person in the face? Pause this audio here.

Zedekiah uses a question to insult Micaiah. Zedekiah asks how the spirit from Yahweh went from Zedekiah to Micaiah. Zedekiah questions why Yahweh would speak to Micaiah and not Zedekiah. Micaiah replies to Zedekiah to say that Zedekiah will know the answer to Zedekiah's question one day in the future. Zedekiah will know when he goes to hide from people in a room away from the outside walls of the city or building. We do not know when Zedekiah knows the answer to his question or what happens to Zedekiah at that time.

Many people think that this last part of the story is part of the same story that the first storyteller tells. After Micaiah tells the king of Israel about the visions, the king of Israel is angry. The king of Israel tells his officials to take hold of Micaiah and take Micaiah back to Amon and Joash. The king of Israel tells the people who hold Micaiah to give a message to Amon and Joash. The king of Israel gives instructions to an official to give to Amon and Joash. The king of Israel uses special words to show that what follows is a royal command for Amon and Joash. The king wants Amon and Joash to put Micaiah in prison and only give him small amounts of bread and water to eat and drink. The king wants Micaiah to only have enough bread and water to keep Micaiah alive.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How would you describe the amount of food that Amon and Joash will give to Micaiah in prison? Pause this audio here.

The king wants Micaiah to be in prison until the king comes back safely after the king fights the Arameans. Micaiah speaks to the king of Israel to say that if the king comes back, Yahweh did not speak through Micaiah. Micaiah then speaks to everybody to tell everybody to hear or remember what Micaiah said.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How would you tell a large group of people to listen carefully and remember something that you say? Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 22:13–28 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has four scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • A messenger
  • Micaiah
  • King of Israel
  • Yahweh
  • Heavenly beings
  • A spirit, one of the heavenly beings
  • Jehoshaphat, king of Judah
  • Zedekiah
  • And the king of Israel's official who takes Micaiah to Amon and Joash

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

The messenger tells Micaiah to give the king the same prophecy as the other prophets. Micaiah says he will say what Yahweh tells Micaiah to say.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing the messenger, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am worried that Micaiah will make the king angry; or
  • I hope Micaiah will give a good prophecy like the other prophets; or
  • I do not think Micaiah will please my king today.

Ask the person playing Micaiah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I know that I will make the king angry again; or
  • I want to be faithful to Yahweh; or
  • Yahweh, please give me your words to speak today.

Micaiah says the false prophecy, but the king of Israel knows Micaiah is not giving a prophecy from Yahweh. Micaiah speaks about a vision from Yahweh where Micaiah sees Israelites who have lost their leader. The king of Israel is angry.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing Micaiah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I know that these words are not true. All of your prophets are telling you lies; or
  • I am worried and sad because I can see that my king will die; or
  • Thank you, Yahweh, for showing me your vision and giving me the words to tell the king.

Ask the person playing the king of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I do not like Micaiah; or
  • I want to hear what Yahweh thinks; or
  • This is what I expected. Now I feel angry and frustrated; or
  • Micaiah is embarrassing me in front of Jehoshaphat.

Ask the person playing Jehoshaphat, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • This will be interesting. Is Micaiah a prophet from Yahweh or just like all the other prophets? or
  • Micaiah is making his king angry. I want to hear from Yahweh; or
  • I feel relieved. This man is a prophet of Yahweh.

Micaiah speaks about another vision where Yahweh wants a spirit to persuade Ahab to attack Ramoth Gilead. Yahweh says Ahab will die if Ahab attacks Ramoth Gilead. A spirit agrees to deceive the king. Micaiah tells the king and everyone around the king that Yahweh put a spirit in the prophets to speak false prophecies. Zedekiah slaps and insults Micaiah. Micaiah says Zedekiah will understand one day in the future when Zedekiah hides away in an inner room.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing Micaiah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am scared, but I must tell the king about my vision from Yahweh; or
  • I know this truth offends everyone; or
  • Zedekiah hates me; or
  • One day, Zedekiah will know everything that he has done wrong; or
  • I feel sorry for Zedekiah. He will be afraid, and I hope he asks Yahweh for help.

Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Here is a final test for Ahab. I know Ahab will fail, and Ahab will die; or
  • Here are my words for Ahab and his people; or
  • I am pleased with Micaiah, who speaks the truth and brings my warning to Ahab.

Ask the person playing the heavenly beings, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We are here to serve Yahweh; or
  • How can we persuade Ahab? What is the best way to tempt Ahab to attack Ramoth Gilead?

Ask the person playing the spirit, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I have a good idea and know how I can entice Ahab; or
  • I am sad to know that I will be successful; or
  • It is always good to do what Yahweh wants.

Ask the person playing Zedekiah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel horrified. How can Micaiah say this about all of us? or
  • I am so angry with Micaiah; or
  • What does Micaiah mean? What day will come when I hide somewhere?

The king of Israel orders people to take Micaiah back to Amon and Joash, and for Amon and Joash to put Micaiah in prison with basic food and water until the king of Israel returns from attacking Ramoth Gilead. Micaiah says if the king returns, Yahweh did not speak through Micaiah and warns everyone to listen to the prophecy.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing the king of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Micaiah has gone too far. How can Micaiah say that all my prophets lied to me? or
  • I do not believe Micaiah; or
  • I have heard enough. Micaiah makes me angry; or
  • I hope Micaiah suffers while I go to fight Ramoth Gilead.

Ask the person playing the king of Israel's official, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Micaiah has offended my king; or
  • I must tell Amon and Joash the king's punishment of Micaiah; or
  • I am pleased to take Micaiah to be punished.

Ask the person playing Micaiah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I knew the king would be angry; or
  • I feel relieved that I spoke the truth, but I am frightened; or
  • Everyone will know that I spoke the truth when the king dies at Ramoth Gilead.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 22:13–28 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

The messenger spoke to Micaiah about the messages the prophets gave to the king. Use the same words or phrases for messenger, prophets, and king as you used in previous passages. For more information on "prophet" and "king," refer to the Master Glossary.

Micaiah uses the words, "As surely as Yahweh lives," as a promise or oath.

Stop here and discuss as a group what words or phrases you will use for this phrase. Look up oath in the Master Glossary. If you have already translated this phrase in another book of the Bible, use the same phrase that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Micaiah says he will say what Yahweh says to Micaiah. Use the same word or phrase for Yahweh as you used in previous passages, and remember that Yahweh is in the Master Glossary.

Yahweh will give Ramoth Gilead into the king's hand or into the king's power. Use the same word or phrase for "into the king's hand" as you used in previous passages.

Micaiah says he saw the people of Israel scattered on the hills. The people were spread out over the hill.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for scattered. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

The people looked like sheep without a shepherd. Use the same word or phrase for sheep as you used in previous passages. A shepherd is a person who protects and cares for sheep.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for shepherd. Look up shepherd in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Each person can go home in peace. The people can go home without fighting and be safe. Use the same word or phrase for peace as you used in previous passages. For more information on peace, refer to the Master Glossary.

Micaiah never prophesies anything good. Use the same word or phrase for "prophesies" as you used in previous passages. For more information on "prophesy," refer to the Master Glossary.

Micaiah tells the people to hear the word of Yahweh. Micaiah wants the people to hear Yahweh's message. Use the same word or phrase for "word" as you used in previous passages. For more information on "word," refer to the Master Glossary.

Yahweh sits on his throne, and the "host of heaven" is around Yahweh. Use the same word or phrase for throne as you used in previous passages. The host of heaven is the heavenly beings who do Yahweh's work.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for "host of heaven." Look up heaven in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this phrase in another book of the Bible, use the same word or phrase that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Yahweh asks for someone to persuade Ahab to attack Ramoth Gilead. When someone persuades someone else, they convince a person to do something. In this case, Yahweh wants someone to persuade Ahab to do something that will be bad for Ahab.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for persuade. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

A spirit came to Yahweh. The spirit is a supernatural being created by Yahweh who lives in heaven with Yahweh. It is not the Holy Spirit, and it is not evil.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for spirit. Look up spirit in the Master Glossary for more information. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Zedekiah is the son of Kenaanah. Use the same word or phrase for son as you used in previous passages. For more information on son, refer to the Master Glossary.

Zedekiah slapped Micaiah in the face.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for slapped. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Zedekiah will one day hide in an inner room. An inner room is a safe place inside a building. Use the same word or phrase for "inner room" as you used in previous passages.

Amon is the governor of the city. A governor is a senior official in charge of a city. Use the same word or phrase for governor as you used in previous passages.

The king of Israel wants Amon to put Micaiah in prison. A prison is a solid building that stops a person who is inside the building from going outside the building.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for prison. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

The king of Israel wants Micaiah to have a small amount of simple food to eat, like bread, and water. Use the same word or phrase for "bread" or food as you used in previous passages.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 22:29–40
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 22:29–40 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 22:29–40 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 22:29–40 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

This story finishes the story about the king of Israel and the prophet Micaiah. You will remember that the king of Israel is probably Ahab. The storyteller only connects Ahab to this story at the end of the story. You will remember that the king of Israel and Jehoshaphat, the king of Judah, want to hear from Yahweh before they go to attack the Arameans at Ramoth Gilead. Micaiah the prophet told the king of Israel that if the king attacked the Arameans, the king would die. After the king hears Micaiah's message from Yahweh, the king decides to ignore the prophecy and attack the Arameans at Ramoth Gilead. Syria is another name for Aram. Aramean people are the same people as Syrian people. Many years later, another storyteller took the story from this book of 1 Kings and retold the story in the book of 2 Chronicles. There are a few differences between this story in 1 Kings and the other telling of the story in 2 Chronicles.

Stop here and listen to your translation of 2 Chronicles 18:28–34 in your own language, if possible. Pause this audio here.

The king of Israel and Jehoshaphat go from Samaria in Israel to Ramoth Gilead. Ramoth Gilead is about 45 kilometers northeast of Samaria. The kings go to attack the Arameans in Ramoth Gilead.

Stop here and look at a map of Israel and find the cities of Samaria and Ramoth Gilead. Pause this audio here.

The king of Israel talks to Jehoshaphat. We do not know when the conversation between the two kings happens. The conversation might happen in Samaria or on the way to Ramoth Gilead. The king of Israel tells Jehoshaphat that he, the king of Israel, will go into the battle in disguise. The king will wear clothes and other items which do not make him look like a king. The king of Israel does not want the Arameans to recognise the king of Israel.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about a time when people or a person wore a disguise. Why did the person or people wear a disguise? Pause this audio here.

The king of Israel wants Jehoshaphat to wear royal robes in the battle. The king of Israel wants Jehoshaphat to look like a king. The king of Israel puts on a disguise and starts to fight the battle at Ramoth Gilead. The king does not fight the Arameans alone. The king fights the battle with Jehoshaphat and soldiers from Israel and Judah.

Before the battle starts, the king of Aram gives orders or commands to his 32 leaders. The 32 men are commanders or captains who ride chariots. The commanders lead the Aramean fighters in the battle and tell the fighters what to do.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Who leads the people in your army or group of fighters? What special title do these people have to show that they have authority over the other fighters? Pause this audio here.

The commanders fight from chariots. A chariot is an open, two-wheeled vehicle. Horses pull the chariot along, and a person or people ride in the chariot and fight from the chariot. A person in the chariot uses strips of leather that go from the horses' heads to the person's hands to guide the horses. The strips of leather are reins. A soldier in a chariot moves quickly in battle.

Stop here and look at a picture of a soldier in a chariot with horses. Pause this audio here.

The king of Aram tells the commanders not to fight with anybody who is not the king of Israel. The king of Aram does not want the commanders to fight with everyone: not the small, ordinary soldiers or the great, important army commanders from Israel.

In the battle, the commanders see Jehoshaphat. Jehoshaphat looks like a king. The commanders think Jehoshaphat is the king of Israel. The commanders tell the people who drive the chariots to turn the chariots. The commanders go towards Jehoshaphat to attack Jehoshaphat. Jehoshaphat cries out or gives a shout. We do not know why Jehoshaphat shouts. It is possible that Jehoshaphat shouts a battle cry, a special phrase to encourage the fighters of Judah. The shout tells the Arameans that they are about to fight the wrong person.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about the battle cries that your people use now or in the past to encourage each other in battles. How do the battle cries help to identify the people fighting in the battle? Pause this audio here.

When the commanders hear Jehoshaphat, the commanders know that Jehoshaphat is not the king of Israel. Now, the commanders do not follow or try to fight Jehoshaphat. The battle continues.

An Aramean soldier draws his bow. The soldier pulls back on the bow string and fires an arrow from the bow into the battle.

Stop here and look at a picture of a man drawing a bow to fire an arrow. Pause this audio here.

The soldier does not know that he hits the king of Israel with the arrow. The Aramean soldier hits the king of Israel by chance.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about a time when something happened without a person meaning to make it happen or possibly by accident. Pause this audio here.

The arrow hits the king of Israel between the pieces of the king's armor. People wear armor to protect themselves in a battle. People make armor from hard leather or flat or shaped pieces of metal. People connect the armor together with straps. There are small gaps between the pieces of armor. A breastplate is a special piece of armor that covers the chest of a person.

Stop here and look at a picture of a soldier with armor. Notice the pieces of armor, the breastplate, and the gaps between the different pieces or sections of armor. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What do your soldiers wear to protect their bodies during a battle? How do your soldiers connect the different pieces of armor together? Which areas of the body does armor not protect? Pause this audio here.

The king of Israel is in a chariot. Another person drives the chariot for the king of Israel. The king of Israel tells the driver to use his hands to pull on the reins to the horses and turn the chariot around.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Have one person pretend to be a horse and one person be the driver of the horse. Take two long pieces of string, rope, or fabric and give one pair of ends to the "horse" and another pair of ends to the "driver." Have the "driver" pull on one string to turn the "horse" to one side. Then use the other string to turn the "horse" to the other side. Pause this audio here.

The king tells his driver that the king is wounded and hurt. The battle continues with people fighting each other. The king of Israel stays in his chariot. Someone helps the king of Israel to stay upright. The king uses the chariot to support himself. The king of Israel looks towards the Arameans in the battle.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Find a cup or something similar to use as a chariot. Now get something about the length and width of your thumb and place it in the cup. Find a way to make your item stay upright in the cup. What do you need to make it stay upright and face one way? Pause this audio here.

The king of Israel bleeds from the wound on his body. The blood runs out of his body and onto the bottom of the chariot. The king of Israel dies late in the afternoon before the sun goes down. At sunset, soldiers start to shout to each other to go back to their hometowns and home countries.
