People start shouting the order in one part of the army, and then other people shout the same order to pass the order to more people.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about a time when you were in a crowd of people, and some people shouted something on one side of the crowd, and more people passed the message on through the crowd to reach everyone. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller ends the story of how the king of Israel died and tells us that people took the king's body to Samaria. It was the custom for the Israelite people to bury dead people in the hometown of the dead person. We do not know who took the king's body to Samaria and buried him in Samaria. The king of Israel's people put the king's body in a grave or tomb, a place where people bury a dead body. The king's servants took the king's chariot to a public pool of water in Samaria to wash the chariot. There was a pool of water in the north-west corner of the city of Samaria. The public pool was a place most people visited and used for washing their bodies and their clothes.

Stop here and look at a picture of a public pool like the one people used to wash the chariot in the story. Pause this audio here.

Prostitutes used the same pool to wash themselves. A prostitute is a woman who has sexual intercourse with people for payment. We do not know if the prostitutes wash themselves in the pool at the same time as the servants clean the chariot, or if the prostitutes wash themselves with the king's blood in the water. At the time of the story, some people believed the king's blood had special power that might help or heal people if they washed with it. The Israelites thought prostitutes were wicked people. The dogs lick the king's blood. It is likely that the dogs in the story did not belong to people and lived on the streets of the city. At the time of the story, the Israelites thought a dog was an unclean animal. Dogs use their tongues to lick and take water into their mouths. The storyteller tells us that this fulfills Yahweh's message in Elijah's prophecy to Ahab in an earlier story in 1 Kings 21 verse 19.

Stop here and look at a picture of a dog licking up water to drink. Pause this audio here.

The storyteller uses a formal set of words to end the stories of Ahab, king of Israel. The storyteller reminds us of some of Ahab's significant achievements. Ahab built a palace and decorated it with ivory. Ahab did not build the palace himself, and the builders did not make the palace out of ivory. The builders probably used a lot of ivory inside the palace to make the palace very beautiful. Ivory is hard and creamy-white in colour. Ivory comes from the tusks, special teeth, of some animals, including elephants, hippopotamus, and warthog. People carve patterns into ivory and use ivory to decorate furniture and wooden structures.

Stop here and look at a picture of an elephant with long tusks of ivory. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and look at a picture of wood that people have decorated with carved ivory. Pause this audio here.

Storytellers made a written record of the events in Ahab's reign in the scroll, or book of the history of the kings of Israel. This book no longer exists and is not the books of Chronicles in the bible. The storyteller of this book ends the story by telling us that Ahab rested with his ancestors. You will remember that this is a formal way to tell us that Ahab died. Ahaziah was the next king of Israel after Ahab. Ahab was the father of Ahaziah.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 22:29–40 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has nine scenes.

In the first scene: The king of Israel and Jehoshaphat, the king of Judah, go to attack Ramoth Gilead. The king of Israel tells Jehoshaphat to wear royal robes in the battle, but the king of Israel will wear a disguise.

In the second scene: The king of Israel puts on a disguise and attacks Ramoth Gilead.

In the third scene: Before the battle, the king of Aram tells his chariot commanders to only fight the king of Israel.

In the fourth scene: In the battle, the chariot commanders see Jehoshaphat and attack him. Jehoshaphat shouts, and the Arameans realise it is not the king of Israel and stop chasing Jehoshaphat.

In the fifth scene: An Aramean soldier fires an arrow from his bow into the battle. The arrow hits and hurts the king of Israel. The king's chariot driver takes the king away from the battle. Someone or something holds the king upright in his chariot, and the king watches the battle.

In the sixth scene: The king's blood runs onto the chariot, and the king dies just before sunset.

In the seventh scene: At sunset, the soldiers from Israel and Judah shout to tell each other to leave the battle and go home.

In the eighth scene: Israelites take the king's body to Samaria and bury the body. The king's servants wash the king's chariot in a public pool in Samaria.

In the ninth scene: The storyteller reminds us that people wrote of the events of Ahab's reign in the records of the kings of Israel. Ahab is dead, and Ahaziah is the new king.

The characters in this passage are:

  • King of Israel
  • Jehoshaphat, king of Judah
  • King of Aram
  • Thirty-two chariot commanders
  • Aramean soldier who hits the king of Israel with an arrow
  • The king of Israel's chariot driver
  • The soldiers in the armies of Judah, Israel, and Aram
  • Israelite servants
  • Prostitutes
  • The storyteller
  • And Ahaziah

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In this passage, the storyteller continues the story of the king of Israel and Jehoshaphat. After they hear Micaiah's prophecies, the king of Israel and Jehoshaphat go up to Ramoth Gilead to attack the Arameans at the city. Before the battle, the king of Israel talks to Jehoshaphat about the plan for the battle. The king of Israel will wear a disguise when he goes into the battle. The king of Israel wants Jehoshaphat to wear royal robes. The storyteller then tells us that the king of Israel puts on a disguise and goes to fight the Arameans at Ramoth Gilead.

In the next scene, the storyteller tells us about the king of Aram's plan. Before the battle, the king of Aram gives an order to his chariot commanders. The king does not want the commanders to fight with anyone except the king of Israel. The king of Aram uses the special phrase "small or great" to mean everyone.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What words or phrases do you use to mean everyone who is in a place or at an event? Pause this audio here.

At the battle at Ramoth Gilead, the chariot commanders obey the order from the king of Aram and look for the king of Israel. The commanders see Jehoshaphat and think that Jehoshaphat is the king of Israel. The commanders turn or move to attack Jehoshaphat. Jehoshaphat shouts or cries out. The chariot commanders hear Jehoshaphat and know that Jehoshaphat is not the king of Israel. The chariot commanders stop following Jehoshaphat.

In another part of the battle, an Aramean soldier pulls the string of his bow back to fire an arrow.

Stop here and look at a picture of a man drawing a bow to fire an arrow, if needed. Pause this audio here.

The soldier releases the arrow into the battle. The soldier's arrow strikes the king of Israel by chance. The soldier does not plan to hit the king of Israel with the arrow.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do you describe something that happens by chance? Pause this audio here.

The soldier's arrow hits the king of Israel between the pieces of the king's armor and the king's breastplate.

Stop here and look at a picture of a soldier with armor, if needed. Pause this audio here.

After the arrow hits and hurts the king of Israel, the king of Israel tells his chariot driver to turn the chariot around. The king wants the driver to turn the chariot away from the battle and drive the chariot somewhere away from the fighting.

The battle lasts all day, and people fight hard all day.

Stop here and do this activity as a group: Talk about how fighting in a battle can change from being less intense to more intense. Notice how you describe soldiers fighting more and harder with each other. Pause this audio here.

The king of Israel stays in his chariot, and someone helps the king of Israel to be upright in the chariot and face towards the battle. The king of Israel uses the chariot for support.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about a time someone needed to use an object to help them keep standing upright. Notice what words you use to describe how the person used the object to help them stand. Pause this audio here.

As the king stands in his chariot, the blood from the wound on the king's body flows out and onto the flat bottom or base of the chariot. The king dies in late afternoon when the sun is getting low in the sky. As the sun sets, soldiers shout to pass the message to go home to other soldiers from Israel and Judah. The message passes from one side of the battle to the other.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What words do you use to describe or name the different times as the afternoon ends and sunset happens? Pause this audio here.

After the battle, soldiers take the king of Israel's body to Samaria and bury the body. Israelite people take the king's chariot to a big pool in Samaria to wash the chariot clean. At the pool, prostitutes use the water to bathe and wash themselves. Dogs lick up the blood of the king of Israel from the chariot. The storyteller reminds us that this happens as Yahweh told Ahab it would happen.

The storyteller ends the story and uses the same formal phrase to finish telling a story of a king's life. The phrase contains a question to which the storyteller does not expect an answer. The storyteller uses the question to tell a person who hears the story to find out more information about Ahab in the scroll, or book of the history of the kings of Israel. The storyteller uses the words, "Ahab rested with his ancestors," as a polite way to say Ahab is dead.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What different ways do you have to say someone is dead? Which ways, if any, are more polite than the others? Pause this audio here.

The storyteller tells us that Ahaziah is the next king of Israel.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 22:29–40 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has nine scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • King of Israel
  • Jehoshaphat, king of Judah
  • King of Aram
  • Thirty-two chariot commanders
  • Aramean soldier who hits the king of Israel with an arrow
  • The king of Israel's chariot driver
  • The soldiers in the armies of Judah, Israel, and Aram
  • Israelite servants
  • Prostitutes
  • The storyteller
  • And Ahaziah

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

The king of Israel and Jehoshaphat, the king of Judah, go to attack Ramoth Gilead. The king of Israel tells Jehoshaphat to wear royal robes in the battle, but the king of Israel will wear a disguise.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing the king of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I want Ramoth Gilead back. I do not care about Micaiah's prophecies; or
  • I am feeling clever. I will hide, and the Arameans will not find me; or
  • Jehoshaphat will look like a king. The Arameans will try to kill Jehoshaphat and not me.

Ask the person playing Jehoshaphat, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I want to help the king of Israel get Ramoth Gilead back from the king of Aram; or
  • It is very strange that the king of Israel does not want to look like a king in the battle; or
  • I want my soldiers to see me and know I am with them in the battle.

The king of Israel puts on a disguise and attacks Ramoth Gilead.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing the king of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • The Arameans cannot see where I am; or
  • I will be safer in ordinary clothes; or
  • It is time to get my city back.

Before the battle, the king of Aram tells his chariot commanders to only fight the king of Israel.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing the king of Aram, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I want the king of Israel to be dead; or
  • My best fighters must look out for the king of Israel.

Ask the person playing the 32 chariot commanders, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We are proud to serve our king; or
  • We will find the king of Israel; or
  • We will kill the king of Israel and keep Ramoth Gilead for Aram.

In the battle, the chariot commanders see Jehoshaphat and attack him. Jehoshaphat shouts, and the Arameans realise it is not the king of Israel and stop chasing Jehoshaphat.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing the 32 chariot commanders, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We can see the king! Let us go and kill him; or
  • We think we are wrong. The person does not sound like the king of Israel; or
  • Turn away! We will not chase this person. We need to find the king of Israel.

Ask the person playing Jehoshaphat, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I want to help Israel get back Ramoth Gilead; or
  • Attack the Arameans! or
  • Come and help me! or
  • Why did those soldiers turn away from me?

An Aramean soldier fires an arrow from his bow into the battle. The arrow hits and hurts the king of Israel. The king's chariot driver takes the king away from the battle. Someone or something holds the king upright in his chariot, and the king watches the battle.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing the Aramean soldier, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I will defend Ramoth Gilead; or
  • There are so many Israelites that I do not need to aim; or
  • I wonder if my arrows are stopping Israelites?

Ask the person playing the king of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • Someone hit me with an arrow! I need to leave the battle and go somewhere safe; or
  • I feel worried. I am bleeding a lot; or
  • I want to watch the battle, but I feel weak and cannot stand up.

Ask the person playing the king of Israel's chariot driver, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I will take my king out of the battle; or
  • I am worried about my king. The arrow has hurt the king.

The king's blood runs onto the chariot, and the king dies just before sunset.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing the king of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel weak; or
  • I can see my blood around my feet; or
  • Yahweh was right, I am going to die today; or
  • I should have listened to Micaiah.

Ask the person playing the armies of Judah, Israel and Aram, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We will fight to win back Ramoth Gilead for Israel; or
  • Keep fighting, our king is watching us; or
  • Keep fighting, the sun is going down, and we will win today.

At sunset, the soldiers from Israel and Judah shout to tell each other to leave the battle and go home.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing the soldiers of Israel and Judah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • The king of Israel is dead, and we have lost the battle! or
  • We are frightened; or
  • We need to go home to our own towns in our own country.

Israelites take the king's body to Samaria and bury the body. The king's servants wash the king's chariot in a public pool in Samaria. Prostitutes wash their bodies in the pool, and dogs lick the blood.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing the soldiers of Israel and Judah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We must take the king's body back to Samaria so that people can bury the body in the right place.

Ask the person playing the Israelite servants, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • We want to wash all the blood off the chariot; or
  • We are sad that our king died and our soldiers lost the battle for Ramoth Gilead.

Ask the person playing the prostitutes, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • What is happening today? or
  • The king of Israel's blood is in the water of the pool that we wash in; or
  • Dogs are licking the blood from the chariot; or
  • How amazing that the blood of the king is here in this dirty and public place.

The storyteller reminds us that people wrote of the events of Ahab's reign in the book of the history of the kings of Israel. Ahab is dead, and Ahaziah is the new king.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing the storyteller, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • There is more to learn about Ahab in the records of the kings; or
  • This is the end of King Ahab. I hope the people who hear the story learn from the story.

Ask the person playing Ahaziah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am sad that my father did not win the battle at Ramoth Gilead; or
  • Now I am king.

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 22:29–40 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

The king of Israel and the king of Judah went to attack Ramoth Gilead. Use the same words or phrases for king, Israel, and Judah as you used in previous passages. For more information on king, Israel, and Judah, refer to the Master Glossary.

The king of Israel will wear a disguise.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for disguise. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

Jehoshaphat will wear royal robes. Use the same word or phrase for royal robes as you used in previous passages. For more information about robes, refer to the Master Glossary.

The king of Aram gives orders to his chariot commanders. Use the same words or phrases for chariot and commanders as you used in previous passages.

Someone drew his bow. The man pulled back on the string of the bow and shot an arrow.

Stop here and look at a picture of a man drawing a bow to fire an arrow, if needed. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for drew his bow. If you have already translated this phrase in another book of the Bible, use the same word or phrase that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

The arrow hit the king of Israel between the sections or pieces of his armor. Use the same word or phrase for armor as you used in previous passages.

The king spoke to his chariot driver. The chariot driver is the person who guides the horses that pull the chariot.

Stop here and discuss as a group what word or phrase you will use for chariot driver. If you have already translated this word in another book of the Bible, use the same word that you have used there. Pause this audio here.

The dogs licked the king's blood as the word of Yahweh had said they would. Yahweh is the personal name for God. Use the same words or phrases for word and Yahweh as you used in previous passages. For more information on word and Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary.

Ahab decorated a palace with ivory. Use the same words or phrases for palace, or king's house, and ivory as you used in previous passages.

Ahab fortified cities. Ahab made the cities stronger. Use the same word or phrase for cities as you used in previous passages.

Stop here and discuss as a group how you will translate that Ahab made the cities stronger. If you have translated a phrase like this before, use the same phrase here. Pause this audio here.

The events of Ahab's reign are in the scroll, or book of the history of the kings of Israel. Use the same phrase for scroll, or book of the history of the kings of Israel, as you used in previous passages. For more information about scroll, refer to the Master Glossary.

Ahab rested with his ancestors. Use the same word or phrase for ancestors as you used in previous passages.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.

title: 1 Kings 22:41–53
content:
Hear and Heart

In this step, hear 1 Kings 22:41–53 and put it in your hearts.

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 22:41–53 three times, in three different translations, if possible.

Pause this audio here.

Now, as a group, discuss the following questions:

  1. What do you like in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  2. What do you not like or not understand in this passage? Pause this audio here.
  3. What does this passage tell us about God? Pause this audio here.
  4. What does this passage tell us about people? Pause this audio here.
  5. How does this passage affect our daily lives? Pause this audio here.
  6. Who do you know who needs to hear this passage?

Setting the Stage

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 22:41–53 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In the previous story, King Ahab of Israel and King Jehoshaphat of Judah went to fight the Arameans at Ramoth Gilead. Ahab disguised himself in battle, but an enemy soldier shot Ahab with an arrow. Ahab bled in his chariot and died at sunset. The people took Ahab's body to Samaria, and dogs licked up Ahab's blood, just as Yahweh promised. Ahab's son Ahaziah became king of Israel.

In this story, the storyteller summarizes the reign of King Jehoshaphat of Judah and introduces the reign of King Ahaziah of Israel. A different storyteller also tells the story of Jehoshaphat's rule in 2 Chronicles 20:31–37.

When Ahab has been king of Israel for four years, Jehoshaphat, son of Asa, becomes king over Judah. Jehoshaphat is 35 years old when he becomes king of Judah, and Jehoshaphat rules in Jerusalem for 25 years, from around the year 872 to 848 before Jesus. Jerusalem was the capital of Judah, from where the kings of Judah ruled. Jehoshaphat's mother's name is Azubah. Shilhi is Azubah's father.

Stop here and, as a group, locate Jerusalem in Judah on the map. Pause this audio here.

Jehoshaphat follows his father Asa's good example in everything. Jehoshaphat does not leave his father's ways. Jehoshaphat does what Yahweh considers right. However, Jehoshaphat does not remove all the shrines on the hilltops where people worship other gods. In 2 Chronicles 17:6, the storyteller says that earlier in Jehoshaphat's reign, Jehoshaphat removed the shrines on hilltops from Judah and tried to lead the people of Judah back to Yahweh. But the people of Judah later rebuild the high places, or shrines on hilltops, and Jehoshaphat does not fully destroy the high places for the rest of his reign. The people of Judah continue to sacrifice animals and burn sweet-smelling spices on altars at those places. The people of Judah often worship Yahweh at shrines on hilltops, but that worship is wrong. Yahweh wants the people of Judah to worship only Yahweh and to worship Yahweh only at the temple in Jerusalem. The people of Judah disobey God when they worship at high places.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: What gods do people in your people group worship apart from Yahweh? Where and how do people worship the other gods? Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Talk about a king or leader from your group or another group who took away all the places where people worshiped other gods so everyone would worship only Yahweh. Pause this audio here.

Jehoshaphat has a peaceful relationship with the king of Israel. The storyteller does not say which king of Israel the storyteller means. The storyteller may mean King Ahab. However, the phrase "king of Israel" more likely describes all the kings of Israel who ruled at the same time as Jehoshaphat, including Ahab, Ahaziah, and Joram. Before the rule of King Jehoshaphat of Judah, the kingdom of Judah and the kingdom of Israel usually acted like enemies and behaved in hostile ways toward each other. Jehoshaphat shows a friendly attitude toward Israel, and this friendly attitude is good. However, Jehoshaphat goes too far. We know from another story that Jehoshaphat makes a marriage alliance with the family of King Ahab. Jehoshaphat allows his son Jehoram to marry a daughter of Ahab and Jezebel. This marriage joins God's people to idol worship, and God forbids idol worship.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: Tell a story about a time when a leader of your people group made an alliance with another people group, and this alliance caused bad things to happen to your people. Why was it a bad idea to work together with those people? Pause this audio here.

Now the storyteller summarizes and evaluates the reign of King Jehoshaphat. The storyteller says that people record the other events during Jehoshaphat's reign in the book or scroll called "History of the Kings of Judah." The people also write about the great things Jehoshaphat did and his military victories in this book. This book records the actions of the kings of Judah during the reigns of the kings of Judah.

Now the storyteller begins again to talk about events in Jehoshaphat's life. Jehoshaphat removes all the male shrine prostitutes who are still in Judah from the time of his father, King Asa. Shrine prostitutes are either men or women who work in temples and have sex as part of pagan religious ceremonies. The original language can mean that Jehoshaphat either puts these people to death or sends them out of Judah.

Now the storyteller gives background for the next event in Jehoshaphat's reign. The storyteller says that Edom has no king of its own. Judah controls Edom at this time, so a deputy king, whom Jehoshaphat appoints, rules Edom. Edom lies south of Judah and stretches down toward the Red Sea. Jehoshaphat makes ships of Tarshish to carry gold from the land of Ophir. "Ships of Tarshish" means large, strong trading ships that people made for long sea trips. We do not know where Ophir was, but it was probably in the southern part of the Arabian Peninsula. Ophir was a large land, not a city, and it was famous for its gold. However, the ships do not sail. The ships break into pieces at Ezion Geber, an important port city on the north side of the Gulf of Aqaba in Edom. We do not know what destroyed the ships, but natural events like a strong storm, powerful winds, or rough seas may have caused it.

Stop here and locate on the map Edom, the possible place where the land of Ophir was, and the port city of Ezion Geber. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a picture of a ship of Tarshish. Pause this audio here.

At that time, Ahaziah, king of Israel and son of Ahab, makes an offer to Jehoshaphat after the shipwreck. Ahaziah wants to persuade Jehoshaphat to build the ships again. Ahaziah suggests to Jehoshaphat that the servants of Ahaziah, or the sailors of Ahaziah, go with the servants of Jehoshaphat in the ships.

We know from 2 Chronicles 20:36–37 that the first time Jehoshaphat built the ships of Tarshish, Jehoshaphat did it in alliance with Ahaziah. This alliance did not please Yahweh, and so Yahweh caused the ships to break apart. Now Ahaziah suggests that Ahaziah and Jehoshaphat build the ships together again. But Jehoshaphat does not agree.

Stop here and, as a group, tell a story about a leader from your people group who said no to a business deal with a leader from another people group. Explain that your leader said no because they had learned a lesson from something that happened with that other leader before. Pause this audio here.

Then Jehoshaphat dies and goes to be with his ancestors. People bury Jehoshaphat in the city of his ancestor David, where people had buried Jehoshaphat's other ancestors. The City of David was part of Jerusalem and had become the burial place for the kings of Judah. And Jehoshaphat's son Jehoram becomes king of Judah in his place.

Stop here and, as a group, locate the City of David on a map of Jerusalem. Pause this audio here.

Now the storyteller introduces the reign of Ahaziah, king of Israel. Ahaziah, son of Ahab, becomes king over Israel in Samaria. Samaria is the capital city of Israel. King Ahab rules Israel from there. This happens in the 17th year of the reign of Jehoshaphat as king of Judah. Ahaziah rules Israel for two years from around the year 853 to 852 before Jesus.

Stop here and, as a group, locate Samaria in Israel on the map. Pause this audio here.

Ahaziah does evil things in Yahweh's sight. Ahaziah acts in the same bad way as his father, King Ahab, and his mother, Jezebel. King Ahab and Queen Jezebel worshipped Baal and encouraged the people of Israel to do the same, instead of worshiping Yahweh. Ahaziah also behaves like Jeroboam, the son of Nebat, who led the people of Israel into sin. You will remember that Jeroboam was the first king of the northern kingdom of Israel. Jeroboam set up golden calves and told the people of Israel to worship them instead of worshiping Yahweh.

Stop here and, as a group, talk about the rulers of your people group who passed bad behaviour from father to son and kept doing evil things from one ruler to the next. Pause this audio here.

Ahaziah serves Baal and worships him. Ahaziah makes Yahweh, the God of Israel, angry by doing all the same things that Ahaziah's father, King Ahab, did.

Defining the Scenes

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 22:41–53 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will define the scenes, characters, and setting of this passage. Then the group will visualize this passage.

This passage has 10 scenes.

In the first scene: The storyteller introduces the reign of King Jehoshaphat of Judah. When Ahab has been king of Israel for four years, Jehoshaphat, son of Asa, becomes king over Judah. Jehoshaphat is 35 years old when Jehoshaphat becomes king of Judah. Jehoshaphat rules in Jerusalem for 25 years. Jehoshaphat's mother's name is Azubah. Azubah is the daughter of Shilhi.

In the second scene: Jehoshaphat follows his father Asa's good example in everything. Jehoshaphat does not turn away from his father's ways. Jehoshaphat does what Yahweh says is right. However, Jehoshaphat does not remove all the high places. People still go to the high places to worship and continue to offer animals and burn sweet-smelling spices on altars at those places.

In the third scene: Jehoshaphat has a peaceful relationship with the king or kings of Israel.

In the fourth scene: Now the storyteller summarizes and evaluates the reign of King Jehoshaphat. People record the other events during Jehoshaphat's reign in the book called "History of the Kings of Judah." People also write about Jehoshaphat's accomplishments and his military victories in that book.

In the fifth scene: The storyteller begins again to talk about events in Jehoshaphat's life. Jehoshaphat removes all the male shrine prostitutes who are still in Judah from the time of his father, King Asa.

In the sixth scene: The storyteller gives background for the next event in Jehoshaphat's reign and says that Edom has no king of its own. A deputy king rules Edom. Jehoshaphat makes ships of Tarshish to carry gold from the land of Ophir. However, the ships do not sail. The ships break into pieces at the port of Ezion Geber.

In the seventh scene: Ahaziah, king of Israel and son of Ahab, makes an offer to Jehoshaphat after the shipwreck. Ahaziah wants to persuade Jehoshaphat to build the ships again, after the destruction of the ships that Jehoshaphat first built. Ahaziah suggests to Jehoshaphat that the sailors of Ahaziah go with the sailors of Jehoshaphat in the ships. But Jehoshaphat does not agree.

In the eighth scene: Jehoshaphat dies and goes to be with his ancestors. People bury Jehoshaphat in the city of David, Jehoshaphat's ancestor, where people buried Jehoshaphat's other ancestors.

In the ninth scene: The storyteller introduces the reign of Ahaziah, king of Israel. Ahaziah, the son of Ahab, becomes king over Israel in Samaria. This happens in the 17th year of the reign of Jehoshaphat as king of Judah.

In the tenth scene: Ahaziah does evil things in Yahweh's sight. Ahaziah acts in the same bad way as his father, King Ahab, and his mother, Jezebel. Ahaziah also behaves like Jeroboam, the son of Nebat, who led the people of Israel into sin. Ahaziah serves Baal and worships Baal. Ahaziah makes Yahweh, the God of Israel, angry because Ahaziah does all the same things that Ahaziah's father, King Ahab, did.

The characters in this passage are:

  • King Ahab of Israel
  • King Jehoshaphat of Judah
  • All the kings of Israel who rule at the same time as Jehoshaphat: Ahab, Ahaziah, and Joram. The storyteller calls them "the king of Israel"
  • Deputy king of Edom
  • King Ahaziah of Israel
  • And King Jehoram of Judah, Jehoshaphat's son

As a group, pay attention to these parts of the passage's setting:

In this story, the storyteller sums up the reign of King Jehoshaphat of Judah and introduces the reign of King Ahaziah of Israel. A different storyteller also tells the story of Jehoshaphat's rule in 2 Chronicles 20:31–37. The storyteller follows the normal way of talking about a king's rule. The storyteller says how long Jehoshaphat rules and gives the name of Jehoshaphat's mother.

When Ahab has been king of Israel for four years, Jehoshaphat, son of Asa, becomes king over Judah. Jehoshaphat is 35 years old when Jehoshaphat becomes king of Judah. Jehoshaphat rules in Jerusalem for 25 years, from about the year 872 to the year 848 before Jesus. For the first three years, Jehoshaphat co-reigns with Jehoshaphat's father, Asa. For the last five years, Jehoshaphat probably co-reigns with his son Jehoram. Jehoshaphat's mother's name is Azubah. Azubah is the daughter of Shilhi, who is Azubah's father. People know Shilhi is Azubah's father and not her mother because, in the Bible, when a woman is called the daughter of someone, the name almost always refers to her father. That was the normal way to show family lines. Also, the words in the original language make it clear that Shilhi is a male.

Stop here and, as a group, locate Jerusalem in Judah on the map. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How do you introduce the reign or rule of a new king or leader when you tell a history story? What important information do you give to your audience? Pause this audio here.

Jehoshaphat follows his father Asa's good example in everything. Jehoshaphat does not turn away from his father's ways. Jehoshaphat does what is right in the eyes of Yahweh, which is what Yahweh says is right. The storyteller now uses contrast language to point out an exception to Jehoshaphat's good behaviour as a ruler. Jehoshaphat does not remove all the high places. People still go to those places to worship Yahweh and other gods. The people of Judah continue to offer animals and burn sweet-smelling spices on altars at those places.

Stop here and do this exercise as a group. Think of three leaders from your people group who were mostly good, but made one mistake. For each leader, say all the good things they did, and then tell the one bad thing they did. How do you show that the one bad thing the leader did was different than the rest of the things he did? Pause this audio here.

And Jehoshaphat makes peace with the king of Israel. The language that the storyteller uses may show that he sees Jehoshaphat's peaceful relationship with Israel as another mistake. This mistake connects to Jehoshaphat's failure to remove the hilltop shrines to idols. The phrase "to make peace" can also mean "to submit to someone" in some cases. Perhaps the storyteller criticizes Jehoshaphat and says that Jehoshaphat submits to the king of Israel. The storyteller does not say which king of Israel the storyteller means. The storyteller may mean King Ahab. However, the phrase "king of Israel" more likely refers to all the kings of Israel who rule at the same time as Jehoshaphat rules Judah. These kings include Ahab, Ahaziah, and Joram.

Now the storyteller uses the common formula to summarize and evaluate the reign of King Jehoshaphat. The storyteller uses a question that does not require an answer. The storyteller says, "Now the rest of the acts of Jehoshaphat, and his strength that he showed, and how he fought battles, have people not written about them in the book called History of the Kings of Judah?"

The storyteller begins again to talk about events in Jehoshaphat's life. Jehoshaphat removes all the male shrine prostitutes who are still in Judah from the time of his father, King Asa. The original language can mean that Jehoshaphat either puts the male cult prostitutes to death or sends them out of Judah. We know from 1 Kings 15:12 that Jehoshaphat's father, King Asa, forced the male shrine prostitutes out of the land of Judah, but perhaps some still remained. Jehoshaphat now removes these people completely.

Stop here and discuss this question as a group: How can you say that when Jehoshaphat removes the male shrine prostitutes, Jehoshaphat either puts these people to death or sends them completely out of Judah? Pause this audio here.

The storyteller gives background for the next event in Jehoshaphat's reign. The storyteller says that Edom has no king of its own. A deputy king, whom Jehoshaphat appoints, rules Edom. Edom lies south of Judah and stretches down toward the Red Sea. Jehoshaphat makes ships of Tarshish to carry gold from the land of Ophir. Jehoshaphat does not make the ships himself, but oversees his workers who make them. Ships of Tarshish were big, strong trading ships that could travel very far, even to a distant place called Tarshish. People made these ships from strong wood, often cedar. The ships usually had one large square sail, though some may have had two sails for longer journeys. We do not know where Ophir is, but it is probably in the southern part of the Arabian Peninsula. However, the ships do not sail. The ships break into pieces at the port of Ezion Geber, which is on the north side of the Gulf of Aqaba in Edom. We do not know what destroys the ships, but natural events like a strong storm, powerful winds, or rough seas may cause it.

Stop here and locate on the map Edom, the possible place where the land of Ophir was, and the port city of Ezion Geber. Pause this audio here.

Stop here and, as a group, look at a picture of a ship of Tarshish. Pause this audio here.

At that time, Ahaziah, king of Israel and son of Ahab, makes an offer to Jehoshaphat after the shipwreck. Ahaziah wants to persuade Jehoshaphat to build the ships again. Ahaziah suggests to Jehoshaphat that the servants of Ahaziah go with the servants of Jehoshaphat in the ships. Here, the word "servants" refers to sailors, or men who work on and steer the ships. Ahaziah suggests that Ahaziah and Jehoshaphat build the ships together. But Jehoshaphat does not agree.

Then Jehoshaphat dies and sleeps with his fathers, which means he goes to be with his ancestors. People bury Jehoshaphat in the City of David, Jehoshaphat's father, where people had buried Jehoshaphat's other fathers. In all these cases, the word "father" means ancestor. Then Jehoshaphat's son Jehoram becomes king of Judah in his place.

Now the storyteller introduces the reign of Ahaziah, king of Israel. The storyteller uses the usual formula that links the rule of Ahaziah with the rule of the king in Judah. Ahaziah, the son of Ahab, becomes king over Israel in Samaria. This happens in the 17th year of the reign of Jehoshaphat as king of Judah. Ahaziah rules Israel for about two years, from around 853 to 852 before Jesus.

Stop here and, as a group, find Samaria in Israel on the map. Pause this audio here.

Ahaziah does evil things in Yahweh's sight. Ahaziah acts in the same bad way as his father, King Ahab, and his mother, Jezebel. Ahaziah also behaves like Jeroboam, the son of Nebat, who led the people of Israel into sin.

Ahaziah serves Baal and worships Baal. The word "serves" means almost the same as "worships," but "serves" also means that Ahaziah places himself under Baal and gives Baal his loyalty. Ahaziah makes Yahweh, the God of Israel, angry because Ahaziah does all the same things that Ahaziah's father, King Ahab, did.

Stop here and discuss as a group: Think of words to explain how Ahaziah "serves" Baal and "worships" Baal. Make sure your words show the difference: "worships" means Ahaziah honors Baal, and "serves" means Ahaziah follows Baal and gives Baal his loyalty. Pause this audio here.

Now, the group should storyboard, draw, or use objects to visualize the passage and the action in it.

Embodying the Text

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 22:41–53 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will dramatize the passage.

This passage has 10 scenes.

The characters in this passage are:

  • King Ahab of Israel
  • King Jehoshaphat of Judah
  • All the kings of Israel who rule at the same time as Jehoshaphat: Ahab, Ahaziah, and Joram. The storyteller calls them "the king of Israel"
  • Deputy king of Edom
  • King Ahaziah of Israel
  • And King Jehoram of Judah, Jehoshaphat's son

As a group, act out the passage twice. You should act out the passage in your own language.

First, act out the passage without stopping. Pay attention to the dialogue, flow, plotline, and chronology of the passage. Make sure you do not skip acting out the difficult or important parts of the passage. Help each other remember every part of the passage.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage.

The group should act out this passage a second time. At certain points in the drama, pause the drama and ask the person playing the character, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person playing the character should answer what they would be feeling or thinking if they were that character. Then continue the drama.

Pause this audio here and act out the passage a second time.

The following is an example of the drama and possible responses.

When Ahab has been king of Israel for four years, Jehoshaphat, son of Asa, becomes king over Judah. Jehoshaphat is 35 years old when Jehoshaphat becomes king of Judah. Jehoshaphat rules in Jerusalem for 25 years. Jehoshaphat's mother's name is Azubah. Azubah is the daughter of Shilhi.

Jehoshaphat follows his father Asa's good example in everything. Jehoshaphat does not turn away from his father's ways. Jehoshaphat does what Yahweh says is right. However, Jehoshaphat does not remove all the high places. People still go to these shrines to worship, and people continue to offer animals and burn sweet-smelling spices on altars at these places.

And Jehoshaphat makes peace with the king or kings of Israel.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am pleased with King Jehoshaphat because he continues to do the good things that his father, King Asa, did. Jehoshaphat trusts me; or
  • I am very sad because Jehoshaphat does not completely remove the shrines on the hilltops. This means that people of Judah can still worship idols there; or
  • I am very angry, because the people of Judah worship me at the shrines on hilltops. The people know they can only sacrifice to me and pray to me at the temple in Jerusalem.

People record the other events during Jehoshaphat's reign in the book called "History of the Kings of Judah." People also write about Jehoshaphat's accomplishments and his military victories in that book.

Jehoshaphat removes all the male shrine prostitutes who are still in Judah from the time of his father, King Asa.

Edom has no king of its own. A deputy king rules Edom. Jehoshaphat makes ships of Tarshish to carry gold from the land of Ophir. However, the ships do not sail. The ships break into pieces at the port of Ezion Geber.

Ahaziah, king of Israel and son of Ahab, makes an offer to Jehoshaphat after the shipwreck. Ahaziah wants to persuade Jehoshaphat to build the ships again. Ahaziah suggests to Jehoshaphat that the sailors of Ahaziah go with the sailors of Jehoshaphat in the ships. But Jehoshaphat does not agree.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing King Jehoshaphat of Judah, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am upset that my ships of Tarshish did not sail after we built them. We wasted so much money to build the ships, and now we cannot even go to the land of Ophir to get gold; or
  • I am sad because it looks like Yahweh did not want me to build these ships. Yahweh stopped the ships from sailing; or
  • I will be careful not to upset Yahweh again. King Ahaziah of Israel wants me and him to build more ships together. I will not do it.

Ask the person playing King Ahaziah of Israel, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I feel hopeful. Perhaps King Jehoshaphat will want to build more ships of Tarshish with me; or
  • I am excited that Israel can hopefully make this business alliance with Judah. Judah is a strong country, and Yahweh blesses Judah. We can sail together to get gold; or
  • I am disappointed that Jehoshaphat refused my proposal to build ships together. Does Jehoshaphat not trust me?

Then Jehoshaphat dies and goes to be with his ancestors. People bury Jehoshaphat in the City of David, Jehoshaphat's ancestor, where people buried Jehoshaphat's other ancestors.

Ahaziah, the son of Ahab, becomes king over Israel in Samaria. This happens in the 17th year of the reign of Jehoshaphat as king of Judah.

Ahaziah does evil things in Yahweh's sight. Ahaziah acts in the same bad way as his father, King Ahab, and his mother, Jezebel. Ahaziah also behaves like Jeroboam, the son of Nebat, who led the people of Israel into sin. Ahaziah serves Baal and worships Baal. Ahaziah makes Yahweh, the God of Israel, angry because Ahaziah does all the same things that Ahaziah's father, King Ahab, did.

Pause the drama.

Ask the person playing Yahweh, "What are you feeling or thinking?" The person might answer things like:

  • I am so angry with the kings of Israel! Beginning with Jeroboam, the first king of the northern kingdom, they have worshipped other gods. And each king has followed in the evil steps of his father; or
  • I am overwhelmed with sadness. I love the nation of Israel so much and want to have a relationship with them. But the Israelites rejected me. The Israelites do not know me; or
  • I feel so sad that the wicked kings have led the people to serve and worship Baal. Do they not know that I am a just God and I will have to punish them for this?

Filling the Gaps

Listen to an audio version of 1 Kings 22:41–53 in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this step, the group will discuss key terms and words in this passage.

When Ahab has been king of Israel for four years, Jehoshaphat, son of Asa, becomes king over Judah. Jehoshaphat rules in Jerusalem for 25 years. Use the same words or phrases for king, Israel, Judah, and Jerusalem as you used in previous passages. For more information on king, Israel, Judah, and Jerusalem, refer to the Master Glossary.

The word son here means a biological son. Use the same word for biological son as you used in previous passages, and refer to son in the Master Glossary if needed.

Jehoshaphat follows his father Asa's good example in everything; Jehoshaphat does what is right in the eyes of Yahweh. Jehoshaphat does not turn away from his father's ways. Jehoshaphat does what Yahweh says is right. However, Jehoshaphat does not remove all the "high places," or places where people worship, often on the hilltops. People still go to these places to worship, and they continue to offer animals and burn sweet-smelling spices on altars at these places.

The word father here means biological father. Use the same word for father as you used in previous passages.

Use the same phrase for doing something good or evil "in the eyes of Yahweh" as you have used in previous passages.

Yahweh is the personal name of God. Use the same name for Yahweh as you used in previous passages. For more information on Yahweh, refer to the Master Glossary.

Use the same word or phrase for high places as you used in previous passages.

Use the same word or phrase for worship and altar as you used in previous passages. For more information on worship and altar, refer to the Master Glossary.

Jehoshaphat removes all the male shrine prostitutes who are still in Judah after the time of his father, King Asa. Use the same word or phrase for "male shrine prostitutes" as you used in previous passages.

Someone wrote everything that Jehoshaphat did in the book or scroll of the history of the kings of Judah. Use the same phrase for "book or scroll of the history of the Kings of Judah" as you have used in previous passages. Refer to scroll in the Master Glossary if needed.

Jehoshaphat makes ships of Tarshish, or trading ships, to carry gold from the land of Ophir. Your translation may just say trading ships. Use the same word or phrase for "trading ships," or "ships of Tarshish" as you used in 1 Kings 10:22.

Stop here and, if needed, look at a photo of a ship of Tarshish again as a group. Pause this audio here.

Ahaziah, king of Israel and son of Ahab, makes an offer to Jehoshaphat after the shipwreck. Ahaziah wants to persuade Jehoshaphat to build the ships again, after the destruction of the ships that Jehoshaphat first built. Ahaziah suggests to Jehoshaphat that the servants of Ahaziah go with the sailors of Jehoshaphat in the ships.

The word son here means a biological son. Use the same word for a biological son as you used in previous passages.

Use the same word or phrase for servant as you used in previous passages. Remember that Ahaziah calls his sailors "servants" here. For more information on servant, refer to the Master Glossary.

Then Jehoshaphat dies and sleeps with his fathers. People bury Jehoshaphat in the City of David, Jehoshaphat's father, where people had buried Jehoshaphat's other fathers. Then Jehoshaphat's son Jehoram becomes king of Judah in his place. The word father means ancestor in all three cases here. Use the same word or phrase for "City of David" as you used in previous passages. The word son here means a biological son. Use the same word for a biological son as you used in previous passages.

Ahaziah does evil things in Yahweh's sight. Ahaziah acts in the same bad way as his father, King Ahab, and his mother, Jezebel. Ahaziah also behaves like Jeroboam, the son of Nebat, who led the people of Israel into sin. Ahaziah serves Baal and worships Baal. Ahaziah makes Yahweh, the God of Israel, angry because Ahaziah does all the same things that Ahaziah's father, King Ahab, did. The word father means biological father in all three cases here. The word son here means a biological son. Use the same word for a biological son as you used in previous passages. Use the same words or phrases for evil, sin, and Baal as you used in previous passages. For more information on evil, sin, and Baal, refer to the Master Glossary.

Speaking the Word

Listen to an audio version of the passage in the easiest-to-understand translation.

Pause this audio here.

In this session, retell the passage, as a group, in your own language.

First, the whole group should practice telling the passage in your own language. Remember to include the things you decided in previous steps, like chronology, words, phrases, emotions, or anything else you discussed.

One group member should tell a few sentences of the passage and pause. Another group member should tell the next part of the passage and pause. Do this until the group has retold the whole passage. The group members can correct each other in this step.

Pause this audio here.

Next, each member of the group should tell the whole passage without interruption at least one time. A group member can use random objects, motions, or their storyboard to help them remember the passage.

Pause this audio here.

Next, the group can decide whose version of the passage you like the most. You may decide that you liked the way one group member told one scene but you liked the way a different group member told another scene.

Pause this audio here.

Finally, the group can put together a final version of the passage that everyone agrees on. Each group member should practice telling this final version of the passage.
